Tuesday, 6th March, 1934.
Senate-which had stood adjourned since

Tuesday, 20th March, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Education:
The Secondary School Examinations
Regulations, Amending Regulations No.'3.
Presented pursuant to Act and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Ministry of Finance:
Accounts
of the Government Loans
Fund established under the Government
Loans and Exchequer Provisions Act
(Northern Ireland), 1925, for the year
ended 31st March, 1933, together with
the Report of the Comptroller and
Auditor-General thereon.
Presented pursuant to Act and ordered
to lie on the Table.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Anderson's Divorce Bill.
The following Report was made from.
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:-
That they had considered Anderson's
Divorce Bill, and that the averments in
the Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the Bill, and made Amendments
thereto.
Gilbert's Divorce Bill.
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:-
That they had considered Gilbert's
Divorce Bill, and that the averments in
the Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the Bill, and made Amendments
thereto.
Henry's Divorce Bill.
-
The following Report was made from
the Joint Committee on Divorce Bills:
That they had considered Henry's
Divorce Bill, and that the averments in
the Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the Bill, and made Amendments
thereto.
Suspension of Standing Order.
Moved: "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's
sitting for the purpose of taking the
Second Reading of the Agricultural
Marketing Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Question to Minister.
SCHOLARSHIPS AT TRINITY
COLLEGE.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I beg
to ask the Leader of the House whether,
in view of the decision of a Tyrone
Regional Committee to award a scholarship at Trinity College, Dublin, which
was not objected to by the Ministry, he
will take steps to have these limited in
future to Northern Ireland, especially
in view of the probability that other
committees may allow them to be taken
up in the National University, of which
the present Free State head is
Chancellor.
as
In putting this question I should like
to explain that I have certainly no
grudge against Trinity College. I think
it is a college that every Irishman is
very proud of. It has produced some
very fine men in the past. It provided
us with our great leader, Lord Carson.
It was from Trinity College that Lord
Carson came to Duncairn to represent
that constituency and to continue
Leader of the Ulster Unionist Party.
But at the same time I was very much
surprised at the action of that particular
regional committee. I know that the
majority in the North of Ireland are
very proud of their own college and
university, and it is somewhat remarkable to award a scholarship to a college
outside Northern Ireland. If that is to
be continued it may lead us to the end
I have already pointed out, and that
would be a calamity, that is, sending
scholars to the National University. I
hope the noble Leader of the House will
be able to assure us that this practice
will not be continued in the North of
Ireland.
Agricultural Marketing Bill 70
Oral Answers
20 MARCH 1934
CHARLEMONT:
It has
Viscount
been the policy of the Ministry in the
past to allow the tenure of university
scholarships awarded by Education Committees in any approved university. In
pursuance of this policy, the Ministry
has approved the award of such scholarships to various universities outside
Northern Ireland, including Trinity
College, Dublin, and the National
University, Cambridge, Oxford, Strassburg, The Sorbonne and Madrid.
I am satisfied that it would not be
desirable to limit the powers of Education Committees in the manner suggested by the hon. Member.
Mr. CAMPBELL: This question
simply perplexes me and it has perplexed
me since I read it. What is the suggestion of my hon. Friend Senator
Joseph Cunnigham, who is personally
most genial, but politically a diehard?
It is that Trinity College should be taboo
to the Ministry of Education in Northern Ireland. What has Trinity Collge
done to deserve scholastic ostracism here?
It is true that for three and a half
centuries it has stood for everything my
hon. Friend holds dear. As he has just
told us it has been the university and
constituency of Lord Carson. Has it
undergone a sea change? Does it not
stand to-day where it always stood, and
why is Senator Cunningham, of all men,
chalking up on the walls of Northern
Ireland, "No Trinity College here"?
There was a famous cartoon in
"Punch" at the time the Ecclesiastical
Titles Act was passed. It depicted Lord
John Russell just after chalking up on
the wall "No Pope here" looking over
his shoulder and seeing a policeman, and
then preparing to run. May I administer one drop of balm to the hon. Senator?
He is in earnest dread, I see from the
end of the question, lest some regional
committee in the North of Ireland in a
lucid interval would allot a scholarship
at the National University. May I
exhort him not to break his sleep for
that, because, as far as I know, there
is no regional committee in the North
of Ireland winging for that flight of
generosity.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have agreed to the
Amendments made by the Joint Committee in Anderson's Divorce Bill,
Gilbert's Divorce Bill and Henry's
Divorce Bill. They have passed the
Agricultural Marketing Bill, to which
they desire the concurrence of this House.
Agricultural Marketing Bill.
Moved: "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
the Second Reading of this Bill I should
like to say that its general purpose is to
enable marketing boards established
under the Agricultural Marketing Act of
1933 to co-operate one with another, and
with marketing boards in Great Britain.
Such powers are in particular required
to resolve the difficulties which, as hon.
Members will no doubt recollect, arose
shortly after the inception of the Pigs.
and Bacon Marketing schemes. It will
be remembered that the volume of
potential home production proved to be
considerably in excess of that anticipated,
and as a result markets were overstocked
until the position was relieved by an
adjustment of the foreign import quotas.
The standard prices which had been
fixed were thus for a time rendered
uneconomic and it was necessary in order
to clear the markets, to indemnify curers
in respect of any losses which they were
shown to have sustained thereby. It is
proposed that such losses shall be assessed
by a committee containing representatives
of the Government and the Pigs and
Bacon Marketing Boards. The ascertained losses will be paid out of a loan
to be raised by the Bacon Marketing
Board under Government guarantee.
The Pigs Marketing Board will be responsible for providing interest and Sinking
Fund in respect of this loan, and will
be required to impose a special levy on
pig producers for the purpose.
The first five Clauses of the Bill are
designed not only to provide the necessary statutory authority for the carrying
out of this arrangement, but also to
enable marketing boards to join in taking
to overcome any similar
difficulties which may arise in the future.
measures
Hon. Members will, I am sure, readily
appreciate the necessity of the provision
contained in Clause 6, which empowers
71 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
marketing boards to co-operate with their
counterparts in Great Britain in the exercise of any powers conferred by the
United Kingdom Agricultural Marketing
(No. 2) Act of 1933. This will, for
example, enable the Northern Ireland
Pigs Marketing Board to sell surplus
Northern Ireland pigs to the Pigs
Marketing Board in Great Britain for
disposal to British curers.
In Clause 7 of the Bill certain modifications of the Act of 1933 which were
made by Order in Council under the
powers conferred by Section 20 of that
Act, are continued as if they had been
enacted therein. These modifications
were necessary in order to bring the Northern Ireland Act into conformity with
that to which the Royal Assent was given
in the United Kingdom Parliament at a
somewhat later date. The Clause also
makes some additional verbal alterations
in the Act of 1933, and revokes the power
to make modifications by Order in
Council. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not imagine for
a moment that anybody desires to impose
the smallest obstacle in the way of
the new marketing boards functioning
properly and obtaining some benefit and
relief for the unfortunate farmers of
Northern Ireland. But at the same time
I feel that I must point out that the Bill
before us is distinctly lacking in what I
may describe as clarity, and I cannot say,
speaking for myself, that the position has
been rendered very much clearer either
by the debates as reported in the newspapers which took place in another place
or perhaps by the speech of the noble
Viscount this afternoon.
The first thing that strikes one in
looking over the Bill before us is that
the Bill itself gives no indication whatever as to whether it is intended to relate
to schemes in esse or schemes in posse.
I think in fact from what one hears that
it is intended to refer to both. But if
that be so, as regards existing schemes,
what provision is there for laying an
amended scheme before Parliament? It
seems to me that if you take the first
Clause it is as good as any other-that
Clause provides that a scheme under the
Act of 1933 may provide for empowering
the board to pay compensation, and so on,
That provision is obviously not in any of
the existing schemes. It has got to be
put there by Parliament, and I should.
like to know what provision there is for
bringing the scheme so modified before
Parliament. The Act of 1933 is quite
plain as regards the necessity for laying
a scheme before Parliament and giving
Parliament power to reject it if it chooses.
But there is nothing said in the Act as
regards the submission of an amended
scheme, and I should like to know how
it is proposed to deal with that situation.
As regards the other point of whether
this Bill is to apply automatically to all
future schemes, I think it is a subject
which ought to be considered and discussed. I do not say that Parliament
ought to reject the proposal, but Parliament ought to consider and discuss
whether the powers which will flow from
this Bill will not be rather too wide, and
whether one can be satisfied that they
will always be exercised within the limits
which the producers of the particular product would like to see imposed, and by
boards acting really in accordance with
the wishes of their own constituents.
The particular Clause, perhaps, that
arouses attention in that way is Clause
5, which empowers the Ministry to
empower a board to make grants to other
boards, and to guarantee payments, and
so on. It goes on to say that the provision of the necessary money may be
enforced by order of the Ministry. When
the question of providing money arises the
only way money can apparently be produced in this country-though it seems
to be somewhat different in England-is
to enable a levy to be imposed. That is,
your pig producer has got to submit to a
further levy on his pig, the pig, perhaps,
which he sold eight or nine months ago.
That seems a little hard on the pig producer, because the one thing he did
expect to get out of the new legislation
was a definite fixed price for his pig. He
did get it, but it is obvious that if there
is now to be a levy imposed on him in
respect of some bonus, discount, or something or other under the powers of this
Act, that man is not going to get a fixed
price for that pig, and one would like to
know how it is proposed to justify the
further imposition.
The first Clause starts by allowing compensation to be paid in respect of any
loss which has been occasioned, and on
that Clause the question again arises,
does it refer to the past, or is it for the
73 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

20 MARCH 1934
Second Reading
future. The other Clauses, particularly
perhaps Clause 4, go in the same direction.
Clause 4 provides that where persons
have during the past purchased the
regulated product, they may be allowed
a discount or similar abatement in respect
of the fixed price of the product payable
for the said period. If you once let
that in, what becomes of your fixed price?
I am not at all certain that the best
way to get a full discussion and further
information on this matter might not be
at a further stage to put down a formal
motion that Clauses 4 and 5 should be
left out, because that would, I think,
enable us to have rather more information than we have at present, and information which, I think, we ought to have.
We ought to be told who is responsible
for incurring the expenditure for which
this Bill is apparently provided to meet,
and we ought to be told whether there is
reason for confidence that producers are
agreeable to the additional levy which,
it would seem, has got to be placed upon
them.
We are all agreed that the scheme or
the schemes flowing from the Act must be
fairly worked; that there must not be
any hanky-panky about it, and we hoped
everything was going to be done in that
way.
Almost immediately we were confronted with information in the papers
that so far from pigs being sold at a fixed
price some had been sold at prices
materially in advance of that fixed price.
There were, apparently, some breaches of
the law, but the effect is to introduce
again that element of uncertainty which
it was the very intention of the Act of
1933 to obviate. The idea was to give
the farmer a fixed price and a fairly
remunerative price to encourage him to
produce pigs. I can observe traces in the
Bill of a possibility that what is usually
described as a slow stroke may be asked
for, that production may be limited.
That, I think, would be unfortunate,
and I do not know whether that is
necessarily inseparable from an attempt
to regulate marketing and production. It
seems to me that there is a sufficient
population in the British Isles to absorb
all the pigs that can be produced within
these isles. I should regret very much
if there were any attempt made to
restrict the production of any agricultural item at all. I do not think anybody will, for a moment, desire to incur
the responsibility of moving the rejection
of the Bill, but I do think we ought to
be given a great deal more information,
and particularly on the lines I have
endeavoured to suggest.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would like to
protest, as I have often done before,
against the principle of taking the First
and Second Reading of a Bill at one
sitting of this House. I think it is a very
bad principle, and it does not give Members sufficient time to study the Bill in
question. It is a principle the House
should not commit itself to without some
very urgent and definite reason being
given.
With the principle of this Bill I am in
entire agreement. Since I came into
public life, and especially in another
place, I have always maintained that in
dealing with agricultural questions the
Government proceeded along the wrong
lines. No matter what the Government
did to relieve the agricultural position in
other directions. I always stressed the
point that if the farmers were only
guaranteed a reasonable and economic
price for their stock and produce they
would produce the goods. That principle
I cordially support in this Bill. I think
this Bill has an additional advantage.
The farmers and their representatives
will, to a great extent, have control in
the matter of the working out of the
schemes provided for under this Bill.
To
a great extent it will be free from Government control, or, at all events, it will
be free from Government monopoly.
There will be popular control, and that
will tend to win the confidence of the
farmers.
I agree with the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) in his suggestion
that the Bill requires to be more closely
examined, and I have no doubt that at a
later stage that will be done, and we will
have an opportunity of dealing more
closely with the details of the Measure.
In conclusion, I should like to repeat that
this House should not, without grave
reason, commit itself to the principle of
taking the First and Second Reading of a
Bill at the same sitting.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am sorry
Senator McAllister is aggrieved because
the House decided to take the First and
Second Readings of this Bill at one sitting,
Dogs Protection Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
but surely he ought to have said so when.
I moved the suspension of the Standing
Order, because it is possible that he then
would have been able to carry his point.
However, I will give as long a period as I
can between the Second Reading and the
Committee Stage.
Regarding what was said by the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie),
generally speaking, Clauses 1, 2 and 3
refer to schemes which may be promoted,
and Clause 5 refers to schemes in being.
It empowers the Ministry by Order to do
certain things. As regards the provision
for assisting schemes, this can be done by
Order under Section 1, Sub-section (4) of
the principal Act, and this Order making
Amendments can then be laid before
Parliament. The fixed price cannot be
maintained absolutely irrespective of the
price of bacon. If, therefore, it is
proved that curers have experienced a
loss through paying the fixed price it is
only equitable that they should be recouped for their loss. With regard to
the remainder of the criticisms, I think
the procedure suggested by the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) would be
the most convenient, and I will give all
the information I can at a later stage of
the Bill.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House.
Dogs Protection Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Campbell.)
CLAUSE 1 (Power to disqualify
persons convicted of cruelty to dogs).
1.-(1) Any court before which a
person is convicted under the Protection of Animals Act, 1911, or any
enactment amending the same, of an
offence of cruelty to a dog may order
him to be disqualified for keeping a
dog and for holding or obtaining a dog
licence for such period as the court
thinks fit.
(2) Where a person who is disqualified by virtue of an order under
*
this section is the holder of a dog
licence, the licence shall be suspended
so long as the disqualification continues
in force and shall during the time of
suspension be of no effect.
•
(3) If any person who is disqualified
by virtue of an order under this section
keeps a dog, or applies for or obtains
a dog licence, while he is so disqualified
he shall be liable to a fine not exceeding twenty-five pounds, or to imprisonment for a period not exceeding three
months, or to both such fine and
imprisonment.
(4) All offences against this Act
shall be prosecuted and all fines thereunder shall be recovered in manner provided by the Summary Jurisdiction
(Ireland) Acts, and a person convicted
under this Act may appeal in manner
provided by those Acts, and a person
disqualified by an order under this Act
for keeping a dog and for holding or
obtaining a dog licence may appeal
against the order in the same
manner as a person convicted may
appeal against his conviction, and
the court may, if it thinks fit, suspend
the operation of the order pending this
appeal.
*
*
*
*
*
*
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Page, line 6, leave out from the beginning of the line "to" in line 8, and insert
"of an offence of cruelty to a dog may,
in addition to or in lieu of any other
penalty, order such person."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not move.
Amendment not moved.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, page 1, line 8, after the word
"may" (may order him) to insert the
words, "in addition to any other
penalty." May I say, at the outset, that
this Bill has been grafted on the
Protection of Animals Act, 1911, which
is the principal Act in the code of cruelty
to animals. This Amendment seeks
simply to enable the court, in a case
where a person is convicted of cruelty
under the principal Act, to disqualify
that person for such a period, short or
long, as the court may think right, from
keeping a dog. It has not been thought
right to interfere in any way with the
20 MARCH 1934
Dogs Protection Bill-

Committee.
other hand, it may be so seriously injured
that its immediate and painless destruction would be called for. This Clause
provides for such contingencies, and I
submit it is a fair and reasonable Clause,
and I commend it to the consideration of
the Committee.
Amendment agreed to.
Act of 1911 with reference to its definition of cruelty. It has not been thought
right to separate dogs from other animals
in respect of the definition of cruelty.
What the Amendment standing in my
names does is this. It simply leaves it to
the discretion of the court to disqualify
or not to disqualify, as the court thinks
right, having regard to the degree of
cruelty inflicted on the animal whose case
is under consideration, and it leaves it
to the power of the tribunal to apply the
law as it stands under the Act of 1911.
It simply says that whatever penalty is
now properly applicable to the offence
under the 1911 Act may still be applied,
and in that respect it leaves the law as it
stands under the Act of 1911, the
principal Act, untouched and unaffected.
I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood upon
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:-
Page 1, after line 10, insert the following new sub-section :-
("(2) Where a person is disqualified
as aforesaid, the court may make such
order for the disposal or destruction of
any dog in the ownership, possession or
custody of such person as the court
thinks fit.")
Mr. LESLIE: I do not move.
Amendment not moved.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move:
Page 1, after Sub-clause (1), insert the
following now sub-section :-
:-
"(2) Notwithstanding anything in
any enactment, where a person is so
convicted and disqualified the court
may make such order as it thinks fit for
the disposal or destruction of any dog
kept by such person.'
""
It is right to give the court the power, as
set forth in this Amendment, and I would
like to express my indebtedness to my
hon. Friend, Mr. Leslie, for drawing
attention on the Second Reading to this
lacuna in the text. Circumstances may
arise in which it would be unadvisable to
allow an animal to remain in the control
of a cruel master. The dog may be so
maltreated as to justify its being handed
over to some humane society, or, on the
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Sub-clause (4), after "licence" ("a dog
licence may") to insert "or aggrieved by
an order under this Act for the disposal
of a dog." The Committee, I trust, will
recognise that when a right of appeal is
given to a defendant from disqualification a
corresponding right should also be given
from an order for the disposal of the dog.
It is only right that that should be so.
The one right of appeal which I now propose is the corollary of the other. I beg
to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in
page 2, at the end of Sub-clause (4), to
insert "Provided that no appeal shall lie
against an order under this Act for the
destruction of a dog." The reason for
the Amendment is that such an appeal
might stay the hands of the tribunal pending the result of the appeal. An order
for the destruction of the dog is only
likely in extreme cases, and to give an
appeal might mean that the agony of the
animal would be prolonged for a considerable period before the appeal would
be disposed of. I submit that in a case
of this kind, if the court thinks the destruction of the animal a matter of
necessity, there should be no appeal.
Mr. LESLIE: I would just like to say
a word in support of that Amendment.
Of course the Committee recognises that
my hon. and learned Friend and myself
have been paying some little attention,
jointly and severally, to get over one or
two snags.
On this occasion we were able
to agree from the very start, not only
as to the difficulty to be surmounted, but
also as to the form of words. I would
remind the Committee that there is a distinct precedent for a provision of this
nature both in the Protection of Animals
Act, 1911, and in the Protection of
Animals (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act
which we passed ourselves in 1926, with
regard to captive birds.
Dogs Protection Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Amendment agreed to.
Clause as amended ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Power to remove disqualification) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Mr: LESLIE: I beg to move, after
Clause 2, to insert the following New
Clause: :-
"Section eleven of the Protection of
Animals Act, 1911 (which relates to
injured animals), shall have effect as
though the provisions thereof applied
to dogs."
This relates to a matter which I mentioned
to the House on the Second Reading. It
seemed to me that if at all possible we
should take advantage of this opportunity
of bringing dogs within the scope of the
section quoted on the Paper. Section 11
of the Protection of Animals Act in brief
provides that if a police constable finds
any animal so diseased or so severely
injured or in such a physical condition
that, in his opinion, there is no possibility
of removing it without cruelty, he shall,
if the owner is absent or refuses to consent to the destruction of the animal, at
once send for a veterinary surgeon, and
if it appears that the animal is mortally
injured and that it is cruel to keep it
alive, it shall be lawful for the police
constable to slaughter it or procure it to
be slaughtered with such precautions and
in such manner as to inflict as little suffering as possible, and if the slaughter takes
place on a highway to remove the carcase.
It goes on to say that any expense
which may reasonably be incurred may be
recovered from the owner of the animal
as a civil debt. It winds up, unfortunately, with a definition of the animals to
which this section applies. The animals
are the horse, mule, ass, bull, sheep, goat,
or pig, but not dog. I am asking the
Committee to take this opportunity of
making the same provision for preserving
the dog from avoidable suffering. I beg
to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Personally I should
welcome this, especially from the hon.
and learned Senator, but I am on the
horns of a dilemma in one sense. I have
reason to fear that any such Amendment
might, in certain contingencies, involve
expense to the public funds, and it might
endanger the passage of the Bill. If
between this and the Report Stage I find
that a fear of that kind is not well
grounded, I undertake to put down an
Amendment exactly similar to the hon.
and learned Senator's on Report. At
present I am really apprehensive of doing
anything that might endanger the safety
of the Bill, and I would appeal to the
hon. Senator not to press the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I ought to have read
the whole sub-section. Any expense
which may be reasonably incurred by any
constable in carrying out the provisions of
this section, including the expenses of any
veterinary surgeon summoned by the constable, and whether the animal is
slaughtered under this section or not,
may be recovered from the owner summarily as a civil debt, and subject thereto
any such expense shall be defrayed out of
the fund from which the expenses of the
police are payable. The expense could
not possibly be large, and I should have
thought that having regard to the various
sources of the money to pay the police no
question of the kind at the back of my
hon. Friend's mind could, in practice,
arise.
He makes an appeal for the safety of
the Measure, and I recognise that it is a
Private Member's Bill and it is the hon.
and learned Senator's Bill and not my
Bill. I do not think I would get anything in the way of sympathy if I pressed
the Amendment and, therefore, I ask
leave to withdraw it. I hope the hon.
and learned Member will re-insert it on
Report Stage.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: May
I point out that the animals mentioned
by the hon. and learned Senator, the
horse, the mule, and such like, may be the
property of owners very well able to pay
the expense, but there are people who
keep dogs and we know they have actually
to be assisted to pay the licence. There
must be a number of people who have not
twopence in the world and they have a
dog as a pet, and it would be a pity to
impose any hardship.
Mr. LESLIE: Is the dog to suffer?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: No.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Dogs Protection Bill-
Committee
20 MARCH 1934
CLAUSE 3 (Interpretation).
The following Amendments stood upon.
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :
Page 2, line 34, at the beginning insert
the following new sub-section :-
"(1) The Protection
of Animals
Act, 1911, and the Protection of
Animals (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1926, so far as
those Acts apply to dogs, shall have
effect subject to the provisions of this
Act."
Page 2, line 35, leave out from
"(Ireland)" to "the" in page 3, line 1,
and insert "Acts 1865 and 1919; the
expression "keeping a dog" means having
the possession or custody of a dog within
the meaning of."
Mr. LESLIE: I am not moving either
of these Amendments. The first is not
necessary and the second is dealt with
more extensively.
Amendments not moved.
CLAUSE 3 (Interpretation).
(1) In this Act the expression "dog
licence" means a licence under the
Dogs Regulation (Ireland) Act, 1865,
as amended by the Dogs Regulation
(Ireland) Act (1865) Amendment Act,
1867, and by the Dogs Regulation
(Ireland) Act, 1919, and the expression
"keep" has the same meaning as in the
Dogs Regulation (Ireland) Act, 1865,
and so much of section seven of that
Act as relates to the method of determining by whom a dog is kept shall
apply accordingly.
(2) References in this Act to enactments of the Parliament of the United
Kingdom shall be construed as references to those enactments as they apply
in Northern Ireland.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in
Sub-clause (1) to leave out from Ireland
("Under the Dogs Regulation (Ireland)
Act") to "1919" ("Act, 1919, and") and
to insert "Acts 1865 to 1919.' I ask the
Committee to accept this as a matter of
accuracy. It is purely verbal.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in
Sub-clause (1) to leave out from "1919"
to the end of the sub-clause, and insert
the following new sub-sections:-
"(2) Any person in whose custody,
charge or possession any dog is found
or seen shall be presumed to be the
person who keeps such dog unless the
contrary be proved;
(3) The occupier of any house or
premises where any dog is found or
seen shall be presumed to be the person
who keeps such dog unless he prove-
(a) that he is not the owner of such
dog or has not the custody, charge,
possession of such dog, and
(b) that such dog was permitted to
live or remain in the said house or
premises without his knowledge:
Provided always that where there are
more occupiers than one in any house
or premises let in separate apartments
or lodgings or otherwise the occupier of
that part of the said house or premises
where such dog is found or seen shall
be presumed to be the person who keeps
such dog unless he prove—
(a) that he is not the owner of such
dog or has not the custody, charge,
or possession of such dog, and
(b) that such dog was permitted to
live or remain in that part of the said
house or premises without his knowledge."
The
I should like to say a few words in
explanation of this Amendment.
word "keeping" occurs in this Bill more
than once, and in order to find the legal
meaning of "keeping" the man in the street
would have to hearken back to the Dogs
Regulation Act, 1865, which uses phraseology of a similar character. As we know
the man in the street is not very often of
the class that would take the trouble to
hearken back to an Act of 70 years ago,
it therefore becomes necessary to define
the keeper of a dog. The Act of 1865
indicates who he is. He is the person
liable to licence duty, and it also tells how
the keeper may be ascertained.
The Amendment I have submitted
adopts similar methods of identification
to those which have been in vogue for 70
They are
years.
methods based on
commonsense. The first method is that
the person in whose custody, charge, or
possession a dog is found or seen is presumed to be the person who keeps the dog,
Dogs Protection Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
unless the contrary is proved. I submit
that is a matter of plain commonsense.
Then we come to the second, dealing with
the occupier of a house. There, again,
we find help and assistance in the Act of
1865. In that Act the occupier of the
house where the dog is found or seen is
presumed, for the time being, to be the
keeper, unless he proves he is not the
owner or has not the custody, charge or
possession of the dog, and that it is there
without his knowledge. Provisions of
that kind are needed to prevent evasion.
Let me illustrate how, without this provision, evasion of the Bill would be an
easy matter.
Suppose the occupier of the house is
disqualified from keeping a dog for a
After that
period of three months.
order is made, and during the period of
disqualification, the man defies the law
by keeping a dog in his house. Suppose
he is summoned for that offence. Being
a man of ingenuity, but no scruple, he is
ready. He admits the dog was in his
house, but he says it was kept by someone
else residing in the house and not by him.
He may have transferred the charge of
the dog to someone else in the house,
and he may have caused a new licence to
be issued in the name of another person,
such as a lodger or a second cousin. He
may come to the court and say, "Undoubtedly I was disqualified. Undoubtedly
I am the occupier of the house, but I have
no responsibility because the dog is kept by
my lodger or second cousin." That of
course is a mere sham, but he escapes
and he evades the law and in that way
the Act of Parliament would become
It
purely ridiculous. It is to prevent
evasion of that type that this definition
has been made particularly clear.
certainly has not the merit of brevity,
but it has the merit of lucidity, and I
hope the Committee will accept it as a
reasonable Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Short title and construction).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave
out sub-section (2) of Clause 4. Again,
this is a matter I mentioned on the
Second Reading. It seemed to me that
it was practically impossible to construe
the Bill before us with the two Acts
mentioned in the sub-section, because if
we did we should be brought up very
sharply against those snags to which I
referred a short time ago. Accordingly,
I thought that if there was any merit in
the reference it could better be done by
means of the Amendment I had down
on page 2, line 34, the Amendment which
I did not move. However, since I put
that unmoved Amendment on the Paper
we have made further inquiry, and everybody is agreed that there is no necessity
to insert that reference there, and no
necessity to keep in the sub-section which
forms the subject of my present Amendment. We want this Act to stand on
its own feet and not to be hampered by
the construction of either of the other
two Acts. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am very happy
to agree to the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
Preamble agreed to.
TITLE:
"A Bill to protect dogs by enabling
courts to disqualify persons convicted
of cruelty to dogs from keeping dogs."
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
the Title, to leave out the words, "protect dogs by enabling," and to insert
instead, "provide for the further protection of dogs by enabling."
Amendment agreed to.
Title, as amended, agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported as
amended to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendments.
Ordered: That the Report of the said
Amendments be received on Thursday
next, and that the Bill be printed as
amended.
Death of Col. Sharman-Crawford 86
Adjournment-
20 MARCH 1934
Motion for Adjournment.
DEATH OF COLONEL RIGHT HON.
R. G. SHARMAN-CRAWFORD.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
the adjournment of this House, I should
like to say that it is with very great
regret that I have heard this afternoon
of the death of Colonel the Right Hon.
R. G. Sharman-Crawford. Colonel
Sharman-Crawford had been a Member
of this Senate since its inception, and I
am sure this House joins with me in
giving expression to that sense of loss
which his death brings.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Colonel Sharman-
Crawford was the bearer of a name long
cherished and still honoured in the
cottages of Ulster in connection with
courageous championship of the cause of
tenant right in Ulster. His words in
this House commanded attention by
reason of the name he bore, the ripeness
of his judgment, and, I would say, by
the outspoken and independent attitude
he assumed on matters which were not
essentially political. His was an outstanding personality, and I remember
being told when I first entered this House
that the only Member of the Assembly
of whom your illustrious predecessor, Mr.
Speaker, stood in some degree of awe
was Colonel Sharman-Crawford. He was
certainly a striking personage. My colleagues and myself differed from him
in many ways. Politically we were as far
apart as are the Poles, but on this occasion,
when death silences controversy and contention, we would like to associate ourselves with the remarks of the noble
Leader of the House, and extend our
sorrow and our sympathy to his bereaved
relatives.
The LORD MAYOR OF BELFAST
(Sir Crawford McCullagh):
McCullagh): Perhaps
you will allow me, on behalf of the
citizens of Belfast, to join with the noble
Leader of the House in expressing our
sympathy with the relatives of the late
Colonel Sharman-Crawford in the great
loss they have sustained by his death.
It was my privilege to know Colonel
Sharman-Crawford for many years, and
I always found him a faithful friend, a
man who had a deep sense of public duty,
and one who always did his best not only
for Ulster but for the Empire. I remember well that before he left on holiday
he said to me, "Please God, if I should
not come back I have at least the feeling
that I have done my duty." He will be
sadly missed not only by the City of
Belfast, but by the Province of Ulster
and by an even wider field. I would
like to join with the noble Leader of the
House in expressing, on behalf of the
citizens, our sincere sympathy with the
members of his family. We know that
he sustained a very great loss by the
death of his son. We know that he felt
that loss very much, and we can realise
that he carried a heavy burden for many
years. I repeat that his death is a sad
loss to this House, and a great loss to the
community.
Mr. LESLIE: We have not been told
when the House will meet again.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: On Thurs
day.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Ten minutes after Four
o'clock until Thursday
next.

Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 22nd March, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Anderson's Divorce Bill.
The Amendments made by the Joint
Committee to the said Bill were considered and agreed to.
Henry's Divorce Bill.
The Amendments made by the Joint
Committee to the said Bill were considered and agreed to.
Gilbert's Divorce Bill.
The Amendments made by the Joint
Committee to the said Bill were considered and agreed to.
Suspension of Standing Order.
Moved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking all the stages of
the Consolidated Fund Bill."-(Mr.
Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Question to Minister.
LAW OF LANDLORD AND TENANT.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Deputy Leader of the House whether the
Government have arrived at conclusions
definitely adverse to the recommendations
of the Departmental Committee in
January, 1929, for the reform of the law
of town landlord and town tenant on the
lines of the Imperial Landlord and
Tenant Act, 1927.
In putting this question I would.
remind the House that a Committee was
appointed in 1928 to inquire into the
relations between landlord and tenant in
non-agricultural holdings. That Committee devoted a considerable amount of
labour and took a good deal of time in
considering evidence and preparing a report. Two reports in fact were prepared,
one a majority report and one a minority
report prepared by my hon. Friend Mr.
McAllister and myself. We were glad
to see in the majority report a number of
recommendations which, in our judgment,
would result in an improvement of the
law. We would have been glad to have seen
those recommendations placed upon the
Statute Book. We in our minority report went farther. We suggested some
other changes which, in our judgment,
would still further improve the law in
various respects, but we did agree with
the main recommendations in the
majority report.
The combined reports, the majority and
minority reports, have been put in cold
storage by the Government and very desirable and beneficial reforms indicated
in these reports have been postponed.
Apparently the Government has neither
the will nor the courage to crystallise
the recommendations into statutory form,
having got a frozen mind on the question
of landlord and tenant in urban areas.
Promises have been made from time to
time and repeated, but they have been
made only to be broken. I do feel that
if this question had remained within the
competence of the Imperial authorities
and the Imperial Parliament it would
have been settled years ago on the lines
of the English Act of 1927. But as
matters stand now I am sorry to say,
but I do say it, that in Stormont this
question is looked upon with cold and
contemptuous indifference.
-
Mr. QUIGLEY: In connection with
the hon. and learned Senator's question,
I was particularly struck with an important speech delivered by Mr.
Campbell at a presentation to one of our
hon. Senators Mr. Gyle
recently,
which I read a few days ago in a publication known as the "Northern Light."
The hon. and learned Senator, Mr.
Campbell, stated that he had a vivid recollection of the hon. Senator Mr. Gyle
in the Senate on the first day he attended,
shaking a very long finger at Lord
Charlemont and demanding the name of
the Bogey Man in the Cabinet who was
responsible for the delay in promoting a
Landlord and Tenant Bill, and that
actually Lord Charlemont trembled before
the accusing finger.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Did I say trembled ?
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
22 MARCH 1934
Mr. QUIGLEY: Yes. I have the
paper in my pocket. And Mr. Campbell
further stated that when he became Prime
Minister one of his first selections would
be Mr. Gyle.
Mr. CAMPBELL:
This is the first
time I ever heard the suggestion that I
made such a statement as that.
I never
made that remark in my lifetime.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I have it here, and I
presume the hon. and learned Senator
will not go back on his word.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I never made the
remark.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I will read it from
the report:-
"He had a vivid recollection of Mr.
Gyle in the Senate on the first day he
attended there, shaking a very long
finger at Lord Charlemont and demanding the name of the Bogey Man in the
Cabinet who was responsible for the
delay in promoting a Landlord and
Tenant Bill. Actually Lord Charlemont trembled before the accusing
finger. When he (Mr. Campbell) became Prime Minister his first selection
for his Cabinet would be Mr. Gyle."
That is the statement and, I presume, the
hon. Senator will not go back on his
word.
Mr. CAMPBELL: That last is a flight
of imagination on someone's part-not on
yours.
Mr. SPEAKER: I would like to remind the hon. Senator that it is usual to
take an hon. Senator's word when he
says he has not made a statement, and
this is not an official report.
Mr. CAMPBELL: What is he reading
from?
Mr. QUIGLEY: I am reading from a
report in a paper called the "Northern
Light."
Mr. LONG: I am not in a position to
add anything to the statements already
made by the noble Lord, the Leader of
the House, regarding this matter.
Mr. MCALLISTER: As one who took
the initiative in bringing this proposed
legislation before the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, I must say I am
gravely disappointed at the attitude of
the Government. The Members of the
Committee referred to by Mr. Campbell
gave their time in preparing a report.
That is now considerably over five
years ago. It is a hardship that all the
witnesses who attended before that Committee and all those who took part in it
over the whole area should have their
time sacrificed by the Government in this
way.
I would like to stress this point of view
on the Government. A great number of
public bodies in Northern Ireland, the
chambers of trade and chambers of commerce sent resolutions to that Committee
urging the remedy of this problem of
expiring leases. Quite a number of the
most prominent councils in the North of
Ireland sent resolutions urging the necessity of doing something, so it is not a
question of Members on this side alone
pressing. It is the universal demand of
the leaseholders of Northern Ireland. It
simply means this and nothing more-it
is the only logical deduction-that one
landlord, who as a rule owns the entire
property in a town in Northern Ireland,
can stop all progress of the people in
the whole town of which he is landlord,
and his influence is sufficient to do away
with the universal demand of the leaseholders of Northern Ireland. That is the
position. There is no other logical deduction to be drawn from the attitude
of the Government.
It is a hardship to think that a business
man who spends thousands of pounds in
building property, a business house, or
dwelling-house, at the end of the lease
of 61 years or 90 years should have that
confiscated and the whole of his lifework
go to the landlord without his having
the opportunity of renewing his lease on
reasonable terms or getting compensation.
With regard to the personal reference
to my hon. Friend, Senator Campbell,
perhaps the House will permit me to
say that I was present at the meeting
referred to and my recollection of what
took place is in entire agreement with
what has been stated by Mr. Campbell.
I never heard the suggestion referred to
by Senator Quigley until I came into
the House to-day, and I have a most
definite recollection that Senator Campbell's statement to this House is a correct
statement of what took place at the
meeting referred to.

Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Consolidated Fund Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of this House.
Consolidated Fund Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First Time."-(Mr. Long.)
Mr. LONG: In moving the Second
Reading of this Bill it is not, I think,
necessary for me to delay the House with
any detailed explanation of its provisions. The Bill is in the usual form,
and provides the statutory authority for
the issue from the Consolidated Fund of
sums voted in Committee of Supply in
another place in respect of the Supplementary Estimates for the current year,
and the Vote on Account for the financial
year, 1934-35.
The Bill is purely a money Bill, and
it is not within the power of this House
to amend it. I beg to move.

the Third Time."-(Mr. Long.)

pass." (Mr. Long.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill
without any amendment.

Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
authorised by the Prime Minister. on

Consolidated Fund.
THE BELFAST HALF-HOLIDAY.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House if he will consider
the advisability of introducing legislation to have the half holiday in
Belfast and Northern Ireland fixed for
Saturday instead of Wednesday and
Saturday as at present, and to apply this
legislation to all shops and business
establishments.
In asking the question, I desire to say
that in my opinion the time is opportune
for such a change. The half-holiday on
Saturday would be a great privilege and
benefit to the workers. They would then
have a full day and a half day's rest at
the week-end and would thereby be
better equipped mentally and physically
for the ensuing week's work. Those
whose homes are in the country districts
would be enabled to spend the week-end
at the family homestead, while advantage
would be taken by others of going to
the seaside in the summer season. If the
Government would approve of the
Saturday half-holiday, which I hope it
will, employers would effect a great
saving in light and heat during the
winter months. I understand that a
number of shops which formerly selected
Wednesday for the half-holiday have
found it just as suitable to close on
Saturday.
In Australia and New Zealand the
half-holiday used to be on Wednesday
and Saturday, but the Government introduced legislation changing it to
Saturday, and the people are well satisfied. At first there was opposition on
Oral Answers.
Dogs Protection Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
the part of some shopkeepers, but after
a little time those who opposed Saturday
became its strongest supporters. A great
deal of purchasing is now done before
the closing hour on Friday as most of
the employers pay wages on that day
and many shops are kept open to
8-30 p.m. If the change suggested in
my question were adopted the closing
hour on Friday night might be extended
by say one hour.
It might be necessary for those employers who pay wages on Saturday to
pay on Friday, so that whatever purchasing was necessary could be done on
Friday night or before 1-30 p.m. on
Saturday, but that is a detail that could
easily be arranged. I do not see that
the half-holiday on Saturday could impose any hardship or loss on the shopkeepers, in fact it should benefit them,
while it would be a great boon to those
in their employment. Of course I
recognise that there are certain shops
and manufacturing concerns that could
not be closed.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that, or
perhaps as a supplementary question,
might I ask the Government whether,
if they are considering the point raised.
by the hon. Senator, they will also take
into consideration the very wide powers
of fixing the weekly half-holiday and
varying the date of that half-holiday
already possessed by the respective local
authorities, and whether they will consult
the local authorities before making or
suggesting any violent change. So far
as I know Tuesday and Thursday are
more favoured days in the country towns
than either Wednesday or Saturday.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Under the
existing law the administration of the
Shops Acts, including such matters as
closing hours and half-holidays, is as the
last speaker said vested in the local
authorities who are obviously the best
judges of the individual requirements of
their respective areas. Under these
circumstances the Government is not
prepared to introduce any legislation.
interfering with the discretion of the
local authorities in such matters.
SHELLS AS FERTILIZERS.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether it is a fact that during the last
century many hundreds of persons were
employed in the industry of collecting
and distributing shells as fertilizers, and
many thousands of loads were distributed
to farmers in County Derry and, if so,
whether the Government will consider the
advisability of reviving this industry.
I put this question because I realise
that the country requires lime, and 1
also know that the price of lime in the
last twenty years has gone up very much.
I came across the fact that in Lough
Foyle there were banks of shells which
formerly had been used for spreading
over the ground as a fertilizer and for
some reason I expect because lime was
at one time so cheap-it was stopped. I
also understand that before you
dredge for shells you require some licence
or some permission from the authorities,
and it is with a view of drawing attention
to what I believe is an unlimited bank of
shells in Lough Foyle that I ask this
question.
can
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I understand, as the right hon. Senator said,
that many years ago seashells were
collected and distributed as fertilizers,
their value for this purpose resting on
their lime content. At present, however,
lime for the soil is obtainable by less
laborious and more economical methods,
and I am afraid that I see no prospect
of reviving, by Government Measures, a
form of employment that was suitable to
more primitive times.
Dogs Protection Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That the Bill be now read the Third
time." I wish to tender my sincere
thanks to the House for their gracious
consideration of the Bill, and particularly
to my hon. and learned Friend, Senator
Leslie, for his most helpful criticism and
constructive Amendment of the Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: 1 just wish to say a
word or two on the Third Reading of
this Bill. In the first place, I wish to
the
congratulate
hon. and learned
Senator upon bringing forward this Bill.
It is one of three or four that he has
brought forward since he came into this
House. Those that he brought forward
in the past have proved to be very bene-
Dogs Protection Bill-
27 MARCH 1934
Third Reading
ficial, and I am sure that this Dogs Bill
will prove to be beneficial also.
as
There is one thing that I did not understand, or perhaps did not catch, when the
Bill was going through, and that was
what definition was given to the word
cruelty. I think that that word covers a
great deal of ground, and there should
be some definition of it. For instance, if
a man kills a dog with a spade we would
look upon that as cruelty, or if he ties
a rope round its neck and hangs it to a
beam, and the dog lives for two or three
hours, we would look
that
upon
cruelty. Or if he puts it in a bag and
throws it into a stream or river it may
live for a considerable time floating about
in the water before it dies. We would
call that cruelty. But we see sometimes
along the road a man, sometimes a young
man and sometimes an old one, on a
bicycle, and a poor dog running after
him. Perhaps it has followed
bicycle for two or three or four miles and
the dog's tongue is hanging out of its
mouth almost the length of your hand,
and that may not be looked upon as
cruelty. I say it is just as cruel to the
dog as if the man put it to death with a
spade.
the
Then we have another case. Some folk
keep dogs and tie them up in backyards
and the poor dog is kept there for days
without water to drink. They throw it
something to eat but never think it worth
while to give it water. I call that cruelty.
Then we come to the field of sport, and
we see cruelty of a different nature there.
I have read in the Press, and I have
heard from conversations that some of
these so-called sportsmen, who have greyhounds on the racecourse with the electric
hare, insert into the body of the dog some
kind of drug. Sometimes it is turpentine. This drug makes the dog run for
all it is worth, so that it may win the
race. If it finished when the dog won
the race it would be all right, but if it
is turpentine that is used it causes the
dog pain and agony for some hours afterwards. I would like these questions
taken into consideration for the benefit of
the dog. I congratulate the hon.
Senator on bringing forward this Bill,
and I hope something will be done to
relieve the pain and agony caused to dog
life in this country. I have great
pleasure in supporting the Third Reading
of the Bill.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
want, just before the Bill is passed, to
say how pleased I am that it has got
through in an improved form, and that
the hon. and learned Senator has accepted
these Amendments which have made it,
I think, a very perfect Bill. Since this
Bill came into this House I have noticed
that a similar new Bill has been in
operation in several places in England,
and it seems a very useful Bill for the
country. We all know that people take
out a dog and they sometimes get into
trouble with it. I have two setters and
they wandered away last night. About
9 o'clock they were brought home, and
my son rang me up to tell me to give
them a good beating to teach them not
to go away again. I did not do it, but
I can understand there is a good deal of
punishment inflicted unnecessarily. I am
pleased the Bill has got so far. I have
met several people throughout the
country, friends of my own, and they
have been very anxious to know that this
Bill has got through. They seemed to
think more of it than of the Birds Bill.
That Bill was of great benefit to people
except those, like myself, who are tormented
with too many blackbirds. I think this
is a good Bill and I hope it will get
through another place without
necessary delay.
un-
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Speaking,
not in my capacity as Leader of the
House, but as President of the Ulster
Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to
Animals, I would like to thank this House
for passing this Bill, as I hope they will
unanimously, and also I hope for its success in another place. There was only
one occasion in history that I know of
where what we now call a dumb animal
became vocal, and that was because it saw
an angel in its path. I can only say that
if the dumb animals in Northern Ireland
suddenly become vocal it will be for the
reason that they see Mr. Campbell.
Mr. LESLIE: In rising to support the
Third Reading of this Bill I should like,
first of all, to thank the hon. and learned
Senator who moved the Bill for his kind
reference to myself. It is a great
pleasure to me to render any help I can
in a matter of this kind. I want to
Dogs Protection Bill-
Third Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie}
deal rather more particularly, if I may,
with the remarks of the hon. Senator on
my left (Mr. Barclay). I want to do that
the more because I am afraid I am a
little responsible.
a
The hon. and gallant Member (Colonel
Sinclair), whom I do not see present
to-day, made an application somewhat
similar to that made to-day for
definition of cruelty. I said at the time
that the best definition I could think of,
and the one that seemed to me most in
accordance with what I remembered of
judicial pronouncements, was wantonly
causing suffering, and I promised to look
into the matter to see if I could get a
better definition. I am afraid I cannot
get a better definition. I should like to
call the attention of the hon. Senator on
my left, as well as the attention of the
House, to the fact that what we are dealing with in the Bill before us is the case
of a man who has been convicted of an
offence of cruelty under the Protection of
Animals Act, 1911. Those words are in
the Bill before us. I was not sure at one
time whether it was necessary to set them
out at length, but the opinion prevailed
that it was much better to do so, because
by so doing we attracted the full force
of the definition contained in Section 1
of the main Act of 1911.
Now what that section says, amongst
other things, is: if any person shall
cruelly beat, kick, illtreat, override, overdrive, overload, torture, infuriate, or
terrify any animal or shall cause or procure or permit any animal to be so used or
shall wantonly or unreasonably, and so on,
cause unnecessary suffering, then that
man would be guilty of an offence of
cruelty under this Act. Another subsection refers specially to the administration, without reasonable cause, of any
poisonous or injurious drug. That, I
think, will catch the case of the greyhound referred to. The hon. Senator also
spoke of undue delay in drowning a dog.
An offence of that sort has already been
dealt with in England under the Act and
the man was suitably punished. I do not
think, having regard to the very extensive
definition given in the section, there can
be any difficulty whatever on the part of
any bench of magistrates in deciding
whether it is an offence of cruelty within
the meaning of the Act.
Mr. CAMPBELL: My hon. and
learned Friend who has just sat down has
made what, I hope, will be considered by
Senator Barclay, a very clear and comprehensive answer to his remarks. I do
think, thanks to Senator Leslie, this is a
better Bill than the English Bill. It is
not a Bill dealing primarily with cruelty.
It is a Bill assuming that cruelty is
proved, and once it is proved it enables
the magistrate to deal as he thinks right
with the offender. In the Act of 1911,
which is the fundamental Act in the
Cruelty Code, there is a most exhaustive
enumeration of cruel acts, omissions, and
negligences which has been worked out
with great care and elaborate detail. At one
time we did consider it advisable to introduce all the section in the present Bill,
but on detailed consideration we thought
it was unnecessary. The Cruelty Act of
1911, incorporated in the present. Bill,
defines cruelty on a clear and satisfactory
basis.
With regard to the administration of
turpentine, I am really a child in these
matters and I know nothing about it. I
am glad to know there is at least one
Senator in this House who knows something about that particular subject
matter. I, myself, do not know what is
the object of administering turpentine. I
do not know whether it is for the benefit
of the dog or the punter, or whether it is
even done. I think that the court which
is dealing with such a matter will have no
trouble in deciding the issue. Everybody
is going to the dogs nowadays, economically and otherwise, according to popular
report.
some
There have been under the English Act
35 instances of disqualification.
Although cruelty cases are not reported
very prominently by the Northern Ireland
newspapers it is surprising to know the
number of cases of cruelty that have arisen
here during the past two and a half
years. If this Act had been in force it
would have acted as a deterrent. I do
think it will deter offenders from acts of
cruelty to dogs and it will certainly, for
a time at least, prevent them causing
unnecessary suffering.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Mr. Campbell.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.

the Chair.
