Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Suspension of Standing Order.
Moved: That Standing Order No.
XXV. be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the Second
Reading of the Railways Bill and the
Destructive Insects and Pests Bill.
(Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. LESLIE: I do not propose to
oppose this motion this afternoon, but
I cannot refrain from pointing out that
the two Bills in question afford very
excellent examples of what I have mentioned again and again in this House,
that is, some Bills, although circulated
sometime in advance-and these were
circulated a fortnight ago-are Bills
which require a certain amount of reference, entailing visits to the library
of this House. The two Bills are
singularly good examples of that, and I
think it is unfortunate that we should
be asked to take both Readings this
afternoon.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Questions to Minister.
UNREGISTERED VETERINARY
PRACTITIONERS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is a fact
that in Great Britain and the Irish Free
State it is illegal for persons not registered in the General Registers of
Veterinary Surgeons of those areas to
practise veterinary surgery and medicine;
whether in Northern Ireland uncertified
persons are free to undertake the
veterinary treatment of animals; whether
cases have occurred here in recent years
of unnecessary cruelty owing to treatment by unqualified persons; and whether
the Government will consider the amendment of the law in this respect.
I put this question in the belief that
it is desirable here in this area to enable
the farmer who requires aid of a
veterinary surgeon for the prevention or
cure of injuries or disease in his horses,
cattle, or other animals, to distinguish
between the qualified and unqualified
practitioners. I do not refer to the
ordinary minor ailments to which the
animals are subject, and in regard to
which the farmer or Owners are perfectly entitled to administer ordinary
medicines and perform operations usual
in the care of animals. With that no
one wishes to interfere. But if I am
correct in the hypothesis underlying my
question, the law here in Northern Ireland enables absolutely incompetent
tyros to practise as veterinary surgeons.
on animals quite unrestrictedly, and
very often with loss to the farmers and
very grave preventible injury to the
animals. It is not the law in the Irish
Free State. I suppose it is by some queer
oversight that the matter has not been
rectified here.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The practising of veterinary surgery in Northern
Ireland is governed by the same Acts as
Oral Answers
Railway Bill
17 APRIL 1934
in Great Britain, and under these Acts
a person is not prohibited from practising
because his name is not on the register
of the Royal College of Veterinary
Surgeons, but he is not entitled to
describe himself as a veterinary surgeon
or to sue for any fee.
It is the case that in the Free State
under an Act of 1931 a person is not
only prevented from suing for a fee but
is prohibited under a penalty from practising or holding himself out as practising
as a veterinary surgeon unless he is
registered. An exception to the Free
State prohibition against practising is,
however, made in the case of a farmer
or other person who habitually keeps
animals for profit. Such persons may
perform any operation or administer any
medicine incidental to the usual care and
management of animals, whether in the
case of their own animals or animals
belonging to others.
I have no information regarding cases
of unnecessary cruelty here owing to
treatment being given by unqualified
persons, but if any cases are brought to
my notice I shall be prepared to consider
whether an amendment of the law is
required.
RIGHT TO COMPENSATION IN
MOTOR ACCIDENTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether, when a
driver is killed in a collision on the highway, caused by his own negligence, the
rights to compensation which persons
seriously injured in the collision would
have had against him if he had not been
killed are extinguished by his death, and
his insurance company's liability is extinguished; and, if so, whether the
Government will consider amending the
law in this respect.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As the hon.
and learned Member is doubtless aware,
a Bill dealing with the further regulation
of motor vehicles and road traffic is at
present before Parliament, and I am glad
to be able to state that the Bill in question
contains a provision designed with the
object of amending the existing law so as
to meet the difficulties referred to in the
question
Mr. CAMPBELL: Thank you.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Railways.
Bill and the Destructive Insects and Pests
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
Moved:
The Railways Bill.
"That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The brief
Measure which I now submit to the House
for Second Reading is not intended to
cover any comprehensive scheme for the
rehabilitation of railway transport, though
this is a matter which is at present
receiving the earnest consideration of the
Government. The present Bill is, however, merely designed to remove some of
the difficulties with which, under the
existing law, railway companies have to
contend. The Bill contains nothing of a
far-reaching or revolutionary character.
Broadly speaking, it is designed to afford
railway companies some measure of liberty
with respect to their lands, to give some
relaxation of certain existing statutory
obligations, and to authorise the use of
electricity for signalling and the moving
of points on the railway track.
decide.
The first, and perhaps the most important, clause of the Bill will enable
railway companies to dispose of superfluous
land or to keep it as they may
Under existing legislation they
have no general power to dispose of
land forming part of their undertaking,
which, through altered circumstances or
conditions has proved to be superfluous.
It sometimes happens that a strip of railway land is required by a local authority
for the widening of a road, or the building
of a bridge, or for other purposes, and
in such cases the power of sale to be con
ferred by the Bill will be very useful.
With regard to the power to retain
land which will be conferred by the latter
part of sub-clause (1) of Clause 1, I
should perhaps explain that heretofore
railway companies have been placed
under obligation, with respect of any
land acquired for the purposes of their
undertakings, to dispose of any part of
such land which proved to be surplus to
their requirements, and to do this within
Railway Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
a limited time after the completion of
the works for which they were acquired.
This obligation was usually imposed by
the incorporation of Sections 127, 128
and 129 of the Lands Clauses Act, 1845,
in the Private Railway Act. The power
to retain land will, however, be of small
consequence, since there is little prospect
I am afraid of railway extensions under
present day conditions.
The next provision of the Bill (Clause
2) deals with level crossings. Subclause (1) will enable the gates to be
closed across the railway instead of across
the road in approved cases, and subclause (3) will remove the existing restriction of the speed of trains to four miles
an hour over level crossings adjoining
railway stations. In the light of present
day conditions and bearing in mind the
great growth and the speed of road
traffic, hon. Members will appreciate the
merits of the proposals embodied in this
clause. Another provision of the Bill
(Clause 3) will relax the obligation upon
railway companies to report accidents of
a minor character.
By virtue of another clause in the Bill
(Clause 4) railway companies will be
authorised to use electrical energy for
signalling and for moving the points.
associated with the signals, and electricity undertakers will be authorised to
supply electrical energy for these
purposes, subject to the aproval of the
Ministry of Commerce. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount
described the Bill which he is introducing
this afternoon as containing nothing of
a revolutionary character. I cannot
help thinking that if he had been speaking twenty or thirty years ago with
relation to a Bill which proposes, amongst
other things, to modify some of the provisions of the Lands Clauses Act, he would
scarcely have ventured to say the proposals were not of a revolutionary character. After all, the incorporation of
the Lands Clauses Act is generally
supposed to bring as much satisfaction to
the mind and heart of the earnest conveyancer as the word "Mesopotamia" is
supposed to bring spiritual comfort to the
afflicted. I do not suppose there is any
Member of this House, with the exception of the hon. Senators on my left, who
is prepared to enter into a disquisition
on the subject of the Lands Clauses Act.
It seems to me the only danger in the
Bill is lest we may be inadvertently doing
something which we do not intend to do,
and there are one or two little matters to
which I will direct attention at a later
stage although I do not promise to
divide the House-in order to get a little
further information. As it happens,
two of the points arise on the first clause.
This clause enables railway companies to
dispose of land. I presume we may take
it that the Government is satisfied that
the alterations introduced by this clause
will not inflict any injustice on anybody,
and that nobody will lose any right to
the acquisition or pre-emption which they
at present enjoy.
I do not myself see any reason to
suppose that such an injustice can be a
result of the Clause, but I should like
an assurance that that matter has been
considered, and that the rights of everybody are sufficiently safeguarded. This
Clause also contains power to retain,
hold, and use the land, and I must confess that I am a little afraid of that.
The land in question is land which, ex
hypothesi, is not required for the purposes of the company's undertaking.
For what purpose is it proposed they
should use the land? I think that will
require elucidation at a later stage, unless we should find that we have given
the railway company power to go beyond
its articles of association, which
usually an Act of Parliament.
are
I think, perhaps, there is some precedent for a Clause like this to be found
in some private railway Bill passed by the
Parliament of the United Kingdom. I
have searched for such a Bill, and I have
up to date been unable to find it, and it
was the desirability of searching for such
a Bill which led to the remarks I made
on the proposal to take two Readings of
the Bill together. I think there must
be some Clause in some private railway
Bill which would serve as a precedent,
and would assist us in considering this
Clause.
On the same Clause I am not satisfied
with the proposal as to the disposal of
the purchase money. The expression
"arising from sales" is perfectly simple,
but it seems to me there might be a
question as to the disposal of what
are really capital moneys arising from the
consideration of fee farm grants or even
Railway Bill-
Second Reading
17 APRIL 1934
leases. I mean by consideration any
lump sums that might be payable; not,
of course, any annual returns. To that
I will direct attention later for the sake
of getting information. Clauses 2 and
3 are borrowed from the English Railway
Act of 1933, and I do not think they will
give rise to any comment.
Clause 4 about
electrical
The new
energy does raise in my, perhaps, horribly
pernickety mind a question somewhat
similar to that I raised on Clause 1.
Are we quite sure that we are not giving
an electricity undertaking power to go
out of its own proper area, otherwise than
by pursuing its course along the line
which it is proposed to serve.
If you
are starting your system of operating
signals and so on by electricity from Belfast, and you take your current from the
Belfast undertaking, and you continue
along the line, even to Londonderry,
and keep your current running along the
line the whole way, that would seem to be
perfectly right and not objectionable, but
if there is any possibility that some other
company might cut across other people's
land and other people's area of supply
in order to strike the railway, that would
seem to be unfair, and it is a matter
that should be looked into.
There remains one other question, and
that is whether we ought not to define
the word "railway." We have defined
"railway company," but we have not
defined" railway. We are referring
in the Bill to some seven or eight Acts
of Parliament in which the word
""
""
railway" is used. Most of them
contain a definition, but no two of them
are alike. They do not agree. The
question that does arise is whether we
intend railways, to which this Bill is to
relate, to include what are called light
railways, and what are called tramways.
The point does seem to be of importance
in Northern Ireland. We have not got
very many railways. I can think of
only half a dozen, and one of them describes itself as a railway and tramway
company. There are gates which ought,
I suppose, to be closed across the public
road, though I confess I have never seen
them so closed of late years. Passing
over the line yesterday I observed that
one of the gates was not there. Nothing
was left but the hinges, and I should
like to know if this Clause is intended
to apply to that railway.
There is also the question of light railways which raise difficulties of their own,
but I am not sure that it is of practical
importance, and I am not sure that we
should not be safer in assuming that
railways included anything that was a
railway if it was not excluded. You
cannot have one part of a railway undertaking subject to particular requirements
under the Notices of Accidents Acts and
the other part not. I shall not be
surprised to be told that in the opinion
of the Government it is safer in this
case not to define "railway" so far as to
deal with the light railway, but the
question of this railway and tramway
mixed does seem to offer a difficulty. I
will put down amendments for the Committee stage in order that we might get
a little more information on the points
I have endeavoured to put before the
House.
,
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The points
raised by the hon. and learned Senator
are mostly points of definition and elucidation, and I think they can be better
dealt with at a later stage.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Will the noble
Viscount also tell us at a later stage when,
under this Bill, a railway is not a railway.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will not
object to doing that as well.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committeed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Destructive Insects and Pests Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is probably unnecessary to emphasise the extreme
importance to Northern Ireland of its
potato industry. I may, however, point
out that the potato crop is the key to the
Northern Ireland tillage system. In an
average year the potato production of
Northern Ireland is some million tons,
worth approximately three million pounds,
and in view of these figures it is clearly
essential that every possible precaution
should be taken to safeguard the industry
from any preventible injury.
Unfortunately there is at present a very
considerable risk of the appearance in
Destructive Pests Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
this country of an insect pest which, if
uncontrolled, would cause irreparable
damage to our potato crops.
This pest
is the Colorado beetle, which in recent
years has established itself so firmly in
France that its eradication from that
country appears to be virtually impossible.
Last summer the beetle succeeded in
crossing the Channel and establishing
itself near Tilbury Docks. Owing to the
immediate and efficient action of the
authorities in Great Britain this encroachment of the insect has been controlled,
although its spread from the area originally affected has not yet been finally
checked, and this Spring it has already
made minor appearances in adjoining
localities. These outbreaks serve to show
the extreme necessity for the provision of
adequate powers to deal with the beetle
in the unfortunate event of its making its
appearance in Northern Ireland.
The infestation at Tilbury was controlled largely on account of the fact that
the authorities in Great Britain possess
more adequate powers to deal with such
an outbreak than are possessed by the
Ministry of Agriculture of Northern
Ireland. In this connection I may
mention that the authorities in the Irish
Free State also possess fuller powers than
our own Ministry of Agriculture, and the
object of the present Bill is simply to
ensure that in the possible event of the
Ministry finding itself called upon to deal
with an appearance of the pest it will find
itself armed with adequate powers.
The main provisions of the Bill are to
amplify existing powers as to the compulsory destruction of crops attacked or
threatened by the beetle and as to the
payment of compensation in respect of
crops so destroyed. Existing powers are
conferred by the Destructive Insects Act,
1877, and contemplate payment out of
local rates of compensation for crops compulsorily destroyed. It is felt that the
eradication of a pest such as the Colorado
Beetle is not a matter of merely local
importance, but is clearly one with which
the vital interests of the Province as a
whole are concerned. The Bill, therefore,
provides for the payment of the necessary
compensation from moneys voted by Parliament. This principle has been recognised
both in Great Britain and in the Irish
Free State, and has been embodied in
existing legislation there.
In regard to this question of compensation, I may point out that the Bill
provides that compensation in any one
year in excess of £250 shall not be paid
without the consent of the Ministry of
Finance, but I am sure that the Senate
will join with me in hoping that we may
be spared the necessity of dealing with
the beetle at all and of incurring any
expenditure in connection with it. I beg
to move,
That the Bill be now read a
Second time."

Mr. CAMPBELL: This Bill makes an
irresistible appeal to some hon. Members
of this House. The very title of the
Bill is fascinating-"Destructive Insects
and Pests Bill." We find that it is
confined to the Colorado Beetle, one of
the innovations that we owe to the United
States
Caliof America-films from
fornia and beetles from Colorado! A
question that suggests itself to one is,
why should this be called a Destructive
Insects and Pests Bill when there is only
one of the tribe singled out for special
mention, here in Northern Ireland? Is
there any likelihood of this beetle coming
into Northern Ireland at all? Who is
it that has discovered this danger from
the Colorado beetle? I wonder what
Department of the Government deserves
credit for the marvellous discovery of
this serious danger that threatens our
crops, particularly our potato crop, and,
I take it, other crops in our area.
thought when I looked at the title of the
Bill that we were dealing with a whole
horde of destructive insects and pests-
I mean outside the field of politics, and
I was wondering what these destructive
insects had been doing in recent times to
emerge into the Parliamentary limelight.
Has there been any danger apprehended
from this Colorado gentlemen? Has it
made any raids in Northern Ireland in
recent years? Has Northern Ireland
ever been infested with anything in the
nature of destructive insects and pests?
Has the Province ever been threatened
with anything of this nature?
I
I would be very glad to be enlightened
on this point, but if a schoolboy in one
of these palatial schools which are now
towering up in Northern Ireland under
the administration of the noble Leader
of the House, the Minister of Education,
were asked to write an essay on destructive insects and pests of Northern
Ireland, I wonder what he would say.
Destructive Pests Bill -
17 APRIL 1934
I would appeal to the noble Lord, not
as Leader of the House, but in his
capacity as Minister of Education, to say
whether if a schoolboy who knew nothing
about the subject was asked to write an
essay on the snakes of Ireland and he
simply put down, "There are no snakes
in Ireland," and made no other comment, he would not be entitled to one
hundred per cent. marks?
""
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
pretend to be an authority on any kind
of beetle and, therefore, I think that
when I am being cross-examined and
questioned by an eminent King's Counsel
it will be far better for me to stick to
absolute facts and merely reply to the
question he has asked. The hon. and
learned Member says that the title,
"Destructive Insects and Pests,' is
plural, whereas I have talked only of
the Colorado Beetle. That is a mistake
on my part. There is more than one
Colorado Beetle. And the second question was whether there was any real
danger in the matter. Well, I cannot
say definitely as to that. I can only
say that a beetle which thought nothing
of flying from Colorado to France, and
from that to Tilbury, would think very
little of the Irish Channel.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: This
Bill applies to beetles and other pests. I
would like someone to define what pests
are. In my young days one used to
describe some of the people who came
about the house as pests. Would this
apply to them? Would it apply to the
Senate or the other House, or to people
in the community? If so, it is going to
cover a very wide scope. I would like if
the noble Leader of the House would tell
us what we may consider as pests before
we vote on this.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am being
dragged into definitions, and I can only
say that an insect has always been a
creeping creature or a flying creature with
six legs. If anybody finds a person with
six legs infesting this House he can put
him down as a destructive insect or pest.
Mr. LESLIE: The only thing to add
is that there is no mention of the Colorado beetle in the Bill whatever. The
Bill is simply the English Act of 1927
adapted and brought into force in
Agricultural Marketing Bill 114
Northern Ireland, the only exceptions
are sub-sections (3) and (5) of Clause 1.
We do not find used the name of the
Colorado beetle good or bad. It may be
remembered the beetle was the Doryphora
Decemlineata. It started in Greek and
ended in Latin of the barking variety.
There is really nothing about it in the
Bill whatever.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Agricultural Marketing Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Before
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
moving "That this House do resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill" I
should like to move the Motion standing
in my name: "That it be an instruction
to the Committee that they have power
to amend the Bill so as to provide for the
payment of licence fees by dealers
registered in pursuance of regulations
under section fourteen of the Agricultural Marketing Act (Northern Ireland), 1933.
In moving this I should like to explain
to hon. Senators that the new clause
which I wish to propose in Committee to
be added to the Bill although not strictly
within the Title of the Bill as it stands
at present, nevertheless relates closely to
the purposes of the Bill,
and may
be
regarded as cognate to it, because the
new clause, in common with all the existing clauses of the Bill, constitutes an
expansion of the principal Act. Since
the Bill was drafted it has been found
necessary for the Ministry of Agriculture
to exercise control over the shipment of
live pigs from Northern Ireland to Great
Britain. This has been done under
Regulations made in pursuance of
Section 14 of the Agricultural Marketing
Act (Northern Ireland), 1933. Experience
of the working of Regulations for the
removal of an agricultural product from
Northern Ireland to Great Britain
has shown that it is administratively
desirable that the Ministry should have
power to charge a licence fee in respect
of registration under Section 14 of the
principal Act. Provision to this effect
is made in the new clause which makes
115 Agricultural Marketing Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE

[Viscount Charlemont]
the required expansion of Section 14 of
the principal Act. In view of the close
relationship between the new clause and
the general purposes of the Bill, it has
been felt desirable to deal with this
matter by means of an amendment to the
existing Bill. rather than by the introduction of a new measure. Accordingly,
I hope that hon. Senators will concur in this proposal that it be an
instruction to the Committee that they
have power to amend the Bill so as to
provide for the payment of licence fees
by dealers registered in pursuance of
regulations under Section 14 of the
Agricultural Marketing Act (Northern
Ireland), 1933.
Mr. LESLIE: This is, I venture to
think, a far more serious question than
those which have hitherto occupied our
attention this afternoon. The Motion, or
rather the subsidiary motion of the noble
Viscount, raises a very large question. If
the House accepts the Motion that will be
tantamount to an assertion that in the
opinion of this House the new Clause is
within the powers of this House, and that
will be the decision of the House itself.
That raises the question as to whether or
not at this stage we should discuss that
question or when we actually approach the
Clause.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: On the
second occasion.
Mr. LESLIE: The difficulty is perfectly
apparent. We all know the limitations
of the Government of Ireland Act. We
know the exception relating to fees for
services under a Bill and for licences. If the
House decides that they should empower
the Committee to consider this Clause
they will then have to consider whether
although they have made it lawful to
themselves to do so, it is expedient for
them to exercise the self-conferred power,
and they will have to consider whether
it is really right for the House to expose
itself to possible criticism on these lines
rather than to allow the particular matter,
if it is considered desirable to bring it in
at all, to be brought in in a separate Bill.
I have not been able to find any exact
precedent. The nearest thing on which
I can put my hand up to date is a decision
at Westminster about the
1853.
year
After there had been, I think, inquiry as
to what should be done in certain matters
it is recorded in one of the books of reference that the House-that is, of course,
the other place, not this-will not consider
their privileges infringed when the object
of the proposed penalty or forfeiture is to
secure the execution of the Act or for the
punishment or prevention of offences.
These questions do not arise. And,
further, when these fees are imposed in
respect of benefits or services rendered
under the Act, and are not made payable
to the Treasury or Exchequer or public
revenue, and do not form a ground of
public accounting from the parties receiving the same. Reference to the Amendment on the Paper shows there is a
distinct proposal that the moneys resulting from the Clause shall be paid into the
Exchequer except in so far as otherwise
appropriated. Therefore it does seem to
me a somewhat serious question has been
raised which the House ought very carefully to consider.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(Viscount BANGOR in the Chair).
CLAUSE 1. (Payment of Compensation to producers under Marketing
schemes).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: On Clause 1, I should
like a little more information than the
noble Lord has now available as to the
class of cases which are likely to be
affected. The reason I ask the question
is because since this Bill received its
Second Reading I have seen repeated reports in the newspapers of pork being sold
at such and such a price well in advance
of the price fixed by the Board. I have
also seen it stated, although I do not
know if it is accurate, that a well-known
firm of curers of Northern Ireland has
been paying 70s a hundredweight in the
Free State, bringing it into Northern
Ireland, and paying the duty, while the
fixed price in Northern Ireland is 65s a
cwt.
117 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

17 APRIL 1934
Committee
I want to know whether if this clause
becomes law a man who sold his pigs
according to law at 65s, is going to be
compensated for the loss of 10s or 15s
which he might have got if the sale was
unrestricted and he had sold at the
higher price. Also the question arises
where the compensation is to come from
-presumably from the fees which are to
be raised off the producer of the
regulated product. The question will
be of general application, and it is rather
doubtful whether the information at the
moment is sufficient to justify our passing
so very wide a power as that contained in
the new Clause before us-a Clause which
will allow a scheme of compensation
apparently unlimited at the expense of
the producer. I should be very glad of
any further information the Government
may have on the subject.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have no
further information at the present
´ moment. Perhaps I may be allowed to
give it at a later stage.
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite content with
that.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill,
CLAUSES 2 (Power of marketing
boards to make loans and grants and to
enter into guarantees.) and 3 (Application of loans and grants made to
marketing boards) ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Abatement in respect of
price of regulated product).
Mr. LESLIE: I put the Motion on the
Paper to omit the whole Clause, that
having seemed to me the least inconvenient method of drawing attention to
the provisions of the Clause, and also
that method has the advantage of giving
more ample notice to the Department concerned than I was able to give of the
fact that it was proposed to raise the
question. Clause 4 seems to me a very
difficult not to say dangerous Clause.
The whole object of these schemes I
understand is to secure for the producer
a regular market and a fixed price for his
products. If you have a scheme which
provides favourable terms to certain
persons in return for services rendered
which is what the first part of Clause 4
comes to-if you allow that, and if a
discount or abatement is to be paid,
somebody has to put up the money to
meet the discount or abatement, and that
can apparently only be the registered
producer. Therefore, the registered producer of the product with a fixed price is
going to be faced some months afterwards
with a demand or levy of so much in
order to meet the costs imposed by this
Clause.
It seems to me extremely doubtful
whether we have got our marketing
schemes at present into such a state of
perfection as to justify us in allowing
such a state of things as I have outlined
to arise. But again on the other hand
we cannot but recognise that these
schemes are more or less embryo and I do
not think anyone desires to impose any
obstacle to the working of them. But I
still think we ought to given a great deal
more information on such a proposal as
that contained in the Clause, the omission
of which, in order to start discussion, I
have proposed.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The object
of this Clause is very simple, and it is
really designed to enable a Marketing
Board to make certain concessions in
respect of the standard price which has
been fixed by them in regard to the sale
of a regulated product in cases where
special circumstances have arisen or where
the market has been relieved of a surplus
of produce as a result of co-operation.
between the Board and other persons.
One may take a very simple example. As
Hon. Senators are aware, there was a considerable glut of pigs at certain markets
in Northern Ireland last Autumn, and
great difficulty
experienced in
relieving these markets.
The only way
was
in which the Board could have assisted in
these circumstances would have been to
have stepped in and bought pigs at the
standard price and subsequently re-sold
these pigs at a loss. In actual fact the
Board did arrange to take over a large
number of pigs which had to be placed
in cold storage at that period, and producers were paid the standard price for
these pigs although it involved the Board
in considerable loss.
It will be appreciated, however, that
normally a marketing board such as the
Pigs Board, which is mainly concerned
with the regulation of prices and not
with trading in pigs, has not the organisation available in order to buy up surplus
119 Agricultural Marketing Bill—

THE SENATE
Committee
[Viscount Charlemont]
pigs and actually to handle these pigs,
especially when they are dead. This
Clause would enable the Pigs Board to
come to an arrangement with a curer to
relieve a market if we were unfortunate
enough to have a recurrence of the
incidents which occurred last Autumn.
It is obviously much more desirable that
the Pigs Board should make an arrangement with a curer to relieve the markets
of pigs in his area and to allow him a
rebate from the standard price rather
than that the Pigs Board should itself
take over these dead pigs and have to
place them in cold storage and then resell
the pigs at a substantial loss.
In order to avoid any abuse of these
powers the special approval of the
Ministry of Agriculture is required. I
hope that in view of this explanation the
hon. and learned Senator will be agreeable to withdraw his Motion for the
omission of this Clause from the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: But the Noble Viscount
has not given us any information on who
is to bear the resultant cost. If the concession is given from the fixed price
someone must be at a loss, and it is on
that point I would like some information.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: If the
hon. and learned Member will withdraw
the Amendment in the meantime I will
give him that information at a later stage.
Mr. LESLIE :
Probably that would
be the most convenient course to adopt.
I do not think there is any great necessity
to move to omit Clause 5. I think we
can probably get all we want on the
other Clause.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Temporary power of the
Ministry to authorise marketing boards
to grant money or guarantee loans).
The following Amendment stood on the

"Leave out the Clause."
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Power of marketing
boards to co-operate with boards in
Great Britain for purposes of s. 6 of 24
Geo. 5, c. 1) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
on
The following Amendment stood
the Order Paper in the name of Viscount
Charlemont :.
After Clause 6 insert the following new
Clause:
7. Where the Ministry by regulations under section fourteen of the
principal Act relating to the removal
from Northern Ireland into Great
Britain of any agricultural product,
makes provision for the registration of
persons carrying on business in
Northern Ireland as dealers in the
regulated product, then, if the regulations so provide, there shall be payable
to the Ministry by each dealer a
licence fee consisting of:-
(a) such annual sum not exceeding ten pounds as the regulations
may fix; or
(b)
a sum payable at such times
and calculated at such rate as the
regulations may prescribe, but not
being in any case a rate higher than
five per cent. of the value of the
regulated product removed into
Great Britain by such dealer; or
(c) the said annual sum together
with a sum payable at such times and
calculated at such rate as aforesaid.
All sums received by the Ministry in
respect of licence fees under the said
regulations shall be paid to the
Exchequer of Northern Ireland, except
so far as they may be appropriated in
aid of the moneys provided by Parliament for defraying the expenses of
carrying the said regulations into
effect.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Under
Section 14 of the Agricultural Marketing
Act (Northern Ireland) 1933, the
Ministry of Agriculture is given power
to make regulations for regulating the
removal from Northern Ireland to Great
Britain of any agricultural product, the
marketing of which is regulated by a
scheme in force under that Act or under
the Great Britain Act of 1931. The
Ministry of Agriculture is also empowered
under that Section to register persons
carrying on business in Northern Ireland
as dealers in the regulated product. It
121 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

17 APRIL 1934
Committee.
case
has recently been necessary for the
Ministry of Agriculture to make Regulations under Section 14 in regard to the
removal of pigs from Northern Ireland to
Great Britain, and it is clear that in the
comparatively near future similar regulations may be required in the
of other agricultural products. Although
the Ministry of Agriculture at present
possesses the power to register persons
who desire to remove an agricultural
product from Northern Ireland to Great
Britain the Ministry has no express power
to impose any fee upon the registration
of such persons. It has already been
found that this presents considerable
administrative difficulty, as the payment
of even a small fee may be regarded as
an earnest of the bona fides of the
applicant. From an administrative point.
of view it is eminently desirable that the
Ministry should be in a position to charge
a fee in respect of registration.
So
It will be understood that the fees provided for in the new Clause are maximum
ones and that except in abnormal circumstances the fees upon registration
may be comparatively small. Much will,
of course, depend upon the particular
product which is the subject of regulation.
It will be apparent, however, that the
Ministry must in all cases avoid a fee
which would have the effect of reducing
competition to an undesirable extent.
far as the variable portion of the fee is
concerned it may not be necessary in all
cases to impose this. The maximum
amount of the variable fee is 5 per cent.
of the value of the product. This may
appear high, but it will again be
appreciated that in the case of agricultural produce considerable fluctuations in
prices frequently occur. It is thus
apparent that a certain margin is required
in order to avoid the danger of fluctuations in prices rendering invalid whatever
fee has been fixed.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I did not intervene
in the earlier discussion on the power of
the Committee to deal with this particular
matter, but I should like to say now that
it is an innovation and marks a very
distinct advance as regards the powers of
this House, I cannot recollect a similar
occasion since I came into this House
where a Bill has been Amended in its
financial aspect. As I have
I have always
understood the position, we have power
to reject a financial Measure or agree to
it, but I never understood that we had
the power to vary or amend a Bill as we
propose to do in this case. Under this
Bill as it has now been amended in this
House a charge is put upon certain
individuals in the community. I did not
understand that this House could assume
that power.
It is a very decided step and
a remarkable innovation, and I think
Senator Leslie has rendered a public
service in drawing attention to the matter.
I remove the rejection of the Clause.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have gone very
carefully into this matter, and I would
not have allowed the Amendment to
appear on the Order Paper unless I was
satisfied that the House was fully entitled
to deal with the matter under the
Government of Ireland Act.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I do not question
that in the slightest, but it does mark
a very distinct innovation in regard to the
powers of this House.
Mr. LESLIE: We have now to
approach this matter from a somewhat
different angle. It is accepted from what
you have just said that it is lawful for
this Committee to act on the power given
to it by the House, and to pass this Clause
if they so wish. It may be lawful, but
another question arises, and that is
whether it is expedient. I must confess
I am strongly of opinion that it
is not expedient that we should pass
this Clause which is in effect an
imposition on certain people of a wholly
indeterminate amount of expense. The
minimum that can be imposed is
apparently £10, which, if a man
happens to be a dealer in butter, may
amount to a considerable sum, plus 5
per cent. of the value of the regulated
product removed into Great Britain.
There is no power under Section 14 to
issue licences, but there is power to
regulate. But if it is necessary to issue
a licence as a convenience of registration
what is the necessity of putting a charge
on it. Why cannot registration be done
without expense? It does not seem fair
to me to put what may be a very heavy
charge on a man who deals in the
regulated product. He will apparently
have no control over the matter once the
charge is put on him. There cannot, 1
123 Agricultural Marketing Bill-
THE SENATE
Committee

[Mr. Leslie]
imagine, be any comparable position
across the water, because there would not,
the
I imagine, be any restriction on
removal of any regulated product from
one part of Great Britain to another,
arising from the necessity of limiting
supply or ensuring a price. There might
be restriction owing to disease either of
animals or vegetables, but that is not the
question.
I must say I am inclined to protest
against this charge being placed on the
dealer. I do not think it was in contemplation when marketing schemes were
first introduced that a charge which
might prove to be such a heavy charge
should be imposed. I referred to the case
Let us suppose it
of a dealer in butter.
is cattle. What is the turn-over in a
year of some of our large cattle exporters.
It runs into thousands, perhaps possibly
into hundreds of thousands, I do not
know. But say a man's turn-over is only
£10,000, he has got to pay his £10,
which will not break him, but he has also
to pay £500 more if the 5 per cent. rate
is imposed in addition to the £10. The
whole Clause is altogether too nebulous,
and, apart from other objections, I do
not think the Committee should pass it.
However one may sympathise with
marketing scheme it is impossible to
support this particular Clause, and I
second the Motion for its rejection.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
know whether the hon. and learned Member noted that the figures in each case are
maximum and not minimum.
Mr. LESLIE : I did.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to oppose
this particular Clause on a question of
principle. It is all very well for the
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Gyle, Mr. J.
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
noble Viscount to assure the Committee
that the figures mentioned are maximum
figures, but from experience in this House
and in the country generally one realises
that in all cases a Government Department will as a matter of practice charge
the maximum rate. I do not know of
any case in which discretion such as this
has been given to a Government Department where they have not exacted their
full pound of flesh. I also object to the
principle underlying this proposal. We
are told in one sub-clause that the licence
fee is to be the maximum figure of £10,
but that is wiped out by a subsequent
sub-clause which contains an alternative
proposal for a levy of 5 per cent. of the
value of the regulated product. I do
submit that there should be a fee for the
licence only, and that it is bad legislation
and bad law to pass the Clause in its
present form. Then it is further provided in the last Clause practically to
amalgamate the two together. As a Bill
I think it is a very bad thing, and from
the point of view of administration it will
be a very difficult Bill to administer, and
therefore, on these various grounds-the
ground that £10 is much too high even
for a maximum figure for a licence in
this particular case where it will probably
wipe out the small dealer altogether and
on account of the basis on which the
Clause is formed, the alternative seeming
to wipe out the original sub-clause
entirely on these grounds I think it is
impossible to justify a Clause drafted in
that form.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Might I appeal to
the noble Lord to reconsider this matter
before the Report stage.

Not content, 7.

125 Agricultural Marketing Bill

TITLE (A Bill to make further provision with respect to the financial powers
of boards administering schemes
under the Agricultural Marketing Act
(Northern Ireland), 1933, and with
respect to the contents of such schemes;
to enable such boards to co-operate with
marketing boards in Great Britain for
certain purposes; to continue in force
certain modifications of the said Act
made under section twenty thereof; and
for purposes connected with the matters
aforesaid).
I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move after "purposes" ("certain purposes; to continue") to insert "to provide
for the payment of licence fees by dealers
registered in pursuance of regulations
under Section fourteen of the said Act."
That is consequential on my Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: It appears to me that
the Amendment just moved by the noble
Viscount gives us a last opportunity of
pressing on the noble Viscount the desirability of reconsidering this particular
question before the Report stage. I was
not at all certain that he would not have
availed himself of that opportunity if
somebody had been so kind as to rise to
continue the debate on the earlier
Amendment before the Question was put.
It seems to me that was the principal
question, and if allowed to stand over to
the Report stage there would be an
opportunity afforded of reconsidering the
matter which gives rise to a very close
difference of opinion.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In answer
to the question raised by the hon. and
learned Senator we must amend the Title
according to my Motion. I will give the
promise desired, that before the next stage
I will give this matter further consideration.
Mr. LESLIE: Then of course I do not
oppose the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Title as amended agreed to.

Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I am authorised by the Prime
Minister, on his behalf, to inform the
Senate that his Grace the Governor of

Railways Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
"That this House do now resolve itself
into Committee on the said Bill," I should
like to deal briefly with one or two points
raised by Senator Leslie on the Second
Reading.
With regard to Clause 1, the hon. and
learned Member rightly assumed that
there was a precedent for this Clause in
private Railway Acts passed by the
Parliament of the United Kingdom.
There are, in fact, three precedents in
practically identical terms, viz., Section
31 of the London Midland & Scottish
Railway Act, 1933, Section 38 of the
Great Western Railway Act, 1933, and
Section 34 of the Southern Railway Act,
1933. I am advised that this Clause will
not inflict injustice on anybody. The
power to retain land will apply only to
land now vested in a company and to
land hereafter acquired. It will not
affect land which by the operation of the
Lands Clauses Consolidation Act is no
longer vested in a company.
The Clause simply gives a railway
company the right to sell superfluous land
now vested in the company, or to retain,
hold or use such land. It is not unreasonable, in the light of present-day
circumstances, that a company should be
entitled to keep land which it has bought
and paid for. The new power to hold
and retain land conferred by the Bill may
have retrospective effect in regard to the
rights of pre-emption with respect to
portions of lands acquired within the past
ten years which are superfluous to the
works for which they were acquired, if,
in fact, there are any such cases. Very
few new works have been carried out
within that period, however. In the use
of any land so retained a railway company
will be limited by its statutory powers,
present or future.
The hon. and learned Member on the
Second Reading drew attention to the
fact that no definition of the expression
"Railway" is attempted in the Bill. The
question was considered, and in all the
circumstances it was thought wiser not
to attempt any definition. It is not
anticipated that any difficulty will arise
in determining whether any particular
undertaking is or is not a railway.
The other matters raised on the Second
Reading of the Bill will be dealt with in
the discussion of the various Amendments
which appear on the Order Paper, and it
is not, therefore, necessary for me to
refer to them now. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: Following the noble
Viscount, perhaps I may be permitted to
say that since the Second Reading the
Minister of Commerce was so kind as to
send me
a copy of the Clause of the
London, Midland and Scottish Railway
Act, to which the noble Viscount referred,
Railway Bill-
Committee
24 APRIL 1934
and consequently I was then in a better
position than I was on the Second Reading to consider the various points at
issue. In view of what the noble
Viscount has said it will not be necessary
for me to move any of the three Amendments I have put down on the Paper. I
think we have had a sufficient explanation to show that the mischiefs I desired
to draw attention to cannot possibly
arise.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]

CLAUSE 4 (Power to operate signals
and points by electricity).
A railway company may, with the
approval of the Ministry, operate its
signals and the railway points associated
with such signals by means of electrical
energy, and any
authorised undertakers
within the meaning of the Electricity
(Supply) Acts (Northern Ireland),
1882 to 1933, may, by agreement with
a railway company and with the
approval of the Ministry, supply
electrical energy for that purpose for
use either within or without their area
of supply.
This section shall have effect notwithstanding the provisions of section.
five of the Electric Lighting Act, 1909,
as amended by section forty-one of the
Electricity (Supply) Act (Northern
Ireland), 1931.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :
:-
Clause 4, page 4, line 6, at end add
"Provided that nothing in this section
shall be construed as affecting the provisions relating to wayleaves contained in
sections thirty-seven and thirty-eight of
the Electricity (Supply) Act (Northern
Ireland), 1931."
Amendment not moved.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Viscount
Charlemont:
-
Clause 4, page 4, line 10, at end add,
"but so that the proviso to the said
section forty-one shall, so far as applicable, extend and apply to the supply and
use of electrical energy under this
section."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Amendment which stands in my name is put
forward at the request of the Post Office
authorities in order to safeguard their
telegraph lines. 1 beg to move
accordingly.
Amendment agreed to.

Bill.

THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: On Clause 1 I should
rather like to ask the noble Viscount if
he can give us any idea of what the
financial effect on the individual farmer
under paragraph (b) is likely to be. That
is the paragraph which gives power to
impose a fee in respect of a certificate.
I am not putting anything on the Paper
because I think the paragraph follows
the corresponding passage in the English
Act, and also, as the Committee knows,
the whole purport of the Bill is to remove from the local authorities certain
expenses for which they are at present
liable, so that there is no disposition to
complain. At the same time I would
like to be told what expenses are likely
to have to be paid by any person whose
land or crops might be inspected. He
might want a certificate for some purpose
of his own or a visit may be paid at the
instance not of the cultivator. It is not
yet a crime to be a cultivator of land or
to be the owner or occupier of land.
visited by potato blight, the Colorado
beetle, or any other such pest.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT:
The
Minister of Agriculture is in the Senate
to-day, and he will be more competent to
answer this question than I am.
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE
(Sir Basil Brooke): The object of charging a fee in this case is to ensure that a
certificate will be supplied at the time of
importation, and it will really be to cover
the cost of inspection of the crop or seeds.
which have been brought into the
country. It will not be a serious charge.

Agricultural Marketing Bill 132
Agricultural Marketing Bill.
receiving Report of
Order for
Amendments read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: On the
Committee stage of the Bill I promised
to consider to what extent it might be
possible to meet some of the objections
which had been raised by hon. Senators
with regard to Clause 7 of the Bill.
As a result of the views put forward by
hon. Senators, I am proposing to move
the Amendment which appears in my
name on the Order Paper, and which will
have the effect of reducing the maximum
amount of the variable proportion of the
fee which may be charged on registration from 5 per cent. to 3 per cent. of the
value of the product.
As I indicated on the Committee stage
of the Bill, the figures
figures which are
included in the Clause are maximum
figures, and it is, of course, improbable
that it will be necessary to charge more
than a small fee. For administrative
reasons, however, it is essential that a
certain degree of latitude should be
allowed, and I trust that hon. Senators
will accept the reduction in the maximum
permitted amount of the fee from 5 per
cent. to 3 per cent. of the value of the
product as an earnest of the desire of the
Government to operate this Clause in a
spirit of great moderation.
On the Committee stage I promised to
give a further explanation with regard
to the intentions in respect of Clause 1.
The compensation which can be paid
under this Clause must ultimately rest
upon the producer. The producer will,
however, be safeguarded in this way,
that before compensation can be paid
under Clause 1, provision for the circumstances in which that compensation will
be payable will have to be made in a
marketing scheme. As I have already
explained, however, there are obviously
circumstances
in which a marketing
scheme may operate inequitably on
certain classes of persons. It is only
right that in such cases these people
should be compensated by the producers
who have obtained the higher price for
their produce as a result of the operation
of the scheme. Before compensation can
be awarded, the circumstances in which
this compensation will be paid will have
to be provided for in the scheme, and
133 Agricultural Marketing Bill-

24 APRIL 1934
Report Stage
compensation will only be paid in so far
as it is provided for by a future scheme.
The Clause is thus purely an enabling
one, and an opportunity will be given in
the case of every scheme which avails
itself of the provisions of this Clause, to
consider the justness of the circumstances
in which it is intended that compensation
should be paid.
Hon. Members will appreciate that
compensation can only be paid at the
mstigation of the producers' board. The
amount of compensation must also be
determined by them. These Boards are
elected by the registered producers
themselves, and it will be seen, therefore,
that it is the producers who determine
whether compensation shall, or shall not,
be paid, and the amounts, if any.
So far as Clause 4 is concerned, here
again any abatement in respect of the
standard price allowed under this Clause
must be made by the marketing board
concerned and must ultimately devolve
on producers. There is, however, the
specific proviso that before an abatement
in the price can be allowed, the approval
of the authority of the Ministry of
Agriculture will be required.
I beg to move, "That this Report be
now received."
Mr. LESLIE: I am not sure whether
one should deal with what the noble Viscount has said at this moment or when
he moves the actual Amendment he has
on the Paper. It would, perhaps, be
more convenient to speak now in so far
as what one was going to say depends on
what the noble Viscount has said rather
than on the motion. He partly dealt
with Clause 7, and he was also good
enough to give us some additional information as regards remarks that had
been made at an earlier stage on the first
Clause and the fourth Clause of the Bill.
As regards the first Clause, I confess
I was not able to follow the noble Viscount when he suggested that the payment of compensation in such a class of
case as might be specified in the scheme
would only arise on the instigation of the
marketing board concerned. It seemed
to me that a claim for compensation
would seem more likely to arise at the
instigation of the person who alleged himself to be damaged, and that he would
start the ball rolling. But one sees, of
course, that unless a scheme did in effect
empower the board to meet his claim he
would not have any locus standi. We
might ask to be told something more to
clear that up.
As regards Clause 4 the position
obviously is very difficult, and I do not
think
any useful object would be served
by pursuing that matter any further. It
is, I think, obvious that the question can
only arise when some difficulty has
already occurred, and when the best steps.
that seem practicable at the moment have
been taken to escape from that difficulty.
I am inclined to think that we should pass
from that and hope nothing very serious
may occur. As regards the revision in
the rate mentioned as applicable to Clause
7, it would perhaps be better to leave
remarks on that until the Motion for the
Amendment has actually been made.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: Perhaps I can
explain the position as regards Clause 1
in this way. If a producers' board should
have a surplus of produce it might not be
possible for the board to dispose of that
surplus, for it would mean setting up
machinery for the purpose. In order to
avoid that difficulty it will be seen that a
board may hand over the surplus of their
particular produce to merchants who are
prepared to sell that produce. I use the
word "merchant" in its widest sense.
The merchants might not be in a position
to dispose of that produce at a remunerative price to themselves, because they
would have had to buy it from the board
at a fixed price. This Clause allows the
board to give a rebate in the event of any
agent of that board selling an article at
a loss.
It further covers another contingency which may possibly arise.
I refer to a scheme which is now being
discussed by the Pigs and the Bacon
Marketing Boards for a system of cooperative prices. Perhaps you will allow
me to explain that. The basic price of
pork is fixed at 65s. If I may take the
example of bacon at 85s., you have a plus
and minus position. That is to say, there
is little or no profit on either side, but if
the price of bacon goes down, then by this
Clause it will be possible for the two
boards to come to an arrangement
whereby the loss is divided between the
curers and the producers. On the other
hand, if the price of bacon rises the profit
will be divided between the producers and
the bacon curers. That is really what the
135 Agricultural Marketing Bill.
Report Stage
THE SENATE

[Sir Basil Brooke]
Clause allows. It permits the division of
profits or losses over and above or below
the basic price of 65s.
("That this
On Question, Motion
Report be now received"), agreed to.
CLAUSE 7 (Payment of fees by
dealers registered in pursuance of s. 14 of
the principal Act).
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 7, page 5, line 3, to leave
out five," and insert " three."
""
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure everybody
concerned will accept with gratitude the
reduction of 2 per cent. in the amount of
their possible liabilities. I think the only
thing that will be regretted is the
possibility that there should be any
charge at all levied in consequence of an
Amendment carried in this House. I
think, also, we still desire to know
whether the 5 per cent., or 3 per cent.
as it will be if this Amendment is carried,
is to be 3 per cent. of the value of the
products shipped at the particular time,
or whether it is to be, so to speak, saved
up and the bill sent in to the unfortunate
dealer at the end of the year or some
other period. We are not given any
indication as to whether it is to be 3 per
cent. of the value of one particular
importation or whether it is to cover a
longer period. Perhaps the Minister will.
be able to throw a little light on that
question also.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I do not think
it would vitally affect the position in any
case, except that in the one case the
unfortunate dealer would get the fee
extracted from his pocket slowly instead
of in the other case in one lump sum at
the end of the year. It was discovered
only recently, after the control of the
export of live pigs was handed over to us,
that a large number of men who had no
experience in shipping or in trading in
pigs were shipping these pigs, having no
knowledge and no system on which to
work. It is anticipated that when the
other marketing schemes like the potato
scheme, comes into force a very large
number of uncontrolled dealers will
suddenly appear. The actual quota
allowed for Northern Ireland will be
200,000 tons. It will probably
incumbent on the Ministry to control that
be
particular quota, and it is therefore necessary in my opinion to allow no persons to
engage in this particular trade unless they
are genuine traders who are prepared to
That
carry on in this particular trade.
is the reason why a fee is being charged,
and also to cover administrative expenses.
It has not been decided whether the 3 per
cent. will be charged at the end of the
year or at the end of a particular period.
Probably, for administrative purposes, it
will be at the end of the year.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: In welcoming
the Minister of Agriculture to this House
I should be glad if he would clear up a
little matter in connection with Clause 7,
to which an Amendment has been moved
reducing the charge from 5 per cent. to 3
per cent. I should like to know whether,
if a dealer takes out a licence and pays
the fee of £10, he will also be liable to
pay this charge of 3 per cent. on the value
of the produce he ships. If he pays the
licence fee of £10 should he not be able
to ship his produce free from any further
charge?
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am not quite
sure with regard to that point. £10 is
the maximum for the licence fee, but
after the licence fee there may or may
not be a tonnage charge. If he is
shipping a very large amount of goods he
will not have to pay more than £10.
That is as I understand it.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Oral Answer
Oral Answer
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 26th April, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Question to Minister.
CENSORSHIP OF FILMS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Campbell:-
To
ask the Leader of the House
whether there has been any instance of
the exercise by a local authority in this
area of its power of veto over films; and
whether the Government see the need for
effective supervision within this
some
area.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In putting the
question which stands in my name
I confess I am moved by what I
believe to be
be a growing feeling of
uneasiness and indignation at the proportion of films the by
no means
negligible proportion of films which
glorify objectionable scenes and belittle.
the most sacred relations of life. I have
heard complaints recently in the city of
Belfast with regard to one film which
shocked decent feelings by catering for
the very basest passions and yet drew
long queues past the pay box of one of
our cinema houses. Such a film is an
ugly evil, in my humble judgment, and a
social danger. It is a source of
contamination to our youth.
this
The power of veto is vested in the local
authority, but it is practically rusty from
disuse. It is hardly ever put into action.
The police seem to be powerless in this
matter. The Board of Film Censors,
judging from the passing of
particular film, have something to answer
for. It is perfectly clear that the great
majority of all sections of the public
would prefer clean films to filthy films,
and I think the same is true of the vast
majority of cinema audiences. There
may be a minority which have different
views, but I would like the Government
to realise that they have a responsibility
in this matter, because it is a matter
which affects the welfare of the public
and especially of the growing population.
I feel sure the feeling of this House will
be practically unanimous that as far as
possible this evil should be checked in
some form or other.
Mr. McMAHON: I quite agree with
the hon. and learned Senator that some
control other than that which is in
existence at the present time should be
exercised over some of the films that are
being displayed in Belfast at the present
time. While I say that, I would also
like to point out that there are other
matters which should also be controlled
by the Government or the local
authority. I refer particularly to certain
cross-channel newspapers. I am sure the
House must agree that some of the
literature produced by
by cross-channel
papers is really not fit to be read. While
I would like to see films controlled, I
would also like to see cross-channel
journals controlled, because there are
matters printed in those papers that are
really a danger to the morals of the
people of Northern Ireland. This is one
of the things dealt with in the Free State,
which has debarred certain newspapers
from entering that area. I hope the
same thing will be done in Northern
Ireland in a very short space of time.
Mr. LONG: The necessity for the
exercise of this power has seldom arisen,
it being the general practice for
exhibitors to consult the local authority
before making arrangements to show any
film of which the suitability might be
questioned. In such cases the local
authority usually see the film at a private
projection, and if they then consider it
an undesirable one the exhibitor does not
include it in his arrangements.
I think the hon. and learned Member
may be assured that local authorities
generally are alive to their duties and
responsibilities in this matter, and act
accordingly.
