Finance (Stamp Duties) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: This Bill is designed in
the first place to give statutory authority
for the payment of annual amounts by
the Ministry of Finance to the Incorporated Law Society of Northern
Ireland. This Society was incorporated
under Royal Charter, granted by His
Majesty in 1922, and is entrusted under
Statute with important duties dealing
with the solicitors' profession. These
duties include:-
:-
(1) The education of apprentices,
the admission and registration of
solicitors, and the issue annually of
certificates entitling solicitors to
practise in Northern Ireland.
(2) The discipline of solicitors through
a Statutory Committee of the council
appointed to hear applications to strike
solicitors off the Roll and applications
to require solicitors to answer allegations of misconduct.
(3) The Society through its President, who is a member of the Rules
Committee, assists in the making of
the Rules of the Supreme Court of
Judicature in Northern Ireland.
Hon. Members will, I am sure, agree
that, having regard to the important
duties which are by Statute laid upon
the Society, the proposed allocation to
the funds of the Society is not unreasonable. The annual amount of £1,200,
mentioned in Clause 1 of the Bill represents the Stamp Duties payable in
respect of apprentices to the number of
fifteen and will be reduced to the actual
amount of Stamp duties payable in
this connection should the number of
apprentices in any particular year fall
below fifteen.
The second matter with which the Bill
deals is the payment of certain stamp
duties by local authorities.
It is proposed to allow local authorities to pay
such duties to the Ministry of Finance
half-yearly in one sum instead of by
means of stamps impressed upon or
attached to the particular documents
which are liable to duty. The arrangement is permissive and is intended to
facilitate the authorities. I beg to
move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Adjournment.
Resolved, "That this House do now
adjourn until Tuesday next."-(Mr.
Long).
Adjourned accordingly at
half after Three o'clock
until Tuesday next.
Agricultural Marketing Bill- 144
Papers Presented
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 1st May, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Education:
Regulations for Teachers' Appointments in Provided and Transferred Public
Elementary Schools, 1934. (By Act.)
Ministry of Finance:
County Court Service, Tranfers and
Promotions: Regulation made by the
Governor of Northern Ireland upon the
recommendation of the Minister of Home
Affairs for Northern Ireland and the
Minister of Finance for Northern Ireland,
and with the concurrence of the Lord
Chief Justice of Northern Ireland, in
pursuance of Section 13 (1) of the County
Officers and Courts Act (Northern Ireland), 1925. (By Act.)
Laid before the House, pursuant to Act,
and ordered to lie on the Table.
Member Sworn.
The following Member took and subscribed the oath :
Robert McBride, Esquire.
Suspension of Standing Order.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That Standing Order No. XXV
be suspended for this day's sitting for the
purpose of taking the remaining stages of
the Finance (Stamp Duties) Bill." The
reason I have for asking the House to
expedite this Measure is that it comes
into operation as from April 1st. It is
only a permissive one, and at the present
moment the local authorities may be in
some doubt as to which expedient to
adopt.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Agricultural Marketing Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: "In moving that this Bill be now read the Third
time," in order to clear up any possible
misunderstanding I have been asked by
my right hon. Friend the Minister of
Agriculture to make a somewhat fuller
explanation of Clause 7 than he gave on
the report stage in answer to a question.
Clause 7 of the Bill will enable the
Ministry of Agriculture to secure the payment of fees by registered dealers as
follows:-Either (a) an annual fee, which
will not exceed £10; or (b) a fee not exceeding 3 per cent. of the value of the
regulated product removed by the registered dealer to Great Britain; or (c) both
an annual charge, not exceeding £10, and
a fee not exceeding 3 per cent. of the value
of the regulated product removed by the
registered dealer to Great Britain.
The intention is that normally there
will be an annual registration fee, the
amount of which may range, say, from £1
or less up to £10. Whether it may be
necessary to charge the additional fee not
exceeding 3 per cent. of the value of the
product exported to Great Britain, will
depend on whether such extra fee is necessary to meet the administrative cost of
giving effect to the Regulations. It might,
in some circumstances, be more convenient
to have a levy up to 3 per cent. on the
value and to dispense with the licence fee,
and that is why the Clause provides for
such an arrangement. As indicated,
however, the normal procedure would be,
first of all, a licence fee, and then a charge
up to 3 per cent. only, if found necessary.
Hon. Senators will realise that when
the Clause is in operation it will be with
the objects of simplifying, as far as our
producers are concerned, co-operation
with marketing boards in Great Britain.
It will enable us to give the necessary
measure of co-operation without imposing
on our producers the restrictive regulations and the somewhat heavy levies which
are operative on producers under the
Marketing Scheme in Great Britain.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
145 Representation of People Bill-

1 MAY 1934
Second Reading
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill, with
some Amendments, to which they desire
their concurrence.
Representation of the People Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As hon.
Members are aware, the entire franchise
system in the United Kingdom was remodelled and extended by the Representation of the People Act of 1918.
Since then a number of amending Acts.
have been passed both by the Imperial
Parliament and by the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, the most important of
these being the measures passed by both
Parliaments in 1928 by virtue of which
the suffrage was extended to women on
exactly the same terms as to men. Hon.
Members will recollect, however, that the
Northern Ireland Measure went further
than the Imperial Measure in that it, for
the first time, extended the local government franchise to commercial companies
and also applied certain additional conditions to the qualifications required of
electors before registration.
Local
The Bill to which I now ask the House
to give a Second Reading makes no
drastic changes or extension in the
franchise but it is, nevertheless, of considerable importance since it is designed
to prevent certain abuses and to improve.
and simplify procedure in connection
with the Parliamentary and
Government franchise in Northern Ireland. It is comparatively short, comprising no more than four effective
clauses. The first Clause extends a
principle approved after extensive debate
in 1928. The 1928 measure to which I
have referred provided that a period of
residence of three years in the United
Kingdom should be an additional and
over-riding qualification demanded from
any person seeking registration by virtue
of a residence qualification, in the case
of the parliamentary franchise, or the
occupation of premises other than
business premises in the case of the Local
Government franchise. This period has
proved too short to prevent the inclusion
in the register of persons entering
Northern Ireland from outside the
United Kingdom with no interestexcept in many cases
somewhat unfriendly interest in the prosperity of
the country; the present Bill, therefore,
proposes that the qualifying period
of residence in the United Kingdom
should be increased to seven years.
I can,
I have no doubt some of the Members
of the House will question the necessity
for this over-riding qualification
and will offer some opposition to the
extension of the period to seven years by
the Bill now under consideration.
however, assure the House that the
Government, with no inconsiderable
volume of data on which to base their
judgment, are not only convinced of the
soundness of the action taken in 1928,
but are completely satisfied that the
additional action now proposed is justified. I might add that the Government's
proposals have been criticised by that
section of the community who regard our
close association with Great Britain and
the Empire as Northern Ireland's greatest
safeguard on the ground that the Bill
does not go far enough.
Before leaving this clause, I wish to
make one point clear to the House. In
proposing the amendment of the franchise
laws in the manner indicated, both in
1928 and now, the Government have
acted with a very definite object in view.
Even in these days of almost universal
suffrage the basic principle still holds
good that the voter must have some form
of personal stake or interest in the welfare
of the State. That is the principle underlying the practice, practically universal
throughout the world, of denying the
franchise to aliens. Provided such an
interest or stake can be presumed the
British Constitution tolerates, one may
almost say welcomes, the expression of
widely differing opinions as to what is best
for the country.
We in Northern Ireland are, however,
faced with a special problem, in that we
are peculiarly vulnerable to an influx of
persons who are not merely antagonistic
to the Government of the day-that in
itself would be no justification for legislative action, since the existence of an
opposition is both normal and healthybut are hostile to the Constitution itself
and to our association with the Empire.
Whether this influx is as extensive as the
information at the disposal of the Government indicates, or as negligble as some
people allege, is a question of degree and
does not affect the principle that it is not
147 Representation of People Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
in the public interest to give such persons
the power to interfere with the conduct of
the government of the country, and this
is the principle by which the Government
has been actuated.
If the hon. Members will examine the
present Clause and the section of the
1928 Measure, which it amends, they will
observe that the over-riding residence
qualification applies only in the absence
of other indications of the potential
elector's stake in the country. If a man
or woman is of Northern Ireland birth,
if, that is, he or she can claim Northern
Ireland as his or her native land, the
Clause does not apply. If a man has
given evidence of his loyalty to the
Crown and constitution by joining the
fighting forces of the Empire, that is
good enough. If a man or woman comes
into Northern Ireland and takes up a
farm or opens a place of business, thereby linking up his or her prosperity with
that of the country and giving employment to Northern Ireland workers, such
man or woman can be relied upon to
exercise the franchise with a sense of
responsibility. It is true that a minimum.
annual value of £10 has been specified
for the premises, but £10 is a very low
figure.
But if a person simply comes into Northern Ireland and merely resides here,
dependent, probably, for support upon
employment, which would have been
welcomed by one of our own unemployed,
or
even upon public relief, then the
Government ask, and I think properly
ask, Parliament to require that person
to give proof of his genuine desire to
become a real citizen of Northern Ireland by serving an apprenticeship of
seven years amongst us before aspiring
to take a share in the government of the
country.
Clause 2 of the Bill is intended to discourage a serious abuse, of which the
recent Parliamentary election revealed
two instances. I refer to the nomination
of candidates who seek election to
Parliament with the declared intention
of not taking their seats if elected.
Much play has been made of the argument that any section of the
electorate is perfectly entitled
withdraw from active participation
in the government of the country
by preventing the effective filling of a
to
Parliamentary seat. Despite the specious
arguments adduced at various times and
in various places, I suggest that the
theory is untenable. The basis of
Parliamentary government is that each
block of so
many thousand electors
shall contribute towards the government
of the country through an elected repreother
sentative sitting, with
sentatives in the House of Commons.
That is a duty, not a privilege, as
study of Parliamentary history will show.
reprea
Moreover, because a certain member is
elected by the votes of a majority of the
electors in his constituency who agree
with his views or the views of the
political party to which he belongs, this
does not mean that he represents that
majority only. He is returned to Parliament as the representative of the constituency, not of part of the constituency,
and his responsibility extends not merely
to all the electors in his constituency but
also to all the inhabitants of his constituency, irrespective of their political
views or sympathies. When, therefore,
a section of the electorate in any constituency, with the collusion of their
chosen candidate, deliberately disfranchise
themselves, they at the same time filch
from the remaining inhabitants of the
area important rights and strike a blow
at the constitution, which provides for a
Parliament representative of the whole
country. That is, I submit the proper
view to take of the matter, and I suggest
to the House that no further defence is
needed for this Clause, which aims at
preventing the abuse by requiring each.
candidate for Parliamentary election to
state in writing, as a precedent to his
formal nomination, that he intends, if
elected, to carry out his duty by taking
his seat in Parliament.
I turn now to Clause III, and I feel
that I need do no more than give a very
brief explanation of its purpose. Hon.
Members will appreciate that it has always
been difficult to arrange for the exercise
of the franchise by blind voters in such a
manner that they may receive adequate
assistance without undue infringement of
the secrecy of the ballot. Hitherto, the
practice has been for the blind voter to
enlist the aid of the presiding officer, who,
in the presence of agents of the candidate,
marked the voting paper in accordance
with the blind voter's wishes. There are
obvious drawbacks to this procedure. In
149 Representation of People Bill-

1 MAY 1934
Second Reading
the first place, the voter's choice is
revealed to several persons, and, in the
second place, the blind person is obliged
to enter the polling station alone and conduct unfamiliar business with strangers.
To obviate this, the Imperial Parliament
recently made provision for an alternative
procedure, which it is now proposed to
follow in Northern Ireland.
It is proposed that in future a blind voter may
elect, instead of utilising the assistance of
the presiding officer, to take into the
polling station with him a companion and
to record his vote with the assistance of
that companion without disclosing his
choice to strangers, or to anyone connected
with the election.
The fourth Clause of the Bill relates to
a change in procedure in connection with
the company franchise in Local Government elections. Experience has shown
that unnecessary trouble and inconvenience has been caused by the requirement,
under the existing law, that
every claim
and designation form submitted by a
company must be authenticated by the
seal of the company, witnessed by several
members of the company. After careful
consideration the Government has come to
the conclusion that the responsibility of a
company in regard to this matter would
not be diffused by a modification of the
law to permit its appointing one of its
officers to submit claims and make designations on its behalf, and this Clause
amends the 1928 Measure to that effect.
The remaining Clauses of the Bill deal
merely with its application, short title,
etc., and I need not detain the House with
any explanation of them. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: This is a bad Bill
and a reactionary Bill. A residence of
three years in the United Kingdom is the
present qualifying period for the local
Parliamentary franchise. A similar residence qualification gives the Local Government vote. This Bill extends the three
years in each case to seven years.
noble Viscount says the Government in
introducing this change have a definite
object in view.
I agree that
they have a very definite
transparently
a
The
object
in view,
patent
object in view. This Bill is a continuation
of a series of Bills which can be summed
up in the phrase that they are in the
nature of billy-goat tactics against the
people of the Free State. Once more
you are butting away at these people.
The Free State comprises an area of
26,000 square miles, bordering the 5,600
square miles of these Six Counties. They
are Irish there, so they must be outlanders
here. The wall of Partition is to be raised
a foot or two higher. John,
Sandy and Taffy can become
citizens here quickly. Pat must
bide seven years-certainly not a Peri at
the gate of Paradise. The pretext for
this Bill is an alleged exodus from across
the Border hitherto. An odd farm servant
or two may stray over. The talk of immigration of any size is on a par with the
fable of those trainloads of Russian troops
rushing through Great Britain in phantom
trains in the early days of the war, or of
the Swilly bus carrying its load of passengers into Derry every week in quest of
the dole. For the sake of common-sense,
let us hear no more of peaceful penetration of this sort. Every man feels that
talk to be nonsense when he hears it, and
so does every man while he talks it.
In 1901 the Catholics in Northern
Ireland numbered 430,390, a percentage
of 34.8. In 1911 they were 430,161, or
34.4 per cent., a slight decrease. In 1926
they counted 420,428, or 33.5 per cent.,
another slight fall. This exodus from the
Free State is a fable. The genesis of
this Bill is hate of the people of one faith.
It reads like a hymn of hate in two
clauses. What attraction has Northern
Ireland for a man of that faith from the
Free State? Here we have impoverished
farmers and 63,000 despairing
workless
What
people.
welcome
awaits them here? How do their
What does
co-religionists here fare?
a vote or two matter in an area
where the Minister of Agriculture has
said lately that any man of his own creed
who gives employment to one of our faith
is a traitor? Has there been one word of
reprobation of that counsel of barbarism
from any authoritative Unionist quarter?
Will there be one such word to-day? The
Prime Minister has said this is a Protestant Parliament for a Protestant people.
The normal avenues of employment,
governmental and municipal, are closed
against the Catholic. His faith, not his
What then would draw
politics, counts.
a Catholic of the Free State here?
In County Fermanagh the Nationalists
have a majority of 7,000 on the census.
They have one member-the Unionists
have two. In Tyrone there are about
70,000 Nationalists and 63,000 Unionists.

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Campbell]
but the Unionists have so scientifically

but they have only 16 members on the
Corporation against 24 Unionists And
so right down the gamut.
By the way, I wonder how you propose
to show that a Free State claimant has
not lived seven years here? Is he to have
"Free State" tatooed all over his face
from the moment he comes
over the
Border, As to the declaration required
of a candidate of his intention to take his
seat in the House of Commons, I object
strongly to the coercion of any section of
the electors, and this is a species of
coercion. If any section think, rightly
or wrongly, wisely or unwisely, that their
candidate should not sit in Parliament,
you ought not to suppress their freedom
of political thought. You will not persuade
them by stifling their expression of
opinion. Neither the British Parliament
nor any subordinate Parliament, so far
as I know, imposes such a condition. If
they do not want to be represented here,
this clause will neither compel them nor
convince them to a change of mind.
The clause enabling blind voters to have
better voting facilities at the polling
booths disarms opposition and criticism.
Your local government company franchise
is peculiar to this area. Your party
invented it in 1928 for party ends, so it
is the antithesis of a democratic franchise.
Clause 3 oils the grating wheels of this
partisan franchise. I am glad that,
unlike you, the Free State recognises
that the people in the North-Catholic
and Protestant- are Irishmen still, by
giving the Parliamentary vote to people
from Northern Ireland after six months'
residence. The Government here want
the people to be perpetually divided on
questions of religion. Hence we have
Bills of this type to buttress a Government of ascendancy-religious ascendancy, race ascendancy, class ascendancy.
They dread the retribution the future
may have in store for them. Like the
wicked man of old, "The number of the
years of his tyranny is uncertain. The
sound of dread is always in his ears, and
when there is peace he always suspects
treason." The spirit of this Bill is an
anti-Irish spirit. That spirit will wither
as a sickly exotic on Irish soil.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I admit it is a pity
that such a Bill as this should have to
be introduced, but, when we have a
determined onslaught made upon
Parliament and Constitution, the Government would be failing in its duty if it
did not take steps to safeguard our
Northern State. The hon. and learned
Senator Campbell, who has opposed the
Bill, stated it is a bad Bill, and spoke of
He
injustice and oppression.
knows
very well there is no such thing, against
If
peaceable and law-abiding citizens.
Ulster was such a bad place as they describe. why do our opponents not go
down South to the New Jerusalem. But
in their own interest they will stay where
they are.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Because they were
born here.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Are we in Northern
Ireland to be seriously told that we are
not to protect ourselves against those
whose plain object is to wreck our Constitution? In the past they tried to
terrorise us and force us into the Free
State, but having failed in their efforts
they are now adopting other methods,
no doubt quieter, but no less dangerous.
Well, we cannot allow invasion by those
who have declared that they are out for
the destruction and extinction of Northern Ireland as a State under the
Crown-whether that invasion be by
open attack or by the subtle tactics of
peaceful penetration. We must repel
such invasion. We would be guilty of a
breach of sacred trust if we did not
do so.
Why, may I ask, are these people.
coming up here? Do they want to be
helpful to the State and to introduce
practical improvements? Certainly not.
Their one aim is to undermine our Constitution and abolish our Parliament. I
can understand opposition to a Government in order to replace it under the
same Constitution, but that is a very
different matter from trying to wipe out
the Parliament and merge all Ulster in
the Irish Free State. I am sorry to say
that some of the Free State leaders only
encourage these people in their hostile
attitude. We are told that we are
foreigners and invaders, and that the
153 Representation of People Bill-

1 MAY 1934
Second Reading
lead and gun should be used against us
if we do not submit. Is there any
country in the world that would permit
avowed disloyalists freely to enter their
territory and to work for the overthrow
of the State? No, not one. That is
treason.
"Eternal vigilance is the price of
liberty," and so our Government has
taken appropriate action. We yield to
no one in our patriotism and love of
country, but our patriotism is based on
loyalty to the King and Constitution,
and under the Northern Parliament we
shall continue to support our King and
to render the same service to Ulster and
the Empire as we have always done.
The facts show that in our relationship
with Great Britain we can best preserve
and advance the interests of the whole
country. We cannot overlook or ignore
the sinister movements now in operation.
Of course we are told by political
opponents that we are unduly alarmed
and that no Republicans
coming up North from
South. If that is so, why then
all these vehement protests against the

but to do it harm, and we must put
restraint upon
them. Like the
66 swallow voters they want to outnumber us and place us under the Parliament in Dublin. If we are to avoid that
calamity we must be on the alert.
some
are
the
Under the circumstances, seven years'
residence before receiving the vote is not
too long, and no one who desires the welfare of Northern Ireland and desires to
preserve its integrity and connection with
Great Britain will have the slightest
grievance. All this talk about tyranny
and injustice is so much rubbish. There
is more freedom and liberty in Ulster
than in any place else, and hon. Senators
know it.
With regard to the provision about
candidates, why should a person be
allowed to stand if he has no intention of
taking his seat? He has no right to disfrancise his constituents, and he should
not be allowed to insult the Ulster
Parliament. Two of the candidates returned at the last General Election have
refused to take their seats.
Mr. McMAHON: Would he be allowed
to take his seat if he came here?
Mr. QUIGLEY: I hope he is in a
position to come here. These Members
have declined to take the Oath
of Allegiance. Could hostility and
bitterness go further? They apparently
glory in their disloyalty and hatred of
the King. That kind of thing will not
be tolerated in Ulster, and people must
be taught that they must obey and
respect the Parliament and Constitution
under which they live or get out.
I noticed this morning in the "Irish
News" that the Newry Urban Council
decided by a majority not to give an
address to the Duke of Gloucester on his
visit to Belfast. That is only in accord
with every urban council in Northern
Ireland in which the Nationalists have a
majority. No address is to be presented.
The law-abiding people of Northern Ireland welcome this Bill and I have great
pleasure in supporting it.
Mr. McALLISTER: It is a significant
fact in public life that the public representative, who is most vocal, is always the
representative who has never made any
sacrifice for his own party or for his
country. The speech we have heard would
be egotistical if the hon. Senator, Mr.
Quigley, occupied the position of the
Prime Minister. He referred to the
danger of invasion of Ulster, and verbally
rolled up his sleeves, buckled his belt, and
told us the part he was going to play. He
has spoken of the danger in years gone
by and of the danger of invasion. If the
Member were willing to make sacrifices,
from his point of view, why did he remain.
in a position where he was politically
muzzled, and dare not open his mouth?
Only now in his manhood or semi-senile
decay-
Mr. QUIGLEY: Oh, no.
Mr. MCALLISTER- -he retires from
his position and comes into public life as
the greatest fighting force of the Unionist
party of Northern Ireland. Having said.
so much about this egotistical, flamboyant
utterance I wish to address myself to the
Bill. I say there is no justification for this
Bill, good, bad or indifferent. First of all,
it is a very reactionary Measure. Even
the noble Lord the Leader of the House,

manly, refined,
resolute waymoderate,
but
who always put his case in the most gentle-
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Mr.
most
MCALLISTER- -has admitted
that the present tendency of all countries
is to encourage universal suffrage. It is
even the franchise by which Members are
elected to the Imperial Parliament from
Northern Ireland. The only parties not
affected in Northern Ireland by this Bill
are those who vote for the Imperial Parliament in Fermanagh and Tyrone. The
period entitling a person to vote in an
Imperial election is one year. Here we
pass this reactionary Bill, depriving these
people for seven years before they can vote
for this Northern Parliament. I never
before in my life heard a Bill presented
by a responsible Government without some
data, some figures, or some concrete facts
upon which it was based. Yet, here we
have a Bill presented to the House, and
there is not one figure or one concrete fact
put forward in its justification. In
addition, there is no evidence produced
by the Government that there was any
demand, much less a clamant demand for
it.
Moderate opinion in Northern Ireland,
amongst Unionists, does not approve of
this irritating, pin-pricking with regard
to
a portion of the country of this our
common country, for Ireland is still a
nation, and the Free State is the greater
part of it. What is the justification for
this Bill? Take another place with 52
representatives. The Unionist party have
officially 36 representatives and three
Independent Unionists. In other words,
they have 39 representatives out of 52.
We all know the representation of this
House. It is a majority of Government
Unionists. The Unionist party have
representation out of all proportion to
what they should have.
At
We are told that the principal reason
for the introduction of this legislation is
the position of the counties of Fermanagh
and Tyrone. What are the facts?
the last meeting of the Fermanagh
Unionist Council it was stated that the
last revision showed a small increase from
the previous revision, and that the
association had a small debit balance
financially. Evidently in Fermanagh
and Tyrone they are not so anxious about
the franchise, and about the preservation
of that majority. They have no anxiety
about it, or, otherwise, they would show
the official Unionist party in these counties of Fermanagh and Tyrone that there
was not a small debit balance for carrying
on the work.
Another thing, though I do not intend
to develop it at any length here, is the
fact, proved over and over again in
another House and in public, that by the
manipulation of the franchise, the gerrymandering of seats in Northern Ireland at
the present time, two Unionist votes are
equal to three Nationalist votes. I do
not intend to follow on the ground so ably
covered by my colleague, Senator
Campbell, but I do say that the Government having made no case for this Bill,
and given no concrete facts in support of
it, should withdraw the Bill altogether.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to support the
Bill, but with a very strong mental reservation as regards Clause 1. I cannot
support Clause 1 in the form in which
it comes before us, and I should have
preferred that the Clause had not been
presented to us in the form it takes in
the Bill. But we do not sit here to give
effect to our personal preferences any more
than we sit, or should sit, to act as
soulless echoes of voices which reach
us from outside. Our duty, as I see it,
to our constituents is to examine such
Bills as they may send to us in the spirit
laid down some three centuries ago by
Bacon-we should take those Bills and
read them, neither to believe nor to contradict, but to weigh and consider. I am
not sure that I ought not to apologise
for a digression which, perhaps, can only
serve to protract the proceedings, and
I had better get at once to that part of
the Bill which deals with the first part
of the somewhat lengthy Title of the Bill,
"to extend the period of residence in
certain cases.' That in effect means
Clause 1 of the Bill as it stands now.
""
I think that to explain the question
it is necessary to recapitulate, and perhaps to explain further what it is that
the Clause really seeks to do. The main
purport of the Clause would be to make
it law that a person not born in Northern
Ireland, and not having certain special
qualifications those that are referred to
in the Section of the 1928 Act, and
especially the qualification arising from
service in the armed forces of the Crown-
157 Representation of People Bill-

1 MAY 1934
Second Reading
will not be entitled to vote for a Member
of our House of Commons, or for a member of any local government authority,
until he has resided for seven years in
the United Kingdom. May I once more
digress to say that I did not quite follow
what the noble Viscount said as respects
service in the forces of the Crown. I
rather thought he gave us the impression
that a
man who had once worn His
Majesty's uniform was exempt from the
special provisions of this Clause. I do
not think that is what he meant to say,
nor do I think it is in accordance with
the fact. It scarcely matters at the present moment, because anybody interested
can refer to the sub-sections which are
specially set out in the 1928 Act, and
which are not affected and will not be
affected by anything in this Clause.
But the Clause seeks to impose a seven
years' period of residence in the United
Kingdom on those persons not born in
Northern Ireland who have not a special
qualification either by service in the
armed forces, or by virtue of the occupation of business premises of the value
specified. Any person to whom the
Clause applies will not be entitled to vote
for a Member of our House of Commons
or for a member of any local government
body in Northern Ireland until he has
seven years' residence in the United
Kingdom, that is to say, residence in
Great Britain or Northern Ireland. That
is what is meant by the United Kingdom. Such places as the Channel Islands,
the Irish Free State, and the Isle of Man
are not included, presumably because
none of those places are directly represented in the Imperial House. But
anybody at the moment resident outside
Northern Ireland and outside Great
Britain can, of course, acquire a seven
years' qualification, if that becomes an
Act, by residing six and a half years in
Great Britain and six months in Northern
Ireland. It can be done that way, so it
might well be that people born in the
Free State, who have been gaining their
livelihood for some years past in Glasgow
or Liverpool would only have to put in six
months in Northern Ireland before
acquiring the franchise.
That leads me to point out that the
Clause is perfectly general in its application. There is no special reference to the
Free State. The Clause lacks the courage
of the hon. Senator on my left (Mr.
Quigley) in assuming that everybody who
comes into Northern Ireland comes from
the Free State, and that everybody who
comes into Northern Ireland is of necessity
disloyal. The Clause does not raise those
points at all, and it is of perfectly general
application. I need scarcely say that the
Clause does not affect elections for the
Imperial Parliament. The effect will be
that a man can get his vote for the Parliament at Westminster, which controls the
destinies of the Empire, and incidentally
the destinies of Northern Ireland, by six
months' residence, but he cannot get a
vote for his own rural district council.
But besides that, the Clause has retrospective effect, and that is the point on
which I am unable to agree with the
Clause as it stands. I will deal with that
a little later on. It will follow if this
Clause is enacted that the right to sit as
a member of an elected body may also be
suspended until the seven years' period
has elapsed. That is to say, a man who
is no longer qualified to be registered as
a local government voter will not be
qualified to sit on a County Council,
nor will a
not qualified to be registered as a Parliamentary elector in Northern Ireland be
qualified to intimate his assent to a
nomination of a candidate for this Parliament. The Clause, of course, does not
interfere in any way with elections for
this House nor for the Parliament of the
man
United Kingdom. So much by way
of preface.
I will now, if the House will bear with
me, draw attention as briefly as I can to
the various arguments urged both against
the Bill and then in favour of it, and
after that I will draw further attention
to the particular point on which I am
unable to agree with Clause 1; that is to
say, the retrospective effect the Clause
would have. The first objection I
take is the main one. Assuming for the
moment the right of Parliament to make
a provision of this nature, the first
objection is that the proposed period of
seven years is too long. It is said there
is no precedent for anything of that
nature other than the precedent which
we ourselves set in 1928 when we imposed
a three years' period. It is also pointed
out that it compares unfavourably with
the period in places elsewhere such as for
instance, the United States of America.
159 Representation of People Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
The United States of America has a
large number of minor legislative
assemblies almost one for every State.
The maximum period of residence in a
particular State there before you can get
a vote is, I understand, two years.
The periods vary in different States. The
period of residence preceding the naturalisation of an alien in Great Britain is five
years; the requisite period of residence
for membership of the House of Representatives in the Commonwealth of
Australia is three years in the case of a
natural born subject, and five years in
the case of a person who has been
naturalised, while five years' residence
in South Africa qualifies a person for
election to the Senate of South Africa.
That exhausts the list of comparative
examples I am able to furnish.
The next objection taken, and it was
taken again this afternoon, is that there
is no evidence that any further action
on the part of the Parliament of Northern Ireland is required. It was said,
I think, that neither the elections of
1929 nor 1933 afforded any indication
that there was, if one may put it that
way, any danger to the continuance of
the present state of political affairs, and
it has also been pointed out, though not
I think so far in this House, that according to the census returns of 1926, in so
far as those returns dealt with the
relative birthplaces of the inhabitants of
Northern Ireland, just over 90 per cent.
of the population were, in fact, born in
Northern Ireland, a fraction over 5 per
cent. were born in the Free State, and of
the remaining-something under 5 per
cent.-4 per cent. were born in Great
Britain and the remaining fraction
abroad.
It has also been said that it is most
unfair and altogether out of accord with
modern constitutional principles that you
should accept local rates from a householder for a period of seven years, and
yet deny him during the whole of that
period any right to have any voice in
the election of those people who are to
spend and administer that money. I
need scarcely remind the House that I
am speaking of rates, not of reserved
taxes. Those are dealt with at Westminster, and the payment and management of reserved taxes is not affected by
this Bill. Nobody affected by this Bill
will lose any right to be an elector
for the Parliament of the United Kingdom.
It is also said that the number of people
who can be affected is not very large, and
if that is so there is the less reason for
making an outcry as regards the vote.
Then and I am bound to say there is
more force in this objection than in any
other I have heard-it is said that the
Clause will be entirely unworkable. I will
refer to that again. I am afraid it must be
admitted that at the election of 1929, after
the 1928 Act had been passed, the provision relating to three years was entirely
ineffective. Meantime, I have exhausted
the list of such objections as have come to
my notice, and I pass to the other side of
the picture, the pros as opposed to the
cons.
The first item in the defence of the
Clause, if I may use that expression, is
one which I have twice before referred to,
once in 1928, and again when speaking on
the Motion for the Address-and the noble
Viscount has said very much the same
thing this afternoon. That is that Northern Ireland does not enjoy an insular
position. We have a land frontier on two
sides. I know it is our own fault, as we
might have had a land frontier on but
one side, but that is the result of action
which, rightly or wrongly, was taken
fourteen or fifteen years ago, and I am
afraid we cannot alter it. We cannot, I
am afraid, get Donegal back to where we
should like to see it. The fact remains
that we have a land frontier on two sides
of us, and people can draw the necessary
inference.
The next point one ought to make is
not only that the Act of 1920 gave us the
distinct right after three years to vary
the period of qualification for electors.
That is common ground. But it is, I
think, not always remembered that the
Act gave the same power to Southern
Ireland as well as to Northern Ireland. That being so, one thinks that
it requires but a small stretch of imagination to assume that the possibility of what
I might call Border incidents between the
Northern and Southern areas was very
much in the minds of those responsible
for that Bill.
There are two well known and accepted
maxims. One is if you enter territory
which is not your own you must accept and
conform to the conditions of Government
which prevail in that territory. The
161 Representation of People Bill-
1 MAY 1934

Second Reading
second maxim is that no man has a right
to the indefinite continuance of any existing law. Our Parliament in 1928 at a
time, as the noble Viscount has reminded
us, when we were making very sweeping
changes by the fuller admission of
women to the franchise the Parliament
of that day thought, rightly as I thought
then and as I still think, it advisable
to require some safeguard that people
coming into Northern Ireland and
intending to vote should have some stake
or interest in Northern Ireland. For
that reason the three years period was
introduced which, having regard to the
great changes we were making in the
electorate, did not seem to me unreasonable and also the arrangement as regards
business premises.
It
There remains one further matter.
has been said once or twice this afternoon
that there is no evidence that further
action to restrict the right to vote is
necessary at all events at the moment,
and it is suggested that evidence must be
in existence and if so it ought to have
been produced. That is I think a fair
statement of the case made. I have no
definite evidence myself, nor should I
know exactly where to look for it. The
only thing I can suggest is to search the
registers last issued and make a comparison of those registers with those of
the year before. I do not know what the
result of that inquiry would be. Nor do
I think, if even such a search was undertaken, it would disclose to us how many
new voters were disloyal people who ought
not to be allowed in and how many of
them were not. But that is the case
made. I can only say in answer to that
that it has never yet been accepted as a
canon of wise administration that one
Ishould wait to lock the stable door until
the horse has in fact been stolen.
Now I should like to deal with the particular objection which I myself have to
the Clause as it stands. My objection
really is two-fold. I reminded the House
that the Clause as it stands will have
retrospective action, that is to say, if it
goes through people who are now lawfully
on the register and entitled to vote
by virtue perhaps of having completed
the three years' qualification imposed in 1928 after the statement
made in another place by the responsible
Minister that we were proposing to deal
with the matter once for all-I think I
am quoting the Minister correctly-at
all events there are people on that register
who got there on the three years'
qualification and if this Clause goes
through as it stands those people will lose
their right to be registered and lose their
right to vote.
Also, this is the second part, persons
who are now on their way to the completion of the three years' qualification
will find themselves treated very much as
the traditional donkey is alleged to be
treated by the uneducated costermonger,
and the carrot in front of his nose will be
taken further and further away. I do
not know that anybody could stand over
a proposal of that sort. I do not really
think it is the intention of the Clause but
it is undoubtedly the Clause as it stands,
and I am going to read to the House for
a moment what it is the Clause really
does say, or rather the Section of the Act
it is proposed to amend, that is, the particular Sub-Section (3A). This Sub-
Section is to be added, the House will
remember, to Section 9 of the old Act of
1918, which relates to disqualification.
A person shall not be entitled to be
registered in respect of, or to vote as
a parliamentary elector or-to skip a
little-a local government elector
unless such a person has during the
whole period of three-now sevenyears ending the last qualifying day
been resident in the United Kingdom.
That leaves no doubt whatever that a
person lawfully on the register at the
moment under the three years' qualification, who has not got or who will not
have got at the next qualifying period
the seven years' qualification, will be cut
out. I should like for a moment to lay
special stress on the expressions I have
quoted from the 1928 Act-the expressions
right "to be registered" and "to vote"
because there is a popular fallacy that if
a person's name is on the register that
person is ipso facto entitled to vote.
That is a deeply rooted fallacy which I
think carried a great many people to the
polling booths in 1928 who ought to have
stayed at home, Section 7 of the Ballot
Act makes it clear that view is mistaken.
I think it is quite possible to escape from
the difficulty. It is quite possible to add
something to the Clause which will
prevent the difficulty I have indicated.
from arising. It seems to me that all we
require is a proviso to protect the rights
163 Representation of People Bill-
Second Reading

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
present or accruing of people who were in
Northern Ireland-I think we must say
Northern Ireland in this instance for
practical purposes rather than the United
Kingdom. It would be impossible to
keep track of people in the United
Kingdom-on a given date. I have
drafted this proviso and I shall submit it
in due course. The date I shall suggest
is the 16th of January in this year that
being as the House knows the first day of
the current qualifying period. It has I
think certain advantages over other possible
dates such as the 8th March when the Bill
was introduced. If we decide upon that
date I think it will help us to meet that
criticism that the new Clause will be
wholly unworkable.
or
I propose also to put on the Paper two
new Sub-Clauses which will direct attention to the manner in which the charge of
unworkability, if there is such a word,
can be met. I do not think the sub-clauses
will prove altogether acceptable, but I
think they may serve indirectly to help us
to consider the question particularly
whether it is necessary to insert anything
in the Bill or whether what is required
can be done by Order in Council
perhaps by the Minister himself in the
way of amendments to rules. The main
point I shall rely upon is the fact that we
use and propose to continue to use the
same register both for the Parliament of
the United Kingdom and of Northern
Ireland. Now I have finished at long
last with Clause 1 and that part of the
Title of the Bill and I launch myself for
a short cruise on the far less troubled
waters of the remaining Clauses of the
Bill.
First there is Clause 2 which has been
attacked outside and in the House this
afternoon. The Clause relates to the
right of a man to allow himself to be
nominated as a candidate without the
least intention of taking his seat. That
has been described as a public scandal
and I do not think I need search for any
other term. I think it is hard to find
one more appropriate. I think the
Government are to be congratulated on
the manner in which they have dealt with
the position. I should like to point out
that not only is every constituency entitled
to be represented but also the whole of
Northern Ireland is entitled to a council
in its chamber of first instance consisting
of the full 52 members. Therefore a man
elected as a Member who does not sit is
sinning so to speak not only against the
constituency but against the whole of
Northern Ireland in general. And I am
glad the Government in dealing with the
question have avoided certain pitfalls
which would have revived the parliamentary absurdities associated with the
names of Wilkes in the eighteenth and of
Bradlaugh in the nineteenth centuries. I
am glad we have got away from that.
I am also glad that nothing has been
said specifically as regards the Oath of
Allegiance. I have sometimes said that
the Oath of Allegiance is the outward
and audible sign that a man
cognises his duty. I do
sometimes too much
rethink that
is said about
it. That allegiance after all is a
duty inherent in the subject. He owes
the duty to the Crown. The fact that he
takes the Oath of Allegiance does not make
any difference to that. It is merely a public
recognition of it. Of course I mean the
bare oath not the more common form of
the Oath of Allegiance coupled with some
other, that he will well and truly serve.
I think we shall find later that we shall
have to provide in this second Clause for
the possibility for a candidate, who is
quite prepared to sign and take his seat,
being away on nomination day and provide that the nomination should be signed
on his behalf.
I
We then come to Clause 3, the Clause
which reproduces the Blind Voters Act of
1933, an Act which is due to Captain
Frazer supported in the House of Lords
by Lords Blanesburgh and Sanderson.
do not think anybody will object. I
suppose it has been considered whether
the same right ought to be extended to
other people who suffer under certain
incapacities. But the House will
remember that the provisions hitherto
applicable to the blind were equally
applicable to men physically incapable and
to persons unable to read and write and
to Jews whose conscience prevented them
from voting on Saturday. I do not know
whether it has been considered whether
these special concessions should be
extended to them. Perhaps I should say,
having said so much, there are one or two
objections to the contrary, and I am not
sure that I should be prepared to support
a proposal for the extension.
The remaining Clauses do not call for
any particular comment, with the ex-

ception of the Clause relating to the
local government qualifications.
I was
rather sorry to hear the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) say that the
company franchise was introduced for
party reasons and had served party purposes. I am afraid that in making that
suggestion the hon. and learned Senator
fell into the same pitfall perhaps, or the
same morass, as he accused the noble
Viscount of falling into he made his
statement without any evidence to support it.
So far as I know the objects of
the alterations which it is proposed to
make are simply to enable the Clause to
become effective in the future, because it
has been ineffective in the past.
that
I should like before sitting down to thank
the House for the toleration shown to me
during a speech of rather unusual length,
and to say that I cordially support the
Bill. I believe it to be a just and proper
Bill, subject of course to the one Amendment which I have indicated,
amendment of the first Clause to save
existing rights, present or accruing.
That, I believe, is an Amendment which
will commend itself to all sober-minded
persons, whether they be in Northern
Ireland, Great Britain, or in the
Dominions across the sea,
Mr. McMAHON: I do not think there
has been any case made out from any
quarter of the House for this Bill. The
speech delivered by Senator Quigley was
done with the object of making one's
flesh creep.
One would have thought
he was a Minister of Foreign Affairs
getting up in the House of Commons to
make a speech committing his country
to war. One cannot identify his speech
with a Bill of this character, a Bill
merely to change certain residence qualifications. In the course of his speech
Senator Quigley mentioned a matter we
would all like to forget, but since he has
mentioned it he is entitled to a reply.
He referred to the time when terrorism
prevailed in Northern Ireland, but he
should also recollect that it was the
party he is identified with which started
that trouble.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Not at all.
Mr. McMAHON: In 1918 the pogrom
was started, and the present Prime
Minister went to the Queen's Island and
said, "Well done, big yard," and "Well
done, little yard." That was the beginning of the trouble, and if it had not
been for that you might not have had
any trouble in 1918.
Mr.
opinion.
QUIGLEY:
That
is your
Mr. McMAHON: I am one of a party
that has always remained constitutional.
My party is the only party in Ireland
that has never taken up guns, and
therefore, my opinion counts for something. I deplore attempts to start
trouble either here or in the South of
Ireland. My party does not believe in
force. We believe in rectifying our
grievances in a constitutional manner.
It seems strange that the party responsible for the introduction of this Bill,
the object of which is to penalise the
minority, were loud in their protestations, during the Home Rule campaign, that they would not go into a
united Ireland Parliament because they
were afraid of their civil and religious
liberty. Yet one-third of the time of
this Parliament is occupied in passing
legislation aimed against the minority
in the Six Counties. We had the gerrymandering of seats. We had
abolition of Proportional Representation,
which was fair to the minority, but because of that fact it was abolished. It
was put in the 1920 Act as a safeguard
for the minority. After that we had
another Act amending the Franchise
Act, so that, on the whole, one-third
of the time of this Parliament has been
taken up by the passing of legislation to
safeguard the Government's position so
far as the elections are concerned. I
would not be surprised if before very
long the Government introduced a Bill
to compel everyone to vote for them,
because that is what it is coming to.
the
Senator Quigley referred to those
Members who had been elected but had
not taken their seats in this Parliament.
That seems to me to be a very strange
complaint in view of the fact that a
Cabinet Minister quite recently declared
that if Mr. De Valera came here he
would be arrested. You cannot have it
both
ways. You cannot expect him to
come here and take his seat, and at the
same time prevent him doing so.
Mr. QUIGLEY:
other Member?
What about the
