Mr. McMAHON: I do not know anything about him. Whether or not Mr.
De Valera would take his seat, in any
case, I do not know. Senator Quigley
has
as
the result that we are to-day discussing
something that does not really exist.
The same hon. Member also stated that
people were coming into Derry city from
the Free State to draw the dole.
much knowledge of Mr. De
Valera's intentions as I have, for I have
none. This Bill displays nothing but
bigotry. This Government always goes
out of its way never to do anything for
the minority but to express hatred of
them. Their one argument is that the
minority are a danger in their midst.
Mr.
the
I want to tell this House that the
Nationalist Party are constitutionalists.
They have never taken up arms, and on
occasions they have even had to fight
Republicans.
At the last General
Election, in the Central Division, we had
a very strenuous fight against the Republicans, and we won it in the absence
of our great departed leader.
McCarroll also had to fight
Republicans. I deplore this constant
bickering and telling the minority that
they are out to destroy this and that. If
you tell me it is a crime to attempt to
change the Constitution, and to say that
I believe in a united Ireland, I plead
guilty to it. Would it be a crime in
England for an Englishman to say he
believed in a Republic? Would it be a
crime if the English people to-morrow
said the time had arrived for setting up
a Republic? Here it is a crime for one
to say he believes in a united Ireland.
I want to tell you quite frankly that I,
and many of my colleagues also, are in
favour of a united Ireland, but that is
no reason why we should be put in
prison.
I think the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell) very effectively answered.
the question why this Bill should not
have been introduced. This is really a
bogey Bill, because there has been no
invasion from the Free State. So far as
the Catholic population is concerned
their numbers have decreased from 1901
to 1926, so that I cannot understand
the necessity for this Bill. It has been
introduced simply to fulfil a promise
made by the Member for Derry City
(Mr. Murphy) during the recent election
campaign, when he stated it was his intention to get the Cabinet to introduce
a Bill of this kind. That statement
was made in the excitement of the
election, but evidently the Derry people
insisted on him fulfilling that pledge, with
a
The
Minister of Labour contradicted that and
said it was utterly impossible. I do
not think the minority will lose
moment's sleep because of this Bill. We
believe there is no invasion from the
Free State, and therefore there is no
reason for us to worry.
Mr.
I
Í
JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to say a word or two in
support of this Bill, the introduction of
which I welcome. I have listened
attentively to the various speeches, and
particularly to that of the last speaker,
who described this as a bogey bill.
am beginning to think that there is some
truth in that description, because there
seems to be no one more frightened of it
than the Nationalist Members. I think
they should welcome it even more than
the Unionist Members, because the
candidates against whom Clause 2 is
directed, and who put the electors to the
expense and trouble of an election when
they had no intention of taking their
seats, are not only opponents of the
Unionist Party, but are the most bitter
enemies of the Nationalist
Party.
Those are the people who have
strenuously opposed Nationalists at
recent elections.
As the last speaker has just told us,
at the last election in the Central
Division of Belfast the late Mr. Devlin,
who had done so much for his party,
was not given a walk-over, as many
people thought he was entitled to, and
therefore I think the Nationalist Party
should be thankful to the Government
for having introduced this Measure,
which will probably save them a good
deal of trouble and expense at future
elections. This Bill is going to ensure
fair and democratic representation of
the people, and it is going to prevent
adventurers, whether as candidates or
electors, coming here to improve their
own position, and at the same time
injure the people of Northern Ireland.
The fact should not be lost sight of
that this Bill will apply to Unionists
and loyalists coming from Southern Ireland as well as to disloyalists. No preference is shown towards Unionists and
169 Representation of People Bill-

1 MAY 1934
Second Reading
Protestants. The Bill is not directed
against respectable and loyal Roman
Catholics, but, in my opinion, the Government has acted wisely in attempting
to put a stop to the influx from Southern
Ireland of dangerous individuals likely
to cause a great deal of trouble by committing arson, murder, and such outrages. Senator Campbell asked what
inducement there was to Southern Ireland people to come to Northern Ireland.
My answer is the peace, prosperity, and
liberty that we enjoy here.
Mr. McMAHON: With 65,000 unemployed?
to
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
number of unemployed has been reduced
to a considerable extent, and here we
have no fanatical statesmen carrying out
foolish experiments that are depriving
the poor farmer and people in other industries of their living. We have here
sane and wise statesmen who are doing
their best in the interests of all classes
of the community, Roman Catholics as
well as Protestants. This Bill is not in
any way designed to injure or interfere with decent, law-abiding Roman
Catholics. It is to put a stop to that
individual coming in here, that rebellious,
murderous individual who wants
undermine our State and prevent us
being able to make it the success which
they recognise it is. Mr. McAllister
asked what justification there was for
this Measure. I think the statement
made by Senator Quigley was a very good
explanation, if Senator McAllister would
take it as the reason for the Bill being
introduced. What I have said myself
already is, I think, sufficient justification
for the introduction of the Bill. Who
desired to isolate themselves more than
the Free State from the very first? It
has not been Northern Ireland who were
anxious to separate themselves from the
Free State. The Free State, in the
first instance, reared tariff walls to prevent intercourse-
MCMAHON : Owing to
Mr.
economic war.
the
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It
was their Sinn Fein policy. They
thought they should close themselves in
and become prosperous by fanatical legislation. They have found their mistake,
They are able to look over the wall and see
how industrious and
prosperous and
better we are than they are in Southern
Ireland. I fail to see how on earth
Nationalist Members have any reason to
complain of the legislation introduced.
There is one matter that I am
extremely sorry is not dealt with, and
that is the other means which are at
present in operation to deprive the
electorate of fair representation. I am
sure the Nationalist Members will agree
with me when I say that the Government should have taken some steps to put
an end to the personation prevalent in
Northern Ireland. The Nationalist Members are just as much affected as we are.
We have, apart from the two principal
parties, that is, the Nationalist Party
and the Unionist Party, a third party,
who are not entitled to get representation
in our Parliament in the House of
Commons. If these people searched the
whole of Northern Ireland they could
not find a ward or division where they
would be returned by a majority vote, but
by unscrupulous manipulation of the
ballot box and the present ballot system
they are able to carry on personation to
such an extent that they are able to get
representation in the House of Commons.
That is not fair so far as the electors of
Northern Ireland are concerned. They
can only secure representation if it should
happen to be a divided division.
In that case the Socialist and Communist Party take good care to take full
advantage of the opportunity, and I am
extremely sorry the Government has
taken no steps to prevent this personation. I know two very large representative bodies in Belfast have passed
resolutions calling upon the Government
to amend the legislation and to try, if
possible, to stop personation so that the
electors would have the representative of
their choice. I am sorry that has been
neglected. The Government would have
the support, not only of the Unionists,
but of all the Members of this House in
putting a stop to that system. One has
to bear in mind that if you have a party
organisation of any extent there will be
on both sides over enthusiastic people who
vote more than once. But we have now
a regularly organised system of personation. It is very unfair, and if one party
practises it the other retaliates. Therefore
171 Representation of People Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
it is growing and growing, and will continue to grow unless a stop is put to it.
I have a recollection of how it was
carried on in trade unions. The Socialist
Party practised it to such an extent that
they removed every trade unionist from
office and secured control. I do warn the
Government that, unless they take steps
and take them very quickly, things will
get so bad that it will hardly be described
as a fair election. I welcome this Bill,
and I hope it will serve not only the
Nationalists but the Unionists.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I really think
this House would be better engaged in
looking after the business concerns of the
Six Counties than in bringing in such a
foolish and childish Bill. Looking at it
from a practical point of view, I cannot
see who is going to have any interest in
it or whom it is going to affect. Now, if
we consider the position at the present
time, it must be apparent that this Bill
is a direct insult to the Free State. From
a political point of view it will be suggested that certain things have been done
which possibly angered statesmen, and all
I will say is this, as far as my politics
are concerned, I am an Irishman and
proud of it. I am anxious that my
countrymen should rule their country,
and I am anxious that we should have a
united Ireland, for which we all stand.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Never.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: If you were a
proper Irishman you would wish it. This
is a political Bill pure and simple, and
it is a Bill to make it compulsory for the
minority to come into this Parliament and
sit and acquiesce in the Bills carried
through this Parliament. It would be a
good thing for this country as a whole, if
our statesmen would consider their people
as a whole, and not be insulting to the
minority in the statements they make. It
is no credit to statesmen, either in the
North or in the South, when they come
out and say they are going to use physical
force or going to use the guns or compel
any portion of the country to come into.
their Parliament or accept their political
ideas. I deplore this thing of physical
force. I am entirely in favour of any
action we may take constitutionally and,
as far as I am concerned, I have always
been for trying to have a united Ireland
in a constitutional way and a united
government to look after the country as it
should be, and not have it governed from
England, which is entirely opposed to the
feelings of any man who loves his country.
I have heard Mr. Quigley referring to
Ulster.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Yes.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: What right has
Mr. Quigley or this Parliament to talk
of Ulster? Mr. Quigley knows quite well
there was a time when you could have had
all Ulster. Why did you not take all
Ulster ? Why? Simply because you
know perfectly well it would not have
suited your political leaders at that time.
The Orangemen of Donegal, Cavan and
Monaghan were cut out. They threw
them on one side and left them in the
Free State. As I said before, this is a
deliberate attempt to insult the people of
the Free State. May I read for the
benefit of the House a statement made by
the Bishop of Limerick, Dr. King-Irwin,
a gentleman who, I am proud to say,
belongs to my own city and my own
county. He was Archdeacon of Armagh
and went down to Limerick on promotion
to bishop. Here is one of the first
speeches he makes in the Free State. It
was made at a Y.M.C.A. function in
Limerick. He states that
As a stranger from the North he had
been received most warmly, which he
very much appreciated. It was all very
well being staunch adherents of one's
own church. He thought very little of
any man or woman that did not stand
up for their own church, whatever it
was. If their forefathers had learned
to play together as children and consort together as young men and women
he thought there would be far more
of that feeling which one was so pleased
to see amongst the different denominations to-day. If it was possible to meet
on common ground, joining in sports
and pastimes, it was one of the grandest
things that could happen to any people.
There is the experience of a gentleman
going down to the Free State. That is
the Protestant Bishop of Limerick, who
is a well-known figure here in the Six
Counties. He is no sooner there than he
recognises he is among friendly people,
who receive him warmly, and he already
appreciates the welcome and consideration
173 Representation of People Bill-

1 MAY 1934
Second Reading
extended to him. I think that that
spirit should exist here in the Six
Counties, and I think that what we
should try to do is this. We should try
to encourage people to come into the Six
Counties and to do business here and to
set up business, and give them encouragement to settle down here. I think, at the
present time, if anyone comes in here and
does not wish to carry on in a constitutional way the law is sufficient, and
that gentleman can be dealt with in a
very short time.
As far as a Member seeking election
having to declare that he is going to sit
in this House I do not see that there is
any necessity for that. We have always
prided ourselves on majority rule. If I
have been elected to this House, and in
my statement to the electors have said
that I have no intention of taking my seat
in this House, I do not see that I should
be compelled to come in here against the
policy on which I stood and was elected.
This is compulsion, pure and simple.
Speaking for the minority, I am entirely
opposed to that. I think, in conclusion,
I will say this, that really there is no
necessity for this Bill. If the Government had put forward facts to justify the
bringing in of this Bill I could have
understood it, but in listening to all that
has been said in regard to it I have heard
nothing to justify it. I am entirely
opposed to it on the grounds that I consider it a nonsensical Bill, and in the
second place, that no case has been made
for it.
Mr. McBRIDE: I have great pleasure
in supporting this Bill, because I think it
is absolutely necessary that we in
Northern Ireland should protect ourselves
in this way. If we look back on what
occurred during the latter part of 1921
and the early part of the following year,
I think we will agree that some such Bill
as this is absolutely necessary in order
to prevent a repetition of what took place
in those days. We cannot forget the
assassinations by bullet and bomb, and
the fearful destruction by fire and otherwise that was responsible for several
hundred deaths and damage to the extent
of upwards of two million pounds in the
city of Belfast alone. Surely we have
the right to protect our people against a
repetition of such outrages in the future.
I am glad to think that Members of the
Opposition in this House have rendered
good service in the business of legislation,
especially the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell). His Protection of Birds
Bill and his Dogs Bill are to his credit,
but surely it is just as necessary that the
Government should protect our people
from those avowed enemies of Ulster who
would come here for the express purpose
of destroying us constitutionally.
One argument used against the Bill is
that there has been no influx from
Southern Ireland into Northern Ireland,
and that, therefore, the Measure is unnecessary. If that is so why should there
be all this talk about the minority suffering under the Bill. I wonder what Mr.
De Valera would do if people from the
North went to the Free State and publicly
declared, as has been done here, that they
had gone there for the avowed purpose of
wrecking the Constitution of the Free
State. I am afraid it would not be very
long before they would have to leave,
or take the consequences. Senator
McMahon referred to the treatment of
people in other countries. What would
happen to persons who entered Italy,
Spain, Germany, France, or America, and
openly declared that they were out to
destroy the constitution of those countries.
It has not been denied by Members of
the Opposition that that is the object of
certain elements in Ulster, and they,
themselves, have to-day declared that
they are out for a united Ireland.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Are you not an
Irishman?
Mr. McBRIDE: I am, and I am proud
of it. These hostile people are being supported and urged on by Mr. De Valera,
but he does not say that I am an Irishman. He says I am a foreigner and an
alien. They are also supported by Mr.
O'Duffy, who advocates the use of lead,
and by Mr. O'Kelly, who advocates the
use of the gun. They are determined
that we shall one day be driven into the
Free State and, therefore, we have got
to face the issue and take whatever steps
we can to protect ourselves. I am proud
that the Government have introduced this
Bill, and I think the Nationalist Members should be in favour of it, because
when it is passed the Republicans will not
trouble to oppose their candidate in the
Central Division of Bedfast, and they
will have every opportunity to elect the
Member of their choice. Therefore, the
175 Representation of People Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. McBride]
Bill will help, in that respect, not the
Unionist Party, but the
Party, but the Nationalist
Party. I have much pleasure in supporting the Bill, and I hope it will pass.
Mr. McHUGH: I think none of us will
complain of your rulings, Mr. Speaker,
because you have allowed the debate to
travel over a very wide field. The last
speaker, for instance, has been travelling
over the Free State. I think the best
thing in life is for everyone to try to mind
his own business, and apply that to his
own country. If Senator McBride would
devote a little more time to trying to
establish peace and harmony in Northern
Ireland and allow the Free State to carry
on in their own way it would be much
better for all concerned.
Mr. McBRIDE: I am prepared to do
that at any time.
Mr. McHUGH: Senator Joseph Cunningham introduced a side issue regarding
personation, and appealed to the Government to put a stop to it. Some two years
ago a Personation Bill was introduced
into Parliament, and when it was before
this House an Amendment was moved
empowering the police in a polling booth
to apprehend persons who were personating. No Member more bitterly opposed
that Amendment than Senator Cunningham, and not alone did he oppose it, but
he voted against it. I made a strong
appeal in support of the Amendment, but
it was rejected. I do not think the
Government gave this Bill its proper
title. If I had been responsible for it
I would have taken my courage in both
hands and called it an "Anti-Papist"
Bill, and I would have told the people we
were bent on one thing, and one thing
only, and that was to make the minority
in the Six Counties common slaves.
very important question that has not
been discussed at all is, when is an Irishman not an Irishman?
One
Mr. BARCLAY: When he is a Scotsman.
Mr. McHUGH: Notwithstanding what
is said to the contrary, every individual
in the Free State forms part of the United
Kingdom. The people of the Free State
are still in the British Commonwealth of
Nations. They have not yet renounced
that and, therefore, this Bill does not
affect them in the slightest degree. As
Senator Leslie has already pointed out,
the Government has not specifically
mentioned the Free State in this Bill.
Why? Because they are not aliens in
any sense until they have finally declared
themselves separated from the United
Kingdom. Another very important
matter is this. Although the supporters of the Government form 70 per
cent. of the people of this country, they
apparently fear the 30 per cent. The
only justification that has been put forward in favour of this Bill is something
terrible that took place in 1920 and 1921.
That is a nightmare that is aways on
their minds, and in that matter they pretend they were innocent children and that
it was those terrible Catholics who caused
all that destruction in Northern Ireland.
I cannot quite understand these people.
One would think you would be courteous
and considerate towards the minority so
long as they are observing the laws of the
country. Such people are sitting in this
House at the moment, and I would like
to ask Senator Quigley what offence they
had committed except this; that one and
all are Irishmen who love Ireland as a
nation; that they will never give up that
sentiment they hold in their bosom; and
that they will never be happy until they
have a united Ireland. This is too small
a country to be separated in the way it is
at the present time, but those who enjoy
the sweets of office will always bitterly
oppose the unity of the country, lest they
should lose the great authority they
possess in the Six Counties.
Senator Quigley referred to two classes
of Opposition, one loyal and the other disloyal. He did not tell us which was
which. The man who speaks about the
unity of Ireland is looked upon as disloyal, but the man who complains about
the state of agriculture and urges the
Government to take steps to improve it
is termed a member of a loyal Opposition.
Any man who wishes his country well is,
in Senator Quigley's opinion, a member
of a disloyal Opposition and should be
crushed. I wish to point out that this
Bill has been brought forward for the sole
purpose of crushing the minority in the
Six Counties, and that minority is known
and recognised as the Roman Catholic
minority. It is bigotry, pure and simple,
and that alone is the reason why the Bill
has been introduced. The Government
177 Representation of People Bill-
1 MAY 1934

Second Reading
say that all people who come into
Northern Ireland are disloyal. The
people who have gone to other countries
and helped to build up those countries are
surely not disloyal. Were all those
people who emigrated to the United
States of America disloyal? Yet a great
many of them were Irishmen, and they
were not denounced by the leaders of
America as disloyalists, although, politically and religiously, they might not agree
with the leaders of different sections in
America. They were never singled out
for special treatment on account of their
religion.
Under the provisions of this Bill a man
who comes into this country and offers his
services to the country must remain a
slave for seven years. Therefore, in the
opinion of the Government, that man's
services are valueless to the country, particularly if he is a Roman Catholic.
If
he is a Protestant he is all right, but if
he happens to be a Papist he is all wrong.
You place no value whatever on his
services. If he possesses a few pounds
and is able to buy a few good animals and
put them on a piece of land he will have
a voice in the government of the country,
but not otherwise. The hands of the
clock are going back instead of forward.
It is not the people who are of any value
to the country, it is the animal browsing
in the field that makes all the difference
so far as Northern Ireland is concerned.
I look upon this Bill as a purely religious
Measure, introduced for the sole purpose
of further crushing the Roman Catholics
of Northern Ireland. The 70 per cent.
of the people must indeed be terrified of
the 30 per cent., but they would never be
able to command a majority in Northern
Ireland for the next hundred years.
they would teem into the country for the
next hundred years they would never be
able to capture Northern Ireland by their
votes. As far as we are personally concerned, we do say we always believed in
constitutional methods, but at the same
time we shall never give up the hope of
seeing a united Ireland and, therefore, I
certainly do oppose the Bill introduced for
the sole purpose of crushing the Roman
Catholics of Northern Ireland.
If
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Bill. I cannot
see anything in it that is detrimental to
the interests of the Opposition. It is a
Bill, to my mind, that is going to give
protection to all loyal law-abiding
industrious citizens of Northern Ireland.
The speeches that have been delivered
here this afternoon would give the
impression that the Bill was against the
Roman Catholics. It is a protection to
the minority and to the Roman Catholic
just as much as to the Protestant. The
Bill has been referred to as a Bill against
the Free State. I cannot see anything in
the Bill referring to the Free State, more
than to any other state outside the United
Kingdom. It is a Bill to protect
Northern Ireland from invasion by those
who perhaps would have for their aim and
object the breaking up of our constitution. It is a Bill that will prevent the
Russian from coming in, and he is as
dangerous a
as you would have
coming into any country. It is a Bill
that would prevent the German coming in
and he is not very favourable to this
country. It is a Bill that will prevent
the Irish Republican from coming into
this country.
man
Mr. McHUGH: He is the bad boy!
Mr. BARCLAY: He is no better than
any of the rest. I think those who are
law-abiding and those who have for their
aim and object the well-being of the State
will welcome the Bill. I would just like
to emphasise something that Senator
Cunningham referred to and that is personation. I think something should be
done in this respect. During election
times I take a great interest in the
elections.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Why did you vote
against my Amendment?
Mr. SPEAKER: I really cannot allow
the debate to take this conversational
tone.
Mr. BARCLAY: I took an interest
especially in one constituency, and personation was practised to a great extent.
They were brought from outside districts
in motors and char-a-bancs and these
people voted against the official Unionist
candidate and for the Socialist with the
result that he is sitting in the House of
Parliament to-day. At the municipal
election the same thing took place. They
brought them in from I do not know
where. I think it is the duty of the
Government to take drastic steps to put
an end to this kind of thing.
179 Representation of People Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Barclay]
About the Amendment that was
mentioned by Senator Campbell which
was that the police should have supervision we thought at the time we would
not bring the police force into contact
with any of the political parties. That
was my aim and object in voting against
the Amendment. But the time has
perhaps changed since then, and I have
not the least doubt that the police having
more power in the polling station would
be of great benefit at the present time. I
have great pleasure in supporting the
Bill. Some of our Friends here in the
Opposition object to a certain Clause, but
I think that this Clause 2 will be very
beneficial to the Opposition. They have
lost the representation of two seats, and
they might have had two intelligent
business men to put their views before the
House which would, no doubt, be of great
benefit to all concerned. Still they are
opposed to this Clause that would help
them. And then reference has been made
to Central Belfast. I really think it was
a shame to see the late Joseph Devlin
being opposed in that seat.
Mr. McMAHON: A Unionist opposed
him.
Mr. BARCLAY: A Unionist was not
going to oppose him. That was only a
mixed vote. I thought it was a great
shame to see this thing taking place,
especially amongst their own party. There
must be something radically wrong
amongst that party. I have great
pleasure in supporting and voting for the
Second Reading of the Bill.
I think
also
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
the case for the Bill has been argued at
such length, that I will not detain the
House very much in summing up. The
substance of the Opposition complaints is
that the Bill is unnecessary and
sectarian. If it is unnecessary I would
ask why there is all this outcry about it.
The outcry would appear to be unnecessary as well. It is in no way sectarian.
The terms apply equally to all denominations. The Government is determined
that no one should have a voice in the
government of this country who has not
shown evidence of a determination to promote the prosperity of the country in
which he is going to reside. I think the
opposition to the second Clause is not
very sensible. The Opposition speakers
have spoken a great deal of coercion in
the matter. Coercion is a word that is
very dear to Nationalists. I think I
have often heard it made use of.
fact is there is a great deal of coercion.
The income-tax is pure coercion, but the
fact that we object to it does make us less
liable.
But the
Mr. McHUGH: What about the
coercion of Ulster?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am only

one main principle in a constituency-in

is that the representative has to vote on

opportunity of amending laws which are
likely to affect them very much in their
private lives. With regard Mr.
regard to

Committee considered this matter and on

has failed in its object?
Mr.
JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It

Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:

Finance (Stamp Duties) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into

Moved: "That this House do now
resolve itself into a Committee on the said

On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
Clause 1.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I should like to
support this Clause in a very few words.
On account of some question elsewhere I
think it is desirable that the House should
know that this is not a contribution from
the general taxation of the country. This
£1,200 paid for upkeep is a refund from
the general receipts from taxation
through the solicitors' body of £5,000 a
year. That is acquired by the Solicitors'
Association for the purpose of administering an obligation imposed on them by
statute. Their duties are purely honorary
and voluntary while they are onerous and
irksome and consist principally of two
things. Their first duty is to ensure that
those entering the profession are competent safely and fittingly to advise the
McLoughlin, Mr. T.
McMahon, Mr. J.
public on vital questions, and in the
second place they have to protect the
public on questions of discipline in their
own profession.
There is one thing in this Bill which I
think requires Amendment although I
have no doubt the Council of the Incorporated Law Society, of which I was not
a member when this Bill was first introduced, although I am now, have to some
extent approved of it. The Clause
suggests that a sum of £1,200 is required
to enable this Committee to carry out its
statutory obligations. I take it that that
is a reasonable sum, in fact the minimum
sum required, but the Council of the
Incorporated Law Society are not even
assured of that sum under this Clause.
The duties payable by the solicitors' profession are two-fold. An apprentice first
pays substantial stamp duties, and when
he becomes a solicitor he pays a yearly
duty, the justification for which I have
never been able to see. The Council may
not be in a position to raise that sum of
£1,200 a year, because the only funds
available are the stamp duties of
apprentices. The Government have
ascertained that it will require £1,200 a
year to enable the Council to carry out
the statutory obligations imposed upon
them, I suggest they should assure the
Council of that sum out of the stamp
duties, amounting to £5,000 a year, paid
by solicitors, in case the apprentices stamp
duty is not sufficient to cover that sum.
Mr. LONG: Before replying to the
point raised by Senator McAllister I
should like to congratulate him on the
distinction which has been conferred upon
him by his election as a member of the
Council of the Incorporated Law Society.
I should add that the Incorporated Law
Society are perfectly satisfied with this
grant of £1,200 a year.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 8th May, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.

is approved by the inspecting officers
appointed under Rule 2 of the Schedule
appended to Council Order, Northern

Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I am authorised by the
Prime Minister, on his behalf, to inform
the Senate that His Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland, in the name of and on
behalf of His Majesty the King, has been
pleased to give his Assent to the following
Bills:
:-
Dogs Protection.
Finance (Stamp Duties).
Destructive Insects and Pests.

YOUTHS SENT TO PRISON OR
BORSTAL.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House if he can state how
the figures for the year 1933 compare with
those for 1932 as respects the number of
youths under 21 sentenced to prison or
Borstal, or dealt with as probationary
offenders, and what are the principal
classes of offences for which such convictions were had.
it

his permission, to circulate the required
particulars in the form of a return in the
Official Report.

POSITION OF "BLACK-COATED
WORKERS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Campbell:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
it is a fact that numbers of business and
professional people, spoken of as "blackcoated workers," who are outside the
National Insurance Acts, are in dire
penury through unemployment; and if
the Government will consider whether
earners of that class, up to a certain
salary, should be included in a compulsory
insurance scheme or given the protection
of some scheme of voluntary contributions.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The class referred
to in this question is a class little heard of
in Parliament, an uncomplaining class,
but a deserving and, in recent years, a
hard-hit class. They suffer in silence,
and very often they starve in silence.
Perhaps they over-value the appearance
of respectability, but their self-denial is
incessant. There are many things they
do without in order to give to their
children an education, the expense of
which they can ill afford. They would
shudder to make their hard lot known to
the world. No unemployment insurance
is at their disposal if they are out of work,
which happens often. No health insurance is for them if they are sick, and sickness will come and is very expensive.
Representation of People Bill 188
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
Doctors' bills and chemists' bills have to
be met. They are a class who do not
advertise. They are a class who do not
agitate, and we know by practical experience that a class who do not agitate nowadays goes to the wall. They have no
trades' unions to speak and fight for them,
I do hope that Parliament will, sooner
rather than later, remember the dire need
of this class.
It may be asked "what class of the community have you in your mind." In reply,
I would instance the case of ships' officers
who are idle in every port in the United
Kingdom. I would also take the case of
musicians. Perhaps one out of every
three musicians is workless at the present
time. With numbers of clerks the
is almost desperate.
position is
Those
classes are only illustrative of the very
large body of persons known as the "blackcoated" workers, professional or trained
people who are out of work and who have
no place in the insurance schemes.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to say that I have come into
touch recently with a considerable number of these people who are in very
straitened circumstances, and while it
may not be possible to include them in
any insurance scheme, I hope the
Belfast
employers of
and Northern
Ireland will be as sympathetic towards
them as possible. There are many cases
of men who were formerly employed as
draughtsmen in the shipyards and who
were not entitled to unemployment
benefit when they lost their positions
some years ago. During such a long
spell of unemployment their savings soon
became exhausted, and they are in a very
difficult position at the present time.
hope the employers will extend every
possible sympathy towards these men
and give them every chance of securing
employment, because there are cases
where employers are advertising across
the water for men for jobs that could
very well be filled by some of the men I
have referred to. I should be very glad
if it were found possible to include them
in some scheme of insurance, because it
would relieve them of a great deal of
anxiety and worry.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As the
hon. and learned Member is aware, all
persons, including "black-coated "
a
workers, who are engaged under
contract of service are, subject to certain
exceptions, insured up to an income
limit of £250 per annum. Any question
of extending the scope of compulsory
insurance will fall to be considered by
the Unemployment Insurance Statutory
Committee which is proposed to be
established by the new Unemployment
Insurance Bill at present under the consideration of the Imperial Parliament.
One member of this Committee is to be
appointed after consultation with my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Labour
for Northern Ireland.
As the hon. and learned Member is
also aware, it is the policy of the Government of Northern Ireland to maintain
parity of legislation with Great Britain
in the social services; and if the Statutory
Committee decided in favour of any proposals such as are suggested in the
question, and the Unemployment
Insurance Acts are amended accordingly,
no doubt similar steps will be taken here.
Representation of the People Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
""
Resolved, That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont.)
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Extension of period of
residence in the United Kingdom
required by s. 9 of 7 & 8 Geo. 5, c. 64,
and s. 4 of 18 & 19 Geo. 5, c. 24).
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, at the end of Clause 1 on page 1,
to add,
"Provided that a person who is
registered as a parliamentary or local
government elector in the register
which is in force at the passing of this
Act shall not, so long as that register
remains in force, be disqualified from
voting as such elector by reason only of
the provisions of this section."
The Bill cannot, of course, affect the
register now in force in so far as the
inclusion of any person in the register
is concerned. Hon. Senators will
appreciate, however, that inclusion in the
189 Representation of People Bill-
8 MAY 1934

Committee
register does not confer upon any person
a conclusive right to vote since that right
is dependant upon freedom from any
legal incapacity, which might, obviously,
be contracted after the compilation of
the register. Immediately this Bill
becomes law lack of the necessary 7 years'
residence qualification will become a legal
incapacity to vote SO that unless
appropriate provision is made a number
of persons included in the current register
by virtue of a three years' residence
qualification will become legally incapable
of voting at any elections which may be
held during the currency of the present
register because they cannot fulfil the 7
years' requirement. This would not conflict with the policy underlying the
Clause, but the administrative difficulty
of giving effect to it on a register already
compiled is patently almost insuperable.
The obvious remedy is to withdraw the
application of the Clause from the current
register, as is done by this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I quite agree, of course,
with the policy the noble Viscount has
outlined, because it follows to some extent
a suggestion which I myself threw out on
the Second Reading. I drew the special
attention of the House to the fact that
inclusion on the register did not of itself
confer the right to vote, but I do not
altogether understand what the intention
of the noble Viscount is. If it is the
intention that this Amendment is merely
in substitution for the first half of the
following Amendment standing in my
name I should readily accept it as being
expressed in rather more concise and
explicit terms than my own, but if it is
intended that this Amendment should
stand alone, and it is not intended to
recommend to the Committee the
acceptance of the latter part of my first
Amendment, I should fail to accept the
noble Viscount's Amendment as fulfilling
in any way the requirements of the situation. If it is intended that the noble
Viscount's Amendment should stand
alone, what will happen will be that
although a man on the current register
that came into force on 15th December last
will be unaffected; yet if he has no other
qualification and has not got the seven
years' residence qualification he will be
cut off the register on the 16th of next
December. Is that the intention of the
Government?
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: It is the intention of the
Government that this Amendment should
be merely a respite and not a reprieve?
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: Then I am to understand that it is the deliberate policy of
the Government to treat these people who
have the three years' qualification as
though they had been convicted of an
offence against the Corrupt Practices Act
and sentenced by a competent court to
the deprivation of civil rights for some
period up to perhaps a maximum of seven
years. Am I to understand that that is
the deliberate intention of the Government that they are going to take away
a right which a man has had up to now
and is to exercise during the current year
and that he is going to lose that right?"
I cannot possibly accept that as a reasonable solution. Although I am prepared,
as the Committee knows, to support the
proposal of imposing a seven years'
qualification for future entrants into
Northern Ireland I cannot agree that we
have any right whatever to deprive
people of rights which have already
accrued or are in process of accruing.
(c
And further with regard to the expression in the Amendment, so long as
that register remains in force,
"it does
not seem to me that this Amendment
affects any practical purpose whatever.
Nobody knows better than the noble
Viscount's advisers that you cannot in
practice prevent anybody on the register
from voting if the opportunity arises.
The concession therefore is no concession
at all. I hope on reconsideration the
Government will see the necessity, from
the point of view of abstract justice, of
accepting an Amendment on the lines of
that I shall move very shortly. In the
meantime the position would seem to be
if the noble Viscount's Amendment is
carried, I shall have to begin my Amendment with the words "and that." As
it stands the Amendment is simply a safeguard of the rights of a man until the
14th December next and then after that
day although the man may have voted
during the current year he is to be put
off the register. That is a most extraordinary proposal.
Amendment agreed to.

THE SENATE
Committee
Mr. LESLIE: Having regard to that
I cannot move the Amendment on the
Paper exactly as it stands. The Amendment I must now move is to continue the
addition of course by adding the words
""
and that any person, who, on the
sixteenth day of January, 1934, was
resident in Northern Ireland and,
throughout the period on the said day
requisite to entitle such person to be
registered has continued to be so resident,
shall have the same right to be registered,
and if registered, to vote, as such person
would have had if this Act had not been
passed." That is the Amendment I shall
ask you in due course to put to the Committee. It seems scarcely necessary to
say very much about this. The Amendment is directed to saving those rights
which have begun to mature. People
who came into Northern Ireland at some
time subsequent to 1928, when the three
years' qualification was imposed, have
begun or may have begun to acquire
the right to vote. In some cases they will
already have voted. The Committee has
just saved the right of anybody on the
register up to December next. At present
the person who is on the register now
and has not been here or will not have
been here seven years at the end of the
next qualifying period in July next will
be cut off the register and disfranchised
as from 15th December next. That is
what will happen, and that cannot be
justified anyhow.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think the hon. and learned Senator has
quite got the idea right. I think the
man will have to complete his term of
qualification. As far as I understand it
he will eventually acquire the right. If
he has got three years now he will be able
to add on the three.
I
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I cannot
plead guilty to misunderstanding.
quite recognise that the three years he
has already done will count towards the
seven. But his right to vote will be
interrupted until these seven years are
completed. That is what the noble
Viscount agrees is the position, and that
I cannot accept. We have allowed the
man to come in and get his vote on the
three years' qualification and we must
stand by that. We are quite entitled to
tell people who come in afterwards that
the term is seven years, but we are not
entitled to interfere with the rights
accruing. The way I propose to avoid
that is by taking 16th January as the first
day, which is one month approximately
after notice was given to the world at
large that a new qualification was about
to be imposed. Notice that the law was
going to be altered was given in effect
on 18th December last when the gracious
Speech was read. Therefore, anybody
who did not at once come into Northern
Ireland has only himself to thank if he
suffers from the subsequent alteration of
the law. That is a different question
altogether.
The question of imposing a new
qualification has already been decided by
the House. This Amendment is not
directed to altering that at all. All it
is directed to is saving those rights which
are in process of maturing. Therefore,
I say also not only must the person be
resident some three years, but it must be
in Northern Ireland and not in the United
Kingdom, because I quite recognise if a
man
came up a couple of years hence
and said he was in the United Kingdom
in January, 1924, he is a man there is no
hope of tracking. Therefore, I say he
should be resident in Northern Ireland.
And of course I require that he must
continue in residence in Northern Ireland
until that day comes when under the
existing law he would complete his three
years and would be entitled to vote if
this Bill were not passed.
I do not think I can say much more
as regards the injustice of sentencing an
untried man to a sentence equal to that
which might be imposed by a judge if he
had committed a criminal offence. We
have people on the register or about to
be on the register and we really have no
right that can be held up for examination for a moment to alter matters to their
detriment. We can, if we think fit, do
anything as to the future and can stand
over it.
That is one thing, but to impose
complete or partial disfranchisement on
people who have conformed to the existing law is to my mind unthinkable. I
do not think there is anything in this
Amendment which can be described as
repugnant to the main principle of the
Bill, which is to obtain further assurance
that the people coming into Northern
Ireland in future shall be persons with a
reasonable stake in the country. It leaves
untouched the question I alluded to on
193 Representation of People Bill-

8 MAY 1934
Committee
the Second Reading about the man who
takes a residence at the seaside and who
has to wait seven years before he can vote
for the district council. I do think that if
the Bill is passed without an Amendment
on the lines I have suggested it will be a
Bill which would be impossible to support
before any tribunal or assembly or anything you like of reasonably fair-minded
men. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The situation which the hon. and learned Senator
has taken exception to arose equally when
the three years' qualification was imposed
in 1928. But as I understand it the

from the Amendment set forth on the
Paper as regards date. If he will agree
to withdraw the Amendment at the
present moment and put it down in the
different form on the Report stage I will
consider his objections in the meantime,
if that will satisfy him.
Mr. LESLIE : Nothing that I can
think of at the moment would induce me
to reject the olive branch extended by the

understand that my proposal differs as
regards date. I have got rid of 15th
December, 1933 but not December 1934.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The misunderstanding is mine. What I offered
is tantamount to reconsider the matter by
the Report stage if he will withdraw the
Amendment now instead of having a
division. Of course I cannot promise
that the reconsideration will entail the
acceptance. I cannot do that but I will
reconsider the matter.
Mr. LESLIE: The olive branch has become a little steeped in seasoning material.
I want to say, having regard to that first
tendered to me that of course there are
various difficulties. If the Amendment
is withdrawn now we may be in a
difficulty on the Question that Clause 1
as amended stand part of the Bill. On
the other hand if I divide the Committee
and the Amendment is not carried the
point cannot be raised again at all. I think
on the whole the Committee will agree
that it is better to withdraw the Amendment in order to give the fullest possible
time to consider it. Therefore, if the
Committee consents I shall ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment, but I shall
certainly bring it up again in some form
at a later date.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move at the
end of Clause 1 to insert the following
new sub-section :
"It shall be the duty of the registration officer, if he has the necessary
information, to insert in the appropriate
register a note indicating the earliest
qualifying period in respect of which any
person therein registered as an elector
for the Parliament of the United Kingdom but disqualified by reason only of
the provisions of the aforesaid sub-section
(3a) as amended by this Act from being
registered as an elector for the Parliament
of, or for a local government constituency
in Northern Ireland can cease to be disqualified in such behalf."
In speaking on the Second Reading it
will be remembered that I said-I am
going to read it lest there should be any
misunderstanding-I said:
I propose also to put on the Paper
two new Sub-Clauses which will direct
attention to the manner in which the
charge of unworkability can be met. I
do not think the sub-clauses will prove
altogether acceptable, but I think they
may serve indirectly to help us to consider the question particularly whether
it is necessary to insert anything in
the Bill or whether what is required
can be done by Order in Council or
perhaps by the Minister himself in the
way of amendments to rules. (Official
Report, Vol. 16, col. 163.)
I drew attention of course to the use
of the same register both for United
Kingdom and for Northern Ireland purposes, having done that I reflected as
to the terms of the Amendment to put
down. I thought that as the Amendment
was one obviously designed to facilitate
discussion the words I have put on the
Paper might serve the purpose, although
of course it will be obvious to anybody
that the words on the Paper were never
intended to be actually enacted. It is
obvious they could not go through in
that form. Afterwards I came to a conclusion as to whether it was worth while
to move any substantive Amendment at
all. I thought and still think we ought
to get something from the Government to
indicate the method by which they pro-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
pose to work the alteration in the register.
Since putting the Amendment on the
Paper I have examined sundry registers.
I have two with me, one issued in the
year 1920 and the other in 1930. One
before and the other after the passing of
the 1928 Act. The register for the year
1919 contains on the first page sundry
notes. The register for 1930 contains a
great many more notes one of which is
this: "The person against whose name the
mark
the ordinary paragraph mark,
a P reversed-is placed is not entitled to
vote as a Parliamentary elector at any
election for the Parliament of Northern
Ireland."
That note, I take it, was added to the
registers by virtue of an Amendment
introduced under the powers of subsection (2) of Section 6 of the Act of
1928, the provision which gives power to
the Governor of Northern Ireland, with
the consent of the Secretary of State, to
make alterations by means of an Order in
Council. I think that probably the Committee will agree that if anything is to be
done at all the proper way to do it
would be, not by inserting anything in
the Bill before us, but by leaving it to be
done by the Governor in Council with
the approval of the Secretary of State,
exactly on the lines laid down in the
Act of 1928, which have apparently to
some extent already been availed of.
That would get over the difficulty, which
I have tried to indicate, as to whether
we are in a position to impose a duty on
the registration officer who, except, I
think, in the case of Belfast, is the Clerk
of the Crown and Peace, not, as in
England, the Secretary of the County
Council.
our
It would also solve the other problem
as to whether these registers, used jointly
for United Kingdom and Northern
Ireland purposes, are sufficiently
property to enable us to make requirements as to the insertion of a note.
If we
can do that the note in my
mind is simply a figure with a ring
round it to indicate the year when
the man would become qualified. I think
we ought to have something from the
Government indicating what their proposal is, and if that is given I shall ask
the leave of the Committee to withdraw
my Amendment, which as I have already
said, was never intended to find its way
to the Statute Book. There are many
reasons why it could not, but in order to
keep myself in Order I beg to move the
Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The effect
of this Amendment would be to throw
upon registration officers an obligation to
investigate with a greater or lesser degree
of thoroughness the qualifications for
future registration of persons, qualified
for registration as electors for the Parliament of the United Kingdom, who by
reason of the over-riding 7 years'
residence qualification are, at the date
of compilation of any given register, not
qualified as electors for the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, or as local government
electors, and a duty to insert in the
register notes relative to such future
qualification.
It must be remembered that under
present arrangements the register of
electors is compiled by or on behalf of
the Imperial Parliament, by which the
whole of the State moiety of the cost is
borne. The registration officers are,
therefore, in that capacity, under the
jurisdiction of the Parliament and
Government of the United Kingdom and
the Parliament of Northern Ireland has
no power to impose additional duties upon
them. The Clause will be worked by
administrative action on the part of the
registration officer.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we are all
obliged to the noble Viscount for telling
us that something will be done by
administrative action. I ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

CLAUSE 2 (Declaration of candidates
intention to take seat in the House of
Commons).
(1) Section one of the Ballot Act,
1872 (which relates to the nomination
of candidates for parliamentary elections) shall have effect as if at the end
of the first paragraph of that section
were inserted the following
there
words:
:-
"A candidate shall not be deemed
to be validly nominated unless, with
a nomination paper by which the
candidate is nominated, there has been
delivered to the returning officer, by
the candidate himself or his proposer or
seconder, a declaration in writing
subscribed by the candidate, in the
presence of a witness, that he intends,
if elected, to take his seat in the House
of Commons after compliance with the
law and the standing orders of the
House in that behalf.'
*
""
*
The following Amendment stood on

Leslie :
:-
In Clause 2, page 2, line 11, after the

on his behalf.”
""
Mr. LESLIE: It occurs to me that here
we ought to follow the precedent set by
Section 1 of the Ballot Act, 1872, and
provide for the possibility of a candidate
not himself being present at the nomination. As the Clause stands the
declaration has to be in writing signed by
the candidate in the presence of a witness
and delivered to the returning officer. It
Occurs to me that the unfortunate
candidate may possibly be unable to be
present on that day. He may be out of
the United Kingdom. It may be the
case of a bye-election coming on suddenly,
and a candidate may not be in a position,
though he may have the will, to sign the
necessary declaration. Therefore, I
think we should provide for someone to
sign on his behalf, and the person I
suggest is the proposer, because he must
be present at the actual nomination.
a man knew an election was coming on he
could sign the declaration in the presence
of a witness and leave it behind him for
production on the day of nomination.
But then there is the difficulty that the
witness might die, and I think we can get
over the difficulty without doing any
harm to anyone by allowing the declaration to be signed, in the absence of the
candidate, by the proposer on his behalf.
If
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The effect
of this Amendment would be to relieve
a candidate for election to the Parliament
of Northern Ireland from the obligation
to subscribe personally a declaration that
if elected he intends to take his seat, and
to enable the proposer of such a candidate
to make the declaration on his behalf.
The idea underlying the Amendment is
presumably that cases may arise in which
the personal attendance of the candidate
for the purpose of signing and delivering
the declaration may not be practicable or
convenient.
Reference to the wording of the Clause
and of the declaration will reveal, however, that the personal attendance of the
candidate to sign and deliver the
declaration to the returning officer at the
time of nomination is not necessary.. The
form of declaration admits of its subscription at any convenient time and
before any convenient witness, and the
Clause itself provides for the delivery of
the declaration to the returning officer by
the candidate himself or by his proposer
or seconder. It is not easy, therefore, to
visualise circumstances
which any
serious difficulty might arise in securing
the candidate's own signature to the
declaration. Moreover, the Government
feels that a declaration of such importance
to the electors should be made personally
in

rest

Representation of People Bill 202
Oral Answers
10 MAY 1934
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 10th May, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. T. MCLAUGHLIN
(Deputy-Speaker) in the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Labour:
Northern Ireland Unemployment Fund
Account for the year 1st April, 1932, to
31st March, 1933.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Question to Minister.
UNPAID MAGISTRACY LEGISLA-
TION.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether it is the intention of
the Government to introduce the Bill for
the abolition of the judicial functions of
the unpaid magistracy in the present
session.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): In addition to the proposed
alteration of the law in regard to the
jurisdiction of magistrates at Petty
Sessions, the contemplated Measure will
comprise a variety of miscellaneous
amendments of the Summary Jurisdiction
Acts. It will be readily appreciated that
the preparation of a Measure of this complicated character must necessarily take
some time, and I am not yet in a position
to give any definite assurance as to the
probable date of its introduction.
I may say, however, that it is hoped
that it will be possible to introduce the
Bill in the forthcoming Autumn Session.
Mr. McMAHON: I should like to ask
the noble Leader of the House if he would
inform us who was responsible for urging
the Government to bring in this legislation. Was it the few busy-bodies who
were responsible for stampeding the Prime
Minister into bringing in legislation
some time ago to curtail the liberties of
the subject. This is just another effort
on the part of a small minority in our
midst to crush the sporting fraternity of
Belfast, including the bookmakers, who
have been responsible for raising large
sums of money in the interests of charity,
and whose meetings the Prime Minister
himself has attended.
Mr.
DEPUTY-SPEAKER:
I am
afraid the hon. Member is out of order,
because he is really discussing the merits
of the forthcoming Bill on the subject.
Mr. McMAHON: I hold that I am in
order, because
I am asking the noble
Leader of the House-
I am
Mr. DEPUTY-SPEAKER:
advised that you are out of order.
Mr. McMAHON: Very well. I bow
to your ruling.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons agree to the Amendments made by the Senate to the
Agricultural Marketing Bill, and to the
Amendment made by the Senate to the
Railways Bill, without Amendment.
Representation of the People Bill.
Order for Receiving Report of Amendment read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I beg to
"That this Report be now
move,
received."
Mr. CAMPBELL: I wish, on behalf
of the party I represent in this House, to
register again our very strong and earnest
objection to this Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendment was accordingly
reported and agreed to by the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in the proviso to Clause 1, page 2,
line 3, to delete the words, "so long as
that register remains in force."
In fulfilment of the promise I made to
the hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie)
on Tuesday last, I have given careful
consideration to his proposals. To accept
these proposals in full would, in my
opinion, and in the opinion of my
colleagues in the Government, have
several undesirable effects.
In the first place, reference to residence
in Northern Ireland is repugnant to the
principle established by the 1928 Act,
203 Representation of People Bill-

THE SENATE
Report Stage
[Viscount Charlemont]
namely, that the over-riding 3 years'
residence qualification should be satisfied
by residence in the United Kingdom.
In the second place, the hon. and
learned Senator proposes to grant special
privileges in regard to enfranchisement to
a section of the potential electorate who
happened to take up residence
in
Northern Ireland on or before an
arbitrarily chosen date. There would
thus be created three distinct sections in
the electorate: firstly, those who by virtue
of 3 years' residence here or elsewhere in
the United Kingdom were on the 1933
register and who, if still resident in
Northern Ireland, will again qualify for
the 1934 and
and subsequent registers;
secondly, those who by chance happened
to reside in Northern Ireland on 16th
January this year and need not have had
prior residence either here or elsewhere in
the United Kingdom, but who will enjoy
the benefit of a reduced period of residential qualification, provided such qualification is acquired by residence from 16th
January, 1934, onwards in Northern
Ireland, as distinct from the United
Kingdom, and thirdly, those who entering Northern Ireland after 16th January
this year must fulfil a seven years'
qualification either here or in some other
part of the United Kingdom.
I am sure that the hon. and learned
Senator would be the last person to press
a proposal which would not only create
invidious discrimination between three
distinct sections of the electorate, but also
could not fail to add very grave difficulties to the annual task of preparing the
register of electors.
I gathered, however, in the course of
Tuesday's debate that the hon. and
learned Senator was especially perturbed
by the idea of disfranchising persons already on the register rather than by the
additional waiting period, to which reference has already been made. I have considered this point separately, and I am
happy to be able to inform the hon. and
learned Senator that I can meet his desires to the extent of allowing those persons who are now on the register to
qualify on future registers even though
they have not the full seven years' residence called for by the Bill as it now
stands. It is with that object I move
the amendment standing in my name.
Mr. LESLIE : I cannot but think
that the House will sympathise with me
this afternoon in rising to express my
great satisfaction that the Government
has been able to meet the views that I
have endeavoured to express both on the
Second Reading and the Committee stage
of this Bill. It will be recognised by all
of us, not only by those who are in the
Senate but by everybody throughout
Northern Ireland, that the effect of the
amendment moved by the Noble Viscount
will be that no person at present on the
register shall be disfranchised by virtue of
the Bill the House is about to pass. That
of course, was the particular point for
which I was contending. There was
what I might describe as the ancilliary
consideration that is the extension in certain cases of the period of three years to
seven years, and I have to admit that if
that had been insisted upon the administrative difficulties would undoubtedly
have been very great, and would probably
have outweighed altogether the advantages that might have accrued from
accepting my original proposal.
The number of people who will have to
extend their residence qualification from
three years to seven years is probably not
very great, and as a matter of fact I do
not know, as far as the Parliamentary
franchise is concerned, that they will be
affected at all. We have very recently
had a General Election, and if this Parliament has a continuance for its normal
period it is very possible that nobody will
be cut out from Parliamentary voting at
all. The local government question is a
little more difficult, and I have still some
sympathy with the man who has taken a
house on the three years basis and now
finds that he has to wait a longer period.
Probably not many persons will be
affected. I had endeavoured to provide
for everybody, and in doing so I was
obliged to introduce the expression
Northern Ireland" instead of "United
Kingdom" on account of the obvious administrative difficulty in keeping track
of a man unless he was in Northern Ireland on some date, and the only da
that could be chosen was some date relative to the Parliamentary register.
Therefore I thought 16th January as the
first day of the qualifying period would
be appropriate, because people who were
in Northern Ireland on that date would
normally appear on the register for the
205 Representation of People Bill

10 MAY 1934
Exceptional Distress
which would come into force on 15th December. That was the reason for that
date, and I think perhaps the whole arrangement would have worked, but it
would have been very difficult. The main
point remains, that the chief difficulty
has been met, and the Goverment has
decided to bow to the opinion, which I
feel sure was the opinion of this House.
that no man must be actually disfranchised by virtue of the new Bill. That
is entirely a different question from extending the period of qualification, for
that is a matter over which Parliament
has complete control. If the Bill had
been limited to that I think it is possible
I should not have said quite some of the
things I did say. The great difficulty
has been removed if the House concurs in
the suggestion of the noble Viscount.
I cannot but think the House will concur
and they will be disposed to thank the
noble Viscount for what he has done to
give effect to the general opinion.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I rise simply
to congratulate the hon. and learned
Member on the other side of the House
on introducing this amendment of his
which the Government have now accepted.
I think it is an amendment that really
would have defeated the Government if
it had been pressed to a division. Therefore we have a right to congratulate him
upon his great ability and his great effort.
Amendment agreed to.
I beg
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT:
to move in the proviso to Clause 1, after
"from" ("disqualified from voting" to
insert "being registered or."
Mr. LESLIE : The words of course
will be recognised by the House as
purely consequential, and I do not think
it is necessary that anything should be
said to commend them to the House. I
only rise to have the opportunity of
thanking Senator Hill Dickson for what
he said on the last Amendment which is
connected with this one.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: There is a question I wish to ask before we part with
the Representation of the People Bill. On
Clause 3, Sub-section (26) (a)——
Mr. DEPUTY-SPEAKER: We have
finished with that Bill for the present.
Perhaps the hon. Senator would raise
the matter on the next stage.
Exceptional Distress (Transitional
Provisions) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: The object of this Bill is
to extend the powers conferred by the
Local Authorities (Financial Provisions)
Act, 1921, SO as to enable local
authorities concerned to meet certain
expenditure of a transitional nature by
payments spread over a period of five
years. In effect, the Bill will enable
local authorities to ease the burden on
rates in respect of relief of the ablebodied unemployed.
The provisions of the Unemployment
Insurance Bill at present under consideration in another place propose
radical alterations in the existing
arrangements for the financing of that
service, and the Government has agreed
to facilitate local authorities by granting
these powers in respect of expenditure
incurred since 1st April, 1933, up to the
date when the revised arrangements
come into force. This Bill provides the
necessary statutory authority. I beg to
move.
Mr. LESLIE :
rose was to
There is really very
little to be said about the Bill. It of
course follows precedent. When the 1921
Bill was before the Imperial Parliament
it will be remembered a special provision was made allowing ten years as
the maximum period instead of the five
years now proposed. The only reason I
ask the Parliamentary
Secretary to tell the House what is
meant by the expression county council.
For one reason or another there 18
perhaps some little doubt about it. I
think we ought to be told definitely
whether the expression county council as
used in the Bill does or does not include
the
governing body of the City of
Belfast or the governing body of the
City of Londonderry. I am not going
to make any assertion myself on the
subject, because for one reason or
another I think the ordinary back bench
Member would be excused for some little
doubt on the point. Perhaps the Parliamentary Secretary will tell us what the
position is or if he likes to expand the
statement he might do it on the Motion
for going into Committee. I mentioned
this specially because it occurred to me
the noble Viscount might like to close up
Exceptional Distress
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
this Bill on Tuesday next owing to the
fact that it relates to the financial year
ending on the 31st March last past.
Mr. LONG: I quite recognise the
importance of the point the hon. and
learned Senator has raised, but I am
advised that no definition of the expression would make the provision any
clearer. If the hon. and learned
Senator will examine the Local Government Act of 1898 which is the basis of all
modern local government he will, I
think, agree that there is no doubt that
the county borough councils have the
powers and duties of county councils in
the absence of any express provision to
the contrary, so that no definition is
necessary in this case.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move,

That this House
I beg to
do now
adjourn."
Mr. LESLIE: Until when?
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
next.

Minutes to

Question to Minister.
SMUGGLING ACROSS THE
BORDER.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the
of Mr.
McLaughlin :-
name
To ask the Leader of the House
whether it is a fact that there was a glut
of cattle and horses in Armagh fair on
Thursday last; whether this was due to
the smuggling of animals across the
Border; whether the farmers were either
unable to sell their stock or had to sell at
reduced prices; and whether the Government will make representations to the
proper authorities so that the farmers of
Northern Ireland may be protected
against this state of affairs.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I put this question to show the harm the setting up of a
Border in this country has done, and the
really little power given to this Government by
by the Imperial Parliament.
Therefore, might I suggest that this
Government should make every effort to
have this matter of the Border settled,
and allow the business of the country to
go on without interference from Customs
or Government officials.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The Government is aware
that according to newspaper reports the
number of animals on offer at Armagh
fair on Thursday, 3rd instant, was fairly
large, and that the prices obtained were
generally somewhat lower than has
recently been the case at this fair.
As regards smuggling, the Government
has made representations to the United
Kingdom Government and as a result
steps are being taken which it is hoped
will remedy the conditions to which the
hon. Senator refers.
Representation of the People Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this Bill be now read the
Third Time.
211 Representation of People Bill-

THE SENATE
Third Reading
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I wish to ask
the Leader of the House a question in
connection with Clause 3 of this Bill. It is
with regard to blind voters. The Clause
says that a presiding officer shall require
a voter to declare orally whether he is
incapacitated by his blindness. I want
the Government to insert after the word
"he," the words or she," because we
have blind women as well as blind men.
In line 23 also I submit that the words
should read, his or her." Of course,
there may be a legal definition to the
""
I
effect that "he" includes "she," and
some presiding officers may be legal
gentlemen, but we have presiding officers
who are not legal gentlemen, and if a
blind woman came forward to vote the
presiding officer might turn her down.
think it would strengthen the Bill if the
words she and her
were inserted
in the appropriate places. I may say
that I have brought this matter forward
at the request of Senator Armstrong who
drew my attention to this point, but he
is not able to be here to-day.
Mr. CAMPBELL: As I said on the
Second Reading, I deplore the spirit
revealed by the first Clause of this Bill.
It is an anti-Irish Clause, I also deplore
the spirit revealed in the second Clause,
which, in my humble jugdment, is
intended to coerce the free expression of
opinion at the polls. Judged by these
two Clauses the purpose of the Bill is
neither long-sighted nor clear-sighted,
and as far as these particular Clauses are
concerned I do believe that in coming
years the Act will be a piece of legislative
futility.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As regards
the objection urged by the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) I do not
think the first Clause of the Bill is
unfair, because it applies to everybody
equally. As regards the second Clause
being a form of coercion, and the hon.
and learned Member's allegation that the
Act will become a futility, I think it is
far more a futility for a constituency to
return to Parliament a Member who does
not sit. I think that is extremely
illogical.
With regard to the point raised by
Senator Hill Dickson, I should like to say
that, as a general rule, in all Acts passed
since 1850, words importing the
masculine gender are to be construed as
including females until the context otherwise requires. Until 1918, in Statutes
dealing with the parliamentary franchise,
words importing the masculine did not
include females, because under the
general law women were not qualified to
vote. Since this disqualification has
been removed the ordinary rule of construction has applied, and accordingly
words in this Bill importing the masculine
gender include females. This unchivalrous method of drafting on the part
of the Parliamentary Draughtsman is to
be deprecated, but I think it makes any
Amendment of the Bill unnecessary.
Mr. LESLIE: The point taken by
Senator Hill Dickson has been raised by
more than one other person, and in
addition to the strictly legal reply given
by the noble Leader of the House I might
perhaps point out one little thing, and
that is to draw the attention of those
interested to line 31 on page 3 of the Bill.
In line 31 you find a distinct reference to
a husband, and I would suggest to anybody who is still in doubt that the use
of the word "husband" irresistibly
suggests a wife, and a wife is necessarily
a female.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved, "That
this Bill do now
pass." (Viscount Charlemont.)
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill with
some Amendments, to which they desire
their concurrence.

Laid before the House (pursuant to
