Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV. be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the Second
Reading of the Appropriation Bill, the
Motor Vehicles (International Race) Bill,
and the Constabulary Bill."-(Viscount
Charlemont.)
Royal Assent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I am authorised by the
Prime Minister, on his behalf, to inform
the Senate that His Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland, in the name of and
on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the
following Bills:-
Representation of the People,
Railways,
Agricultural Marketing.
Administration of Summary Jurisdiction.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
it is proposed to consult representatives of
the magistracy-other than Belfast and
resident magistrates-before any Bill
making any material alteration in the
administration of summary jurisdiction is
introduced in Parliament.
Mr. LESLIE: The object of the
question on the Paper is not, of course,
to belittle any advice which the two
classes of magistrates to whom, for the
sake of clarity it was necessary to refer,
might give the Minister of Home Affairs,
but the object is to draw attention to the
fact that there are other classes of magistrates. I am thinking particularly of
magistrates who operate in the country
districts in which they reside-I mean
literally reside, not as resident magistrates
in the usually accepted sense of the term
-and also magistrates who operate in
towns, and particularly of lady magistrates, of whom there are a certain
number on the bench who have been put
there in recent years and who, in certain
classes of cases, I think, are of a very
great value. One would like to be
assured that before any alteration is made
all such people as I have mentioned will
be consulted, and that any observations
they might make will receive full consideration.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I need
hardly assure the hon. and learned
Senator that the Government is at all
times prepared to consider representations
in regard to contemplated legislation
from any source or quarter, and in connection with the proposed amendment of
the Summary Jurisdiction Code, the
Government will be happy to receive any
suggestions which magistrates may desire
to put forward with a view to the
improvement of the existing system of
administration of summary jurisdiction.

which they desire the concurrence of this
House.

Appropriation Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a first time."-(Mr. Long.)
Mr. LONG: This Bill is purely a
finance Measure. It is in its usual form,
and I do not think it is necessary for me
to detain the House with any explanation of the Clauses. I beg to move,
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now Committeed."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third time on Thursday next.
Motor Vehicles (International Race) Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
proposes to give statutory authority for
the repetition in respect of the Tourist
Trophy Race to be held this year of the
arrangements whereby the Royal Automobile Club are empowered to make a
charge of 2s 6d for each car conveying
spectators into the race area.
It will be a matter of great satisfaction
to hon. Members that the race is again to
be held in Northern Ireland and the
House will, I am sure, join with me in
expressing the thanks of the Government
and the people of Northern Ireland to
Lord Rothermere, who has generously
promised to present £1,500 for prize
money. I beg to move, "That the Bill be
now read a Second time.”
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Constabulary Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First Time.”—(Mr. Long.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The object
of this Bill is to continue, until Parliament shall otherwise determine, the
existing arrangement whereby the contribution of the Belfast Corporation
towards the cost of the employment of
members of the Royal Ulster Constabulary on police duties in the City is
assessed at £25,000 annually.
It will be remembered that in 1928 by
the Constabulary Act of that year, the
annual contribution, which had previously amounted to approximately
£40,000 per annum, was fixed at £25,000
per annum for a period of seven years.
That period terminated on the 31st
March, 1933, but the Constabulary Act of
1933 extended the period for a further
year to 31st March, 1934.
Having regard to the fact that a
revaluation of the Province is at present
in preparation, and for other reasons the
Government does not consider that it
would be desirable at the present time to
make any change in the existing arrangement, and it has, therefore, decided to
Constabulary Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
ask Parliament by this Bill to continue
that arrangement until such time as a
permanent settlement of the matter has
been achieved. I beg to move,
"That
this Bill be now read a Second Time."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not rise to oppose
this Bill, but I am anxious to make it
quite clear that I am not to be taken in
any way as being of the opinion that the
sum of £25,000 is in fact a reasonable
contribution from the great city of
Belfast towards the expenses of the Constabulary. I do not think—and I gather
from what the Noble Viscount has said
that the Government does not think
either, that the arrangement accepting
£25,000 should be allowed to continue
indefinitely. I also took note of what
the noble Viscount said as regards the
desirability of waiting, before a relatively
heavy imposition on the rates is
sanctioned, for the results of revaluation.
What the result will be on the city of
Belfast I have no idea. There are those
here who could enlighten the House if
that matter were under discussion, but
as regards revaluation it is not only
Belfast that is involved. There are other
communities in the Six Counties, and
when a final settlement is reached with
the city of Belfast as regards its contribution for the constabulary it may be
that some of the smaller country towns
perhaps may be required also to bear some
share If so, I am inclined to think that
the Province generally will agree that it
is not inequitable that some contribution
should be made primarily from local
sources towards the very heavy cost of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary. Meantime I
think that this proposal is probably the
best that could have been devised to carry
us over probably the next two or three
years. I hope three years will be the outside limit.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved,
That this Bill be now Committeed." (Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third Time on Thursday next.
Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First Time."-(Mr. Long.)
Ordered: That the Bill be read a
Second Time on Thursday next.

Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move," That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday next." On that day I
hope to be in a position to take the
Second Reading of the Motor Vehicles
and Road Traffic Bill.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Could the noble
Viscount indicate to the House what the
business on Thursday will be?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Second
Reading of the Motor Vehicles and Road
Traffic Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not know whether
the House generally will consider that
two days, that is to say to-night and tomorrow, are sufficient time to allow to
elapse between the First and Second
Readings of the Motor Vehicles and Road
Traffic Bill. This is a Bill of some length
and very great complexity. There are
some fifty or more Clauses in it, and I do
not think there is one of them that does
not refer at least to two other enactments.
I am inclined to suggest to the Noble
Viscount that he should allow a little
more time between the First and Second
Readings.
Mr. BARCLAY: I think hon. Members have had this Bill in their hands for
several days, and I cannot see why we
could not proceed with the Second Reading on Thursday.

Appropriation Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read the Third time."
Mr. McMAHON: I would like to take
this opportunity of voicing my exception
to certain action which has been taken
by the Ministry of Home Affairs in
reference to a local authority.
I am
sure the Members of this House must
have read in the local Press this morning
that certain Members of the Belfast Corporation are threatened with a surcharge
a result of their action in trying
to keep work in Belfast in connection with the rebuilding of a
number of tramcars for the Belfast Corporation. I say that this is
a step that should never have been taken
by the Ministry of Home Affairs. After
all, what would encourage these members
as
Appropriation Bill-
Third Reading
7 JUNE 1934
of the Belfast Corporation to run the
risk of surcharge other than a desire to
follow the policy which the Prime Minister himself has advocated on many
occasions, and that is to support Ulster
industries. I do say it is a very
humiliating position for members of the
Belfast Corporation to be placed in, that
they should be called upon to appear
before a civil servant to account for their
action in doing what they believed was
the right thing to do under the circumstances. As I said before, the members
of the Corporation are imbued with one
idea, and that is to help as far as possible
the artisans who unfortunately find themselves unemployed at the present time.
Because of their action in giving this
particular contract to a local firm they
are now held up before the public, as
the Press of this morning indicated, as
persons who have not been doing their
duty as they should have done. I contend it is time the Ministry of Home
'Affairs set up some standard of preference
regarding local contracts. I understand
that at the present time local authorities
are allowed to give a preference to local
firms to the extent of 5 per cent., but
5 per cent. is not enough. I would say
that 20 per cent. is a proper standard
of preference to give to local industries.
I take the strongest possible exception to
the action of the Ministry of Home
Affairs in threatening to surcharge those
members of the Belfast Corporation who
voted in favour of this contract being
given to a local firm, because of the
position in which it has placed them in
the eyes of the public.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM :
After all, it is a great safeguard for
the citizens of Belfast to have the
accounts of the Corporation audited by a
competent person who they know will
act stricty fairly and inpartially.
Mr. LESLIE: On a point of Order.
Are we not discussing a matter which is
sub judice?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
understand that on the Appropriation
Bill a Member is entitled to deal with a
matter such as this. I was going to say
that I was a member of the Belfast Corporation at the time this particular
matter was decided, and there was an
alternative suggestion put forward to the
acceptance of the local tender. That
was that this work should be done by
direct labour, and that would have given
local employment. But the Corporation
decided to give the contract to a certain
local firm whose tender was considerably
higher than the lowest tender. There
was never any likelihood of the work
going out of the city. I think it is a
splendid safeguard for the ratepayers to
have an auditor who is prepared to take
such action as he has taken in this case
if he considers it necessary to do so.
When I was one of the members affected
in another case I had no reason to complain of the auditor's action.
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member is
quite entitled to discuss the question
generally, but I cannot allow him to go
into the details.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: All
I want to say in conclusion is that I am
quite satisfied that it is right and proper
that an auditor should have the power to
ask members of a local authority for an
explanation of their actions which the
auditor thinks fit to question. As I
have said, it is a great safeguard for the
ratepayers of a city like Belfast.
Mr. LONG: Might I point out for
the information of the two hon. Members who have spoken on this subject
that the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) was quite right when he suggested
that this matter was sub judice. The
auditor, a person over whom the Ministry has no control, is acting in an
official capacity, and I think it would be
quite improper to discuss the matter at
the present time.

Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is
hardly necessary for me to dwell upon the
circumstances which justify the Government
in submitting to the House for
approval this Bill, the provisions of which
are designed with the object of reducing
the appalling toll of life and suffering
levied upon our people by road accidents,
when it is considered that in the year
1933, 141 persons were killed and 1,757
injured in such accidents. Hon. Members will, I am sure, readily agree that
the problem is an urgent one, and
demands the immediate attention not
merely of the Government, but the community as a whole, in devising means
whereby the ever increasing dangers of
the highway can be lessened, and in seeking a reasonable measure of safety for
those who use it, whether they be
pedestrians, cyclists or motorists.
It would, I think, be unnecessarily
tedious for me to enter into a detailed
explanation of the numerous Clauses of
the Bill. The Measure is divided into
seven parts. Part I deals with motoring
offences and I should, I think, direct
particular attention to the penalties proposed in respect of reckless and dangerous
driving, and in respect of driving or
being in charge of a motor when under the
influence of drink or drugs. It will be
noted that for such offences the Bill has
re-enacted the existing penalties as laid
down in the Act of 1926, but provides in
addition that the court may impose a
sentence of imprisonment for a period not
exceeding three months in the case of
dangerous driving, and six months for
drunkenness in charge, whether for the
first, second, or third offences. Furthermore, the Bill provides, in Clause 3, that
where a licence has been suspended for
reckless or dangerous driving, or
drunkenness whilst in charge of a motorcar the court shall not have authority to
remove the suspension until a period of
at least twelve months has elapsed, and
at the same time the minimum period for
suspension has been increased from three
to six months in respect of dangerous
driving. One can only conclude that
persons guilty of offences of this nature
are criminally careless of the safety of
other road users, and it, is, therefore, the
opinion of the Government that the
courts should be given adequate powers to
treat proven cases with the utmost
severity.
The
The House will observe that it is proposed to restrict the use of the Probation
of Offenders Act in the case of certain
classes of motor offences, which are
mentioned in the second Schedule.
offences are of the most serious character
and it will at once occur to the House
that the Probation of Offenders Act was
never intended to apply to offences of
these types, nor to the kind of person
who commits them. Clause 5 provides a
means of dealing with offences of a less
serious character under the heading of
careless driving with less severe penalties
and takes away the last vestige of argument in favour of the application of the
Probation of Offenders Act to the offences
of greater gravity.
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
7 JUNE 1934
was
Another important provision contained
in Part I of the Bill is the application.
to motor vehicles in general of the principle of police inspection which
applied, to public service vehicles only,
by the Act of 1926. Under Clause 10
any duly authorised uniformed officer of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary is given
power to stop any motor vehicle and to
satisfy himself as to the mechanical
working of the vehicle and its equipment.
In order to minimise the risk of accidents
caused by the fatigue incurred by lengthy
periods of continuous driving, powers are
sought in Clause 13 to regulate the hours
of duty for drivers of public service and
goods vehicles.
Part II of the Bill deals with the question of Third Party Insurance. Parliament has already recognised the principle
that members of the public are entitled
to some guarantee that in the event of
compensation being awarded by the court
against the driver of a motor vehicle, the
money will be forthcoming to satisfy the
award made. That principle was enacted
in the Act of 1930, but there are loopholes in the existing system which may
cause hardship
hardship in individual
cases.
con-
Insurers may become insolvent; the
insurer may repudiate the policy of
insurance on legal grounds; or the
insured person may have died and thereby relieved the insurer from the
responsibility for the consequences of the
deceased's acts. To provide adequately
and fairly for all these various
tingencies is undoubtedly a matter of
considerable difficulty, but I trust that
when hon. Members study Clauses 14 to
22, which comprise Part II of the Bill,
they will be prepared to agree that the
enactment of these provisions will go a
long way towards perfecting the existing
system, while at the same time preserving
a reasonable and just balance between
the rights of the public, the insurer and
the insured.
In dealing with Part III of the Bill
I should perhaps inform the House that
the provisions contained therein are not
intended to provide a comprehensive
scheme for the regulation of goods traffic.
This is a matter which as part of the
larger question of transport in general
within the province is at present receiving
the earnest consideration of the Government, and with regard to which proposals
will in due course be submitted to you.
Broadly speaking, the Clauses in this part
of the Bill are concerned with road safety
in relation to goods vehicles, which
present their own particular problems in
this matter. Powers are sought with a
view to the maintenance of such vehicles
in proper mechanical condition; the
restriction of loads to the capacity of the
vehicles upon which they are borne; the
prevention of the carriage of unauthorised
passengers on goods vehicles; and the
imposition of suitable speed limits. In
order to meet the administrative cost of
these provisions it is proposed to subject
goods vehicles to payment of an annual
licence fee not exceeding two pounds.
the
Part IV of the Bill lays down certain
requirements for the lighting of vehicles.
The text of the Bill is self-explanatory,
and I do not think I need dwell
upon
matter save to direct attention to the
rather unusual power given to the
Ministry of Home Affairs in sub-clause.
(3) of Clause 29, which enables that
Ministry by regulation to add to or vary
the requirements of this Part of the Bill.
This power corresponds to that accorded
to the Ministry of Transport in Great
Britain, and having regard to the fact
that the matters dealt with are matters
of detail and that such regulations, if
made, must be submitted to Parliament
and subject to the authority of Parliament
as laid down in Clause 52 of the Bill, I
do not think the House need hesitate to
grant the power sought.
Miscellaneous amendments of the Road
Traffic Acts are dealt with in Part V.
The most important Clause is, I think,
Clause 41, which collects, and in some
particulars amends, the various existing
provisions relating to false statements,
forgery and the fraudulent use of
licences and so forth. The advantage to
be gained by dishonest persons in avoiding the statutory requirements of the
Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Acts
would undoubtedly provide a a strong
incentive to fraud in connection with
licences, insurance certificates and other
documents, and it is hoped that the provisions of this Part of the Bill will act as
a deterrent to offences of this nature, and
facilitate their discovery and punishment
when they have been committed.
Motoring offences give rise to special
difficulties in the prosecuting of the
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
offender owing to his mobility. Part VI
of the Bill is concerned with this
problem, and in Clauses 47 to 51 various
alterations in the law of summary jurisdiction are made which are intended to
facilitate and render less expensive the
prosecution of offenders.
I should perhaps, particularly direct the attention of
the House to Clause 51, which gives to
the police the power to arrest without a
warrant persons resident outside the
United Kingdom who are to be charged
with one
or other of the more serious
offences.
I have now given you a very brief summary of the principal objects of the Bill,
and I shall be very pleased to give further
information on matters of detail during
later stages if hon. Members will be good
enough to give me notice of any particular point which they wish to have
elaborated.
In conclusion, I should like to impress
upon the community the
that
fact
although Parliament can no doubt do a
great deal, by passing this Bill, to deter
the wilfully reckless user of the roads and
to minimise the risk of accidents, no real
measure of safety can be assured without
the willing and intelligent co-operation of
all concerned. Safety first" is not
perhaps a policy which is universally
desirable in all our activities, but in these
days of super efficient machines of great
potential danger any user of the roads,
whatever his mode of progression, who
does not constantly and attentively adopt
the policy of safety first is not merely a
lunatic but a criminal lunatic. I beg to
move.
Mr. McALLISTER: I am sure Members in all parts of the House will
approach this Measure from the same
viewpoint, namely, that it is an
endeavour, whether a useful one or not,
to prevent the tremendous litany of
accidents occurring in Northern Ireland
and to safeguard human life. I do not
think I can be considered hostile to the
motoring community as a whole. I have
been driving since I drove one of the first
three cars introduced into my own
district, and I cannot help contrasting
the courtesy of the road as one experiences
it to-day with what it was in the early
days. There is a sort of speed fever at
the present time in Northern Ireland.
One of the reasons why the usages,
customs and courtesies of the road were
so much better observed in the early days
was because of the fact that the motorists
of those days had been transferred from
horse-drawn vehicles to motors. They
had learned with horses the courtesies and
the legal liabilities, whereas at the
present time you have a young man or
girl of 17, 18, or 19 years who walks in
and gets a licence. They have never had
experience of the usages of the road and
they are placed in a position to drive a
car at 60 or 70 miles an hour without any
road sense at all.
be
I think want of road sense is one of the
causes of so many accidents in Northern
Ireland. If one might refer to one's own
experiences I scarcely ever drive
drive to
Belfast that I have not to stop or slow
down to 5 or 10 miles an hour on my own
side of the road to allow some on-coming
vehicle to pass another on-coming vehicle.
Another cause of difficulty on the road
at the present time which should
addressed by the Government is this. We
have these lorries driving at 25 or 30
miles an hour, and I have seen four perhaps of them following one another with
a space of perhaps 30 or 40 yards between
them. Unless one takes a risk which no
experienced motorist will take there is no
opportunity to get past these vehicles at
any reasonable speed limit. I think a
large number of motorists, certainly no
infinitestimal number but I believe a
substantial majority, seem to have the
idea that the roads are made for them
or that they have a monopoly of the road
or at all events that they have a prior
right or first call upon them. That class
of motorist should have his mind disabused of that idea at
once, because
pedestrians have a far greater claim or at
least they have a prior right to the road
compared with the motorist. These
selfish and perfectly irresponsible
motorists, I think, could not be too
heavily dealt with. That is my own
opinion.
After all a motor-car is not a toy.
It is capable of doing great injury. Those
of us who engage in my own profession
have seen some of the greatest possible
hardships through reckless driving where
the father of a family has been taken
away and a large number of children left
behind without that father to support
them.
7 JUNE 1934
Road Traffic Bill-

Second Reading.
vidual official. Before the Bill reaches
a further stage we shall have an opportunity of studying it more closely, but
I am sure every Member of the House
I will direct his attention to the Measure
with the object of making it as perfect
as possible with a view to safeguarding
the lives of the people of Northern Ireland.
There is another matter I would like to
draw the attention of the Government
and this House to, and that is I think the
Government should scrutinise the form
of policy issued by all insurance companies at the present time in Northern
Ireland because in my opinion there are
far too many loopholes whereby insurance
companies can by reason of technical
defence as to some slight mis-statement
in the application escape liability.
Insurance companies are at the present
time taking advantage of these technical
defences, after receiving the premium, to
repudiate liability and that inflicts the
greatest possible hardship on injured persons. I do approve of the Clause whereby
it is now possible to take proceedings
against the personal representatives of
the man who was killed in a motor
accident for which he himself was responsible. I think it is a very necessary
Clause and a very good Clause to introduce into the Bill. I am sorry to see that
the Government are still proceeding on
this very unmeritorious principle of the
minimum fine. I do say that is bad
legislation. I know of no lawyer whom
I have ever heard commending it. After
all, if you have a tribunal administering
this Act and trying a case you should
give them a free and unfettered hand to
deal with it, because to fix this minimum
fine is more or less to pre-judge the case
and to make every case, no matter what
the circumstances are, subject to a
similar fine.
With regard to the making of regulations, very much more weighty
opinions than mine have denounced the
system of administration by regulations.
If you take the motoring code at the
present time, the regulations made under
the various Acts are more comprehensive
and more voluminous than the Acts
themselves. When I have been under
the necessity of reading through these
regulations I have been astounded at the
many matters that can be made lawful
without the sanction and authority of
Parliament. I think it is an exceedingly
bad system, and I for one will always
oppose this shirking of its liability by
Parliament and the handing over of this
responsibility to officials. Surely the
collective judgment and experience of
Members of Parliament is more likely to
be right than is the opinion of one indi-
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to welcome this
Bill, and to join in the expression of
hope already voiced by the noble
Viscount that the Bill may have some
effect in inculcating what is commonly
called road sense- -I think Senator
McAllister used that same expression
-inculcating a little more road sense
amongst the users of the roads in Northern Ireland. I should also like to congratulate the Ministry on having, in the
preparation of the Bill, avoided one or
two rather obvious pitfalls which I
think were put before them as a very
specious temptation. I am glad the
Ministry has not fallen a victim in that
direction.
I find myself on the whole in very
cordial agreement with the principle of
the Bill, but not by any means in agreement with sundry of the details. I
agree, of course, that the present
position is almost intolerable. I think
the noble Viscount spoke of 141 deaths
in Northern Ireland last year, and 1,750
minor casualties. It is obvious that if
Parliament can do anything towards
stopping a continuance of that state of
affairs Parliament ought, at all events,
to try.
We should be very careful to
avoid anything which can de described as
panic legislation, and I think we should
give most careful consideration to almost
every one of the fifty or so Clauses which
occur in the Bill. There are fifty-five,
and I think hon. Members should be encouraged to express their individual views
on almost all the questions that arise.
That means, in other words, that I do
not think the Committee Stage of this
Bill should be in any way expedited, but
that a relatively considerable amount of
time should be devoted to it. I do not
think we should endeavour to get through
the Bill at the same pace as perhaps
other assemblies may do. I think that
rather more than one afternoon may not
unreasonably be regarded as required.
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
Taking the Bill as a whole. I am
afraid I must say that there is a little
too much Ministry of Home Affairs about
it. I should attack that perhaps rather
vigorously were it not that I am wholly
unable to suggest any reasonable and possible alternative, and also I think the
House will agree with me that the efforts
of the Ministry in connection with motor
traffic in the past have not been wholly
unsuccessful, and we may therefore hope
that some of the new powers to be given
will also be used carefully, and ultimately
to the public advantage. There is one
thing that stands out very plainly from
this Bill, and that is that the time has
come when, difficult as it may be when
you are dealing with legislation which is
not intended to last very long, we should
press for consolidation of our Motor
Traffic Acts. We have at the present
moment three Acts, and this Bill will make
a fourth-the Acts of 1926, 1929, 1930,
and 1934. As soon as may be we ought
to consolidate those into one Act.
know they are not very much better off
across the water. There are two Acts
there, the Acts of 1930 and 1933, which
have been made use of in this Bill, and
I believe there is another on the stocks
at the present moment. As I said, I
think the time has arrived when we
should consolidate our Motor Traffic Acts.
I
Passing to the Bill itself, one naturally
begins. as the noble Viscount began, with
a reference to the specially punitive
Clauses, Clauses 1 and 2, dealing
generally with offences of reckless driving
and offences of being drunk in charge.
Both those Clauses are completely, as it
seems to me, once more in the melting
pot. Both these matters have received
attention in every one of the three Acts
of our own Parliament to which I have
referred, and we are now about to
examine them again. As regards the
first of these Clauses, I should like to
congratulate the Ministry once more on
having got rid of that silly word
"wanton" which was the cause, it may
be remembered, of a division in this
House when it last came before us. But
I am afraid I cannot whole-heartedly
carry on my congratulations as regards
the resurrection of that phrase relating
to the amount of traffic "which might
reasonably be expected on the highway."
That form of words appeared as long
and
we
ago as the Act of 1903,
got rid of it, I think, as long ago as
1926, and I am rather sorry it makes
its appearance again. It has been
objected to, I know, a good deal outside. and I should be disposed to object
to it myself were it not that I find it
has been introduced in an English
Statute, and also, I believe, in one of
the Free State Measures. Therefore, as
I cannot very well ask the House in Committee to get rid of it, I propose to try
and make use of one of the words it contains in dealing with another Clause
later on which has given rise to some
discussion.
I have no particular faith in the administration of motor traffic law by
resident magistrates, not that I object to
resident magistrates at all, but I do
object to the principle of one man sitting
alone dealing with offences such as those
referred to in these two Clauses, which
are abstract but not concrete offences,
and are very difficult, and decisions on
which do surely call for the opinion of
more than one man. Particularly is that
the case when there is a question of imprisonment involved. I have no great
faith in imprisonment as a cure for reckless driving, and I have no faith whatever in the use of imprisonment at the
will of one man, especially when the only
appeal from that one man is to another one
man. Nor have I any sympathy whatever
with the absurdity of the minimum fine.
I have said that again and again, and I
am very sorry to see it is proposed to
continue that in regard to the Clause I
am speaking of, and to re-introduce it in
connection with another offence referred
to later in the Bill. I do not know that
I shall direct the attention of the Committee to that particular question myself
again, but if anybody else does I shall
be happy to support their objection.
I am prepared to say now one thing about
the minimum fine which, so far as I
remember, I have not said before, and
that is that the form of words used in
these Acts of Parliament, to my mind,
are really more an attempt to bluff unfortunate resident magistrates than they
are to effect their purpose. If you found
a resident magistrate strong-minded
enough to impose a penalty less than the
prescribed fine, and the matter was taken
to the High Court, I think you would
find that the High Court would say that
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
7 JUNE 1934
the expression in the Act was directory.
and not mandatory, and therefore the
magistrates are perfectly entitled to impose a less penalty than that prescribed.
So far as I know the point has never
been decided, although it has been discussed in more than one circle.
I have spoken as having no particular
faith in imprisonment, but I want to
make it clear that I am referring to
imprisonment imposed by a court of summary jurisdiction. I have myself more
faith in summary jurisdiction matters in
this connection in the more free use, and
perhaps a sterner use, of the power of
suspension of licences. I think that is
more likely to do good than imprisonment being imposed by a court of summary jurisdiction, subject, of course, to
appeal. There are three coures at least
open. One is to cut the suggestion out
of the Bill altogether, another is to leave
matters as they are, and the third will be
to follow the later English Acts and make
the maximum period of imprisonment
four months in each case; that is under
Clauses 1 and 2, subject, of course, to
appeal. There is something to be said
on behalf of each of those alternatives,
and I do not know which is best. On
the whole, I myself would prefer that
there should be no imprisonment by order
of a magistrate, but instead of that I
would rather make far freer use of the
new power taken in this Bill to send
these cases forward for trial on indictment.
I am
I
Some Members may remember that we
have discussed matters of that kind on
an earlier Bill, and I rather suggested
then that that was the proper course to
take; either to do it in that way or make
the offence somewhat similar to the
offence under the Children Act.
very glad we are now taking power to
put forward an indictment in matters of
this kind, but I do not think we have
gone quite as far as I could wish.
think we ought to take this opportunity
of getting rid of that illogical position
as regards manslaughter. That was the
possibility of indicting a man for manslaughter, and allowing the jury to return
a verdict of guilty of reckless driving.
It did meet with certain small success
when it was brought out, but I think
that success faded away. I am not sure
from recent cases that it has not come
back into its own again, but we should
now follow the decision of the English
Court of Appeal in the case of Rex v.
Stringer, which Members will find reported in "The Times" newspaper of
12th January, 1933. We should follow
in these cases the course recommended by
that most distinguished court, and I will
suggest that at the
time.
proper
There is another suggestion which
appears really for the first time almost
in this first Clause, and that it what I
might describe as a more merciful suggestion as to allowing some kind of
moratorium in the case of second and
third offences. I think, on the whole,
public opinion will approve of that proposal, that after the expiration of
sufficient time the earlier offences should
seem to be wiped out. I am not sure
that the wording of the sub-clause may
not receive attention later on, but I
think it will not receive any violent criticism. There remains the question in the
second Clause of allowing a police constable to apprehend without a warrant.
I confess I cannot see any definite reason
why in that case we should not require
that the police constable should be in
uniform. It seems to me that if a man,
who may be a policeman in plain clothes,
tries to arrest a drunken motorist and if
he is not in uniform he is simply inviting
a breach of the peace. If he wears
uniform or presents a badge I think it
will make for peace and security. Before
leaving this Clause, on a small matter of
personal preference, I am going to ask
the Committee to divide sub-clause (2)
of the second Clause in order that the
resulting sections shall be complete in
themselves.
The remaining Clauses of the Bill are
full of interest. I do not want to take
up more time than I can help, but I
think it might fall in with the suggestion
of the noble Viscount if one mentioned
very briefly the Clauses to which one
desired to direct attention. There is a
Clause applying to people in charge of
vehicles drawn by horses being under the
influence of drink, and so on. I wish to
ask the House in Committee to give to
such persons the same protection from
double prosecution as they give to
motorists. There is the question of the
pedal cycle. That, I think, will require
a certain amount of attention. I am not
at all certain that the definition of a
pedal cycle has not been made strong
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
enough to include a child's toy. I do
not think that can be the intention.
There is a further matter as regards the
pedal cycle, that is, the prohibition of
carrying more than one person in addition
to the rider. I do not quite know why
that Clause is suggested to us. If it is to
stop the practice of putting a baby in
a basket in front of the handlebars I
should be entirely in favour of it. If
it is to prevent a small boy of ten or
so riding behind with one foot on the
footrest and his two hands on his big
brother's shoulders I do not think Parliament should interfere. Young people have
to learn by steps. You cannot ride a
bicycle properly unless you do certain
tricks of that kind. I think it may be
worth while to examine that Clause carefully.
Then we come to the proposal as to
causing an obstruction by allowing a
vehicle to stand or remain on the highway. Most of us have experienced, when
motoring through the smaller towns,
instances of the road being very badly
blocked by omnibuses left a little way
out from the kerb on one side and the
other. That happened to me this morning. That state of things existed on my
left and a similar state of things on my
right. What was left was limited to a
little bit of the middle of the road.
Under this new Clause there could be
about four summonses in that case. I
think we must get something stronger
than that rule, which requires you to get
some definite person who was obstructed.
I think this Clause might be a little bit
plainer, and I will make a suggestion
The Clause relating to reporting
accidents is, I think, very much improved now compared with what it was
when it made its first appearance in
another place.
I
Then we come to the question of an
authorised officer wearing uniform
stopping and examining a motor-car.
That bears some resemblance, as I think
the noble Viscount reminded us, to what
was applied to public service vehicles,
but it goes a great deal further.
think we shall have to alter that Clause
a little more than it has already been
altered. One thing suggested is that if
you limit it to the case of public service
vehicles you would be doing all that was
required. Another safeguard you must
attach is you must give the driver of
the vehicle the right to object to carry
out the instructions of the officer, or
you must relieve him of liability for the
consequences of an accident if he has
carried out the instructions and comes to
grief. One can well understand that a
man driving a motor lorry may be told
He
to drive at twenty miles an hour.
may say, "No. I know my lorry is not
good enough for 20 miles an hour. I
will go at 12." If he goes at 20 and an
accident occurs the responsibility should
not be left on him.
There is the Clause making sure that
applicants subject to disabilities should
not get licences. There are too many
people blind or deaf or otherwise incompetent driving motor-cars. It appears
the declaration they have to sign when
applying for licences is not sufficient to
secure reasonable physical competence to
drive. I cannot agree with the proposal made in Clause 11 of the Bill.
The proposal is that a police officer or
constable may serve notice requiring a
man to submit himself for examination
by a specified doctor. The House will
remember that the expression used is
licence holder. The licence holder may
very well be a woman. In any case, I
do not think any intelligent Parliament
in the 20th century will tolerate that a
person, if a policeman thinks fit, must go
and submit himself for examination by a
doctor. If that power is to be used it
should be by order of the court. The
licence holder should be before a court.
and the court in nine cases out of ten
can tell by looking at the man whether
he is fit or not. Then if the court says
so, he should be required to submit himself for examination. It is, I submit, a
matter for the court and not for the
police. I may say that is one of the
matters I am disposed to press very
earnestly on the attention of the Committee.
I cannot pass Clause 13 without an
expression of high approval of the limitation of driving hours. We all know
that very often there are cases of lorry
drivers being shamefully overworked and
the result has not been at all to the
advantage of the public. As a matter
of fact the limitations proposed do not
appear to be as extensive as they are in
England. I do not think they are in
any way unreasonable. I think we shall
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
7 JUNE 1934
find in practice the effect of that Clause
will be very much to advantage of the
world in general.
I have not gone through Part II, relating to third-party risks, with any
definite care. I do hope to have an
opportunity of examining a little more.
carefully. The particular point to which
I hope the Committee will direct
attention is that referred to by Senator
McAllister, that is, to prevent any hanky
panky in the wording of the policy. If
Parliament can do anything to see that
the insurance companies give fair value
for the premiums they receive, Parliament will be doing a good day's work.
After all, a policy-holder may have made
some mistake which would not be material,
and policy issuers who do not give satisfaction ought to be black-listed by the
Ministry and the policies they issue ought
not to be accepted as being policies
within the meaning of the appropriate
Acts. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
I do not think I need detain the House
with any further observations on part 2.
Part III relates to goods vehicles.
Again, generally speaking, I find myself
in almost complete agreement. There
are one or two matters that need attention. One of these I should like to
mention and that is sub-clause (3) of
Clause 24. I cannot understand the subclause myself. I am inclined to think it
perhaps does not give expression to what
is its intention. The suggestion to my
mind is that a condition can be inserted
in a goods vehicle licence which will not
be presented to Parliament as a regulation made under the Act. I am sure
that cannot be what it means, but attention should be given to the matter and
the whole thing cleared up.
We get now to the lighting of road
vehicles in Part IV. There is very little
there I think appropriate for discussion
on Second Reading. I am not satisfied
that the proposals of Clauses 31 and 32
are altogether desirable. I think it is a
little rash for the Ministry to rush in in
matters of that kind. I am almost
inclined to think that the Bill would not
lose very much if those two Clauses or
the greater part of them were taken out.
There is another matter which arises in
Clause 38, the penalty Clause in respect
of lights. There ought nowadays to be
some protection for the electric tail light.
Once upon a time it was the rule in
England if a tail light was not in action
and the man did not know it was not in
action he should not be held guilty of an
offence. We have departed from that,
but I think something might be done
there to protect the bona-fide victim of
an accident from being proceeded against
unnecessarily.
Part V contains one sub-clause which I
do not like at all. It is sub-clause (3)
of Clause 41 which relates to persons forging or fraudulently altering this or that
with intent to defraud. The suggestion
there is that the man is punishable on
summary conviction. I do not think we
ought to stand for that. To my mind
that is not a matter for summary jurisdiction at all. It seems to me purely a
matter which ought to go before a judge
and jury. I am not certain that one
should press that to such an extent as to
cut out the reference to summary jurisdiction, but we ought to add to it power
to send the man forward for trial before
a judge and jury.
the
I should like to welcome Clause 44, the
Clause allowing further payments to be
made to hospitals. We are putting it
now at £50 and that, speaking from
memory, is the same safeguard as that
given in the most recent English Acts.
I am not certain whether the Bill now
before the Parliament of the United
Kingdom does or does not make any
alteration. I think everyone will regard
£50 as a distinct advance on £25 and
perhaps the people of Northern Ireland
will feel indebted to one of the hon.
Members who represent the University
in another place for insisting on
Government introducing this Amendment. The Amendment was due to him;
it was not in the Bill as originally produced. There is a minor matter in Part
VI which I shall not mention to-day.
Now we come to Clause 50.
It prohibits the application of the Probation of
Offenders Act, 1907, in certain cases.
The noble Viscount mentioned that
specially and reminded the House that
whatever cases the Probation of Offenders
Act was intended for it certainly was not
intended for cases relating to motor-cars.
I am in entire agreement with the noble
Viscount about that, but at the same
time I am rather inclined to think that,
inapplicable as the provisions are to
motor cases, Parliament ought not to
enact the Clause before us. The position
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
is really a little ludicrous. We entrusted
this jurisdiction entirely
entirely to resident
magistrates. I do not say that it was a
unanimous decision. We have also
imposed and again our decision was not
unanimous a minimum fine. Some of
us who objected to the minimum fine
pointed out not once but twice that an
attempt to impose a minimum fine would
undoubtedly lead to the application of
the Probation of Offenders Act. I do
not know how often I urged that in this
House, but I have done so, and you have
had resident magistrates who did not
want to impose even the minimum fine
taking refuge in the application of the
First Offenders Act, which admittedly
was never intended for this particular
purpose.
But the absurdity of the matter comes
in here. We can only restrain the
application of these Acts in cases of
summary jurisdiction-it is merciful that
it is beyond our power to dictate to the
High Court-so that comparatively minor
cases cannot under this Bill have the
benefit of the First Offenders Act, but
major offences heard before a judge can,
because we cannot prevent it. Perhaps
we should be wise if we left this Clause
out, leaving the matter to the effect that
Parliament has expressed a
a general
opinion that this Act was not intended
for this purpose, but Parliament does not
wish to make itself ridiculous by prohibiting in small cases what it did not
touch in the large.
I am sure the House will be rather glad
that there are only three or four more
Clauses, and none of them dealing with
matters which arouse particular interest.
I have occupied a certain amount of time,
and I have endeavoured to go a little bit
into detail to give some indication of the
matters I propose to raise at a later stage.
I will endeavour to put in my amendments as soon as may be, but I cannot
undertake to do so to-day. I understand
it is proposed to take the Committee
stage next week, not before Wednesday,
and it may be that we shall find that
gives us sufficient time. I think the Bill
will be found of use, and I hope it may
materially assist in lightening what is
commonly called the toll of the road, but
what I prefer to describe as the unnecessary series of preventible accidents, which
are due, in so far as they are caused by
motorists, chiefly to the minority and
not the general body of motor drivers,
who are, as a rule, very careful. Senator
McAllister spoke of drivers on the main
Londonderry-Belfast Road. I myself
drive on that road very frequently, and
the amount of reckless driving is far
greater than it ought to be. The usual
offence is exactly what was referred tocutting in and passing at bends. I hope
that the effect of this Bill may be to stop
that, and perhaps reduce accidents.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I do not
rise to discuss this Bill in detail now,
because in my opinion the time to do that
is in Committee. If there is anything
wrong with this Measure it is the fact
that it is overdue. The state of the roads
to-day is such as to call for legislation to
enable people to drive and walk with
some safety. There is one thing in connection with the Bill in which I am
disappointed, and that is that everybody
who is on wheels of any description seems
to have been marked out for attention,
whereas the pedestrian can behave as he
likes and make himself generally
objectionable on the road and be the
cause of accidents without anything being
done to prevent him. I think
experience of all motorists is that there
are good and bad motorists, and also good
and bad pedestrians.
the
does
I am sure there is scarcely a Member
of this House who drives a car who has
not at some time or other had experience
of that type of pedestrian who, if you
do not blow your horn, makes faces at
you,
and if you do blow it refuses to give
way. That type of pedestrian
exist although he may not be in the
majority, and he is a public danger.
Many serious accidents can be caused by
bad pedestrians, and if it is possible to
do so I should like to see that class of
meet
pedestrian dealt with. We can
hooliganism on foot as well as on wheels,
and I am sure the Government has no
sympathy with that type of pedestrian.
I should like to give the House one
example of what I am referring to.
Driving home last week I had to pull out
to pass a standing donkey and cart.
There was another car coming on, and
the road was wide enough for three buses,
but the man in charge of the cart, instead
of moving to the head of his donkey,
stepped out six or eight feet into the
middle of the road. But for the skill of
Road Traffic Bill-
Second Reading
7 JUNE 1934
the driver of the approaching car and
my own experience as a driver the cart
would have been struck, or I should have
run the man down. If I had I would
have been held up as a reckless driver,
whereas the man in charge of the donkey
and cart was at fault, and deliberately at
fault. Motorists should extend every
courtesy to pedestrians, but on the other
hand every pedestrian has a duty towards
the other users of the road.
There is one other matter I should like
to refer to. I hope the hon. and learned
Senator behind me (Mr. Leslie) will not,
with his usual skill, press for Amendany
ment of Clause 6. That little brother
who stands on the back of a bicycle with
his hands on the shoulders of his bigger
brother may be indulging in a very
estimable form of amusement, but it
should not be regarded as necessary in
learning certain tricks of the road. I
respectfully submit that the highway is
no place for trick cyclists, and I think it
is highly desirable that cycles which are
constructed for carrying one person only
should be restricted to that use.
times I have had my heart in my mouth
-to use a popular expression-through
things of this kind make me feel that it
is absolutely essential that this Clause
should stand as it is.
The
You have the chivalrous cyclist who
carries his lady friend on a thin bar in
front of him, and when they hear you
coming behind he or she wobbles, and on
the way he or she wobbles depends the
direction in which the cycles swerves.
Then there is the cyclist who has another
sitting on the handle bars, and a person
standing on the back wheel with his
hands on the rider's shoulders, and if
either passenger moves the balance of the
bicycle is affected. I have had so many
narrow escapes myself from this cause
that I heartily welcome this Clause. I
do not want to labour the matter, but I
would like to see the pedestrian doing his
share to prevent accidents as well as every
other user of the road. The Bill is long
overdue, but I hope it will be passed,
with such Amendment as this House may
think necessary.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I welcome this
Bill, although I agree with the Mayor of
Derry that it is long overdue. I would
like to support Senator Leslie in his
appeal for the codification of the laws
relating to motor traffic so that the
general public will be enabled more easily
to read and understand them. The hon.
and learned Senator has given such a
clear and meticulous review of the provisions of the Measure that it is not now
necessary for me to say much that I had
intended to say on the subject. I should,
however, like to refer to the Clause
raising to £50 the maximum amount that
may be paid to hospitals to indemnify
them for their loss in dealing with motor
accident cases. Quite a number of
minor motor cases come into hospital for
a few days and then leave when they are
found to be trivial cases, but however
generous £50 may seem in the matter of
hospital finance it by no means covers the
of the larger and
accident cases that come our way. Not
very long ago we had in one of our local
hospitals a case which cost us, at a minimum computation, £90, and whether the
maximum that a hospital may now
cost
more severe
.
receive from an insurance company is
sufficient to cover their costs I have very
grave doubts.
It has now become common knowledge
that hospitals which are supported by
voluntary contributions are to a very
large extent exploited by these accident
cases. They come to these hospitals,
whether they are small hospitals in the
country or large hospitals in the towns
and cities and cases of this kind cannot
be denied admission. You would be
surprised at the number of beds that are
occupied for a very considerable number
of days or weeks and perhaps even
months by cases which ought not to be
there at all. When you consider how
hospitals are supported by the contributions of the working classes, who have
maintained them during the period of
depression and unemployment, the fact
that these beds should be occupied to such
an extent by motor accident cases is not
altogether fair to them. The waiting
lists of hospitals is really incredible, and
they are people who really have a prior
claim on the beds. Yet their cases cannot be dealt with because of the preoccupation of the beds by cases which,
as I said, ought not to be there in such
numbers. In certain cases of severe
injury involving a cost of £90 or £100
it seems a pity that voluntary hospitals
should not be more adequately compensated.
Second Reading
Road Traffic Bill-

THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
In my opinion tests for fitness should
be carried out in the case of persons
applying for driving licences. In other
countries tests are applied not necessarily
very elaborate tests but sufficient to prevent people who are deaf or blind or
otherwise unfitted being in charge of
motor vehicles. Take for example the
deplorable accident that occurred in front
of Buckingham Palace not very long ago
where an epileptic ran into a crowd that
happened to be watching the changing of
the guard, with the result that five deaths
ensued and he lost his own life as well.
Some safeguard should be taken before a
licence is granted to a first applicant.
Some elementary examination at least in
regard to physical fitness should certainly
be insisted on. Certain forms of eye
trouble may exist, for example, inability
to see at night. A person so affected
might be a driver during the day, but a
secret defect of that kind is not easily
detected. There is also colour blindness
in various degrees and certain other
nervous cases. It is a pity that at the
proper time some enactments to secure
this object are not enforced. We have
in the United States and certain
European countries a physical examination to ascertain on a primary application.
that the people are worthy to be licensed.
I welcome the strengthening of the
punitive Clauses. But it is not so much the
strengthening of the punitive Clauses as
a much more vigorous and impartial
application of them that is needed. There
is no doubt that leniency of a discreditable sort has prevailed for a number of
years. One would like to see the penal
Clauses not inoperative but enforced or
applied with some stringency. I am rather
inclined to agree with Senator McAllister
and Senator Leslie with regard to the
inutility of the minimum fine. That is a
detail which can be discussed in Committee. I think I shall not weary the
Senate with further observations since
Senator Leslie has covered the ground so
fully and in Committee the various
details may be discussed.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I certainly welcome the introduction of this Bill, but
there are one or two matters that the
public would like to have an assurance
upon. In Clause 10 any duly authorised
officer can stop a car on the production
of his authority. Of course it is quite
obvious if a motorist is stopped he
generally does not think of asking the
officer for his authority. It is particularly the anxiety of the public at the
present time that every uniformed officer
should not have power to stop a motor
vehicle on the road unless he is especially
authorised to do so. I am sure Viscount
Charlemont will understand what I am
driving at, that the ordinary policeman
on the street should not have power. If
this power is given officers they should be
specially authorised for the purpose and
qualified in such a manner that they will
understand the mechanism of the motorcar and understand if anything is wrong.
Again there is a very important Clause
here, and that is Clause 8, which refers to
motor trailers. I think anyone with
experience of motor traffic will agree with
me that these trailers at the present time
are a menace on the roads.
I do say
that a trailer that goes at 30 miles an
hour is travelling far beyond the speed it
is entitled to go at. As a motorist I
would say that trailers driven at 30 miles
an hour are an absolute danger. I
certainly never would venture to pass a
trailer going at that rapid rate. I would
respectfully suggest that as far as trailers
are concerned the speed should be half
that-15 miles an hour. Let anyone consider just for a moment a trailer going
round a corner at 30 miles an hour. We
must confess that is a danger and a very
serious danger.
The Mayor of Derry pointed out that
pedestrians have a duty in regard to the
rules of the road. Pedestrians should
look before they rush, and if any of us
pay attention to the traffic control lights
we will find that where the motorist is
obliged under penalty to pay attention to
the traffic control lights the pedestrian
just walks across the road and never
bothers about them at all. You will see when
the green light goes up motorists start to
go across the road but the pedestrian takes
no notice at all. Not alone are the
motorists held up by the traffic signals
but you have got them held up by
pedestrians. I submit that the pedestrians
should be amenable to traffic control
regulations as well as the motorists. I
congratulate the Minister in bringing in
this Bill and I respectfully point out
these various clauses, particularly that
where policemen are concerned. The
general public are perturbed by it and I
Adjournment-
Business of the House
7 JUNE 1934
suggest that the duly authorised officer
should be a specially authorised police
officer.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
to hear that the principle of the Bill at
any rate has been received so well. Most
of the points raised, if only for my own
sake, had better be raised on the Committee stage, and I shall have a better
opportunity of answering them in detail.
There are one or two small points I may
go into.
One was raised by the last
speaker about policemen stopping motorcars. They have to be in uniform
in any case, but only a specially
authorised policeman, one trained in
the mechanism, will be competent
to examine a car. With regard
to the point raised at the beginning by
Senator McAllister about some insurance
companies evading their responsibilities,
of course that can only apply to the less
reputable ones, and I think if he
examines Clause 19 of the Bill he will see
that that matter has been dealt with.
I think it is very advisable, as there are
a great number of Clauses in the Bill,
that as much time as possible should be
given to the Committee stage, which will
really be the important part, and perhaps
therefore we may take the beginning of
the Committee stage next Wednesday
afternoon, and should this House desire
it I think there will be no objection to
adjourning the Committee stage and
finishing it on the following day.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Wednesday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
Will the noble
Mr. MCALLISTER:
Viscount indicate the business?
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Perhaps
I might give the programme which I
hope to be able to adhere to during the
next two weeks. As Senators who have
been Members of this House for some
years know perfectly well there is
generally at this time of the year a kind
of rush of legislation to the head. It
cannot be avoided, but in order that as
much time as possible may be given I
would like to have the Second Reading of
the Unemployment Bill and the Finance
Bill and the Bill dealing with the grading
of potatoes in this Province on Tuesday
next. On Wednesday I would like to
have the Committee stage of the Bill we
have just read a Second time or the first
part of it, and the Second Reading of
the Marketing of Milk Bill. On the
following day, Thursday, June 14, the
Unemployment Bill Committee, the
Finance Bill Third Reading, and the
adjourned stage of the Committee on the
Motor Bill, if it still wants another day.
The following Tuesday the Unemployment Bill Third Reading and the Motor
Vehicles Bill Report, the Milk Bill Committee and the Potatoes Bill Committee.
On Wednesday, June 20, the Milk Bill
Report, the Potatoes Bill, and the Third
Reading of the Motor Bill. On Thursday, June 21, the Third Reading of
anything outstanding. If that cannot be
done in the time, Mondays, Fridays,
Saturdays, and even Sundays perhaps,
will be available.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter before Five
o'clock until Tuesday
next.
until Tuesday next."
Unemployment Bill-
