THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 12th June, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the Second
Reading of the Marketing of Potatoes
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Milk and
Milk Products Bill, and the Marketing
of Potatoes Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
Milk and Milk Products Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Ordered, "That the Bill be read a
Second time to-morrow.
Marketing of Potatoes Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Unemployment Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The main purposes of this
Bill are described in its title: to amend
the Unemployment Insurance Acts and
to make further provision for the training and assistance of persons who are
available for work but have no work or
only part-time work; and probably the
most convenient way of giving the Senate
a view of the principal provisions of the
Bill would be to group what I have to
under the three headings-Insurance Amendment, Training,
Unemployment Assistance.
say
and
The first insurance amendment to become operative, and probably the amendment of most interest to insured persons,
is the restoration of cuts in unemployment benefit. Clause 5 raises the rates
of benefit to those which obtained in
1930. They are set out in detail in the
Second Schedule of the Bill, with the
exception of the rate of adult dependants' benefit, which is restored by
Clause 5 from 8s to 9s. Everyone is
agreed that the restoration of these cuts
should be one of the first fruits of improved conditions generally, and I do
not think that I need say anything
further about this matter, important
though it is.
The second change to which I wish to
direct attention is contained in Clause 3
which gives an advantage to the worker
with a good record of employment over
a worker with a poor record. At
present any claimant, even if he has been
continuously employed for many years,
can get no more than 156 days benefit in
his insurance year.
After that he goes
on transitional payments and is subject
to a means test. Under Clause 3 a
claimant with a good record will get not
only the 156 days of benefit, but
additional days at the rate of three days'
benefit for every five contributions paid
in the past five insurance years, less one
day for every five days' benefit drawn
in the same period. Without going into
any details on this point, I may say that
the result of this concession is that the
man with the best possible record, that
is, full contributions for the past five
years and no benefit drawn, would get
52 weeks' benefit without any means
test.
The third important change in unemployment insurance legislation is the
lowering of the age of entering into insurance from 16 years to the schoolleaving age. At present there is a gap
of two years between the date on which
a child may leave school and the date
on which he becomes insured. That gap
will be closed by Clause 1 of the Bill.
As hon. Senators will observe, however,
the Clause is worded in such a way as
not to interfere with the raising of the
school
time if it appears
age at any
advisable and practicable to take that
course.
In order to offer a further inducement to parents to keep their children
at school after the present compulsory
age, contributions will be credited to
Unemployment Bill-
Second Reading
12 JUNE 1934
such children in the manner set out in
the First Schedule. They will have to
spend at least twelve months at school
after the compulsory attendance age in
order to get any of these credited contributions, and the maximum number of
contributions to be credited will be 20.
In the Unemployment Bill at present passing through the Parliament at Westminster
there is a provision for the establishment
of an unemployment insurance committee.
This committee will consider from time
to time the amendments required in the
structure of the scheme, having regard
to the financial condition of the Unemployment Fund and to other circumstances. One member of this committee
is to be appointed after consultation
with my right hon. Friend, the Minister
of Labour for Northern Ireland; so
that I have no doubt the Northern Ireland point of view will be placed
adequately before them. Power is taken
in the present Bill in Clause 18 to adopt
by Order in Council any changes that
be made on the recommendations
may
of this committee. This power is taken
so that we may be able to seep
parallel lines if the changes are made at
time when our Parliament is not
sitting; but the control of the Northern
Ireland Parliament is maintained by
sub-clause 4 of Clause 18, which
provides that any order made under this
Section shall cease to have effect unless
it is approved by each House of Parliament within twelve months.
a
certain other smaller classes such as outworkers and share fishermen. The conditions for obtaining allowances are set
out in Clauses 31 and 33, and, broadly
speaking, amount to this: that the
claimant must be registering for work,
but not obtaining sufficient work and
be in need of an allowance. Appeal
tribunals will be set up, to which
claimants may, in certain circumstances,
bring their cases, if aggrieved by decisions of the board or its officers. The
funds required to meet the cost will be
the responsibility of the State, but as it
is evident that the scheme will relieve
local authorities of considerable expenditure, local authorities will, under Clause
40, make an annual contribution towards
the expenses of the board.
The third purpose of the Bill is the
provisions of instruction and training,
and this is referred to in both the first
and second parts, that is, in connection
with both unemployment benefit and unemployment assistance. In Clause 14 it
is laid down that it is the duty of every
education authority to provide courses
of instruction for juveniles who
on
under 18 and have no work, or only
part-time work, and powers are proposed to require the attendance of
juveniles at such courses. The Ministry
unemployment
There are a number of other changes
in the detail of unemployment procedure which are explained in the White
Paper, and which I need not delay the
House to discuss.
The second main purpose to which I
wish to direct attention is the subject
of Part 2 of the Bill. Clauses 30 and
31 provide for the establishment of an
unemployment assistance board which
will take under its care all persons who
are insured under the Contributory
Pensions Acts, and persons over the age
of 16 who would have been so insured
were it not for the industrial circumstances of their district. This is a very
much wider field than is covered by
unemployment benefit. It includes not
only all the persons insured for unemployment, but persons engaged in
agricultural and domestic service and
are
of Labour and the
assistance board may also provide
training courses for unemployed persons.
The general intention behind
behind these
various provisions is that SO far as
practicable when a person is unemployed
he shall be eligible to receive such education or training as is calculated to fit
him for work whenever an opportunity
for work presents itself. It may be
made a condition of an applicant receiving either benefit or an allowance that
he should take advantage of the courses
that are open to him, and I need hardly
say that I regard these provisions for
the physical and mental improvement of
persons out of work as nearly, if not
quite, as important as any actual payments made to them in such circumstances.
This is the most comprehensive scheme
for dealing with unemployed persons
that has ever been placed before the
Senate of Northern Ireland.
It may
not go so far in some directions, perhaps, as some might wish it to do; but
taken as a whole it is a courageous,
Unemployment Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
practical, and, I may say, generous
Measure, and it is in conformity with
the policy the Government has pursued
with success for the last dozen years, of
working on parallel lines with Great
Britain.
I am pleased to be able to state that
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Labour, is present to-day and he will
be able to reply to any questions
arising out of the debate. I beg to
move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I
understand
from those who have a much better opportunity of looking into this Bill than I
have, that this is a distinct advance in
many instances on the present legislation.
It is a good thing, I think, to see the
restoration of the cuts, and it is a great
advantage to see the extension of the
insurance scheme to young people. Those
are distinct advances on the legislation of
the country at the present time.
But I
do not think it matters so much what is
in any Act of Parliament. It is really
the administration of the Act that is the
main thing. I have no doubt that if this
Bill is administered in a sympathetic way
it is capable of conferring great advantages on the working people of Northern
Ireland. But if it is going to be
rigorously administered it is only going to
create discontent, because, in my judgment, it is always one of the greatest
mistakes in legislation of this kind to
picture the benefits to the people and lead
them to believe that, as a right, they are
entitled to certain benefits, and then for
the people to find out that by some regulation they have been denied them.
think that is a certain cause of discontent,
and rightly so.
I
I would just like to say, as I see the
Minister of Labour present, that there has
been, I think, well founded criticism with
regard to availability for work and those
I have
not genuinely seeking work.
been interviewed very considerably in my
own district by the unemployed, and I
can speak fairly impartially, and I must
that in many cases I think the
Ministry has ruled not at all sympathetically. There is one other instance,
and it is a question of administration and
not legislation, because the Bill enables
the Ministry of Labour to give these
say
people the benefit if they so desire. I
think the ruling with regard to seasonal
employment could not be justified. For
instance, the House, I am sure, would be
surprised to know that in my own town
of Ballymena tailoring has been ruled by
the Ministry to be seasonal employment.
I do not know whether the Ministry of
Labour considers that in those primitive
parts we do not clothe all the round.
year
I think if there is any business or any
work which is not seasonal it is a business
like tailoring. It is really far more
important how the Bill is administered.
With regard to another Clause, requiring five years' residence in the United
Kingdom before a man becomes entitled
to unemployment benefit, I think it is
ridiculous to introduce legislation of that
sort. First of all, suppose a man here
does two, three, or four years' honest good
service and then becomes unemployed, it
is a hardship on him to find himself not
The
entitled to unemployment benefit.
Minister of Labour should give some
assurance to the House and to those who
have been associated with things like
these, that such matters as seasonal employment will receive sympathetic consideration, especially as the economic
position must be very much improved, if
we accept the assurance of the Minister
of Finance, compared with what it was
when these cuts were made in unemployment benefit.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
wish to say that, generally speaking, I am
in entire agreement with the Bill. I
think it is a great improvement and it is
a right and proper thing to do. I wish
to criticise the Bill on only one point,
simply one, and I hope my right hon.
Friend (Mr. Pollock) will be able to meet
me and, I am sure, other Members of this
assembly. The Bill follows the Imperial
Act very closely, except on one point, and
that is on the percentage, or 60-40 basis.
I think the Minister has designed something rather different, that is, the rate of
1ld. I am not quarrelling with that at
the moment. It is just and right and
fair, because 11d will pretty well equal
the 60-40 basis.
It may vary a little.
In fact, if we take this year, you will
find that, perhaps, it would be
advantage.
Mr. POLLOCK: Hear, hear.
an
Unemployment Bill-
Second Reading
12 JUNE 1934
I
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH:
want to give the Minister of Finance full
credit. There will be an advantage to
Belfast Corporation, which I have the
honour to represent. There will possibly
be an advantage of £2,000 of £3,000.
But then when we come on to next year
under the ordinary normal increase in
valuation that balance will likely be
turned to the other side, and the following year it will also be an increase. I do
not think the Minister can deny that. In
addition to that you have the normal
increase in employment. It is only right
to assume that the unemployment figures
will go down, so that taking it for three
years it is a sporting offer. I do not think
the Government will either win or lose.
Perhaps they will gain a little. As far
as that is concerned I am not finding
fault.
What I am finding fault with-if I may
say finding fault is this. You have
departed under the Bill from the 60-40
basis to the rate of 11d. But we all know
that when the new valuation comes into
operation it will be an increase. I do not
know how much. It may be 25 or 50 per
cent. It may be even more. Assuming
it goes up 50 per cent., you will see what
the 1ld will turn into. It is quite
possible, if the valuations go up in the
way some people anticipate, the Government will have nothing to pay and the
rates will provide the entire amount.
That is the position. What I want done
is this, and I hope my right hon. Friend
will agree to meet me. I agree to a rate
of 11d until the new valuation comes into
force, if he gives me the assurance that
they will reconsider the whole position on
the new valuation. I will take his word
for it.
I think my right hon. Friend will also
agree that governments come and governments go-I hope this one will stay for a
very long time, and I hope the right hon.
Gentleman will be at the head of Finance
-but I want it put beyond a shadow of
doubt that when the matter is to be reconsidered it will be reconsidered or considered on the basis which now exists, that
is, the 60-40. Consequently, I have put
down an Amendment that it shall be reconsidered and that the rate, whatever it
may be, if the Government continue the
rate, it will be considered on the basis of
60-40. I hope my hon. Friend will do
that.
Mr. SPEAKER: I do not wish in the
least to limit the full discussion of this
Measure. Of course it is within the
power of this House to reject the Second
Reading, but I think the Amendment
which the Lord Mayor suggests is one
which is definitely beyond the powers of
the Senate to insert in this Bill. It is an
Amendment which deals with the incidence of taxation, and as such comes under
the provisions of section sixteen of the
Government of Ireland Act. Further, if
this provision had been inserted in another
place it would, I understand, have been
subject to the machinery which deals with
financial Measures.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to say a few words to welcome
this Bill. It is another Bill in connection
with the unemployment insurance scheme,
and it is a considerable improvement on
any previous Bill. When we look back to
the time when this scheme was introduced
in 1912, when the bait was dangled before
the people of 9d for 4d, and when we
remember that few only were included in
that scheme, and when we examine this
Bill and see that provision is made for all
classes of workers and even for boys of
14 years of age and that the financial
position has also been considerably
improved, I do think a great advance has
been made. The workers particularly
will be very thankful when this comes to
be put in operation. Just imagine the
great improvement in benefits. A man
with a good record is to get 52 weeks'
benefit. That never was expected under
the old insurance scheme first introduced.
At that time, also, only a few workers
were included, such as engineers and
others like that.
I think the greatest concession of all,
considering the financial stringency, is
the restoration of the cuts in unemployment benefit. No doubt there is a great
improvement in trade and a very large
number of workers have been taken in the
different concerns, but there are still an
unfortunate number not yet absorbed and
who probably will not be for a considerable time. It is very nice to see the cut
in unemployment benefit restored to these
men. I am sure they will appreciate
very much what has been done, especially
considering the financial stringency of the
present time. Training centres also are
provided for them. That is something
E
Unemployment Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
very necessary, because in the city of
Belfast we have young men who left school
at 14 years of age and have not been able
to do a hand's turn of work. A man
came to me the other day and told me his
boy was 19 years of age and had never
done anything since he left school. These
training centres are good institutions to
induce boys to take an interest in some
manual work.
I had an opportunity of going through
the training centre in Belfast, and I
think they get a very good idea of carpentering work and other very useful work
for young men. I believe our Belfast
centre is one of the foremost in the
United Kingdom. If other centres
throughout the United Kingdom were all
formed in a similar manner it would be
as
So far Belfast
very satisfactory.
centre is concerned it is extra good. The
training for boys and girls is splendid.
There are many of those poor creatures
out of the mills who never had much
knowledge of cooking, and it certainly
will be a great advantage to them in after
life to be taught cooking and all kinds
of domestic work. Reference has been
made to administration-that legislation
hardly matters so much and that administration is of the greatest importance. I
can say here, with some little knowledge,
that the administration is conducted in
the most sympathetic way so far as the
insured workers are concerned. In all
cases I think the law is strained in favour
of the worker, to give him fair, honest,
and just treatment. So far as administration is concerned it is in very safe
hands when it is in the hands and care of
the Minister of Labour. I have very
little more to say, only how much I
welcome this further great improvement
in this insurance scheme of ours.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I also heartily
welcome the Bill introduced this afternoon. I regard the Measure as a great
step in social progress. It is, no doubt, far
in advance of anything we have had
before. I think no country outside the
United Kingdom has made better provision for the assistance of their people.
As to Clause I, young people on leaving
school at 14 will, I see, become insurable,
and if these young people have no work,
their parents, if unemployed, are to receive
dependants' benefit for them.
I was
very pleased to see that young people are
to be encouraged to continue at school
after the school-leaving age, and that
those between the minimum age and
eighteen, who have no work, are to
receive courses of instruction and training.
The provision of assistance for our unemployed able-bodied workers, I am glad
to say, is provided for in the Bill. It is
indeed very satisfactory that the cuts
which were made in 1931 are to be
restored on 1st July, and that those who
have a good employment record can
receive now up to 52 weeks' unemployment benefit in a benefit year. I would
very much have liked to see the Measure
include agricultural labourers and the
black-coated profession whose income was
under £500. I have great pleasure in
supporting the Second Reading of the
Bill.

Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of the Bill. I agree with
the previous speakers that it is a Bill
that will be of great benefit to the unemployed as a whole, and especially to those
men who have drawn all the benefit they
can draw and who are to-day without any
benefit. I am very glad to see by the
Bill that for those men who have been
insured there is hope for the future.
may also say I am glad there is provision
made for the young. At the same time
I am afraid this provision may create an

age of fourteen, and now, according to
the Bill, they are going to be dependent
upon the State for a certain time.
will interfere with the independent spirit
young people should begin to manifest at

me.

This provision is rather Socialistic for
I would rather see some other provision made if it were possible, but
perhaps it is not possible, so that they
would have something to do, something to

State
to
for assistance, is reckoned
destroy and sap that independent spirit.

without which no nation can exist.
The
future of those young people is what
troubles me, but I know I may be wrong.
I agree with the Lord Mayor of Belfast
regarding the contributions asked from
the Belfast Corporation and also from the
urban councils. Now, as a working-men's
representative, I speak here to-day on
behalf of the working men. We have
encouraged working men in the past to
buy their own houses. Some of them had
sufficient capital to do so, but others
bought their houses through building
societies or insurance companies, and to
both these bodies these men are paying
very high contributions every year in the
way of capital and interest. They have
become responsible for water and all other
rates. I am speaking of the rural district
in which I live. I know a great many of
the men who have purchased their homes
on those conditions. Their wages have
been reduced, and the burden at the
present time is more than they are able
to bear.
I see by this Bill there is going to be
another 23d added to assist unemployment, with the prospect in the near
future of an education rate of 6d, and
after that of a shilling. I cannot for the
life of me see how any working man with
reduced wages can afford to pay these
extra rates. The fact of the matter is
that he is looking for relief. His only hope
is of getting relief through re-valuation
when it comes into force next year.
would like the working man to be taken
into consideration because he is not able
to pay any more than he is paying to-day.
I have great pleasure in supporting the
Bill, and I raise these two points because
I cannot understand them and would like
some information on them.
I
a
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount in
introducing the Bill spoke of it as
courageous Measure. It is frequently
my privilege to agree with, if not echo,
the observations of the noble Viscount,
but I do not think he ever used the word
""
courageous more appropriately than
he used it this afternoon. The courage
which I am thinking of is the special kind
of moral courage which disregards the
claims of finance. I do want to know
definitely what extra burden this Measure
is going to cast on the resources of
Northern Ireland, and how it is expected
that that burden will be met.
If I have picked up correctly what I
have seen in various publications we may
expect that the additional cost will be
something in the region of £300,000 a
year. It is necessarily to some extent
hypothetical, but I believe it is thought
that £300,000 a year will foot the bill
provided and here again courage is being
displayed the rate of unemployment does
not rise above 21 per cent.
If the
Minister of Labour, whom we are always
so charmed to see in this House not only
when these Measures are being discussed
but at any other time, can assure us that
he has good grounds for believing the
rate of unemployment is not going to rise
above 21 per cent. I am sure we shall
receive the information with the greatest
satisfaction.
But when we look back, and I am
afraid we have not very far to look, we
must admit that 21 per cent. is a conservative estimate for unemployment, and
it would seem to me to follow that if the
rate of unemployment should rise the
£300,000, if that is the correct figure,
will rise also. Looking at the figures
which our other right hon. visitor (Mr.
Pollock) laid before another place one is
almost amazed when one considers how
anything beyond £300,000 is to be
expected to be found. We are very much,
I am afraid, up against the old question,
which we all dislike, "Can we afford to
carry on in these matters of social service
on the same lines as they do across the
water?'
We know that when we started some
years ago we were able to do so. We
were able to pay a relatively handsome
Imperial contribution. The allowance of
a sum of, I think, £13,000 is now made
to balance the Budget and to meet the
Imperial contribution. It seems to me
that we are very nearly in the last ditch
in this matter. I am very much afraid
about it, and that is why I regard the
proposals as courageous. Courage has
always its attractions, and I am bound to
say on general principles the great bulk
of the Measure is singularly attractive.
We have heard about that from some of
the hon. Members on my left. It contains many improvements which we would
all welcome if we were satisfied that we
could afford it.
There are other little matters here and
there throughout the Bill, some of which
I have not been able to detect up to date,
Unemployment Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
but others I will possibly ask questions
about at a later stage. I shall want to
know, for instance, taking an example,
why in one case we impose punishment of
six months' imprisonment whereas the
corresponding Section in England only
goes as far as three months. Little questions like that will arise. Again, why is
a person not to be sixteen until the commencement of his seventeenth birthday
instead of at present on the last day of
his fifteenth year? Those are matters
which are for the future.
I notice throughout the Bill an indication of a desire to get work done for the
Ministry, shall we say, by the employers
and by the school authorities. There is a
horrible suggestion that returns are to be
required. And why an unfortunate
employer should be compelled to report
that this or that young person in his
employment has left it, or why school
authorities should be expected to compile
statements as to what happens to their
former pupils I confess I do not know.
No doubt the Minister will be able to
tell us something about that. I am
bound to say I always like to be quite
fair, even to my friends. So far as I can
see the proposals do not greatly differ
even from those embodied in the English
Bill. All the same, I think we ought to
be told whether there is not some other
way of meeting the question.
I want to say a word or two about what
might be described as the contributions
to which the Lord Mayor has referred.
The proposals in the Bill are for a rate
of 11d in the case of Belfast, 2d in the
other towns, and 1d in the rural districts,
to be levied in each case on the underated
portion of the rateable valuation. But
the Bill does say quite expressly that those
rates are to continue for a period of only
three years.
It seems to me that the
position in the future is left completely
in the air, and it also seems to me as not
unreasonable to suppose that by the time
the third year has expired the revaluation
may be, if not actually in force at all
events very nearly in force, and it might
be that the Government would wish to
extend the same arrangement for perhaps
one year.
But I understand that the Minister of
Finance has stated in another placethough I must confess that I am not sure
whether it was in connection with this
Bill or in connection with another one
of which we shall hear later on-that it
is the intention of the Government to
apply Section 24 of the last Revaluation
Act when the time comes. That
the
Section which states
academically as was said at the timethat:
-
is
rather
It is hereby declared that it is the
intention of this Act that when the
effect of the revaluation is known consideration shall be given as to the
capacity of local authorities to bear the
expenditure cast upon them by the
laws then in force.
I am not sure whether that statement was
made in connection with this Bill or with
the other one but it would seem to me as
applicable to the one as the other, and it
is of rather distinct importance. It cannot be denied, of course, that the local
authorities should make some contribution
to the expenses of public assistance,
because it necessarily follows that they
will be relieved of a very large portion of
the expenditure they are making at
present. Whether the actual figures, so
to speak, balance out or not it would be
idle to express an opinion, but I cannot
help thinking that a rate for such a sum as
11d would scarcely be arrived at without
such consideration as it was possible to
give it. The more ordinary person, perhaps, would have jumped at 1s or 6d, but
when you talk about 11d on the one hand
and 24d on the other it does suggest that
some consideration has been given and an
endeavour made to arrive at what is in
the circumstances the most equitable
figure.
I do not think one could oppose this
portion of the Bill, even if you, Mr.
Speaker, My Lord, would allow us to
discuss it in Committee, but there does
remain the question of the future. In
regard to the future, the Lord Mayor of
Belfast has expressed a preference for a
percentage rate, and he quoted, of course,
the sixty, and forty per cent. applied
across the water. I must say that, as
regards a permanency, I am inclined
myself to favour something other than
what one might describe as a flat rate,
and I do think that the experience we
shall get during the ensuing three years
will be of the greatest value to Parliament
when the time comes for considering what
the future method of assessment is to be.
I do not think myself that we can carry
Unemployment Bill-
Second Reading
12 JUNE 1934
that particular matter any further than it
has already been taken at present, and,
even if we had the power, I do not think
it would be at all wise to reject the proposals with regard to the 11d, 2d, and
ld at present or to interfere with them
during the course of the next three years.
So far as I can form an opinion I do not
think those flat rates will in fact cost the
local authorities any more than they are
paying at present, that of course is, and
must remain for the moment, a matter of
conjecture.
My hon. Friend (Mr. Barclay) on my
left made some remarks which seemed to
me to be of great importance. He was
speaking of the entry of young persons
into insurance and he voiced, as I
thought, some trepidation lest that might
weaken self-reliance in the young. I
must say that statement appeals to me
very much. It was not very long ago that
I heard of inquiries being made of some
school boys. I am afraid it was in a
town not unrepresented in this House.
Inquiry was made of these young people
as to what they wanted to do when they
left school, and the answer given was
"To qualify for the dole." That is a
pretty lamentable exhibition, and I can
only hope that the noble Viscount, as
Minister of Education, wili. if possible,
urge-
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: That would not
be in Belfast.
Mr. LESLIE: It was not in Belfast.
I can only hope that the noble Viscount
will urge his teachers to impress on their
pupils something of these virtues of
reliance and self-control to which Senator
Barclay alluded. It is certainly not
possible to do other than to support the
Bill as a whole, but we must get some
more information from the Minister of
Labour when we come to examine the
Measure in Committee. If I remember
rightly the noble Viscount suggested that
the Committee Stage should be taken on
Thursday. I do not know if anybody
thinks that is really sufficient time to
allow for the consideration of the
Measure. Of course we can take the
Second Reading pretty quickly, and to
run through fifty or sixty Clauses does
not take long, but if anybody wants to
think about what the Clauses really mean,
and to refer to any previous enactments
or regulations mentioned in those Clauses,
it seems to me that two days is a wholly
insufficient allowance. The question
arises whether a revising chamber is
exactly performing the functions for
which it was constituted if it allows an
important Measure, and especially a
Measure which may cost £300,000 a year,
to go through with such extraordinary
rapidity.
Mr. ANDREWS: It is always a particular pleasure to me to visit this
honourable House where the debates are
conducted in such a friendly and helpful
spirit and free from all objectionable
personalities. I feel that it is not really
necessary for me to say very much to-day
about the Bill itself. It is particularly
gratifying to know that it has met with
general approval from every hon. Senator
who has spoken, and, therefore, it is not
necessary for me to attempt to influence
those who already feel that this is a Bill
which ought to become law at the same
time as the British Measure, for that is
exactly the opinion which my colleagues
and I hold.
I do, however, desire to refer to some
of the criticisms which have been made
by hon. Senators. In the first place,
Senator McAllister said that it did not so
much matter what was in the Bill as how
it was administered. Of course both are
important, but I can assure my hon.
Friend that it is the desire of my Department to administer these great social
services as sympathetically as the law will
permit. But the decision as to whether
or not a person is entitled to benefit does
not rest with me. Parliament has set up
courts of referees and a very capable
umpire to decide these most important
matters. It is their duty to decide such
matters and not mine, but, so far as my
Department has any influence in connection with the matter, it is always
exercised in the direction of seeing that
justice is done. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
The G.S.W. condition, to which the
hon. Senator also referred, no longer
exists. It has not existed for some time
in our Acts of Parliament, and it is not
revived in this Bill. Of course there is
very frequently a doubt as to whether or
not a man is available for work, and
sometimes a decision may seem harsh to
those who are not conversant with all the
Unemployment Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Andrews]
facts of the case. I would like to cite a
case which has caused some heartburning
I understand. Take a case where a man
industrial centre
comes
from
an
in
England, we will say a coal mining
centre. He has been a coal miner for a
great many years, and coal mining in
some parts of Great Britain, as we all
know, is in a depressed condition at the
present time. Say that man leaves that
part of Great Britain, where he has
resided for a considerable time, and. as
I have said, comes over here and settles
down in a country district in County
Down or County Antrim. It has been
held, and I think rightly if I may say so,
that that man is no longer making himself
available for the work he has been
accustomed to follow.
who
As regards the residence Clause, I
would like to repeat what I said in the
House of Commons, namely, that this is
not applied in any vindictive spirit. It is
simply applied for the purpose of seeing
that Northern Ireland work is preserved
as far as possible in these times for
Northern Ireland people. Further it is
for the purpose of seeing that
persons
live outside the United Kingdom are not
enabled to come in here and enjoy higher
social services than they would enjoy if
they remained in that other part of
Ireland known as the Free State, where
these social services are on a less generous
basis than they are here, as my hon.
Friend knows.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast raised what
is considered by some people to be a rather
thorny matter at the present time, but he
has raised it in a spirit which I very much
appreciate. I think I am correct in saying that he does not object to the 11d
rate but he is sorry that we have departed
from the British basis of sixty and forty
per cent. Of course the conditions here
are different to those which exist in Great
Britain in this connection. As the Lord
Mayor knows the peak year here was
different to the peak year in Great
Britain, and here we have taken the peak
which was most generous to our local
year
authorities. Again he alluded to the
fact that when the new valuation came
into force the burden on local authorities
might be considerably increased, and in
addition to the personal pledge that I
gave he would like to have something in
an Act of Parliament. I can quite
understand that, because of course men
come and men go, and it is quite right
that these matters should not be left on
the basis of verbal promises. But if he
will refer to Clause 40, sub-Clause (4) he
will find that the matter is really provided for along the basis of my promise.
Clause 40, sub-Clause (4) reads
follows:
as
"The contributions to be paid by a
council under this section in the years
subsequent to the period mentioned in
sub-section (2) of this section shall be
calculated in such manner as Parliament may determine."
You will see that the matter is really
left for Parliament to decide at the end
of the three years. Of course, it would
be very improper for us to attempt to tie
Parliament beyond that period. The
British Parliament is not tied beyond
that period, so in that respect we are
following exactly the course that has been
pursued at Westminster.
The Lord Mayor did make a point
in which there is some force, namely,
that there is a normal, ordinary rise in
valuations in the city of Belfast, and we
are all very glad of that; but I am
advised that this really is a very small
matter. It only means that if the
normal increase in valuation goes on
over the three years it might mean an
increased contribution from the city of
Belfast of £2,000. Considering the
large figures with which we are dealing,
I think that is a mere bagatelle. I am
entitled to point out that the total expenditure, on the basis of 21 per cent.
unemployment is £2,370,000, and all
that the local authorities in Northern
Ireland are being asked to produce is
£88,000, or only between 3 and 4 per
cent., so I venture to suggest in this
respect that the local authorities are
making a very good bargain.
were
As regards the remarks that
made by my hon. Friend, Senator Quigley,
I think the most important thing he
said was that he wished agricultural
labourers were brought within the insurance scheme. There are arguments
for that, and arguments against it, but
I will point out that by this Bill they
are brought into the unemployment
assistance scheme, and they are brought
in under very favourable terms both
from the farmers' point of view and
from the workers' point of view, because
it is on a non-contributory basis. The
Unemployment Bill
Finance Bill-
12 JUNE 1934
whole of the cost will be borne by the
local authorities to a very minor degree
and by the Exchequer.
There is provision in the Measure to
set up a standing committee to advise
the Minister of Labour on quite a number of matters, and we in Northern Ireland are to have what I may
call a
representative on that committee, because
one of those who are to form that committee is to be appointed by the Imperial Minister of Labour after
consultation with me. One of the duties
of that committee will be to consider
whether agricultural labourers should
be brought into the insurance scheme as
well as into the unemployment
assistance scheme which they are being
brought into, so that really the position
of agricultural labourers, which has
been very difficult and somewhat sad up
to the present, as they were entirely
dependent upon the local authorities for
whatever assistance they needed, will be
vastly improved under this Measure.
Senator Barclay spoke about the young
persons. I appreciate very much that it
is desirable that young persons and old
should have a
persons
sense of independence and not depend on others; but
I would point out in this connection
that although young persons are being
brought into insurance at the schoolleaving age, instead of as at the present
time at 16. they will not be entitled to
receive benefit until they are 16, and any
benefit which they will be given will go
to the parents if the parents are unemployed. That is intended to assist the
parents to maintain the young persons
during the period of unemployment between the school-leaving age, whatever
that may be, and 16. The young persons will get benefit if they
unemployed at 16, instead of at 16 and
30 weeks as at present, and inducements
are being given to parents and young
people to keep their young people at
school longer than they do at present
time, because for those who remain full
time at school beyond the compulsory
school age contributions will be given
free, so that when the young persons
reach 16, and become unemployed,
say 10
10 weeks afterwards, they will
be entitled to receive benefit which
they would not be entitled to get at
the present time.
are
The other matter to which I wish to
refer was raised by my other kind
Friend, Senator Leslie, and he asked me
a question which I am not prepared to
give a very definite answer to with all
due respect I say that because no one
could answer it, that is. what is to be
the trend of unemployment during the
next three years? We all rejoice in the
fact that unemployment has so much improved during the year that has gone,
and I think the prospects for the
immediate future are quite bright; but
what is going to happen in world
affairs which very largely affect the
position here neither I nor anybody else
can say.
Senator Leslie referred to the fact that
the noble Lord, the Leader of the
House, said that this was a Bill that
required some courage to introduce. I
agree, but I think it will be admitted
that not to introduce it would require
still more courage, because I think he
would indeed be a courageous man who
would seek to deny to our working people
in this part of the United Kingdom, in
connection with these social services, the
benefits which were enjoyed by their
fellow-countrymen across the Channel.
We have been told that he who ploughs
ought to plough in hope, and I plough in
hope to-day that this Measure will really
prove to be a boon and a blessing to our
working people who will be unemployed
through no fault of their own during
the years that lie ahead.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Finance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: This Bill deals with the
changes in taxation arising out of the
Budget proposals of the right hon.
the Minister of Finance. The first
two Clauses are concerned with
motor taxation. It will be seen that in
Clause 1 opportunity has been taken to
repeal the existing scale of excise duties
payable in respect of licences for
mechanically propelled vehicles, and to
enact in the Schedule to this Bill a com-
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
plete scale setting forth both the new
and the unchanged existing duties in
comprehensive form.
as
The main changes consist in the classification of motor-cycles by cylinder
capacity instead of by weight
hitherto; an increase in the duties payable in respect of heavy lorries. trailers
and haulage vehicles; an increase in the
duties for vehicles using oil fuel, as a
result of which such vehicles will be
liable to duty of about 33 per cent.
greater than petrol-driven vehicles of
the same class and finally, the increase
by 25 per cent. as from the 1st January,
1936, of the duties payable in respect of
vehicles not fitted with pneumatic tyres.
Clause 2 of the Bill is designed to
secure that where a private car is used
temporarily and without structural
alteration for the carriage of goods, its
owner will not thereby be rendered liable
for payment of duty on the car as a
goods vehicle. In Clause 3 will be found
the Government's proposal for the charge
to be imposed on local rates for centralised educational services. It will be
noted that the charge will apply only to
hereditaments which are not derated,
and will consist of a rate of 6d in the
£ for the year 1934-35 and 1s in the £
in subsequent years. The Clause provides temporary borrowing facilities to
enable the councils to meet the payment
in respect of the current year.
We are privileged to have with us
the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister
of Finance, who will deal with any
points that may not be quite clear to
hon. Senators. I beg to move. "That
the Bill be now read a Second time.”
Mr. LESLIE: I presume you would
regard this as a Bill which is not capable
of alteration-shall we say?-in this
House, but I do not know whether you
would go so far as to say it would be
impossible to reject an entire Clause in
the Bill. There is always, I think, that
difficulty about a financial Measure, but
in any case I gravely doubt whether this
House would agree to accept the responsibility of rejecting any part of a taxation.
Measure which had passed without very
serious controversy, I think, in another
place. Certainly I do not myself propose to
attack in any way the first Clause of
the Bill or the ancillary Clause which
follows it. The Clause on which I want
to make a few remarks is Clause 3, which
requires a contribution from the councils
of the counties and the county boroughs
towards the cost of educational services.
The whole question seems to me to be
a very difficult one. It is impossible to
deny that taking the ordinary view, the
view which obtains almost, I think,
throughout the world, the local authority
ought to bear some of the cost of educational services, and I think that that
theory can be maintained even in the
face of conditions like those existing in
Northern Ireland, where the State
requires the compulsory attendance of
children at school. The local authorities
here, as elsewhere, have no voice as regards the attendance of school children.
They have, under a recent Act, some
voice as to the ages of the childrenthey can, for instance, require attendance
I think six months earlier than was provided by an earlier Act. and so on. But
that does not affect the main question.
The point remains that the local
authorities have now to provide school
accommodation whether they like it or
whether they do not, and they have to
bear a certain proportion of the cost of
education. The actual rate which is
necessary to meet such obligations as
exist at present is not a very heavy one.
I think, taking the average throughout
County Antrim, it does not exceed ninepence. I think that is right, and it
must be remembered that as regards
those contributions there is an arrangement whereby the Exchequer recoups
local authorities to the extent of twothirds of the product of a shilling rate,
and equally, of course, the Exchequer
bears the entire cost of the teachers
salaries. We have to remember all those
things, for they are very important.
Now Parliament is about to ask the
local authorities to collect the product of
a sixpenny rate during this year, and a
shilling rate in the future. That does
impose a new burden, and the question is
whether it is, in the circumstances, unreasonable. I have just said that it cannot be contended that local authorities
should not bear some portion of the cost,
and equally it is beyond dispute that
across the water the local rate for educational purposes is 3s or 4s in the £. I
think 3s 6d would be a fair average.
We
are not, therefore, in Northern Ireland,
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
12 JUNE 1934
putting up from local resources anything
approaching the proportion put up in
Great Britain. But then we are not
doing that in a good many other things.
There is no doubt that the proportion paid
by local authorities towards the cost of
centralised services is considerably less in
Northern Ireland than it is in Great
Britain, and for that there seems to be a
great many reasons, both historical and
practical, into which we need not enter.
The point is that we have to bear a rate
of sixpence this year, and a shilling afterwards.
I should have preferred that this
question had stood over until the results
of revaluation were before us. Failing
that, I should have preferred that the
rate had been something less than a
shilling during what I would describe as
the intervening period, because of the cost
of revaluation which is falling at the
moment on the local authorities.
I do
not remember the figures, nor, of course,
do they matter very much for our purposes, but I undestand that in a full year
the Minister of Finance expects that the
return to the Exchequer will be something like £144,000, and that, of course,
is considerably more than the cost to the
local authorities year by year of their revaluation.
We
Still the fact does remain that there are
certain matters standing over until that
result is arrived at, and they may be
standing over by the deliberate intention
of Parliament, or, perhaps, to some extent
by the accident of circumstances.
have just given a Second Reading to a
Bill which provides that at the end of
three years certain local contributions are
to be on such a rate and such a scale as
Parliament may determine. We passed
the other day a Constabulary Bill continuing the existing contribution of
£25,000 a year from the City of Belfast
until further notice for the cost of the
constabulary. Both those Measures, and
this one also, will come up for reconsideration at some future date, and
that future date is likely to be the date
when the new valuation, whatever it may
be, is in force. I am not to be taken as
suggesting that the result of revaluation
will necessarily be a very large rise in
valuation throughout the Province. I am
coming to the conclusion that it will not.
I think there will be a rise in the large
towns, but I very much doubt whether
the rural areas and the smaller towns
will be very much affected one way or the
other. The point is, that we are asked
to pay sixpence now and a shilling next
year, and the principal argument is that
we have not been bearing our proper share
in the past, and we are not bearing a
share comparable with the share borne by
local authorities across the water.
Consequently it follows that the suggestion must be that while claiming for
ourselves the same standard of social
services as exists across the water, a standard which, I hope, we shall always be
able to maintain, and I know it will not
be the fault of the right hon. Gentleman,
the Minister of Finance, if it is not maintained, but still, the suggestion is that
if we claim the same standard of social
services as is claimed by Great Britain we
ought to bear, approximately at all
events, the same share of those expenses
which are properly attributable to rates
rather than taxation. It is a very difficult corner to get out of, and it seems, in
fact, that the claim for imposing a further
burden on the local authorities is almost
unanswerable.
The only thing I can
think of on which one can fall back is the
opinion which most of us hold, that the
rateable capacity of Northern Ireland is
so very near its limit that it makes it
necessary to scrutinise most carefully any
proposed encroachment on that margin.
That is, I think, more so because so many
of our local areas, particularly in the rural
district, do still lack many of the
amenities of life common across the water.
If you take what answers to villages
throughout Northern Ireland you will
find they are behind in such matters as
water, and very often sewerage, against
similar communities across the water. It
has to be remembered that we have, in
Northern Ireland, only one big town.
We have the sixth or seventh largest town
in the United Kingdom. Apart from
that we have only one really large town,
the City of Londonderry, which is a town
of only moderate size compared with
English towns, and after that what have
we? Bangor, I think, is our third largest
town, with a population of about 14,000.
We have probably half-a-dozen towns
with a population of 10,000-Lisburn,
Ballymena, Larne, Portadown, Newry
and, perhaps, Lurgan. That about
exhausts the list. We have so very few
towns of any size and, therefore, it
Finance Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
follows-because the number of towns
must be some index to the wealth of the
country-that we have much less to draw
on than they have across the water.
Therefore, It is right and proper that our
contributions should be on a very much
lower scale.
I think that if this shilling rate is paid
in the future it ought to be understood
that that is the very limit, and it should
also be understood that when this revaluation does come into effect the figure
will be reconsidered. I may once again
quote that section of the Revaluation Act
which I mentioned a few minutes ago in
another connection, because that applies
rather more to this question of national
expenditure than it did to the unemployment and public assistance question.
Those matters ought to be borne in mind,
and I should like to hear from the right
hon. Gentleman that it is the definite
intention of the Government that this
imposition of a shilling-I do not want to
use the word "imposition" in an offensive
sense but I would like to hear that it is
the deliberate intention of the Government to reconsider that at the earliest
convenient opportunity. I do not mean
to-morrow or the day after that. On the
whole, it is impossible, I think, to object
to the Bill. By "object" I mean in the
Parliamentary sense of opposing it in the
metaphorical lobbies that we have, but we
ought to ask for that undertaking I have
mentioned. I think we must accept the
Bill, and I am afraid we must be content
to leave it at that.
I
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH:
am afraid I must agree with Senator
Leslie when he says there is nothing for
this House to do but to accept the Bill.
It is impossible to move Amendments to
it or to alter it. Under these circumstances I am not going to criticise the Bill
at any great length. I am here to-day
with one object, and one object only, and
that is, on behalf of the Corporation I
have the honour to represent, to protest,
in the strongest possible manner, against
the way this proposal is being carried out.
The members of the Belfast Corporation
think that the right hon. Gentleman's
action is extraordinary, and that it is unwarranted. We had a meeting to-day at
which the matter was discussed, and I
would like to tell the right hon. Gentleman that after a dignified debate the
meeting, while recognising the difficulty
the Government is in, again expressed the
opinion that an unfair burden is being
placed on that body.
Let us go into the position for a
moment. Under the Education Act of
1923 Parliament passed certain regulations, which were accepted by local
authorities. Under that Act the Government undertook to pay the first eightpence
of the education rate. The local authorities and I am speaking particularly on
behalf of the Belfast Corporation-went
into the matter and, recognising the difficulty, have endeavoured to carry out the
intentions of the Act. What has
happened? The Belfast Corporation
have entered into large commitments.
Their expenditure, up to the present, is
something like three-quarters of a million
pounds for schools and ground. Their
programme represents an expenditure of
another million and a half. Towards
that the Government do not contribute
one penny, and never did. It is true they
have contributed the first eightpence of
the rate, but that contribution has, in
fact, during the past five years, scarcely
paid for the cost of administration, while
the rates are mounting up to an alarming
extent and are bound to go on increasing.
The hon. and learned Member (Mr.
Leslie) said that our rating capacity is not
equal to that of Great Britain. If he
took the trouble to go into the matter I
think he would find there is absolutely no
comparison. We here have always
received special treatment, apart altogether from this Bill. Indeed, I think
the report of the committee, presided over
by Senator Leslie, showed clearly that
the rateable capacity of Northern Ireland
was only 47 per cent. of that of Great
Britain. Yet at this period, when we are
passing through such difficult times, our
burden is to be still further increased. I
should like to pay a tribute to both the
Minister of Finance and the Minister of
Labour who, to a very large extent, have
assisted the Corporation to keep down the
rates. This year we were faced with an
increase of 3s 2d in the rates. With the
chairman of the Finance Committee I
interviewed the two right hon. Gentlemen, and we were able to arrive at an
arrangement whereby the rates
increased by only 1s 5d in the £. We
recognised it was right and proper that we
should do all in our power to keep the
were
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
12 JUNE 1934
rates down, particularly as our industries
and agriculture were passing through
difficult times.
I
Having done that, the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Finance, now
comes along and, without having consulted us or anybody else, imposes this
rate of 6d this year and 1s next year.
think he has said that, as far as the
question of balancing his Budget is concerned, he could have found other means
of doing that.
The MINISTER OF FINANCE
(Mr. Pollock): Hear, hear.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: Is
the Government going to turn itself into
a rating authority and put 6d on the rates
this year and ls next year? It is all very
well to tell us to borrow the money, but
I believe in paying our debts as we go
along. After facing all these difficulties,
and when everybody seemed rather happy,
we are now called upon to strike a rate of
6d in the £, or carry the amount over to
next year. That is the position we are
up against at the present time. I have a
suggestion to make. My hon. and
learned Friend (Mr. Leslie) touched upon
it. I make it in all good nature and not in
the way of finding fault with the Government. I make a suggestion which ought,
at least, to be considered, if not agreed to.
Every rating authority in the Northern
area is just the same as the City of
Belfast, only Belfast has to pay every
time. That is the trouble.
Mr. ANDREWS: Not all the time.
County Down pays.
We
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: The
suggestion I want to make is that you
should leave this year alone. Drop your sixpence. I do not know how much it will
realise. Drop the sixpence until the commencement of the new rate year, and
consider the whole question at a conference to see what is best to be done.
recognise that the Government have to
make ends meet. My right hon. Friend
says Belfast does not pay all the time. I
would like to ask this question of the
Leader of the House. What will sixpence in the pound raise in Belfast, and
what will it raise in the rest of the area?
I would like to ask that.
Mr. POLLOCK: £42,000 in Belfast.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH :
And what in the rest of the area?
Again,
take
the schools and what
do you find? You find from the last
report of the Ministry of Education that
Belfast has only 209 public elementary
schools out of a total of 1,837 in the
rest of the Province. That is the proportion. Belfast pays again. I would
not have raised this question only it was
raised in the other House. I do not
believe it is a good thing. I believe in
all standing together and stopping this
nonsense about what Belfast pays.
Take derating, and again I should like
to ask the right hon. Gentleman why he
left derated premises out of this Bill.
I do not want to enter into a discussion
about derating. I think the whole system is wrong, and I think it is out of
date. I will say this. that there are
plenty of farmers prosperous and making
money in the country. I tell you
what I do know. There are plenty of
large industrial concerns making money
and paying big dividends, and the Government pay a large proportion of the
⚫ rates. I will be blamed for saying that,
but I cannot help it. You will find that
derating has cost the Government,
roughly, a million a year.
Rather more,
I think. Belfast, I think, contributes
perhaps 50 or 60 per cent. of the entire
revenue of the country, and what does
Belfast get out of it? Roughly
£100,000. What do my poor friends in
the country and elsewhere get? Nearly
a million. There, again, Belfast pays.
I did not intend to raise this issue at
all. All I wanted to do was, on behalf
of the body I represent. to enter a protest. Again I make a suggestion to the
right hon. Gentleman. Take a little
time and leave the matter over, if possible. It is not a big thing. It is
neither going to make nor mar the Government. Leave it all over until the
commencement of the new financial year.
Let us settle down and discuss the whole
question and I will promise to get all the
support I can to arrive at a satisfactory
conclusion. I do think it is causing
a good deal of unrest and a good deal of
trouble, and, believe me, it will react
upon the Government.
Sir DUDLEY McCORKELL: I do
not wish to detain the House by any
long criticism. Like other speakers I
will refrain from saying anything about
the Bill until we come to Clause 3. I
am bound to say I have to agree to a
Finance Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Sir Dudley McCorkell]
great extent with the right hon. the
Lord Mayor of Belfast in his criticism,
but I approach it from a different angle,
and really, without being indifferent to
the claims of Belfast, I am not at the
moment concerned with what Belfast
pays or does not pay. The smaller place
from which I come has got to bear its
share, and we realise that there comes a
point when we can bear no more. A
shilling in a full year and sixpence in the
current one is going to bear very heavily
on the city of Derry, and we are faced
with that most unpleasant thing, which
the Lord Mayor mentioned, the striking
of a new special rate. Having already
budgeted for the year it is very unpleasant to go out and say we have to
strike an additional rate. I think there
is a great deal in the plea that we may
be allowed to finish this year without
budgeting afresh. We are inclined to
wander
that point.
from
In this
country, as in every other one, we must
cut our coat according to our cloth. I
quite agree that those in charge of financial affairs must do that. But my learned
Friend behind me (Mr. Leslie) referred
to the standard which we should meet.
I think the standard in this country that
we should seek is the standard that we
need, and the reason we have to look for
this sixpence or shilling now from the
rates to pay for education is that we have
a standard of education which the people
are now being asked to pay for and
which they never asked for and never
wanted and could have done without for
many years. I am not saying that in
criticism of the noble Leader of the
House, who so ably conducts the Ministry or those with him. But when it was
started we started with grandiose ideas
which we were not able to live up to.
Perhaps if we have to pay the shilling
now out of the local rates to pay for the
system which is no better than we had
years ago we shall have learned a lesson
which will be valuable to us in the
future. When we embark on other
schemes we may contain ourselves a little
and not launch upon schemes which however idealistic they may be are not
utilitarian. When I go back to my own
times I think the boys of the national
schools were just as good as any of the
boys of to-day. We are trying to put
quarts into pint pots all round. We are
I
trying to make small boys and girls
absorb what they are not able to absorb,
and the result will be we shall have jacksof-all-trades and masters of none.
certainly look askance at the top-heavy
scheme and I think if we were modest in
our ideas we should do better. I do
not say the Government are not right in
trying to balance their Budget, but I do
think there is something in the suggestion
of the Lord Mayor that we should be
allowed a breather so that these towns
might budget afresh when the financial
year is ended and not have to start in
the middle of a financial year through
the fault of those above us to make a
fresh application to the ratepayers.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM :
There is no one in the North of Ireland
to-day commanding greater attention.
than the right hon. the Minister of
Finance. Everyone seems to be paying
particular attention to
to everything he
says, particularly with reference to the
Budget. There seems to be misunderstanding with reference to this sixpenny
rate. It is described as for education.
I notice that in some of the discussions
they state that it is not definitely
to be used for educational purposes. I would like when the Minister
of Finance is making an explanation of
other matters if he would clear that up
for us. It is not a very good thing to
have any misunderstanding about that.
So far as the Belfast Corporation is concerned, I certainly hope, as one of the
ratepayers, that they will not be handicapped either this year or in future years
in
the building of ordinary public
elementary schools. While the Mayor of
Derry stated it is quite possible we have
given far more education than is required, at the same time I am satisfied
that public elementary schools are necessary, and Belfast is very much in need
even yet of a large number of good,
healthy public elementary schools.
hope no matter what other burdens they
have to contend with they will not be
handicapped in the building of schools
for the present generation. Perhaps the
Minister of Finance will make it clear
whether this rate is directly for educational purposes.
I do
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I have listened
to the Mayor of Derry and other
speakers and I think it is time that
Finance Bill-
