12 JUNE 1934
100-21
SE77
something was stated with regard to this
education rate. In my town they cannot
raise the extra 6d this year for the simple
reason that they have already budgeted,
and they have budgeted for what they
require during the year. It means that
for next year, with the education rate
and the further insurance business, we
will have an extra 2s in the pound. We
have to add 6d for this year to the
shilling for next year. I quite understand what the Lord Mayor of Belfast
has stated and I think it would not be
unreasonable to forego that 6d this year
and have a conference. I would suggest a conference with regard to the
working of the whole Education Act to
give us Catholics a chance. I do not
This
want to raise any undesirable question,
but I do put it to you that I am
asked to pay a rate for which I am
practically getting no value. That rate
is going to become a great burden.
year I am asked to pay an extra rate of
6d and next year I will be asked to pay
an extra rate of 1s for what I am getting
practically no value for. I would respectfully suggest to the Leader of the
House, if it were possible, to review the
whole situation and see if an agreement
could be come to whereby my people
could enter into this question of education and take advantage of it as well as
pay for it. I also suggest that now is
a desirable time for considering the whole
matter, and if the Ministry will just give
it a little consideration there may possibly
be ways and means of meeting us.
Everything should be done to try to
bring the children up as good citizens
and have them educated in the manner
in which their parents and people
desire.
If
Mr. POLLOCK: I should not like that
any hon. Senator here to-day should think
that I am under any illusions as to the
difficulty that exists in connection with
the imposition of an education rate.
any other expedient had been possible
under the conditions as they arose I am
quite sure that no one would have been
more anxious or desirous of avoiding any
such difficult method not of satisfying
ourselves or of balancing our local
accounts but of satisfying the British
Government that we were living up to a
sense of our responsibilities. I think
that perhaps if I do not weary the House
I had better touch upon the incidence of
this education question, because it is not
a thing of to-day or yesterday.
The Arbitration Committee was
appointed to settle the taxable capacity
of Northern Ireland, and its relation particularly in regard to the contribution
which it was expected would be paid by
Northern Ireland towards the cost of the
protection given by the Forces of the
Crown and towards the National Debt.
This Committee was an independent
Committee, not controlled by the Government, and they absolutely and definitely
disallowed a portion of our educational
expenditure to be charged against our
revenue.
That was on the ground that
there must be some relation between the
burden that we bear as well
as the
advantages that accrue between Great
Britain and Northern Ireland.
From that time to this, that is now ten
years ago, this question of an education
rate has been a blot upon our relations
with the Imperial Government. We have
claimed that in every case we must receive
the same advantages in regard to our
social services as our fellow-citizens in
Great Britain, but when it comes, as it
is alleged, to the question of bearing
similar responsibilities we fight shy. As
a matter of fact, during the time I mentioned we have not been subject to
anything like the same burdens as our
fellow-citizens in Great Britain in regard
to education. Perhaps I may give some
comparisons very shortly in order that the
House may realise how greatly we have
been relieved during the years that have
passed since the matter was first considered.
Let us take any city comparable with
Belfast. I am dealing with Belfast
specially in this case because it is typical
and also because the most violent protest
has issued from the city of Belfast. I
may say that I have read carefully the
pronouncement made by prominent
officials of the Belfast Corporation, in
regard to which I make no complaint at
all. I am quite sure they carried out
their duties as they were expected to do,
perfectly reasonably and perfectly fairly,
but as I go along perhaps I may be permitted to join issue with them on some
of the statements they have made.
have said, this has been a burning question in our relations with the Imperial
Government, and when we remember
that those relations should be as far as
As I
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Pollock]
possible amicable hon. Members will perhaps feel the position I occupy, coming
as I do definitely and frequently into
touch with the, is not very satisfactory
or very pleasant.
I would like to make some comparisons
between Belfast and some city that is
comparable in Great Britain I take
Leeds, as it carries on its services under
circumstances comparable with Belfast.
It is an industrial city similar to ours.
The cost per head of elementary education in Belfast is £8 5s 9d, of that the
ratepayers pay 13s 9d. In the case of
Leeds the rate per head for elementary
education is £12 11s 10d, of which the
ratepayers pay £6 5s 11d, as against our
13s 9d. That is a comparison that may
be taken as a fairly average contrast
between the conditions obtaining in Great
Britain generally and those obtaining in
Belfast.
What can I say when I am faced with
that? From time to time it has been
brought before the Government here that
something must be done in relation to
this matter of education, and that if we
were going to be benefited to the extent
we are by the British connection we have
got to bear some burdens. I mention
these particulars in order that hon. Members might know that this matter has
been constantly before me for the last
ten years. I am
sure my right hon.
Friend the Lord Mayor is not ignorant
of the fact that the education question
has always been a difficult, a doubtful,
and a burning question in regard to our
position here in Northern Ireland.
was
However, things came to a head, and
recently when matters of a very
important character relating to the
interests of our Province were brought
before the British Government it
made perfectly clear to us that no discussions in regard to these matters could
be entered into unless we regularised our
position in regard to education. I had
postponed, or temporised, or delayed,
hon. Members may use any word they
like-in regard to this matter. I put it off;
indeed, I did not bring it even before our
own Government, it came before my personal notice, but our Government
perfectly well aware from time to time of
the conditions that faced us in regard to
our relations with Great Britain on this
subject.
was
But, as I say, I temporised on the
matter until it came in a very definite
shape and form before us comparatively
recently. In the interests of this Province and having regard to major matters
of more than ordinary importance our
Government undertook that they would
ask the local authorities to bear at least
a share, even though it be a small share,
of the cost of education. When I mentioned the figures which I have submitted
to the House it must have been perfectly
evident that there was no answer to the
British Government. I could not answer
them. I did argue the matter for
probably half
hour before the
Arbitration Board.
an
I want to be quite frank about this
business because suggestions have been
made from time to time, very improper
suggestions, that the Government had
some sinister object in asking authorities.
to impose this rate for education, and
that it was part of our general finances
and necessary for the
purpose of
balancing the Budget. I contradicted
that most emphatically, but yet from time
to time during the past two or three weeks
it has been referred to as if what I was
saying was untrue, because it has nothing
or very little to do with our finances this
year. The Budget is the only method of
imposing a rate. There was
no other
method of doing it. That is the only
sense in which it has any touch with
our ordinary finances. In regard to the
greater interests of the Province the
Government found it necessary to adopt
this course.

I take full responsibility for it myself,
but the Government is equally with me
in this matter because the Government
has been, as a whole, introduced into it.
Even
Friend
my right hon.
(Mr.
Andrews), who has taken such a wonderful
interest in all that relates to the social
affairs of the Province, and who, I
suppose, has done more than any other
any burden that could
possibly exist with regard to the peopleeven he, who is introduced into this
matter as part of the Government, will
agree with every word I say when I assure
the House that it was essential in the
interests of Ulster that this rate should
be imposed on the local authorities for
education.
man to ease
I think that so far as Belfast is concerned my right hon. Friend the Lord
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
12 JUNE 1934
Mayor, with whom I have had the
pleasantest relations all my business life
-I may say I certainly can call him without any qualifications my friend-has
known from time to time, and the Belfast
authorities have known from time to
time, that this education matter was
bound to come up over and over again
and would have to be settled. If the
members of the Corporation approach
this matter in a proper spirit they will
see it is not so very serious after all in
regard to an increase of rates. It is
different, I admit, in other parts of the
Province. I agree that there are some
places where the rate is so high that it
will be a very inconvenient and uncomfortable burden, and it is just possible,
as revaluation has been referred to, that
some arrangement may have to be made
in regard to the burden which they would
have to bear in connection with education. But these matters are in the air,
and can only be dealt with as they arise.
It must not be assumed that the
Government has any sinister object in
imposing this rate or is not concerned or
has no sympathy with all the difficulties
that arise or have arisen in connection
with this matter. It is the business of
the Government to endeavour to promote
the interests of the whole Province and
not those of Belfast alone. Belfast is a
very important part of the Province.
There is no doubt about that. No one
realises that more than I do, but at the
same time there are the interests of the
entire Province which it is the business
and the duty of the Government to be
concerned with.
as
Hon. Members must not think for one
moment the Government would take one
step that would impose a burden upon
the people of Ulster that could be avoided.
That would be an action which I regard
almost impossible. Otherwise the
Government has no business to be here
and should be turned out. I stated that
the burden would not be so great on
Belfast at any rate. I am not in a
position to calculate exactly how it will
affect the rest of the Province, but
between the relief that will be given in
regard to unemployment liability and this
education rate it would not appear that
there would be any necessity for an
additional rate this coming year.
My right hon. Friend mentioned to-day
that the British Government selected a
year in regard to unemployment.
Although there is no connection necessarily between the two I do not know
whether I am verging upon another
matter which is perhaps not germane to
the question of education but is germane
to the burden that the Corporation has to
bear. The British Government selected
the year 1932-33 because it was the year
in which the local authorities had spent
the largest amount of money, and when
they assessed the sixty and forty per cent.
they were getting the largest amount of
money from the local authorities. We
agreed here that we would do the same
thing, although 1932-33 was not the peak
year with us. As I say, that may not
be regarded as germane to this discussion
on education, but at any rate it will
enable me to assure this House that, as
between the cost of education and the
unemployment relief that will be given by
Belfast, there is no necessity for the
imposition of any rate additional to that
which they are bearing at the present
time. I have said that this education
imposition was not a matter that the
Government desired in itself. It was
indeed a matter that the Government had
postponed and put off for a very long
time until it could not go on any longer.
Senator Leslie spoke of rating capacity
and the coming valuation. As a rule it is
far wiser to wait until you get near your
fence before you try to jump it. If the
revaluation makes any material change
upward, or indeed downward, in the
valuation of the Province it would, of
course, be a matter for the Government,
in the interests of the people of the
country, to review the whole situation
and, therefore, the rating capacity-or
taxable capacity is the better word-of
the Province would have to be considered.
It was this whole question of the taxable
capacity of the Province which governed
the decision of the arbitration committee.
When they declined to admit that the
education expenditure should be wholly
borne by the national purse they were
working on and guided by the taxable
capacity of Northern Ireland. When this
change arises, if any material change does
arise in regard to valuation, it will be the
duty of the Government to review the
whole situation, so that the burden on the
local authorities, which means the burden
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Pollock]
on the ratepayers, will not be such as cannot be borne easily and satisfactorily, or
at least without great hardship.
I have given the House the comparisons
between Great Britain and ourselves, and
I have now only to comment upon the
Lord Mayor's statement that this was
totally unwarranted. My right hon.
Friend, the Lord Mayor, must have
known perfectly well that on matters relating to the taxation of the country any
premature discussion would have been
unwarranted. To have called all the
different interests involved into conference would have rendered it impossible for the Government to have
arrived at any decision that could have
been carried out at the only time at which
it could be carried out, namely, through
the Budget statement. Therefore, I
think it is rather unfair-at least I did
not expect it from the Lord Mayor-that
he should charge me with an unwarranted
proceeding. It was something that simply
had to be done. There is no getting over
that. We may take it for granted that
the British Government were, and are,
insistent upon it, and it is because of that
insistence that we have carried it out in
what I believe is a moderate waycertainly it is moderate as compared with
anything existing in Great Britain.
When I tell the House that the ratepayers in Belfast pay 13s 9d per head for
education and that in the City of Leeds
the ratepayers pay £6 5s 11d for the same
purpose, how can any argument be
adduced to controvert the claim of the
British Government in regard to our
responsibility? This is something which
it was not the desire of our Government
here to carry out, but, as I stated when I
began to speak, there were other matters,
and there are still matters of very grave
importance that will require to be settled
and that are in course of being settled, I
hope to our advantage, with the British
Government. This education question
was queering the pitch in all our negotiations, and was interfering very materially
with the prospect of
reasonable
arrangement with the British Government
in regard to other matters.
any
I do not know that I can say any more
than that on the subject. I cannot see
that the Belfast Corporation are really
going to be so very greatly affected in the
matter as to warrant the violent protests
that
have been made, because the
language used by some members of the
Corporation was not such as one
ordinarily uses in debate or discussion.
Suggestions and hints have been made as
to some sinister action on the part of the
Government for the purpose of balancing
our accounts, the amount involved being,
in fact, really so trivial that it would offer
no great difficulty from the point of view
of imposing some form of taxation of a
character.
very minor
The amount
imposed upon the Belfast authority is
something like £74,000 a year, and yet all
this fuss is made about it. I really must
assume that there is no warranty at all for
the implications and suggestions that have
been made in regard to the action of the
Government, but I can say that, in asking the House to pass this Finance Bill,
the Government know and feel that they
are acting in the best interests of all the
people of this Province. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.)

House

Wednesday, 13th June, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The sixpenny rate for educational purposes proposed to be levied under the Finance Bill
in the present financial year is estimated
to produce:-
(a) In the City of Belfast, £39,545.
(b) In the remainder of Northern
Ireland. £31,500.
Questions to Minister.
GOVERNMENT CONTRIBUTIONS
TO LOCAL AUTHORITIES.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether he can state the total amount
contributed by the Government of Northern Ireland since its inception in
respect of roads, housing, unemployment
relief schemes, education, derating and
other services towards assisting:-
(a) The Belfast Corporation,
(b) The other local authorities of
Northern Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In view
of the short period available for the
preparation of this reply I can only give.
approximate figures in answer to the
right hon. Senator's question.
The total amount contributed by the
Government of Northern Ireland since
its inception up to the 31st March, 1934,
in respect of roads, housing, unemployment relief schemes, education, derating
and other services towards assisting the
Belfast Corporation was approximately
£5,542,150, and towards assisting the
other local authorities of Northern Ireland was approximately £14,381,200.

I should perhaps remind the right

of the motor vehicles in Northern Ireland are registered in Belfast, but are
not limited in their use to the streets of

Milk Bill-
Second Reading.
13 JUNE 1934
Between 75 and 80 per cent. of the
annual expenditure on the maintenance
and improvement of roads in Northern
Ireland is incurred by local authorities
other than Belfast Corporation.

Viscount CHARLEMONT:
of the House, I would like to alter

Milk and Milk Products Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I introduce this Bill with the knowledge that
its object, namely, the assistance of the
milk industry in Northern Ireland, will
receive the support of all Members of
this House. The milk industry in
Northern Ireland, however, is not a
single industry, but is a two-fold one,
and consequently the introduction of a
scheme for the reorganisation of milk
marketing in this country presents
difficulties which are not present in
Great Britain. In the first place, it is
essential to remember that there are two
classes of milk producers in Northern
Ireland. First of all, there are the
farmers who produce milk which is sent
to creameries, and, secondly, there are
the farmers who produce milk principally for sale for human consumption.
In Northern Ireland only some 30 per
cent. of the total output of milk is used
for human consumption and the great
bulk of our milk supply is used for
manufacturing purposes.
The decline which has recently been
experienced in the prices paid by our
creameries for milk supplied to them
has been the cause of very grave concern
to the Government. The fall in
creamery prices was due to the great
increase which has recently taken place
in imports of dairy produce into the
United Kingdom, many of these imports
being subsidised or bounty fed in greater
or less degree.
The low prices prevailing for butter
on the British Market resulted in the
prices being paid by our creameries for
milk supplies falling to a very low level.
and it is not too much to say that a
continuance of these disastrous conditions for another year would have led
to the collapse of the creamery movement in Northern Ireland. Such a result would have been disastrous to our
agriculture because with the maintenance of our creameries is linked up
the maintenance of such important
branches of our agriculture as the rearing of store cattle, the fattening of
and
pigs,
the production of eggs
and poultry. Butter production at
creameries and the rearing of calves go
hand in hand, while an important factor
in connection with the development of
our pig industry is the presence of a
plentiful supply of separated milk.
For some months past the Government has been making strenuous efforts
to secure the application to Northern
Ireland of the same provisions in regard
to the guaranteeing of a minimum price
for milk used for manufacturing purposes as
have been applied in Great
Britain, and we are fortunate in that
Northern Ireland has been included
within the scope of the Milk Bill which
is at present before Parliament in Great
Britain. Under that Bill a sum not
exceeding £200,000 will be made available this year, and will enable payments
to be made out of the milk fund to be
established under this Bill to an extent
which will render it possible to raise the
average price paid by creameries for
milk to 5d per gallon in summer and
Steps are
6d per gallon in winter.
being taken to enable licence fees to be
imposed upon the sale of all classes of
milk for liquid consumption; the proceeds of these licence fees will be paid
into the Milk Fund, and will be available in order to pay a bonus in respect
of graded milk supplies delivered to
creameries.
On account of the fact that only 30
per cent. of our milk production in
Northern Ireland is used for liquid consumption it is impossible for us to apply
a pooling system under which milk sold
for liquid consumption and milk used
for manufacturing purposes should be
paid for at exactly the same price.
This is what is being done in Great
Britain, but it would be clearly inequitable to call upon the liquid milk
producers in Northern Ireland to bear
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
(Viscount Charlemont.)
levies sufficient to enable the price of
milk delivered at creameries to be
brought up to the same level as the price
realised for milk sold for human consumption. A very definite limit has
thus been imposed upon the amount of
licence fee which can be charged. This
licence fee will be heaviest in the case of
Grade C milk, that is, milk which is
most nearly akin to manufacturing milk,
and will be lowest on the higher grades
of milk sold for liquid consumption.
So far I have spoken of what is being
done in order to assist the position of the
producer of manufacturing milk. I
turn now to the position of the liquid
milk producer. During the past year or
so the position of the farmer supplying
milk to Belfast has been rendered almost
impossible on account of the severe price
undercutting which has been taking
place and which has resulted in milk
being delivered from door to door in
Belfast at the present time at a price
of 4d a quart. On account of the low
prices paid by the creameries milk which
would normally be used for manufacturing purposes has been pushed on to the
liquid milk market and sold at prices
with which the genuine liquid milk producer, who has to keep up a relatively
level supply of milk in both winter and
summer, could not compete. If such a
state of affairs were allowed to continue
unchecked there would be a real danger
of a shortage of milk in the winter owing
to the winter milk producer having been
driven out of business by the
petition of cheap manufacturing milk in
the summer months. It is proposed to
establish a Joint Milk Council composed
of representatives of all the interests
concerned, which will have the power to
prescribe the prices to be received by
farmers for milk sold for human consumption. In this way the liquid milk
producer will be assured of his market
and will be safeguarded from the ruinous
price undercutting which has recently
taken place.
com-
The Government has, however, been
fully conscious of the necessity of protecting the interests of consumers. Consequently, upon the Joint Milk Council
which will be established there will be
representatives of consumers as well as
representatives of producers and distributors, and three persons nominated
by the Minister of Agriculture. I am
afraid that a certain amount of misconception exists as to the special powers
conferred on these three nominees of the
Minister of Agriculture under subsection (3) of Clause 5, but I hope to
be in a position to put down an Amendment for the Committee Stage which will
have the effect of modifying to some extent the functions of the three nominees
of the Minister of Agriculture.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In one
further aspect the interest of the consumer is to be guarded. The Bill provides that all milk sold in Northern
Ireland shall be sold under one or other
of the following designations: -Grade
A, Grade B, Grade C, and Grade D.
The exact standards for these various
grades of milk will be laid down in regulations to be made by the Ministry of
Agriculture. Grade D milk, however,
will not be allowed to be sold for human
consumption, and all milk sold as Grade
B or Grade C will be produced from cows
which have been examined twice a year
by the Ministry's veterinary inspectors
and are clinically free from tuberculosis.
There will be a direct incentive for a
farmer to produce the very highest
grades of milk, because in this way the
amount of licence fee which he will be
called upon to face will be reduced. It
is hoped that there will be a steady increase in the standard of purity of our
milk supplies and it will be the
endeavour of the Ministry of Agriculture to bring this improvement about as
rapidly as possible. I am pleased to say
the right hon. the Minister of Agriculture is here in the House to reply to
the debate on the Second Reading.
beg to move.
I
Mr. McALLISTER: With the very fickle
and uncertain climate we have in this
country I always took the view that it
would be a great matter for the farmers
of Northern Ireland if the dairying
industry could be improved and made a
paying proposition, because that would
more or less render the farmers
independent of the very fickle climate we
have here. So far as this Bill improves
the position of the milk producers of this
area it will have the support of the whole
House, but I support it in the hope that
it is only a preliminary step towards
improving the whole dairying industry of
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1934
Northern Ireland. It seems to me that
those who will benefit most as a result
of this Bill will be those producers who
supply the creameries, as undoubtedly
they are supplying the creameries at the
present time at an uneconomic price.
Therefore it is a matter for gratification
that this class of farmer, who forms a
substantial part of the agricultural community, will get a better price, if not an
adequate price, in the future for the milk
he produces.
With regard to the constitution of this
milk council, I will put this view before
the House; that for the sake of the
reputation of this House as a responsible
legislature that particular Clause must be
drastically amended. In my opinion a
more ludicrous Clause-I do not mean
from the point of view of draughtsmanship, but because of the matter it contains
—was never put into a Bill presented to
any Parliament. If this Clause is
adopted as it stands it simply means that
for the Government it is a question of
"Heads I win; tails you lose." It
means that if this scheme goes well the
Government will say, "We have stablised
the dairying industry of Northern
Ireland," and if it fails the Government
will say,
"This is not our work; it is the
work of the Joint Milk Council." What
man with any responsibility would take a
position on such a council? He has only
a certain power, yet he is responsible to
the public for what is done.
You have seventeen members on that
council, and the three who are appointed
by the Government have the power to
dominate the council and to undo, to set
aside, and to render futile the almost
unanimous views of the other fourteen
members. That Clause provides that
unless three sets of people with
diametrically opposed interests financially
are unanimous, then these three members
appointed by the Government are to come
in and set aside the judgment, findings,
and wisdom of the other fourteen members. These three independent members
are to monopolise the whole machinery of
the council. No legislative assembly
could ever for a moment take the responsibility of allowing such a Clause as that
to stand in a Bill.
There is another matter to which I
take exception and that is sub-Clause (4)
of Clause 4. It provides:-
A licence granted under this section
shall be in the prescribed form, and
may be revoked or suspended by the
Ministry of Agriculture by reason of
the non-compliance of the holder of
the licence with the prescribed conditions applying thereto, or for any
other reason which the said Ministry
may deem to be sufficient.
Was there ever a more autocratic
Clause put into any Act of Parliament?
Speaking of my own knowledge, I have
never seen in any Statute a Clause so
drastically drafted as that. The Minister
is to have power to confiscate the business
of a man without giving any reason, good,
bad or indifferent. I do suggest to the
House that the power of the Minister
should be limited so that he can refuse to
give a licence to any of these people only
for some well defined and justifiable
reason.
more
With regard to this sum of £200,000
which Great Britain has contributed to
our milk fund, I would remind the House
that the subsidy to the wheat growers of
Great Britain very much
than
counter-balances that grant of £200,000,
so that we are really not even getting
back our own money.
I will conclude by
saying that in so far as this Bill will
improve the present position of the dairying industry every Member of the House
-will, I am sure, support it, and I support
it with the idea that it is only a preliminary step towards consolidating and
stabilising the whole dairying industry of
Northern Ireland.
con-
Mr. McMAHON: I am sure that anything that will help the farmers of
Northern Ireland will have the whole.
hearted support of every section of this
House. There are certain Clauses in this
Bill to which, so far as the County
Borough of Belfast is concerned, some
exception has been taken. For a
siderable number of years the Belfast
County Borough Council has had entire
control of the distribution of milk within
the city of Belfast. I understand that
they are responsible for the registration.
of 1,100 shops, and that the work is done
entirely free, whereas under this Bill this
work will have to be done over again and
probably new inspectors appointed. At
the moment the Belfast County Borough
Council have their own inspectors doing
this work and it is not costing the Government anything. The proposal in this
Milk Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. McMahon]
Bill will mean a duplication of inspectors.
When one considers the importance of the
milk supply to Belfast, which is controlled by a very eminent superintendent
medical officer of health who has had
London experience, one would have
thought the Government would have been
inclined to exempt Belfast entirely from
their executive control. The quality of
the
milk supplied to Belfast has been
raised to a very high standard during the
past number of years.
As far as Belfast is concerned it has no
objection whatever to any proposals that
may suit the country districts, but I do
say on behalf of the chairman of the
Public Health Committee, who is
tremendously interested in this matter,
that Belfast should be excluded entirely
from this Bill. The Public Health Committee have suggested that a Clause
should be introduced in this Bill to the
effect that the Ministry will give such
powers as to Grade B, Grade C, and
pasteurised milk to the Belfast County
Borough Council as to granting distributors' licences for the city, subject to
the council being satisfied with applicants'
premises equipment, appliances, conditions
reproduction, treatment, handling, and
transport.
There are other Amendments that the
Belfast Public Health Committee are
very anxious
to have passed. They
request that the Ministry will empower
the Belfast County Borough Council to
issue the producers' licences and carry out
all necessary inspections of animals and
cowsheds by their own officers instead of
being replaced by the officers of the
Ministry, and also as regards the question
of giving or not giving a producer's
licence, seeing that we have the staff
already in existence. It is really a question of economy, and I ask that some
attention should be given in the Committee stage to the suggested Amendments of the Public Health Committee
of the Belfast Corporation.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I am very
much in sympathy with my right hon.
Friend who is bringing this Bill forward
in his endeavour both to improve the
quality of the milk and to improve, if
possible, the position of the producer of
milk in Northern Ireland. If this Bill
succeeds in carrying out its intention it
will prove to be a great blessing to the
country. We spoke yesterday of the
Unemployment Bill as a courageous
Measure, and I think this Bill may also
be termed a courageous Measure, because
it is rather a leap in the dark. I shall
be very curious to see the financial aspect
of this matter after we have got rid of
the £200,000 which the British Government have given us.
There are one or two points on which,
as a producer, I shall be very glad to
have the views of the Minister of Agriculture. As producers of sweet or drinking milk we are to pay a licence fee of
2s 6d a year, and also a sum which is to
be 1d per gallon of the amount of milk
produced, I presume in the day or week.
That is to be deducted from the amount
we are to get from the person to whom
we sell the milk. Supposing I sell 140
gallons a week, I have to pay a licence fee
of 2s 6d a year, which is a mere trifle,
and I have also to pay a 1d per gallon
per day as the foundation of a fund to
reimburse other people, or to make the
scheme pay. Therefore, as
Therefore, as a milk producer I am going to pay a rather large
sum of money for selling milk. At any
rate, it seems to me to be a large sum.
The unfortunate farmer is already loaded
with all sorts of regulations under other
Acts of Parliament and they are going
to be increased by this Bill, under which,
if he does not keep a proper record, he
will have to pay 2d a day if he sells
Grade A and Grade B milk, or 6d per
day if he is unfortunate enough to sell
Grade C milk. It seems to me that the
scheme will weigh very heavily on the
producer.
With regard to the question of the
purity and the grading of A, B, and C
milk, there are a large number of farmers
who, unfortunately, without knowing it,
have in their herds a number of good
cows that are tubercular. It is possible
to have a tubercular cow without being
aware of the fact that it is tubercular,
and if on inspection a cow is found to be
tubercular and it is destroyed, I presume
the unfortunate owner will be paid only
the sum of £4 allowed under the Diseases
of Animals Act. If that is not so, is
there any fund out of which he will be
reimbursed better than that. I know
that a good many of us are rather slack,
but there are many farmers who possess
tubercular cows without doing so wilfully
or knowingly, and it will be very hard on
these people if they are to lose these
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1934
I
cattle without being compensated. There
is a tremendous number of tubercular
cattle still in the country, and I hope the
Minister of Agriculture will do all he
can to lessen the owners' loss if it comes
to a question of killing these cows.
hope the Bill will have the result it is
meant to have, and that it will enable
the producer to obtain more for his milk
than he has been getting up to the
present. For this reason I give the Bill
my blessing and hope for the best,
although it seems to me that on the
financial side it will be rather heavy on
the producers.
Mr. McHUGH: Like the other Members who have spoken on the subject I
give this Measure my hearty support
because it is very encouraging as it
appears in print. I hope that we may
not have disappointment in connection
with it when it is in operation as was the
case in the Pigs Marketing Act. I have
my whole life been connected with
creameries, and so I take more than an
ordinary
interest in the Measure.
Residing on the Border I realise that a
very important question arises as to how
the Minister of Agriculture is to deal
with farmers from Northern Ireland who
send their milk to Free State creameries.
The Minister, I am sure, is aware that
creameries at Clones, Belleek, Pettigo,
and Termon are built alongside the
Border, and that farmers from Northern
Ireland have invested their money in
many of them. A number of them also
took
a very deep interest in
creameries a long number of
the Border was created.
years
those
before
According to this Bill milk producers
in Northern Ireland are to receive 5d per
gallon during the summer season and 6d
during the winter season. The question
then arises, will those farmers who send
their milk to the creameries in the Free
State be paid the same as those who send
their milk to creameries in Northern
Ireland? I may be told by the right hon.
Gentleman (Sir Basil Brooke) that as he
has no control over the Free State
creameries he might not be able to see his
way to pay the farmers supplying them;
but I think this could be met by the
creameries establishing offices in Northern
Ireland and having separate books which
they could produce to any inspector who
might be sent. If this is not done the
farmers will be deprived of the benefit of
this Bill.
Then I might be told that all the
farmers on the Northern side of the
Border might be compelled to abandon
these creameries and send their milk by
lorries to creameries in Northern Ireland.
That would be a very difficult and costly
proposition. The vast majority of the
farmers along the Border who reside in
the Brookhill division would have to
bring their milk upwards of two miles
down a mountain road to the main road.
If they were compelled to bring that milk
that distance and put it on lorries it
would be of no benefit whatsoever to them
because of the costly cartage. The
nearest creamery in Northern Ireland
would be about eight miles distant, and
therefore they would gain no advantage
from the scheme. I submit that this is
a very important question.
I am
Take Termon creamery, which is built
on the Northern side of the Boundary.
Sixty per cent. of the suppliers of that
creamery reside in the Free State.
sure the right hon. Gentleman will not
be inclined to give those suppliers the
benefit of this particular grant in this
Bill. The result will be that the
creamery will be wiped out. It could not
exist when 60 per cent. of the suppliers
would be withdrawn from the creamery.
I should like the Minister to give very
careful consideration to this matter.
Another important question which
arises is, how is this money to be paid?
Is it to be paid each month? Under the
existing system producers are paid on
the first day of each month, and they are
anxious to know if the right hon. Gentleman will pay on the first of the month or
how the money will be paid. According
to the Bill this grant will be paid and no
expenses will be deducted. I am quite
sure the right hon. Gentleman himself
took into consideration the fact that
expenses must follow even the payment of
this particular grant such as in the
issuing of cheques, etcetera. The question
is what per cent., if any, he will allow
to the creameries for the payment of this
particular money. Will it be by a special
cheque issued by itself apart altogether
from the monthly payments?
I trust the right hon. Gentleman will
take into consideration all these questions. First, there is that of the
creameries along the Border, because the
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATI
[Mr. McHugh]
people would find themselves in a
miserable position owing to their distance
from Northern Ireland creameries if
they were deprived of this benefit. I am
sure the Minister, who knows the Border
well and resides convenient to it, will give
these matters his most serious consideration. I hope he will also consider the
suggestion that offices shall be erected on
the Northern side for the purposes of the
inspection of milk which shall be received
at those Free State creameries and where
a true account will be given which will
satisfy the inspector or
any special
instructions that may be issued. I have
great pleasure in supporting this Bill.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
would like to support the Second Reading
of this Bill because, as far as I can see,
it is going to benefit the farming class
particularly, whilst it will also be of great
benefit to the working class in the city of
Belfast and in the other towns of
Northern Ireland. The farmers certainly
deserve all the assistance possible because
they have had a very difficult time to eke
out an existence. Then the town dweller
is anxious to see the farmer doing well.
The principal part of the Bill, in my
opinion, is that in which an attempt is
made to deal with that terrible scourge,
tubercular disease, which is very
prevalent in congested cities like Belfast,
and which has taken away such a large
number of citizens. The Belfast City
Council has been referred to. They have
done a considerable amount of work in
trying to ensure a supply of pure milk.
For the last thirty or forty years the
City Council have been engaged in this
work. One man who stands out prominently in my memory is Dr. O'Neill.
Long before it was admitted that tubercular diseases were preventible he urged
that there should be a better examination
of the milk and a better supply of pure
milk.
There is no doubt that to-day the
Belfast Corporation have a
very fine
system of milk inspection so that milk of
the best quality may be supplied to the
citizens. I can readily understand how
they feel in regard to this matter. They
have a number of very capable officials
who are examining not only the milk but
the cowsheds, and they have veterinary
surgeons who are examining the cattle.
Then surely it is not impossible for the
Ministry of Agriculture to deal in a
sympathetic way with a Corporation who
have such a fine system and such capable
officers. I do not see how it is impossible
to utilise those officers in carrying out the
legislation which is under consideration
to-day.
The cowsheds of the city of Belfast are
examined four times a year, but the Bill
requires them only to be examined twice.
Why should there be any slackness in that
respect after the very particular way in
which they have been examined in the
past? Why should that system not be
continued? It has been urged to-day
that Belfast should be left out
the operation of this Bill. That is
impossible because what handicapped the
Belfast Corporation for the past twenty
or thirty years was the fact that they
were confined within the boundaries of
the city.
of
They were not able to go outside the
boundary to examine the cowsheds from
which the milk came. They were not
able to go out and examine the cattle
which supplied the milk. In those circumstances it would be impossible to leave
the city of Belfast or any town in the
Province out of this Bill. If Belfast was
left out Derry would very probably claim
the same treatment, as would also Ballymoney, Ballymena, and other places. I
do not know where the line would be
drawn. It is, therefore, altogether out
of the question that Belfast should be left
out.
I do think this is one of the finest Bills
that have been introduced into this House
for a long time. It is a Measure that is
going to benefit the poorer class people to
a great extent as they have not hitherto
been able to obtain a very reliable supply.
The Corporation have done their best.
We have some firms in Belfast who sell
pasteurised milk and endeavour to supply
it in as good a condition as possible. But
I believe this Bill will be the crowning
matter and that in the future we are
likely to have a very good supply of milk,
and, furthermore, we will be satisfied that
the farmer is not being pauperised in
supplying the milk.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I do
not rise to ask on behalf of Derry that
we should be exempted from the inspection of milk-shops, cowsheds, and so on,
because I realise that we are legislating

B milk. I would also suggest, in the case
of people who have to put up cooling
plants and to invest in bottles, that they
also might be allowed a little time to
switch over. After all, those people will
have to expend capital, whereas the milk
vendors will have really no capital

here for the community and not for any
particular section or place. I would like
to congratulate the Minister of Agriculture on introducing a Bill which I feel
sure will directly benefit the farming community and indirectly the urban
community. I have a great many notes
which I made on the Bill, but for the
sake of the Members of this House, for
my own sake, and for that of everybody
concerned I do not intend to mention
them all.
There are just one or two matters I
should like to refer to, seeing we have the
Minister of Agriculture here. I mention
them in the spirit not of asking for any
specific promise or amendment-I do not
intend to table any Amendments because
I am satisfied the Bill as a whole is a
good one but that one or two points
may receive, what I am sure they will get,
the sympathetic consideration of
of the
Ministry because, after all, it is their
object, as it is ours, to bring in a Bill
which will be of use.
There is no doubt that at first blush
one is forced to the conclusion that the
producers of the 30,000,000 gallons of
liquid milk will appear to be taxed for
the benefit of the producers of the
70,000,000 gallons of manufacturing
milk. I do not propose to argue the pros
and cons, but I just throw out the
suggestion that it does appear they are
bearing a little more than their share of
the burden. I would like to put in a
plea for the Grade C man. I would ask
that he might be treated at first with a
certain amount of lenience, that is, that
he may be given time so that he may
adjust himself to the position by degrees.
It seems to me that this is going to be
a very heavy burden on him in the first
place, and although I fully sympathise
with the intention of the Ministry that
we should ultimately get rid of Grade C
milk and have nothing worse than Grade
B, I can see it is very difficult to change
overnight. At the same time, the levy
of 3d is very heavy. Take the producer
of 75 gallons of Grade C milk daily. That
will mean a levy of £342 per annum.
That is rather a heavy sum for a farmer
of that size to have to pay as a levy
to-day when he is supposed to be in need
of help. I would seriously suggest that it
might be adjusted in some way, or, at all
events, that he might be given time in
which to change from Grade C to Grade
There are a great many other points,
but they are minor ones, to which I am
sure the Ministry are alive. But last of
all I would like to make a plea for one
section of the dairying farmers, that is
those who produce milk and buttermilk
for sale retail. The £200,000 is undoubtedly and admittedly a sum for the
benefit of dairy farmers. It has been
stated that one of the reasons why it was
granted was because of subsidised articles
coming in from other countries and
swamping the producer here. One subsidised article that is coming in here,
and that is being sold extremely cheaply, is
butter, and one would assume that it would
be only fair that the producer of butter
should get his share of this £200,000. I
do not say that he should get anything to
which he is not entitled, but he should
get his share. After all, buttermilk is
still a very much used commodity in
many parts of our Province, and butter
too. At all events, it is a dairy product which has not been ruled out of
order as a food fit for human consumption, and I would appeal to the
Minister to see that that class of dairy
farmer gets his share. I do not ask for
anything more. I congratulate the
Minister on bringing in the Bill, which
shall certainly have my support.

Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
would like to give my hearty support
to this Bill. It will go a long way to
remove the trouble in regard to our milk
supply. With reference to what hon.
Members opposite have said about the
city of Belfast, it is quite true that
Belfast is entirely different from any
other town in Northern Ireland.
have given years of thought to this very
troublesome question and have done a
lot of good in that direction. But all
along the trouble has been, as far as
milk from the outside areas has been
concerned, that, when we sought powers.
to examine the cowsheds in those areas,
we were always met not only by individuals but by authorities who said,
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Sir C. McCullagh]
"You have no right to come and examine
the places where the milk is produced.'
(An hon Member: Hear, hear.) Surely
the people who use the milk and pay
for it have a right to know how it is
produced. Surely that right should be
common to any authority. This Bill, as
I understand it, gets over that difficulty,
and one authority is to be set up to deal
with the whole question of milk production.
In

As far as the Belfast Corporation is
concerned I cannot see how you are to
leave it out of this Bill. I do not see
how it could be done, for it would mean
in reality setting up two authorities, and
that would not be a good thing.
my opinion there should be one authority
over all. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
But there is just this to be said.
the Corporation have gone to great expense and have an organisation second
to none that is in their own localityit would be a great pity if the right hon.
Gentleman were to scrap that organisation. Advantage could be taken of that
organisation and it could be used, not
as against the Government at all, but, on

nothing but the purest milk comes into
