The
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I am in
a somewhat different position to any of
the hon. Members who have hitherto
addressed the House on the subject of
this Bill. I think everybody who has
spoken so far has said that he rose to
support the Bill. I am afraid I am in
the position of not knowing whether I
am going to support it or not.
decision of that possibly unimportant
detail will depend to a great extent on
what the Minister of Agriculture tells
us when he rises to wind up the debate.
The first difficulty that occurs to me in
looking over the Bill-I have looked at
it rather hastily, perhaps, because, as
the House knows, during the last few
days we have had rather a spate of Bills,
some of them of some complexity, and
it has not been very easy to find time
to consider all the details involved in a
Measure of this class-but the thing
that seems to me to stand out in this
Bill is this, and indeed I think the noble
Viscount admitted it. The Bill seems
to me to be entirely in the interests of
those milk producers who send their milk
to creameries. Something is said about
a grant, or something in the nature of a
grant, at all events a payment, from the
Exchequer of the United Kingdom to
Northern Ireland. There is nothing in
the Bill, as far as I can see, as to the
amount of that payment. It is generally
understood that it is to be £200,000.
There is nothing in the Bill, as far as
I can see, as to whether that payment,
if it materialises, is to be treated as a
gift or as a loan, or if it is to be a loan
how it is to be repaid. That is one
point to which I think the right hon.
Gentleman should address himself.
Furthermore, if I understand the Bill
correctly, any money that finds its way
into the Milk Fund, which it is proposed to establish, and into which it is
proposed to pay any of this £200,000
that may be available, is apparently to
be used primarily in payments in respect
of milk supplied to creameries. Unless
the producer sends his milk to a
creamery apparently he is to get nothing
whatever out of the £200.000 or out of
the Milk Fund. That is how it would
appear, and one naturally wants to know
if that is the intention. Moreover,
apparently the man who does not send
his milk to a creamery is to pay certain
levies, which have already been alluded
to, and apparently they also are to go
into the Milk Fund. What is going to
happen to them? Is any of this money
coming back to the producer of milk
who does not send his milk to a
creamery? That seems to me to be a
very important matter.
Apart from that again, a good deal
has been said about Belfast. The noble
Viscount, in fact, laid stress on the importance to the city of Belfast of obtaining a regular supply of milk, apparently
paying for it a price which would reimburse the farmer and not be subject to
undercutting competition. That would
be very well for Belfast. I quite agree
that Belfast does require milk on account
of the large number of children,
and adults as well, who live in the
city. But Belfast is not at all the
only pebble on the beach. There
are other districts where there are
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1934
children, and those children also require
milk. There are throughout Northern
Ireland sundry small communities,
scarcely large enough to be called
villages, and they require a certain
amount of milk. In days not so very
long ago when the creameries were paying better than they are now the complaint was always made that directly it
became advantageous to send the milk to
the creamery all the milk produced
locally was sent to the creamery and
there was none left for sale to the
cottagers and their children, neither of
whom could be termed farmers. There
is a special sub-clause in the Bill which
will exempt the farmer who sells his
milk to his own servants, but there are
a great many other people who draw
milk from small suppliers and whose interests ought to be considered.
I am
speaking now rather more of the country
even than of the country towns, certainly more than I am speaking of
Belfast. Again, one wants to know
whether a farmer who is going to sell
milk to his servants under sub-clause (7)
of Clause 1 will require any form of
licence, and, if so, what he is to pay
for it.
I
In fact it seems to me that the
creameries will come very well out of the
proposals in the Bill, but I am not satisfied that the same will apply to the small
local distributor, if I may venture to
use the word in that connection. It is
really a little difficult to find an appropriate term, because the right hon.
Gentleman has used up all the language
and has used the term "distributor" in
this Bill in rather a special sense.
refer now to the personal retailer rather
than the man who has a dairy shop or
anything of that sort, the very small
man who deals with a very limited
clientele. That man again is not going
to set up a pasteurising plant, or any
other form of plant, and he is not going
to put his milk in a bottle if he can
help it. And I may digress for a
minute to point out that there seems to
be a popular impression that all milk
called Grade A or Grade B is to be sold
in bottles. That is not so. It is to be
sold in bottles or containers of approved
design-not by any means the
thing. There does not seem on the face
of it any reason why a certain amount
of milk should not be sold out of tin
same
cans, properly washed of course, as at
present. The whole thing seems to me
to point to the danger of creating a milk
famine in the country districts, and that
is a danger against which Parliament
ought to be very careful to provide, and
to provide adequately.
Something has been said about Clause
5 establishing the Joint Milk Council.
Certainly that Clause does contain a
very extraordinary provision, but,
having regard to what was said as to the
intention to amend it, I do not propose
to comment on that particular Clause
this afternoon. One sees, of course,
something of the difficulties that must
arise. The method adopted to overcome
those difficulties is, perhaps, unusual,
although, curiously enough, it strikes me
as being not very different in principle
from certain Parliamentary arrangements of our own which may be called
into action if there happens to be a
disagreement between the two Houses.
There really is something in the nature
of a second chamber for this Joint Milk
Council provided in the persons of these
three Ministerial nominees. However,
we may leave that until the Amendments
to the Clause are before us..
There remains the point to which, no
doubt, the right hon. Gentleman will
direct his attention, in connection with
sub-clause (4), of Clause 4, alluded to
hon.
by my
Friend (Sir Dudley
McCorkell) in front. I am afraid he
will find that Clause, or a very similar
Clause, in a good many of the Bills
promoted by the Ministry of Agriculture. I do not, of course, myself
approve of it for reasons which the
House thoroughly understands, but at
the same time I am afraid there are
occasions when you must leave matters in
rather a loose condition, and I do not
know whether it would be worth while
or practicable to establish any form of
appeal from the decision not to renew
a licence. I cannot at the moment say
how far back in the history of
the Ministry the power goes, but it goes
back some way, and I am bound to say,
although I do not approve of the
principle, that I have not yet heard of
any case in which the powers have been
abused. I am bound to say that, in
fairness to the Ministry.
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
I do not think there is anything else
I can usefully say on the Second Reading, but I should like to have some
definite assurance that the small countryman who is going to sell liquid milk is
going to get something out of the
£200,000, if it is £200,000, and that he
is going to have ample time to set his
house in order if it is decided that his house
must be set in order. I am glad to notice
in one Clause at the end of the Bill a not
unusual power to bring this part of the
Bill into operation on some appointed
day. I am not at all sure that it might
not help if we were to provide that the
appointed day in this respect should not.
be earlier than the 1st day of January,
1936, or some moderately distant date,
which would give ample time and also
allow opportunity for consideration of
any representations made from the
country districts respecting the Bill, for
since this Bill was introduced in another
place I do not think there has been anything approaching reasonable time for
discussion in the rural districts.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to congratulate the Minister of Agriculture on
this Bill. I believe that the health and
well-being of men, women, and children
depend a great deal on the milk which
they receive while they are young.
I have
spoken on the milk question before in this
House, and I am very glad to see
embodied in this Bill some of the points
that I have raised. I hope that when the
Bill is put into operation there will be the
strictest supervision to ensure that the
various provisions are carried out to the
very letter, and that there will be nothing
slipshod in the administration of the
Measure in the city of Belfast, in towns,
or in the country districts generally.
Sometimes Acts of Parliament read very
well, but somehow or other there
always some part that can be evaded.
hope for the sake of the rising generation
that there will be no evasion in this case.

I
I should like to know how the Ministry
propose to deal with the farmer who keeps
five or six cows, and takes the milk night
and morning into a town or district for
sale. Is he supposed to put up some kind
of apparatus for the purpose of sterilising
or pasteurising the milk? If he is, that
will involve him in a good deal of
expense, and perhaps what he gets for his
milk will not cover the outlay.
The suggestion has been made here this
afternoon that Belfast Corporation should
be exempt from this Bill. I would not be
in favour of that at all. I say that the
Ministry of Agriculture should have
supervision over the Belfast Corporation,
so far as milk is concerned. As someone
has said here to-day, this should be
managed from the one source. In that
way things would be a great deal better
done than if the supervision was carried
out by one or two different authorities.
I do not think that twice a
year is
sufficient for the examination of the
cattle, if it is desired to keep the milk
pure. It is possible for an inspector to
come round this week and the cattle to
pass the test, but perhaps in a fortnight's
or three weeks' time if he came round
again he would find one or two cows
suffering from disease. At this season of
the year, when cattle are on the grass, if
a thunderstorm occurred and a cow got a
thorough drenching, there is every
probability of its developing tuberculosis.
If the inspector only goes round every
six months the milk from that cow might
be going in among the rest for two, three,
or four months and be distributed among
the people.
cow,
Then about the price got for a diseased
I understand from what I have
heard to-day that only £4 is given. I
was wondering if some system of
insurance could not be brought into
existence. Farmers who produce milk on
a large scale, or even on a small scale,
could insure their cows against this
disease, and in the case of the disease
occurring they could make application to
the insurance company, hand the cow
over to the company, and get the price of
the cow.
The trouble is this that, when
a cow is found to be diseased, she is
taken to the market or the fair and sold,
with the result that the milk is still going
out to the public. We must wipe out
diseased cattle altogether, and that is the
only way to do so, by compensating
through some scheme of insurance the
farmers who own the diseased cows. I
have great pleasure in supporting the
Second Reading of this Bill.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I hope that the
inspection provided under this Bill will
not be left in the hands of the local
boards. I find that it is very hard to get
those bodies to carry out any reforms
that are suggested. There is a great deal
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
13 JUNE 1934
of wire-pulling in many boards. The milk
producers have friends on those boards, and
the result is that the desired reforms are
not carried out. I sincerely trust that in
future the inspection will be carried out
by the Ministry of Agriculture.
One thing that struck me in connection
with this Bill is that I would not like to
see the small producer thrown overboard.
I am thinking of a producer living, say,
on the outskirts of a town and distributing milk to a number of friends or
customers of his own. He also makes his
own butter, which is certainly of the very
best quality, and distributes buttermilk. I
should like to see that man protected, and
not crushed out. I have personal
experience of this matter. My milk is
supplied by a farmer who also makes his
own butter, which is certainly of the very
best quality, and distributes buttermilk. I
certainly hope that so far as that producer is concerned as much protection as
possible will be given to him, without
putting him to too much trouble and
expense. I have great pleasure in supporting the Second Reading of this Bill,
and I hope it will have the desired effect.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I do not wish
to detain the House, as what I had
intended to say has already been said
very much better than I could do it.
Senator McAllister has spoken about the
arbitrary powers to be given to three
representatives of the Department on the
Joint Milk Council. I quite agree with
him that their position would be unfair
to the remainder of the members representing the producers, the distributors,
and consumers, and that altogether the
proposal is unreasonable.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast has spoken.
of the desire of the representatives of the
city to send into the country for the purpose of inspecting the dairies which
supply milk to Belfast. We have
inspectors of our own in the County of
Down, and we will strongly object to a
veterinary surgeon coming from Newtownards to inspect our dairies, and then an
inspector from Belfast doing the same
thing. That is the reason why we have
always been opposed to the Belfast
inspectors coming into the country, where
we have our own inspectors.
The other matters to which I had
intended to refer have already been dealt
with. The price given for a diseased cow
is three-quarters of the value of the cow,
and a smaller price for an emaciated
animal. The question of tuberculosis in
cattle is a very thorny one. It has been
considered very frequently. A cow that
has been sent down as affected with tuberculosis might give perfectly healthy milk.
If cows suffering in some degree from
tuberculosis were to be ruled out, I am
afraid 50 per cent of the COWS in
Northern Ireland would be affected. A
cow might not pass the tubercular test
but it does not follow that her milk would
be of a tubercular character.
The question of tuberculosis in milk depends on
the condition of the udder. This debate
has gone on so long that I do not wish to
detain the House any longer.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
agree with the previous speaker that the
powers given to the Ministry of Agriculture under this Bill seem to be far too
extreme.
know
We all
the present
Minister of Agriculture, and we have
every confidence in him, but we do not
know who will occupy the position in the
future, and I think it is a pity that those
extreme powers should be vested in the
Department. There is another point on
which I should like to say a few words.
A friend of mine in England tells me that
the milk produced there is examined
regularly. If it is tainted, the milk is
traced to its source, and the diseased cow
is rooted out as soon as it is found. I
presume that the same thing will happen
here. If the inspector finds that certain
milk is tainted, he traces it to its source,
and has the affected cow rooted out. My
friend assures me that within a very short
time the diseased cow is traced and wiped
out. I think this Measure is a good one,
and I hope that with certain Amendments it will be passed.
Mr. QUIGLEY: This interesting
debate has been so prolonged that I do
not desire to take up much of the time of
the House, but I certainly was very
forcibly struck by the admission of
Senator Hill Dickson that some 50 per
cent. of our cattle suffer from tubercular
trouble. That is a very alarming statement. Surely it is not a fact? There is
the greatest necessity for this Bill in view
of that statement. Reference was made
here to-day by some of our Senators to
the late Dr. Henry O'Neill. I knew him
very well, and I am quite sure that had
Milk Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
it been possible for him to be here to-day
he would have supported the Bill which
we are delighted to have before the House
to-day. I am delighted that the Minister
of Agriculture is here when this very
important Bill, which will have a great
effect not only on the city of Belfast but
on the Six Counties, is being considered.
I have great pleasure in supporting the
Bill.
The MINISTER OF AGRICUL-
TURE (Sir Basil Brooke): May I first
of all thank the Senators for the manner
in which they have received this Bill.
I fully realise that a Bill of this sort
is open to criticisms from all angles,
because, after all, we are doing something which is entirely novel. We have
not even got an example from England
to work on. The scheme which we are
putting forward in Northern Ireland is
entirely different, and is based on the
Grigg report. Hon. Senators are
probably aware that in England milk is
paid for at a definite level rate, whereas
here, although it is not actually paid
for, the levy is less the higher the class of
milk. The object, of course, is to improve or to induce the farmers to improve and clean up their byres.
Now, one of the criticisms which have
been made on this Bill is the constitution
of the committee, and, of course, it can
be argued in different ways as to how that
committee could have been set up. I might
point out to hon. Senators that in England the producers' committee and the
distributors' committee were unable to
come to an agreement as to price and
outside arbitrators were brought in who,
as far as I understand, were unable to
satisfy either of the interests. So that
we
are confronted with this problem:
We are either to wait until the producers and distributors fail to reach an
agreement and then call in arbitrators,
or we are to set up arbitrators as part
of the committee to decide as to the
price, because-I do not think this is
the time to mention the actual Amendment here--the Amendment will show
clearly to the House that the arbitrators
only function as arbitrators where it
becomes a question of the price of milk.
On no other matter in connection with
the discussion of the board will they
arbitrate.
see
It seems to me that if these arbitrators
are present and have heard the discussion of the various members of the
board, they will
where some are
bluffing and where some are merely
making a bargaining point, and they are
more likely to arrive at a just and
proper conclusion if they have been
present through the whole course of the
discussion of the question. I admit
quite freely, as hon. Senator Leslie
mentioned, that it does perhaps appear a
second chamber. I think, possibly, that
is the function the arbitrators will
occupy. Obviously one has to have an
arbitrator. If the two interests concerned failed to come to any agreement
the scheme could not function, because
no price would be fixed, and therefore
none of the various consequences of the
scheme would work until they had come
to an agreement.
Therefore hon. Senators will agree
with me that though the method is novel
-after all, the whole scheme is novelit will work. May I say that if this
scheme is set up, it is, after all, new
machinery, and obviously in the position
I have the honour to hold, if I find the
machinery is not working properly it is
my business to pull out the bits which
do not work and put in some that will.
That is the way we have to make these
things. They are not final, but they are
the best way, so far as we can see, to
benefit not only the producer but the
consumer as well.
Various Senators referred to the
question of whether the Corporation or
the city of Belfast could not be
exempted. I am afraid that would be
impossible. In the working of the
scheme the producers of milk must be
the first persons in any inspection that
takes place.
It is necessary for the
Minister's veterinary officer inspecting
these premises to see that they are kept
in a proper and fit state for the supply
of milk. It will be quite obvious that
if we have divided authority the results
would be disastrous and the ends sought
would not be achieved.
Hon. Senator McAllister made some
references to the losses which are incurred in this country to the extent of
some £200,000 in paying for wheat. Of
course it can be argued that we have
really nothing to thank Great Britain
for in getting the £200,000. At least we
Milk Bill-
Second Reading.
13 JUNE 1934
can say we are getting the money back,
which is always something. I regret
very much we are unable to help those
Northern Ireland farmers who are
supplying the Free State creameries. It
would be obvious that the sum of money,
the basis of the whole of this scheme,
is the £200,000 which has been lent, and
I will explain later on the method of
repayment. That money has been
definitely lent to Northern Ireland milk
producers, and it would therefore be
quite impossible even in a remote way to
benefit a creamery which is not situated
in Northern Ireland. I think I am
right in saying that in the Free State
there is a slight bonus paid to producers of milk, rather higher than has
been paid in Northern Ireland. The
same thing of course applies to Free
State milk which is coming into Northern Ireland. These producers will not
receive any benefit either. Again the
same thing applies. The £200,000 is for
the
of
area
Northern Ireland. A
creamery manager who will produce his
books once a month showing the amount
of milk brought to his creamery will
pay these producers in Northern Ireland
according to the calculation bringing it
up to 5d a gallon in the summer and 6d
in the winter. With regard to the
question of the
the repayment
of the
£200,000, if in the year 1935-36 the
economic price of milk reaches 6d in the
summer and 7d in the winter--
Mr. LESLIE: Where
that?
do you find
Sir BASIL BROOKE--insomuch as
that price exceeds 6d in summer and 7d
in winter, that amount will be repaid
and will form a fund to repay the loan.
Mr. LESLIE: May I interrupt? Do
we find that in the Bill about the repayment of the £200,000?
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I think it is
in Clause 12 of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Is that all there is?
Sir BASIL BROOKE: That, I may
say, is the only liability which we have
got in the matter. With regard to
tubercular cows, a matter raised by the
hon. Senator, Colonel Pakenham, should
a
man report his animal as being
tubercular and that animal is not in an
advanced stage, he gets paid threequarters of the value of the animal. If
he waits or delays too long he gets the
sum the hon. Senator mentioned. While
I am on the subject of tuberculosis, I
think it was Senator Hill Dickson
mentioned the percentage of diseased
animals. On an investigation in England, it may be of interest to the House
to know, although a very considerable
number of animals are affected, there is
only about 4.7 per cent. of the milk
affected, because the affection of the milk
depends on whether the tuberculosis is
seated in the udder or in the bowels. I
think it should not go out that 40 per
cent. of the animals are affected, and
therefore 40 per cent. of the milk.
There was some uneasiness expressed
because it appeared that the creameries
were going to get all the benefit from
this scheme. I think the Leader of the
House in his opening statement
mentioned the fact that in England 70
per cent. of the producers of milk are
on the liquid market and 30 per cent.
on the manufacturing market. In this
country it is exactly the reverse
practically to a figure. Thirty per cent.
of the producers are on the liquid side,
and 70 per cent. on the manufactured
side. I think it will be apparent to all
hon. Senators that unless some fund was
produced to help the creameries it would.
be impossible for the liquid producer to
carry the creamery on his back, and if
the creamery was not financed in some
way it would be quite obvious in fact
it has happened already, owing to the
low price amounting to 2d a gallon, it
would be quite apparent that many of
these producers who have heretofore
been supplying the creameries would
tend to bring their milk on to the market
and compete with the legitimate, genuine
liquid milk producer in Belfast and
Londonderry. The result would be that
although in summer there would be a
plentiful supply of milk to the consumer
the fact of this cheap milk coming into
the market would put the genuine man
out of business and he would be unable
to supply milk during the winter
months. So that I think the first matter
we have to consider in evolving any Bill
is, first of all, the creameries must be
put on their feet.
Potato Marketing Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Sir Basil Brooke]
Now, the £200,000 loan is the basis
on which the creameries are placed on
their feet and on which the whole scheme
is based, and the levy which has been
mentioned is in all cases a maximum
levy. But it does not necessarily mean
that the farmer is going to receive only
the present price for his milk and still
have to pay the levy. The price which
will be fixed-it would be impossible to
anticipate what the fixing of the price
will be the Milk Board which will be set
up, I am sure, will make quite definite
provision that the producer of liquid
milk, even after paying the levy, will be
in a better position than at present. I
cannot state what the amount will be.
That will be one of the functions of the
Milk Board. I think that covers most
of the points that were raised.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Finance Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved: "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That the Bill do now pass."
-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree
to the said Bill without
any Amendment.
Marketing of Potatoes Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: A Potato
Marketing Scheme is now in operation
in Great Britain and it, therefore,
becomes necessary that a complementary
scheme should be in force in Northern
Ireland in time for the next potato
harvest. Fortunately, the foundations
have been well and truly laid by the
Potato Marketing Act passed by this
House in 1928.
That Measure, I am glad to say, has
been most successful and has, even in the
last couple of years of depression, enabled
us to maintain our position on the
British Market. We have, for example,
been able to sell our crop in Lancashire
and elsewhere across the water when local
growers were unable to market their
supplies. The reason is, of course, that
by the Act of 1928 our exports are
graded. Therefore, bad though
our
potato trade has been recently it would
have been much worse but for the legislative measures promoted in this Parliament six years ago.
New developments are, however, now
taking place in Great Britain so that our
machinery needs tightening up if we are
to retain the position already captured.
I am informed that our potato export
business is, on the whole, well conducted
except that there are a number of what I
might call part-time shippers whose
activities are an injury to the industry as
a whole and probably of comparatively
little benefit to these shippers themselves.
Casual exporters prejudice the trade by
quoting cut
cut prices in cross-Channel
markets with which our regular shippers
cannot compete except at a loss, and this
uneconomic price-cutting must, of course,
ultimately be paid for by the Northern
Ireland farmer in the form of reduced
prices.
to
In the Bill now before the House we
propose to discourage the incursions of
these irregulars by increasing the licence
fee to £5 per annum, and it is also proposed that at the commencement of each
season a shipper must make a deposit up
a maximum of £15 in respect of
inspection fees. The charge of 6d a ton
for inspection is, however, not being
increased so that an exporter of 600 tons
a year or over, while paying an extra
annual licence charge, will not be paying
any more for inspection. I may say also that
no shipper will be refused a licence who
is willing to pay the requisite fees and to
comply with the conditions laid down.
One of the great drawbacks of the
potato export trade in the past has been
that we have had periodic slumps due to
satiated markets. The British authorities
are tackling the problem of over-production
by, among other things, limiting the
acreage to be grown by each farmer. It
is not proposed in Northern Ireland to
321 Potato Marketing Bill-

13 JUNE 1934
Second Reading
limit the acreage because we do not consider such a course either practicable or
desirable in this area. Apart from the
difficulty of administering any such law
its operation would be a very serious
handicap to feeders of livestock and
poultry. We must, however, be in a
position to co-operate with the British
Marketing authorities. so that, when
necessary, a united effort may be made to
prevent an overstocked market. The
Agricultural Marketing Act passed by
this Parliament already gives the power
to regulate the flow of agricultural produce from Northern Ireland to Great
Britain, so that with the Measure now
before the House the legislative machinery
will be complete so far as the potato
export trade is concerned.
I have dealt up to now only with the
export market, but a stage has arrived
when grading for the home market must
Representanecessarily be considered.
tions have from time to time been made
by my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Agriculture, that there should be grading
for the home as well as for the export
trade and, speaking generally, this argument has much force. There is also the
fact that potatoes can be sold in the
Belfast market without complying with
any standards of quality. The result is
that a low price standard emanates from
the Belfast market with a depressing
effect on potato prices generally throughout Northern Ireland. Therefore, both
from the consumers' and producers' point
of view it is highly desirable that, as far
as is practicable, standards should be
prescribed and enforced for potatoes sold
in the Belfast market and possibly in one
or two of the other local markets.
Experience will enable us to determine
how far such grading for the home market
can be extended.
I hope that the measures outlined,
coupled with what is being done in Great
Britain, will enable our producers to
obtain a more adequate return for their
labour, while at the same time benefiting
the consumer by giving him better value
for his money.
In conclusion, I wish to
assure the House that producers exporting potatoes grown by themselves will
continue to be granted free permits without any licence charge or any deposit in
respect of inspection fees. I beg to move,
"That this Bill be now read a Second
time."
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Bill because I
believe it will be of great benefit to the
farming community. The potato crop is
one of the principal crops the farmers of
Northern Ireland produce, and I think
this Bill will go a long way to enable
them to obtain a fair price. I would like
to take this opportunity of asking the
Minister of Agriculture if it is his intention to remove from certain districts in
Northern Ireland the ban that was placed
on them owing to their being made
scheduled areas as a result of outbreaks
of black scab some years ago. I understand there is such a district in County
Down, but the one I am more interested
in is the district of South Derry. It is a
very large district and there has been no
black scab found there during the last
nine years.
Nine years ago it was found
in two different places, at Tubbermore,
at a place called Black Hill, and
Minniemore. The two places are twelve
to fourteen miles apart. The scheduled
area at Minniemore comprises something
like thirteen or fourteen square miles.
at
The farmers in these scheduled areas
cannot bring their potatoes into Belfast
to sell, nor can they sell them over the
Border. In July of last year I visited a
farmer who told me he had all his
potatoes, about twenty tons, at his farm
and they would be a total loss. He said
that had he had the opportunity of taking
them to Belfast he could have turned
them into money. Therefore, this particular farmer practically lost his last
year's crop of potatoes. I would be very
glad if the right hon. Gentleman could
see his way to remove this ban so that
the farmers in these scheduled areas
might find a market at home for their
potatoes. I have great pleasure in
supporting this Bill.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I regret to say
that we cannot remove the ban from these
scheduled areas, because in England this
disease has been found in the ground for
as long as twenty years after the first
outbreak. To remove the ban and allow
potatoes which are liable to this disease
to come into the market might jeopardise
the whole position of the potato market.
I regret that I am not able to accede to
the request of the hon. Senator, but in
my opinion it would not be wise to do so.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
F
Adjournment
Road Traffic Bill-

THE SENATE
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The debate
on the Milk Bill has been rather
more protracted than I thought yesterday would be the case. In fact, the
time at our disposal has been milked
rather dry, and I was wondering whether
the House would like the Committee
Stage of the Motor Vehicles and Road
Traffic Bill to be postponed until tomorrow. It is true that yesterday I said
I thought this House would not sit tomorrow, but I did not foresee the trend
of the debate on the Milk Bill. I am
willing to agree to whatever the House
decides, but I would like to give Members the opportunity of saying whether
they would like to postpone the Committee Stage of the Motor Vehicles and
Road Traffic Bill until to-morrow, or
proceed with it now.
Hon. MEMBERS: Adjourn.
The Committee Stage was put off until
to-morrow.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, ""That this House do now
adjourn."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
five minutes before Five
o'clock.

Mr. McMAHON: Arising out of that
reply, I desire to ask is it not time the
Government relieved the Belfast Corporation of this very heavy burden in
connection with the cost of the police.
Other municipalities in Northern Ireland have nothing, so far as I know, to
contribute to the cost of the police, and
why Belfast should be singled out for
this heavy additional burden passes my
comprehension. Before the passing of
the 1920 Act the Imperial authorities.
gave a certain quota of free police to
the city of Belfast. Since the passing
of the 1920 Act that has been entirely
done away with so far as the Government are concerned, with the result that
Belfast Corporation have to impose a
heavy burden to the extent of 6d in
the pound upon the citizens for
the purpose of the upkeep of the police,
and judging from indications that have
been made by Ministers in another place
we are likely to be called upon to bear
an additional burden. I do appeal to
the Government to hesitate before they
place any further burdens on the ratepayers, who are already suffering very
severely at the present time.

Order for the House to be put into

In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Penalties for reckless or
dangerous driving).
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
(1) Section one of the Motor
Vehicles (Traffic and Regulation) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1926 (in this
Act referred to as the "principal
Act") shall have effect as if the followsub-section were substituted for subsection (1) thereof :-
"(1) If any person drives a motorcar on a public highway recklessly
or at a speed or in a manner which
is dangerous to the public, having
regard to all the circumstances of
the case, including the nature, condition and use of the highway, and
the amount of traffic which is
actually at the time or which might
reasonably be expected to be, on the
highway, he shall be liable-
(a) on summary conviction to a fine
of one pound or any greater sum
not exceeding ten pounds, or in
the case of a second conviction to
a fine of two pounds or any
greater sum not exceeding twenty
pounds, or in the case of a third
or subsequent conviction to a fine
of five pounds or any greater sum
not exceeding fifty pounds, or, in
the discretion of the court, in the
case of any conviction under this
section to imprisonment for a
period not exceeding three months
in addition to or in lieu of any
such fine aforesaid;
(b) on conviction on indictment to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years, or to a fine, or to
both such imprisonment and fine.
On the trial of any indictment
for manslaughter in connection with.
the driving of a motor-car, the jury
may acquit the defendant of manslaughter and find him guilty of an
offence under this section."
(2) Where a person is convicted of
an offence under sub-section (1) of
section one of the principal Act, as
amended by this Act, and a period of
three years or more has elapsed since
his last previous conviction for a like
offence, the court may, if it thinks
fit, deal with such first-mentioned
offence as a first offence under the said
sub-section, and where the court so
deals with such first-mentioned offence,
paragraph (i) of the proviso to subsection (1) of section four of the principal Act shall have effect accordingly.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
2, to leave out lines 26 to 30 inclusive
("On the trial of any indictment . .
under this section'). The object of the
Amendment is in effect an invitation to
the Committee to continue, and to make,
perhaps, rather more effective the good.
work which has already been done by
the insertion of the four preceding lines,
lines 21 to 25. The present position is,
as the Committee will remember, that
following our Act of 1929 a person may
be charged either with manslaughter or
with doing serious bodily harm by means
of a motor-car, and if he is so charged
the jury may acquit him of manslaughter
or doing or causing bodily harm and find
him guilty of driving recklessly, and so
on. That is a position which has always
been recognised as somewhat illogical,
but I think Parliament consented to it
on account of the practical impossibility
of getting convictions for manslaughter
in these unhappy cases. Why it is so
difficult to get a conviction for manslaughter I really do not know.
always thought myself that the jury may
have in their minds the fact that usually
in the final stage of the disaster the
motorist makes some attempt to avoid
the catastrophe, and to them the making
of that attempt absolves him from the
felony of manslaughter.
I
We are now proceeding on what seems
to me proper lines, that is, we are enabling a man definitely to be indicted
for the offence of reckless driving. That
is a great step. I rather hoped we
should have done that in 1929, but we
are doing it now, and the moment we do
that we take away the last vestige of
excuse for maintaining the illogical
course. The proper course is to indict
a man for manslaughter and also indict
him for dangerous driving. That could
be done on two indictments or on two
counts of the same indictment. The
question of separate indictments
separate counts is one for Mr. Attorney.
But if we leave things as they are, we
not only perpetuate the existing
absurdity but we create a new one for we
appear to be taking away, as the Committee will see if they look at Sub-clause
(2), the possibility of a man being charged
with causing bodily harm under the
Offences Against Persons Act and yet
being convicted of reckless driving.
or
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
14 JUNE 1934
The whole matter has recently been
considered in England. In England, of
course, they have no such provision as
we put in, that is, allowing a man to
be charged with manslaughter and to be
convicted of dangerous driving. They
have not got that, but they have Section
11 of their Act of 1930 which
for practical purposes reproduces Clause
1 on the same page of this Bill which
we are seeking to substitute for the
existing Clause in the Act of 1926. Now
it happened that a man was driving a
lorry, and we must assume driving
recklessly. There was a gang of workmen on the road and he was driving
towards them. From the opposite
direction came another lorry. The reckless driver made an attempt to avoid the
second lorry and crashed into the gang
of workmen and killed one of them.
He was, accordingly, indicted for manslaughter, and by another count in the
indictment for the offence of dangerous
driving. The jury acquitted him of
manslaughter and convicted him of reckless and dangerous driving.
He went to the Court of Criminal
Appeal-and for some reason I do not
recollect the Court of Criminal Appeal
sat twice on the ground that having
been acquitted of manslaughter he could
not be charged on the same facts with
the offence of dangerous driving. The
Court of Criminal Appeal was constituted of the Lord Chief Justice (Lord
Hewart), Sir Horace Avory, Sir Travers
Humphreys, Mr. Justice Charles, and
Mr. Justice Branson. I cannot imagine
a stronger court in criminal matters than
that. The Lord Chief Justice is one
of the most distinguished judges, and two
of the others have had great experience
as Treasury Counsel. I am going to read
the brief report from "The Times" newspaper of the judgment delivered by the
Lord Chief Justice:
"In regard to the two counts of the
indictment, it was to be remembered
that each count was a separate indictment and it was to be observed that
on the first count for manslaughter the
appellant could not have been convicted of dangerous driving under
Section 11. In other words, on the
charge of manslaughter the appellant
was never in jeopardy on the charge
under the Road Traffic Act, 1930, and
if there had been two separate indictments tried at separate times he could
not successfully have put forward a
plea of autrefois acquit. The court
had come to the conclusion that the
conviction must stand, but desired to
make two observations. In the opinion
of the court it was not desirable that
a charge under Section 11 of the Road
Traffic Act, 1930, should be made a
count in an indictment charging manslaughter. If the two charges were to
be made they should be made in two
separate indictments, and any confusion would then be avoided."
His Lordship went on to make other
observations, and reduced the sentence.
Bearing in mind that the common law
in Northern Ireland is the same as in
England we cannot do better than govern
ourselves in accordance with that authoritative decision of the Court of Criminal
Appeal, and the method I propose to do
that by is first of all to take these words
out of this Bill. There will be no necessity to
do anything more, although I shall have
a similar Amendment on the next Clause
where the reasons for the course I suggest
are slightly different, but we shall have to
take these words out of this Bill, and then
it will rest with Mr. Attorney whether to
indict a man simply for manslaughter or
simply for reckless driving, or by two
indictments or two counts for both
offences. I beg to move.
a
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I cannot accept this Amendment.
The provision in the Bill is, of course,
substantial re-enactment of Section 2 of
the Motor Vehicles Act, 1929, and my
right hon. Friend, the Attorney-General,
whose special responsibility these matters
are, informs me that he is satisfied that
on the whole the provision in the Act of
1929 has been useful in the administration
of the law.
It does not seem that this procedure,
which is convenient, puts the accused
person at any real disadvantage, as he is
aware beforehand that the jury has the
alternative of finding him guilty of manslaughter or of finding him guilty of
dangerous driving, and he is in a position
to put forward his defence fully. It is
extremely difficult to see what would be
the advantage to the accused of having
two separate indictments, so that if he
were acquitted on the first indictmentthe charge of manslaughter-he would
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
have to undergo a second trial on the
same evidence on a charge of dangerous
driving, which involves unnecessary and
useless expense without any corresponding benefit. Having given the procedure
a trial for four years, and having found
that it works with reasonable efficiency
and fairness, it would be unwise on the
part of the Government to make any
change.
Mr. LESLIE: I am always sorry to be in
any way obstructive, but, having regard to
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Londonderry, The Mayor of
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
the authoritative decision of the Court of
Criminal Appeal to which I have alluded,
I cannot even negatively consent to the
perpetuation of one absurdity and the
creation of another, the other being the
omission of the offence of causing bodily
harm. I must ask you to collect the
voices.
Question, "Leave out lines 26 to 30
inclusive.'
""
The Committee divided: Contents, 8;
Not Contents, 10.
CONTENTS
McHugh, Mr. J.
McLaughlin, Mr. T.
McMahon, Mr. J.
NOT CONTENTS
Cunningham, The Right Honourable
Samuel
Dickson, Mr. James H.
Dixon, Sir T. J.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the end
of sub-clause (2), to leave out the words,
"have effect accordingly," and insert
"not apply." This is a small matter
which is little more than a drafting
Amendment. It has been put up to me
by legal circles outside the House that the
words as they stand in the text are open
to misconstruction, because it is thought
that the paragraph referred to would have
some effect in this case, whereas, as a
matter of fact, it would have no effect at
all. I am not to be taken as meaning
that the difficulty is of any great substance, but it is better to meet it by
taking out the words, "have effect
accordingly," and inserting "not apply."
I may say it may be that we shall find
it necessary, at a later stage, to make
some trivial verbal alterations elsewhere in
the Bill if the Committee accepts this proposal, but that is a matter for the future,
and the bridge can very well wait until
we are about to get across it. I beg to
move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
Page 2, after line 40 insert as a new
sub-section:
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
Gyle, Mr. J.
Lavery, Mr. T. R.
Long, Mr. John A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
"(3) Section two of the Act of 1929
is hereby repealed."
Mr. LESLIE: I trust the Committee
will not think me unduly faddy in this
matter. The effect of Clauses 1 and 2 of
this Bill will be necessarily to require the
repeal both of Section 2 of the Act of
1929 and also Section 3. The Committee
will see, if they look at page 3, Clause 2,
sub-clause (2), that provision is made for
that, but it seems to me to be more in
accordance with our recent legislation that
the two Sections, 1 and 2, should stand on
their own legs and, therefore, they should
both be made complete. Therefore, I
suggest we should, so to speak, split the
proposed enactment in Clause 2, and
insert here so much of it as relates to the
Clause with which we are dealing. That
means simply saying here "Section 2 of
the Act of 1929 is hereby repealed," and
a little later on we shall say "Section 3
of the Act" instead of "Sections 2 and
3." I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: For the
reason detailed in the answer to the first
Amendment I am afraid I cannot accept
this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it would be
advisable that the Amendment should be
withdrawn now, but, perhaps, it might be
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee.

brought up again on Report, after one
has had time to consider the effect of what
the noble Viscount has said.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand

CLAUSE 2 (Penalties for driving
motor-car when under influence of drink
or drugs).
On the trial of any indictment for
manslaughter in connection with the
driving of a motor-car, the jury may
acquit the defendant of manslaughter
and find him guilty of an offence under
this section.
*
*
(2) Sections two and three of the Act
of 1929 are hereby repealed.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
2, to leave out lines 20 to 23 inclusivethe last part of paragraph (b). This is
to some extent the same question as that
which the Committee decided a few
minutes ago when they refused to take
out similar words in Clause 1. I am now
asking the Committee to take out the same
words in Clause 2, and the special reason
is that there is a different reason, perhaps,
for
the proposal on this occasion.
Practically everything I said, except the
reference to the Court of Criminal Appeal,
as regards the first proposal stands again,
but if we leave these words in here we
shall be perpetrating yet another
absurdity, because we shall be inviting a
jury to find a man guilty of being drunk
in charge of a motor-car when there may
be no evidence whatever that he was
drunk. A man indicted for manslaughter
may be acquitted and found guilty of the
offence of being drunk in charge. That
is an absurd possibility. If you want to
find a man guilty of being drunk in charge
why not indict him separately. We are
simply putting into the hands of jurymen
the possibility of returning an absurd
verdict. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I could not
possibly accept this Amendment after refusing the first Amendment moved by
the hon. and learned Senator. They
must stand or fall together and, therefore,
this Amendment, like the other, must fall.
Mr. LESLIE: I am really in a difficulty as to whether it is worth while to
leave the matter over for possible reconsideration. It is a technical point, as the
noble Viscount knows, and I will ask leave
to withdraw it for the present.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Clause 2, page 3, line 33, leave out the
line and insert, "Section three of the Act
of 1929 is."
Mr. LESLIE: This Amendment should
also stand over, because it belongs to the
one we spoke of a few minutes ago.
Amendment not moved.
CLAUSE ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Amendment of s. 4 of the
principal Act as to period of suspension
and disqualification).
(1) Section four of the principal Act
(which relates to suspension of licences,
and disqualification) shall have effect as
if in paragraph (i) of the proviso to
sub-section (1) thereof for the words
"three months,' where those words
occur in the said paragraph, there were
substituted the words "six months."
*
""
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out sub-section (1) of Clause 3. This is
really a matter of some difficulty. The
Committee may remember that when
speaking on the Second Reading I said
that I, myself, attached very much more
importance to the power of suspending
licences in the case of dangerous driving
than to almost anything else.
But we
have to consider matters very carefully for
fear lest, owing to the heavy toll of the
road, we should be driven into any form
of panic legislation. I think it is very
necessary that before rejecting this
Amendment the Committee should know
exactly what it is they are about to do.
The proposal in the sub-clause is in effect
that a court of summary jurisdiction in
the case of a second or subsequent conviction for the offence of dangerous
driving shall suspend a man's licence for
not less than six months. The existing
law is that the period of suspension shall
be for three months, and the proposal is
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
to make it obligatory on the resident
magistrate, sitting alone, to suspend for
six months. It is no use talking about
an appeal to the county court, because
there can be no appeal against the
sentence. There can be an appeal against
the conviction, but that is not the point.
If this Clause is allowed to stand the
court must, in the case of a second or
subsequent conviction, suspend the licence
for six months. The court may not have
before it any evidence as to what sort of
dangerous driving took place on the
earlier occasion, and the reason why, in
spite of my preference for the suspension
of a licence as against imprisonment or
even fine, is because, as the Committee
will see if they look back to line 6 on page
2 of the Bill, driving recklessly may
depend on the amount of traffic which
might reasonably be expected to be on the
highway. A man who drives recklessly
or is considered to drive recklessly when
there might, perhaps, be traffic on the
highway and there is not, is really only
guilty of an error of judgment. You cannot say that that is the same thing as
driving recklessly in a crowded street or
opposite a school. There is a distinct
possibility that a man may be unduly
punished for what is, in effect, a repetition
of an error of judgment. I do not think
that ought to be. We ought to leave
three months as the maximum which the
magistrates must impose, and that, of
course, is entirely without prejudice to his
power to impose six, twelve, or twenty
months, if he sees fit, in really serious.
cases.
The Committee will bear in mind, of
course, as I had myself in mind before
putting this Amendment on the Paper,
that the old offence of driving negligently,
has been taken out of the category of
dangerous driving. They will also, I
think, have regard to Sub-clause 2 of
Clause 1, which provides that after a
certain period, three years I believe it is,
without a conviction, a second offence may
be treated as a first. They will put both
those things into one side of the scales.
There remains the other. Is it right to
require a magistrate, whatever the circumstances may be, to impose such a severe
sentence as six months' suspension for
what may be merely the repetition of an
error of judgment? This is a matter on
which opinions may differ. It is a point
of great difficulty. I should certainly not
give support myself to a proposal that a
magistrate should not impose a. six
months' suspension if he thought so fit.
What I object to is his being required,
irrespective of the circumstances,
impose that sentence. I will never accept
the right of Parliament to prejudge a case
before it is heard. The proper course is
to leave out this Sub-clause.
to
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am sorry
It
I cannot accept this Amendment.
must be recognised that we have a very
serious problem to deal with in our efforts
to reduce the number of accidents upon
the roads, and this Bill is intended to and
does increase the penalties in various
for
respects
offences committed by
motorists. The hon. and learned Senator
who moves this Amendment stated on the
Second Reading and has again stated just
now, that he felt in many cases the most
appropriate penalty was the suspension of
licences and in this the Government agrees
with him. The persons who commit the
serious offences which entail the suspension of licences are the minority of the
motoring community and it is that
minority which is responsible for the
majority of the accidents which can be
laid at the door of motorists. The most
effective way of reducing the number of
accidents caused by these people is to prevent them from driving motor cars.
Government have given much consideration to this matter and they have come to
the conclusion that six months should be
the shortest period of suspension and I,
therefore, am unable to accept the
Amendment.
The
Mr. LESLIE : In the absence of
support I do not think I should press this
matter. I beg leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Penalties for drunkenness
in charge of horse-drawn vehicle or pedal
cycle).
If any person is found, when driving
or attempting to drive, or when in
charge of a vehicle drawn by a horse
or other animal, or when riding or
attempting to ride a pedal cycle, on any
highway or other public place, to be
under the influence of drink or drugs
to such an extent as to be incapable of
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
14 JUNE 1934
having proper control of the vehicle or
cycle, he shall be guilty of an offence
under the principal Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move at end of
Clause 4 to add: "provided that any
person liable to be charged with an offence
under this section shall not be liable to be
charged under section twelve of the
Licensing Act, 1872, with the offence of
being drunk while in charge on a highway
or other public place of an animal or
carriage.
""
The words I propose to add at the end
of this Clause are, of course, the same
words as are inserted as Sub-clause 2 of
Clause 2 on the preceding page. It seems
to me that if it is necessary to give a
drunken motorist a special protection we
cannot deny the same special protection to
the man dealt with in Clause 4 who is
driving a horse-drawn vehicle or riding
a pedal cycle. Speaking for myself I
should be perfectly prepared instead of
moving to add the words at the end of
Clause 4 to cut them out of Clause 2
because under the common law supported
by a well-known Section of the
Interpretation Act, a man cannot be
vexed twice for the same cause. But still
as we put the words in in one case I am
afraid I cannot see any reason, though
perhaps the noble Viscount may furnish
one, why we should not put them in in
the second case.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot,
as a matter of fact, adduce any reason,
and therefore I am prepared to accept this
Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 5 (Careless driving or
riding) and 6 (Provisions as to pedal
cycles) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Obstruction of traffic on
public highway).
If on any public highway any person
causes or permits any vehicle to stand
or remain in such a position as to cause
or to be likely to cause an obstruction
he shall be guilty of an offence under
the principal Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
7, page 4, line 32, after "be" ("or to
be") to insert "reasonably".

""
The proposal here is to insert the word
in front of the word
reasonably
likely
so as to make the Clause read:
"If on any public highway any person causes or permits any vehicle to
stand or remain in such a position as to
cause or to be reasonably likely to cause
an obstruction . . . "

""
I have got to confess I do not like that
Amendment myself very much, but I
think we really have got to do something
because the word likely is so very
vague. We use the word over here in
Northern Ireland very often as a substitute for " may be." It is not very
distinct. So far as I can ascertain the
word " likely " has never been construed
by the courts in recorded cases and,
therefore, it seems to me it might perhaps
be necessary to have the word inserted.
I could not help feeling that I was
justified in my proposal when I looked
back to Clause 1 of the Bill and in line 7
on page
2 saw a reference to "traffic which
might reasonably be expected to be on the
highway." I think, perhaps, the Committee may feel justified in inserting this
word.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: I am
advised that the word which the hon. and
learned Senator proposes to insert is quite
unnecessary. The Clause makes it an
offence to leave a vehicle in such a
position as to be likely to cause an
obstruction. Before the court can convict
under this Clause they must be satisfied
that the vehicle is likely to cause an
obstruction, and if there is no reasonable
likelihood they cannot convict. I am,
therefore, satisfied that the Amendment
would not be an improvement and I cannot accept it.
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot possibly press
this Amendment, and I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Mr. HILL DICKSON: I beg to move
in Clause 7, line 32, to leave out
obstruction" ("cause an obstruction ")
and insert any obstruction or danger."
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Hill Dickson]
I think it would strengthen the Bill and
tend to the public safety if "danger"
was added to this Clause. There may be
a car on the side of a hill which may be a
danger to the public and yet not
obstruction.
an
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I beg to move after
Clause 7 to add the following new
Clause:
and
"Motor and horse vehicles
motor and pedal bicycles shall not be
driven past tramcars which have
stopped for the purpose of taking on
and letting off passengers. Those who
violate this regulation shall be liable on
conviction to a fine not exceeding forty
shillings."
I hope the Committee will accept this
new Clause. I believe such a regulation
is in force in Dublin, in Edinburgh
and in other cities across the Channel,
and also in quite a number of
cities in the United States of America,
Many fatal accidents have occurred
in our city which might have been
averted if this regulation had been.
in force. A stop of one minute might
mean the saving of a valuable life.
time is now opportune for having such a
regulation included in the Bill which we
are now dealing with and which has for
its object the safety of the public. Every
means for increasing safety should be
adopted owing to the toll of the road.
The
It may be said, and rightly too, that
our tram conductors do all they possibly
can in the matter, but it frequently
happens that the conductors are collecting
fares when the tram stops and consequently are not on the platform. It
may be said that this Clause would cause
congestion. There is only one place in
the city where it would cause congestion,
and that is at Castle Junction. It should
be possible for the Government to get over
this matter. I think myself the arrangement with regard to tram traffic should be
altered so as to prevent so many cars at
the centre of the city.
That is the only place in Belfast where
there would be any congestion. The
noble Viscount in referring to the Clause
brought up by the hon. and learned
Senator, Mr. Leslie, this afternoon spoke
of reducing the number of accidents on
the road. The Clause which I am bringing before the Committee is one which I
hope will be accepted. I brought this
matter before the House on another
occasion. I beg to move.
Mr. McMAHON : I would like to
support the Amendment put down by Mr.
Quigley in connection with this particular
matter. I am sure that anything any of
us could do to save life we would gladly
do. A point was raised in connection
with the centre of the city. That is a
matter which has given the City Council
of Belfast much concern, and in particular the Tramways Committee. There
would be a considerable amount of congestion in the centre of the city, I understand, in connection with the stopping of
motorists to allow passengers to alight
from tramcars. I find from inquiries I
have made that they stop at the moment.
That is an unwritten law amongst
motorists, although they are not compelled
to do so, therefore it would be no harm
to make illegal for motorists to pass a
stationary tramcar.
I understand most difficulty comes from
the pedal cyclists. They are more likely
to pass tramcars than motorists, and I
suppose it would be a good thing if we
made it illegal for either a horse-drawn
vehicle or a cyclist to pass a tramcar. At
the moment I do not think that many
motorists pass tramcars, except perhaps
the drunken motorist who does not realise
what he is doing. As far as the ordinary
motorists are concerned they almost all
stop; at least it is my experience as a
motorist that most motorists stop when
passengers are alighting from or getting
on to a tramcar. I think it would be a
step in the right direction that this
Amendment should be passed.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I am afraid I
must oppose this Amendment. There is
only one city in Northern Ireland that has
tramcars, namely, Belfast, and it is a
matter for the Corporation of the city of
Belfast and their police authorities to
bring this in as a by-law. My own
impression is the same as Senator
McMahon's. I do not think most
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
14 JUNE 1934
I
motorists attempt to pass tramcars.
drive a motor-car through the city very
often myself and I aways stop under such
circumstances and, as a matter of fact,
the people who keep us waiting are the
people getting on and off the trams. The
majority of motorists are more than
kindly to everybody and always stop.
There may be one or two ferocious gentlemen who do not stop, but they can always
be had for dangerous driving. I will
oppose this Amendment, as I do not think
it is wanted.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I intended to say that
many motorists in the city are very very
careful and do stop, but, unfortunately,
there are others who have little consideration for other people.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
oppose this Amendment for this reason: I
go home by a tram which stops opposite
the City Hall. Sometimes I have to wait
there seven or eight minutes for a tram.
My observations tell me that there is
nearly always a tram coming or going past
that point. They all stop there, and
there are always passengers passing to and
fro. If we adopt this Amendment we will
have no motorists practically passing there
at all. They might be able to slip
through every fifteen minutes or so when
there is no tram stopped. The same
thing would apply at the Junction. Sometimes I wait there for a tram, and there
is never a time when there is not a tram
stopped in the middle of the road. A
person coming along in a motor could not
tell whether there are people going to get
on or off that tram, and he would have
to remain until the tram moved off. It
would be impossible to enforce this without practically stopping motor traffic on
our streets altogether. It would be far
better to leave the matter in the hands of
the Corporation. Personally, I think it
is not in these parts of the city that
accidents are likely to occur, but rather in
parts where there is not much traffic and
people are inclined to get careless.
know I am not likely to be run down at
those busy corners, though I might be at
some of the slack corners.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT
: This
matter has been considered more than
once and the police, who are responsible
for regulating traffic, have been consulted
and have investigated the matter
thoroughly. As a result of these investigations it was decided not to put in force
a regulation such as is proposed by the
hon. Senator. The question, of course,
affects only the city of Belfast. I am
aware that such a regulation has been put
in force in a limited number of cases in
Great Britain, particularly in the City of
Glasgow, but the problem of Belfast is a
very different one owing to the conformation of the centre of the city and the fact
that practically all the tramway services
converge on Castle Junction. The
Government is advised that such a regulation would cause a congestion of traffic in
the centre of the city which would create
an infinitely worse situation than exists
at present.
There undoubtedly have been cases
where motorists have, under these circumstances, driven dangerously, and there
have been other cases where they have
driven without proper consideration for
the convenience of pedestrians. There
have been convictions for dangerous
driving, and I would draw the hon.
Senator's attention to Clause 5 of the
Bill, which will enable the police to deal
with cases where there may not be actual
danger, but where there is a want of care
or a want of consideration for other
persons using the highway. I think that
the new powers contained in this Clause
will go a long way to meet the difficulty
which the hon. Senator has in mind. As
we are not satisfied that the Amendment
would improve the situation, but rather
that it would increase the difficulties, I
am unable to accept it.
Mr. QUIGLEY: In view of the statement of the noble Viscount I can do
nothing but withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Sir DUDLEY McCORKELL: I beg to
move, after Clause 7, to insert the following New Clause:—
en-
"Any pedestrian who on a public
highway, through negligence,
dangers his own safety, or that of any
other user of the road, shall be guilty
of an offence under the principal Act."
On another occasion in this House I
drew attention to the point which I now
wish to raise briefly. That is, as hon.
Members will see from the Order Paper,
in reference to doing something to legislate for pedestrians.
I was rather sur-
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Sir Dudley McCorkell]
prised when I read the text of this Bill to
find that, although it was a Road Traffic
Bill, no mention was made of pedestrians,
and there is no doubt that they form a
considerable part of the traffic on our
roads, both urban and rural. I should
like to disabuse the minds of anybody who
thinks, as some people apparently do
think, that in suggesting this I have anything in my mind in the way of penalising
the pedestrian. On the contrary, I want
to assist him. For every one bad pedestrian there are many good ones, but the
sins of the bad endanger the lives of the
good, and my object in introducing this
Amendment is to help the pedestrian himself. As I say, everybody does not seem
to think so. I have here an anonymous
letter with which I find myself largely in
agreement. Indeed, I only wish the
writer had signed his name in order that
I might have told him how fully I agree
with him on one or two of his points. He
says here:
"I see you wish pedestrians legislated
for and suggest that the first step would
be to make it clear where a pedestrian
should go, and what he should do in
order to save his own life. I have
lived, no thanks to the mad fellows on
wheels, for over 75 years, and haye
never caused an accident or death, nor
have I ever known of a pedestrian running anyone down. Moreover, I do not
know of any place on or near a road
where motor vehicles go where one's life
is safe."
To a great extent I agree with that, and
it is for that very reason that I would
make some suggestions to the pedestrian
and to my anonymous friend. I would
reply and suggest to everybody at large
that some of the things the pedestrian
might do are as follow:-
When a footpath is provided for him
he should walk on it.
He should not suddenly step off it without looking where he is going or what is
coming along.
He should not regard the sounding of
a motor-horn as an insult, but rather as a
signal for his own benefit.
When a number of pedestrians are
being passed on the road I would suggest
that they should all go to the one side
of the road, and not some to one side and
some to the other like a flock of sheep.
I would also make a suggestion which
I think would be strongly for the safety
of pedestrians, particularly on country
roads. That is that where no footpaths
are provided it should be the rule that
pedestrians should walk so as to meet oncoming traffic, in order that they might
see as well as be seen. Any of us who
drive a car, particularly in the winter,
and perhaps on a wet, dark night, with
the headlights of other cars coming and
going, know the difficulty there is when
pedestrians loom up out of the dark,
sometimes, unhappily, too near the front
of the car. If a pedestrian were going the
same way as the traffic he might not see
or even hear an approaching car at all on
a windy night, whereas, if he were walking to meet the car, I do not say it would
make him any smaller but it would make
his chance of safety much greater. I do
not want to labour the point, but I do
think it a matter of some importance.
I am bound to confess that I do not
suppose the noble Viscount, the Leader
of the House, will tell me that the Govern-
That
ment will accept this Amendment.
is too much to hope for. The only thing
which makes me hope he might is that
he has said that the Government are faced
with a very serious problem in seeking to
reduce the number of accidents on the
road. I am quite confident that the suggestion I make would help to reduce those
accidents. I feel myself that there are
many bad motorists on the roads as well as
good ones, and I am really trying to help.
the pedestrian to take care of himself and
other people. I do not feel quite like
my anonymous friend, when he says:-
"Large, monstrous lorries laden with
cement, bricks, slates, etc., rush past
here almost as fast, if, indeed, not
altogether, as express trains, and I do
not know when one may dash in on
me."
I am bound to confess that some of those
things do rush about the roads, and in
the pedestrian's own interests I suggest
that he should be made take some care of
himself, and incidentally, to have some
care for other people.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to support the Mayor of Derry's proposal.
From his point of view and my own it
would be safeguarding the pedestrian if he
had to take some precautions which he
does not take at the present time. All
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
14 JUNE 1934
Why
I,
our motor legislation has brought in the
motorist very heavily. He is fined for
this, he may not do this, or he may not
do that; in fact, he may not do anything.
Everything is put on his back. It is an
undoubted fact that a great many
accidents are caused by the fault of the
pedestrian and not of the motorist. That
has been proved in many cases.
should a man who causes accidents be
entirely left out of any legislation in
which his opposite number, so to speak,
is implicated? I consider that it is time
the pedestrian learned that, although he
undoubtedly has a right to the road, other
people have a right also, and that he must
conform to the rules of the road.
therefore, am very pleased to support this
Amendment. I see that London has just
started a new system of crossing roads. It
began last Monday, and under the rules.
drawn up for that system a pedestrian
who does not obey the regulations is subject to a fine of 5s. It is the first time
in the British Isles that pedestrians have
been pleaced under any form of punishment. I do think it is time that we here
began to recognise that the motorist is
not responsible for all our accidents, but
that he sometimes is involved in accidents
which are directly the fault of the pedestrian. As I say, I am very pleased to
support the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: It ought not to escape
attention that the pedestrian comes off
extremely lightly under this Bill. A good
many suggestions were made for controlling him, but they have not been
embodied in the Bill, and the pedestrian
has been left with things very much his
own way. Not only the motorist, but
the person in charge of a horse and cart
and so on are to be put under very severe
control by this new Measure, and there
is nothing whatever that I can see done
to restrain, or rather to direct, the
energies of the pedestrian. We all know
that, not only in towns but in the
country, pedestrians do simply ask to be
run over, and if anything could be done
to impress upon them that they must bear
their share of responsibility it would be
a very admirable thing.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The problem of the pedestrian is an extremely
difficult one. There is no doubt that the
want of care by pedestrians in the use of
the roads is responsible for a very considerable proportion of road accidents,
and in addition to those who exercise a
lack of care there is a small minority of
pedestrians which deliberately obstructs.
Regulation of pedestrian traffic under
certain circumstances and in certain places
must play a part in the efforts to minimise
road accidents, and power for this purpose exists under Section 8 of the Motor
Vehicles Act of 1929, but the regulation
of pedestrian traffic is a matter of
enormous difficulty, and no less difficult
would be the enforcement of such regulations.
I do not think, however, that a sweeping Amendment of such a general
character as is now proposed could be enforced in practice, nor do I think that it
is desirable. It is extremely questionable whether a fine is going to deter
pedestrians from being careless when they
are not deterred by the possibility, and
even the probability, of being killed if
they are negligent.
The Government think that, in the first
instance at any rate, the useful regulation
of pedestrian traffic should be in the
direction of prescribed crossing places and
matters of that kind in centres of population where there would be some prospect
of effective enforcement, and in this connection my right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs, is following with
great care the experiments which are now
in progress in Great Britain.
While,
therefore, we have considerable sympathy
with the objects which the hon. Senator
has in view, I am unable to accept the
Amendment which is put down.
Sir DUDLEY McCORKELL: So
many people have spoken about this
matter that, with a view to getting the
opinion of the Committee, I respectfully
press the Amendment.
Question put, "That those words be
there inserted."
The Committee divided: Contents, 9;
Not Contents, 7.
