CLAUSE 8 (Speed of motor-cars
drawing trailers).
If on any public highway any person
drives a motor-car to which a trailer is
attached at a speed exceeding thirty
miles an hour, he shall be guilty of
an offence under the principal Act:
Provided that nothing in this
section shall prejudice or affect the
operation of any local enactment or
any regulation for the time being in
force under the Road Traffic Acts
fixing a speed limit of less than thirty
miles an hour for observance within
any limits or place or for any particular class of motor-car.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to move,
in Clause 8, page 4, line 36, to leave
out the words "thirty miles" ("speed
limit of less than thirty miles") and to
insert instead thereof "fifteen or twenty
miles."
It is deplorable at the present time to
see the speed of a large number
of heavily laden motor lorries with
trailers, and to my mind they are certainly a danger to the public. I think
it is time that a speed limit was fixed
for those lorries, particularly those with
trailers, and I do think that so far as
motor-cars with trailers are concerned
20 miles an hour is quite a sufficient
speed. It is quite customary to have
these small trailers behind cars. I also
see motor-cars with small trailers
actually delivering parcels in the various
towns around Belfast. I respectfully
ask the noble Lord, the Leader of the
House, to let the Committee know if the
Government
insert
prepared to
are
a Clause to this effect in the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I am not quite sure,
from what the hon. Senator said, what
he proposes to substitute for the 30 miles
an hour in the Clause-whether 15 or 20
miles an hour.
McMahon, Mr. J.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
Quigley, Mr. A.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Twenty.
Mr. LESLIE: I can only say that
there is a very strong feeling about the
country that trailers do go very much
too fast, particularly at corners. What
happens is that you get a lorry trying
to get round a corner quickly, and the
trailer swings about in a most dangerous
manner. If there is no definite
objection to a speed limit of 20 miles an
hour-and I have not discovered one myself-it would make for the safety of
the roads.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I rise to support this
Amendment. I think it is very necessary, and I do hope the Government will
accept it.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
should like to be clear as to the effect of
this Amendment. This Clause apparently does not apply to lorries; it
applies to motor-cars with trailers.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I am prepared
to withdraw this Amendment if the noble
Lord assures me that an Amendment
will be inserted in the Bill on the Report Stage covering motor lorries with
heavy trailers. I do say that I consider
a speed limit of 20 miles an hour very
necessary in the case of motor-cars with
trailers, particularly when they are
going round corners.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: Before
I support this Amendment, I should like
to be clear as to what its effect is going
to be. If it means a speed limit of 20
miles an hour for heavy motor lorries
with trailers, I would support it; but if
it means small cars, perhaps with little
trailers carrying some persons, or a light
caravan, I could not support it. If
however, the effect of it would be to
stop these big heavily-laden lorries with

enormous trailers, which are very dangerous to pass, from going at a speed
over 20 miles an hour, I would support

clear on this point.

that my
say
intention was to deal with motor lorries,

Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Clearly
an indefinite Amendment of this

drawing light trailers, because under the

heavier

CLAUSE 9 (Duty to furnish reports
and give information in cases of motor
accidents) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 10 (Power to stop and
examine motor-cars).
(1) Any duly authorised officer
wearing uniform shall have power to
stop and, on production of his
authority, to examine any motor-car
for the purpose of ascertaining if it
complies with the regulations prescribed for the use and construction of
motor-cars, and such officer shall be
entitled, as part of such examination,
to do all or any of the following
things, that is to say-
(a) to require the person having
charge of such car to drive the
car for such distance (not exceeding one
such
mile) and in
direction and manner and at such
speed as the officer making the
examination shall direct;
(b) to travel in such car while it is
being driven under his direction;
(c) to test the working and efficiency
of the mechanism, fittings and
equipment of such car.
In this section the expression "duly
authorised officer' means a police
officer or constable authorised by the
Inspector-General or the Deputy
Inspector-General of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary for the purposes of this
section.
(2) If any person fails to comply
with any requirement made by a duly
authorised officer under this section he
shall be guilty of an offence under the
principal Act.
The following Amendments stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
Page 5, line 42, after ("car") insert
("being a goods vehicle or a public service vehicle').
Page 6, line 2, after ("of") insert
("such").
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
in Clause 10, page 5, line 42, after
the word "car" ("any motor-car") to
insert the words, "being a goods vehicle
or a public service vehicle.
""
I have three Amendments on this
Clause, and I hope they will all appeal
to the noble Viscount, because I am
afraid they are all of a somewhat fishing
description. In regard to the first
Amendment, we were discussing a
moment ago what is meant by a motorcar. The expression "motor-car" has
the same meaning as the expression
"light locomotive" in the Act of 1896.
That, of course, makes it perfectly clear
to everybody. But I could not help
thinking that in dealing with this Clause
the Committee might like a little more
information, and therefore I boldly put
down an Amendment to insert after the
word "car" the words "being a goods
vehicle or a public service vehicle."
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
I shall be told, no doubt, that this is
a class Amendment, but the whole
Clause is a class Clause directed against
low-class vehicles of whatever description
it may be intended to include. I have
had some difficulty, because of the (a),
(b) and (c) paragraphs, as to what is to
be done. For instance, if a motor-car
is meant here to include motor-bicycle it
will be a little difficult for an inspecting
officer to travel in the car while it is
being driven under his direction. However, I do not think there is really any
reason to suppose that this particular
Amendment will be accepted. The real
question that arises under this Clause is
that really dealt with in the third
Amendment, but in the meantime, in
order to get from the noble Viscount
some further information about the
intent and extent of this Clause, I beg
to move the first Amendment standing
in my name.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: This
Amendment, if made, would destroy a
very large part of the value of the
Clause. The 1926 Act gave the Ministry of Home Affairs certain powers of
supervision of public service vehicles.
This Bill gives them a certain power of
supervision of goods vehicles, but there
is no examination of private cars.
There are upon the roads to-day a very
considerable number of private cars which
are in an unfit mechanical condition,
and these cars are a danger not only
to those who travel in them but to other
road users. It is essential that steps
should be taken to prevent vehicles in
such a condition being used upon the
roads, and the powers conferred by this
Clause are the alternative to the regular
examination of all motor vehicles. Such
a regular examination would involve a
very costly organisation, and the Government would be extremely reluctant to
undertake such a task. They attach
great importance to the powers contained
in this Clause which, as hon. Senators
are no doubt aware, has been considerably modified since it was first proposed
for the purpose of meeting objections
made by the motoring community. I
cannot, therefore, accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the Committee will be pleased to have that explanation from the noble Viscount. I
ask leave to withdraw this Amendment,
and I do not move the one immediately
following.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 10, page 6, after line 20, to
insert:
:-
Provided that if the driver of the
car objects to comply with any requirement under paragraph (a) of this
sub-section the officer, if he does not
withdraw or vary the requirement,
shall then and there record the
objection in writing and if, after such
objection, any accident shall occur
during and arising out of compliance
by the driver with the requirement of
the officer neither the driver of the
car nor the owner thereof shall be
liable to any prosecution or to any
action for damages in respect of or
arising out of any accident occurring
as aforesaid.
The point of this Amendment, which
is in the same Clause, is to direct
attention to what is to happen to a man's
policy of insurance if he is required by
the examining officer to do something
with his car which he does not want
to do. It is quite possible that a man
may be driving something in the nature
of a crock, and if he is told to drive at
20 miles an hour to see how it works
he may perfectly well say, "No, this
car is not capable of going at 20 miles
an hour. I cannot make it do more
than 10 or 12 miles an hour." At all
events, the man ought to have the right
to object, I think, for the sake of his
insurance policy, and if he does object,
it is only fair that his objection should
be taken note of. Then the question
arises, what is to happen to the man if
he goes on doing what he is told, and
has some sort of accident. I think he
ought to be protected against the consequences. It is a little difficult.
might very well be charged with driving
to the danger of the public. I think
perhaps the common law would enable
him to defend himself against that. At
all events, I have put in words which
will limit anything that does happen
during the compliance with the order
and arising out of it. That will stop
a man, so to speak, from going on and
driving his motor-car recklessly and then
He
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
14 JUNE 1934
turning round and saying, "Please, sir,
I was doing what I was told." I think
we are in some difficulty. Is this man's
insurance policy going to be prejudiced
by the fact that he did something which
he did not want to do and in the doing
of which he was not a free agent?
If
there is any serious difficulty, I think
my proviso will meet the case.
It may
be the noble Viscount may be able to
tell us something which will avoid adding
some eight lines to a Bill that is already
growing.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I hardly
think that the hon. Senator has fully
considered the implications of
this
Amendment. If it were accepted and a
constable stopped a motorist and asked
him to drive along a road for half a mile
applying his brakes at intervals, the
motorist might drive in the most
deliberately reckless and dangerous
manner and yet he would not be liable for
the
either
consequences
civilly
or
criminally. If during the course of the
drive he saw a motor-car conveying somebody to whom he had an aversion he
might deliberately run that motor car
down and yet be liable to no civil or
criminal proceedings.
The hon. Senator
surely does not wish to effect this revolution in the law governing the driving
of motor vehicles.
I do not really think that there is any
serious objection to the Clause as it
stands. The officers who are to perform
this duty are to be specially authorised by
the Inspector General, and I think we are
entitled to assume that they will carry out
their duties in a reasonable manner and
be qualified to do so. If they do not the
House may be perfectly certain that their
superiors will deal with them appropriately.
With regard to the question of insurance
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Home Affairs, has had an interview with
the representatives of the insurance companies and he is assured that they would
regard driving under the conditions of
this Clause as covered by the terms of the
ordinary policy, and if this were not
sufficient the hon. Senator may rest
assured that my right hon. Friend under
the powers conferred upon him by Clause
19 would prescribe it as a condition which
should not be inserted in a policy—a condition or
limitation avoiding liability
when the car was being driven under the
circumstances laid down by this Clause.
I cannot possibly accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: While venturing to congratulate myself that I did take sufficient
means to preserve the noble Viscount from
the pitfall into which he has walked, and
disclaiming any intention to encourage
offences I may ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment, having got the assurance
that a man's policy will be safeguarded.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 11 (Medical examination of
licence holder).
(1) A police officer or constable may,
before making application to a court of
summary jurisdiction under sub-section
(6) of section three of the principal Act
for the suspension of any licence issued
under Part I of that Act, serve with the
notice required by the said sub-section.
to be served upon the licence holder a
notice in the prescribed form requiring
such licence holder to submit himself
for examination by the medical
practitioner named in the notice.
(2) A licence holder submitting himself for examination under this section
shall be entitled to have a medical
practitioner chosen by him present at
the examination.
(3) If it is proved to the satisfaction.
of the court that a licence holder served
with a notice under this section failed
or refused to submit himself for medical
examination as required by such notice,
the court may suspend the licence held
by that person for such time as the
court thinks fit, and may also declare
that person disqualified for obtaining
any such licence for such further period
after the expiration of the licence as
the court thinks fit.
(4) Where a licence is suspended.
under the provisions of this section or
under the provisions of sub-section (6)
of section three of the principal Act
the court may order the costs of the
application and of the medical examination, if any, to be paid by the person
whose licence has been suspended.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out sub-sections (1) and (2) and subsuspend
section (3) to the word
" and
insert:
:-
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
"(1) Where an application is made.
to a court of summary jurisdiction
under sub-section (3) of section six of
the principal Act for the suspension of
any licence issued under Part I of that
Act, the court may direct that the
licence holder shall, in the presence, if
he so desires, of any person he may
choose, be privately examined by a
medical practitioner approved by the
court, and the court may adjourn the
further hearing of the application until
evidence of the result of such examination has been given to the court."
""
(2) If it is proved to the satisfaction of the court that a licence holder
has, without reasonable excuse, failed
or refused to submit himself for medical
examination as directed by the court
under this section, the court shall."
Clause 11 is likely to be one of the
most useful Clauses in the Bill. It is
directed to ensure that people physically
incompetent shall not be allowed to continue to drive motor-cars. Ex hypothesi
the people affected have already got
licences to drive, and power is required, I
think, to enable a competent court to
take away the licence if satisfied that the
person is really incompetent. It may be
blindness or deafness or epilepsy as mentioned by an hon. Senator the other day.
If the Committee thinks that is
to be done they may very possibly
feel that they are not prepared
to do it in the manner suggested
by the Bill. The Bill suggests that any
policeman may go to the licence holder
suffering from some disability or other
and tell him he is to go to be examined
by some medical officer selected by the
policeman. I do not think we can stand
for that at all. We may agree that a
competent court shall direct the man to
be examined under some conditions of
privacy as suggested in the Amendment
standing in my name. I feel sure that
the Amendment proposed will be regarded
a reasonable substitute for the form
of words appearing in the Bill itself.
as
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 12 (Driver of a car presumed to be servant or agent of owner),
13 (Limitation of time for which drivers
of certain vehicles may remain continuously on duty), 14 (Requirements in

CLAUSE 16 (Extension of personal
liability for negligent driving).
(1) The liability of any person for
injury occasioned by the negligent
driving, control or management of a
motor-car by himself or by his servant
or agent after the commencement of
this Act shall not be limited to such
person himself nor be terminated by his
death, but shall continue after
his
death and be a liability of his real and
personal estate in like manner and with
the like priority as a debt or other
liability under a simple contract.
(2) Proceedings (including proceedings under the Fatal Accidents Acts,
1846 to 1908) instituted against any
person for damages for negligent
driving, control or management of a
motor-car by such a person or by his
servant or agent after the commencement of this Act shall not abate on the
death of such person but may be
continued against his personal representative.
(3) Proceedings (including proceedings under the Fatal Accidents Acts,
1846 to 1908) for damages for negligent
driving, control or management of a
motor-car by any person or by the
servant or agent of any person after the
commencement of this Act may, after
the death of such person, be instituted
and prosecuted against the personal
representative of such person:
Provided that no such proceedings
shall be maintainable unless commenced
not later than six months after the
personal representative took out representation.
""
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subclause (1) after "this " (" of this
Act") to insert part of this." There
are three of these very simple matters for
the printer. It will be observed on looking towards the end of the Bill that
different parts are to come into operation
at different dates and therefore we must
take some steps to indicate that it is this
part of this Act we are referring to. It
will not be necessary to say anything on
the other two Amendments.

CLAUSES 17 (Rights of third parties
against insurers), 18 (Bankruptcy, etc.,
of insured persons not to affect certain
claims by third parties), 19 (Certain conditions in policies or securities to be of no
effect), 20 (Duty of persons against whom
claims are made to give information as to

22 (Power to make rules of court), and
23 (Goods vehicles not to operate except

CLAUSE 24 (Provisions as to goods
vehicles licences).
(1) Goods vehicle licences shall be
granted by the Ministry of Home
Affairs and shall be in such form as
may be prescribed.
(2) A goods vehicle licence may be
granted upon payment of such fee (not
exceeding two pounds) and on such conditions, be subject to revocation or
suspension in such events, and generally
be dealt with, in such manner as may
be prescribed, or as the Ministry of
Home Affairs may determine:
Provided that-
(a) a goods vehicle licence shall, if not
revoked or suspended, be in force
for one year;
(b) the fee to be payable in respect of
such licences shall be uniform in
respect of all goods vehicles of a
particular class.
(3) Any condition on which a goods
vehicle licence is to be granted may be
either prescribed by regulations or
inserted in or endorsed on the licence
in respect of a vehicle; and a condition
so inserted in or endorsed on a licence
shall have the like effect as if it were
prescribed by regulations.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out sub-section (3). It may be
remembered that on the Second Reading
I drew attention to this sub-clause (3), of
Clause 24, and said I was sorry I did not
understand it. I am afraid I am still in
the same position. I do not quite understand what is the intention of this subclause. Since I spoke on the Second
Reading I have discovered that it does not
now make its first appearance. It is
really paragraph 1 (b) of sub-section (1)
of section 6 of the Act of 1929, but where
it appears in the Act of 1929 it is used
in connection with routes and time cards
for the purposes of public service vehicles.
There it did not seem to give rise to any
at
difficulty all. The difficulty that
occurred to me here is that there is an
apparent proposal that the conditions on
which goods vehicle licences are to be
granted are to be endorsed on the licences
and therefore by a kind of backstairs rule
the duty of laying regulations before
Parliament would be evaded. I am sure
that is not the intention at all. The
intention is likely to be that some particular condition may be put on the
licence. It appears to me that the subclause is not clear at all and, therefore,
I suggest leaving it out. I do not suggest
any substitute so that an explanation may
be given and any verbal alteration made
at a later stage.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This provision is based on experience gained under
the 1926 Acts in connection with the
licensing of public service vehicles.
was necessary to lay down under that Act
definite standards with which public
service vehicles had to comply but there
were a number of vehicles which when
mechanically safe did not comply with
these requirements. It was possible to
allow them to be used under a limited
licence whereas it would not have been
possible to give them a general licence and
the only practicable method of imposing
the limitations was by endorsement on the
licences restricting the use of the vehicle.
in certain ways. For instance, vehicles of
undue length which were in the possession
of operators were allowed to operate on
routes where the extra length was not a
source of danger and this was conveniently
Road Traffic Bill-
Comm.ctee
THE SENATE
Another
[Viscount Charlemont]
carried out by endorsement.
outstanding example is the case of doubledecker buses. It would be unsafe to
allow the general use of these vehicles
throughout Northern Ireland, but they
can be safely used and are of great value
on certain routes and the limitations as
to use are endorsed upon the licences.
Again, in respect of these vehicles certain
routes
can be safely used subject to
specified conditions such as a compulsory
stop before going under certain bridges.
This condition is also endorsed. The provision is really intended not to operate
harshly but to mitigate the severity of the
general conditions in appropriate cases.
I cannot, therefore, accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: We are very glad to
have that explanation. I think I should
ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
If necessary a word or two may be considered at a later stage to get over the
difficulty of so reading the Clause as to
suggest that by the use of it the obligation
to lay the regulation before Parliament
can be evaded. That is the important
point I wished to draw attention to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: That can
be raised at a later stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 31 (Conditions regulating use
of lamps on vehicles).
The Ministry of Home Affairs may
by regulations prescribe the conditions
subject to which lamps, showing a light
to the front, may be used, and such
conditions may include conditions as to
the angles at which beams of light may
be projected, the height, width and the
range of illumination (to be ascertained
in accordance with the regulations) of
such beams of light, the extent and
method of obscuration to be employed,
and the position on the vehicle of any
lamps
Mr. LESLIE: I have two Amendments
here, one to leave out Clause 31 and the
other to leave out Clause 32. It is
scarcely possible, I think, to consider them
separately. Both of these Clauses deal
with the arrangements for lighting
vehicles and both of them have given rise
to considerable controversy outside. All
sorts of suggestions have been made. One
of them is that Clause 31 should stop at
the word "used." Another is that at
the end of Clause 32 we should add words
"other than headlights projecting a beam
which may move in conjunction with the
deflecting front wheels as actuated by the
steering mechanism." The only thing is
there is a considerable body of opinion
that the insertion of these two Clauses is
overloading the Bill, and there is a fear
expressed lest the developments in steering and so on may be adversely affected
if the words are allowed to stand.
think the best thing to do is to move to
omit the Clause so as to give an opportunity to anyone competent to speak on
the subject, to give us his views and
generally to enable this important Clause
of an important Bill to receive in this
House that discussion which, perhaps, was
denied to it elsewhere.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The object
of this Clause is to enable regulations to
be made to prevent the use of unsuitable
lights, and particularly to prevent the
use of lights which are likely to dazzle
the drivers of on-coming traffic, and which
are, therefore, a nusiance and a danger.
It is obviously undesirable to lay down
the requirements in an Act of Parliament.
They must of necessity be very largely
of a technical character, and further, with
the rapid development of motoring equipment they may need alteration from time
to time. We think the Clause is useful
and should be allowed to stand.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 32 (Restriction on movement
of lamps).
Mr. LESLIE : Does the noble
Viscount's reply to Clause 31 also relate
to Clause 32, the deletion of which I
formally move, as I understand other hon.
Senators desire to speak on it.
14 JUNE 1934
Road Traffic Bill-

Committee
ping method, where the beam is deflected
on to the side of the road and you cannot
see the pedestrian ahead of you.
Viscount CHARLEMONT : With
regard to Clause 32, this is aimed at a
type of light which some motorists have
attempted to use and which is most
dangerous and objectionable. It is in the
nature of a searchlight, which can be
swivelled in any direction. No motorist
who has the slightest consideration for
other people would attempt to use it, and
it should be prohibited. I cannot accept
the Amendment.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
think it is as well to leave this matter of
lights in the hands of the Ministry of
Home Affairs. I do not think there is
anything that causes more fatal accidents
on the roads than the glaring headlights
that some motorists use. They are a
danger not only to pedestrians but to
other motorists, and if it is possible it
would be better to legalise only lights that
can be dimmed. On the whole, I think
it would be better to leave this matter in
the hands of the Ministry.
I
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I should
like to say a word or two for the reason
that I think a little light on this question
of lights would be an advantage.
looked up a dictionary to try to find a
definition of the word " dipping," but I
was unable to do so. I imagine it means
lights reflected downwards. I quite
appreciate the point made by the noble
Viscount about those abominable searchlights with which inconsiderate motorists
blind everybody else on the road, and I
think they should be made illegal. But
the point I want some information on is
this. The Clause says that no light shown
by a vehicle, other than a dipping headlight, shall be moved by swivelling, deflecting, or otherwise while the vehicle is
in motion. In my opinion one of the
most up-to-date methods of avoiding glare
is a device on the switchboard of the car.
It turns out the right light, and the left
light is slightly swivelled or deflected
towards the near side. I know this is
one of the things that can be dealt with
by regulations, but I do not think it can
be the intention of the Government to
scrap this safety device which is fitted to
a large number of cars. Undoubtedly it
does move or swivel or deflect the light,
because the off light is cut off altogether,
but the side lights remain on to show the
width of the car. That is a far more
satisfactory method than the strictly dip-
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: You
have effectively dealt with the pedestrian.
You should have no more trouble with
him.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I should
not like a swivel light of the kind I have
described to be abolished, and I should
like to have some information as to what
is meant by the expression in the Clause,
"moved by swivelling, deflecting, or otherwise while the vehicle is in motion."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: If the hon.
Member will kindly renew his request at
a later stage I shall be very glad to give
him the information.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 38 (Penalties).
If any person causes or permits any
vehicle to be on any public highway in
contravention of any of the provisions
of this Part of this Act or of regulations
made for the purposes of this Part of
this Act, or otherwise fails to comply
with any such provisions, he shall be
guilty of an offence under the principal
Act or under such regulations as the
case may be.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
38, page 23, line 27, after the word
"person" (If any person) to insert the
word "wilfully." This is a difficulty
which we have had very often before.
There has been a feeling that this provision is a little too strict as it stands.
The word "wilfully" has been inserted in
other Acts, while in others the Government have seen fit not to insert it. I
think this is one of those occasions where
the insertion of the word "wilfully" might
perhaps assist us. I am not going into
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
""
dissertation on the word wilfully,"
because we have had it so often, but it has
this merit. It does prevent a person who
really did not mean to break the law from
being hauled into court on account of some
accidental infraction of the law. The
common case is the electric tail light of a
motor-car, which is very difficult for any
man to manage. Mistakes and accidents
do arise, and it is possible that the
insertion of the word "wilfully" might
protect the really innocent person from
unnecessary prosecution. I beg to move.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
hope the Government will accept this
Amendment. I do not think it will
weaken the Clause in the least, and I
think it will be a protection to motorists
who may innocently commit a technical
offence.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The insertion of the word "wilfully" would, I
submit, be contrary to the policy of the
Motoring Code, which definitely places.
upon the owner or the person in charge
of the motor vehicle a definite responsibility of a positive character, to see that
his vehicle is not a danger to other people.
In the insurance provisions, for instance,
not only is the owner responsible for not
using his vehicle, unless it is properly
covered by insurance, but he is responsible for seeing that nobody else does
So.
The insertion of the word "wilfully"
would derogate from the positive responsibility of the owner to ensure that his
vehicle while on the road is not a danger
to other persons and that it is properly
equipped. If, through no fault of his
own, the lighting system fails and he takes
all proper precautions to see that the car
is not a danger to other persons, the court
should, if he is prosecuted. take these
factors into account and deal with the
case accordingly. For these reasons I
cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: With the consent of my
right hon. Friend (Mr. Samuel Cunningham) I ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
:-
Clause 38, page 23, line 30, after
"otherwise" insert wilfully.'
""
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

(3) If any person forges or fraudulently alters or uses or fraudulently

other person any mark for identifying
a vehicle, any badge, or any licence
under the Road Traffic Acts or under

This
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
41, at the end of sub-clause (3) to insert,
"or on conviction on indictment to

two years with or without a fine.”
is rather a curious little thing. The
development of motor traffic, amongst
other things, has brought into being a new
class of offence. That is, forgery and so
forth of insurance certificates, certificates
of security, and so on. We have not
allowed our penal law to keep in step,
a fault we rarely commit. Some of these
offences should not come before a court of
summary jurisdiction, but should be dealt
with on indictment before a judge and
jury, and the penalty for ordinary misdeamour ought to be provided for.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.

Road Traffic Bill-

14 JUNE 1934
CLAUSE 43 (Amendment of s. 8 of 20
Geo. 5, c. 21, as to traffic regulations).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of Clause 43, to add as a new subsection:-
"(3) In this section the expression
"animals" means asses, cattle, goats,
horses, sheep, and swine."
This again, as no doubt the Committee
will recognise, is rather put down with a
view of getting a little more information
and finding out what is the intention of
the Government or rather what is the
intention of the proposal which the
Government lays before Parliament,
which is a different thing. We are taking
now, or are seeking to take powers to
regulate traffic, which includes the
passage of animals, and power to prescribe lights to be carried by persons in
charge of animals. There are certain
little difficulties, chiefly, perhaps, because
of the well-known ways of animals, which
do not pay much regard to the regulations of the Ministry of Home Affairs.
When Clause 8, as it then was, of the
Act of 1929 was before us I remember
asking the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Home Affairs, when he made
his traffic regulations, what he proposed
to do with a flock of sheep, because I
could not help thinking-and later I
found I was right-that a flock of sheep
pays very little attention to traffic signal
lights. The Minister's idea was that he
could make the man in charge responsible,
but he could not make him competent to
manage the sheep. Now they want to
take further powers, and I want to know
what sort of animals they are referring
to. Are they referring to dogs, amongst
other things. I am not sure that cats do
not stand in a different class altogether,
but are we giving, or is the Minister asking for powers to regulate dogs and things
of that sort, which are really more
attributable to local authorities.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: Giraffes.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not know whether
the Committee will think the words I
suggest will help us or not, but I think
we ought to be told a little more about the
proposal.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think the
word "animal" as it stands in the Clause
It is comhad better be left untouched.
prehensive, and if we seek to define
animals we might be driven to include
animals in connection with the zoo lately
established in Belfast. It might even be
necessary to have a professor of some kind
attached to the Ministry of Home Affairs
to advise my right hon. Friend as to the
proper definition of animals in transit
from the steamer or railway station to
the zoo.
That would be objectionable and
would add to the expense of administration. Dogs are left out, as there are
by-laws dealing with them. Therefore I
think the expression "animals" had better
be left as it is.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think the
Amendment need be pressed. I do not
think anything will happen.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

pounds."
Colonel SINCLAIR: I beg to move, in
Clause 44, page 27, line 20, to leave out
the words fifty pounds," and insert
instead thereof the words, "the full cost
of the hospital treatment." I should like
to express my satisfaction that when this
Bill was in another place a suggestion by
a surgeon colleague of mine to increase
from £25 to £50 the maximum amount
which a hospital may receive from an
insurance company for the treatment of
an injured motorist was accepted by the
Government. But the point I wish to
make is, is it really necessary to have a
maximum at all? I am sure that a maximum of £50 will cover most of the
accident cases admitted to the hospitals,
but occasionally they may exceed the £50
limit. Not long since there was
accident which cost £90. One Member
speaking in a Committee of the Imperial
House on the question said, "I have cases
before me where the cost ran to as much
as £165."
an
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
The hospitals, of course, have great
trouble in raising funds for carrying on
their work, and they take in motor cases
as emergency cases at extraordinary inconvenience. These cases are not by any
means easily or economically dealt with.
It may be that the extension from £25
to £50 will cover most of the cases, but
my point is, why should there be a
maximum at all? Why should it not be
as in the Amendment that instead of
£50 there should be the full cost of hospital treatment? It seems to be
question of whether the prosperous insurance companies are paramount,
whether the necessities of the hospitals,
more particularly those hospitals that
are supported by voluntary contributions and which are very hard pressed
for funds, should receive the most
attention.
a
or
I would point out further that in the
event of motor accident cases being
taken into hotels, or lodging-houses, or
private hospitals, the full amount is to
be obtained by those establishments.
Then in a case of a hospital
struggling for funds,
funds, where accommodation is first-class, where nothing
is charged for the medical and
surgical services rendered, and where
nursing and other assistance is available, why should there be a limit
fixed beyond which we cannot go? It
should be competent for the Boards of
Management of these hospitals either
to reduce the amount of their bill
or waive it altogether in selected.
cases using their own judgment and
discretion, but, as a principle, the
large hospitals should be considered to
such an extent that if they accommodate
insurance societies by taking in these
cases and doing their best for them, they
should not be limited in the furnishing
of the bill for the full amount of the
expenses incurred.
I am sure the rate is easily ascertainable. It will be a moderate rate per
week according to the scale which can be
seen by anyone in the annual reports of
these hospitals. The proposal I make is
that the £50 maximum, which is very
gratifying at the moment, should be
passed over and the words, "the full
cost of the hospital treatment" inserted.
I beg to move.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: While every
Member of this Committee will have
every sympathy with Colonel Sinclair in
his effort to assist the hospitals, I am
going to oppose this Amendment on the
ground that it will increase the insurance
of all motorists very considerably. I
think that when £25 was first inserted
in another place, and afterwards
doubled, that is a very good start for
the hospitals. There is nothing to prevent a man who can afford it, if the cost
is more than £50, paying the full cost.
If private hospitals and nursing homes
and places of that sort thought they
were going to get the full cost, I am
afraid they would keep the man there
for the rest of his natural life.
(Laughter.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This matter was discussed at considerable length
in another place and the present provision was put in to meet an objection
raised on behalf of the hospitals to the
limitation of £25, which was contained
in the Act of 1930. It must be remembered that this provision does not preclude the hospital from taking the
appropriate steps to recover the full
amount of the cost in the exceptional
case where the amount would exceed
£50. The original provision provided
the hospitals with an exceptional remedy
to meet the cases where the amount of
the maintenance due was not large and
where there might have been difficulty in
obtaining payment in the ordinary
course, and from the examples given in
another place it is clear that the limit of
£50 is amply sufficient to cover the vast
majority of cases.
was
One case was quoted in which the cost
was over £90, but the circumstances of
that case were such that it
eminently one where the hospital should
have adopted the ordinary procedure
and have obtained payment of the full
amount. They elected, however, to
avail themselves of Section 12 of the
Act of 1930 and were, therefore, limited
to £25. The Government are of opinion
that £50 is a reasonable limit for this
exceptional procedure and that as the
hospitals are in no way precluded from
recovering the larger sums if they adopt
the appropriate procedure, I regret I
cannot accept the Amendment.
Road Traffic Bill-
Committee.
14 JUNE 1934
Colonel SINCLAIR: In view of the
explanation given by the noble Viscount
that we are not precluded in exceptional
cases from going beyond £50, I ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
The explanation is satisfactory.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 45 (Powers of Fares and
Rates Tribunal), 46 (Payments into and
out of the Road Fund (Northern Ireland)), 47 (Provision as to services of
summonses), 48 (Proof of previous convictions), and 49 (Costs in prosecutions
under Road Traffic Acts) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 50 (Non-application of Probation of Offenders Act).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave
out Clause 50.
The difficulty I am in about this Amendment is that I do not myself approve of the
application of the First Offenders Act,
1907, to the cases specifically dealt with
in the Second Schedule. The First
Offenders Act undoubtedly never was
meant for offences like this. There
were not so many of them at the time
the Act was passed. It was not intended for this sort of case at all, neither
was it intended nor expected that a Parliament of the twentieth century would
start going back to minimum fines and
minimum terms of imprisonment, and
distrust of its own courts, the
effect of which would be to drive
thoughtful magistrates to take refuge in
applying the First Offenders Act when,
if they were left alone, they would not
do it. They would simply impose a
small fine or deal with the matter in a
simple way. Therefore, if Parliament
insists on judging these cases which they
have not heard instead of leaving the
matter to the jurisdiction of the magistrates who have heard them it is not
much use saying anything.
A further thing I want to point out
about this Clause is the absurdity of
refusing to allow magistrates to apply
the First Offenders Act in comparatively
minor cases of reckless driving, when you
cannot stop the High Court judges from
applying the First Offenders Act in the
case of the man who has committed a
more serious offence of reckless driving
with which he is charged on indictment.
The position is ridiculous. Although,
as I say, I do not think the Act was
intended for this purpose, the fact remains that it is absurd to say that a
magistrate shall not do what you allow
a High Court judge to do in more serious
cases. The only difference would seem
to be that the First Offenders Act is
being applied by a person of greater
authority than an ordinary resident
magistrate. That may be the case, but
there seems to be no course left except
to move the rejection of this Clause.
cannot refrain from expressing the hope
that very great care will be taken before
the advantage of this Act is given in the
cases of offences for which it was not
intended. I think this is a matter which
should be debated in this Committee.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Probation of Offenders Act,
1907, was
originally passed mainly to deal with
juvenile offenders. First, as to the
question of character I think the Committee will agree that the character of
the accused does not enter into the case
of an offence against the motoring laws.
Whether a man is a good character or a
bad one, if he commits a motoring offence
he is equally liable to punishment in
the eyes of the law. The antecedents of
a defendant are also immaterial.
Neither does the age of the delinquent
apply, because if a person is so old as
to be incapable of managing a
car he
should not be on the road, and he cannot complain if he is convicted of an
offence. On the other hand, if he is so
young as to be incapable of looking
after himself he is not entitled to have
a licence, and so on. With regard to
the trivial nature of the offence, I must
point out that none of the offences
enumerated in the section of the Schedule
to which this Clause applies can be considered trivial.
Mr. LESLIE: Comparatively.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: They comprise reckless or dangerous driving, being
under the influence of drink or drugs
whilst in charge of a motor-car, using a
car in contravention of Section 6 of the
Act of 1930, driving a car during a period
of suspension of licence, driving a car
Road Traffic Bill-
Business of the House
THE SENATE
a
[Viscount Charlemont]
while disqualified from obtaining
licence, or obtaining a licence while disqualified from obtaining such licence.
They are offences which must be stamped
out if we are to have any safety upon the
roads, and I regret, therefore, that I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I have given an opportunity of ventilating the question. There
is nothing to be said in reply to the noble
Viscount except that dangerous driving
now can be inadvertently committed
having regard to "reasonably expected
traffic." As nobody seems to take
advantage of the opportunity to discuss
the matter I beg leave to withdraw. The
following Amendment will not be moved.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 51 (Power of arrest in
certain cases), 52 (Power of Ministry of
Home Affairs to make regulations), 53
(Interpretation), 54 (Commencement of
Act), and 55 (Short title, citation and
construction), ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
FIRST SCHEDULE (Enactments as
to Lighting repealed by Part IV), agreed
to.
SECOND SCHEDULE (Offences in
relation to which the Probation of
Offenders Act, 1907, is not to apply).
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie: Page 32, line 22 to
27, leave out the Second Schedule.
Amendment not moved.
Schedule agreed to.
Title and Preamble agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported
with Amendments to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendments.
Ordered: That the Report of the said
Amendments be received on Tuesday
next, and that the Bill be printed as
amended.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
That this House do now
move,
""
adjourn."
Mr. McMAHON : To what date?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Tuesday.
Mr. LESLIE: I would like to know
what the programme is likely to be for
next week. Matters have not been going
quite according to schedule and I am very
much afraid it looks as though it might
be necessary for this House not to sit on
Wednesday but to sit on Friday. (Hon.
Members: No, no.) Evidently that is
unpopular, but the question is whether
we can get through the work in time.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: All I can
say at the present moment is that the
business on Tuesday will be the Unemployment Bill, Committee; the Milk and
Milk Products Bill, Committee; Marketing of Potatoes Bill, Committee, and the
Report Stage of the Bill we have just
been dealing with. I think we ought to
be able to manage it next week without
sitting on Friday, but of course if any
hon. Senator wishes to sit on that day we
might have to sit on Saturday as well.
(Laughter.)
Mr. LESLIE:
What I was at was
rather the question of the time allowed
before the Report Stage of the Bill with
which we have just been dealing.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter after Five
o'clock until Tuesday
next.
Unemployment Bill-
Committee
19 JUNE 1934
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 19th June, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Return of Registered Clubs under the
provisions of Section 11 (4) of the Intoxicating Liquor and Licensing Act, 1927,
for the year 1933.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Unemployment Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee on the Unemployment Bill
read.
Moved: "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. LESLIE: May I take the opportunity for the sake, perhaps, of the newer
Members of saying that the Amendments in
my name on the Paper do not necessarily
move
that I propose to
the
rejection of the Clauses. It is to direct
attention to the Clauses and have the
matter cleared up in that way.
mean
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Age for entry into
insurance and rate of contributions in
respect of persons under sixteen), 2
(Power to enlarge or restrict excepted
employments), 3 (Right to, and duration
of, benefit), 4 (Definition of benefit year),
5 (Provisions as to rates of benefit), 6
(Amendments as to third statutory condition), and 7 (Amendments as to fifth
statutory condition) ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Amendment as to the
sixth statutory condition).
An Amendment stood in Mr. Leslie's
name to omit the Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: The point on which I
want a little more information from the
Minister of Labour on this Clause-I
am afraid there are two or three points
-is, first of all, is there any counterpart to this Clause in the Measure now
before the Parliament of the United
Kingdom, and, secondly, whether I may
conclude that the Minister has power by
regulation to deal with what one might
describe as hard cases, such, perhaps, as
service in the British Army, which would
prevent the man putting in the full five
years before getting unemployment benefit. Matters of that kind, is seems to
me, almost certainly arise. I observe in
another Clause of the Bill, Clause 31,
the public assistance part of the Bill,
paragraph (d) to which I have also
drawn attention really only in order to
connect with the point now before us.
There is in part of the Bill definite
power taken to make regulations, and it
did occur to me the Minister will probably be able to assure us he has power
to deal with these special cases that may
arise under this Bill.
There is another point. I would have
made it on the Second Reading if I had
thought of it. I want the Minister to
give an undertaking that when this Bill
is passed he will, as soon as the United
Kingdom have consolidated their Acts,
take the opportunity of consolidating
our Acts over here. The reason I hang that
on to this Clause is this, although I am
quite prepared to believe that every
Member of this House, except myself,
knows what the sixth statutory condition
is, it took me a very considerable time
to find out. It is obvious that when we
are dealing with an Amendment to the
sixth statutory condition the first thing
we ought to know is what the sixth
statutory condition is. If the law was
consolidated we could find out at once.
As it is it is a matter of some little
difficulty. In the meantime, I ask the
Minister for an answer to these points.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to point out that this is a
very important Clause so far as the
working-class people of Northern Ireland
are concerned. We have a tremendous
number of unemployed people and unable
to find work, and I think the Minister
of Labour would be very negligent of
the interests of the workers of Northern
Unemployment Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
Ireland if he did not put something like
this in this Bill and see that people across
the Border did not come in and take
our people's work. We have boys who
have attained 19 years of age and have
never been able to obtain employment.
it would be a bad thing if, possibly,
strangers could come into our small State
and take work that really belongs to cur
own people. I certainly hope the
Minister of Labour will insist on this
particular Clause being retained.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews) My hon. Friend, Senator
Cunningham, has really explained very
thoroughly the reason for this Clause so
that I need not add to what he has said.
There is no corresponding Clause in the
British Bill-I understand it has not
become an Act yet. The reason is they
have no land boundary and we have,
and circumstances alter cases to that extent. As regards discretionary powers, I
have certain powers which I hope I will
discreetly use. For instance, when an
Ulsterman born and bred has lived the
greater part of his life in Ulster and goes
for a certain time to the Free State and
returns here, I could arrange under the
regulations to waive the residence qualification.
As regards consolidation of these Acts,
which I admit require consolidation, I
understand the Imperial Minister has
said he is prepared to consolidate the
Acts in Great Britain, and when he has
done so we shall do so here.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Might I just ask
the Minister of Labour what is the
position of the man who leaves Northern
Ireland and goes to take employment in
the new factories which have been started
in the Free State by employers in Northern Ireland. There are factories started
from day to day, branches from factories
in Northern Ireland, and necessarily in
a new industry started in these places
workers from Northern Ireland must go
and get employment in these factories
which are now started in the Free State.
I would like the Minister of Labour to
tell the Committee what is the position of
these employees with regard to insurance.
Mr. ANDREWS: Of course,
it is very
hard to give a direct answer to that
many
question, because there are SO
factors that may influence one in giving a
decision. But if a person is a bona-fide
North of Ireland person who goes for a
time to obtain employment in the Free
State he would not have to spend the
full five years qualifying period before
being entitled to benefit.
Mr. LESLIE: Really what it comes
to is that the Clause is not capable of
what you might describe as a narrow
interpretation at all. If a man who
happens to come from the Free State
puts in five years in the Glasgow shipyards and then comes to the Belfast
shipyards he would be just as much qualified as if he had been in Belfast all his
life, and it does not matter whether he
comes from the Free State or the United
States, his position is just the same.
Mr. ANDREWS: The Clause is not
harshly administered-I can give the
Committee that assurance-as regards
Northern Ireland workers.
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.


CLAUSES 9 (Period of disallowance
of benefit in certain cases),
(Definition of dependent child),
(Amendments as to increase of benefit
in respect of dependants), 12 (Amendment as to anomalies regulations), and
13 (Determination of claims and questions), ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 14 (Provision of authorised
courses and other courses of instruction
and training, and payment of allowances
to persons attending thereat).
The following Amendment stood cn
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
Leave out the Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: This is, as it seems to
me, a rather difficult Clause. If I have
got it right, it differs very much from
anything to be found in the English
Measure. If I remember correctly, in
England it is for the education authority
to prepare schemes and put them up to
the English Ministry of Labour, and if
they are approved, well and good. Here
the proposal appears to be the contrary.
Unemployment Bill-
Committee
19 JUNE 1934
Here it is for the Minister to say what
he wants, and, according to this Bill, it
is for the education authority to provide
it. I have not the least objection to
education in these matters-it is likely
to be very useful, but the question that
does arise is, who is to bear the cost?
Is the cost to be borne by the education
authority, which usually means the
county council, having behind it the
county rates? There is power later on
in the Clause to defray the cost of an
authorised course provided by the Ministry. That is one thing, but who is going
to provide the buildings in which these
courses of instruction are to be undertaken.
Is there really a suggestion here that
the education authorities are to incur
the expense of setting up something in
the nature of technical schools for the
special purpose of providing the special
courses of instruction referred to in this
Clause. It seems to be a question in
which three Ministries are concernedthe Ministry of Labour, the Ministry of
Education, and perhaps the Ministry of
Finance; in
in fact,
fact, the Ministry of
Finance is referred to in sub-clause (5).
We ought to be told something more, in
fact a good deal more, as regards the
cost which is likely to be thrown, first on
the education authorities, and subsequently on the ratepayers.
Mr. ANDREWS: The question of the
arrangements to be made for the courses
of instruction will be a matter, in the
first instance, for the local education
committees, and also the problem of
arranging for the buildings. It is not
anticipated that new buildings will be
required. It is hoped that existing
buildings can be utilised for this purpose.
Some of our primary schools, I understand, have got classrooms that will be
available. Some of our technical schools
are in a similar position, and it is hoped
that no serious expenditure will require
to be embarked upon. I can assure the
Committee that my Department will
confer from time to time with the Ministry of Education and with the education
authorities in our area in order to have
this Clause in the Bill carried out as
economically as possible.
Mr. LESLIE: There still remains the
question of what I might describe as the
voluntary schools. The non-provided
school exists mostly in the country districts.
Has the Minister taken those
into account at all?
Mr. ANDREWS: There are schools
actually vacant in Northern Ireland at
the present time in districts where new
schools have been built and old schools
have been abandoned.
It may be possible to use some of them.
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 15 (Power to require
attendance of persons under eighteen at
authorised courses).
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(6) It shall be the duty of the
education authority to furnish to the
Ministry such reasonable information
as the Ministry may require, for the
purposes of this section, with respect
to persons leaving day schools, and
with respect to the attendance at an
authorised course of any person
required to attend thereat under this
section.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave
out sub-clause (6), of Clause 15. This
is the sub-clause which casts on the
education authority the duty of furnishing
information to the Ministry with respect
to persons leaving day schools. I am
afraid this is a sub-clause it is rather
difficult to agree to, chiefly on account of
the voluntary schools to which I
have already referred. An education
authority has, I suppose, some authority
over the teachers in its provided schools,
but so far as I know the education
authorities have no power to direct the
manager of a voluntary school to do anything. Therefore, so far as voluntary
schools are concerned, I am afraid that
if this sub-clause goes through we shall
be rather put into an impossible
position. At a later stage perhaps some
slight modification of that wording would
seem to be desirable. I think the Committee will see the point. Here Parliament is proposing to cast a definite duty
the education authority, and the
education authority has not the means
of complying with the requirements of
Parliament. I cannot help thinking
that some modification could be made,
more conveniently at a later stage, that
would get over that difficulty. As it
stands we are seeking to impose a condition that cannot be fulfilled.
Unemployment Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
think that such cases as are referred to
in this Clause will be very few, but it
is a good thing that provision is being
made for such people as these, because
it is much better that they should be
receiving some kind of instruction than
that they should be running about the
streets getting into mischief. Young
people who have no work are generally
as mischievous as they can be,
especially with the property of the
citizens like trees and the parks.
I
Mr. ANDREWS: The question which
the hon. and learned Senator has raised
is a very interesting one. I may say
that in the past my Department has
obtained from time to time a great deal
of information from education authorities and from various school boards, and
we have had no compulsory powers at
all. Even if in certain instances we have
not got compulsory powers to obtain this
information I am quite sure we would be
able to get it on a voluntary basis.
think everybody will appreciate how
desirable it is that we should have this
information from time to time, for two
purposes. The first is in order to see if
my Department can help in any way a
young person who has left school to
obtain employment, and the second is
that if it is not possible to obtain employment for a young person that, at any
rate, we will be able to provide for the
training and further education of that
young person in order to prevent him or
her,
as the
case may be, wandering
about the streets doing nothing.
Mr. LESLIE: Probably the Committee will think that the expression "reasonable information" will be
sufficient to prevent any
any difficulty
arising between the Ministry and a
voluntary school. It is really only
voluntary schools I am interested in.
After what the Minister has said I think
I might ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 16 (Power to make grants
out of Unemployment Fund towards
expenses of attendance at authorised
courses) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 17 (Power to require notification when persons under eighteen
leave employment).
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
:-
Leave out the Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: Here, again, something of the same sort of question arises.
This is a new power which the Ministry
seeks to have the right of exercising over
all employers, whether the people employed are normally workers in
insured trade or not. I should like some
assurance that employers have been consulted, and that the Ministry anticipates
no difficulty.
an
Mr. ANDREWS: The employers are
to be asked to furnish that information
for exactly the same purpose that the
education authorities are being asked to
furnish somewhat similar information.
The object is that when employers are
obliged to discharge these young people
my Department shall know in order that
we may endeavour to get employment
for them in some other direction, and,
failing that, that we may be able to
arrange for courses of training for them.
I do not think employers will object to
furnish this information. I hope, in
fact I think, that all employers are very
sympathetic towards these young people
when stress of circumstances may oblige
them to dismiss for a time. I am sure
they are only too anxious to help them,
and that they will be very glad to do
sɔ in this small way.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
should like to ask the Minister if he is
satisfied that notification will be given
in the case of a boy or girl whose services are dispensed with. The Clause
requires notification from an employer
of a young person who leaves his employment, but does that include the
young person whose services are dispensed
with, or who, to use a more popular expression, is sacked.
Mr. ANDREWS: Yes. I think that
in either case the employers should
furnish the information to the Ministry.
Mr. LESLIE : Does the Minister
mean to say that a man who is dis-
Unemployment Bill-
Committee
19 JUNE 1934
who has a
pensing with the services of a kitchen
maid under eighteen or
kitchen maid who gives him notice, must
notify the Ministry of the fact?
The
Clause is worded so very widely that one
is left in doubt, and one is inclined to
be a little persistent.
Mr. ANDREWS: I think the employer will be quite willing to furnish.
the required information. The Clause
gives compulsory powers to the Ministry
which I hope it will not be necessary to
exercise.
Mr. BARCLAY: What is the penalty
on an employer who neglects to furnish
this information.
Mr. ANDREWS: I really do not think
we can enforce that, but I do not anticipate any great trouble in connection with
this matter. There are always isolated
cases of employers who take a rather
narrow view, but this Clause is intended
mainly to deal with those employers who
are employing a reasonable number of
workers. In those cases I do not think
there will be any difficulty at all.
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

of the Statutory Committee), 19 (Advances by Ministry of Finance to Unemployment Fund), 20 (Amendments as to

26 (Payments out of moneys provided by

Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I should

By the council of the county borough

result of the revaluation at present taking

CLAUSE 43 (Penalty for fraudulently
obtaining allowance, and recovery of
overpayments).
*
(1) Any person who, for the purpose
of obtaining any payment or allowance
under this Part of this Act, either for
himself or for any other person, knowingly makes any false statement or false
representation, shall be liable on summary conviction to imprisonment for a
term not exceeding six months.
A court of summary jurisdiction
shall, in the case of a person convicted
under this sub-section, have the same
power as a court of summary jurisdiction in England of imposing a fine not
exceeding twenty-five pounds instead of
imprisonment if the court is of opinion
that the justice of the case would be
better met by a fine than by imprisonment.
*
*
*
*
*
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie :-Clause 43, page 39,
line 29, to leave out "six" ("exceeding
six") and insert "three."
Mr. LESLIE: I preceive that under
Sub-clause (1) if a man makes a false
statement or otherwise misconducts himself in that matter he is to be liable to
six months' imprisonment. My recollection is that in the English Measure now in
the English Parliament, the similar
Clause provides for a penalty of three
months. It occurs to me that we might
not unreasonably ask the Minister why a
North of Ireland offender should be subject to a penalty of twice the amount to
be imposed on his fellow criminal in
England. I am afraid this is a case
where I ought to move first of all to leave
out "six" and insert
"three."
Not
impossibly there may be an explanation.
It is three months in England and it is
proposed to make it six months here.
say nothing as to the adequacy of either
penalty.
I
Mr. ANDREWS: My hon. and learned
Friend is a wonderful investigator.
think he would have made a remarkable
detective, because he is right in what he
has discovered. Three months is the
maximum term for fraud in the British
Measure; here it is six months. This
term corresponds with the provisions in
some of our other Acts here. It corresponds, for instance, with our unemployment insurance scheme, and the reason
that six months is inserted as the maximum penalty is because it is felt that a
little more discretion should be left with
those administering the law. Hon
Senators will agree that in all cases a
penalty of three months for fraud may
not be sufficient, but because the maximum of six months is inserted in the
Measure it does not necessarily mean that
six months will be the term of imprisonment that will be decided upon. We all
abhor fraud, we all want to put down
fraud, and I think we are quite safe in
leaving six months as a discretionary
period for those who will be responsible
for dealing with these matters. I would
ask my hon. and learned Friend not to
press his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I would ask leave to
yield to the appeal of my right hon.
Friend, the Minister.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie:-Page 39, leave out

power."
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure there must
be an explanation for this somewhat un-
Unemployment Bill-

usual phrase in the Bill, and it occurred

have the same power as a court of summary jurisdiction in England. I rather
wondered why the power is, as it were,

is distinctly unusual. I would like to hear

The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon. and
learned Senator move?
Mr. LESLIE: Yes, for the moment.

Mr. ANDREWS: This is a similar

am advised that in addition to enabling

(5) Where for the purposes of this

person shall on presenting a written

The following amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie:-Leave out Subclause (5) of Clause 45.
Mr. LESLIE: This is a small question.
There is apparently provision here for the
issue of birth, marriage, and death certificates at a lower rate. It occurred to me
to ask whether anybody would be at any
loss by the issuing of these certificates at
a lower rate than usual, or whether there
is anything fresh in this legislation. I
have not had time to check my memory,
but I think that somewhat similar provisions occur in other Acts. The Minister will be able to tell us about them.
The Committee will see the point. The
point is that we should not inadvertently
do an injustice to any of the registrars
who depend upon these fees.
Mr. ANDREWS: I do not think any
injustice will be done under this Clause.
Applicants for unemployment assistance,
my hon. and learned Friend knows, in
certain circumstances have to prove age,
et cetera, and this Clause is proposed in
order that they may be able to get the
necessary proof cheaply. It is true that
the registrars who will give these certificates will not obtain as large fees as they
do normally, but on the other hand it is
anticipated that the number of fees which
they will obtain, although the amount in
each case may be smaller, will be more
numerous, and to some extent, at any
rate, they will be compensated. I feel
we must, in a matter of this kind, put
first things first. To enable a person
seeking unemployment assistance
obtain the necessary information
cheaply as possible is the first consideration in a matter of this kind.

CLAUSE 46 (Rules and regulations).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: I did not put anything
on the Paper because I had not time, but
I observe that in the House of Lords,
when the corresponding Clause, which
appears to be Clause 52, of the English
Measure, was reached, there was moved
by Lord Rochester, Paymaster-General,
who is in charge of the Bill for the
Government, an Amendment, providing
that a rule made by the Unemployment
Assistance Board may be annulled by
resolution of either House of Parliament,
but without prejudice to the validity of
anything previously done under the rule
or to the making of a new rule. That is
an ordinary provision, but it is not a provision which appears in the Bill before
us. It occurred to me to inquire from
the Minister whether he would not take
the opportunity before this Bill leaves
this House of bringing that particular
Sub-clause into line with the English
Measure as it stands at present. This is
a matter one would suggest which might
very well be dealt with after the Third
Reading. There is scarcely time to consider it this afternoon.
Mr. ANDREWS: A more convenient
way of making the change, if I may say
so, will be to leave it to His Grace the
Governor to do so by Order in Council.
Under this Bill he will have the necessary
power. That will be more convenient
than having to refer the matter back to
the House of Commons. We have all the
power that is required to enable the
change to be made to which the hon. and
learned Member has referred.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not like being in
any way obstructive, as the Minister
knows, but this is really a strong thing
to leave to His Grace by Order in Council.
It affects the powers of the Houses of
Parliament. I should like the Minister
to take it into further consideration. He
Mr. ANDREWS: As the considered
policy of the Government is to follow in
all essential matters the course pursued
at Westminster in connection with unemployment insurance legislation, I do not
think we are giving a very free hand or
very great power to His Grace the
Governor in asking him, by Order in
Council, to make this change. It is
simply to keep our legislation in the main
in accordance with unemployment insurance legislation as existing in the rest of
the United Kingdom.

(2) For the purposes of this Part of
this Act, a person shall be deemed
according to the law in Northern
Ireland not to have attained the age of
sixteen years until the commencement
of the sixteenth anniversary of the day
of his birth, and similarly with respect
to other ages.
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie :-Clause 49, leave
out Sub-section (2).
Mr. LESLIE: Sub-clause (2) seems to
me to be rather an extraordinary provision, but no doubt the Minister of
Labour can give us some explanation.
The Sub-clause suggests that a person shall
be deemed, according to the law of
Northern Ireland, not to have attained
the age of sixteen years until the commencement of the sixteenth anniversary
of the day of his birth. The commencement of the sixteenth anniversary of the
day of one's birth is one's seventeenth
birthday, because one's first birthday is
the day on which one is born. You are
not one year old until your second birthday. Normally the law is that a person
389 Unemployment Bill-

19 JUNE 1934
Committee
attains a given age on the commencement
of the day preceding the next anniversary,
so one would have expected, according to
the ordinary method of calculation, that
a person would attain the age of sixteen
years on the last day of his sixteenth
year, instead of, as in the Bill, on the
first day of the next year. I want to
know why the Minister has done this
thing and if he really means it.
Mr. ANDREWS: This is a very
interesting little point, though I do not
think in practice there will be very much
in it. A definition of some kind is
obviously required and this Clause is
similar to those which already exist in
connection with several other of our
Measures such as Health Insurance, Contributory Pensions, and Unemployment
Insurance. It is very hard to draft a
Clause to which exception cannot be
taken in this connection, because it seems
to me it is all a question of hours.
Mr. LESLIE: No.
Mr. ANDREWS: A person might be
born in the afternoon. In practice I feel
there is very little in it. Of course it is
a very interesting point to discuss.
Mr. LESLIE: It really does seem to
me that there is a departure being made
here from the ordinary law. I might remind the Minister that the law takes no
account of fractions of a day. You are
equally born on the 1st October whether
you are born at one o'clock in the morning, one o'clock in the afternoon, or
twelve o'clock at night. We have also to
consider what effect, if any, this is going
to have on other things. If the Minister
will refer to page 23 of the Bill before us
he will see at the bottom of the that
page
For the purposes of this Part of
this Act a person shall be deemed to be
between any two
therein menages
tioned if he has attained the first
mentioned age but has not attained the
second mentioned age."
I do not see how he is going to reconcile
these two passages in the Bill. I am
quite inclined to believe that this is a
matter of very small importance, but I do
not think a good case has been made out
for this departure from the ordinary law.
However, I am not going to move an
Amendment. It is not worth it.
Mr. ANDREWS: If the hon. Member
wishes to press the matter I will look into
it before the Third Reading. When he
says it is a departure from the ordinary
law I would not dispute his opinion on a
matter of the kind. It really might be.
But it is not a departure from the law
as regards our social services.
Mr. LESLIE: I will try to put something down for the Third Reading.

The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :
Page 63, paragraph 10, leave out the
paragraph.
Mr. LESLIE : I want to direct the
attention of the Committee to this paragraph because, as far as I know, this is
the first occasion on which we have given
power to acquire land without also
attaching the privilege of holding that
land without licence in mortmain. I am
quite prepared to accept the explanation
which I feel sure the Minister is about to
give, but at the same time, as I believe
Potato Marketing Bill
Unemployment Bill

THE SENATE
the
[Mr. Leslie]
this is the first time this is being done,
I feel that we ought to be told the reason
for doing it. I may tell the Committee
that in the first copy of the corresponding
Bill presented at Westminster
ordinary condition to the effect that the
Board was to have perpetual succession
and power to hold land without licence in
mortmain was included. Later editions
of the same Bill have dropped that, so I
presume some alteration was made in
Committee. It is rather an important
constitutional point and I think we should
be told definitely why we have felt able-
I put it that way-to discard the ordinary
provision.
Mr. ANDREWS: The words "without
licence in mortmain,' " which appeared in
the original British Bill were omitted at
the same time as a paragraph corresponding with paragraph 9 in the Northern
Ireland Bill was introduced providing
that the functions of the Board shall be
exercised on behalf of the Crown. I am
advised that such a licence is not required
by a body acting on behalf of the Crown,
and if my hon. and learned Friend will
refer to paragraph 9 he will see that the
functions of the Board, and of all officers
and servants appointed by the Board,
shall be exercised on behalf of the Crown.
In the original British Measure paragraph
9 did not appear and paragraph 10
appeared in the form quoted by the hon.
and learned Member. It has been held
that when paragraph 9 was introduced
into the British Bill it was not necessary
that paragraph 10 should remain as it
previously was, and we have followed
along the same lines.
Mr. LESLIE: I am much obliged to
the Minister for that explanation. It is,
as I say, rather an interesting constitutional point in a small way. This is
the first time that we have recognised a
Board of this sort as acting on behalf of
the Crown. That, of course, is the reason
for the decision taken.
