19 JUNE 1934
Milk and Milk Products Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
""
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill."
Mr. LESLIE : I do not think this
House has had proper time to consider
the details of this distinctly difficult Bill.
I should have preferred not to have
opposed this Motion this afternoon but to
have submitted later an Amendment to
the penultimate Clause of the Bill to the
effect that the provisions of the Act relating to the sale of milk according to grades
of quality and so on should not come into
operation until such date as Parliament,
and not the Minister of Agriculture,
might hereafter determine, and that they
should then be subject to modification.
But the question then arose whether you,
Mr. Speaker, My Lord, would accept an
Amendment of that sort at that stage.
Mr. SPEAKER: As far as I have been
able to consider the Amendment-and I
had, of course, only a few moments to
look at it before the House assembled-I
'think there is no doubt that the effect of
it would be to disagree with certain
important principles in the Bill, and, as
those principles have already been
accepted by the House when they passed
the Second Reading of the Bill, I consider that such an Amendment would not
be in order.
trying not to make a Second Reading
speech but it is a little difficult. I am of
opinion that, desirable as it may be from
the point of view of the creamery, this
House should not put this Bill into Committee until after some little time which
would enable the effect of it both on what
is called the liquid milk producer himself
and on the supply of milk, particularly in
the country districts, to be properly considered. I am afraid that if this Bill goes
through as it is, and there has not been
time in which to consider or to put down
reasoned Amendments on those points,
the result will be a milk famine in the
first case, and, possibly as a secondary
consequence, that the children in the
country will have nothing to drink except
separated milk returned from the
creameries. I am afraid I must on those
grounds oppose the proposal of the noble
Viscount to take this Bill in Committee
this afternoon, although, as I say, I
would have much preferred that we should
do what we could on what I call the
creamery side of it and leave the rest for
further investigation. That being
impossible, and having regard to the
interests I have mentioned, I could not
properly agree to taking this Bill so soon.
There has not been time.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to support
the point of view put before the House
by Senator Leslie. I can speak from my
own experience. This morning, on
coming into my office at a quarter to ten,
I received over 20 Amendments to these
important Bills, and I have not had a
moment to look into them. I am afraid
that
other hon. Members are in the
many
same position. The result is that we have
come here inadequately informed as to
the effect of these Amendments. I quite
agree that this is not by any means the
fault of the Leader of the House or the
Parliamentary Secretary, who are always
willing and anxious to meet the views of
every Member of the House as far as they
possibly can. I should think that the
pressure to rush these Bills through this
Speaking
Mr. LESLIE: That being so one is
thrown back and one's only hope is to
object to taking the Committee stage with
such undue haste. It is an example of
the undue haste with which this Bill is

suggested to your Lordship a few minutes
before the House sat, an Amendment
which, in fact, could not have been communicated to the House any earlier. The
draft Amendment was posted from where
I happened to be on Sunday and only
reached this House this morning. This

personally, I certainly am not in

which I received only this forenoon.
cannot be argued that the Bill has

a
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is
rather a difficult matter to reply to.
There is, undoubtedly, some substance in
the objections made by the hon. Senators
Milk Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
who have spoken. Unfortunately, it is
not possible to pass some Clauses
immediately and postpone others for later
consideration. We cannot do that. At
the same time there are some very good
reasons for urgency in this matter. If
we were to postpone the Committee stage,
we could not possibly have the Bill
finished by the time another place meets
next week to consider what Amendments,
if any, we have made to it. There is also
great urgency in getting this Bill through
Parliament, because there are many
excellent reasons for its coming into
operation immediately which have been
mentioned in another place-200,000 of
them, as a matter of fact.
Mr. LESLIE: For the creameries.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: More time
has been given here for the consideration
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Cunningham, The Right Honourable
Samuel
Leslie, Mr. James G.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
of this Bill than was given in another
place, though admittedly the time has
been short. At the same time, as I have
said, the Minister of Agriculture is
anxious to bring the Bill into operation
as soon as it possibly can be done, and a
great deal of administrative work is necessary before the Act begins to function.
If we were to postpone the Bill over the
present date, it could not receive the
Royal Assent next week, and the whole
operation of the Bill would have to be
postponed until early in 1935. I hope
that under these circumstances the House
will decide, despite the objections that
have been raised, to proceed with the consideration in Committee of this Bill.

agreed to."

CLAUSE 1 (Sale of milk according to
grades of quality).
Colonel PAKENHAM: On Clause 1,
sub-Clause 2, dealing with grades of milk.
I should like to ascertain from the
Minister of Agriculture in this connection
what he is going to do with tuberculous
COWS. Are they to be slaughtered, or is
a man with a tuberculous cow going to be
I am
allowed to sell it to another man.
naturally very anxious to know what is
going to be done. If these tuberculous
cattle are not to be slaughtered, it means
that they will be bought and sold and will
go on producing milk. I want to know if
the Government are taking power compulsorily to slaughter cattle whose milk is
tuberculous. It seems to me that, unless
you are taking power to slaughter these
cattle, you are going to perpetuate
tuberculosis in herds. If the Government
are going to slaughter these cattle, are
they going to pay more than threequarters of their value as compensation?

McMahon, Mr. J.
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE
(Sir Basil Brooke): No provision of the
kind is being taken in this Bill, and there
is no provision for the purpose other than
the Tuberculosis Order which I mentioned
on a previous occasion.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that, in spite of
the shortness of the time, I ought to move,
on page 3, lines 25 to 30, to leave out
sub-Clause 6, which says, in effect, that
what is Class D milk may not be
sold for buman consumption. If
this House passes that Sub-clause, and if
the Minister of Agriculture brings this
part of the Act, as it will be then, into
operation within any short space of time,
the milk famine of which I have spoken
will certainly arise in the country districts. It will not be possible, as it seems
to me, for people to get all their milk up
to Grade A, B, or C, even if they want to
do it, within a reasonable time.
We have just heard about the
tuberculous cows. We heard the other
day from an hon. Senator opposite, that
although a cow might be tuberculous, it
Milk Bill-
Committee
19 JUNE 1934
did not follow that its milk would be
tuberculous. If there are anything like
the number of tuberculous COWS in
Northern Ireland which there are
supposed to be, and if those cows are
taken out of the milk factory at an early
date, there will inevitably be a very
serious shortage of milk. It seems to me
that the only hope for the small
supplier in the country districts who does
not want to incur the expense attendant
on raising the quality of his milk to A,
B, or C, and who still less wants to pay
a levy for the benefit of the farmers who
sell their milk to the creameries, is to
allow him to go on selling Class D milk
until Parliament otherwise provides. I
do not think that the time is ripe for
introducing suddenly so complete a change
as that involved by the sub-clause before
us. Accordingly, I think we ought to
leave out that sub-clause, and I move
accordingly.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I regret
that I cannot agree to the Amendment proposed by the hon. Senator,
but I can assure him of this,
that the introduction of the Grades
will be carried out very gradually, and
that we will certainly take all possible
measures to avoid any shortage of milk.
I understand that that is the hon.
Senator's fear. Therefore, to begin with,
the Grades will start at a comparatively
low level.
The CHAIRMAN: Amendment, by
leave, withdrawn?
Mr. LESLIE: I think I must ask you
to collect the voices.
Question, "That those words be left
out," put, and negatived.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

of milk intended for sale), and 4
(Licensing of sellers of butter and
margarine), ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I should
like to ask the Minister of Agriculture to
give a little sympathetic consideration to
one point in this Clause. I have not put
an
Amendment on the Paper partly
through lack of time, and partly because
I prefer to bring the point to the notice
of the Minister in this way. On page 5,
lines 15 to 20, it is provided that-
When the holder of a producer's
licence sells milk to the holder of a
distributor's licence, the latter shall
deduct from the price paid by him for
the grade of milk so sold a sum equal
to the fee per gallon . . .
pays
and so on. In that case the producer
pays the full amount of the levy, whatever
it may be, and the distributor
nothing at all except the £1 referred to
higher up for his licence, and I am quite
willing to concede that the producer who
is selling direct to a customer is entitled
Milk Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Sir Dudley McCorkell]
and so on.
to pay the full levy, which is not a hardship in that case; but where the producer
is selling wholesale to a big distributor he
is getting very much less for what he has
produced, yet he has been put to considerable expenditure in setting up new plant
In other words the distributor
has got the best of the bargain. I appeal
to the Minister to see if it would not be
possible in the case where the producer is
selling wholesale to the distributor, and
where the distributor is getting the best
of the bargain, to have a division of the
levy between the two. Because after all
if the levy has to be paid by the producer
who has got a good price selling by retail
to the consumer he has got very much less
when he sells to the distributor, and to
put it perfectly candidly it is playing into
the hands of the big distributor. This
Bill is not promoted purely for the benefit
of the big distributor. I make an appeal
to the Minister that he might consider
splitting the levy.
Mr. SPEAKER: Would the hon.
Senator say exactly which Clause he is
speaking on?
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: Clause
2.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think if Members
of the Committee wish to ask any information on a Clause they should say so
when the Clause is put, but perhaps I
was going too fast.
Sir DUDLEY McCORKELL:
apologise. It is a mistake of mine.

Mr. SPEAKER: We have passed that
Clause, but as it was merely a case of

question.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL:
apologise for the mistake.

Sir BASIL BROOKE: I think the
difference is this, that the producerretailer, as the hon. Senator mentioned,
would have the advantage of the higher
price, but he would have to set up cooling
equipment and bottling equipment. In
the case of the producer selling to the distributor, and I understand that is what
the hon. Senator meant, the producer
would not have the expense of setting up
equipment and, therefore, that expense
would be on the distributor.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: Would
the distributor not have to purchase a
certain grade of milk?
Sir BASIL BROOKE: Yes. It would
have to be a certain grade, but the actual
bottling and cooling would be carried out
by the wholesale distributor.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Would the Minister say whether the producer would not
be put to considerable expense in raising
the grade of milk?
Sir BASIL BROOKE: Yes. He
would.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Duties and powers of
Joint Milk Council).
(1) It shall be the duty of the
Council to regulate sales of milk by
determining, from time to time, the
respective prices at, below or above
which, and the respective terms on
which milk of Grade A, Grade B or
Grade C may be sold by the holder of
a Producer's licence or a Distributor's
licence.
(2) Without prejudice to the generality of the provisions of sub-section (1)
of this section, the Council shall have
power to prescribe the terms on which
and the form in which contracts shall
be made for the sale of milk of Grade
A, Grade B or Grade C by holders of
Producers' licences to holders of Distributors' licences.
(3) Notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained in this section-
(a) a determination of the Council
thereunder shall not apply to the
sale or delivery of milk to a
creamery, and
(b) a determination of the Council
thereunder as to the price at, below
or above which milk may be sold
by retail shall not have effect
except with the approval of the
Ministry of Agriculture.
(4) It shall be the duty of the
Council to give advice and assistance to
the Ministry of Agriculture in relation
to the standards to be prescribed for the
sale of milk, the conditions for the issue
of Producers' and Distributors' licences,
Milk Bill-
Committee
19 JUNE 1934
and such other matters as may be referred to the Council by the said
Ministry.
(5) Any person who-
(a) sells or offers or exposes for sale
milk of Grade A, Grade B or
Grade C, or
(b) being the holder of a distributor's
licence, buys milk of Grade A,
Grade B or Grade C
otherwise than at a price and on conditions which are in accordance with a
determination of the Council under this
section shall be guilty of an offence
under this Act.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move at the end of sub-section (2) to add
the following sub-section :-
"(3) If, at a meeting of the Council
held for determining the price at, below
or above which, or the terms on which,
milk of Grade A, Grade B or Grade C
may be sold as aforesaid, the members
present, other than those appointed by
the Minister of Agriculture, are not
unanimous upon the question of the
price or terms to be so determined,
then the three members of the Council
appointed by the said Minister shall
decide the question and communicate
their decision to the other members of
the Council and to the said Minister,
and the decision so communicated shall
have effect as if it were a decision of the
whole Council.
The Ministry of Agriculture may by
regulations provide for carrying this
sub-section into effect, and in particular may prescribe the procedure to
be observed thereunder."
The Government has put down this
Amendment with the object of allaying
certain misgivings which have apparently
been experienced by hon. Senators with
regard to the powers conferred on the
three members of the Joint Milk Council
nominated by the Minister of Agriculture.
In order to make the position perfectly
clear I wish to point out that there will
be two types of questions which may be.
considered by the Joint Milk Council.
On all ordinary questions there will be
no distinction between the nominees of
the Minister of Agriculture and the other
members of the Council, and such
questions will be settled by a majority
vote of the Council. That is, questions
of standards of the milk itself, conditions
for issue of licences and questions of
administration generally. On a question
of the price and the contract terms on
which Grade A, Grade B and Grade C
milk may be sold, the position will be
that before a decision of the Council will
be valid the fourteen members of the
Council, other than those nominated by
the Minister of Agriculture, must be
unanimous.
In view of the divergent interests concerned-producers, distributors and consumers-it is clearly desirable that in the
question of prices a unanimous decision
should be required so as to prevent one or
two interests imposing their will upon the
others. In default of unanimity among
the fourteen members of the Council
representing producers, distributors and
consumers, the matter will be determined
by the three nominees of the Minister of
Agriculture who will act as arbitrators.
Their presence on the Council will mean
that they will bring to their task of
arbitration a knowledge of the matter to
be determined far greater than would be
the case if they were called in to arbitrate
only when negotiations among the other
members of the Council had broken down.
It will mean, also, that in addition to
their functions as potential arbitrators,
the three nominees of the Minister will
frequently be in a position to exercise a
valuable influence as mediators in the discussions.
I may add that this arrangement under
which the nominees of the Ministry of
Agriculture will act as arbitrators in the
event of the other interests on the Council
failing to agree has the unqualified support of the representatives of both producers and distributors who have been
consulted. This position is now made
clear by the Amendment which stands in
my name. The Amendment also enables
the Ministry to make regulations so as
to secure that the provision is such as to
give effect to the arrangement which I
have described.
Colonel PAKENHAM: Would it be
possible for the Government to tell us
what type of men is going to be selected
as the Minister of Agriculture's representatives? Are they going to be civil
servants or independent gentlemen who
know something about milk or who are in
Milk Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Colonel Pakenham]
the milk trade, distributing, producing,
or making butter? I am very anxious to
hear. It would make a big difference if
we knew the calibre of the men. It is a
very big job to arbitrate where the fourteen have not come to an agreement.
I
would like to be sure that the persons
doing the arbitration are perfectly able
to do it and have the requisite knowledge.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I hope the
gentlemen whom I shall pick to carry out
this very difficult and useful work will be
men of high standing in this country,
not necessarily connected-in fact I think
it would be inadvisable to have them connected either with the producing or distributing. They will be
men with
business knowledge and men who will be
able to consider all the facts.
Amendment agreed to.
Question proposed, "That the Clause,
as amended, stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: We have dealt with
Clause 6 up to line 12. The point I
wanted to raise was on sub-clause (4),
which begins at line 22. I am going to
ask the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Agriculture, to tell us a little more
of what he means by the standards to be
prescribed for the sale of milk, and
whether advice on these matters will be a
matter for the whole of his Joint Milk
Council if they agree or if they do not
agree. Supposing they do not agree is
this one of the questions to be decided
by the three representatives of the
Ministry of Agriculture?
Sir BASIL BROOKE: The only
question to be decided by the arbitrators
is the question of the price of milk. The
question of the actual grade or standards
will be decided by the Committee. There
is no over-riding power.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Seeing that the
suggested New Clause contains not only
the price but the "terms" would the
Minister assure us that the arbitrators will
keep in mind entirely the question of
price and not terms?
Mr. LESLIE: May I take it the expression "standard" used there is the
same as "grade" used elsewhere? If you
refer to sub-clause (2) of Clause 1 the
designations indicating the quality of
milk are referred to as grades. I did not
discover the word "standard" anywhere
else.
I want to be sure it refers to the
milk and not price. It may be either
milk or price as it stands there.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: As I read that
sub-section it refers to standard of
quality. Where price comes in the
arbitrators would have an over-riding
power.
Mr. LESLIE: Standard does mean
grade?
Sir BASIL BROOKE: Yes.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 1 (Penalties for reckless or
dangerous driving).

(2) Where a person is convicted of
an offence under sub-section (1) of
Section one of the principal Act, as
amended by this Act, and a period of
three years or more has elapsed since his
last previous conviction for
a like
offence, the court may, if it thinks
fit, deal with such first-mentioned
offence as a first offence under the said
sub-section, and where the court so
deals with such first-mentioned offence,
paragraph (i) of the proviso to subsection (1) of Section four of the
principal Act shall not apply.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in subsection (2) after the word "offence"
("First mentioned offence") to insert
"sub-section (4) of Section twenty-one
and." I ought to explain to the House
that sub-section (4) of Section 1 of the
principal Act is the sub-section which provides that any police officer or constable
who appears on any information, complaint or application shall have the right
to appeal against any order made in such
court and so on. I do not think that it
could have been the intention, with this
New Clause so constructed, allowing a
period of three years I do not think it
could have been the intention that the
police constable should have the right to
appeal against that. I confess I am not
too certain that the wording of the
original sub-clause (4) would have the
effect of giving him a right of appeal, but
I think, on the whole, it would. I do not
think it was the intention that the constable should be let in, and in order that
there should be no misundertanding I
move the words on the Paper.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of Clause 1 to insert as a new subsection:
"(3) Section two of the Act of 1929
is hereby repealed."
This is a proposal we withdrew at the

I think everybody must agree on reflection that it
would be better that the two new
Sections should stand on their own legs,
and therefore we ought to say here in

driving that the other Section is
repealed.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government is prepared to accept this

CLAUSE 2 (Penalties for driving
motor-car when under influence of drink
or drugs).
*
*
*
*
*
On the trial of any indictment for
manslaughter in connection with the
driving of a motor-car, the jury may
acquit the defendant of manslaughter
and find him guilty of an
offence
under this section.
*
*
*
(2) Sections two and three of the
Act of 1929 are hereby repealed.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave
out the last paragraph of sub-clause (1),
lines 20 to 23 inclusive. This also was
referred to on the Committee Stage, and
an Amendment was withdrawn in the
hope that the point of view I took might
on further consideration commend itself
to the Government. It must, I think,
be fairly evident to the Committee that
if you leave that paragraph in as it
stands it gives the jury a chance of returning an absolutely ridiculous verdict.
Road Traffic Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
There may be nothing whatever in the
evidence relating to drink or drugs, and
you are giving a jury another chance
of escaping a manslaughter verdict by
returning this verdict. It is quite unnecessary to put these words in, because
we are taking power to indict a man
separately for being drunk in charge of
a car. If a man is charged with manslaughter when he was drunk the proper
course surely is to put up an indictment
for manslaughter, and, if you like,
another indictment for drunkenness, or,
if that course is considered preferable, to
make a second count in the indictment
for drunkenness. But there certainly
should be two separate indictments. It
should not be left to the jury to return
a verdict which there may be no evidence
whatever to support. You might just as
well provide that where a person is
charged with arson, setting hire to a
house, the jury might find him guilty
of breaking and entering. The two
things are not necessarily connected, and
we should not go out of our way to put
in words that are not necessary, and
might possibly lead to ridiculous
result.
a
Viscount CHARLEMONT: When the
hon. and learned Senator moved this
Amendment on the Committee Stage of
the Bill he pointed out that the proposal
was, to some extent at any rate, similar
to that which the Committee had rejected
in the previous Clause. He based his
arguments
on this subject on the
decision given by the Court of
Criminal Appeal in England in the
case of the King v. Stringer, and
it is hardly necessary for me to say that
my right hon. Friend, the Attorney-
General, had given very careful consideration to this case before advising
the Government as to the proposals
which they should put before Parliament
in this regard. The point is really one.
of practice rather than principle, and I
think this House will agree that provided
the accused is not put in a
vantageous position, and provided he is
not hampered in making his defence, the
procedure to be adopted should be as
simple and as inexpensive as possible.
disad-
The gist of the matter is this. The
hon. and learned Senator says that where
the driver of a motor-car is accused of
causing death by having been under the
influence of drink or drugs when in
charge of the vehicle and is sent forward
for trial to the Assizes there should be
one indictment charging him with manslaughter and a second indictment
charging him with being drunk in charge
of a car. If the jury acquit him of
manslaughter he should then be put up
and tried a second time upon the second
indictment charging him
him with being
drunk in charge of a car. There would
be, presumably, the same evidence
tendered, and the whole procedure of a
trial would have to be repeated on the
same set of facts.
In
man-
On the other hand, the proposal in the
Bill is that an indictment should be set
with
up charging the accused
slaughter, but if the jury come to the
conclusion that the facts do not warrant
a conviction of manslaughter they may,
if they think right, convict him of
being drunk in charge of a car. There
is only one trial. The accused and his
advisers know that the jury have this
alternative, and it is extremely difficult
to see wherein the accused is at a disadvantage.
one direction the
accused has a distinct advantage as a
result of the provision proposed to be
inserted in the Bill. In Great Britain
if the accused is indicted for manslaughter the Crown can put him on
trial again for the statutory offence. On
the other hand, in Northern Ireland if
the jury find the accused not guilty of
manslaughter and do not bring in a verdict of guilty of being drunk in charge
of a car, on this charge the accused cannot be tried a second time for the statutory offence. I do not think that the
House will have any difficulty in coming
to the conclusion that the procedure
under the Clause, which is similar to that
to which the House has already given
approval under Clause 1, is convenient
and that it does not put the accused at
a disadvantage.
We have had experience for some years
of this procedure, and His Majesty's
Attorney General, who is particularly
qualified to judge in this matter, has
given it as his opinion that the procedure
is one which has assisted the due administration of the law, and he has given this
opinion after having considered very
carefully the case to which the hon, and
learned Senator has referred. On the
Committee stage of the Bill Senator
Road Traffic Bill-
Report Stage.
19 JUNE 1934
a
no
I
Leslie suggested that by retaining this
Clause we should be inviting a jury to find
a man guilty of being drunk in charge of
motor-car when there may be
evidence whatever that he was drunk.
must confess that I am entirely unable to
follow this line of reasoning, and that if
we are to attach any weight to it we would
be assuming that the trial judge would
not perform one of his most important
functions which is, when there is no
evidence on which a jury can convict, to
direct acquittal. For these reasons I
regret that I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I have to
say that there is scarcely a word which
the noble Viscount has said with which I
am able to agree, but I do not propose to
divide the House again.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 2, to leave out line 33 (Sections
two and three of the Act of 1929 are), and
to insert instead, Section three of the
Act of 1929 is." This is the other half
of the Amendment the House did me the
honour of accepting a few moments ago.
We want to leave out the whole line to
get the plural into the singular. The
result will be that the work will be complete.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 25 (Provisions as to goods
vehicle licences).
*
(1) Goods vehicle licences shall be
granted by the Ministry of Home
Affairs and shall be in such form as
may be prescribed.
*
*
(3) Any condition on which a goods
vehicle licence is to be granted may be
either prescribed by regulations or
inserted in or endorsed on the licence
in respect of a vehicle; and a condition
so inserted in or endorsed on a licence
shall have the like effect as if it were
prescribed by regulations.

Mr. LESLIE: 1 beg to move, in Clause
25, sub-clause (1), page 16, line 33, to
leave out "and shall be," and insert the
words, on such conditions and." The
House may remember that in Committee
I found a difficulty in sub-clause (3) of
this Clause which appears near the top of
page 17. We discussed it at some little
length, and I ultimately withdrew an
Amendment to leave out the whole subclause with the intention of substituting
something new later on. But in looking
into it further it seems to me that the
difficulty in sub-clause (3) is the expression "conditions on which a goods vehicle
licence is to be granted.'
"" It seemed to
me that you could not very well talk
about conditions on which a licence is to
be granted and at the same time prescribe
by your regulations for inserting and
endorsing licences which apparently did
not then exist. I think that is what gave
me trouble, and in looking into it it
seemed to me that the way out was to
make the two alterations I have on the
Paper. That is, in line 33 on page 16 to
put in that goods licences are to be on
such conditions and in such form as might
be prescribed, and so, as it were, throw
on the Ministry the necessity of prescribing regulations, more or less general, on
which these licences are to be granted,
which regulations would necessarily have
to come before Parliament.
Road Traffic Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
Then it would be necessary to follow
that up by the alteration I have on the
Paper on page 17, lines 4 and 7. It would
enable the particular condition which was
spoken of by the noble Viscount on the
Committee stage, that a vehicle must not
be used on a particular road for some
reason, to be inserted on licences. But I
do not think we ought to leave the thing
in the hands of the Ministry in such a
vague manner as the Clause leaves it. As
the Clause stands I still think it leaves
it open to the Ministry to make some
condition which is not sanctioned either
generally or particularly by Parliament.
I do not propose that we should come to
Parliament for every little trifle, but I
think we ought to leave the matter in such
a position that Parliamentary control is
not avoided altogether. The first step.
seems to me to move the alteration which
appears on the Paper at page 16, line 33,
to leave out the words "and shall be "
and insert on such conditions and," so
that the Clause will read, "Licences
shall be granted by the Ministry of Home
Affairs on such conditions and in such
form as may be prescribed." I beg to
move.
""
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think if
the House agreed to the hon. and learned
Senator's Amendment it would make the
administration in matters of this character
extremely cumbersome and slow. I dare
say that such hon. Members as were
alive at the time of the South African
War will remember a picture in a comic
paper referring to the extraordinary
difficulty in catching General De Wet.
Owing to the columns operating and the
men engaged the hero of the picture was
represented as saying it was like using a
traction engine to catch rats. I think
that would rather be the effect of the
Amendment suggested. My right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs,
will as it is be responsible to Parliament
for his administration and any action he
may take in this particular case can be
questioned either in this House or in
another place in numerous ways. I am
afraid, therefore, I am unable to accept
the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I will not press it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page, 17, lines 4 to 7, leave out from
the beginning of line 4 to "and" in
line 7, and insert "where a goods vehicle
licence for a particular vehicle is granted
subject to any special condition relating
to that vehicle such special condition may
be inserted in or endorsed on the
licence."
Amendment not moved.
CLAUSE 32 (Conditions regulating
use of lamps to be carried).
Viscount CHARLEMONT: May I
make a remark on Clause 32 in reply to
a question which the Mayor of Derry
asked the other day? He asked the
effect of lights that swivel to the left.
The answer is that this device will not
be barred and that the necessary regulations will be made exempting this
device from the operation of the Clause.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I am
very much obliged for the answer to the
question. It is quite clear.
CLAUSE 51 (Non-application cf
Probation of Offenders Act).
Where before a court of summary
jurisdiction a person is convicted of
any of the offences set out in the Second
Schedule to this Act, the provisions of
the Probation of Offenders Act, 1907,
shall not apply to that person.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 51, page 29, line 26, to leave out
"Act" ("Act, 1907") and insert Acts,
1887, and".
I have got to confess I do not know
the answer to the little problem I am
putting before the House. I do not like
the Clause at all, but that does not
matter. The point is that the Probation of Offenders Act, 1907, had a provision in it which repealed three other
Acts, one the Act of 1887. There were
two others. One did not apply to Ireland and the other has since been
repealed. There remains the Probation
of Offenders Act, 1887, and the question
in my possibly horrible mind is whether
now that we are saying definitely that
the provisions of the Act of 1907 shall
not apply to a particular person we are
not reviving the Act of 1887.
Road Traffic Bill
Adjournment
19 JUNE 1934
The House, I am sure, knows the
ordinary rule. The ordinary rule is that
where an enactment which repeals
another enactment is itself repealed the
repeal of the repealing enactment does
not revive the first one unless it appears
to be the intention of Parliament that it
should do so. The trouble and difficulty
I am in is whether in this case before
us we can rely on what we have here as
being sufficiently a repealing enactment
to enable that to operate. If the noble
Viscount is going to advise us that it is
all right the Amendment falls, but if on
the contrary the matter is in any doubt
or we are not satisfied, then I think the
words I have put on the Paper ought to
go in. I do not remember any occasion
on which I have had greater doubt as to
whether a proposal I was making was
right or wrong. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
advised that the Amendment is unnecessary. I would sooner give the hon. and
learned Senator the assurance that the
Clause as it is is correct than go into any
explanation which talks a great deal about
repealing an Act which has repealed a
previous Act which has revised an Act
before that which has repealed a previous
Act. All those words have failed to produce any feeling of satisfaction in my
mind but rather confusion. I would
prefer to give him the assurance that the
Clause as it stands is correct.
Mr. LESLIE: That is all I wanted to
know.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following New Clause stood in the
name of Mr. McLaughlin :
"If on any highway any person
drives a heavy goods vehicle to which a
trailer is attached at a speed of more
than twenty miles an hour he shall be
guilty of an offence under the principal
Act.
""
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I do not move
this New Clause because I have had a
letter from Mr. D. L. Clarke, of the
Ministry of Home Affairs, explaining the
Order, No. 137, which was issued in 1931
and which fixes the speed limit of
public motor vehicles at 25 miles an
hour and that of motor lorries with
trailers at from 20 miles an hour down
to as low as 12 miles an hour. In
indicating that I do not move the Clause
I wish to express my appreciation of and
thanks for the valuable information
supplied by Mr. D. L. Clarke.
Amendment not moved.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third Time on Thursday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this House do now adjourn
until to-morrow."
I would like to say that to-morrow I
hope to take the Third Reading and
final stages of the Marketing of Potatoes
Bill and the Report stage of the Milk and
Milk Products Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Ten Minutes past Five
o'clock.

Milk and Milk Products Bill.
Order for receiving report of Amendments read.
Moved: "That this Report be now
received." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. McHUGH: I beg to move the
following new Clause:
"Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, the Minister of
Agriculture may interpret the word
creamery as meaning a creamery in
the Irish Free State, provided that
any payments made to producers
under this Act, are confined to producers in Northern Ireland who have
been in the habit of sending their
milk to a creamery situated in the
Irish Free State.
I was compelled to introduce this at
the last moment as I had no opportunity
of having it placed upon the Order
Paper during the Committee Stage,
owing to the way the Bill was rushed
through this House. Listening to the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Agriculture, one would naturally think
that this was a trifling Bill and merely
a matter dealing with a
very small
portion of the people. But when one
considers that farming in
in Northern
Ireland is the principal industry, one
would naturally consider that some
more serious consideration should be
given to a Measure and not have it
rushed through at the last moment as is
generally done with a large number of
Bills in this House.
I have introduced this Amendment
for the sole purpose of inviting the
Minister of Agriculture to reconsider
the matter. He surely cannot penalise
the farmers of Northern Ireland who
unfortunately live along the Border.
They did not make the Border and they
are not responsible for it in any way
whatsoever, and I think they should
receive the same treatment as any other
in Northern Ireland.
milk producer in
That was the sole reason why I introduced this Clause, to invite him to reconsider the position. After all, the people
along the Border have many other
difficulties to contend with. At the
same time, if we look at the Free State
-which was referred to on the Second
Reading-they granted a subsidy for all
producers in the Free State, and in doing
that they allowed the same grant to be
given to all milk suppliers from Northern Ireland who sent milk into the Free
State. If you consider the treatment
extended by the Free State Government
to the people who send milk from Northern Ireland, you will see they are
more lenient towards these people and
do not draw the line so harshly as the
Minister of Agriculture here has determined to do on the present occasion.
I will invite the Leader of the House
to look at page 15, which points out
clearly that all milk suppliers in Northern Ireland shall receive equal treatment. If that is so, then why should
Milk Bill-
Report Stage
20 JUNE 1934
these milk producers be deprived of the
bonus given to all other milk suppliers
and milk producers within the Six
Counties? I am speaking for people who
do not think as I do either politically or
religiously, because 70 per cent. of
them differ from me in every way, but
I put forward their grievances in this
House and I think it is the duty of
the Government carefully to consider
them. I have no axe to grind. If I
were pleading for the minority milk
producers in Northern Ireland then I
would have no hope of getting their
grievances redressed in this House.
But these people whom I plead for cling
to you and support you no matter what
your policy may be, and yet in this small
Act, introduced for the benefit of producers, they are to be crushed simply and
solely because they send milk to a Free
State creamery.
Speaking on this subject on a former
occasion I told the House that the
creamery situated at Pettigo, one I am
closely associated with, are quite prepared to do anything to comply with the
wishes of the Ministry of Agriculture.
They are prepared to establish offices in
Northern Ireland and produce their
books with the names of all milk suppliers who send milk from Northern
Ireland to a Free State creamery, so that
there could be no fraud. But if these
milk suppliers are compelled to go elsewhere, then it will mean that the cost
of carriage will absorb the whole of the
benefit they receive. I submit this
Amendment for the consideration of this
House and I do appeal to the Government to reconsider the matter. It is
quite possible they may see their way
to give some benefit to these people, and
I would suggest to the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House, that he might be
in a position between this and the Third
Reading to clear up this very awkward
position which these people find themselves in. It surely cannot be said that
because these people live along the
Border they should be penalised and
that they should not receive the same
benefits as any other citizens of Northern Ireland. On these grounds, I submit this new Clause for the serious consideration of this House.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I am very glad
this Clause has been introduced because
it brings to my mind that along the
Border of County Armagh we have a
creamery which is just a mile, or less
than a mile from the Border, and if
this Clause, or something like it, is not
inserted in the Bill it certainly will have
a very serious effect on the farmers along
the Border. I venture to say that inside the Border there is not a creamery
within four or five miles. Therefore, I
respectfully urge the noble Viscount,
the Leader of the House, to place this
matter before the Government and ask
them to consider the position of these
farmers along the Border who are sending their milk to a creamery which is
very convenient for them, but is in the
Free State. If they are obliged to send
their milk to a creamery inside the
Border of Northern Ireland it will mean
very serious inconvenience. It will
mean they will have to go four or five
miles and possibly suffer serious loss as
well, because I venture to say that some
of these farmers have a financial interest
in the creamery, and they would not
like to have to give up their business
connections with it. For their convenience I would strongly urge that the
Government should very seriously consider the Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: I, of course only saw
the Amendment put down on the Paper
by the hon. Member this morning, and,
as the House knows, I am entirely in
sympathy with him in regard to undue
haste. But it happens that the
question which
which has been raised by
the hon. Member has directed my mind
towards another inquiry which seems to
me to arise out of what has been said.
Without prejudice to what the noble
Viscount is going to say, I am afraid I
will have to put another question. The
new Clause, moved by the hon. Member,
talks about a creamery and refers to a
creamery in the Irish Free State.
cannot help wondering whether perhaps
the noble Viscount will refer the House
to the definition of creamery on page 18
of the Bill, and if he does that will
raise the question, "When is a creamery
not a creamery?"
I
The point I am trying to get at is,
if this milk is sent out of Northern
Ireland to a creamery situated, I understand, in the neighbouring territory,
called the Free State, the place to which
it is sent is not a creamery for this pur-
Milk Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
pose. Then will it be required of the
man who produces that milk that he
shall have a producer's licence under
sub-clause (5), of Clause 2, of the Bill,
and if so- -if he has to have a producer's
licence has he got to pay not only
half-a-crown, but also the levy at the
rate of 3d per gallon in respect of milk
which has been sold or delivered otherwise than for the purpose of the
extraction of cream at a creamery?
am afraid that is a question one will
have to ask the Government to explain
to us, if not to-day, in view of the short
notice although I do not think the
Government is entitled to any consideration on that ground under present conditions-but I think we ought to have
an answer to that either now or before
we finally part with the Bill on Third
Reading.
-
Viscount
I
CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): I regret that I am
unable to accept this Amendment.
There are, I know, a number of farmers
in Northern Ireland who are sending
their milk to creameries in the Irish
Free State, but I think it will be
apparent that there would be insuperable
difficulties in the way of arranging for
Barclay, Mr. William
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
McHugh, Mr. J.
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Cunningham, The Right Honourable
Samuel
the payment of the equalisation payment in respect of milk delivered to
creameries situated outside Northern
Ireland over which my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Agriculture,
would have no control. But although I
think it is not possible for this Parliament to legislate with respect to Free
State creameries, I will bring to the

difficulties of the producers to whom
reference has been made.
With regard to the definition of a
creamery, I am not in a position at the

definition that would satisfy the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie), and I will
be obliged if he will kindly postpone the

Mr. LESLIE: And about the licence

Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.

Unemployment Bill
21 JUNE 1934
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 21st June, 1934.
The Senate met at Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented.
Ministry of Finance:
Accounts of the Receipts and Payments for the Financial Year ended the
31st March, 1933, of the Ministry of
Finance Pensions Account and the
Pensions Account, Northern Ireland,
together with the Reports of the Comptroller and Auditor-General thereon.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Question to Minister.
RETIREMENT OF CONSTABULARY
OFFICERS.
Mr. GYLE: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether it is a fact that
County Inspector Atteridge, R.U.C.,
was retired, having reached the age
limit, and whether this was the reason
for his retirement; whether D.I. Nevin
was also retired, having reached the age
limit; whether D.I. Nevin was reinstated on two different occasions; whether
he can state the reason for this reinstatement, and whether this officer is at
present serving in the Force.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): County Inspector Atteridge,
of the Royal Ulster Constabularly,
reached the age limit on the 31st July,
1933, and this was the reason for his
retirement.
District Inspector Nevin reached the
age limit on the 11th May, 1932, and his
retirement took effect from this date.
It is incorrect to say that this officer
was reinstated on two different occasions
as suggested by the hon. Senator, but as
at the time of his retirement there was
considerable unrest in parts of Northern
Ireland it was decided to form a reserve
force for which trained officers were required immediately. District Inspector
Nevin was available and entirely suitable, and accordingly his service was
extended and he is still employed.
I may add that had there been any
urgent demand at that time for additional
trained county inspectors the services of
County Inspector Atteridge would have
been availed of as he was entirely suitable.
Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved: "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree to the said Bill with
Amendments, to which they desire their
concurrence.
Unemployment Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
"That this Bill be now read the Third
time," I would like to reply to a point
raised by the hon. and learned Senator
Leslie on Clause 49, sub-clause (2).
The method of determining age which
is laid down in sub-clause (2), of Clause
49, corresponds exactly with the method
prescribed in Section 132, of the
National Health Insurance Act of 1924,
for the purposes of the National Health
Insurance and Contributory Pensions
Code, and with that prescribed in
Section 47, of the Unemployment Insurance Act of 1920, for the purposes of
the Unemployment Insurance Code.
Having regard to the manner in which
the various schemes for our social services dovetail into one another, it is
essential that the method of determining
age should be identical in all, so that no
persons may fall between two schemes
for even one day.
In the circumstances the hon. and
learned Member will, I am sure, appre-
Milk Bill-
Third Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
ciate the value of uniformity in this.
matter, and, I trust, therefore, that he
will be prepared to accept the wording
of the sub-clause as it stands.
Mr. LESLIE: I am much obliged to
the noble Viscount for looking into the
matter to which he has referred and for
making such exhaustive inquiry on the
subject.
I think the House will agree
if they have pursued their own inquiries.
that the method suggested is quite unobjectionable although in another part
of the Bill the more ordinary method of
computation is distinctly suggested.
But the proposals are in different parts
of the Bill and I am satisfied that no
confusion can arise.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill
without any amendment.
Milk and Milk Products Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to refer again to the position of
farmers in Northern Ireland who are
sending their milk to creameries in the
Irish Free State. As I indicated yesterday in the debate on the new Clause
which Senator McHugh proposed should
be added to the Bill in the interests
of these farmers, it is impossible for our
Parliament to undertake to make any
payment to Irish Free State creameries
in respect of milk received by those
creameries from Northern Ireland. At
the same time the Ministry of Agriculture is fully aware of the position of
a number of farmers along the border
of County Fermanagh who may
have difficulty in qualifying for the
guaranteed price of 5d and 6d per
gallon for their milk. The Ministry
hopes, however, that in co-operation
with the Ulster Agricultural Organisation Society it may be possible to make
arrangements for these farmers to send
their milk to a creamery in Northern
Ireland, or else possibly to establish a
separating station in Northern Ireland
which will deal with the milk supplies
of these farmers and then send the
cream to the Irish Free State.
The creameries principally affected are
the creameries of Clones, Belleek and
Pettigo, which all receive some quantity
of milk from farmers in Northern Ireland. In the case of Clones creamery,
however, there is an auxiliary creamery
at Rosslea from which cream is sent to
Clones, while the central
creamery at
Belleek has also an auxiliary creamery
at Garrison in Northern Ireland
attached to it. It is hoped that it will
be possible to arrange for a larger
quantity of milk to be dealt with at
these auxiliary creameries at Rosslea and
Garrison, and for the cream then to be
sent to the Irish Free State.
The extent to which this arrangement may prove practicable, however,
will depend upon whether the Irish Free
State will be prepared to accept this
cream free of duty. At present a limited
amount of cream is sent from the Rosslea
and Garrison auxiliaries into the Irish
Free State free of duty, but any excess
over a given quota is subject to a duty
of 3d per gallon. An alternative possibility might be the establishment of an
auxiliary creamery at some spot near the
Border which would tap the supplies of
milk at present sent into the Free State.
I would like to assure the House that
both this possibility and any other
alternative solution will be carefully and
sympathetically considered by the Ministry of Agriculture in consultation with
the Ulster Agricultural Organisation
Society.
With regard to the fear which was
expressed that farmers sending milk to
a creamery in the Irish Free Free State
would require to be licensed and pay a
producer's licence in Northern Ireland
under Sub-section 5, Clause 2, of the
Bill, I would point out that in the case
of producers of Grade D milk, the milk
which would normally be supplied to a
creamery, it is not necessary to hold a
licence under the Act. No fee or levy
would consequently be payable by a producer who supplied milk to a creamery
in the Free State. In the case of suppliers to creameries in Northern Ireland
there is an incentive to become licensed
and to qualify for the production of
Grade C or Grade B milk, because in
Milk Bill-
Third Reading
21 JUNE 1934
this case a special bonus will be payable
on milk of these grades delivered to a
creamery in Northern Ireland. This
bonus will not, however, be payable in
the case of deliveries of milk to a
creamery outside Northern Ireland, and
consequently there will be no inducement to a farmer to become licensed
under the Act. Farmers who continue
to send their milk into the Free State
will, therefore, not be interfered with
in any way and will not come within
the scope of the Bill. I hope this
assurance will be satisfactory to the hon.
Senator who raised this question. I beg
to move, "That the Bill be now read
the Third time."
Mr. McMAHON: I rise to renew the
protest I made at an earlier stage
in connection with Belfast being included
in this Bill. Each Member of the
Senate, I understand, has received a
copy of a resolution here to-day which
was passed at the Public Health Committee meeting. Much indignation was
expressed at the undue haste with which
this Bill was rushed through both
Houses of Parliament. They also protest in the strongest possible manner
against the insult offered to the Public
Health Committee in not being consulted in any way in connection with
this Bill. One of our own Acts of Parliament, the
the Agricultural Marketing
Act (Northern Ireland), 1933, has not
been followed. Part I of the United
Kingdom Act reads, "The Ministry shall
when framing a scheme take into consideration representatives of the products of agriculture proposed to be
regulated and representatives of any
other interests which may be affected by
the scheme." The Belfast County
Borough are representatives who should
have been consulted in connection with
this matter and they have been ignored.
As I said before, the Committee was
insulted because for a considerable
number of years they have been dealing
entirely with the administration of milk.
They have their own officers functioning
at the present time in a very economical
manner, and under the present Bill now
about to pass duplication of officers will
take place. It is an outstanding disgrace to Northern Ireland at the present
time that it is carrying such a burden
so far as officialdom is concerned. Indeed, officialdom at the present time is a
prosperous industry in Northern Ireland.
This House we are sitting in cannot
contain all the officials appointed after
each Bill has been passed. We will soon
have to build a second Stormont if we
go on at the rate we are going. I would
just like to say on behalf of the Belfast
Corporation that they strongly resent
this matter. The Lord Mayor made
an appeal asking that some working arrangement might be come to
between the Belfast Public Health Committee and the Government in connection
with the inspectors operating at the
present time.
After all, this friction between the
Belfast Corporation and the Government
cannot be for the good of the citizens.
The members of the Corporation are responsible to the ratepayers, and they are
looking after the interests of the ratepayers to the best possible advantage.
The inspectors employed by the Belfast
Corporation who are functioning at the
present time are to be, as it were, thrown
on to the scrap heap. The Corporation
operate the different schemes of public
health in different ways, and the Government are going to appoint inspectors
to deal with one particular subject. The
inspectors under the Belfast Corporation
have many functions to perform
addition to looking after cowsheds and
the cleanliness of shops that sell milk.
in
I think this is false economy, and
even at this late stage I would appeal
to the noble Viscount to arrange a conference between the Minister of Agriculture and the Superintendent Medical
Officer of Health for Belfast, Dr.
Thomson, an eminent medical officer to
whom the ratepayers are paying a salary
of £2,000 a year.
He has succeeded in
bringing down the death rate to the
lowest in the three Kingdoms, and under
his able management the milk supply of
Belfast has been brought to a high state
of perfection. I appeal to the right
hon. Gentleman to arrange such a conference between the Minister of Agriculture and the Medical Officer of
Health to see if something cannot be
done when another House sits next week.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
think it is admitted by Members of this
House that there has been a little hasur
in the manner in which some Measures
Milk Bill-
Third Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
have been passed through Parliament,
but I should like to ask if it is not a
fact that the Members of the House in
which these Bills were first introduced
are directly responsible to the electors,
and not to the Belfast City Council or
any other body. My opinion is that the
Members of the other House are solely
responsible to the electors for their
actions, and, if they have been too hasty,
in passing certain legislation, the
electors are perfectly entitled to criticise their actions.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like first to
deal with the explanation the noble
Viscount was good enough to give as
regards the position of the Northern
Ireland producer who sends his milk to
a creamery across the Border. I confess that it took me some time to satisfy
myself, apart from the explanation
given to-day, that the farmer would not
be penalised, but I think we may take
it now, from what the noble Viscount
said, that it is the fact that such milk
would all be Class D milk, which
can be sold without a licence, and
the question whether, although it is
sold as Class D milk, it is in fact of
Grade A, B or C will not matter. In
any case the Bill does provide that it is
not to be an offence to sell milk under
a lower grade than that under which it
is entitled to be retailed. There is the
further protection in sub-clause (6), of
Clause 10, that a prosecution cannot be
instituted for any offence which can be
dealt with by a court of summary jurisdiction except with the consent of the
Minister of Agriculture, and I think I
may take it that the consent of the
Minister of Agriculture would not be
given to prosecute a North of Ireland
producer for sending his milk to the
Free State without having any licence
or without payment of any levy. I have
no doubt that that was the intention of
the noble Viscount.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: Passing from that, the
time has gone by for saying anything
more as regards haste in connection with
this particular Measure. But I should
like to say once more that I do fear
very greatly that unless the undertaking
given by the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Agriculture, the other day
to bring those parts of the Act, as it
soon will be, into operation graduallythose parts of the Act which deal with
the sale of milk according to grades of
quality, and the sale of milk, butter
and margarine-there may be a milk
famine. The undertaking the right hon.
Gentleman gave was that those provisions should be brought into operation
very gradually indeed, and I am very
greatly afraid that however gradually
those provisions may be brought into
operation the result will be something in
the nature of a milk famine.
A milk famine can be caused in four
ways.
First of all, if there is any
serious and sudden reduction in the
number of cows. We have been told
during the debates on this Measure that
there is an unknown percentage of cattle
in Northern Ireland suffering from
tuberculosis. Very large figures have
been mentioned, but no evidence was
adduced to justify them.
But if there
are anything like forty per cent. of
tubercular cows in Northern Ireland it
is obvious that suddenly putting those
cows out of business, as it were, must
have a serious effect on the milk supply.
I think it has been said before that it
is by no means an accepted fact that
because a COW is tubercular, except
in the udder, the milk produced by that
cow is of itself of a tubercular character.
That is the first cause.
The second way
in which a milk famine can be produced
is by putting out of business those people
whose business it is to supply milk, and
I am thinking particularly of the small
man
with probably half-a-dozen cows
who serves a very limited clientele in a
country district. If one of those little
men is put out suddenly there will be
a shortage in that particular area.
The third method would be by raising
the price of milk unduly, and there the
famine caused would be of the indirect
character. It would simply be due to
the fact that a person who ordinarily
bought milk could no longer afford to
pay the price. During the short time
at my disposal I have referred to the
accounts of one or two of our District
Nursing Societies. Nearly all the District Nursing Societies of Northern Ireland have, as part of their activities,
a child welfare scheme, and it is
the custom under these schemes to dis-
Milk Bill-
Third Reading
21 JUNE 1934
tribute a relatively considerable number
of pints of milk to the children of
indigent parents throughout the year.
The necessity for that can only arise because the parents cannot afford to buy
milk at the present price. If milk becomes dearer that will be another phase
of the milk famine.
There remains a fourth cause. If it
becomes more profitable to send milk to
the creameries than to sell it for liquid
consumption the supplies will be diverted
from the country districts, and the local
children will run the risk of going without their milk. Complaints have been
made that that has happened before,
and I am very seriously afraid that it
will happen again. If it should be that
there is anything approaching this milk
famine affecting the children of Northern Ireland, the effect on the future of
the race will be very serious indeed, and
I do not envy the Minister of Agriculture the responsibility which will fall
upon him.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I recognise that
this is the Third Reading of this Bill,
anl therefore I do not intend to travel
over any of the ground covered on the
Second Reading. I associate myself entirely with the anxiety expressed by
Senator Leslie as to the effect of this
Bill on the milk supply of Northern
Ireland, and I think the Government
should be careful to fulfil their undertaking to put these provisions into
operation very gradually. With regard
to the statement of Senator Joseph Cunningham that those who originated legislation were responsible only to the
electors, that may be true, but when
you get the most influential and experienced local body in Northern Ireland, the Corporation of Belfast, or the
Public Health Committee of that body,
unanimously expressing a certain view,
surely it is the duty of this House to
pay some attention to it, and not to entirely ignore a resolution coming from
an experienced body of that description.
After all, the Belfast Corporation has
spent years in fashioning machinery to
protect the health of the people of
Belfast, and I do not wonder if, after
all those years of work and experience of
public health they should feel alarmed
lest their work might be imperilled by
those who have had no such experience.
I do think that in connection with this
and every other Bill it is the duty of
the Government to take into consultation
all local bodies who have had practical
experience of the matters dealt with, and
what body has had more practical experience in the matter dealt with in this
Bill than the Public Health Committee
of the Belfast Corporation.
This is an entirely new marketing
scheme. In fact, the Government seem
to have developed an hysteria for
marketing schemes, because three or four
marketing schemes have been rushed
through Parliament in a very short space
of time, although for over
ten years
previously no such scheme was ever put
into force. Regarding the anxiety expressed by Senator Leslie that the
operations of this Bill might result in
a shortage of milk, I think the greatest
cause for anxiety is the high percentage
of tuberculosis in cows in this country,
and the very faulty-I say so deliberately
-method of awarding compensation to
farmers whose cattle have been destroyed.
In my opinion, a very serious flaw in
this Bill is the absence of any assurance
that the farmer will be properly compensated for the loss of any animals that
may have to be destroyed on account of
the fact that they are suffering from
tuberculosis. Those who are in constant
touch with the farming community know
that, in the case of a small farmer with
a few cattle, if one or two of those
cattle are condemned it will be practically impossible for him to replace those
cattle unless he is fully compensated. I
think that as regards this and all other
Bills it is the bounden duty of
the Government to get the best possible
advice they can from local bodies before
putting into operation schemes of this
description.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have
previously explained the reasons the Government had for endeavouring to put
this Bill through both Houses with as
much expedition as they possibly could.
I am afraid the Belfast scheme of inspection cannot be applied in relation to
this Bill, as it would be obviously impossible for the Ministry to have two
different kinds of inspection in one area.
It is incorrect to say that the Belfast
authorities were not consulted, because
the Superintendent Medical Officer of
Milk Bill-
Third Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Health for Belfast had a lengthy discussion with the Secretary to the Ministry of Agriculture in regard to this Bill.
With reference to Senator Leslie's
point about a possible milk famine, I
can give the assurance that this Bill will
be brought into operation as gradually
as possible. The milk sold for liquid
consumption in Northern Ireland comes
from less than a quarter of the cows in
Northern Ireland, and therefore there
should be little fear of any milk
shortage on account of this scheme.
regards the price of milk, this will be
considered by the Joint Milk Council,
and I am certain that a satisfactory
solution will be reached so that there
will be no shortage on this account.
As
In
Colonel SINCLAIR: Before we finally
part with this Bill there is one little
matter I would like to allude to.
reviewing the Bill and the methods to be
adopted in connection with the milk
supply of the country one would be
inclined to think that most attention
should be centred on the question
of tuberculosis. I want to draw
attention to a point that has recently
emerged from an investigation carried
out across the water. It was found that
from twenty to thirty per cent. of the
milk taken at random from cows contained the germ known as brucella
abortus. That is a germ that has to do
with contagious abortion in cattle. It
has caused a great deal of loss to the
farmer, and a lot of anxiety and trouble
to the veterinary profession.
It has recently been ascertained that
that germ is the cause of a fever
known as "undulant fever." There are
about 500 cases of that character annually
across the water apparently traceable to
the influence of brucella abortus, the
germ which underlies contagious abortion
in cattle. It is a matter of common
knowledge that a good many other
diseases are circulated by the careless
and slipshod handling of milk after it
has been yielded by the cow. These
diseases include diphtheria, scarlet fever,
typhoid fever. dysentery, and infantile
diarrhoea. Some of those, no doubt,
which affect the intestines have to do
with bacillus coli. Bacillus coli is
mentioned in the first Clause of the Bill,
and is an impurity in the milk, but in
our discussions in connection with the
various Clauses of the Bill I do not think
any Members of the Senate alluded to
bacillus coli, which issues from the intestines with the excreta of both animal
and man.

I do not wish to detain the Senate in
regard to these diseases, but there is one
that has created a good deal of anxiety
in our own district, and elsewhere to a
greater degree, that is what is known
as septic sore throat." There has been
an epidemic of septic sore throat at
Hove, in Sussex. There were 1,200
cases, and 65 deaths, which represents a
mortality of 5 per cent. Here is
another disease which while not directly
traceable to cattle is believed to be due
to impurity in milk, and while it may
take some time to classify the germ concerned it may be added to the list of
diseases that may be conveyed by milk.
The diseases I have mentioned are
pretty well known and many of them are
very serious. From the list I have given,
so far as pathology is concerned, it would
seem that we are within sighting distance
of compulsory pasteurisation even of milk
from very carefully selected and tested
herds. I do not wish to press that
point at the present time, but if we are
to have a whole array of new germs and of
diseases caused thereby, against which
our measures of tuberculosis testing
would have no bearing, it looks as if
we have a long way to go yet before we
can get absolutely pure milk for our
people.
It may come eventually to the Ministry of Agriculture having to insist upon
pasteurisation with properly equipped
pasteurisation machinery before we can
really attain anything like the ideal we
all have in view, namely, a pure milk
supply, particularly a pure and abundant
supply for the children of the nation.
There is no doubt that a copious supply
of good milk free from all these germs
to which I have alluded is of the utmost importance in connection with the
physical development of our people.
thought it might be of interest to Members of the Senate to draw attention
particularly to the undulant fever risk,
and to add to our troubles there is
this other microbe which we have to get
on the scent of because in from 20 to
30 per cent. of raw milk taken from
the cow at random this germ abounds.

tended, and if so whether it was made
clear, that the prices and terms to be

of uniform application throughout Northern Ireland, or whether it was open
to the Council to provide this or that
price and this or that condition for this
or that area. The question should be
looked into, and no doubt the noble
Viscount will advise us as to the view
taken. I think we ought to be clear on
that point. Are these terms and prices
to be uniform throughout Northern Ireland or are they to vary, or may they
vary, according to different areas?
seems to me the position is not clear as
the Bill stands, and it also seems to me
that a little elasticity ought to be introduced by providing in accordance with
the words on the paper.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I rise to
thank Colonel Sinclair for his most interesting remarks. Coming from the
source they do they will be of very great
interest to all the Members of the House.
I am quite aware that cattle suffer from
many diseases besides tuberculosis. We
have a greater mortality, I think, from
liver fluke and heart disease and other
diseases that all animals are subject to.
There is no compensation except for
tubercular cattle. Mr. McAllister referred to the difficulty small farmers
would be in in connection with the
slaughter of tubercular cattle. I think
he is not aware of the rules that apply
to compensation for cattle. If a man
hears a cow coughing he has simply to
go to the nearest police barrack and
report. A veterinary surgeon is sent
out, not at his expense but at the expense of the county, to examine the
animal, and if he condemns her as
tubercular cow the owner gets threefourths of her value, so that really the
loss on a tubercular cow is only onequarter of her value.
a
Mr. MCALLISTER: Why not the
market value?
Mr. HILL DICKSON: You might
say
it should be the market value for
animals suffering from liver fluke as well
as tuberculosis.

CLAUSE 6 (Duties and powers of
Joint Milk Council).
(1) It shall be the duty of the
Council to regulate sales of milk by
determining from time to time the
respective prices at, below, or above
which, and the respective terms
on which milk of Grade A, Grade B
or Grade C may be sold by the holder
of a producer's licence or a distributor's licence.
