Arrangement of Public Business.
The
Mr. LESLIE: The Motion or Resolution which I have on the Paper is not
to be interpreted, of course, as a definite
Vote of Censure on anybody in particular, but especially it is not to be
interpreted in any way as being a formulated complaint against the noble
Lord, the Leader of this House,
because, amongst other reasons, we all
know perfectly well that the noble Lord
does do all he can to ensure for this
House reasonable facilities for the discharge of its important duties.
object is really rather to strengthen the
hands of the noble Viscount in his representative capacity as spokesman of
this House in order that he may be
able to speak with the authority of the
House behind him when he says that
this House really does require, and is
disposed to insist on, a rather longer
time for the consideration of the
Measures submitted to it. And it is
with that view that I use on the Paper
the word "imperative." It may be that
that word may strike some of our Members as being rather strong, and it may
be that it might make it easier for some
of us if we substituted a milder word,
such as the word "important." As to
that, no doubt, we shall receive suggestions during the course of the debate.
The Resolution is directed towards
in the
avoiding in
future, as far as
practicable, certain difficulties which of
late have been experienced. That is the
main object of the Resolution.
To a
lesser extent it is directed to allaying
that sense of public dissatisfaction—even
I may say public resentment, which has
manifested itself during the last week or
two in more than one direction.
I am making the suggestion on my
own part because I have a deeply-rooted
conviction that the Members of this
House cannot discharge their duties as
a revising Chamber or as a second
Chamber at all in any of the aspects of
the position-they cannot discharge
their duties with efficiency unless they
are given full time for study, for
reflection, and for consultation; it may
be with officers of the administration or
it may be with people outside whose personal interests are affected by the matter
under discussion. It is not in order to
obtain more sittings of this House that
Arrangement of Business
Arrangement of Business
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
I have put this Motion on the Paper.
That, I think, is not a necessary consequence. I am not concerned with
more sittings of the House nor that
these sittings should necessarily occupy
very much longer than they do at present,
but I do want to secure that there
should be a longer interval between the
several stages of the Bills as they come
before us.
The only method that occurs to me
whereby that can be secured would seem
to be a somewhat better arrangement of
public business, and as to that our own
Unless
powers are distinctly limited.
we choose to exercise that decisive power
of rejection which we possess but which
we do not want to be driven to use we
cannot really do much beyond voicing
our complaint and recording our protest, and to that extent perhaps putting
ourselves right in the eyes of the public,
who, as our constituents, not unnaturally expect that we will do what
we can to see that matters concerning
them receive full consideration before
legislation dealing with them is enacted.
If we look back on the facts as disclosed
in that portion of the present Session which
is now drawing to a close, we find that
Parliament was opened on the 18th of
December. From the 18th of December
till to-day, the 28th of June, this House
has sat 22 days. We all know that
having regard to the General Election,
Parliament could not very well have met
earlier than it did in fact meet, 18th
December, but after the Christmas
Adjournment, it has been remarked
more than once, that it might perhaps
have been possible for Parliament to
meet again before the 6th of March, and
it has also been suggested that there
would not seem to be any absolute necessity for Parliament to adjourn for the
summer recess on 28th June. During
that period we have, apart from local,
including Provisional Order Bills and
Divorce Bills, which, of course,
examined by Joint Committees outside
the House-apart from that, we have
dealt with 16 Bills. Of these one was
a private Measure which originated in
this House, and it seems right to say of
the remaining 15 there was perhaps only
one small Bill, re-enacting for this year
the International Motor Race Bill which
followed the lines of the Bill before,
are
which could properly have originated in
this House.
Of the other 15 Bills, four were what
we call Money Bills, which do not, of
course, require a Committee Stage, and
these four Bills, as well as three others,
received First and Second Reading on
the same day. One Bill, the Consolidated Fund Bill, passed through all its
stages on one day. That was perhaps
unimportant in the particular case, but
I cannot say I altogether like it, even
in a Consolidated Fund Bill, because,
after all, Members of this House may
well desire to obtain information
very
financial matters and to express
their opinions on those questions which
arise in connection with these matters.
At all events, there is still supposed to
be some importance attached to what has
been said in Parliament whether it becomes effective in the Lobby or whether
it does not.
on
I am asking really for reasonable time
between the stages of the Bills, and I
think the House will expect me to give
some idea of what seems to me to be
reasonable time in normal cases. Perhaps it is aiming at a counsel of perfection, and I fully recognise that the
period I suggest is almost certainly impossible in Money Bills and in many
simpler Bills it is not really necessary,
and there will be a few other cases
where it cannot be obtained. The period
that I consider should be aimed at between the First Reading of a Measure
in this House and the Third Reading
in normal circumstances is six weeks.
CHARLEMONT:
Viscount
many weeks?
How
Mr. LESLIE: Six weeks. And I
make that up as follows: Between First
and Second Reading normally, I think,
a week should be allowed to elapse; between the Second Reading and the Committee Stage, two weeks or perhaps longer;
between Committee and Report, a week;
between Report and Third Reading, a
week. That leaves a week to come and
go on, and for exceptional cases and
also to provide for possible messages
between the two Chambers. Messages
of that class have not yet come before
us, although I am told there was a
reasonable prospect the other day of
such a message being sent from another
place. However, the message we re-
441 Arrangement of Business
28 JUNE 1934
Arrangement of Business 442
ceived was not of that class, but there
is the possibility.
The House will appreciate at once that
that six weeks has great possibilities of
reduction, where, for instance, for one
reason or another, neither a Committee
Stage nor a Report Stage is called for.
Let me try to indicate why, apart from
that general question time for
thought, to which I will refer again, the
periods I have mentioned seem to me to
be the minimum it is possible to allow
if it is desired that the work of this
Chamber should be well done. First of
all, it has to be remembered that many
of the Members of this House live a
very considerable distance away, and
the time occupied in journeying to and
from this House is relatively very considerable. Some of us live nearer than
others. I am certainly not myself one
of the extreme party, but it is a fact
that after a sitting of this House, under
normal conditions I cannot get home
until eight o'clock, and if the House is
sitting the following day I have to start
about twelve-thirty. That does not
leave anybody living at that distance,
and still less, people living a greater
distance, very much time for intermediate thought and study.
Secondly, I think it will be agreed
that we ought never to approach the
succeeding stage of a Bill until Hansard
of the preceding stage is in our hands.
That we are not always able to obtain,
but I think we are entitled to have the
report, particularly of the Second Reading speeches, before we approach the
Committee Stage.
Thirdly, there is the question of
Amendments, if any. Those Amendments ought certainly to reach this
House in time to appear on the Paper
something earlier than the morning of
the day on which they are to be discussed. If they are not printed the
night before the sitting day it is obvious
that they cannot reach Members in the
country in time to allow of adequate
consideration, and very often this means
that the Department concerned
have not always sufficient time to examine
the results that might follow from the
acceptance of the Amendments, or the
desirability of Amendments, or the
objections to them, as the case may be.
It must not be forgotten in this connection that the
the House sitting on
Thursday and meeting again on Tuesday means that Saturday and Sunday
intervene. Neither Saturday nor Sunday are very effective days from the
printers' point of view, and as regards.
the country, again we have to remember
that there is no delivery of letters on
Sunday in the country, and in most
cases there is no despatch of letters
either. Therefore the time for postal
communication is very limited indeed.
As the House knows, we have just
passed through a period of some stress.
We have had three important Bills, all
of them very full of detail, and they
were all going through this House at
once. One of those Bills was a Bill
That Bill
dealing with motor traffic.
had fifty-six Clauses and two Schedules.
I made a rough calculation this morning
and I found that in that Bill altogether
there were between fifty and sixty other
enactments referred to, many of them,
of course, duplicates, but there were fifty
or sixty other enactments referred to in
that one Measure.
I put it to the
House, does anybody really think that
any one of those three Bills received in
this House all the attention it deserved,
and all the attention it really required.
I suggest that our debates would have
been of greater service perhaps if more
Members had been able to supply themselves with the necessary material to
enable them to join in those debates, and
I think it may be that some of the
Measures might have been improved if
they had had more attention bestowed
upon them. In fact, if a Bill is to emerge
from this House as good as we should
like to see it there must be, as it seems
to me, full time given for reflection and
study between the stages of the Bill.
I think I have said enough. The
Resolution on the Paper is in effect selfexplanatory, but I am not particularly
wedded to the word "imperative."
However, the words are on the Paper,
and I beg to move,
"That in the opinion of this House
it is imperative, in order that matters
brought before Parliament should
receive adequate consideration, that
Public Business should be so arranged
as to provide for intervals longer than
those allowed by recent practice,
between the several stages of the
Measures submitted to this House."
443 Arrangement of Business
Arrangement of Business 444
THE SENATE
sure the
Mr. MCALLISTER: I am
House will appreciate the high sense of
duty which has prompted the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) to put down
this Motion. I think it will be common
ground that adequate time has not been
given to discuss Bills coming before this
House. I rather differ from the hon.
and learned Senator in one respect. I
think the vital time is between the
Second Reading of a Bill and the Committee Stage. After all, the Second
Reading is the first opportunity we have
of assimilating the contents and effects
of a Bill. It is the first opportunity we
have of studying a Bill and ascertaining
what is in the Measure and what it
means. The Members of this House
must have only a very limited knowledge of the Bills coming before them,
and if they are really to know what a
Bill means and how it is going to affect
the public or particular interests, whether
the farmers or anyone else, they must
have an opportunity of discussing the
Bill with the particular interests
affected. That cannot be done adequately in a shorter period than one
month, and, accordingly, I suggest that
the time allowed between the Second
Reading and the Committee Stage should
be at least one month.
There is no doubt that at the present
time there is grave dissatisfaction
amongst the public, both on the part of
individuals and local authorities, at the
haste with which legislation has been
passed through this House. I associate
myself with all that Senator Leslie has
said regarding the inadequate time in
which Members of this House have to
discharge their duty. When one takes
into consideration the high sense of duty
and wonderful industry of Senator
Leslie I think the very fact that he has
tabled this Motion shows the necessity
for some alteration in the present system.
I entirely associate myself with his
Motion, and I fully appreciate what he
said. I quite realise that the noble
Lord, the Leader of the House, is always
anxious to facilitate us and to do everything he possibly can to make the work
of the Members of this House as easy
as possible. I heartily associate myself
with the Motion.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to join my hon. and learned Friend
(Mr. Leslie) in his appeal to the Government to give us more time for the
consideration of Measures that come
before this House. I do not want this
Motion to be regarded as a vote of
censure on the Government, but I do
think the time allowed us for the consideration of the large number of important Measures that have come before
us this Session has been very short.
After all, it is the duty of this Chamber
to try to make the best of a Bill. It
may be a good Bill when it reaches us,
but we want to try to make it even
better. The hon. and learned Member
(Mr. Leslie) has done his best to improve
Bills in this House, and he has been
very industrious in this respect. I am
very much in favour of this Motion,
and I only hope the Government will
see their way next Session to give us
adequate time for the consideration of
Bills, which we have not had this
Session.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
that a contrast must necessarily arise
between the time allotted to Bills in
the Northern Parliament and the time
allowed in the Imperial Parliament. I
am aware that none of the previous
speakers have referred to this, but it is
a
favourite device of politicians to
answer and knock down an argument
which nobody has used. At least I have
been told so, and I know by reading the
Press that it is so.
It is true that, as a general rule, the
intervals which elapse between the
stages of the various Bills at Westminster are considerably longer than
corresponding intervals in the Parliament of Northern Ireland, but the
length of time necessary to pass legislation at Westminster is not a matter of
universal satisfaction, and, indeed,
attempts have been made to bring
about an alteration of the legislative
system with a view to speeding up the
passage of Bills. In this country Ministers are much more accessible and Members are much closer to their constituents
and in closer touch with local public
opinion regarding Bills under consideration.
I have been looking into the particulars in connection with the three
major Measures, the passage of which
445 Arrangement of Business

28 JUNE 1934
Arrangement of Business
gave rise to the congestion of public
business in this House in the past three
weeks, namely, the Unemployment Bill,
the Motor Bill and the Milk Bill. I
find that the Unemployment Bill was
circulated to Members of the House of
Commons on the 24th May, the Motor
Bill on the 17th April, and the Milk
Bill on the 2nd June. All these
Measures received their Third Reading
by this House on the 21st June.
As regards the Motor Bill, I do not
think that even the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) would maintain
that the time given for consideration
between the publication of the Bill and
its passage by this House was inadequate. Admittedly it was not equal to
his proposed time-table, but I think the
spirit that engendered the idea that six
weeks should be given to every Bill
betrayed an extraordinary hopefulness
which I am glad to know the hon. and
learned Senator possesses. As I said, I do
not think the time devoted to this particular
Bill by this House was entirely inadequate,
nor do I think the hon. and learned
Senator would have pressed for more
time for consideration of the Unemployment Bill having regard to the very close
connection which must be maintained
between Northern Ireland and Imperial
legislation on this subject.
As regards the Milk Bill, I am prepared to admit that, had circumstances
allowed, the Government would have
wished to give further time but, as I
think I stated during the debate on one
of the stages, it was felt that circumstances justified the Government in
treating this Bill as an urgent Measure,
though even at that I would point out
to the hon. and learned Member that
a full fortnight had elapsed between the
date on which this Measure was first
circulated to Members of this House and
the date on which it was passed.
It must be remembered that this
House possesses an advantage in that,
except in the case of Bills which originate here, the debates in another place
thoroughly disclose the nature of the
Bill and the general principles underlying it, and, of course, the contrary
arguments.
The hon. and learned Senator's great
difficulty arises, I think, from the fact
that he takes his duties as a Member of
this House very seriously indeed, and
gives a very careful and thorough
examination to not merely such Bills as
interest him particularly but to them
all. I fully agree that the fact of these
three important Bills coming under the
consideration of the House at one and
the same time may have prevented him
from making that meticulous examination of their provisions to the same
extent as we know he is accustomed to
do. Nevertheless, I feel that the Government cannot on that account merely
give a general undertaking to allow the
period necessary for the passage of Bills
to be unduly lengthened, nor indeed do
I feel that this would commend itself
to the majority of the Members of this
House or to the community as a whole.
At the same time, I do feel a certain
sympathy with the object of the Motion.
I think, however, that the word
"imperative" has a rather dictatorial
sound which grates on the musical ear
of perhaps the most musical Member of
the Government of Northern Ireland.
Therefore, if the hon. and learned
Member can see his way to change it to
something of a softer and more melodious
nature such as "highly desirable"
"greatly advantageous" or any other
like expression he can think of I would
be perfectly ready to accept the Motion,
and do all I can to further its objects.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
or
Mr. LESLIE: I am always anxious
to accept any suggestion from the noble
Viscount. If it would meet his wishes
that the word "imperative" should be
changed into "desirable" I would, with
the consent of the House, be happy to
suggest that course. As regards some
other things that have been said, I
Iwould like to remind the House that the
Assemblies at Westminster contain a very
great many more Members than either of
our Chambers, and this is a fact that
is not without its bearing on the situation. Also I think one ought to register
mild protest against regarding the
introduction of Measures in another
place as being the same thing as the
introduction of Measures in this House.
I do not think there is anything more
I have to say except that I ask leave
of the House to change the word
"imperative" into the word "desirable."
447 Arrangement of Business

THE SENATE
Adjournment
Mr. SPEAKER: Would the noble
Viscount, the Leader of the House,
move an Amendment to the effect that
the word "desirable" should be used
instead of the word "imperative"?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I move
the Amendment as suggested by you,
that the word "desirable" be substituted for the word "imperative.”
Amendment agreed to.
On Question, Motion, "That in the
opinion of this House it is desirable, in
order that matters brought before Parliament should receive adequate consideration, that Public Business should
be so arranged as to provide for intervals
longer than those allowed by recent
practice, between the several stages of
the Measures submitted to this House,"
agreed to.
Adjournment.
Moved, "That this House do now
adjourn until such date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint."-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter before One
o'clock until such date
as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint.

Papers Presented
Fisheries Bill
THE SENATE
The Unemployment Insurance (Associations) (Amendment) Regulations
(Northern Ireland), 1934.
The Unemployment Insurance (Joint
Maintenance of Dependants) Regulations
(Northern Ireland), 1934.
The Unemployment Insurance (Benefit) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1934.

Contributions for Persons over 65)

2), 1934.

PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Hume's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time.
Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order
No. XXV. be suspended for the purpose of taking the Second Reading of
the Fisheries Bill and the Expiring
Laws Continuance Bill.-(Mr. Long).
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have read a Second
time Hume's Divorce Bill. They have
passed the Expiring Laws Continuance
Bill, the Fisheries Bill, and the Adoption of Children (Workmen's Compensation) Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
Fisheries Bill,
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long).
Mr. LONG: If this is the third time
during the thirteen years of its existence
that this House has been called upon
to legislate with regard to fishery matters, that is quite a moderate version
of the tradition hitherto maintained in
Irish fishery legislation. Since the year
1842, when the foundations of the
present code were laid, an Act dealing
with the subject of fish has been passed
on the average, at least every two years,
to say nothing of earlier attempts, extending as far back as the time of
Edward IV, to entangle them in the
meshes of the law. It is not usual to
regard fish as particularly sensitive
creatures, or we might think that they
have become so accustomed to the perpetual solicitude of Governments that
they would feel a certain grievance if
they did not continue to receive their
fair share of our attention.
Hon. Members will recall that the last
Act-that of 1933-dealt only with sea
fisheries. It is with the inland fresh
water fisheries that the Bill now before
the House is chiefly concerned. It has
been drafted to give effect to the recommendations of the Committee on Local
Fishery Administration which reported
last year to my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Commerce.
The Bill leaves untouched the principle by which the fisheries are placed
under the general control of a central
department, while the local administration of the relative Acts and bylaws
is in the hands of boards' representative
of local interests. At the same time,
under the provisions of the Bill the
element of central control, which of
course stands for the interests of the
community at large, will be somewhat
strengthened. There is, for instance,
provision for the audit of the accounts
of the board of conservators by an
auditor who must be approved by the
Ministry of Commerce, and for the
presentation to the Ministry of an annual
return prepared by the clerk of the
board. It is also proposed to empower
the Ministry by order to make
alteration in the amount of the various
licence duties payable and to impose a
licence duty in respect of trout fishing
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
17 OCTOBER 1934
in particular areas, where this may seem
to be justified.
This is something of an innovation,
because at present no licence is required.
for trout fishing; and it is of course
possible that this state of affairs may
continue; although a demand for a small
licence duty in respect of trout-rods has,
in fact, been definitely expressed in certain quarters on behalf of the anglers
themselves. In any case, neither of the
powers of the Ministry of Commerce to
which I have just referred is to be exercised except after consultation with the
local board of conservators and the holding
of a public inquiry; and it must be remembered that the functions of these
boards and of the Ministry are intended
to be used not by any means for the
discouragement of fishing but solely for
the improvement of the fisheries, both
from the commercial and sporting point
of view. To this purpose the whole of
the moneys received in licence duties is
devoted.
Although the natural division of a conservancy district into separate fresh
water and tidal divisions for electoral
purposes was destroyed by the setting up
of the Free State boundary, which removed the whole of the tidal electoral
area from what was the Ballyshannon
and is now the Enniskillen district, the
committee which investigated the matter
did not consider that the retention of such
divisions would in every case be of advantage in securing a suitable balance of the
various interests represented on the conservancy boards. Accordingly, while
they did not suggest any change in the
boundaries of the four existing districts
of Londonderry, Coleraine, Enniskillen
and Ballycastle as fixed by the Order of
the Home Secretary in 1925, they recommended that in future the elected representatives should represent the different
classes of licence holder rather than the
different divisions of the areas, except
that in the case of the largest area, viz.,
the Coleraine district, it was thought
desirable to secure separate representation for the professional fishermen in the
upper division, which embraces Lough
Neagh. These recommendations are
embodied in the Bill and constitute what
are, perhaps, its most important provisions.
I should explain that the boards as at
present constituted consist of three
classes of members:-
(1) those elected by the votes of all
persons who have paid any licence
duty during the year, the number
of votes to which the licence holder
is entitled varying with the
amount of licence duty paid;
(2) owners of several, or exclusive,
fisheries rated at £100 a year or
upwards;
(3) magistrates paying licence duty
and being owners of land adjoining
a river or lake in the district.
The latter two classes are members exofficio and do not have to be elected.
The value of the inland fisheries,
especially the salmon fisheries, both as a
commercial asset and from the point of
view of sport has of course long been
appreciated in Ireland, although we in
Ulster have not given the salmon any
such signal mark of recognition as our
neighbours in the Free State, who have
paid him the unusual honour of placing
his portrait upon one of the highest
denominations of their silver coinage.
There is, however, bound to be a certain
opposition between the interests of
anglers and the commercial fisheries, and
it has been found that under the present
system one or other of these interests
has tended to predominate at the expense of the other in representation
upon the boards. It is hoped to remedy
this state of affairs by providing for the
separate representation of these two interests to which I have referred, the
numbers of each class to be elected for
each district being set out in the First
Schedule of the Bill. The Bill also contains consequential provisions with regard to the method of conducting the
elections, and for continuing the tenure
of office of the present boards up to their
normal date of expiry in October next
year.
It is also proposed to alter the qualifications for ex-officio membership, by
lowering the qualification to be required
for the second class of ex-officio members,
namely, owners of several or exclusive
fisheries in the district, from £100 to £30
of annual rateable value, and to substitute for the third class, consisting of
magistrates who are also licence-holders,
a new class consisting of riparian owners
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
whose lands carry a Poor Law valuation
of £50 per annum or more and have a
total frontage of at least half-a-mile to
any river or lake, provided that such
owners have the right to fish the adjoining waters and have paid licence duty
to the board within the preceding twelve
months.
I think that I have now outlined the
most important matters dealt with by
the Bill. I would, however, call the
further attention of the House to the
small increase in the rates of licence duty
on certain classes of nets and other
engines for taking fish under Clause 13.
The Committee on Local Fishery
Administration reported that whilst
there were frequently divergencies of
opinion as to other matters between the
witnesses they examined, there was complete unanimity as to the need for
additional funds to enable the boards to
afford adequate protection to the spawning grounds in the upper waters and in
the smaller rivers, these not being in
general the property of large fishery
owners who might otherwise be expected
to provide such protection themselves. The increases provided for in
the Clause are those recommended by the
Committee, with the exception of that on
draft nets not exceeding 750 yards in
length. In this case the Committee recommended a licence duty of £5. This
amount was inserted in the original Bill,
but was reduced to £4 during its consideration in another place. The salmon
rod licence duty is not being altered,
because this was revised as recently as
1928.
to
Advantage has also been taken, in
Parts IV and V, of this Bill to effect
certain minor amendments of the Fishery
Acts, in particular, in Clause 20,
make provision to ensure that the packing and handling of fish on wholesalers'
premises shall take place under properly
hygienic conditions. Somewhat similar
provisions, generally under the administrative control of public health authorities, are, as hon. Members are aware,
already in force with regard to retailers'
premises and cover other types of
perishable food as well as fish.
I should not be able, without presuming too far on the patience of the
House, to touch specifically at this stage
upon all the miscellaneous provisions of
the Bill. From a perusal of its terms, I
think, however, that hon. Members will
have observed that it contains several
useful improvements on the existing
law, besides those of which I have spoken
at length. The Committee stage will,
however, provide a better opportunity for
their detailed review. I beg to move.
Mr.
MCALLISTER: I think the
Deputy-Leader of the House has reason
to congratulate himself that he was able
to secure the attention of the House while
he was presenting to us, and asking us
to support, a Bill of the nature of the
one he has just moved. He said the
fishing industry was an important industry in Northern Ireland, and with
that the House generally will agree.
But what does he propose to do in this
Bill to foster and encourage the building
up of that industry. He proposes two
things, first, a reactionary method of
electing the governing body, and
secondly, and more reactionary still, he
proposes to elect the most undemocratic
body that could possibly be appointed to
rule over any business or undertaking in
Northern Ireland. Then he proceeds to
foster the industry by increasing the
licence fees of people who at the present
moment are unable to make ends meet.
Having nothing concrete or substantial
to offer the House through the medium
of this Bill, the Deputy-Leader very
ingeniously falls back on the expedient of
giving us a very interesting history of
fishery legislation in Great Britain and
Northern Ireland, since, I think, the year
1842. I do object again to this legislation by reference, and I think that if
that objection was ever justified it is
justified to-day by the statement of the
Deputy-Leader of the House when he
said that since this House was set up
twelve years ago
we had had three
Fishery Acts, and that every two years
since 1842 there had been a Bill introduced in Parliament to improve the
fishery interests of the country.
It is a strange thing that in this Bill
the poor fishermen on the coast who go
out to sea to fish are to have their licence
fees increased by one-third, while a large
proportion of inland fishermen and
fishery owners are not to have their
licences increased by a single penny. I
think there is room for complaint there,
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
17 OCTOBER 1934
and I intend at a later stage to give the
House an opportunity of expressing its
opinion upon it. At the present time
the vital matter affecting the fishermen
of Northern Ireland is the question of
where
Lough Neagh,
two hundred
families engaged in a legitimate occupation by fishing in Lough Neagh are now
being deprived of a right which their
forefathers for generations have exercised over that Lough. The Government thought that question so important
that they called a conference a short
time ago to deal with it, and yet here
we have a Fishery Bill which gave the
Government every opportunity of dealing with that most important question,
and there is not a word about it in the
Bill.
I must say that when the fishermen of Lough Neagh and those similarly
situated find a Bill introduced in this
Parliament which increases their licence
fees and creates this most reactionary
body by which they are to be governed,
they will not be satisfied with the Government's course of procedure.
Another thing I would like to say is
this, and I have said it on many occasions
before. The more experience I have of
parliamentary and professional life the
more grave becomes the objection I have
to empowering a Minister to make regulations to carry out the terms of a Bill.
I think it is one of the worst features
of Northern Ireland at the present time
when we are falling into the spirit of
officialdom. When I say that I do not
intend to reflect on the Civil Service as
a body, because I think, bearing in mind.
that they are serving a Government with
a very narrow vision on the great political questions, it is very much to the
credit of the civil servants that they have
tried and succeeded in playing the part
of truly genuine civil servants.
I do not
intend to reflect upon them, but I protest against allowing the Government
power to make regulations nullifying the
provisions of the Act. Many times I
have had to look up regulations under
the Motor Act and other Acts and I
have no hesitation in saying that very
often the regulations made have been
such as never would have passed through
this House. They were entirely contrary to the spirit of the Act which received the sanction of Members of this
House.
On this question of legislation by reference I, myself, took up this Bill, and I
found when I went to read it that it
went back to 1840. No layman has a
legal library and no ordinary lawyer has
a library that can follow that up and,
therefore, these Bills that pass through
this House are absolutely meaningless to
those who read them. I do not see why
the Government should not take the
opportunity of bringing in a comprehensive Measure to codify the whole law.
I am sure those of us who looked for
some constructive programme to improve
the position of the fishing industry in
Northern Ireland will feel gravely disappointed, and they will be justified.
Colonel SINCLAIR: As this is an
amending Bill I suppose there would be
some difficulty in making it as comprehensive a Measure as the hon. Senator
demands, but on the whole I take a
similar view. I would like to see the
fishery Acts codified in such a way that
any person reading the last Act passed
would understand the situation. I am
not concerned with the earlier part of the
Bill, but I wanted information from the
Deputy-Leader of the House in regard to
licensing. It rather bears on what
Senator McAllister has just alluded to,
namely, that this legislation, by reference, is on the whole very inconvenient
and more or less obscure. I was glad to
learn from the Deputy-Leader of the
House, in regard to one of the Clauses
in which I was specially interested, that
is trout fishing, that it was not-I understood-intended immediately to apply
this or carelessly or arbitrarily to enforce
it unless after due inquiry in the various
districts. One would be sorry to think
that hasty legislation would interfere with
the innocent and simple amusements of
a large number of young people and
humble people who find trout fishing a
very great solace and enjoyment.
Coming to the licensing in regard to
salmon fishing, I am not quite clear from
the references where people stand. If I
understood the Deputy-Leader correctly
he said there was no change in the
ordinary annual licence for a salmon rod,
but when we come to Clause 13, Section
(2), I find that the conservators in any
district issue a licence for the limited
may
period of seven days for the use of a
single salmon rod. I should like to know
Fisheries Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
why. It used to be a short term licence
could be had for 14 days. What is the
explanation of its being reduced to seven
days?
I have before my mind the development of the Province in another way.
Quite a large number of agencies and
associations exist for the purpose of
developing the tourist traffic in Northern
Ireland, and their efforts have been successful. In this connection I could quite
imagine, as the ordinary annual holiday
is a fortnight, that they might find a
seven-days' temporary and restricted
licence for salmon rods not very inviting.
I do not see at the moment what the
reason was for reducing the 14-day limit
to a seven-day limit. Bearing on that
point there is a subordinate question, is
it competent for the conservators to reissue a seven-day licence for another seven
days in such a way that a term, coterminous with the ordinary average
holiday might be in that way achieved,
or are we to conclude from the New
Clause that a seven-day licence is the
farthest we can go?
As regards the previous Acts to which
the Third Schedule gives reference, I cannot avoid sympathising with our colleague, Senator McAllister, in wishing
that it would not be necessary to go back
to ancient enactments and amending Acts
of various stages in order to ascertain at
any time what really the regulations were
in relation to salmon propagation and
salmon preservation, and the licensing of
fishing by sportsmen and others who take
a keen interest in this question.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to offer a few observations on this Bill.
I agree with Senator McAllister that it is
not a very interesting Measure. It does
not enthuse me very much. There are
one or two good points, especially dealing with the fish themselves. Clause 10
is a very good point, keeping the close
season. We know quite well that unless
fish have a close season you are going
to destroy them. You must give them a
chance of breeding. I like Clause 11
about immature fish. I live on the banks
of Lough Neagh, and I know a great
number of fishermen there. I know that
a certain number of fishermen are really
destroying their own livelihood. They
will catch too small fish. They use a
one man.

small hook and too small a mesh net, and
they are destroying their own livelihood.
A great number of them know it quite
well, but until you get legislation it will
go on. Of course it is very difficult for
He says,
My neighbour goes
out and catches these fish. Why should
not I?" I think Clause 11 is a very
good one. It is going to protect the
fishermen themselves, and that is a very
important thing in an industry like that
which is certainly going downhill. It is
deteriorating through the action of the
men themselves. They will catch immature fish-eels and trout.
I do wish, with Senator McAllister,
that the Government could have come to
some settlement about the Lough Neagh
fisheries. I do honestly feel that some
settlement might be come to between the
fishermen and the Eel Fishery at Toome
and the fishery on Lough Neagh. I know
there is a sense of injustice among these
fishermen, which I think it would be very
well to allay. They might come to some
compromise. I strongly urge the Government to take some action. As regards
the rest of the Bill, it is mostly about
voting, and I do not know much about
it. It is a rather complicated system
about one man having two votes because
he has two miles of frontage and so on.
I support the Bill without thinking it is
a very great Measure.
Mr. LONG: On the whole, I wish to
thank Members for the reception they
have given to the Bill. With regard to
the points raised by Senator McAllister
I should like to point out that in so far
as Lough Neagh fishermen are concerned
that problem is not one which could be
dealt with in the present Bill, as the
question is one involving private rights.
As the hon. Senator has indicated, an
attempt was made a few months ago to
secure an amicable agreement. The
problem was to arrange an agreement
between the fishery company on the one
hand and the professional fishermen on
the other. I regret it was not successful.
I shall convey to the Government Colonel
Pakenham's wish that further attempts
should be made to settle this matter.
Another point that Mr. McAllister
raised was as regards the undemocratic-
I think he called it-method of election.
So far as the present constitution of the
Expiring Laws Bill-
Adjournment
17 OCTOBER 1934
board of conservators can be held to be
undemocratic such a criticism is less
applicable to the new boards, which
include representatives of all the persons
interested, whereas, under present conditions, a substantial body of licence
holders are wholly unrepresented.
Furthermore, the qualifications are more
substantial than they have hitherto been.
To be a magistrate alone will no longer
be a sufficient qualification. The other
point that those who took part in the
discussion agreed on is the objection to
legislation by reference, and in that
regard I shall represent their views to
the Government.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That the
Bill be now read a Second time."
This Bill is in the customary annual
form and is, as usual, directly related
to the corresponding Imperial Bill, which
applies to Northern Ireland in so far as
it deals with matters upon which this
Parliament is not competent to legislate.
The Schedule is, therefore, in two parts,
relating respectively to Imperial and
Northern Ireland Acts. The period of
continuance in both cases is up to 31st
December, 1935.
Since the statutes enumerated in the
Schedule are identical with those contained in the Schedule to last year's
Expiring Laws Continuance Act, I think
it is unnecessary for me to comment
further upon the Measure now before the
House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.

Mr. McLAUGHLIN: May I ask the
Deputy-Leader of the House to give us
an indication of the order of business for

that the Members of the House should
have the necessary Bills and papers a day
or two before the House sits. A Member
comes down to the House and finds on
the Order Paper business which he has
not been aware of. If he had the Bills
and papers a day or two beforehand he
would be able to read them and be
prepared for debate.
I
Mr. LONG: It is intended to meet
again on Tuesday, 23rd October. The
Local Government Bill is the Measure
of major importance to come before the
Senate after to-day, and I expect that
Members will have it by Saturday.
should like to take the First Reading on

CLAUSE 1 (Tenure of office of existing boards) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Constitution and tenure
of office of future boards).
*

(3) If at any time it appears to the
Ministry of Commerce that all or any
of the numbers of elected or nominated members of a board of conservators for a fishery district should be
altered, the said Ministry may, after
holding a public inquiry in the said
district, by order alter any such numbers as aforesaid.

The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie : :-
Clause 2, page 2, at the end of subclause (3) insert:-"
-"Provided that an
order made under this sub-section shall
be laid as soon as may be after it is made
before each House of Parliament and
shall be of no force or effect unless and
until it has been approved with or without modification by a resolution passed
by each such House."
Mr. LESLIE: This is really a very
small matter arising out of the introduction of nominated members on the
boards of conservators. The adoption of
that principle, which was recommended
by the Committee, and to which nobody
has taken any objection whatever-in
fact, it is a principle which I think most
of us believe is likely to be a useful
one-does entail some minor difficulties
of detail, and this Amendment is to deal
with one of them. It is obviously necessary, I think, that some Parliamentary
check should be imposed on the possible
power of a Minister to upset entirely the
balance of a board. That was never the
intention, but the possibility exists, and
consequently some words of limitation
seem necessary. The words on the
Paper are, I think, the simplest that can
be devised. The only thing I have to
say about them is that since I put those
words on the Paper it has been pointed
out that the precedent I followed in the
exact wording, a precedent set by one of
Fisheries Bill-
Committee
23 OCTOBER 1934
the Electricity Supply Acts, has been 1
found not to work in England, because
it happened that one House of Parliament made one modification and another
House of Parliament made another modification. Consequently, in moving the
Amendment, I am asking that the words,
"with or without modification" may be
left out. I beg to move accordingly.
Question put: "After line 21 insert,
Provided that an order made under this
sub-section shall be laid as soon as may
be after it is made before each House
of Parliament and shall be of no force or
effect unless and until it has been
approved by a resolution passed by each
such House."
Mr. LONG: The Government is prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 3 (Method of election of
representative members of salmon rod
fishermen), 4 (Method of election of
representative members of fishermen
other than salmon rod fishermen), and
5 (Qualifications of ex-officio members)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Resignation of
servators).
con-
(1) Any elected member of a board
of conservators may at any time by
notice in writing delivered to or served
by post upon the clerk of the board,
resign his membership of the board,
whereupon the seat of such member
upon the board shall become vacant.
(2) Section two of the Fisheries Act
(Northern Ireland), 1928, shall cease
to have effect.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the
first line of Clause 6, to leave out the
word "elected" ("Any elected member').
This is another little matter arising out
of the same circumstances. It occurred
to me that it was just as likely, or just as
unlikely, that a nominated member
might want to resign as an elected
member. Accordingly, I beg to move.
Mr. LONG: The Government is prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

(2) When the seat of any member
of a board of conservators becomes

continue to act notwithstanding such

The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :
Clause 7, page 6, lines 30 and 31,
leave out from "co-opt" in line 30 to the
first "a" in line 31.
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 7, to leave out from "vacant" in
line 35, to "continue" in line 37, and
to insert instead, "the other members of
the board may, until the vacancy is
filled,". I move this Amendment to
provide for the continuity of a board of
conservators during the existence of a
vacancy, perhaps amongst its nominated
members. Again we have to provide
that a board may continue to act notwithstanding any vacancy amongst their
number. That is all it comes to. I beg
to move.
Mr. LONG: I am pleased to say that
the Government accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 13 (Alteration of certain

Question proposed: "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
THE SENATE
Fisheries Bill-

Committee
each of these classes of engine as proposed in the Bill amounts only to £1
per annum.
Mr. LONG: On the Second Reading
debate Senator Colonel Sinclair raised
the question of the abolition of the
14-day licence for salmon rod costing £1,
and the substitution of a 7-day licence
at a charge of 10/-. It is believed that
the issue of licences for a shorter term
at a lower rate will not materially affect
the number of licences issued at the full
rate of £1 10s for the season, and that
the concession will be appreciated by
tourists and those of our own people who
can spare only a few days for angling in
the year.
The reason for the abolition
of the 14-day licence at £1 is in order
to secure uniformity throughout the
Province as, if the longer-term licences
were left in force, some of the boards
might not adopt the shorter-term licences,
and an angler changing from one district to another might have to take out
a licence for 14 days when he had only
a few days available for fishing. It will,
of course, be open to any angler to take
out two 7-day licences for consecutive
periods, if he so desires, and the two
licences to run consecutively could be
issued by the same board at the same
time, thus obviating the necessity for a
second application by the angler.
Then Senator McAllister, in referring
to the alterations in licence duty on
salmon fishing engines provided for in
Clause 13, complained that the effect of
the Clause was to increase the licence
duty which would be payable by poor
fishermen on the coast "while a large
proportion of the inland fishermen and
fishery owners are not to have their
licences increased by a single penny."
The Committee on Local Fishery
Administration reported that, whilst
there were frequently divergencies of
opinion as to other matters between the
witnesses they examined, there was complete unanimity as to the need for
additional funds to enable the boards to
afford adequate protection to the spawning grounds in the upper waters and in
the smaller rivers which are generally
not the property of large fishery owners
who might be expected to provide such
protection themselves. To provide these
additional funds the Committee therefore recommended moderate increases on
certain classes of fishing engines, amongst
which were draft nets used in tidal
waters and salmon drift nets exceeding
750 yards in length. The increase on
The fact that in the case of the draft
nets the alteration in duty has been confined to nets used in tidal waters, and
not applied generally to draft nets irrespective of the kind of water in which
they are fished, is explained by the
desire of the Government to place no
additional burden upon the relatively
poorer class of fisherman who uses a draft
net in non-tidal waters, that is, the
pollen and eel fishermen in Lough
Neagh and Lough Erne. The hon.
Member will presumably not complain
that the increase of duty is not being
extended to these nets as well as to those
used in the sea. The argument that the
sea fishermen are alone being subjected
increase of duty
an
is not well
founded, inasmuch as the Bill also provides for an increase in the duty on
boxes, cribs, cruives, etcetera, used by
the owners of salmon fisheries in the
rivers, and to a considerable extent the
same owners will be subjected to an increase of duty through the alteration
proposed in the case of bag nets.
to
Mr. McALLISTER: My criticism is
based upon the ground that the powerful companies such as the Foyle and
Bann Fishery companies are not affected
by this Bill, and their licences are not
increased, whereas the poor fisherman's
licence is increased. Of course if I am
I can be corrected, but that is
wrong
the information I have. The Deputy-
Leader said there was no real opposition
to the increase of licence duty on draft
nets. I hold in my hand a telegram,
addressed to Mr. Leeke, from Portstewart, in which they protest in the
strongest possible manner against the in-
At the present time the expenses are not met by the fishing, and
last year there were twelve nets, whereas
during the present year there were only
five. The industry has been killed. I
think no justification can be put forward for this burden on these fishermen
at a time when the business is depressed.
crease.
After all, we must consider, too, the
human point of view, the hardships
under which these fishermen carry on
their avocations in all sorts of weather.
and exposed to every condition at night.
I think it is very poor encouragement
Fisheries Bill
Committee
23 OCTOBER 1934
for men of this type, to increase their
burden. I would ask the Government,
notwithstanding the speech of the
Deputy-Leader of the House, to consider
this matter between the Report stage
and Third Reading. After all, the increase of a pound on these poor fishermen cannot raise very much revenue.
The revenue raised will not be worth the
grave dissatisfaction that will be felt by
all fishermen round the entire coasts of
Northern Ireland.
Colonel SINCLAIR : I thank the
Deputy-Leader of the House for his explanation with regard to the seven-day
licence. My mind is relieved by his
further statement that a second sevenday licence is not prohibited, that it is
competent to issue a second seven-day
licence. The point I wished to make
was that holidays extend commonly to
a fortnight, and that a seven-day licence,
however cheap, might not attract the
same number of visitors and tourists to
Northern Ireland that we would like to
see here and which would be a source of
revenue for other industries in the
Province. After all the explanation
which the Deputy-Leader of the House
has given satisfies me in regard to the
seven-day licence, and the reduction of
50 per cent. in the cost will no doubt be
appreciated by a certain section, but I
do not know that it will be altogether a sufficient bait for tourists from
a distance across the water who, we all
hope, will come here to spend their holidays. I thank the Deputy-Leader for
his courtesy.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 15 (Power of Ministry of
Commerce to vary amount of licence
duties).
Question proposed: "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr.
the
LESLIE: I think that
Amendment I am rising to move, that is,
to leave Clause 15 out of the Bill altogether, is probably the most debatable
point which will come before us this
afternoon. It will be remembered that
the Commission which sat to inquire into
matters generally relating to inland
fisheries were very concerned about the
necessity for increasing the revenue of
the boards of conservators in order that
the rivers might be better protected and
perhaps better stocked. After hearing
evidence from practically everybody that
came before them, some of them very
qualified to express an opinion and some
not qualified, the Committee made a
recommendation, and part of that recommendation I propose to read. It will be
remembered that the object of the whole
thing is to get revenue for the conservators, not the Government of Northern
Ireland. The Committee say:
The proposal to make a licence
necessary for trout fishing has many
attractions and the ordinary trout
angler would not object to the payment of a small licence duty if he
knew it would be spent on the protection or stocking of rivers, but it
would be unfair to ask the farmer or
his son who fishes in a brook running
through his land to pay for a licence,
and the exemptions which it would be
necessary to grant would make it
difficult to impose a general licence
duty. We think, however, that there
may be parts of a district where an
angler for trout might fairly be required to take out a licence, and we
recommend that the Ministry, after
consultation with the board of conservators, and after a public local inquiry, should be authorised to impose
a licence duty for the taking of trout
in such parts of the district and on
such conditions as the Ministry might
determine. The proceeds of such
licence duty should be paid into the
funds of the board concerned.
Really the first question to be considered is, is the game worth the candle.
Will the proposal be received with anything like universal sympathy or will it
arcuse objection. In my own part of
the country, that is to say, for this purpose, North Antrim, and to some extent
North Derry, I have heard numerous
views expressed on various Clauses of the
Bill, but this particular Clause has not
come in for anything in the shape of
approbation. It has, however, . come
before us as a recommendation of the
Committee, and I think we ought not
lightly to turn it down. At the same
time, there is the difficulty of collection
for one thing, and also if you make the
licence duty very small the amount of
revenue obtainable will also be very
Fisheries Bill--
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
small, and
if you make the duty
relatively high the people who pay that
duty will ask for representation on the
board of conservators. If they are to
get that it will rather upset the balance
which is provided for in the schedule,
and it would necessitate application to
More-
Parliament for the alteration.
over, we have to remember in that connection that the man will not get the
vote unless he pays licence duty amounting to one pound.
It seems to me also that if you impose
a licence duty to catch trout you will
bring in a demand from riparian owners.
As trout fishing will have a slight value
they will at once charge a little rent to
anybody who wants to catch fish on their
land. The Committee, it will be remembered, paid a substantial tribute to the
courtesy of riparian owners and their
generosity in allowing the public to fish.
It would be a great pity if that were
disturbed. Again, we will have some
difficulty in defining what we mean by
the expression, "licence to fish with a
trout rod and line." We, all of us, could
say off-hand that one rod was a trout rod
and that another one was a salmon rod.
But there must be a point between a
heavy trout rod and a light salmon rod
where definition would be difficult. I
am inclined to think that whether a man
was fishing for trout or salmon would
depend on the cast and the size of the
fly he was trying to use. That brings
us to the other point. If a trout licence
is to be imposed, does a salmon licence
cover a trout licence? The Clause does
not tell us.
The same difficulty was discussed in
England. In the English Act of 1923
they have put in a special paragraph
that salmon licence shall cover the trout
licence. If it is decided that we should
take power to impose a trout licence,
I think the same thing ought to be
followed. I think we ought to put it
beyond argument that a salmon licence
includes trout. You could argue that
the expression salmon necessarily includes trout. There is a great deal to
say for that point of view judging from
the passages in various Acts of Parliament, and also, perhaps, from certain
decided cases. I believe it was admitted
before the Committee that the expression
salmon included sea trout, but they did
not say anything about brown trout.
But the Committee will remember that
the ordinary trout is known as salmo
ferox and the salmon is known as salmo
salar, and it would be rather hard,
therefore, to say that the expression
salmon includes trout. If a decision is
taken to retain this Clause we ought to
add on the Report stage a simple paragraph following the lines of the English
provision to which I have referred.
On the whole, I submit that it is not
worth while inserting this Clause in the
Bill. On that I hope we shall be
favoured with the opinions of other
Members with greater knowledge of
trout fishing than I have. It seems
doubtful to risk awakening a certain
amount of public resentment. Therefore, I beg to move, to leave out the
Clause.
as
Mr. MCALLISTER: I am opposed to
the Clause on general grounds. In the
first place, I think it is unwise for Parliament to delegate to the Ministry so
many powers we have been doing
recently. I think that, after all, Parliament should retain the power to do these
things itself. As I said before, it is
appalling to me to see some of the regulations made that are not consistent with
the Acts of Parliament under which they
are made. In the second place, I am
opposed on the ground that this means
issuing another licence for the raising of
revenue. We will soon have arrived at
a point when we will not be allowed to
walk the street without receiving a
licence from the Government. Why
should the Minister be empowered to say
what these trout-fishing licences should
be, whereas the fishermen on the various
coasts are tied to the amount of duty
within the Act of Parliament itself. I
must say I can see no logical reason why
absolute discretion should be given to
the Ministry to fix one licence, and why
the other should be fixed definitely by
Act of Parliament. On these grounds,
I am opposed to the Clause.
I
Sir DUDLEY McCORKELL:
should like to support the Amendment,
but for rather different reasons. It
seems to me that to fish for sea trout
a licence is essential and that the salmon
licence covers both. This would seem
to come down to the question of brown
trout, and if in one district even with
the approval or by the Ministry such
Fisheries Bill-
Committee
23 OCTOBER 1934
licence is imposed, that, to my mind,
would be a hardship on a certain type
of person who fishes for brown trout. It
may be very primitive fishing. Nevertheless it is a form of amusement, and
there is nothing commercial in it. Today anybody can indulge in it.
With regard to what my hon. and
learned Friend said about judging a
trout rod or a salmon rod more by the
tackle, I think most brown trout are
killed by small boys with a line, a hook,
a worm, and a stick. These little boys
will require a licence in one district,
while in other districts they will not
require it. Such a thing would be
ridiculous and we would not be able to
enforce it. There are certain districts
where it might be considered desirable to
have a brown trout licence so that a
fisherman cannot present himself on a
river and proceed to catch salmon as well.
But this will not prevent that as he can
pay the small licence. If this Clause
were removed it would clear the air. If
the Clause remains and licences are imposed I think it will be difficult to enforce it, and where you cannot enforce
the law it is better not to have the law.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I rise also in
support of Senator Leslie's contention
that it would be better not to have this
Clause. A salmon licence ought to include salmon and brown trout for
several reasons. At the present time the
fashion seems to be to catch salmon with
trout tackle. If a salmon fisherman is
using trout tackle he may catch salmon,
but he may catch trout and he will be
summoned for not having a brown trout
licence. If the Committee decide not to
delete this Clause altogether they should,
at any rate, see that a salmon licence
should include a trout licence. Although
I think a licence for brown trout fishing
would be of considerable value in regard to
the preservation of brown trout fry and
capturing of immature fish, I do not think
the advantage gained from that would
justify the retension of the Clause.
I
do not see how the brown trout licence,
costing 2/6 as in England, can be limited
to one stream. If it is not to apply to
other little streams and fishing grounds,
I do not see how it is to be exacted or
insisted on. I think with the limitation,
the sport would be heavily restricted,
and the difficulty of enforcing the 2/6
would be insuperable. I support
Senator Leslie's suggestion.
Mr. LONG: In the evidence tendered
to the Committee on Local Fishery
Administration whose recommendations
are embodied in this Bill, the principle
of requiring trout anglers to make some
contribution to the expenses of the boards
of conservators in carrying out their
duties of protection found a considerable
measure of support. It is recognised,
however, that the application of the
principle to juveniles and to small land-
'owners fishing in indifferent waters
adjoining their property would be
difficult to justify. Το meet this
difficulty the Committee recommended
that the Ministry should be empowered
to impose by way of bylaw a duty on
trout rods in either a part or whole of
any fishery district. Such a bylaw will
only be made after all the interests
affected have been given an opportunity
of being heard, and the Fishery Acts provide for an appeal to the Privy Council
against any bylaw made by the Ministry. I think, therefore, it will be
agreed that there are ample safeguards
against this power being exercised without due consideration for the interests
affected. I regret that I am unable to
accept this Amendment.
I may point out that it is not intended
that a salmon rod licence holder should
be required to pay also for a trout rod
licence. If the hon. and learned
Senator will submit an appropriate
Amendment for the Report stage it will
be accepted by the Government.
Mr. LESLIE: In answer to the
Deputy-Leader, I may say I am in somewhat of a difficulty. He has, as regards
one of my objections, offered to accept
an Amendment, but there remains the
question as to the wisdom of the Clause.
As regards that, support has been furnished from different quarters of the
House. While I shall certainly take
advantage of the suggestion at a later
stage made by the Deputy-Leader, I do
not think I ought to refrain from having
the views of the Committee on this particular Clause.
Question put: "That this Clause be
left out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 10;
Not Contents, 7.

(1) Any person guilty of an offence

a first offence to a fine not exceeding

or any subsequent offence to a fine not

three months, or to both such fine and
imprisonment.

Adoption of Children (Workmen's Compensation) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: There have been so many
complaints lately in various quarters and
in this House with regard to the practice
of what is called legislation by reference
that I think it well to make this Bill
the opportunity of explaining to hon.
Members that this method of drafting
is often really impossible to avoid. This
Bill is an excellent case in point for,
while it is to be cited together with the
Adoption of Children Act, that is to
say, forms part of the code of statutes
dealing with the subject of adoption of
children, its immediate purpose is to
effect an amendment-or rather I
should say, to clarify the existing state.
of law in the matter of Workmen's Compensation, a subject dealt with by a
quite distinct code of statutes.
Hon. Members will appreciate the impracticability of any proposal to re-enact
the whole of the Workmen's Compensation code in order to deal with a minor
point, especially when the point at issue
is one more directly related to another
code dealing with adoption of children.
In deference, however, to the view
which certain hon. Senators expressed
the other day in dealing with another
Bill, and with which I have much sympathy, I shall be at pains to make clear
to the House exactly what is contained
in any existing statutory provisions to
which reference is made in this Bill.
The Workmen's Compensation Act of
1927 provided, as hon. Members are
aware, that if a workman is killed in
certain circumstances the members of his
family dependent upon his earnings
should be entitled to a certain scale of
compensation. The definition of "members of his family" the relevant part of
which is repeated in Clause 1 of the Bill
now before the House, did not, however,
make it perfectly clear whether it was
the intention of the Act that adopted
children should count as children of the
deceased for compensation purposes.
The point has not so far actually been
tested in Northern Ireland, but a case
has lately arisen in England in which
the Court of Appeal decided that
adopted children were not dependants
within the meaning of the Act. I think
hon. Members will agree that there
would have been a manifest inequity in
allowing the law to continue in
accordance with that decision. This
was certainly the view taken by the Imperial authorities, who, this year, gave
their blessing to a Bill, introduced by a
private Member, and now passed into
law, upon which this Bill is almost
exactly modelled-the purpose in both
cases being to secure that adopted
children should rank equally with other
Adoption of Children Bill
Appropriation Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
dependants in securing benefits of compensation provided for by the Workmen's
Compensation Acts operative in each
country. I think in justice that this
object can hardly be challenged and it,
therefore, needs no further defence upon
my part.
I should explain in connection with
Sub-section (2) that Sub-section (5) of
Section 11 of the Adoption of Children
Act (Northern Ireland), 1929, to which
reference is made, simply provides that a
sealed or stamped copy of an entry in
the adopted children register shall be
received as evidence of an adoption and,
where such a date is specified, of the
date of birth, without further proof
being adduced.
When the British Bill was under consideration my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Labour, in co-operation with
the Imperial Home Office, took the first
step towards securing reciprocity between
Great Britain and Northern Ireland with
regard to the evidence of adoption receivable in the courts for the purposes of
compensation claims by securing the insertion in the British Bill of a provision
that extracts from the Northern Ireland
adoption register shall be receivable as
evidence in Great Britain. Sub-clause
(2) of this Bill now completes the reciprocal arrangements by providing that
extracts from the British adoption registers shall be so receivable in Northern
Ireland.
There is little more to be said about
the Bill. It deals with a small matter,
but a matter of justice, and I am, therefore, quite sure that it will be welcomed
by hon. Members on both sides of the
House. I beg to move.
Question proposed: "That the Bill be
now read a Second time.”
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we shall all
accept this Bill, perhaps, if I may say so,
chiefly because we believe it carries out
the intention of this Parliament when
in 1929 we passed the main Measure
dealing with the adoption of children.
There are only two points that occur to
one that ought to be mentioned. One
is, is it likely or possible that the Bill
will have any retrospective action? I
rather gather from what the Parliacases
I
mentary Secretary said that no
have as yet arisen in our courts, and
that therefore there has been no necessity for a judicial decision which might,
or might not, have accorded with the
decisions which were given by the county
court, I think it was, in England.
imagine there is no likelihood of any
cases pending, or anything of that sort.
I observe that the Bill follows as closely
as can possibly be done the English Bill,
which has now become an Act, and I
think there is no question that whatever
we may do here will be on the English
lines.
That seems to dispose of the first prob-
The
lem, but there is another one.
English Bill received the Royal Assent
on 12th July last, and therefore has been
law for about four months. I am not
at all sure that we ought not to provide
at the next stage something to ensure
that this Bill shall be deemed to have
come into operation on the same date
as the Measure which now applies to so
much of the United Kingdom as is comprised by the term England, Scotland,
and Wales. I will put that on the Paper
in order that it may be considered.
I
do not think it is a question that the Parliamentary Secretary ought to be asked
to answer off-hand. There may be something in the points. Is there anything to
be gained by dating this Bill back to the
date when the English Measure received
the Royal Assent? That can be appropriately considered when we take the Bill
in the Committee stage. Meanwhile, I
heartily support the Bill as a whole
chiefly for the reason that it carries out
what I believe was our intention five
years ago.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.

Mr. LONG: As this Bill is a Money
Bill and is introduced simply in order
to give effect to a small Supplementary
Estimate of the Ministry of Commerce
I shall content myself with a summary
23 OCTOBER 1934
Appropriation Bill

Adjournment
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
explanation. The purpose of the Supplementary Estimate was to meet the
cost of a subsidy to the Northern Irish
herring fishing boats. This was intended to tide over the unfortunate
situation created by the failure of the
herting fishing season this year and to
enable the owners of the boats to hold
them in readiness for the next season in
the hope of better times. The amount
involved is only £811.
I beg to move,
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."

of the Clock.

CLAUSE 1 (Effect for purposes of

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie-Clause 2, page 2, after line 22,
insert the following new sub-section :-
"This Act shall be deemed to have
come into operation on the twelfth day
of July, nineteen hundred and thirtyfour.'
Mr. LESLIE: The Committee may
remember that I mentioned this matter
on the Second Reading. The difficulty,
if there is one, arises in this way. The
corresponding English Act came into
operation on 12th July. We dealt with
the matter, so far as Second Reading is
concerned, on 23rd October, and there
was therefore a possibility, which at that
time it was thought possibly did not
exist, that some cases might have arisen
in the meantime. There is also the possibility that between that date and the
date when our own Measure receives the
Royal Assent, which we will assume will
be the 14th November, another case
might arise, and it might be that some
injustice might occur. Therefore, in
order that it might be seen whether any
addition was desirable, necessary, or
practicable and I think the question of
practicability is the most difficult oneit was better that the matter should be
looked into, and the Committee will be
guided by such advice as the Government
may be in a position to tender to us.
Accordingly I beg to move the Amendment, which would have the effect of
dating our new Measure back to the
corresponding date of the commencement
of the operation of the English Act.
Mr. LONG: The Government regret
that they cannot see their way to accept
this Amendment. In the first place,
should any case have been decided since
12th July last on the basis that an
adopted child was not a dependant
within the meaning of the Workmen's
Compensation Acts, the effect of ante-
485 Adoption of Children Bill
25 OCTOBER 1934
For
an
dating the operation of this Bill might
be to disturb existing rights.
example, there is a maximum of £600
fixed as the total compensation payable
in respect of a widow with young
children. If this maximum had already
been allocated on the basis that
adopted child was excluded, and it was
subsequently decided that the adopted
child should be included, the amount
could not be re-allocated without
reducing the portion awarded to some of
the beneficiaries. It might even seem to
confer rights upon a person and at the
same time leave that person without any
machinery for enforcing them. I feel
sure that the hon. and learned Member,
with his legal experience, will be able
to appreciate the complications which.
might ensue in such circumstances.
He mentioned, I think, during the
Second Reading debate, that he had
examined the corresponding British Act,
and he will, no doubt, have observed it
was not made retrospective. Whether the
authorities in Great Britain were influenced by similar considerations I cannot say, but it is possible that such was
the case. At all events, they did not
ante-date their Act, even with the
knowledge that a claim for compensation in respect of an adopted child had
already been rejected by the Court of
Appeal.
Apart altogether from what I might
call the technical considerations to which
I have referred, there is the fact that,
so far as the Government is aware, there
are no cases to which the Amendment
would apply, and in view of the fact that
the number of registered adoptions is
very small, the possibility of any such
case having occurred is so remote as to
be, for all practicable purposes,
negligible.
If
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the
Parliamentary Secretary for looking into
the matter, and for his full reply.
any mischance does occur we are not responsible. We are wrecked on the rock
of practicability.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
