Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: The Measure which I am
introducing to the House to-day is, of
course, a Money Bill. It contains three
provisions, the first two of which are
concerned with financial administration
and are somewhat technical.
Clause 1 is to be construed in the light
of the Exchequer and Financial Provisions Acts of 1923 and 1927. Under
these Acts any surplus revenue accruing
in respect of a financial year is to be
paid into the Reserve Fund, which exists
primarily for the reduction of public
debt. It has happened that during the
past financial year my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Finance, was successful
in establishing a case for the transfer of
a considerable additional sum of money
to Northern Ireland in respect of the
residuary share of the reserved taxes for
previous years. This money being, therefore, surplus revenue in respect of those
years, although it represented more than
a normal single adjustment, would under
the present statutory authorities have
been paid into the Reserve Fund. But
as my right hon Friend explained in
making his Budget speech, the present
Financial Provisions Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
state of our finances made it necessary to
retain these amounts for revenue purposes in the Exchequer. The Clause of
which I am speaking is intended to regularise this position. It also provides for
similar disposal of the adjustments of
last year's residuary share of reserved
taxes to be received during the current
year.
Clause 2 deals with the Government
Loans Fund and provides for an increase
of £1,500,000 in the present statutory
limit to issues from the Fund. The
gross statutory limit thus will now stand
nominally at £8,500,000. I should explain
that this does not necessarily imply any
real increase in the scale of operations,
as would at first sight appear, for the
reason that although money received
back into the Fund in repayment of
former loans may be used to make new
ones, the sums so re-issued must be
counted as additional for purposes of
Parliamentary sanction. As a matter of
fact, the loans outstanding at the end
of last month, taking repayments into
account, amounted in round figures to
only £5,874,000. As hon. Members are
aware, the Government Loans Fund is
used to make loans not only to local
authorities, but for agricultural, fishery,
and educational purposes, as well as to
finance advances to the Claims Fund,
and to the Exchequer in connection with
housing, police barracks, the Bann
Drainage, and the like.
I now come to the third Clause, which
provides that a certificate of birth,
marriage, or death required for any purpose in connection with Ulster Savings
Certificates may be obtained for a fee of
1/-, instead of the normal fee of 3/7.
The concession proposed by the Clause
has in view the mitigation of a certain
hardship which is felt to exist in a few
cases, when, for instance, certificates of
death have to be obtained solely for the
purpose of realising a small holding of
Ulster Savings Certificates. Hon. Members will appreciate that in such instances
the normal fee is likely to be unduly
high in proportion to the estate of the
deceased person. The principle involved
is by no means a novel one; it has already
been applied in the case of National
Savings Certificates and in a variety of
other circumstances, as, for example,
under Section 32 of the Unemployment
Insurance Act, 1920, for the purposes of
that Act.
It will be seen that there is nothing
controversial in any of the provisions of
this Bill which I have outlined and I
hope that what I have said will serve
as a sufficient explanation of its con-
I beg to move.
tents.
Mr. LESLIE: I think we might fairly
ask the Parliamentary Secretary to tell
us at some stage during the career of
the Bill what has given rise to the suggestion in Clause 2, that the Minister of
Finance shall be deemed always to have
had power to issue excess sums. It rather
suggests that there is something that
ought to be made clearer to us than
appears in the text of the Clause. I do
not for a moment oppose the Bill. It is
merely a matter of machinery, but I
think we ought to quite understand what
we are doing.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Might I ask the
Deputy-Leader of the House if he can
give us a few more reasons for the
increases of loans the Government are
asking for, and if he would give the
agricultural and farming community
greater benefits than those they are receiving at the present time?
Mr. LONG: I cannot furnish an
answer to the point raised by Senator
Leslie at the present time. I will let
him know on a future occasion. With
regard to the question by Senator
McLaughlin, the amount actually issued
up to date is £7,492,206, and of that
there has been repaid £1,682,227. As
to the items for which he has asked, they
are made up of loans to local authorities
amounting to £3,734,204. That is for
road making, housing and so on. Bann
Drainage, £125,000; development loans
to agriculture, £72,728; fisheries,
£10,609; education, £23,850. That is
building of schools and so on. Advances
to Claims Fund, £424,700. Hon.
Senators will, perhaps, understand that
under the Licensing Act off-licences were
to be abolished and compensation was
paid to the holders. The Government
advanced the money, and that is to be
repaid by the existing licensed houses over
a period of forty-two years. Of that
£35,733 has been repaid. Housing
amounted to £2,391,205; police barracks,
£301,759; voluntary schools, £20,500.
25 OCTOBER 1934
Local Government Bill-

Second Reading
change in the character of the local
government system, and in the second it
does not impose new functions upon local
authorities of such a kind as necessarily
to involve increased public expenditure.
So far as the latter part of this statement is concerned, I am confident that
the Bill will meet with unanimous
approval, but I anticipate that criticism.
may be levelled at it in so far as it makes
no attempt to carry into effect the main
recommendations of the Local Government Commission.
Mr. McMAHON: In Clause 2 of this
Bill it is proposed to take power to issue
additional money to the extent of
£1,500,000. I take it that money is to
assist industries, and to that I have no
objection whatever. But I take the
opportunity of this Clause to make my
protest against statements made from
time to time by people across the water
about the Loans Guarantee Act. I base
my objection on these grounds, that any
money guaranteed by this Government is
raised from the taxpayers of Ulster or
Northern Ireland, and we are entitled
to be masters in our own house. I think
the time has arrived when the Northern
Government should have a little more
backbone and tell the English authorities
that any money that has been spent here
has been raised by the taxpayers of
Northern Ireland, and that we resent
any criticism. We are entitled, if we
think fit, to assist Northern Ireland
industries with our own money.
We are
told by the Minister of Finance that we
are making a contribution to the Imperial
I want to put it quite
Exchequer.
frankly, that if I had my will not one
penny of Northern Ireland
would
money
go into the English Exchequer. I would
reserve that money to help our own
industry. The
sooner the Northern
Government faces up to the English
Government and tells them to mind their
own business and we will mind ours, the
better.
Mr. LONG: By leave of the House
might I point out that the Senator who
has just spoken seems to be under a misapprehension.
This Bill has nothing
whatever to do with the Loans Guarantee
Fund to which he referred. It is entirely for our own local purposes.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Local Government Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: Before I attempt, so far
as is appropriate at this early stage, to
define the general purport of this Bill,
I should like to make quite clear at the
outset what it is not. In the first place,
it is not intended to make any sweeping
I would remind hon. Members that
two or three years ago a Bill was, in fact,
projected which would have embodied the
substance of those recommendations.
The proposal met, however, with strong
public opposition, particularly upon the
part of those immediately concerned in
local government and the local authorities
themselves. This opposition was based
chiefly upon considerations of finance,
and the supervention of the severe financial
crisis of 1931 finally determined the
Government to postpone indefinitely the
reorganisation proposals. Moreover, the
working out of the Commission's scheme
would have demanded substantial modification in the light of several important
pieces of subsequent legislation cutting
across the whole system of local government, such as the Derating Act, the
Medical Benefits Act, and finally, the
Revaluation Act, the implications of
which must continue to receive careful
and prolonged examination.
In the meanwhile, however, a considerable body of suggestions from various
quarters had accumulated, most of which
had in view the improvement of such
parts of the existing machinery as would
remain unaffected by the major changes
in the system, and it was determined that
these should be put into effect without
further delay. The Bill now before the
House is, therefore, intended, if I may
so express myself, to lubricate the wheels
of the existing local government machine.
I should like to have been able to say
with confidence that the Bill would,
therefore, have been regarded as
non-controversial. The Government
certainly anticipated that it would be so
regarded, and with every reason, because
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Home Affairs, took care to consult the
bodies representative of the various
classes of local authorities upon every
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
question which is dealt with in the Bill
and had, in fact, secured their approval
to practically all its provisions.
In particular, I would like to mention.
that the Association of Municipal
Authorities specifically endorsed the
Government's proposals, with one or two
exceptions, and that subsequently,
following the formal views expressed by
the Association, numerous conferences
were held at the offices of the Ministry
with representatives of the Association
and of individual authorities. During
the course of these discussions many of
the points raised were conceded, and the
reasons why a comparatively small
number of them could not be admitted
were fully explained. Its, perhaps, too
animated passage through another place,
was a somewhat ironical comment upon
the value of the best intentions. Since
its introduction there it has, however,
been very considerably amended in order
to meet any reasonable criticism, and I
hope that I may now present it to this
House as an agreed Measure.
As hon. Members will have been able
to infer from what I have said, the provisions of the Bill now before the House,
though they touch on the system of local
government at many points, are
necessarily of rather miscellaneous
character, and few of them are immediately related to
related to each other. It is
essentially a Committee stage Bill. Such
remarks as will now be suitable for me
to make must, therefore, either be very
extensive in order to cover the whole of
the 47 Clauses and 3 Schedules, or else
be kept as brief as possible. Hon. Members probably know me well enough to
have guessed which alternative I shall
prefer to choose. (Laughter.)
The system of local government as it
exists to-day has grown up gradually over
the past century, and is a combination of
several codes of laws which were distinct
in origin and have never, except to a
small extent, been expressly co-ordinated.
The oldest of these codes is that which relates to the poor law. Next in order of
time comes that relating to town government, upon which was grafted the
administration of matters connected with
public health. Rural administration
under county and rural district councils
-the latter a close link with the old
poor law system-is of the most recent
date. In consequence, county and rural
district councils are, perhaps, on the
whole, in possession of more up-to-date
and more practical administrative
machinery than is the case in urban
administration outside the county
boroughs.
The Bill is so sub-divided as to bring
its different parts so far as possible into
intelligible relation with these separate
existing codes. Part I relates to urban
government and, in the main, extends to
urban authorities provisions which have
been found convenient and effective in
relation to the work of the more recently
constituted bodies. But the experience
of nearly forty years, and especially of
the last ten since our last Local Government Act was passed, has proved that
these, too, are not beyond the reach of
improvement, and Part II contains sixteen Clauses to deal with anomalies existing in local government generally. Part
III deals with public health matters, and
Part IV with poor relief. Part V contains a few miscellaneous provisions which
do not fall clearly within any of the preceding categories.
The greater part of the Bill being.
therefore, of a rather technical character,
I propose to confine my remarks upon this
occasion to those Clauses which most
directly concern "the man in the street."
Those hon. Members who are members of
local authorities, or have a special interest
in local government, will, of course,
understand the effect of the administrative changes and, I hope, approve of
them, without the help of anything I can
say, and I think it must be admitted that
the public at large, not being in any
case conversant with the details of the
powers, rules, and regulations which
govern the procedure and functions of
local authorities, will be less likely to
appreciate the value of the various proposed changes in this direction, important as many of them are, and would
prefer to hear something of the more
obvious ways in which the Bill will affect
them personally.
There is one matter in which practically
everyone is compelled to take some
interest, sometimes a morbid interest,
and that is, the matter of rating. The
Bill contains two provisions in this connection. Clause 4, which is, however.
of the less importance from the ratepayers' point of view, consolidates the
various existing rates in urban districts
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
25 OCTOBER 1934
into a single rate for collection purposes,
as has already been done by Private
Acts in some urban areas. This is really
only a matter of administrative convenience and will not make any difference to
the amount which a man will be called
upon to pay. But Clause 17 contains a
proposal which will, in some cases, make
such a difference. Following the model
of provision already obtaining under
Private Acts in certain towns, it makes
general the principle that rateability is
to be proportionate to the period of rateable occupation. The existing law contains the manifest inequity that a man
may be called upon to pay rates for the
whole year, although he has only been in
occupation of the rateable premises for
part of the time, and also imposes upon
a person coming into occupation of
premises in respect of which the rate has
not been paid the liability for arrears
incurred by his predecessor. I should
imagine that there are few people who
will not grant that the Bill here effects a
much needed improvement in the existing law.
I would like to call attention to one
provision which deals with a matter
rather outside the range of the Bill as a
whole. This is Clause 30, which relates
to the licensing of places of public entertainment. The Clause seeks to COordinate the various procedures which
must be followed at present with regard
to licensing of various types of places of
entertainment and to extend the licensing provision to all kinds of these places.
Cinemas, which are already dealt with
in detail by the Cinematograph Act of
1909, and theatres and music halls,
covered by the Intoxicating Liquor and
Licensing Act (Northern Ireland), 1927,
have, however, been excluded from its
scope. The licensing authority is to be
a sanitary authority, that is to say, the
urban or rural district council, as the
case may be.
The conditions under which licence
may be granted or refused are laid down
fairly strictly, and provision has been.
made for appeal to the county court
from any decision of the sanitary
authority. The only other Clause in
Part III of the Bill to which I shall
specially refer is Clause 32, which
extends to Northern Ireland generally an
Act which has hitherto been in operation
only in those sanitary districts which have
adopted it, namely, the Tuberculosis
Prevention (Ireland) Act, 1908. This
Act relates to the notification of cases of
infectious diseases, the disinfection of
premises in which it has occurred, and
the like. This is a step which can hardly
fail to be greeted with approval and,
indeed, is one which is already long
overdue.
Without, of course, suggesting that
hon. Members of this House are likely to
be personally affected by them, I fancy
that the provisions of the Bill which relate to poor law relief in Part IV are
those which are likely to arouse the most
general interest. Clause 35 enables outdoor relief to be granted to a destitute
widow having a single legitimate child
depending upon her. It also amends a
provision in the Poor Law Relief Act
of 1862, which forbids out-door relief to
be given to the occupier of more than
quarter of an acre of land by substituting a valuation qualification of £3 for
a specified area. It stands to reason
that the real value of quarter of an acre
of land, that is its value as a means of
livelihood, must vary considerably, and
that the official valuation assessment
affords a fairer criterion of the resources
of the applicant for relief.
They
Clause 36 attempts to deal with a
rather curious abuse of the poor law
system, which has grown to be something
of a nuisance. It has become the practice
of a certain number of paupers to seek
admission to a workhouse simply in order
to obtain board and lodging for the
night and to leave the first thing in the
morning without having done any work
in respect of this accommodation.
even go so far as to return to the workhouse that very night, or shortly afterwards, to secure readmission, and again
depart in the morning, and so on. It is
hoped to remedy this state of affairs by
giving power to the workhouse authorities to refuse the discharge of any person
who has been found guilty of this
practice and to detain him there for a
period of not more than three days. It
is thought that this will have the effect
of preventing any person whose circumstances do not really warrant his recourse
to poor relief from availing himself of
the workhouse in this way.
I wish to say
a few words about
Clause 43 of the Bill, which is the last
Clause apart from the formal Clauses
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
dealing with interpretation, construction,
and the like. This provides that where
a local authority is bound, by statute, to
make any payment into the Exchequer
or any fund under the control of a
Government department, that the sum
due may be deducted from any Parliamentary grant made to the local
authority or, as sometimes arises, to any
other local authority on its behalf. The
principle underlying this Clause is, of
course, not a new one, and recent events
have shown the necessity for its continuance and extension; it has already
received Parliamentary approval in the
Local Government Act of 1923, which,
in turn, more or less re-enacted a similar
provision in the Act of 1898. The
reference in that case was, however, only
to certain specific grants, in 1898 the
chief grants paid by the Exchequer to
local authorities, namely, those formerly
paid out of the local taxation account--
the Workhouse Officers Salaries Act, the
Union Medical and Educational Expenditure Act, the Sanitary Salaries Act, and
so on. As their names show, these grants
were appropriated to particular local
services and, although the existing
powers could, no doubt, legally be made
use of in a wide variety of circumstances,
it has not been considered quite in order
that where a local authority defaults in
some payment due to a Government
department in respect of a particular
service, that default should be made good
by deduction from grants which Parliament has earmarked for use in connection with another service altogether.
The remarks which I have been able to
make in the time at my disposal have,
of course, been quite inadequate, taken
by themselves, to give any indication of
the complexity of the subjects dealt with
by the Bill. Hon. Members have, however, now had time, and I hope opportunity, to make themselves conversant
with those parts of its text in which they
are individually interested.
I would
emphasise once more that the Government
has been at pains to meet the views of
those immediately concerned with each
particular branch of local government.
Many of the points covered by the Bill
are matters in which such expert help
and guidance has been particularly
desired and appreciated; and I should
like to take this opportunity of expressing the thanks of the Government to all
who have assisted with criticism and suggestions. There seems to me little doubt
that the efforts of all parties throughout
which, despite differences in points of
detail, upon the whole a spirit of the
most friendly co-operation has
has been
shown, will be rewarded by a smoother
working of the local government system,
greater efficiency in its results and a
better adjustment of the delicate balance
which has continually to be maintained
between the many independent or semiindependent authorities within the
system, in order that the public interests
may best be served. I beg to move.
Question proposed: That the Bill be
now read a Second time."
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I consider this
a very important Bill and certainly a
very useful one, but I am only expressing
public opinion in saying that this Bill
has been rushed too much. The public
bodies concerned have not had the opportunity of seeing the Bill and discussing
it, and I think there should not be such
a rush as far as the Measure is concerned.
The Government have already been
criticised with regard to other Bills
rushed through both Houses, and I
thought that would have at least done
away with this rushing of Bills and so
give public bodies more time to study
them and express their views. With regard to the Clause making the landlord
responsible for the payment of rates on
houses with a valuation of £8, I respectfully suggest that the £8 should be reduced to £5, because I would say that
the majority of houses in the towns are
valued at a great deal less than £8. I
think the majority of houses in the towns,
and particularly in the poorer areas, have
valuations of £4 10s and upwards.
I notice with reference to the auditing
of accounts that there is nothing stated
as to stocktaking in connection with the
property of urban councils or anything
of that kind. I have always thought in
the auditing of accounts some explanation should be given by the auditor in
regard to the stock held by a particular
council. In the council of which I have
the honour to be chairman I never
heard of stocktaking. I always thought
it was most necessary to have a check on
the
money expended on stocks from time to
time, and also to see that the stock was
497 Local Government Bill-

25 OCTOBER 1934
Second Reading
accounted in proper order. I also think
that the auditor should see that moneys
other than rates due to the council are
accounted for. We had experience in
the Armagh Urban Council where these
moneys were not accounted for, and I
think it should be the duty of the auditor
to inquire into these things when auditing
the accounts.
-we
The auditor should also give a statement as far as he can as to the collection
of the rates and as to how the ratecollectors are carrying out their duties.
In the Armagh Urban Council we had a
great deal of difficulty with the officials
all remember the rate-collector.
The rate-collector on her own information
was arrested. We discovered that for a
number of years the system with regard
to the rate-collector and officials was anything but what it should be. We discovered that the rate-collector, instead of
lodging the rates as she received them,
lodged two-thirds of them within the last
week, or two or three days of the end
of each financial half-year. That was
not a proper system. In cases like that
the rate-collector and officials in charge
should be brought to book. I am surprised that the Ministry allowed such a
state of affairs to go on and did not
bring it to the notice of the council concerned. I agree with Clause 17 in regard to the apportionment of rates.
has always been a bone of contention and
a great hardship that a tenant going into
a house in the middle of the year on
which the previous tenant had not paid
his rates should be held responsible for
them. It is well to clear the air on that
matter. With regard to sub-section 4,
of Clause 17, which permits local councils
on the prompt payment of accounts to
allow discount. That is a good thing and
follows the line of the Electricity Board
in allowing 5 per cent. discount on bills
paid within a month after the account is
furnished.
It
This Bill is a very good Bill if it were
not for the fact that a certain Clause is
introduced. The Clause I refer to is
Clause 24. I strongly object to this
Clause, which is brought in for no other
purpose than to still further disfranchise
the minority and to make sure that
though the Catholics may increase in
numbers, and as in Tyrone and Fermanagh have the majority of votes on the
register, they will not be able to secure
control of the county councils, important
borough, urban or rural councils. The
Government foresee that, despite all the
penalties placed on Catholics on account
of their religion and for no other reason,
Catholics in the Six Counties are gaining
ground and increasing their numbers.
Already the Government see the writing
on the wall and are arranging now that
no matter what happens their party will
hold control. Take County Tyrone, the
Catholics, who number 74,000 of the
population, have nine members on the
county council, and the Protestants, who
number 59,000 of the population, have 24
members on the county council, thus
giving the minority of the population of
the county a majority of 15 over the
majority. It is the same thing in
County Fermanagh, and equally so in
the other counties, where owing to the
gerrymandering of the Government the
Catholics have not
not anything like a
fair representation on the county councils
or other public bodies.
Can anyone guarantee that these
councils under this Clause will not avail
themselves of its provisions and see that
the boundaries are left in such a way
as to secure their position and power on
these councils for all time? The Parliamentary Secretary of the Ministry of
Home Affairs stated in the other House
that there was no intention to injure the
Catholics under this Clause, and that
their position will not be interfered with
under this Bill. Will he give a guarantee that Lisnaskea Electoral Division,
where the Catholics hope to secure a
majority, will not be interfered with? If
he meant what he stated and if the Government were honest they would withdraw the Clause and at least show
Catholics that they could go on in a
constitutional way and, where possible,
elect representatives to the boards concerned. If the Government refuse to
withdraw this Clause the Catholics must
conclude that the Government's intention
is to victimise still further Catholics and
refuse them the fair representation on
public boards to which their numbers
entitle them.
In the other House reference was made
to the Armagh Urban Council. Why
were we not told what led up to the dissolution of that council and of the work
of the Unionist party, together with
three unworthy Nationalists who helped
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr.. McLaughlin]
them in league with officials, some of
whom had been forced to resign and who
were not doing as they should or whose
dealings in the affairs of the council were
looked on with grave suspicion? I have
already referred to the rate-collector.
We all know how the rate-collector acted,
that about £4,000 of poor rate and town
rate "went west," that a week before
the rate collector was arrested the Ministry decided that the rate-collector's affairs
should be investigated, and that though
the rate-collector had refused to send in
her lists of uncollected and irrecoverable
rates, on the proposition of a Unionist
member, with the solid votes of six
Unionist members, together with three
Nationalist members they carried a resolution refusing to inquire into her affairs,
though they knew quite well what was
going on. A few days afterwards she was
arrested on her own confession.
The stage was well set. The Unionist
members of the council were set on
having that council dissolved. They did.
their work well, and the Ministry
finished it for them. Mark you,
although it had been pointed out to the
Ministry's officials that unauthorised
receipts were being issued by the ratecollector, and they inspected her books,
they could find nothing wrong, and had
to wait for her confession to find her
out. The history of the workings of the
Armagh Urban Council by their officials
for the past number of years would not
make nice reading. The Ministry are
not blameless in this matter, but the only
conclusion one connected with that
council could come to is the Ministry
made up their minds that that council
should go, and
the officials congave
cerned all the rope they required. No
wonder the Ministry refuse to hold any
inquiry. Catholics form the majority of
the population of the city of Armagh.
A Commissioner has been appointed, and
he has already made two very important
appointments. Neither was given to a
Catholic.
Let us turn now to the fair play Catholics
get from the county council and rural
councils in my native county, Armagh.
Remember, the Catholic population is
50,000 against a Protestant population of
60,000. In the Armagh County Council's
Secretary's office, in the county surveyor's
office, the accountant's office, in the
regional education office; the Armagh
Union office, the Armagh Rural Council's
office, and the Armagh Carnegie Library,
not a single Catholic is employed.
Catholics are not allowed even to sweep
the floors of any of those offices. So much
for fair play so far as our county is concerned. Take the Grand Jury of County
Armagh. No Catholic is allowed to act
on it, and as far as the visiting justices
appointed by the Grand Jury are concerned no Catholic is appointed either.
So much for the liberties extended to
Catholics.
These are a few of the injustices the
Catholics in my county have to stand.
What must it be in other counties? And
then you talk of religious liberty. Yes,
liberty of a kind, but on account of his
religion a Catholic is debarred from his
right to public and Government appointments under your Government. We all
know that the county councils all over the
Six Counties have been left with
Protestant majorities, and under this
Clause you are giving them a perpetuity
lease so that they can gerrymander the
counties to suit their wishes and so hold
their majorities on these councils for all
time. And this you call fair play.
The history of the Six-County Government since it was established has been
nothing but victimisation of Catholics.
First you brought in your redistribution
and gerrymandering Bill and made sure
you had a majority on every one of the
county councils. After having done
this you brought in your Education Bill
and left the regional committees to be
set up by these county councils to work
the Education Act. They take the rates
paid by Catholics to spend on so-called
regional schools, which, in reality, are
Protestant schools. Now, this Clause is
putting the finishing touch to your
efforts. If you refuse to withdraw this
Clause and persist in putting it on the
Statute Book then I say you have gone
as far as you can constitutionally go to
victimise your Catholic citizens. I sincerely trust the Government will see
their way to withdraw this Clause and
give the party I represent a little bit of
confidence in the future. It will help us
to get along with our various vocations
as best we can instead of having these
irritating enactments brought in to
penalise us still further.
501 Local Government Bill-

25 OCTOBER 1934
Second Reading
Colonel SINCLAIR: Elsewhere disappointment has been expressed in connection with the various changes outlined
in this Bill regarding medical services,
and perhaps I will be allowed to develop
it a little bit. No doubt a great change
for the better was instituted when a
number of workhouses were properly
equipped and turned into district hospitals with committees appointed to look
after them, but the Government appear
to have missed their opportunity of
making mandatory what up to the
present has been more or less voluntary.
The provision of medical treatment for
the destitute sick, or those nearly belonging to that category, should be placed
under the county councils. If the boards
of guardians, who are very heavily
worked, were relieved of the duty of
attending to these medical matters I
think it would be to the very great
advantage of the sick people of the
Province. The whole drift of medical
treatment has been in the direction, or
ought to be in the direction, of the coordination of the different hospitals, the
poor law hospitals and also the district
hospitals. It seems to me the boards of
guardians are not the proper authorities
to deal with these medical matters, which
should be an additional burden upon the
county councils. In that way more good
would result, as there would be less dissipation of medical efforts and medical
skill. I think a considerable reduction
would be effected in human suffering and
that economies would also be brought
about. Instead of a number of places
equipped with costly appliances used only
at intervals, there should be a linking up
of the various hospitals in such a way
that cases requiring the most modern
treatment with apparatus of a costly
nature would be easily available by one
medical authority co-operating with the
next medical authority.
There is one minor matter about which
I would like to say a word. I think it
a pity that in this Local Government Bill
an attempt was not made to insist on the
transfer of the care of the sick from the
boards of guardians, who have a great
deal to do, to the county councils with
separate committees to look after the
various details. One other minor point
seems to require some attention, that is
the provision in the Bill in the case of a
woman in labour. The dispensary
doctors under the present régime are
required to attend such cases when
called upon. If people go the right way
about it the necessary "red lines" can
be obtained, but in the case of a large
number of people not exactly under the
dispensary system but who refuse to pay
fees for attention to women in labour,
these dispensary doctors are to be called
upon to attend those people outside their
proper sphere. It would be very much
better if it were clearly understood that a
midwife in distress over a difficult case
would be able to call in any medical man,
and that medical man should at least
have the satisfaction of knowing that
somebody will pay a reasonable fee for
his services without demur. The
primary consideration, of course, is the
safety of the mother, and, in a secondary
degree, of the child.
If this service on the borderline of the poor law is to be left in an
indeterminate fashion it will place members of the medical profession in a very
awkward position indeed. No doctor, I
think, is unwilling to attend in an emergency case, but fees are not likely to
be paid if the regulations are left in a
nebulous form. These regulations require some remodelling. The question of
transforming the whole medical service
for the poor people in the Province
should be tackled. That would be something that would demand true statesmanship and would be worthy of our
admiration and confidence. The improvements contained in this Bill are all
of a trivial nature and are not quite what
might be expected in an amending
Measure of this description.
Mr. GLENDINNING: There was no
genuine hostility shown to this Bill
during its passage in another place, as
was evidenced by the fact that it passed
without a division, and notwithstanding
the very able speech of Senator
McLaughlin I do not anticipate there will
be any serious hostility here. The most
serious objection from the Government
benches was that it did not go as far as
had been hoped, particularly in regard to
the poor law. But, as the Deputy-
Leader of the House has informed us,
there were very good reasons for postponing some of the proposed changes, and
we have the assurance of the Prime
Minister that when better circumstances
arise further reforms will be carried out.
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Glendinning]
The only passionate outbreak against
the Measure has come from Portrush,
where some members of the urban
council and the town clerk, who, by the
way, is an exceedingly efficient officer,
had some very hard things to say against
the Bill and against the Government.
The principal criticism of the council
there seems to be that some people, who
take business premises during the season
and reap a good profit, escape paying a
proportion of the rates. While the contention that that is unfair is very popular
in Portrush, it is to my mind a very onesided and narrow view for the Portrush
Urban Council to take. The council
might be surprised to know that another
seaside resort, partically similarly situated to Portrush, actually asked for this
legislation, and since the remarks were
made the Association of Municipal
Authorities have themselves approved of
the Measure.
The argument might be advanced that
if the premises to which the town clerk
refers would otherwise be vacant during
the whole year, then Portrush will be
in a happier position than many other
towns which have not a seasonal trade
in getting at least some proportion of
the rates from those premises rather than
none at all. It would almost seem to be
case in Portrush of "He is a stranger.
Take him in." Of course the main argument in favour of the proposal in the
Bill is that it is ridiculous that a person
should be called upon to pay rates upon
premises with regard to which he has no
responsibility whatever, having vacated
them. Besides, to my mind, it would
be a very difficult thing to formulate
legislation which would remove the dissatisfaction of Portrush without destroying a provision which removes many
injustices and without creating greater
grievances elsewhere.
The Measure contains many provisions
which deserve public approval. As we
have heard from the Deputy-Leader, the
Bill contains provision giving power to
alter boundaries, and for local councils
to make bylaws with regard to good
government. A member can now, under
the Bill, retire from a public board without being fined. That fine should have
gone by the board long ago. The Bill
also provides for the licensing of places
for public entertainment, the extension
of urban powers to rural areas, the extension of outdoor relief, and many other
things. I shall only refer to one of them.
I have never been a member of the Derry
Corporation, but as a party official I
have had long experience of municipal
elections, and I am sure the citizens of
Derry will rejoice in the knowledge that
their elections will no longer be carried
out in the depth of winter with its slushy,
dirty streets and other discomforts.
While not a candidate at these elections
I have had to attend meetings, look up
doubtful voters, attend at the municipal
polling booths, and SO on, and my
memories are not happy ones.
Only on one occasion, if I may be
allowed to refer to it, did I stand as a
candidate. The occasion was the first
municipal election. under the Local
Government Act of 1898, when a Corporation of ten aldermen and thirty
councillors, as constituted under that
Act, had to be elected on the appointed
day. In one of the wards the present
town clerk of Derry was a candidate for
an aldermanship and myself and one
other for councillorships. This was
done for the purpose of testing the voting
strength of a doubtful ward but without
any hope of success, and of course
were defeated. But after a very exhaustive poll a very extraordinary fact
became apparent, proving the perfect
organisation and the loyalty of the voters.
The three Unionist candidates who stood
at that election polled exactly the same
number of votes. There were eleven
candidates at that ward election, and it
is a rather sad reflection that of them
all only Sir Henry Miller and myself are
alive at this day. I think this Bill is a
distinct advance in local government and
shall have my support.
we
Mr. McMAHON: I would like, at the
outset, to congratulate the hon. Senator
(Mr. Glendinning), who has just spoken,
on his very able speech. I am sure I
am voicing the opinion of the whole
Senate when I say that we hope to hear
him very often in this House. With
regard to the Bill which we are discussing at the moment, I would just like
to say that it is like the parson's egg,
good in parts. With regard to Clause
24, of which Senator McLaughlin spoke,
that is a Clause that is indeed objectionable to the minority. As a minority we
have had long experience of what happens
in connection with the altering of
505 Local Government Bill-

25 OCTOBER 1934
Second Reading
boundaries, and when you place the power
to alter boundaries in the hands of a local
authority we are in the new difficulty that
we will not know whether the local
authority will treat us more unjustly than
the present Government have treated us.
From the passing of the 1920 Act I
do not know how many Acts of Parliament have been passed in order to
penalise the minority, and it was done.
in such an insidious way that it was
passed before we knew what was happening. We can well remember the first
gerrymandering Act that was passed to
gerrymander the late Mr. Devlin out of
West Belfast. That was the worst piece
of work ever done in the city of Belfast,
because everybody must recognise that
Mr. Devlin worked and gave his whole
life for every creed and class, and loved
his native city. Yet after all that he
had to go.
Then we had the latest
gerrymandering Act, whereby one-third
of the population is deprived of almost
any representation worth talking about.
The only place that the Nationalists now
control is the town of Omagh, and an
attempt has been made recently to gerrymander them out of the control of that
town.
I do not know what the Government
expect to gain by all this. Surely they
have sufficient control at the moment to
be able to leave us with some control.
Surely they must recognise that you cannot crush one-third of the population
with impunity, that it leaves behind a
sting that is not easily healed.
Every
time you pass any of these Acts to
penalise the minority you are antagonising the minority against you. I would
warn the Government that they have
tried the minority to the limit of their
patience by their latest attempt to
penalise them in this Bill, and I join
with Senator McLaughlin in what he said
with regard to the matter.
With regard to Clause 36, which deals
with the detention of people who go into
the workhouse, I do not know why this
Clause has been necessary. If a man
goes into the workhouse at night he only
goes there because he is so poor that he
cannot afford lodgings, and if he goes out
the next day he is probably going out to
look for work. If it is a question of insubordination you have sufficient power
under the law at the moment.
pauper goes to the workhouse and creates
any trouble the board of guardians have
If a
power at the moment to call in the police,
a thing that happens pretty frequently.
There must be something sinister in this
Clause, because I do not think there is
very much wrong with those workingmen
who happen to be so poor that they have
not the price of lodgings, who go to the
workhouse, and come out the next day
probably to look for work. I would not,
under any circumstances, stand for any
man breaking the rules of the workhouse,
but, as I have said, there is sufficient
law at the moment to cover that.
There is another Clause in the Bill with
regard to the removal of a member of a
public body who is supposed to be an
obstructionist. What is an obstructionist? We are not told in this Bill what
an obstructionist is. For instance, I
might have been described, on occasion,
as an obstructionist myself, and I suppose
I am one. Am I to be removed simply
because I criticise the actions of a certain
body? I want to tell you quite frankly
that I have no fear so long as the present
Lord Mayor controls the City Council of
Belfast that he will unduly exercise that
power (hon. Members: Hear, hear)-
but I could tell you of people who obstruct
without losing their tempers, who talk
for the sake of talking. They are real
obstructionists. Will you tell me what
they can be removed for? Then from
the minority side there is this danger:
Some chairman probably rules you out of
order; you think you are unfairly dealt
with, lose your patience for the time
being, and say things, and in all probability you are removed. You do that
simply because you are a representative
of the minority and have no redress
except to express yourself in that particular manner. But there is the danger
that when you are removed the majority
party may co-opt say, some Unionist to
represent a Nationalist ward. There is
always that danger. I do say that the
Government should give a fuller explanation of what an obstructionist is, because
at the moment I am at a loss to know.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I rise to call
attention to what I consider rather a
grave omission from this Bill. I refer to
the necessity of providing fire escapes and
proper exits from the hotels in our Six
Counties. In this House on the 26th
May, 1931, I put a question to the Leader
of the House regarding the provision of
proper fire escapes from hotels. I will
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Colonel Pakenham]
eventually read the answer of the Leader
of the House. This is really quite a
serious thing. We are always priding
ourselves on the increase of tourists
coming to Northern Ireland, and we want
to make their stay here as pleasant and
as safe as we can. I do not make any
allegation or accusation against any particular hotel. In the larger towns no
doubt proper arrangements are made for
the safe escape of any inhabitants of a
hotel if fire should occur, but in some of
the smaller towns, which are not governed
by urban councils but probably by town
commissioners or even rural councils,
there is great necessity for some alteration
of the law. The Government have been
appealed to on several occasions by
various rural district authorities to make
some bylaws, or to allow them to make
bylaws by which these things could be
made safe.
When I asked the question it was à
propos of hotels in the town of Antrim,
and the answer of the noble Viscount,
the Leader of the House, who, I am very
sorry to see is not in his place, was as
follows:
The matter has been carefully noted
and will be dealt with when a suitable
opportunity occurs. But I would
remind the House that dealing with it
in such a way would require legislation.
Here we have a Local Government Bill,
and it seems to me that this was the
occasion on which a provision of the kind
desired might very easily have been inserted. In Clause 29 the Ministry of
Home Affairs is taking power to
declare any additional public health
provisions in force in urban districts to
be in force in any rural district or
contributory place therein.
I would appeal to the Government to
help our rural authorities in this matter,
because it is a very important matter.
There are hotels without adequate means
of egress and ingress, and where the
safety of the inhabitants is left entirely
to chance. I would press the Government to see, before this Bill leaves this
House, if they could not insert a provision to deal with this matter.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I do
not propose to detain the House very
long, but I feel that I should like to say
a word or two about this Bill in general
terms, and perhaps one or two words on
a particular matter. About the merits
or demerits of the Bill, this is perhaps
the time to speak in general terms. It
is a strange thing that when the word
"gerrymandering" comes to the surface
we
are always treated to a spate of
oratory; it ripples over the shallows,
glides through the deep, and crashes in a
regular cascade over the floor of the
House, where it is broken into a million
particles, and, I am glad to say, vanishes
in an evanescent mist. Would it not be
much better for us to confine ourselves to
finding out what is good in the Bill and
supporting it, and if we can find anything
which we can amend seeking to amend
it? In general terms I think the Bill
should be welcomed. It removes many
anomalies, and goes a long way towards
bringing our Province, of which we are
proud, up to date.
I sup-
There is one particular matter I should
like to mention. The Urban Council of
Portrush have paid all the Senators the
compliment of sending them a copy of a
debate concerning a rather unfortunate
dispute which has taken place between
them and one of the Ministers.
pose they paid us that compliment in the
hope that we would read the report, and,
if we spmpathised with them, take their
part, and if we did not, do the other
thing. It would be much better if some
oil could be poured on the troubled
waters, and if a place of the importance
to our community of Portrush would help
to make this a workable Bill, rather than
showing annoyance about something
which was probably said in the heat of
the moment or was a lapsus linguae.
With Senator Glendinning I sympathise very strongly. He has evidently
been reading the report. It does seem
wrong that persons should occupy a
house for a very limited time and pay
rates as though they occupied it for the
entire year.
The burden of the complaint in Portrush seems to be that such
people come for what is known as the
season, make a considerable amount of
money, and depart with it, but that they
pay rates only for the time they are in
occupation. It may, however, be pertinent to ask, why do they depart?
Because the source of their revenue
comes to an end, which goes to prove that
they get it not from the residents of
Portrush but from the people who go
Local Government Bill
Adjournment
25 OCTOBER 1934
there when they do and leave when they
leave.
Therefore it is not unreasonable to say
that these people, to use a colloquialism,
rather than doing Portrush in are doing.
Portrush good. They go there and help
to cater for the visitors; if the visitors
were not catered for they would not go
to Portrush. I think the residents themselves would say that the hotels, boardinghouses and shops, benefit materially from
the influx of visitors. As we have been
paid the compliment of receiving the
document, I would appeal to the people
of Portrush to look at the matter from a
reasonable point of view, and to join the
rest of us in supporting a Measure which
is for the common good. I do not wish
to detain the House. If anything arises
on a particular Clause it can be dealt
with in Committee, and now I merely
say that as a whole I support the Bill.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I join with the
Mayor of Derry in expressing satisfaction
with this Measure, which I think will
prove a very useful one. I understand
from the Government that this is an experimental Measure, and that later on we
are to have another Bill. When that
Bill comes forward I should hope that it
will be by repeal and even enactment and
not by further amendment, because I think
this Bill, whatever its merits, is overwhelmed with cross-references. My hon.
and learned Friend opposite (Mr. Leslie)
has all these Measures at his fingers'
ends, and I dare say there are other
Members of the Senate who sit surrounded by the many Acts of Parliament
that are referred to in the Bill, but laymen like myself, I must say, have been
rather bewildered by it.
I hope also that when this further
Measure is introduced the question of
workhouses will be dealt with fully. We
hear from time to time of the taint that
is associated with workhouses. I
suppose I
ought to be ashamed of it, but I have not
been able to discover that taint. There is
no necessary shame in being poor. The
taint must come from being associated
with undesirable people. If that is so
surely that might be remedied by classification or segregation or in some other
way. I think it is a pity that we are
spending enormous sums of money in
maintaining these costly establishments
with expensive staffs, and are at the same
time discouraging people from taking
advantage of what they provide by saying
that there is a taint. It seems to me to
be most unreasonable. I visit workhouses
from time to time, and I must say I have
not observed any taint. The inmates
seem to be quiet respectable people, with
a friendly feeling for the master, which
was very pleasing to see.
A
The Bill, I notice, provides for the
compulsory notification of tuberculosis.
Tuberculosis is diminishing, and I think
that if this Clause-Clause 32-is really
capable of being enforced it should put
an end to this terrible scourge.
Clause that I do not like is one to which
Senator M'Laughlin referred, that is the
Clause providing that the landlord shall
pay the rates on houses of certain rating
value. We know that taxation without
representation is universally condemned,
but I think that representation without
taxation is equally objectionable. The
people who occupy these houses-they do
not pay a high rent-have a vote, and
this vote deals with the distribution of
the money raised by rates. I think it is
wrong that people should have a voice
in the voting of money and not pay any
at all. On the whole, I think this will
prove a very useful Measure, and from
the reception it has received in another
place and in this House I think it will
be of great benefit to the community
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, "That
the Debate be now adjourned."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered, That the Debate be resumed.
on Tuesday next.
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That
this House do now adjourn until Tuesday next."
Mr. McMAHON: Perhaps the Deputy-
Leader of the House would state the
order of business for next week?
Mr. LONG: On Tuesday next the
debate on the motion for the Second
Reading of the Local Government Bill
will be resumed. At the moment I cannot state the other business, but it is of
less importance.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Twenty minutes before
Five o'clock until Tuesday next.

THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 30th October, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. DEPUTY-
SPEAKER (Mr. McLaughlin) in the
Chair.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Hume's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That the promoters of
Hume's Divorce Bill, which has been
introduced into this House in the present
Session of Parliament shall have leave
to introduce the same in the next Session
of Parliament, provided that notice of
their intention to do so be lodged in the
Library not later than 3 o'clock on or
before the third day on which the House
shall sit after the next meeting of Parliament with a declaration annexed
thereto signed by the agent stating that
the Bill is the same in every respect as
the Bill at the last stage of the proceedings thereof in this House in the
present Session."-(Mr. Long).
Resolved, "That the proceedings on the
Bill shall be pro forma only in regard to
the stages through which the same has
passed in the present Session and that no
new fees be charged in regard to these
stages. (Mr. Long).
Adoption of Children (Workmen's Compensation) Bill.

pass." (Mr. Long).

of Commons to acquaint them that the

Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Bill.

Financial Provisions Bill

Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill."-(Mr. Long).
In Committee accordingly.
[Mr. McLAUGHLIN in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Bringing into account of
certain receipts in respect of adjustments
of residuary share of reserved taxes)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Power to issue additional
money for Government loans).
Question proposed: "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LONG: On the Second Reading
the hon. and learned Member, Mr. Leslie,
asked to be informed as to the reasons
for the insertion of the words giving
retrospective effect to this Clause. As I
should perhaps have explained in introducing the Bill, the purpose is to cover
the amount by which the existing powers
of the Government Loans Fund to make
loans have already been exceeded in anticipation of Parliamentary sanction. For
the reasons which I have already outlined it will be appreciated that this
sanction is of a more or less formal
character and my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Finance naturally did not
wish to hold up necessary schemes promoted by local authorities for which
money had to be advanced.
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I may be permitted to thank the Parliamentary
Secretary for looking into the question.
I did not imagine for a moment there was
anything to which anybody could take
exception, and if there were it would
seem to me to be a matter rather for
I do
those in another place than for us.
not oppose the Clause.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 3 (Fee for birth, marriage
and death certificates for purposes of regulations as to savings certificates), and 4
(Short title and construction) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
