Local Government Bill.
Order read for resuming adjourned
debate on Question (25th October):
"That the Bill be now read a Second
time."
Question again proposed.
Mr. LESLIE: For the purposes of the
Second Reading, at all events, I should
desire to claim to be reckoned as a warm
supporter of the Bill before us.
But in
saying that I cannot shut my eyes to
certain obvious facts. Rather, on the
contrary, I feel bound to say that in my
judgment this is not a Bill for an
autumn Session. I think that it is a Bill
which might very appropriately have been
considered by a Joint Committee of both
Houses. The Parliamentary programme,
as I understand it, really does not allow
of sufficient time for full justice being
done to a Bill of so very heterogeneous
and so very complicated a character.
There are,
as it seems to me, two
special objections to undue acceleration.
in dealing with a Bill of this description.
First, we have to remember that if the
Bill is hurried through those stages
which succeed the actual printing and circulation-if that happens both Houses
are deprived of the advantage of hearing
reasoned and considered public criticism
of the various Clauses of the Bill. The
second reason is perhaps more peculiar to
ourselves. As the time has been allotted,
if my information is correct, there does
not appear to me to be any time whatever left for those communications by
way of Message between one Chamber of
this Legislature and the other such
Messages as might not unreasonably be
expected to be occasioned by a miscellaneous Bill dealing with so many
different subjects.
There has not been hitherto as I said,
I think, last June, any Message of that
sort between the two Chambers, but there
is always the possibility that it might
occur, and I do think that time should
always be left to enable anything of
that sort to be properly attended to.
However, I am not going to make any
general criticism either on such public
criticism of the Bill as has reached me or
to any great extent as regards the shortage
of time. I am prepared to accept the
Bill without carping unduly at the fact
that certain recommendations of the Local
Government Commission have not been
attended to. I do not want to say anything about that. I am quite prepared
to accept the Bill as a stopgap Bill dealing with some matters which cannot wait
and which have in fact been too long
delayed. I do not regard the Bill so to
speak as a monument of creative law,
but I regard it rather as a step in the
development of the existing laws. I
accept, therefore, the explanation which
has been given by the Parliamentary
Secretary that the present time is not
suitable for any revolutionary changes in
our local government procedure, and I
also accept his statement, in which I
concur, that this Bill is essentially a
Committee Bill.
I noticed with satisfaction that he is
reported to have said when moving the
adjournment on the day on which the
Bill was read a First time that the Committee Stage would not be taken until this
day week. Even so, this House will
have approximately eight days to deal
with a Bill on the consideration of which
we could usefully have employed even
eight months, rather than eight days.
Thinking over this Bill in my own mind
I pictured it to myself as a forest and
the Clauses of it as trees. It seemed to
me at first sight that it was one of
those woods we hear of so often-a wood
which you cannot see on account of the
trees, and I confess that I almost
despaired of being able to do anything
to make the wood a useful and permanent feature of our Parliamentary landscape unless a most vigorous clearing were
applied to the forest as a whole. However, the wood, as I have called it, was
there. The problem had got to be
tackled, and so one plunged into it.
To continue the metaphor, one may
claim to have found the undergrowth
and brambles very dense indeed and extremely prickly, and to have found, or
I
Local Government Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
thought one had found, bypaths leading here and there and bringing you.
up against nothing. But as one got a little
bit further into the Bill itself one was
rather driven to the conclusion that the
difficulty one encountered was to a great
extent that difficulty which is so often
spoken of as solvitur ambulando, and the
ambulation was between the chair and
the bookshelf.
were
One found as one went on that there
in the Bill various little signposts to help you on your way. If the
House will be so kind as to look at Subclause 2, of Clause 4, and Subclause 13, of Clause 30, I think
they will be able to follow what I am
driving at, because they will find
in those two sub-clauses practically all
that they need know of the previous
history of the particular matters to which
these sub-clauses relate. One thinks as
one goes on that perhaps the wood does
not require so much pruning as at first
sight seemed possible. I came to the
conclusion ultimately that really only one
tree ought to disappear, and that another
could be dealt with, as I think, by pruning, possibly by transplantation. Many
other Clauses, with the exception of that
one I have in my mind, could be dealt
with in the ordinary manner by the
arboriculturist who concerns himself with
trees of that description.
But there were two facts borne on me
very strongly indeed, and they were that
that heavy undergrowth and the thick
brambles could never efficiently be cleared
away except by two Bills, which I hope
we shall have in the not too distant
future-one to consolidate our Public
Health Acts, and the other, perhaps, in
two parts, to deal with the administrative provisions of local government.
However, when one gets so to speak out
of this wood I think one cannot but admit
that there are two rather tall trees
topping and overshadowing the others.
These two trees for this purpose I should
like to describe as simplification and development, because these two words, I
think are the keynotes, in so far as it is
possible to have keynotes at all, of the Bill
before us. With regard to this question
of simplification, the first example, I think
if the House looks at it, is Clause 7. I am
sure the Lord Mayor of Belfast will agree
with me about Clause 7. It will relieve
him of the perambulations which I
gather the original statute required him
to take on foot round the various city
boundaries. Clause 16 really sets out in
full the new Clause that is to be substituted for a Section which gave, I
gathered, some trouble and is no good to
anybody.
I would also take a third example,
although I know that this example will
not, perhaps, meet with universal
approval. I regret that and I will return
to the matter again. The example is
Clause 24. I regard that Clause as a
very outstanding example of simplification in certain matters. Passing from
that to the question of development or
progress, I think that, perhaps, the best
examples I could give the House are to
be found in Clauses 32 to 34. These are
public health Clauses, to be found in Part
III of the Bill. I would also couple with
those Clauses the whole of Part IV, dealing with poor relief.
And now I have done with my wood,
with the possible exception of one or two
seedlings which, perhaps, can be dealt
with at a later stage. I will ask my
fellow foresters and others concerned with
the Bill to accompany me through certain
of the Clauses. I want to do that not
because I want to dwell unduly on the
details, but because I think it would be
a convenience if some indication were to
be given at as early a date as possible of
the main points to which one proposes to
direct special attention at the appropriate time.. There are, as the House is
aware, 47 Clauses in the Bill, and the
first 43 of those are practically Bills in
themselves. One does not want, on the
Committee stage, to make 43 Second
Reading speeches, and happily it will not
be necessary for anybody to put themselves to that exertion or put the House
to the necessity, perhaps, of stretching
its endurance. The first Clause on which
I want to say anything is a rather curious
one--it is Clause 14. This Clause requires,
amongst other things, that a minute shall
be made by the local authority and
forwarded to the Ministry
the Ministry of Home.
Affairs, explaining the reasons for the
non-acceptance of the lowest tender.
When this Bill was under discussion
in another place an hon. Member there,
according to the reports, pointed out
that the law of libel seemed to him to
be involved. I regret to notice that he
Local Government Bill-
30 OCTOBER 1934
Second Reading
did not get an answer to that, and as the
point is one which might present itself to
the minds of some of our rural district
councillors and possibly urban district
councillors, also the procedure being new,
I venture to think it is a point on which
an authoritative statement should be made
in this House. Therefore, for the Committee stage I propose to put something
on the Paper in order to give the Parliamentary Secretary an opportunity of
giving a considered opinion on the
subject. I will do that rather for the
guidance of those local authorities into
whose hands this Bill will go when it is
enacted than for any other reason.
Passing from that to Clause 17, this is
a Clause which, as we know, aroused a
good deal of criticism, mainly from a seaside town in the north-west corner of my
own county. The Urban District Council
of Portrush appear to object very
strongly indeed to certain provisions in
this Clause, and I must say that to some
extent I have some secret sympathy with
one of their contentions. The point is
made, and quite temperately made, in
a circular issued by the clerk of the urban
district council, or rather in a reprint
of something he said which was reported
in the papers.
That circular was issued
sometime, if I may put it that way, after
the controversy had become acute. One
hon. Member has said that this circular
expressed itself in temperate language.
The principal thing that seemed to be
objected to is the requirement that the
rates should be collected in one lump sum.
That is one objection, and the other
objection is what was called the
temporary occupier of a lock-up shop who
runs away after two or three months of
harvesting in the town and is not liable
for the remaining nine or ten months
during which his shop is empty. When
I say empty I should explain I mean unoccupied in the strict sense, because the
House knows that there is a good deal of
difference between "residence " and
"occupation" when it comes to a question
of rates.
As regards that latter question, the
services of a town have to be continued
for the whole twelve months whether Mr.
Temporary Lock-up Occupier is there or
whether he is away, and that does give
the local authorities some sort of a
grievance. On the other hand, the
temporary occupier can very well say, "I
have to supply your visitors with such
things as I have to sell; I had your
services for a couple or three months, and
do not see that I should pay for any
other portion." I do not know if anything could be done to bring these two
lines of thought into more agreement, but
I am afraid nothing concrete can be
brought before us. I feel bound to say
that taking Northern Ireland as a whole
and as it is, you really cannot have one
system for Portrush and another for the
rest of the area. I am afraid that is so.
There may be hardships and injustices,
but hardships and injustices are not
necessarily synonymous terms.
There is another point arising on this
Clause apart from one or two minor
points to which I need not refer to-day.
There is, it seems to me, one point of
some little difficulty. If this Bill goes
through this House in exactly the form
in which it comes before us to-day this
particular Clause, amongst others, will
come into operation on the day the Bill
receives the Royal Assent. Now, if that
happens, it seems to me there is a
distinct risk that the budgets of some of
the local authorities may be to some
extent upset. It is true that Subclause (3) is adoptive and not mandatory,
and nothing need be feared in that
direction, but the earlier part of the
Clause would come into operation at once
and the local authorities would, therefore, run a risk of having their current
budgets disturbed. I am afraid I must
say disturbed for the second time, within
the year. The proper course would seem
to be to add a sub-clause to this Clause
providing that this Clause should come
into operation and should have effect from
1st April next, that being the first day
of the local financial year.
Passing from Clause 17, I do not make
a very long jump to Clause 18, which
seems on the face of it to be a useful
Clause. This is the Clause giving the
Ministry of Home Affairs power to make
regulations prescribing the qualifications
of the several officers appointed by the
council of such county, and so on, or by
the committee of such county, and so on.
The Clause at first seems to be perfectly
in order, but I am not satisfied that the
position of the education authorities is
sufficiently safeguarded. It seems to me
that amongst other things a schoolmaster
or schoolmistress appointed by an educa-
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
tion authority, be it by a committee or
council or whoever may be acting under
the Act, is an officer of the county. The
expression "officer" after all must be construed in connection with the office. We
have got a definition of the word "office"
in the Local Government Act, 1898, which
certainly covers a schoolmaster, and I am
afraid that something in the way of
clarification is required here. I do not
think the powers of the Ministry of Home
Affairs to make regulations respecting
the matters dealt with in this Clause
should extend to making regulations respecting the qualifications of schoolmasters.
Now we come to Clause 21, and this
Clause, if I may say so, gave me more
trouble than all the other Clauses in the
Bill put together. This is the Clause
which I compared to the very troublesome
tree which at first I thought might have
to go altogether, and which I finally came
to the conclusion might be dealt with by
pruning or, perhaps, by transplanting. It
is really only to Sub-clause (1) of Clause
21 that my remarks apply. When this
Clause first made its appearance in
another place the third part of the subclause, beginning on line 9 of page 20,
was not there. In fact, there was no suggestion that there should be given compulsory power of acquisition of drains,
buildings, works, materials, and so forth.
I do not know what county council it was
that made the particular push first for
the inclusion of the power in the Bill, but
I have a very strong impression that it
may have been the County Council of
Down. However, I do not see any
Senator representing Down with us
to-day, and so I will not attack it on that
ground.
This is really a most extraordinary proposal so far as one can gather from the
Bill. Hitherto the county councils have
had no power to acquire drains, and so
on, for the discharge of surface water
from their roads. They have been using
other people's drains for discharging surface water to my certain knowledge for
years and years, and apparently they
have done so by availing themselves of
the exercise of a voluntary courtesy. If
you take out the words giving compulsory
powers which were put in in another
place, rather at the last moment and
towards the end of what must have been
a somewhat exacting evening, you will
leave the county councils with power they
had not previously and which most of
them seem to have done very well without, and there will be no great difficulty,
but if you leave it as it is with compulsory
power, without any appeal beyond the
Ministry of Home Affairs, you are going
to land yourselves, it seems to me, in very
serious questions.
The object of the proposal is, as will
be seen, the draining of surface water,
and in order to do that it is proposed that
there shall be power to take not only a
drain, whatever that may be, and the
right of user, and so on, but also "any
buildings, works, materials, or things
belonging thereto." I do not know for
this purpose what a drain is, but I do
know that a drain has been held to
include what was practically a canal, and
when you couple that with works and
buildings one wants to know whether it is
intended solely for the draining of surface
water. I do not know how far you are
to follow surface water. Is it proposed
to take power to acquire a mill race and
the mill, and so on? I do not think that
could be intended for a moment.
I think that part of the Clause should
be shorn, if necessary, of the compulsory
feature. If it is still thought necessary
that in the background compulsion should
be applied I think we might transplant
that tree and bring it into the next subclause, which is simply a simplification
of the existing law. I think the powers
sought to be taken in Clause 21 are far
in excess of what are really necessary,
and I do not think it is desirable that
they should be given except, of course,
by agreement. That will, of course, be
developed more at a later stage.
I come now to Clause 24, the Clause I
spoke of as being, to my mind, an
example of simplification. This is the
Clause that was to some extent attacked
by an earlier speaker. To me it seems
that this Clause, in the form in which it
comes before us, is again an instance of
the development of the existing law, and
that existing law seems to me to be
based, not on our own Public Health
Acts, but to go right back to the English
Act of 1888, on which our own Act of
ten years later was so very extensively
modelled. The only part that seems to
me to be at all new is the power to alter
the total number of councillors, and so
521 Local Government Bill-

30 OCTOBER 1934
Second Reading
on, a power which seems to me to be
absolutely necessary if you give power to
alter the boundary. That seems to
follow, but suggestions were made that
the Ministry of Home Affairs might use
such power as is given them for an unworthy purpose - I I think the word
"gerrymander" was used and rather
objected to by the hon. Senator, the
Mayor of Derry.
At all events, it seems to me, on thinking things over, that if there were any
fear whatever that this power in the
hands of the Ministry could be improperly used the remedy seems to be
to introduce into the Clause some short
protection, some sub-clause, such as we
had, for instance, in the Fisheries Bill
the other day, throwing the responsibility for alteration directly on Parliament, say providing, if you like, that
the order should not come into effect until
confirmed by Parliament. Make Parliament responsible, not the Ministry.
Whether a proposal to insert words of
that sort in the Bill would meet with the
Government's approval or not I do not
know, but I am inclined to think if one
were put forward I, myself, would be
willing to support it, if only because I
think it would make no difference to the
equity with which the Clause would be
administered. There is something to be
said for the fact that if there is to be
no alteration without the consent of
Parliament that can be done in rough
and ready manner by Resolution in
preference to proceeding by a a Bill,
which has to be carried through so many
stages.
I
Now I come to Clause 30, a very
lengthy Clause of about three pages.
am not at all sure that the House
would regret the disappearance of that
Clause. I do not like the Clause very
much, and I think it ought to go. That
is the Clause to which I referred as the
one tree which required felling. I
rather expect the woodman who will wield
the axe will be the hon. Senator, Mr.
McAllister, who is not in his place to-day,
because, as hon. Senators are aware, that
Senator is a member of the Council of the
Incorporated Law Society of Northern
Ireland, and the Incorporated Law
Society has addressed all of us to the
effect that they would prefer that the
existing arrangements should be maintained. They give reasons for their pro-
The
posal. There are,
are, of course, various
things that might be said about the
Clause, but I think they really refer
almost entirely to the next stage.
English Clause-I call it the English
Clause but it is a section of the Imperial
Act of 1890 which is referred to in the
Bill had, as hon. Members may remember, at its very head an introduction running, if I remember rightly, something
like this:
"For the purposes of regulating
places ordinarily used for public
dancing, music," and so forth.
We have not that in here, and it seems
to me that this Clause has been made
strong enough to catch every ex-Service
men's hut or Orange hall in the country.
I do not think that could be the intention. That is one of my objections to the
Clause.
I go from Clause 30 to Clause 32, the
Clause to which I referred as an instance
of the development to which the Bill
lends itself. I regard this Clause as a
distinct recognition of the fact that
Northern Ireland is too small an area to
allow the efforts generally made to combat
a common evil such as tuberculosis to be
held up by something in the nature of
obstinacy on the part of some particular
community therein. I welcome the first
sub-clause very heartily indeed. Now,
as regards the second sub-clause, it is
obvious that nothing of that sort could in
these days be left in, but I should have
liked myself that some, may I say local,
substitution should be made for the
president of the Royal College of
Physicians in Ireland and the president
of the Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland.
I do not know whether the hon. and gallant
Senator (Colonel Sinclair) who represents
the University and who addressed us last
week on this subject takes my view of the
matter, and if so, whether he would be
able and willing to frame some Amendment which would bring that in. I think
the public generally would prefer that.
The duties of the Home Office under
this Clause are principally prescribing
various things, such as the forms and
stages of tuberculosis which are to
be affected, and are based on the fact
that those forms and stages must be
such as are dangerous owing to their
infective capacity. I think the public
generally would like some outside
doctor of eminence, and perhaps we could
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
find one, associated with the Ministry of
Home Affairs in connection with that
matter.
Now we come to Clause 33, a very
admirable Clause indeed, relating to the
substitution, so far as we can manage it,
of the district hospital for the oldfashioned infirmary. There is very little.
in that Clause to awaken comment, certainly nothing to cause criticism of any
violence, but I am inclined to think, and
I am going to suggest later on, that in
the committees for administrative control,
which are to be set up under the Bill,
Parliament should provide for the inclusion of a certain number of women.
I think we should definitely provide for
that and not leave it, as it apparently is
in the Bill, optional with the boards of
guardians who appoint the committees.
We all know that women are of great
use on boards of guardians and we ought
to see that they have representation here.
I
The next Clause of which I want to
speak for a second or two is Clause 42,
which has aroused some comment. That
is the unpleasant Clause which permits a
local body to remove and to get rid of
a member who persistently obstructs.
am afraid, partly from experience and
partly from what I have seen in the
papers, that that power must be given,
but I am not at all sure that, in spite of
the safeguards set out in the Clause, we
ought not
not to provide for a definite
majority before expulsion is decided on,
that is to say that, if the question goes
to a vote, the majority to be effective
must consist of not less than such proportion as may be thought proper.
might say, perhaps, that three-fifths of
those present and voting must vote for
the expulsion of a member before it is
effective.
One
The following Clause-Clause 43deals with difficulties which may arise
when a local authority does not treat the
Ministry of Finance, that is the Treasury,
with the consideration which is its due.
I do not think there is anything new
in the Clause. There are references to
certain earlier enactments, but I do not
think there is anything new. But when
we come to the question of the abatement of grants being carried out in
accordance with regulations I should be
inclined to suggest that those regulations
should be laid before each House of Parliament. I do not say any more than
that, I think they ought to be laid before
Parliament, and I leave it at that.
And now I think I have finished with
the Clauses, except for one little matter
in connection with Clause 44, and I may
say that I approach it with the greatest
trepidation. I do not think I should
dare to suggest what I am now going to
suggest had it not been for certain proceedings in another place a few days ago.
Judging from the newspaper reports,
which are all we can go on, there would
seem to have been some confusion of
thought on the point as to whether the
expression "county council" did or did
not include the council of a county
borough. We had the question raised
ourselves not very long ago in connection
with an earlier Bill, but in it there was
nothing like so much to guide us. We
know, of course, that the expression
"county council" does include the council
of a county borough, but I am very much
inclined to say that, for the sake of the
people outside who do not know, it might
almost be worth while to proclaim it from
the housetops so to speak. It is altogether inartistic and not really necessary,
but I am not sure that it would not be
worth while. Thinking of that, I was
surprised to notice that it was done in
an English Act of not very distant date.
Now I have finished with what is in
the Bill, and there remain only a very
few remarks on the subject of things left
out. There are three points which I
should have expected or which I should
have liked rather, to have seen dealt
with in the Bill. First of all, there is
the Local Government (Emergency
Powers) Act. That has been on the
Statute Book since 1921, carried on year
by year, and the House gave it a further
lease of life the other day. I really do
think that we might have taken this
opportunity of bringing it more up-todate and more in accordance, so to speak,
with the more civilised conditions of the
present day.
There has also been put before me
something in the nature of a complaint
with regard to the practice in relation.
to the surcharging of the people, rather
than the officers, concerned in local government. The complaint is made and
instanced by something that happened in
connection with a regional education committee. The members of that committtee
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
30 OCTOBER 1934
had signed cheques which ought to have
been signed by the county council, and
so on. They were surcharged in really
quite a large sum of money, but there
was nothing to be ashamed of.
The surcharge, of course, was remitted, but the
council concerned felt that the whole
question ought to be re-examnied and
that something ought to be done to prevent an ordinary practice being regarded
as something in the nature of a misdemeanour. I have not really had time to
go into the matter, but if I can, and if
there is anything in it, I will try to
bring something before the House for the
Report stage. I cannot do it before the
Committee stage, which I understand is
to be next week.
There remains the question of fire
escapes, to which my hon. and gallant
Friend (Colonel Pakenham) who is not
with us this afternoon, has more than
once directed our attention. I hope he
will be with us again before long, but
he has asked me to keep an eye on the
matter for him during his absence. It
might be possible to connect that proposal with some kindred subject, and if
I can do that I will bring it up on, the
Report stage.
Now I am finished. I have made
some considerable demand
on the
attention of the House, and I am much
obliged for the courteous and quiet hearing which I have been afforded. I cannot sit down without saying that this is
a very difficult Bill, and the more you
study it the more are you convinced that
it has been very carefully drawn. Every
assistance that circumstances rendered it
possible to give has been given. Those
of us who think it necessary to try to
understand what we are going to deal
with cannot be saved many hours of
reference and toil-that is impossiblebut I do say that every assistance has
been afforded to us, and the completed
work, if I may put it that way, redounds
very much to the credit, ability, and
industry of those responsible for its production.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
rise principally to support the Bill, but
particularly do I wish to express my
appreciation of those responsible, namely,
the Ministry, for the drafting of it. I
know they have given every consideration
to the suggestions made to them by the
local authorities. They have been glad
to receive them, and in many cases their
suggestions have been put into practice
in the various Clauses of the Bill. The
only criticism I have heard against the
Bill is that it does not go far enough.
With that, perhaps, in a degree I might
agree, but I want to say that the Bill
does go a very great length to bring upto-date legislation which was out-of-date,
and it does that without upsetting our
local government system at all. The
Bill, in my opinion, will result in the
smoother and better working of all local
authorities.
The hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) who has just sat down referred in
passing to Clause 7. I am only mentioning this to show the degree to which some
of our Acts are antiquated. I question
if there is an individual in this Assembly
aware, and certainly there are very few
outside, that it is the duty of the Lord
Mayor and the Town Clerk to go round
and examine the boundaries once a year.
Fancy going to the slopes of the Cave
Hill and then to Stormont to examine the
boundaries. The thing is absurd and
out-of-date. That has been done away
with by this Clause 7.
If it were because of nothing else than
the postponement of the elections I say
the Bill is deserving of our support. Only
those of us who have had to fight an
election, the agents and workers who
have to go out in what is perhaps the
worst patch of weather during the year,
the first fortnight in January and the
last week in December, who have had to
stand in a draughty polling booth on a
cold, snowy, or rainy day trying to get
the voters in, can realise the benefit of
this Clause. A lot of old people are
afraid to go out in such weather, and in
that way it has been impossible to get a
true result of an election.
I am sure
that the change will be appreciated not
only by those who are seeking civic
honours but by the general public who
have to go out to vote.
I only want to make one or two observations with reference to the Clauses of
the Bill. While I appreciate very much.
what has been said by the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie), to my
astonishment he passed over one of the
most important Clauses in the Bill. I
do not know why. Perhaps he has not
seen it in the light in which I see it. I
Local Government Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Sir C. McCullagh]
refer to Clause 3. Clause 3, Paragraph
(c) of Sub-clause (1), provides that
"the names of the members or commissioners present at a meeting as well
as of those voting on each question
shall be recorded, so as to show whether
each vote given was for or against the
question.'
""
That applies to every local authority.
They are all interested as well as the
Belfast Corporation, but I will tell the
House how it will work out as far as
we are concerned. Personally I know
that the Clause is meant to do away with
what is known as the ballot vote. I do
not know whether it is adopted very
much in country districts or not, but I
do know that it has been a very useful
means of getting a vote at the Belfast
Corporation. Sometimes it is for
you and
sometimes it is against you, but on the
whole I think you will get a more honest
vote by a ballot than in any other way.
This Clause is directly aimed at doing
away with that. Take my own case at
the first of the month when we have at
least forty or fifty votes. I am advised
legally that, whether it is unanimous or
not, a vote is a vote. On every occasion
I will have to ask that a poll be taken,
whether the vote is unanimous or not.
It is absurd. You do not do it here.
When the Speaker or Deputy-Speaker
gets up and puts the Question, if we are
agreed the House passes on.
It is only
when it is asked for that a Division is
given.
In this connection I ask the assistance
of the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie), who never misses a mark. He is
a stickler for having everything right.
This Clause must have escaped his
memory, and perhaps he will assist me
to devise something that will meet the
case. My hon. Friend who sits on my
right (Mr. Long) said that he hoped this
would be an agreed Measure. I ask him,
before the Committee stage is reached, to
prepare some words that will meet the
point I have raised. It is not a case of
going against the Government; it is a
case of making clear what the Government want. The Government do not
intend that a vote will have to be taken
on every question-that would be absurd
when the local authority is unanimousand I hope a form of words will be
inserted providing that when a local
body is unanimous a vote will not have to
be taken.
The next observation I have to make is
in reference to the criticism that this Bill
has been rushed. That criticism has
been expressed in Portrush. I am sorry
that the Portrush controversy has been
mentioned. Like others, I got a
pamphlet on the subject. No doubt
strong words were used in the other
House, and perhaps stronger words in
Portrush, but that sort of thing cuts no
ice and gets you nowhere. If Portrush
has a grievance it relates to rating. I do
not agree with what they want. Lest I
should be misunderstood, let me say that
I know and appreciate what the Portrush
authorities are doing for the good of the
community. Portrush is one of the
finest watering-places in the North of
Ireland. The Council are doing a noble
work there, and I should like to see them
drop this controversy and settling down
to work for the common good. I am sure
they will.
The hon. and learned Senator referred
to some traders who go there every year.
They are really birds of passage. They
come with the visitors and go away with
the visitors. They must fill a want, and
their business must pay, or they would
not carry it on. They may not have to
pay rent for the whole of the year I do
not know, but I expect they do notand the Portrush authorities want to levy
rates for the time that the shops are
vacant. Any local authority may go in
for a local Act and may get something in
it of interest to themselves; but this is a
Bill for Northern Ireland, and if you
adopt the principle that you are going
to levy rates on vacant houses and shops
what is going to happen? The thing is
impossible; you cannot do it. You will
create great hardships. When you come
to analyse the whole thing you will find
that it is not worth talking about. All
that it would mean so far as Portrush is
concerned would be infinitesimal. I
think they have no grievance in that connection, and I hope there will be less said
about Portrush during the remaining
stages of this Bill.
Clause 13 proposes to abolish fines
when members of local bodies resign. I
understand that that Clause was
originally provided with an object. I am
not going to oppose it, but this is how
it will work out. Those of us in public
30 OCTOBER 1934
Local Government Bill-

Second Reading
that as an illustration. In this connection I think the Government are to be
congratulated, and I do so most heartily.
life know that sometimes in a committee
you meet a member who becomes annoyed
because he does not get what he wants.
He feels that he has a grievance, and he
says, “I will have nothing more to do
with you." He writes out his resignation then and there and flings it across
the table to the town clerk. The town
clerk says,
"I want £5." The member
says, "I will give it to you later on."
But he never brings the £5. His temper
has cooled off the next morning, and he
does not persist in his resignation. That
is how it works out. Under this Bill, if
his resignation is flung across the table
the town clerk will have to accept it, and
the member has finished. You had better
know where you are in this matter.
Another Clause that has been referred
to two or three times in this debate is
Clause 42, which makes provision for the
removal of an obstructionist. Candidly,
I hope that I shall never have to order
the removal of a member for obstruction,
but on the whole, I think it is a good
Clause. When a member knows that the
chair has the power to remove him I
think he will stop obstructing. I think
this is a good Clause, and I should be
sorry to see it omitted. Objection has
been taken to the power of co-option, and
it is quite true that a representative of
the political party that loses a member
may not be co-opted; but that is the
fault of the electors for sending that type
of man forward. The electors are responsible. This matter will right itself
in time. I believe this is a power that
will never be exercised, certainly not in
the city of Belfast.
I am putting down an Amendment to
Clause 37, which deals with borrowing for
relief of distress. I appreciate what the
Government have done, and I want to
express my thanks publicly for their
action. The Government are taking
power under this Bill to make things
right. I need not dwell
the subject
of my Amendment at this stage. It will
be a moderate Amendment, and I hope
the Leader of the House will be able to
accept it on behalf of the Government.
upon
That
I also wish to thank the Government
for the Clause enabling borough councils
to acquire lands in certain cases.
is important so far as the Belfast Corporation is concerned. It will give them
power to complete the arrangements for
the purchase of Belfast Castle. I am giving
The hon. and learned Senator referred
to Clause 30, which relates to the licensing of places used for public entertainments. I think he is quite right. So far
as Belfast is concerned I think the existing arrangement whereby the Recorder
issues licences is much better and simpler
and, in my opinion, works more satisfactorily than if the local authorities had
to undertake the duty. However, that is
a proposal I would not go so far as to
quarrel with.
The Bill might have gone further, but
I recognise that the codification of the
laws relating to local government and
public health would take twelve months
or even longer-it would be a tremendous
job-and to wait for that would involve
holding up one of the most useful
Measures the Government have ever
introduced. Therefore, while I think it
would have been better to have gone
further, I am delighted with the Bill on
the whole, and again I wish to express
my thanks and congratulations to the
Government for bringing in such a comprehensive Measure.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I do not desire to say
much about this Bill. In my opinion it
is a Measure that was long overdue, and
though it may not go as far as some of
us would like, yet it is a Bill which we
all welcome. Reform in local government
and health administration was badly
wanted, and we may take it that this is
only a forward step. Others will follow
in due course. Senator McLaughlin has
suggested that the Measure has been
rushed and that local authorities have not
had time to consider it. I should have
thought that the very reverse was the
We have had the Report of the
Local Government Commission before us
for years, and what is contained in this
Measure is merely carrying out some of
the suggestions made in that Report.
case.
I am sorry that Senator McLaughlin
and Senator McMahon have introduced
the eternal religious question.
Mr. McMAHON: I never mentioned
religion.
Mr. QUIGLEY: They seem to thrive
on it. They have spoken of gerrymandering. I admit that in the past there was
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
gerrymandering, but it was done by the
Nationalist Party, to which the hon.
Senators belong.
Mr. McMAHON: We have never had
the power to gerrymander anything.
Mr. QUIGLEY: They showed themselves to be adepts at that sort of thing.
Objection is taken to Sub-clause 1 of
Clause 24, which gives county councils
power to take certain steps in connection
with the alteration of boundaries.
May
I point out that in substance this subclause is identical with Article 26 of the
Schedule to the Local Government Order,
1898. That article has been in operation
since the passing of the Local Government Act of 1898, so that the criticism
of the hon. Senators is entirely misdirected. They are simply beating the
air to say that this sub-clause embodies
any new principle and introduces any
new method of gerrymandering areas. It
does nothing of the kind. The Clause, as
it stands, simply reproduces something
that has been in operation, without
protest, since 1898.
Mr. McMAHON: Not without protest.
Mr. QUIGLEY: It is distasteful for
me to have to refer to gerrymandering or
the religious question-
Mr. McMAHON: Hear, hear.
Mr. QUIGLEY -but since it has
been raised I would ask what about the
gerrymandering carried out in Belfast
years ago to please the Nationalist Party.
We all know how electoral divisions were
carved out in the interests of Roman
Catholics.
Mr. McMAHON: In the interests of
justice.
on
Mr. QUIGLEY: Shortly after 1898
gerrymandering was carried out a
large scale throughout Ulster by the very
party to which Senator McLaughlin and
Senator McMahon belong. They perpetrated such glaring inequalities in
Tyrone and Fermanagh that it was the
merest justice on the part of this Parliament to pass legislation which would put
these inequalities right.
Let me give one or two instances. In
County Tyrone the divisions of Curryglass, Derrabar, Draughton and Taltmoyle had 324, 325, 534 and 565 of a
population respectively. Each of these
divisions sent a Nationalist to the Council
in Tyrone, while Fintona, which was a
Unionist division with a population of
1,611, nearly equal to the other four combined, had only one representative on the
Council.
Mr. McMAHON: Was it not the
British Government, to whom you owe
allegiance, who did that?
a
Mr. QUIGLEY: Take County Derry.
Castledawson, with Unionist population of about two thousand, had one
representative, while Sixtowns, with a
Nationalist population of six hundred,
had one representative.
commissioners.
The
After the passing of the 1898 Act the
Nationalists had control and could get
The
what they liked in this country.
County of Armagh had for long years
Unionist town
Unionists were in the majority in the
city of Armagh. After the passing of
the Act of 1898 Armagh was divided into
three wards, and it was divided in
such a way that the Unionists were
all put into one ward. There was
one ward in which the Nationalists
managed to get a majority of about
twenty. They controlled the district council, and that council built
over eighty artisans' dwellings, and not a
single one was ever given to a Protestant. That was the kind of fair play
meted out by our political opponents.
Let us take Fermanagh and compare
the rates paid by the Unionists and
Nationalists. The Unionists, including
companies, own property with a gross
valuation in the rural and urban districts of about £160,000, while the
Nationalists occupy property of a gross
valuation of only £83,300. Take rates.
The total amount of rates paid yearly by
the Unionists comes to about £47,000,
while the Nationalists pay £24,000.
Thus it will be seen that the Unionists
in County Fermanagh own 75 per cent.
of the land and buildings, and pay 75
per cent. of the taxes. Take representaof
tion. In the old Enniskillen rural district five Nationalist electoral divisions
with 474 voters and a valuation
£6,912 returned five representatives to
the Council, while five Unionist electoral
Local Government Bill-
30 OCTOBER 1934
Second Reading
divisions with 2,058 voters and a valuation of £25,747 returned only the same
number. Talk about gerrymandering!
The Nationalists, as I say, are adepts at
the game.
Of course, the whole object of this
attack on the part of Nationalist
Senators is to bring in the religious.
question. Every fair-minded man knows
that there is no boycotting of Roman
Catholics by this Government-none
whatever. Appointments are made on
merit, without reference to religion. Do
the hon. Senators forget that the late
Lord Chief Justice in Northern Ireland
was a Roman Catholic?
Mr. McMAHON: He was a Unionist.
Mr. QUIGLEY: The head of the
Ministry of Education is a Roman
Catholic, and I believe that the Chief
Establishment Officer, who holds the
rank of Assistant Secretary, is a Roman
Catholic. Our political opponents never
mention these facts because, I suppose,
they do not regard a Roman Catholic
who is a loyalist as a good Roman
Catholic. I suppose he is a renegade in
their eyes. The only Roman Catholics
that they hold in favour, apparently, are
those who are opposed to the Government of Ulster.
If the hon. Senators who complain so
much want to see boycotting let them go
to the Free State, where they will find
it brought to a fine art.
Mr.
MCMAHON : The Protestant
Ministers do not say that.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Little wonder that
the Protestant population is rapidly
dwindling in Southern Ireland. Is the
Roman Catholic population decreasing in
Northern Ireland? Senator McLaughlin
boasts that despite all the boycotting,
gerrymandering and victimisation, they
are progressing and increasing. Surely
that is the best answer to his allegations.
If the Roman Catholics are receiving unfair and harsh treatment in Ulster why
do they flock into Northern Ireland and
stay here?
Mr. McMAHON: Because they were
born here.
Mr. QUIGLEY: The charges which
have been so recklessly made are entirely
mythical. They are so much windowdressing and propaganda. They were all
trotted out recently in another place, and
now we have them re-dished with frills in
the Senate. Is it not time that that
kind of thing was stopped. I hope we
shall hear no more of these manufactured
complaints In Northern Ireland the
people of every class and creed receive
evenhanded justice. They are all treated
alike. They are dealt with generously,
and have a freedom and liberty that does
not exist elsewhere. That is why Ulster
is attractive to Roman Catholics as well
as Protestants.
If you
want to see
religious persecution in full swing go to
a Roman Catholic country like Spain, or
to Germany where Hitler, a
a Roman
Catholic, is at the head of affairs.
Ulster there is no persecution of any
kind.
In
I have been told a tale of a hen which
I have never yet been able to verify. It
is said that if a thick white circle is
drawn on a farmyard floor and a hen can
be induced to follow completely round
that circle, the poor bird will continue
walking the line unceasingly, until it
falls dead from exhaustion. Many
human beings resemble that hen. They
have walked round and round the circle
so often that they have become hypnotised and cannot break away. They are
the slaves of habit, and so I might say
of the hon. Senators here who are always
blaming the Government and the
Unionist party. They cannot get away
from the age-long habit of manufacturing
grievances and making allegations which
have no foundation. I have pleasure in
supporting the Second Reading of this
Bill.
Mr. McHUGH: After listening to the
remarkable speech we have just heard
from Senator Quigley I could not help
wondering where he got all his information. Looking at him one would think
he is not so old, but he appears to be able
to fly back to days beyond his date of
birth. He goes back to 1888, and he
quotes and requotes events that took place
in those days. I wonder whether it was
the gentleman in the corner, or the Government that supplied him with all this information. He could not have got it
from any documents here, unless he got
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McHugh]
it from the Home Office in Belfast, and
I charge the Government with supplying
him with that information.
Mr. QUIGLEY: That is not so.
false.
It is
Mr. McHUGH: You could not have
got that information otherwise. I know
something about local .government
matters. I spent twenty-two years as a
member of the Fermanagh County
Council, and for eleven years I was chairman of that body and I could not have
got as much information about County
Fermanagh as the hon. Senator has
given in the course of his speech. He
has given some most remarkable quotations. He said we attempted to gerrymander the Fermanagh County Council.
That is utterly false and untrue. No
attempt was ever made by the party I
represent to gerrymander that council,
but the first attempt that was ever made
to turn that Council into a Unionist
Council was turned down by the Local
Government Board after a public inquiry
at which the present Lord Chief Justice
represented the Unionist party. I was
present at that inquiry. The hon. Senator talks about gerrymandering!
The
case of County Tyrone has often been
quoted by those who believe they have
built up a case against us, and it was
quoted again to-day by Senator Quigley.
If you take the population of County
Tyrone you will find there are 74,000
Roman Catholics, and 59,000 Unionists.
Those 74,000 Roman Catholics have nine
councillors to represent them on the
County Council, and the 59,000 Protestants, Quakers and other denominations have no less than fifteen members
to represent them on that Council. Yet
Senator Quigley says that is a fair representation. He can see nothing wrong
with that state of affairs, and at the same
time he charges us with trying to abrogate the rights of the Unionist party. I
challenge him to prove any individual
case of gerrymandering against us.
To me there is nothing so painful as
to have to listen to the continual raising
of this religious question, but on this
occasion it was first broached by the
Mayor of Derry, who expressed the belief that Senator McLaughlin was not
justified in dragging that terrible monster known as gerrymandering across the
floor of this House. I would remind the
Mayor of Derry that he never would have
sat in this House at all had it not been
for the gerrymandering which took place
in the city of
The
of Londonderry.
majority in the City of Derry were
crushed by the Government, and the
result is that the Mayor of Derry is
here to-day representing not the majority
but the minority of the people of Derry.
If he thinks an honour has been conferred upon him by being sent here to
represent a minority he is welcome to it.
I have not the slightest objection to the
Mayor of Derry personally, because I
have always found him a very courteous
gentleman.
You must bear in mind that from the
very commencement the whole policy of
the Government has been to impress upon
the people I represent the fact that we
will have no voice in the government of
this country. We are, so to speak,
white slaves, and the Government are
determined to keep us in that position.
We find the Prime Minister reminding
us from time to time that this is a
Protestant Parliament. You would
think that a Prime Minister, who is
supposed to represent people of all
political opinions and all denominations.
and who is in charge of the entire people
of the Six Counties, would pause before
making such an outrageous statement as
that. He made that declaration with
the one sole purpose of reminding us that
we shall never have a voice in this Parliament, and that any suggestions or proposals we may bring forward will be
ignored.
I was surprised to hear the Lord Mayor
of Belfast making suggestions to his
Friend. He was quite sure he would
accept these Amendments. Well, if I
were to mention the word "friend" I am
sure all the Members of this House would
be shocked that I ventured to term the
Deputy-Leader of the House a friend in
the same sense as the Lord Mayor did.
That shows how the line is drawn and the
distinction between the two parties in this
House. I have been associated with this
Parliament, in this House and the other,
for seven or eight years and, during all
that time, especially in the Lower House,
I never knew the Government to accept
one Amendment put forward by the
Opposition.
Local Government Bill-
30 OCTOBER 1934
Second Reading
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: They
had a Bill put forward and accepted.
Mr. McHUGH: Yes; about birds. I
am just reminded that the Government
condescended to accept from Senator
Campbell a Bill. Apparently he is a
faddist about birds, and spent some time
in drafting a Bill and the Government
condescended to allow it to pass. It was
about birds and birds only. There was
nothing of gerrymandering about it, or
I am quite sure it would not have passed,
especially with Senator Cunningham in
this House.
There is no use in talking about this
Clause 24. It is here just for one purpose. No matter what can be said about
it, it is quite evident that the Government wishes to place power in the hands
of the present majority in the county
councils. They can do it at any
moment.
If they see that the Roman Catholic
population is increasing they can immediately apply to their friend, the Minister
of Home Affairs, to have an inquiry held,
and I need not tell you what the decision
will be. One reason put forward in support of this Bill is that the Prime Minister
stated that he consulted all the public
bodies in Northern Ireland. That is not
so, because I have made it my business
to make inquiry, and he did not consult
the Urban Council of Omagh. There was
no intimation whatever, and the council
were never consulted. In a few months
from now I am quite sure they will crush
that council out of being. Then they can
say they will have every council in the
Six Counties prepared to follow whatever
policy they put forward.
So far as the minority are concerned we
will never be permitted to have a voice in
the legislation. We may put forth a
protest or an amendment, and one and
all they can be crushed without the
smallest consideration for those who
represent one-third of the Six Counties, as we
do in this House. There is no use in my
following all the evidence that has been
put forward by the Senator who has last
spoken. He has been furnished with a
brief.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Oh, no.
Mr. McHUGH: He certainly took
plenty of time and delivered it with
pauses and emphasis and everything
possible. I am quite sure the gentleman
who drafted the speech will be highly
pleased, and will congratulate him upon
the way he discharged his duty as a
Member of this House. I wish him every
success in continuing to deliver speeches
of that kind. It is just the thing you
would get in Belfast. I would not
expect anything else. Any person investing a penny in the Belfast papers
to-morrow will see in big, large print the
address or impressive speech which has
been delivered by my Friend behind me,
and there will not be a word about myself.
Mr. QUIGLEY: You will get every
word in the "Irish News."
Mr. McHUGH: I am quite sure they
will just say such a man followed. That
is the way everything is manœuvred in
Ulster. The voice of the Opposition is
crushed by the Press. It is a conspiracy
on the part of the Government and the
Press combined, for the sole purpose that
our grievances shall never be heard outside the buildings in which they are
uttered. I think the Mayor of Derry
said what is true. All our utterances
vanish into thin air, because they will
never be heard out of this House.
Mr. BARCLAY: 1 wish to say a few
words in welcoming this Bill. I am not
going to enter into the controversy which
we have just listened to. But I would
like to congratulate the Government on
introducing this Bill, if for nothing else
than the first Clause. I have had a great
deal of experience of electioneering, and
I know the month of January is a very
severe month to be out electioneering. I
am very glad to see that the date and
month are going to be altered to the
month of May, when there will be finer
weather, and it will be better for the
health of all concerned. I know that in
the past a great many aged people and
delicate people could not be brought out.
They would have risked their lives.
course their votes were lost. But now,
in May, the weather will be better, and
they can register their votes along with
the rest.
Of
There has been a great deal said to-day
and also on the last day regarding Portrush. It would seem to me from the tone
of all the speeches that this House is more
or less against the Urban Council of Portrush. I would not like to think that that
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
impression would go abroad or even to the
people of Portrush. I, myself, have great
sympathy with the urban council. They
carry on a great work there in making
provision for visitors during the summer
months. They have roads to make and to
keep good. They have footpaths to make
and keep good. They have the electric car
to run and to keep in proper order. They
have all the sanitary arrangements to
look after and to keep good, and they pay
for them.
That is the question; it is paying for
all. And I think that those people who
come for three months-I would call them
swallow businessmen-and fly into Portrush at that season just to pick up what
they possibly can and then, at the end of
the season, they fly away again and carry
away with them all they possibly can.
They fly to some safe haven where they
can settle down for eight or nine months
of the year and live in the lap of luxury.
And those who remain in Portrush for
52 weeks of the year have got to foot the
Bill for all the expenses of local government. I am in sympathy with the local
body and only wish that something could
be done to help them, but it seems to me
that the tide is against them. That is
all I have to say on that.
There is another Clause in the Bill that
I appreciate very much. That is the one
referring to tuberculosis. I think that is
a very good provision about the notification of this particular disease to the
authorities, so that the house might be
disinfected and the disease be kept from
spreading. What I would like to know
is, who is going to be responsible for this
notification. If it is the doctor who
attends the patient that is all right. But
I am afraid if it is left to the friends of
the person who has had this disease and
died, in a great many cases it will not
be notified, because some of the friends
look upon this disease as something that
they do not like. They think it is a kind
of stain on their character, and they try
to keep it as close as they possibly can.
I will give you an instance which will
help you to understand what I mean.
I knew a family who were pretty well
off. They were living in the country.
The father was a member of the local
authority. One of the girls, unfortun-
It was
ately, developed tuberculosis.
kept a secret. She died. The following
year one of the sons developed the disease
and he died. Another girl and then a
second boy died, and after that another
girl died. Five members of that family
were called away through this terrible
disease. These people tried to cloak the
thing. No neighbour could say they
died from consumption. If he did he
would have the wrath of these people on
his head. I have not the least doubt that
the people in the country districts will
try to cloak it. If it is left to them to
report to the authorities so that the home
may be disinfected and the disease stopped
I am afraid they will neglect their duty.
I would like to know if the medical officer
who has attended the patient will have the
responsibility of reporting the case. I
support the Bill, which I believe will be
a great benefit to all concerned.
Mr. LONG: I shall not attempt to
reply in detail to all the points which
have been raised in the course of this
debate. There are, however, one or two
matters upon which a few words seem to
be called for. In particular, I should
like to refer to the hon. Member's (Mr.
McMahon's) remarks and also Mr.
McHugh's remarks with regard to Clause
24. I am sorry that the hon. Member
has such difficulty in exorcising the bogey
of gerrymandering. I can assure him
that the sinister intentions which he has
read into the Clause are entirely the product of his own imagination. As a
matter of fact, this is one of the cases in
which drafting has proceeded, not by
reference, but by reenactment, and the
Clause makes no change in the existing
law, except to provide for an appeal from
the county council to the Ministry of
Home Affairs. The hon. Member remarked that he did not know what the
Government expected to gain by all this.
The answer to that is that the Government do not expect to gain anything
whatever, and that the whole object that
they have in mind is to ensure the more
satisfactory working of the present system
from the point of view of the local
interests.
The hon. Member also suggested that
existing powers make the introduction of
Clause 36 unnecessary. There are, in
fact, no powers of the kind at present, and
an inmate of the workhouse is entitled to
Local Government Bill-
Second Reading
30 OCTOBER 1931
take his discharge on giving a few hours'
notice to the master. The Clause is not
intended to deal with insubordination in
the workhouse, but simply with the particular kind of abuse of the workhouse
system to which I referred in my speech
on Thursday.
There were some criticisms of Clause 17.
The hon. Member (Mr. McLaughlin), for
instance, suggested that the figure of £8
specified in the Clause should be reduced
to £5. I should explain that the figure
of £8 has been chosen because it already
applies in a number of Acts affecting local
government in analogous cases as, for
example, the Belfast Corporation Acts
and the Rent Restriction Acts; but apart
from this, the whole question of such
valuation limits in Acts of all kinds will
fall to be reconsidered when the general
revaluation now in progress has been
completed. The criticism made by the
right hon. Member (Mr. Armstrong) was
based upon wider grounds. His objection
was to the whole principle of rates being
paid by the landlord instead of the
tenant, on the ground that this would
amount to representation without taxation. It is, however, to be remembered
that this provision in the Bill does not
relieve the tenant of paying his rates.
It
is intended, merely, to simplify the task
of collection by imposing upon the landlord the duty of collecting rates as part
of the rent, in return for which he may
be allowed a certain discount. The hon.
Member (Mr. McLaughlin), on the other
hand, wished to extend a similar concession with regard to discount to all ratepayers who pay their rates within a certain
period, and drew a certain comparison
with the owners' practice in connection
with the charges for gas and electric light.
I would express the opinion that this
additional inducement should hardly be
necessary as a general rule in view of the
exceptional powers of recovery which ratepayers possess as compared with any other
class of creditors.
There will, no doubt, be many to
sympathise with the regret expressed by
the hon. and gallant Member (Colonel
Sinclair) that the Bill does not undertake
the reorganisation of medical services. He
urged that this should have been put on
a county basis, as was, of course, also
recommended by the Local Government
Commission. I would remind the House
that, as I explained at the outset, the
Bill makes no pretence to effect important
changes of this kind in the local government system. But apart from that, it
must not be forgotten that even in the
relatively short time that has elapsed
since the Commission reported the work
of county councils has increased very considerably. It is indeed very questionable
whether they are in a position to undertake more work of any kind, and they
have themselves voiced their objection to
any proposal to impose new functions.
upon them.
I should also like to thank the hon.
and gallant Member, Colonel Pakenham,
for his suggestion that the Clause to deal
with the provision of fire escapes at hotels
should be included in the Bill, and to say
that if the hon. and gallant Member will
be good enough to put down a Clause for
consideration on the Committee stage the
Government will be prepared to support
it in principle.
I have listened with interest to the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie). I can
give him the assurance that the various
suggestions which he has made, with a
view to the improvement of certain
Clauses of the Bill will have the careful
consideration of the Government, and no
one will appreciate better than the hon.
and learned Senator that it would be
impossible to venture any opinion upon
the merits of his subtle proposals upon the
spur of the moment. I might, however,
say that the objections which he has
stated in connection with Clause 21 do
not appear to me to be serious. Where
the greater power to acquire land by way
of compulsory acquisition already exists,
the granting of the lesser power to acquire
easements of the kind specified in the
Clause seems to be but a slight concession.
The Government, I may add, will accept
also an Amendment to Clause 3 on the
lines proposed by the Lord Mayor.
Question put: "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."
The House divided: Contents, 9; Not
Contents, 2.

Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That

Mr. LESLIE: I wonder would the
Parliamentary Secretary tell us when we
are adjourning to; I think, probably,
Thursday.
Mr. LONG: Yes.
Mr. LESLIE: Can he give us any idea

Leslie, Mr. James G.

Mr. LONG: Yes, most decidedly.

Mr. LESLIE: I understood from what

Oral Answers
Oral Answers
1 NOVEMBER 1934
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 1st November, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
THE VALUATION OFFICES IN
ARMAGH.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to ask the
Deputy-Leader of the House whether it
is proposed to remove the Valuation
Offices from Armagh to Portadown, and,
if so, for what reason, and how much was
spent recently in fitting up the offices in
Armagh; and whether, in view of the
fact that Armagh is the county town in
the centre of the county (whereas Portadown is almost on the border of the
county) and more convenient for the
people of Armagh as a whole, he will
undertake to have the Valuation Offices
kept where they are in Armagh.
I wish to point out that Armagh is
practically the centre of the county, and
certainly the most convenient town for
the inhabitants of County Armagh at
large.
To show how convenient Armagh
is, I may say that in a great many instances people travelling from portions of
the county have actually to pass through
Armagh to go to Portadown. The feeling is that offices such as the Valuation
Office and the Labour Exchange should
certainly be situated in the county town,
and the most central portion of the
county.
Mr. LONG: There is no present intention of removing the Valuation Offices
from Armagh, pending the completion of
the necessary revaluation work, but no
undertaking can be given as regards the
permanent establishment of Valuation
Offices in Armagh.
THE UNEMPLOYMENT BOARD
FOR ARMAGH.
I
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: This question is
with regard to the new Unemployment
Board office for County Armagh.
understand that it is suggested to have
these offices set up in Portadown. As
pointed out in the previous question,
Portadown is not central for County
Armagh, and is not convenient for the
County Armagh people to visit. I do
not see any reason why the Government
in selecting places for the situation of
offices should go practically to one end
of the county and practically ignore the
convenience as well as the wishes of the
people of the county. As pointed out on
the previous question, the people of South
Armagh require to travel through Armagh
to Portadown. In other words, they
have got to go from one end of the county
to the other. In a great many instances,
because the offices are not situated in the
county town, they have actually to travel
nine miles farther than if the office were
in Armagh. I sincerely hope the Government will consider the convenience of the
public in County Armagh and also remember that Armagh is the county town.
Heretofore, before this Parliament was
set up, it was the centre of the law
courts and the centre of the county
offices, and practically the centre of any
official business that had to be done in
the county. Previous to the setting up
of this Parliament they had the Probate
Court, and the Probate Court has now
been removed to Londonderry. I certainly hope that Armagh is not going to
be further curtailed by offices to which
they are entitled being taken away from
them. I beg to ask the Deputy-Leader
of the House whether it is the intention
of the Ministry to set up the offices of
the new Unemployment Board for County
Armagh in Portadown, and, if so, why
the county town (Armagh), which is in
the centre of the county and more central
for County Armagh workers, was not
selected.
Mr. LONG: The Unemployment
Assistance Board are of opinion that the
principal considerations to be regarded
in the selection of centres for the local
offices of the Board are the geographical
distribution of those persons who will
come within the scope of the Unemployment Assistance Scheme and the facilities
for transport. In accordance with this
policy it has been decided that for those
persons living in the northern part of
County Armagh, Portadown will be the
most convenient centre, residents in the
southern portion of the county being
dealt with from a local office which the
Board propose to establish at Newry.
Oral Answers
Fisheries Bi!!
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
An officer of the Board will, however,
be stationed in the city of Armagh, and
those residents within the city to whom
allowances may be granted will receive
them at the Armagh Employment
Exchange.
Mr. McMAHON: Arising out of that,
I would just like to ask the Parliamentary
Secretary the cost of setting up this office
in Portadown. I always wondered when
I read in the papers about the Government coming down on the county councils
and the local boards for making certain
expenditure, who looked after the Government in their expenditure. The Government seems to be above suspicion in these
things, and yet the taxpayer has got to
find the money.
If the Government go
on as they are doing in trying to find
a solution for the unemployment question
you will find that Portadown will not
be the only place to have a labour
exchange; they will have a labour
exchange in every street, because they are
simply doing nothing to relieve unemployment.
Mr. LONG: I am afraid I would
require notice to allow me time to
ascertain the cost.
KINNEGAR RIFLE RANGE.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Quigley:
To ask the Leader of the House whether
his attention has been called to the proposed change from west to north in the
direction of fire at the Kinnegar Rifle
Range; whether the new danger zone
includes portion of the Channel through
which steamers pass to and from Belfast
and a large part of the foreshore at Holywood, which forms the playground of
hundreds of children during the summer
months; whether an accidental variation
of fifteen to twenty degrees in the direction of fire would bring bullets amongst
the houses of Holywood; and whether he
will make representations to the proper
authorities with a view to the reconsideration of the proposed change.
Mr. SPEAKER: With regard to this
question on the paper, I am afraid it is
entirely outside the purview of this
Government. It is a matter entirely for
the Imperial Parliament and the War
Office. Therefore I regret I cannot allow
the question.
Mr. McMAHON: I happen to notice
to-day from the minutes of the Corporation
that this matter has been dealt with by
the Improvement Committee, so that it
must not be outside their jurisdiction.
(Hon. Members: Order, order.)
Mr. SPEAKER: As I have ruled the
question out of order I think any discussion of the matter is out of order.
Fisheries Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Resolved: "That this Report be now
received." (Mr. Long.)
(6
Mr. Long: I beg to move in page 11,
line 54, after the second "the" to insert
crown and." This is purely a drafting
Amendment. Section 93 of the Fisheries
Act of 1842, which this clause reproduces
and amends, required the deposit of bylaws with the Clerk of the Peace. This
office is now combined with the office
of Clerk of the Crown, and the official
holding it is known as the Clerk of the
Crown and Peace.
Mr. LESLIE: I wonder if on that I
might ask the Parliamentary Secretary
whether we may understand now that
there is no such office as Clerk of the
Peace in any of the Six Counties. I need
not press for an answer at the moment.
Amendment agreed to.
Bill ordered to be read the Third time
on Tuesday next.
Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Long).
Resolved:
"That this Bill do now
pass." (Mr. Long).
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree to the said Bill without
any Amendment.
Adjournment--
Sittings of the House
1 NOVEMBER 1934
Local Government Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Mr. LONG: I do not intend to proceed
with the Committee stage of this Bill
to-day.
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn."
Mr. LESLIE: Following what the
Parliamentary Secretary has said about
postponing the Committee stage of the
Local Government Bill, I do not think he
stated when he proposed to take it, but
I imagine it will be on next Tuesday.
I was going to ask the Parliamentary
Secretary to give us some idea of what
will happen during the remainder of that
week, that is to say, what days next week
it is suggested that the House will sit.
Mr: LONG: The intention
was to
take the Committee stage of the Local
Government Bill on Tuesday, and then
the Report stage on Thursday. It is a
matter for the consideration of the House
whether they would be agreeable to
take the Third Reading also on Thursday
or whether they would prefer to come back
on Friday for the Third Reading. That
is to say, the Committee stage on Tuesday, the Report stage on Thursday, and
the Third Reading on Friday, or the
Report stage and Third Reading on
Thursday. It is entirely a matter for
the House.
Mr. MCLAUGHLIN : Surely the
Deputy Leader of the House does not
suggest that we should go through this
large sheaf of Amendments on Tuesday?
Mr. LONG: Yes.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I do think it is
rushing matters rather too quickly to
Ideal with all these Amendments in Committee on Tuesday, and then to take the
Report stage and Third Reading on
Thursday. I suggest we should have an
extra day, and sit on Friday.
Mr. LONG: I am quite agreeable.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at half
after Four o'clock until Tuesday next.

Local Government Bill.

Mr. LONG: In moving "That this
House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said Bill" I wish to state
that we are privileged to have with us
the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister
of Home Affairs, who has kindly consented to deal with any points that may
arise. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I
beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Date of election and
annual or quarterly meeting in boroughs,
urban districts and towns), and 2
(Monthly meetings of urban district
councils and town commissioners)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Voting at meetings of
borough and urban district councils and
town commissioners).
(1) In the case of the council of any
borough or other urban district or the
commissioners of any town-
(a) all acts of the council or commissioners, and all questions coming
or arising before the council or
commissioners, may be done and
decided by the majority of such
members of the council or commissioners as are present and vote
at a meeting held in pursuance of
the enactments applicable to the
council or commissioners, the
whole number of members or commissioners present at the meeting,
whether voting or not, not being
less than the number required by
those enactments;
(b) if at any meeting there is an
equal division of votes upon any
question, the mayor, chairman, or
other member or commissioner
acting as chairman at the meeting
shall, in addition to his own vote
as a member or commissioner,
have a second or casting vote;
(c) the names of the members or commissioners present at a meeting as
well as of those voting on each
question shall be recorded, so as
to show whether each vote given
was for or against the question;
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
beg to move, at end of paragraph (a) to
add:
Local Government Bill-
Committee
6 NOVEMBER 1934
(b) such majority as aforesaid shall be
determined either by a show of
hands or by voices, unless a
division is demanded-
(i) by such number of members or
commissioners present as is not
less than one-tenth of the whole
number of the council or commissioners; or
(ii) by not less than three members or commissioners present,
number 1S the
whichever
greater.
I fully explained this matter on the
Second Reading and it is unnecessary
for me to go into details to-day. It is
sufficient to say that it is to determine
how the vote will be taken. I will just
give you one example. On Thursday at
the monthly meeting of the Corporation
we had 47 motions to be voted on as well
as several amendments which were
moved. Now there are only very few
votes or polls taken, and on those 50
motions I timed how long it took to
take a poll. It took over five minutes
in each case when we had to record the
names. Consequently, it would mean if
the Clause stands as it is that at least
two or three hours would be taken each
first of the month to go through the
business. Of course I think nobody
wants that. I beg to move.
I
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS
(Sir Dawson Bates): May I first of all
express my thanks to the House for the
privilege they have afforded me of
coming here this evening. I am glad to
be able to say that the Government is
prepared to accept the Amendment
moved by the right hon. Senator.
might, at the same time, point out in
case this House might think the Government are negligent in the way they
have drafted the Bill that the Local
Government Enactments Order has
been in force since 1898 in every rural
authority without giving rise to the
difficulties foreshadowed by the hon.
Senator. I agree that there is a possibility of misunderstanding which we
ought to avoid.
Amendment agreed to.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
beg to move, in paragraph (c) to leave
out from "meeting" to the end of the
paragraph, and to insert: "Shall be recorded, and in every case where a
division is taken the names of those
voting for, voting against, or abstaining
from voting on, the question shall be
separately recorded."
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

county or district council or board of
guardians or town commissioners or
any committee of, or joint committee
or joint board partly appointed by,

in the exercise of any of their powers
