Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 11,
line 21, after sub-clause (4) to insert:
(5) Any such minute made as aforesaid shall be absolutely privileged and
any proceedings alleging libel in
respect of anything contained therein.
shall be subject to the provisions of
this sub-section.
I mentioned this matter on the Second
Reading. It may be recalled that the
question was raised in another place and
no answer was given, and therefore I
thought that I ought to look into the
subject if I could. I could not find that
there was any definite statutory protection. It did occur to me that an
unlearned member of a rural council
might think he ought to be clothed with
full protection. Therefore, I thought I
would put down something short and
snappy which the Committee could include in the Bill, or the Minister could
say something which would allay any
public doubt or difficulty-or rather
semi-public doubt and difficulty-that
might possibly arise. I am not going
to say any more. I am not going to
address the Committee on the difference
between absolute and qualified privilege.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I would remind the Committee that the purposes
of the Clause affected or purported to be
affected by this Amendment is to require
local authorities to make a minute in
the prescribed form in the event of
accepting a tender other than the lowest.
This, if passed in its present form, will
apply to all local authorities, whereas
the existing provision in the Local Government Act, 1902, applies only to
county and rural district councils. But
it is evidently apprehended by the hon.
and learned Senator-
Mr. LESLIE: No; not by me.
Sir DAWSON BATES- -that the
reasons given for not accepting the
lowest tender might in some cases amount

to libel on the contractors, and accordingly he suggests that the Bill should
provide that any minute of this kind
should be absolutely privileged. I
think the hon. Senator will realise that
it is well established in law that qualified privileges extends to the proceedings
of local authorities. Therefore there are
obvious objections to go beyond this and
to make such proceedings absolutely
privileged. The position of course
that a member of a local authority may
make any statement, unless it is made
maliciously, in which case if the contractor can prove malice the qualified
privilege thereupon disappears and an
action will lie for slander. It seems to
me that this is a perfectly adequate and
reasonable provision, and it might be
added that any such minute as is referred
to would obviously simply embody discussions that had taken place at the
meeting of the local authority when the
question of the tenders was being
decided and, apart from anything else,
it is difficult to see any good reason or
justification. for extending absolute
privilege to a record of the discussion
which is covered only by qualified privilege.
I have tried to make myself as clear
as I can. I think most hon. Senators
will appreciate the great danger of suggesting that everything on an occasion.
like this should be absolutely privileged.
It would put the contractors in
absolutely impossible position. Therefore the Government cannot accept the
Amendment.
an
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite sure that
those really interested will be satisfied
with that explanation. I ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
NEW CLAUSE (Amendment of law
as to appeals against surcharges, 34 and
35 Vic., c. 109).
(1) Where any payment, charge or
allowance made, or authorised to be
made, by any individual member of a
local authority acting as such member
is surcharged by a local government
auditor upon such individual, it shall
be lawful for the local authority to
resolve that an application shall be
Local Government Bill-
Committee
6 NOVEMBER 1934
made to the High Court in accordance
with the provisions of section twelve
of the Local Government (Ireland)
Act, 1871.
(2) On the hearing of any such
application, and without prejudice to
any power by the said section twelve
conferred, or otherwise possessed by
the court the court may notwithstanding that the application does not
succeed, remit the whole or any part
of the amount surcharged.
(3) Where an application is made in
pursuance of such resolution as aforesaid the costs of such application in
any event and the amount of so much
of the surcharge as may not be remitted shall be payable by the local
authority and be defrayed out of the
rates that local authority has power
to make.
(4) In this section, subject to the
provisions of sub-section (5) thereof,
the expression "local authority" means
the council of a county or an urban
district and the commissioners of any
town and includes also the Belfast
Water Commissioners.
(5) Where the council of a rural
district desires that the provisions of
this section shall apply in respect of
a surcharge arising from a payment,
charge or allowance made by a member of such council, such council may
apply to the council of the county concerned and such last mentioned council
may, if it sees fit, pass a resolution
under this section and then and in
such case the provisions of this
section shall have effect accordingly.
(6) Section twelve of the Local Government (Ireland) Act, 1871, shall
have effect subject to the provisions of
this section.
Mr. LESLIE : This, again, is a
matter which I mentioned on the Second
Reading. I said then that I thought I
would endeavour to see if anything
could be done by way of amendment
introduced on Report. I did not expect
to be able in the time at my disposal to
try to bring anything forward for the
consideration of the Committee. However, on looking into it I came to the
conclusion that it would be much better
to put something on the Paper, however
imperfect and incomplete, in order that
we might have something in the nature of
a preliminary canter, and it then might
be decided whether anything could be
properly and usefully done at a later
stage. Possibly, in fact I may say almost
probably, the Committee, looking at the
words on the Paper, will say that in
some ways the proposed Clause goes too
far, and the Committee will not be unmindful of the great dangers that lie
latent in any attempt to interfere with
the general practice of surcharge in
appropriate cases. That consideration
must necessarily present itself to any of
our minds.
Another point that may strike the Committee is that time scarcely admits of our
dealing with a not uncomplicated case at
the present moment, and the Committee
may feel that when an attempt was made
to protect rural district councils something ought to have been said about
boards of guardians also, although they
are in one sense more composite bodies
than rural district councils. To take a
country board as an example, the Ballymoney Board of Guardians includes members representing two urban districts.
within the geographical boundary, and the
Belfast Board of Guardians has jurisdiction not only over the county borough
but over the Belfast rural district in
County Antrim and the Holywood and
Castlereagh rural districts in County
Down. But I did not see any possibility
of bringing them in, because a board of
guardians is not a rate-making authority,
and although it is possible to connect a
rural district council with its main county.
However, I do not think we can do anything for a board of guardians.
Now, as to the origin of this question.
Representations were made to me, and I
think to other Members of this House,
by the Association of Ulster County
Councils, that they were very anxious to
secure in effect two main principles.
Their idea is that in the event of its being
desired to appeal against a surcharge
made in the ordinary manner they want
to make it rather easier than it is at
present to appeal to the High Court
instead of to the Ministry, and if that
appeal goes on they want to give the High
Court powers such as the Ministry possess
at present, declaring that the surcharge
was properly made but remitting it either
in whole or in part. They also wish that
the costs of any such appeal to the High
Court may, with the distinct authority of
Local Government Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
the local authority itself, be borne by the
rates which they are entitled to
administer. That is their case as briefly
as I can put it. What we can do for
them while keeping clear of the danger of
making the practice of surcharge nothing
but a farce is one question, and whether
we ought to extend what I have put on
the Paper is another. It might be
thought that the words on the Paper
would not adequately protect a member
of a committee of a county council or a
member who was not a member of a
county council itself, but who was COopted, not by the county council but by
the committee.
All those considerations might be taken
up at a later stage, but the point I want
to make this afternoon is, is it possible
for us to do now in this Bill something
which will make it a little easier for a
local authority to get in an appeal to the
High Court, at the cost of the ratepayers
ultimately, than it is at present. I should
make it quite clear that at present an
appeal can be made to the High Court,
but it must be by the appellants themselves and at their own expense, and the
High Court has no power to remit the
surcharge. Those powers are possessed
by the Ministry and are exercised, and
although the Association of County
Councils wish to have this further power,
or this easier power, they want to have it
made a little easier, to make it simpler for
them to obtain the opinion of the High
Court on a point of law.
There is no suggestion that appeals to
the Ministry have ever received anything
but the most sympathetic consideration,
but it is thought that it should be made
less expensive for the individual to appeal
to the High Court if the local authority
concerned really thinks it is a case where
an appeal should go to the court.
Accordingly, in the hope that we may hear
the views of others on this question, I
beg to move the Amendment on the
Paper, and if it should be acceptable it
will need alteration on Report.
Mr. McMAHON: I agree with a good
deal that Senator Leslie has said regarding the question of surcharge. Members
of the Belfast Corporation have sometimes been threatened with surcharge,
and the usual procedure is to appeal to
the Ministry of Home Affairs. Up to the
moment the Ministry has not exercised
its right to make any surchage, but the
day may arrive when the Ministry may
decide that a surcharge must be imposed.
If such an occurrence took place the
members of the Corporation who were
surcharged would naturally have to appeal
to the High Court at their own expense.
As the hon. and learned Senator has
pointed out, that is unfair to members of
the local authority, who are trying to do
the best possible in the interests of the
ratepayers, and in the interests of the unemployed. If the Ministry of Home
Affairs decide that there must be a surcharge it is unfair that people who are
giving their time voluntarily in the
interests of the ratepayers as a whole,
should have to foot the bill in the event
of the appeal going in their favour.
Therefore, I hope the Government will
accept the Amendment.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
think everyone will be inclined to be
sympathetic towards those members of
local boards who conscientiously and
honestly decide to take a certain course of
action, and afterwards find themselves
surcharged by the auditor. It is rather a
difficult position, but one has to remember
that there may be cases of members who
do not act conscientiously or honestly,
and that there is a reason for the law
being as it is. It must not be forgotten
that members who are surcharged always
have the opportunity of appealing to the
Ministry of Home Affairs, and all such
cases are considered very sympathetically
by the Ministry. It is not necessary for
members who are surcharged to go to the
High Court. If power was given to a
local authority to appeal to the High
Court and to defray the expenses of the
appeal out of the rates the majority,
whose action was the cause of the surcharge, would certainly vote in favour of
the appeal to the High Court and for the
payment of their expenses out of the rates.
I think it would be a pity to remove the
check which at present exists on local
authorities and which makes members
very careful to accept the lowest tender
or, if they do not, to give a reasonable
explanation for accepting a higher tender.
As I have said, members of a local
authority who are surcharged have an
opportunity of appealing to the Home
Office, and the surcharge is generally remitted if a reasonable excuse for their
action is given.
Local Government Bill-
Committee
6 NOVEMBER 1934
came
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I think there
is a great deal in what Senator Leslie
has said in moving this Amendment.
In connection with the Down County
Regional Education Committee a certain
procedure had been carried out for six or
seven years without question, and at the
end of that time the Ministry suddenly
me down on three members of that
Committee and surcharged them to the
extent of £1,000. They appealed to the
Ministry and the surcharge was remitted,
but I would like the Ministry to understand that we would have liked to have
had a legal decision on the point. If
this procedure was wrong the Ministry
had no right to allow it to go on for
six or seven years.
If the three members
who were surcharged had had the right
to appeal to the High Court they would
have had to do so at their own expense,
and I think that in a case of that kind
the expense of an appeal should be
defrayed out of the rates.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think there
is some misunderstanding in connection
with this very important matter. I desire
to point out in the first instance that the
Amendment, as drafted, would cover
county councils, rural district councils,
and urban local authorities, but it would
not cover many other local authorities,
as, for example, boards of guardians,
district hospital committees, and regional
education committees. Therefore, to that
extent I suggest that the Amendment
would not carry out what the hon. and
learned Member desires.
It is necessary to bear in mind that the
whole principle of surcharge is to protect
the ratepayers against having to bear
burdens which could not properly fall
upon them. I hope hon. Members will
appreciate that point. Accordingly, if,
as the Amendment proposes, unremitted
surcharges are to be paid, not by the
persons surcharged, but by the ratepayers
themselves, in any case where the local
authority chooses to go to the High Court
on appeal, the whole purpose of surcharge disappears. Not only will the
ratepayers have to bear the loss which is
the subject matter of the surcharge, but
they will also have to bear the legal costs
in connection with the appeal.
What is the position to-day? It is
open to any individual who is surcharged
to go to the High Court, which can remit
the surcharge or not as the court thinks
right, on legal grounds. If the court
decides that the surcharge was illegally
made the court will award costs to the
appellant. Therefore, if a member of a
local board justifiably made a mistake
and the court decided in his favour he
would get his costs. If, on the other
hand, he has made what may be described
as a bona fide technical error in ignorance
of the law he has a right to go to the
Home Office, and members of local
authorities will, I am sure, agree with me
when I say that the Ministry have always
tried to distinguish between a bona fide
technical error made by a member of a
local council and a deliberate attempt to
go against the law. Senator Hill Dickson referred to a case where members of
the Down Regional Education Committee
were surcharged by the local government
auditor, but he did not tell the Committee
that the Ministry of Home Affairs, to
which an appeal went, remitted that
surcharge.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Oh yes, I did.
re-
Sir DAWSON BATES: It was
mitted on the ground that the regional
committee acted entirely bona fide and in
ignorance of the law, and that from every
point of view it was a case in which the
surcharge ought to be remitted. Therefore, I suggest to the Committee that it
would be very wrong to carry out the
suggestion contained in the Amendment.
It would open the door to fraud on the
part of those individuals who get on to
local councils with the intention, not of
protecting the ratepayers, but to do things
they know are entirely wrong. Under
these circumstances the Government cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not gather, from
the very interesting reply of the right
hon. Gentleman, whether he thought there
was any possibility of doing something to
assist local authorities in such cases to
get the legal opinion of the High Court.
I think he meant to deal with that, but
he did not do so. The point is rather to
get the opinion of the High Court on the
matter than a mere question of the
pounds, shillings, and pence involved; the
point being that if legal opinion was
definitely desired the ratepayers ought to
pay for it.
Local Government Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Sir DAWSON BATES: Surely the
hon. Senator must realise that he has not
attempted to introduce an Amendment to
cover that point. It seems to me it would
be a grave injustice to the ratepayers.
It simply amounts to this, that anybody
could knowingly run no personal risk; he
could vote for the expenditure of a
certain sum of money and would be surcharged, and then the majority could
apply as a council to the High Court,
which would give a decision one way or
the other. It would be immaterial which
way the decision would go, because the
ratepayers would lose, not alone the
amount surcharged, but would have to
bear the costs. The hon. and learned
Senator mentioned one point. I think
he said that the Association of Local
Authorities asked us to include this in the
Bill. I am sorry to say that I have no
record that I received this request from
the local authorities.
It
Mr. LESLIE: I think I ought to ask
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
has served its purpose.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

The expression "area" means any such

The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Armstrong:
Page 13, line 40, leave out "eight
pounds" ("not exceed eight pounds")
and insert "four pounds in all areas to
which this sub-section applies save in the
city of Belfast.”
Mr. LESLIE: I had a telegram a few
minutes ago from the right hon. Gentleman, His Majesty's Lieutenant for
County Armagh, stating he was unwell
and unable to attend to-day. I take that
to mean that he wished me to move on
his behalf the Amendments standing in
his name. The first of them is in page
13, line 1, to leave out "eight pounds"
and insert "four pounds in all areas to
which this sub-section applies, save in the
city of Belfast." Those who were present
on the Second Reading will remember
that the right hon. Gentleman spoke to
us at that time on the difficulty that he
experienced in accepting the wording of
the Clause as it appeared, on the ground
that £8 was an unduly high figure to be
imposed in country districts and on some
of the smaller towns. He was aware, I
imagine, that £8 had, if I may say so,
special merits in relation to the city of
Belfast, owing to local enactments, and
also, I think, he must have been aware
that £8 had some merit under the Rents
Restrictions Act. At all events, he
thought that £4 in all areas, other than
the city of Belfast, would be an improvement on £8 as it appears in the Bill.
Therefore, as he is not here to move it
himself I beg to move the Amendment.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am sorry the
Government cannot accept this Amendment There are two implications in this
Amendment; the first being the reduction
of the limiting valuation figure from £8
Local Government Bill-
Committee
6 NOVEMBER 1934
In
to £4, and the second being that of excluding the County Borough of Belfast
from the operation of the Clause. I must
deal with each of the two implications
under this Amendment separately.
the first place, I would like to point out
that the sub-section affected by the
Amendment is optional and not mandatory. It lies entirely in the hands of
the local authorities whether they wish to
adopt that section or not. The County
Borough of Belfast have, for years past,
adopted that principle in one of their
local Acts with a saving to the ratepayers.
In addition to that a great many of the
other local authorities, such as Bangor,
Lisburn and other towns have adopted a
similar Clause. It is a case in which the
Government gave power to the local
authorities to do as they liked best, and I
fail to see what objection can be taken
to it. This is one of the Clauses that are
inserted at the request of the local
authorities.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure if the right
hon. Gentleman were here he would ask
leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment also stood in
the name of Mr. Armstrong:
Page 15, line 2, at end add :-
"either by personal action or at the
option of the owner by such ejectment as can now be brought to enforce payment of arrears of rent
the said sum payable by the tenant
being deemed to be a rent issuing
out of the premises in respect of
which said sum has been paid by
the owner."
Mr. LESLIE: This is another Amendment put down by the same right hon.
Gentleman. I think that the meaning of
it is sufficiently plain and, therefore, I
simply formally move it without detaining
the Committee.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am sorry
that I am unable to accept this Amendment. It is a most drastic Amendment.
I think the penalty of ejectment is one
of the most serious things that can
possibly affect anyone. It is only
applicable at present to the non-payment
of rent, and to introduce the principle
that
a landlord can eject a tenant
because the rates have not been paid
under certain circumstances is one that
the Government cannot agree to.
Mr. LESLIE: Again, I think that
should be withdrawn.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
16, after line 4, insert as a new subsection:
-
"(4) Where any owner is rated under
sub-section (3) of this section such
owner shall be entitled to such
relief in respect of any non-occupation of the premises as he would
have been entitled to receive had
he been the rated
Occupier
thereof."
I now rise, if I may say so, on my own
brief. This is a small Amendment which
I think will appeal to the Committee. It
seems fairly obvious that where the owner
has to pay rates instead of the occupier
he should have the same relief, if the
premises are unoccupied, as the occupier
would have. The only thing I have to
say to the Committee is as to making a
slight alteration in the wording on the
Paper. In the last line but one of the
Amendment I am altering the expression
"had he been the rated occupier thereof"
to "had he been rated as the occupier in
respect thereof." It will be seen that the
words as down are an unconscious and
subtle bull.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I am glad t
inform the Committee that the Government is prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
page
17, after line 21, insert as a new subsection:
-:
"(8). This Section shall come into
operation on and shall have effect from
and after the first day of April, nineteen hundred and thirty-five."
I mentioned this also on the Second
Reading. It seems to me it would be
convenient that we postpone the operation
of the Clause until the commencement
of the next financial year.

The foregoing paragraph (g) shall

holds a paid office or place of profit

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
19, after line 3, insert as
a new subsection :--
"(4) Nothing in this section shall
apply to any officer of an education
authority acting under the powers.
conferred by the Education Act
(Northern Ireland), 1923,
statute amending the same.
or any
I hope this Amendment will appeal to
the right hon. Gentleman as one of the
apostles of safety first. The Clause, I
am sure, was never intended to apply to
schoolmasters and people of that kind,
but I am afraid it has that effect. The
only thing I have to say about it is that
the first line should read, "Nothing
under sub-section (1) of this section." I
wrote that rather hurriedly and failed to
copy it correctly. The point is to prevent the Minister, or somebody acting
for the Minister, by inadvertence,
making some order in respect to officers
of the education authority, which, if
made, ought to be made by the education
authority and not the Home Office.
There is always some difficulty in respect
of overlapping between departments. I
think Parliament should as far as it can
do everything possible to discourage it in
any case. The sub-section as a whole
certainly is not meant for schoolmasters
and so on, but I am inclined to think
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Amendment as it stands could not be accepted
as I think the hon. and learned Senator
will appreciate. It goes too far. I
would like to give an assurance to him
that it is not the intention of the Government to interfere in any way with
teachers and those coming under the
Education Authority.
Perhaps with
that assurance he would withdraw the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the Committee will accept that assurance. I
ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the

CLAUSES 19 (Amendments as to
returns of receipts and payments), and
20 (Annual rent of petty sessions' room)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 21 (Public roads).
21.-(1) Any county council may,
for the discharge of surface water from
a public road, purchase or otherwise
acquire from any person any drain, or
any right of making or of user or other
right in or respecting a drain
(with or without any buildings, works,
materials or things belonging thereto)
within their county, and any person
may sell or grant to such county
council any such drain, right
property belonging to him. Any purchase money paid by a county council
in pursuance of this sub-section shall
be subject to the same trusts (if any)
as the drain, right or property sold
was subject to.
or
Any person who, previously to the
purchase of a drain in pursuance of
this sub-section, has acquired a right
to use such drain shall be entitled to
use the same or any drain substituted
in lieu thereof, to the same extent as
such person would or might have done
if the purchase had not been made.
The provisions of section one of the
Roads Improvement Act (Northern
Ireland), 1928, and the Schedule to
that Act (as amended by sub-section
Local Government Bill-
Committee
6 NOVEMBER 1934
*
(4) of this section) shall apply in any
case where a county council desire to
acquire, otherwise than by agreement,
any such drain, right or property as
is mentioned in this sub-section.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
19, line 34, leave out "buildings, works"
("without any buildings, works').
Speaking on Clause 21 on the Second
Reading I think I said that it had given
me more trouble than all the rest of the
Bill put together. I continued to hold
that opinion for quite a long time. I
have had, between the Second Reading
and to-day, several conferences with
people who understood the Clause and
were able to discuss the matter more
discursively than it can be done in this
House. The conclusion ultimately
arrived at was that the manifest
objection to the Clause as it came before
us could be met if we did two things,
first if
we limited the Clause more
definitely to its purpose, that is the purpose of relieving the county roads of
surface water, and secondly, if we gave
some indication of what we mean by the
expression "drain." Accordingly, one
came to the conclusion that what was
required to be done was to make two
Amendments, one of which is to leave
out the two words on line 34, which
means that "drain" will now include
materials or things belonging thereto
without the special reference to buildings
and works which seems inappropriate.
The second Amendment I will mention
when I come to it.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government are prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move,
20, line 14, at end add:
:-
in page
"In this sub-section the expression
"drain" includes any ditch,
culvert, dyke or sluice."
cut,
This is the other half of what we are
going to do, and it defines "drain."
The terms used are based on the terms
used in an Act of 1926 applicable to
rural areas, but they are rather less than
the definition there. I think introducing the word "culvert" will give the
county councils everything they need
instead of "works and buildings" which
they do not need. The effect of the two
Amendments will be to make the whole
Clause perfectly workable and entirely
unobjectionable. In fact it will enable
us to appreciate what we did not appreciate before, that is, the submission of the
right hon. Gentleman in another place
that there was nothing in the Clause that
anybody could object to. With these
alterations I entirely agree. The procedure adopted will then be really the
most simple and most convenient that
could be.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government are prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to move in
Clause 24, page 25, line 13 at end (“make
an order') to add "subject to approval of
both Houses of Parliament."
I move this Amendment on the suggestion of my hon. and learned Friend,
Senator Leslie. I sincerely hope the
Local Government Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
Government will see their way to accept
it and let us have some protection as far
as this Clause is concerned.
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I am
rather surprised that it should be the hon.
Senator who is fathering this Amendment for the hon. and learned Senator
behind me, especially as this is a case
where the local authority is given a little.
control of its own house in matters of
which the Senator who has just spoken
and myself would assume that the local
authority had more knowledge, perhaps,
than people in other places. I sincerely
hope that the Committee will not support such an Amendment. There is little
object in giving a local authority something they desire with one hand and
taking it away with the other, or at least
delaying the operation of it. This Clause
is one which should be welcomed by any
local authority, and I hope that Senator
McLaughlin is merely fathering the
Amendment at the request of my hon.
and learned Friend behind me without
realising that it is impossible to insert.
it at the place mentioned. The Amendment, in my opinion, should not be
accepted.
Mr. LESLIE: I am not altogether
inclined to accept the imputation of
paternity in regard to this Amendment.
If I remember rightly what I did suggest in the course of the Second Reading
debate was that if the hon. Senator and
those associated with him really wanted
a little protection they could get something by putting the necessity for confirming the order on the shoulders of
Parliament, but it never occurred to me
that the words on the Paper should be
inserted in the place where it is proposed
to insert them. My idea was that some
little phrase could be used much further
on in the Clause, after the matter had
gone to the Ministry and after everything was in order, saying that the
Ministry's order should require confirmation by Parliament. That is what was
in my mind if anybody really wanted to
do it. I am afraid the right hon. Gentleman is not going to accept the words
standing on the Paper, but he may consider the matter on Report. I, myself,
could not support those words.
Sir DAWSON BATES: It is quite
impossible for the Government to accept
this Amendment. If an urban counci
wishes to increase its area it goes to the
county council, and the county council,
after holding an inquiry, decide whether
to accede to or refuse the request. In the
event of their refusing the urban council,
or any ratepayer dissatisfied, can go to the
Ministry of Home Affairs as an appeal
court. Here you have the extraordinary
proposition that the county council, after
they have held their inquiry and before
they make an order, must go to both
Houses of Parliament, and it is left to the
Houses of Parliament to decide whether
or not their request will be granted. A
very great compliment is paid to the
Ministry of Home Affairs, because they
would be able to over-ride both Houses
of Parliament. On consideration the
Committee will agree that the proposal
is not democratic, and it is not even one
which the Minister of Home Affairs can
accept.
Mr. McMAHON: I am really disappointed at the Minister of Home
Affairs, because we were told the other
day, in the course of the discussion, that
there was nothing to fear from this particular Clause. This is the acid test of
whether there is anything to fear or not.
I want to be quite frank with the Committee when I say that it makes very
little difference whether it is the Ministry
of Home Affairs, the House of Commons,
or the Senate that is appealed to in connection with the extension of boundaries,
because when the question of gerrymandering comes in you will find the
members of the Government party all
agreed in regard to their political
opponents. The only real object we have
in putting down this Amendment is that
when you impose an injustice, which you
will do, we shall have the right to get up
in either House and express our indignation at the treatment meted out. As I
said at the outset, this is the acid test.
You have read into this Amendment
what we thought you would read into it.
It is really a question of muzzling the
minority in addition to the other injustices
imposed upon them. I would have
thought that in regard to a simple
Amendment like this you might have
made some gesture towards your
political opponents in order to let them
see there was some justice in your heart,
but the only thing I can conclude is that
so far as this Government is concerned
there is no fair play for the minority.

The following Amendment stood in the
name of Colonel Pakenham:
NEW CLAUSE (Means of escape from
fire in hotels and boarding-houses.)
(1) Every building exceeding twentyfive feet in height used or intended to
be used as an hotel or boarding-house
shall be provided, on all the storeys of
which the upper surface of the floor is
higher than twenty feet above the level
of the street or road, with such means
of escape in case of fire for the persons
dwelling or employed in, or resorting
to, such building as may be required
in the circumstances of the case by the
sanitary authority of the urban or rural
district in which the building is situate.
Pakenham, Col H. A.
Porter, Mr. John Porter
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
A new building shall not be occupied
for use as aforesaid until the sanitary
authority have issued a certificate that
the provisions of this section have been
complied with in relation to such building; and for the purposes of this
provision the conversion into an hotel
or boarding-house of any building not
originally constructed for such purpose
shall be deemed to be the erection of
a new building.
(2) The provisions of this section
shall not apply to any building used at
the passing of this Act as an hotel or
boarding-house, unless and until the
sanitary authority shall have given to
the owner or occupier of such building
not less than three months' notice of
the provisions of this section, and shall
have specified their requirements thereunder in relation to the building.
Such owner or occupier shall comply
with those requirements within the said
period of three months, or within such
extended period as may be allowed by
the sanitary authority, or by the county
court upon any appeal under this
section.
(3) An person aggrieved by any
requirement of the sanitary authority,
or by the withholding of any certificate of a sanitary authority, under this
section in respect of any building may
appeal to the county court having jurisdiction where the building concerned
is situate, and the decision of that
court shall be final and conclusive.
Rules of court regulating the procedure
and costs of an appeal to the county
court under this sub-section shall be
made by the authority having power
to make rules and orders for regulating
the practice of the civil bill courts.
Local Government Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
(4) Any person who contravenes any
provision of this section, or fails to
comply with any requirement made by
the sanitary authority thereunder, shall
be guilty of an offence under the Public
Health (Ireland) Act, 1878, and shall
be liable on summary conviction for
every such offence to a penalty not exceeding five pounds for every day
during which the offence is continued.
(5) The provisions of this section
shall not apply in any urban sanitary
district in which there is in force any
enactment of a local Act requiring
means of escape from fire to be provided
in the case of hotels and boardinghouses.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I apologise to
the Committee for the length of this
Amendment, but as the subject matter is
practically new legislation it had to be
put down at length. There is a great
necessity that we should provide for the
safety as well as for the comfort of the
people who stay at our hotels. I conceive that this Amendment would cover
the case and would make people who
come here feel more secure owing to the
fact that in case of fire there would be
proper means of escape. I do not think
I need say any more, as the Committee
is well informed of the necessity for such
a provision.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government is prepared to accept the Amendment. I would like to express my
indebtedness to the hon. and gallant
Senator for drawing the attention of the
Government to a very much needed
reform.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I am much
obliged.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 29 (Investment of rural
authority with urban powers) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 30 (Licensing of places for
public entertainment).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out the Clause.
This is really, it seems to me, the first
serious difficulty we have encountered this
afternoon. I am moving to leave out the
Clause, the whole of three pages, more or
less, and the reason that I am doing so
is that I want to appeal to the Government to take the Clause away and bring
it up again as a separate Bill at some
time when circumstances permit of its
being adequately considered. I do not
think we have time between to-day and
to-morrow week-rather less than thatto consider the Clause adequately. I
must tell the Committee why.
In the first place, the Clause comes to
us, so to speak, entirely without any introduction whatever. Nothing in detail was
told us on the Second Reading. We do
not know why it is proposed to bring the
Clause in at all. We do not know what
it is proposed to do. We are given no
reason to suppose that if it is proposed to
do something that requires doing it will
be actually facilitated by imposing a 5s
licence fee. We are not given any reason
for changing the authorities as regards
one part of the Clause, and for giving
new powers to a rural district council in
another part of the Clause. All those, I
suggest, are something in the nature of
questions of principle.
There is yet another one. The Clause
is to take the place of Section 51 of the
older Act, the Public Health Amendment Act of 1890. This Clause, then, is
to take the place of that section, which
means that in three months it will cease
to have effect if the Clause goes through.
That Section 51 started in life, so to
speak, with a kind of preface at the head
of it. It was to the effect "That for the
purpose of regulating houses, gardens,
places, and that sort of thing, ordinarily
kept or used for such and such purposes,
etc." Therefore, that section was
limited in its operation, where it was
adopted, to places ordinarily used for
those purposes. The word "ordinarily"
does not appear in the Clause before us,
and it seems to me, though speaking with
some hesitation perhaps, that the fact
that by making so serious a departure
from the section of the Act which it is
proposed to displace, we land ourselves
into appalling difficulties as to how far
numerous decisions on words and phrases
in that Act will stand now that we are
starting off on fresh lines.
It seems to me it is really a question of
principle rather than a question of detail
whether the Clause, as it appears before
us, is intended to apply to a solitary act
of user during the year or whether it is
only intended to apply to the places
Local Government Bill-
6 NOVEMBER 1934
Committee
indicated, which were ordinarily kept and
used for those purposes. If it is to apply
to individual cases one wonders whether
it has been thought exactly how far it
appears to go. I think I mentioned
ex-Servicemen's huts the other day.
Those are houses or halls where occasional
dances are held. I do not think the
section of the Act of 1890 could possibly
have been intended to apply to ex-Service
men's huts, because the Great War had
not then taken place; and I do not think
the section was ever intended to apply to
the Orange hall or any other sort of
country hall where dances are very
occasionally held. I do not think it was
meant to apply to those. But I do think
that the Clause, as it is drawn, is wide
enough to catch a church or place of
worship, because churches or places of
worship are undoubtedly used for singing.
I am not speaking of singing during the
ordinary church services, but it is quite
ordinary and customary to have cantatas
and things of that sort in churches, and it
is equally customary if not to take money
at the door to have collections. In fact,
they sometimes go so far as to say in the
advertisement that there will be a silver
collection. I do say that a cantata in a
church is a public entertainment, though
I do not suggest for a moment that this
Clause is intended to apply to it.
Again looking at the wording of the
Clause--and we have to look at it now,
because, as I say, this is in fact the
Second Reading of this Clause. If we
decide that it is to stand we cannot have
a
Committee and Report stage on it
before the time when it will be necessary
to send it back to another place for reconsideration. We have no time to go
through it with a comb. Let us look for
a minute at one or two of the words.
"shall not be kept or used for public
dancing..."
Without the guidance
guidance of previous
decisions to help me I do not know what
is meant by public dancing. Does it
mean dancing in which members of the
public participate, does it mean exhibition dances, or is it intended to cover
both? Again the question arises as to
how you are to construe the words
"or other public entertainment of the
kind"
immediately following public
singing, music, boxing. In
dancing,
the old
Section public dancing, singing, and
music went together so to speak, and
they do go together because some form
of musical accompaniment is common to
all of them. But immediately we come
to boxing we get into difficulties. I am
not saying a boxing hall should not be
regulated just as anything else, but I
find a difficulty about doing it here.
Going on to further details, if the
licensing authority is to be the urban district council or the rural district council
what are you gong to do with a town
hall belonging to an urban district in
which dances and so on are undoubtedly
held, or which is unquestionably used for
purposes of the kind indicated here?
Apparently the urban district council
which is in control of the town hall must
apply to itself for a licence to use its own
town hall. That really seems to me to be
rather absurd. Again, in the event of
there being any difficulty about the
granting of a licence, the licensing
authority will apparently require two
meetings before they can settle the
question. I think you will find that on
page 33.
of
new
some
reasons
Now I want to come back to the
question--again a question both of
principle and of detail of whether
the rural district council is the proper
authority to entrust with the licensing
halls, of whatever class, intended
for any purpose whatever. I cannot
say that I think they are. If
we must have a
licensing
authority I should for
prefer the county council, but I do not
like to place the responsibility upon the
county council because, for one reason,
they have more work than they can do
already, and for another they have the
same fault in one respect as the rural
district council, that is to say that their
area may be too big for them to have the
local knowledge of each particular place.
I do not think either of those authorities would be a satisfactory one.
Now let us switch back to what it is
proposed to do. Why do you want to
Is it to
regulate these places at all?
deal with such matters as providing fire
escapes such as my hon and gallant
Friend (Colonel Pakenham) has just
mentioned. If it is that everybody will
be disposed to help. Is it to provide for
K
Local Government Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
additional exits, or in order that there
shall be so many feet between the rows
of seats or is it-and, oh, I wish it
were-to provide for reasonable softness
in the seats of the chairs on which one
has to sit on these occasions? Is it for
that sort of thing? Is something connected with the physical aspect of the
place to be regulated, or do you want to
go further and wish the authority to
say, "You may have dancing in this hall,
but not on certain specified dates, and
they are not to go on after ten or twelve
o'clock as the case may be?" Is that
what you want to do? What exactly do
you want to do? That is what we have
not been told at all. In any case, if it is
the moral side of the thing as opposed to
the physical side that you wish to regulate and to control, still less do I think
that the rural district council is the suitable authority. You may get in some
parts of the country a very stiff rural
district council which would not let you
have dances at all, or only under conditions which would not be popular with
the people who wanted to dance.
another part you might get one far too
lax whose regulations would not really
be of any value to anyone.
Further,
there is a proposal that the authority
may delegate its powers to a committee
which may have all the bias of the parent
body.
In
In the end and to sum up, the Clause
is not apparently to come into operation
for three months after the passing of
this Act. It seems to me that that fact
alone is sufficient to justify me in asking
the right hon. Gentleman and the Government to take the Clause back and to
bring it up next Session in the form of
a separate Bill, when we can consider it
both in principle and in detail. I do
not wish to be understood as having any
rooted hostility to do anything that may
be proved or shown to be necessary, but
I do say that nothing has yet been so
shown. I therefore beg to move that the
Clause be left out.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I was rather
shocked at the wholesale indictment of
the rural district councils by the hon. and
learned Senator. He seems to think that
we are adopting an entirely new principle. I would like to tell him what the
practice is in Belfast to-day. In regard
to practically all buildings used for
entertainment purposes the owners have
to get a licence or a permit from the local
magistrates, and that involves a certain
amount of expense. It is perfectly true
that those buildings may be used only
five or six times in the year for that particular purpose. There is one hall, which
hon. Members on the back bench
opposite know very well, down in York
Street. The owners of that hall take out
a licence every year, and I have yet to
learn that it has caused the slightest inconvenience to them. In addition,
before a building can be licensed it must
be shown that it is properly conducted,
that the entertainments to be given in it
are of a decent character, and that precautions are taken for the safety of the
public using it.
But, unfortunately, while this is so in
the urban areas and in Belfast, in the
country districts anybody can use any
building, no matter how dangerous it
may be from the point of view of fire or
of its having no proper exit, without control from anybody. That is the position.
in which we find ourselves to-day, and I
do not think it is right. What this
Clause proposes to do is to take away the
power from the magistrates and to give
power to the local authorities, either the
urban district council or the rural district council, to issue licences to the
owners of these places if they wish to
use them for the purpose of entertainments. The hon. and learned Senator
foreshadows that these rural councils will
be either what we might call very slow or
very fast, and the result is that he thinks
injustice will be done to the promoters.
of these entertainments. But I would
remind the Committee that there is an
appeal to the county court judge against
the refusal of the local authority to grant
an entertainment licence.
The hon. and learned Senator says
again that he disapproves of the principle of local authorities issuing these
licences. I might point out that under
the Cinematograph Act the local authorities issue the licences, and I have never
known a case where they have done their
duty improperly. As far as I know
they carry out the duty excellently, and
I do not anticipate the slightest danger
of the local authorities failing to do their
duty in this very important matter.
After all, I have seen in the country
Local Government Bill-
Committee
6 NOVEMBER 1934
small halls, very often built of wood,
with only one exit, lighted by oil lamps,
crowded to the doors, and I have often
asked myself what would happen if a fire
occurred in such a place. Under the
Clause no hardship is done to anybody,
but if the public pay for entertainment
they ought to be protected from any
danger to themselves while they are at
that entertainment.
As regards the suggestion that a church
will have to apply to the local authority
for a licence, I do not really think the
hon. and learned Senator was serious in
that. I am quite certain that if a local
authority were so foolish as to impose a
licence, or to prosecute a church for not
having a licence to enable its parishioners
to go to hear their minister preach, the
county court judge would reverse that
decision.
There is just one other point to which
my attention has been drawn. In certain parts of Belfast very undesirable
entertainments take place, low-class
boxing entertainments, over which
there is absolutely no control, and which
very often result in rows. This will not
happen in future, because these houses
of entertainment will have to secure a
licence. Up to the present time boxing
has not been included as an entertainment. I hope the Committee will realise
that this Clause has been inserted very
largely at the request of the local
authorities themselves and will, therefore, reject the Amendment moved by
the hon. and learned Senator.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
hope the right hon. Gentleman does not
make reference to those boxing entertainments on Sundays as being typical
of the entertainments that take place
in the hall in York Street to which he
referred.
Sir DAWSON BATES: No. I pointed
out that, as the law stands at present,
boxing is not regarded as an entertainment, and, therefore, the magistrates have
no control over boxing in any place either
on weekdays or Sundays. I want to
make that clear. The effect of this
Clause will be to bring boxing in as an
entertainment in regard to which a hall
must be licensed. I was not referring
to the hon. Member's premises at all.
The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon.
Member press the Amendment?
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry but I am
afraid I cannot withdraw the Amendment. The right hon. Gentleman
referred to Belfast and said he wanted
to change the method of licensing in
Belfast. We understood on the Second
Reading that another hon. Senator, the
Lord Mayor of Belfast (Sir Crawford
McCullagh), was quite satisfied with the
licensing arrangements in Belfast. I
think that was what he said. The right
hon. Gentleman spoke of a hall in York
Street which is used perhaps five or six
times a year.
Mr. JOSEPH
Every Sunday.
CUNNINGHAM :
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman says five or six times a year and my
hon. Friend on my left says every Sunday. That does not quite come into my
argument. The right hon. Gentleman
also spoke about the necessity for regulations imposing decency and safety. I am
entirely with him. I quite agree that all
these things should be regulated in this
way but he did not answer my point
as to whether one entertainment in the
year would require a licence. When he
came to speak of the churches he said he
could not doubt for a minute that if the
churches appealed to the county court
judge they would, to put it briefly, get
away with it.
But that is not quite the
point. The point is the same in the case
of the churches, and the question of
whether it is lawful or not arises afterwards.
My
Again the right hon. Gentleman rather
suggested, I think, that you could not
have a public entertainment within the
meaning of the Clause unless people
paid to go in. That is a most excellent
example of one of my difficulties.
idea of a public entertainment is an
entertainment to which the public are
admitted, whether on payment or otherwise, and I want to know in what sense
you are using the words in the Clause
before us, because, as I have said before,
if
you alter the mainstay, the foundation,
I do not quite see what is left.
Again, the right hon. Gentleman spoke
of appeals to the court. I do not observe
that there is any power of appeal to the
court against the conditions inserted on
the licence. Finally, he wound up by
Local Government Bill-
Committee

[Mr. Leslie]
suggesting he did not actually say sothat such a state of things existed that
he could not tolerate it any longer. That
is the general purport of what he said,
but he is going to tolerate it for three
months. If that is so, why cannot the
right hon. Gentleman let us have a
separate Bill, with time to discuss it,
The Lord Mayor of Belfast.
Barclay, Mr. William
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Cunningham, The Right Honourable Samuel
I may
dealing with the whole matter?
be, like Athanasius, standing all by

CLAUSE 31 (Extension of power of

CLAUSE 32 (Notification and disinfection in cases of tuberculosis).
(1) Part I of the Tuberculosis Prevention (Ireland) Act, 1908, shall
after the commencement of this Act
extend to and take effect in every
urban and rural sanitary district in
Northern Ireland for which it has not
been adopted, as if it had been so
adopted, and accordingly in sub-section
(1) of section one of the said Act for
the words "district to which this Part
of this Act extends" there shall be
substituted the words "urban or rural
sanitary district." Section three of
the said Act shall cease to have effect,
but without prejudice to the continuance in force of the said Part I in any
sanitary district for which it has been
adopted before the commencement of
this Act.
(2) In sub-section (2) of section one
of the said Act the words "after consulting with the President of the
Royal College of Physicians in Ireland.
and the President of the Royal College
of Surgeons in Ireland" shall be
omitted.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I beg to move,
in page 35, line 21, at the end to add:
Pakenham, Col H. A.
Porter, Mr. John Porter
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
"and the words 'after consulting with
the Medical Faculty of the Queen's
University of Belfast' shall be inserted
in lieu thereof."
If we go back to the Tuberculosis Prevention Act of 1908, which was passed
when Ireland was a single legislative unit,
we find that the consultative work
devolved upon two eminent medical
authorities the president of the Royal
College of Physicians and the president of
the Royal College of Surgeons. Those
two Royal Colleges are situated in
Dublin, and for the most part the
presidents are recruited from the profession in Dublin. A noted exception
was my late colleague, Professor Fullerton. That being so, and this being a Bill
for Northern Ireland, it would seem to
be more appropriate and convenient that
the Medical Faculty of Queen's University
should replace the two eminent presidents
to whom I have referred, for the purpose
of advising the authorities here.
might contemplate two members of the
professoriate of Queen's University discharging the duties which hitherto
devolved upon the two presidents of the
Royal Colleges. The president of the
Ulster Medical Society varies each yearhe is elected annually-and, therefore, it
seems better to have the Medical Faculty
of the University, which includes not
merely the professors of Queen's
University, but also the representatives
of the staffs of the tuberculosis hospitals
under the control of Belfast Corporation
and of other hospitals, who serve in the
University as lecturers. If it be con-
We
Local Government Bill-
Committee
6 NOVEMBER 1931
numerous
a
It
tended that the Medical Faculty is too
or cumbrous for consultative
purposes, I would point out that that body
is presided over by Dean, who,
fortunately, happens to be the Professor
of Public Health and Sanitation.
would not be necessary to call together the
large body. The Dean could consult a
particular colleague by telephone or by a
short communication at any time, and a
prompt decision could be obtained by the
Government in that way.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I think the
suggestion made by the hon. Senator is
a most excellent one, and I am prepared
to recommend that the Committee should
accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 33 (Powers of boards of
guardians as to infirmaries and district
hospitals).
*
*
*
*
*
*
(3) A board of guardians, with the
consent of the Ministry of Home
Affairs, may resolve to establish a district hospital, and for that purpose
may, with the like consent, appoint a
committee of governors which shall be
constituted in the manner provided by
sub-section (2) of this section. The
committee shall have, for the purpose
of enabling them to provide a district
hospital, the powers conferred on a
board of guardians by sub-section (1)
of this section with respect to the
erection of a workhouse infirmary;
Provided that the powers specified in
paragraphs (b) and (c) of the said subsection (1) shall, in relation to the provision of a district hospital, be vested
in and exercised by the board of
guardians and not by the committee.
(4) Subject to the regulations of the
Ministry of Home Affairs and to the
powers of that Ministry with respect
to guardians and their officers, the
guardians acting through the said committee, shall properly manage and
maintain the district hospital established under this section, and may
appoint and remove officers, and regulate expenditure, and may receive and
apply for the benefit of such district
hospital any endowments or subscriptions given by private persons for
that purpose.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
36, after line 30, to insert as a new subsection:
"(4) A board of guardians when
appointing a committee under this
section shall so provide that such committee shall include at least three
women members."
This Amendment is one of those on
which it is possible to take two divergent
views, although starting from the same.
standpoint. It seems to me that everyone will agree that a committee of a board
of guardians running a district hospital
should include a certain proportion of
lady members. Women have been members of boards of guardians for a good
many years now, and those of us who
have served on boards of guardians will,
I think, admit that they have been extremely useful members. Their advice
and assistance is of very great value, and
I think we ought to make sure that in
any district hospital committee there
should be a proportion of ladies. The
proposal in the Bill is that the committee
should consist of not less than fifteen
members, and I am suggesting that in
appointing those fifteen, ten of whom, I
think, are to be members of the board of
guardians and five to be outsiders, the
board should arrange for the inclusion of
at least three women. I think that that
ought to be done, and that is what I suggest should be done. That is one point
of view.
The other point of view is that if we
do not put anything in the Bill we are
not doing anything to stop it being done,
and it may be thought that in this Amendment we are interfering unduly with the
discretion of the boards of guardians. On
the whole, after weighing both views, I
have come to the conclusion that, it is
so overwhelmingly important to secure
the services of ladies on these boards, that
we ought to do it in this way, and insert
something definite in the Bill.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
for one admit that women members of
boards and committees contribute very
valuable services, but there are some
Local Government Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
boards that are not a bit fond of women.
I would suggest, therefore, that this
should be left out, and that we should
leave this to the boards of guardians to
select more than three or none at all.
Sir DAWSON BATES: I hope that
my hon. and learned Friend will not
press this Amendment. I think it is
capable of being taken as a suggestion
that boards of guardians do not want
women on these committees. After all,
you cannot force men to be gallant, and
you cannot compel electors to return
women if they do not want to do so. The
boards of guardians are elected by the
people, and many of the members of the
boards are women. I have yet to learn
that boards of guardians as a whole have
shown any reluctance to appoint ladies to
these district committees. I think it
might be taken as rather insulting to the
boards of guardians, who are doing such
good work at the present time in connection with these hospitals, to say that
they must appoint three women. I think
we must take the circumstances as we find
them. Some boards do not include any
women at all, and it might not always be
possible to obtain the services of three
suitable ladies who would be prepared to
act on the committee.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not believe that
any board of guardians would have any
difficulty in finding three women, even
if they are not elected to the boards,
but in view of what the Minister has
said I do not like to press the Amendment, although I hold an opposite view.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
""
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in subclause (4), after "any" ("any endowments") to insert "gifts. I think it
Iwould be desirable to insert the word
"gifts" here, because it generally does.
connote something different from either
endowments or subscriptions. I do not
think I need elaborate that.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government are prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

Where the medical officer of a dispensary district has been called to the
assistance of a midwife in a case of
emergency within the meaning of
section twenty-two of the Midwives
(Ireland) Act, 1918, as amended by
section two of the Midwives and
Local Government Bill-
Committee
6 NOVEMBER 1934
Nursing Homes Act (Northern Ireland), 1929, then notwithstanding
that medical relief under section nine
of the Poor Relief (Ireland), Act,
1851, has not been applied for by the

that medical officer under the said
section twenty-two if the board of
guardians of the union in which the
case has arisen are satisfied that the
patient is a person to whom such medical relief might lawfully have been
afforded.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, after
"1929" to insert, "arising in his district." I think this Amendment explains itself. There is no reason why a
doctor called in in an emergency should
be worse treated than any other doctor
merely because he is a dispensary doctor
called into a neighbouring and not his
own district.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government are prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

*
*
re-
(b) the member concerned has
ceived fourteen clear days' notice
of the resolution proposed to be
brought forward for his removal
from office; and
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, after
paragraph 1 (b), to insert:
(c) the resolution is unanimous or, if
the question is put to the vote is
carried by a majority comprising
at least two-thirds of the members
present and voting.
This Clause, as you know, has given
rise to some discussion, and it is generally
accepted as being very necessary. The
only point is whether any further protection for the minority is desired. On
the whole, I came to the conclusion that
we ought to ensure that even if the body,
whatever it is, is not quite unanimous
about the suppression, let us say, of a
singularly troublesome member, we
ought to be sure there is a thoroughly
strong majority in favour of the necessary action. There are precedents with
which I need not detain the committee.
I therefore suggest that the majority
must be one comprising at least two-thirds
of the members present and voting, that
is to say, if there are 15 people there
would have to be 10 voting in the
majority.
Sir DAWSON BATES: The Government are prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

THE SENATE.
Thursday, 8th November, 1334.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented.

Questions to Minister.
carry
