I
LOSSES FROM WARBLE FLY.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether he can state the loss to the
country caused by warble fly, and
whether the Government will
measures to reduce the serious loss.
ask this question because I noted that
the Leather Sellers' Hall had recommended some work to be done. They
seemed to have a remedy for these warble
flies, and as far as I can learn it would
cost a very small amount to
it
out. The Ministry of Agriculture at
the annual show at Balmoral have always
exhibited a skin showing the injury done
by the warble fly. I was hoping the
Ministry of Agriculture might promote
a Bill by which they would make it compulsory to eradicate these flies. That
could be done at a very little cost. They
might have to provide a drug with which
to rub the back of the beast, but so far
as I can see it would be a very small
item, and I think it would soon eradicate this curse in the country. It would
not be a new principle, because already
we have compulsory dipping of sheep and
other compulsory remedies for similar
diseases. I hope the Minister of Agriculture will consider taking some steps
to do away with this trouble.
Mr. McHUGH: This question that has
been raised by the hon. Senator has been
under the observation of farmers for a
large number of years. During the time
of the British régime in this country they
issued pamphlets on the subject, but that
is the distance they went. I do not know
whether this question will cover a very
important matter, and that is, that this
remedy which they suggest should be
supplied to farmers. If you take a rural
district you will find the people will know
practically nothing about it. Unfortunately the farmers have no opportunity
of knowing, and they will not go to the
trouble of writing to the Ministry of
Agriculture for these pamphlets on the
subject. I think until the Governn ent
take the matter in hands and are prepared to supply the remedy there will
not be much use in making any aggestion.
The point is if you compel the farmers
to do this, they will complain that it is
another penalty, because when they put
the animal on the market they are never
asked whether it has been affected with
warble fly or not. Therefore, I think if
the Government intend to do anything
they should at least see that this remedy
they suggest is stocked in the local districts, because if they have to write to
Belfast or elsewhere these people will not
bother. I do admit that cattle badly
affected will be prevented from thriving.
It is throughout the winter and the spring
season they are so affected. I would
suggest that the Government should see
that this serum is stocked in chemists'
shops all over the country so as to give
farmers an opportunity of getting it. It
would be much better if the Government
would give free samples to farmers. That
is what I suggest.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I am very
glad the hon. Senator has mentioned this
matter about the warble fly, because it
costs the country millions of money. I
think the only remedy for it would be
to make this compulsory, the same as
sheep dipping. In connection with
Senator McHugh's remark, the dipping
of sheep is compulsory and the dip they
use is not supplied free for that purpose.
The cure used for this warble fly can be
had in any chemist's. The only remedy
for doing away with the trouble would be
making it compulsory. It would benefit.
the farmers and benefit the community
generally by saving millions of money.
Mr. McHUGH: I can assure you
is not stocked in rural districts.
it
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
8 NOVEMBER 1934
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I think it can
be got in any chemist's shop.
Mr. LONG: It has been estimated that
in Great Britain the losses from warble
fly pest in respect of damage to the hides
and condition of cattle amount to about
£1,000,000 annually. Having regard to
the comparative cattle population of
Great Britain and Northern Ireland, it
may be assumed that the annual damage
caused by the pest in Northern Ireland
approximates to £100,000.
Experimental work has shown that
there is an inexpensive wash available
which can readily be applied by any
farmer and which effectively destroys the
pest without damaging the skin.
The
Ministry of Agriculture, in conjunction.
with county committees of agriculture,
has during the past six years carried
out a large number of demonstrations
with this wash throughout Northern
Ireland, and these demonstrations have
been uniformly successful.
A leaflet in regard to the warble fly
has also been published by the Ministry
and circulated to the farming community.
through the usual channels.
This contains full information as to the nature
and use of the wash to which I have referred. The Ministry is continuing to
urge all farmers to apply this treatment
annually to their cattle.
For the further information of the
right hon. Senator I am sending him a
copy of the leaflet.
In regard to Senator McHugh's
question, I understand, like Senator
Hill Dickson, that most chemists stock
this derris root. With regard to the
further suggestion that it should be compulsory as sheep dipping is, I will convey
that suggestion to the Ministry of Agriculture.
Mr. BARCLAY: Arising out of that
reply, I would like to know in what
season of the year this cure would be
applied to cattle.
Mr. LONG: Spring.
Mr. BARCLAY: It is in the summer
season that this fly inserts the egg into
the skin, and it is concealed until the
spring of the year until the warble flies
begin to grow in the animal's back. Is
it possible to have the liquid put on the
cow's back, or the young bullock's or
heifer's during the summer season while
they are grazing to prevent the fly inserting the egg into the skin? I cannot
see how this cure affects the skin if
the egg comes to maturity. The hole
will still be in the hide even if you apply
this liquid. That is my idea.
AMALGAMATION OF ROMAN
CATHOLIC SCHOOLS.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House if his attention has
been drawn to the resolution passed
unanimously at the meetings held recently
of the Catholic managers of schools in
the dioceses of Down and Connor, at
Downpatrick, Kilkeel, and Ballymena,
protesting most strongly against the
amalgamation of Catholic girls' and boys'
schools; and whether, in view of this, it
is the intention of the Government to
proceed with this policy.
For the information of the House I
should like to read the resolution
referred to and which was passed unanimously by the Catholic managers of
schools in the Dioceses of Down and
Connor:
In view of the recent action of the
Ministry of Education of Northern
Ireland in amalgamating and attempting to amalgamate separate Catholic
boys' and girls' schools, we, the
Catholic managers of schools in this
area, protest against and will oppose by
every means in our power all such
attempts to amalgamate our Catholic
boys' and Catholic girls' schools.
I sincerely trust that this resolution.
will receive the sympathetic consideration
of the Ministry of Education. The
amalgamation of boys' and girls' schools
has been condemned by his Holiness Pope
Pius XI and by the medical profession
generally. Since the Ministry of Education for the Six Counties was set up, I
understand this question of the amalgamation of Catholic boys' and girls'
schools has caused a great deal of trouble
and uneasiness, as the tone of this
resolution shows. The Catholic people
of the Six Counties are justly proud of
the management of their schools by their
clergy. They Owe them everlasting
gratitude for the splendid way they have
Oral Answers
Adjournment
THE SENATF
[Mr. McLaughlin]
managed under serious difficulties and in
many serious crises, and everyone agrees
that through their unselfish and devoted
services the Catholic schools have been
most successful.
The bishops, priests, and managers
have the entire and complete confidence
of the parents of the children attending
the Catholic schools under your Ministry,
and they are in entire accord with the
terms of the resolution. Moreover, they
are determined that any action the
managers of their Catholic schools may
decide to take, should the Ministry persist in their
amalgamate
efforts to
Catholic boys' and girls' schools, will
have their entire and whole-hearted
support. I sincerely trust that the
Government will give the resolution
their sympathetic consideration.
Mr. LONG: The resolution referred
to has been received in the Ministry of
Education.
The rule for the amalgamation of boys'
and girls' schools in close proximity or
under the same roof is Article 13 of
Statutory Rules and Orders No. 41 of
1934, which reads as follows:
Grants to separate schools for boys
and girls or for infants and senior
pupils may be withdrawn if, in the
opinion of the Ministry, these schools.
can be more efficiently or more
economically conducted as one establishment; provided always that where
the average daily attendance of pupils
at one of the schools for the preceding
calendar year has been less than 50
amalgamation shall take place on the
occurrence of a vacancy in the staff of
either school, unless for special
reasons it shall be otherwise directed
by the Ministry.
This rule was framed in the interests,
first of efficiency and secondly, of
economy. In the Code of the Commissioners of National Education amalgamation was applied to separate boys' and
girls' schools with an average attendance
of under 30. In the report of the Viceregal Committee of Inquiry into Primary
Education (Ireland), 1918-the "Killanin
Report"-it was stated that the policy of
amalgamating small schools should be
pressed by the Commissioners; and that
the figure of 30 pupils for separate boys'
and girls' schools should be raised to 35.
I would call the attention of the hon.
Member to the fact that this report was
signed without reservation by the late
Cardinal O'Donnell and a Roman
Catholic clerical representative on
committee.
the
The Ministry believe that the policy
might be pressed with advantage and
that no hardship results from the application of the present regulation. I
would add that the Regulation, of course,
applies equally to schools of all denominations, and not solely to those under
Roman Catholic management.
Local Government Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Resolved, "That this Report be now
received."(Mr. Long).
The said Amendments were accordingly reported and agreed to by the
House.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the
Third time to-morrow.
Adjournment.
Resolved: "That this House do now
adjourn." (Mr. Long).
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty-five minutes before
Four o'clock.
Local Government Bill-
Third Reading
9 NOVEMBER 1934
THE SENATE.

Friday, 9th November, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Local Government Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read the Third time."
Mr. LESLIE: I think it will be agreed
that the Bill we are about to part with is,
taking it altogether, a very good Bill
indeed, and it is likely to effect a good
many of the purposes with which it apparently purports to deal. But I cannot but
regret that we did not find it possible to
do anything to meet the grievance put
forward by the Association of County
Councils in the matter of surcharges. I
must not dwell upon that to-day, but I
might, perhaps, express the hope that it
may be found possible at a future date to
do something to meet some of their wishes,
without, at the same time, doing violence
to those principles to which the hon.
Senator on my left and others alluded.
Passing from that, which is not in the
Bill, to something which is, I still maintain my regret that the Committee
declined to suspend operations as regards
Clause 30, dealing with halls used for
music, dancing, and so on, until a full
and more comprehensive Measure
was
before them. I am afraid the Measure,
as we have passed it with that Clause in,
will awaken a good deal of unfavourable
criticism when the working of the Clause
becomes apparent. It will be said, and
I think it will be said with justice, that
the Clause is one which will give rise to
irritation and annoyance, and will show
that there has been rather partial discrimination between one class of entertainment and another. I think the public as
a whole would have preferred a more farreaching Measure which would have dealt
with all kinds of halls used for public
entertainments, and which would have
enabled similar regulations to be introduced as respects that class of hall left
outside the ambit of that particular
Clause, and they would have liked something done which would have connected
the New Clause with another Clause in
the Act of 1890, a Clause originally
intended for adoption by urban areas, but
which dealt with all kinds of halls, and
specifically included churches, as I am
sure the House will remember. I think
we should have found, if we had dealt
with the matter on those lines, that we
should have produced something more
useful and something more enduring.
Passing from that, I think, perhaps,
the two Clauses in the Bill which will be
received with the most general approbation are the extension Clauses. There is
the Clause permitting a local authority to
use its waterworks for the assistance of
another district, not necessarily, as before,
an adjoining district. I fancy that that
will be found of very great use in the
future, and I think it will be widely
appreciated. There is also the new provision as regards poor relief, which did
not attract much attention in this House,
but which will be received with universal
gratification. Taking the Bill as a whole,
I think it will be recognised as a determined, and not wholly unsuccessful effort
to deal with many matters which did
require attention.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Mr. Long).
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill with
some Amendments, to which they desire
their concurrence.
Motion for Adjournment.
SUGGESTED TAX ON BICYCLES.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn until Wednesday
next."
Mr. MCLAUGHLIN : Would the
Deputy-Leader tell us the order of
business for the next sitting day?
Mr. LONG: We propose to meet at
12-5 p.m. on Wednesday next for the
Royal Assent.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to draw the
attention of the House to a practice which
has been carried on during the summer
and autumn by pedal cyclists when they
Adjournment-
Tax on Bicycles
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
leave the city of Belfast and get out a
few miles outside the city. A great
number of the bicycles they are riding
do not come up to the proper standard,
and when a policeman pulls them up he
finds their brakes are inefficient, perhaps
there is no bell, and perhaps their tail
light is not in very good working order.
The policeman asks the cyclists for their
names and addresses, and a great many of
them have given wrong names and
addresses in this particular district. I
am speaking of the Carrickfergus district,
and I am informed that between fifty and
a hundred wrong names and addresses
have been given to the police in that district during the late season. We can
realise the time that is wasted by a policeman taking a name and address, writing
out a report, then writing to the district
in the city of Belfast in which the address
of the cyclist was supposed to be, and the
time wasted by a policeman in that
district in making inquiries for the
address, and at the end of the day being
unable to find any such address. I raise
this question to see if anything can be
done to prevent this waste of policemen's
time, and also to see if it would not be
possible to make those persons who hire
out bicycles for 6d or 1s an hour responsible for any deficiencies in the
bicycle.
I have been talking to one or two
friends regarding this question, and we
have come to the conclusion that the only
way to remedy the present state of affairs
would be to licence all pedal cyclists, to
impose a licence fee of 2s 6d or 5s a year,
and to compel cyclists to have a disc placed
on their bicycles with their name and
address and licence number. That is one
way in which a policeman could find out
who the real owner of a bicycle was. It
would also prevent the police wasting their
time as they have been doing during the
past summer.
There is another objectionable practice
on the part of pedal cyclists that I would
like to mention. It is against the law
for three cyclists to ride abreast on the
road, but in some cases the police are
helpless. Cases occur in which three
cyclists do not actually ride abreast,
but they take up as much space
as if they were riding abreast. Two
of them ride abreast, and the third rides
with his front wheel just at the back
wheels of the two 'cyclists in front. They
thus take up a great deal of the road,
and they make it dangerous for motorcars. A policeman is powerless to interfere, because he cannot say they are
actually riding three abreast, although
they are taking up the same space.
think these pedal cyclists should be confined to a specific number of feet, six or
seven, from the curb where there is a
footpath. I raise this question in the
hope that some action will be taken in the
matter.
I
Mr. McMAHON : There are some
remarks made by Senator Barclay with
which I cannot agree. I quite agree that
it is a very dangerous practice for pedal
cyclists to ride three abreast, but I cannot
agree with him when he says that bicycles
on the road at the present time are inefficient. Very few people to-day hire
bicycles. As a matter of fact, the bicycle
trade at the present time is a very
flourishing one. I understand you can
procure a new bicycle by paying a deposit
of a shilling, and paying off the balance
at the rate of a shilling a week. I look
upon the bicycle as the poor man's motorcar, and I am entirely opposed to imposing a licence on bicycles. Senator Barclay
suggested that a tax of 2s 6d or 5s a year
might be levied on cycles. He must
know, as everyone should know, that that
would not in any way cover the cost of
collecting the licence fee. When you consider how useful a bicycle is to the farmer
in enabling him to travel very long distances along the road at a very cheap
rate I think it would be a hardship to
impose a licence fee.
With regard to Senator Barclay's complaint about the police not being able
to get proper names and addresses, if I
was spoken to to-morrow on a bicycle I
know the policeman would get my name
and address, because the first thing he
would do would be to hold me there or
take me to the nearest police barracks
and look up the directory and find out if
I had given a correct name and address.
I really do not think the police are embarrassed in the way the hon. Senator
suggests. I think when you take into
consideration how useful the bicycle is,
not only to the people of the countryside
but to the people in the city, it should
not be taxed. In the city of Belfast there
is no ground to be had for building houses
inside the penny stage, and
any house
Adjournment-
Tax on Bicycles
9 NOVEMBER 1934
built at the present time is in the 2d
stage, and you will find that families of
four or five that have come to the city
have to use the bicycle as a cheap means of
transport to get to the centre of the city.
Without a bicycle in all probability these
people could not afford to pay the rents
they have got to pay and, in addition to
that, pay the tramfare. When you consider all these things I think the House
would be well advised not, under any
circumstances, to take into consideration
imposing a licence on bicycles.
Mr. LESLIE: The hon. Senator who
initiated the debate seemed to be troubled
mostly by the practice which, he alleged,
is going on of the people concerned in
riding bicycles in some way illegally,
usually probably without a lamp, giving
the wrong name and address to the policeman. Something in the nature of a very
small epidemic of that class of thing
arose in my own district not long ago.
The first occasion the offender was ultimately detected and brought before the
bench my colleagues and I intimated our
disapproval of the practice by imposing a
fine of 20s instead of the more normal
10s. We also said we would not tolerate
It
a continuance of the practice.
happened once more, and in the case of
that gentleman 40s was imposed. Since
that we have had no trouble. I venture
to suggest to other magistrates that if
they adopted that means the practice
would speedily come to an end.
I say
nothing about licensing bicycles. I know
that is a proposal favoured by Chancellors
of the Exchequer, but I do not think it
has met with public approval.
Mr. LONG: The question of putting a
tax on cycles is a very far-reaching
matter, as it would involve the question
of taxing all other non-mechanically propelled vehicles. The cycle is used mainly
by working men and children going to
school, and it will be agreed those are
not the classes of people, generally speaking, to stand a tax. The wear and tear
on roads caused by bicycles is negligible,
and it would, therefore, be hard to justify
a tax on the ground of paying towards
the upkeep of roads.
With regard to the suggestion of
Senator Barclay that a licence should be
imposed of 2s 6d or 5s, I wish to point
out that it would require legislation and
would have to be included in the Finance
Bill. I am inclined, however, to agree
with Senator McMahon that a very small
sum would accrue from this licence, and
also with him as to the hiring of bicycles
being negligible owing to the low first
cost at which a bicycle can be procured at
the present time. As to the false name
and address, this would give rise to complex legal issues, but I shall report the
remarks of Senator Barclay to the proper
quarter.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
to
Adjourned accordingly at

Four o'clock until Wednesday next at Five minutes
after Twelve noon.

The Senate met at five minutes after

Prorogation of Parliament.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have it in command from his Grace the Governor to
read forthwith in this Chamber to the
Senators and Commons of Northern
Ireland therein assembled his Grace's
Proclamation proroguing this present
Session.
Moved: "That the Gentleman Usher
of the Black Rod be commanded to inform the Commons that the Proclamation
proroguing the present Session of Parliament is to
to be read forthwith, and
inviting the Commons to attend in order
to hear the same."-(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
was
The Commons, being at the Bar, with
their Speaker, the Proclamation
read to both Houses of Parliament by
the Speaker of the Senate (in pursuance of his Grace's Command), as
followeth:
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
For Proroguing the present Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said Act,
that the Governor of Northern Ireland
shall, in his Majesty's name, summon,
prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of
Northern Ireland:
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that
this present Parliament of Northern
Ireland should be prorogued to the
twentieth day of November, one thousand
nine hundred and thirty-four:
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD.
DUKE OF ABERCORN, KNIGHT OF
THE MOST NOBLE ORDER OF THE
14 NOVEMBER 1934
Prorogation
Prorogation

the House of Commons of Northern Ireland are hereby required to give their
attendance accordingly.
GARTER, KNIGHT OF THE MOST
ILLUSTRIOUS ORDER OF SAINT
PATRICK, Governor of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation do publish
and declare his Majesty's Pleasure that
the said Parliament be, as from the
reading of this Proclamation in the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, prorogued to Tuesday, the twentieth day of
November, one thousand nine hundred
and thirty-four.
AND I do hereby further declare his
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be
that the said Parliament shall on the said
twentieth day of November, one thousand
nine hundred and thirty-four, assemble
and be holden at Stormont for the
despatch of divers urgent and important
affairs, and the Senators and Members of
Given this Fourteenth day of November,
one thousand nine hundred and thirtyfour.
(Signed by his own hand),
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the First Session (opened

Tuesday, 20th November, 1934.
The Second Session of the Fourth
Parliament of Northern Ireland appointed to meet at Stormont on 20th
November, 1934, in the Twenty-fifth
year of the Reign of King George V,
was opened by the Governor (His Grace
the Duke of Abercorn, K.G., K.P.) in
person.
The House met at Twelve of the Clock
noon, Mr. SPEAKER (the Right Hon.
Viscount Bangor, O.B.E., D.L.) in the
Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor, being seated
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is His Grace's
pleasure that they attend him immediately in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, His Grace delivered the
following Speech from the Throne:-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
In addressing you at the opening of
the Second Session of this Parliament
it is a matter of great satisfaction to
me to learn that, although there have
been fluctuations in the number of
persons out of work who are registered
the employment exchanges, the
improvement in employment which
began in 1933 has been sustained.
During the last twenty-one months the
Register has shown a rise in only six
of the months For fifteen of the
months a fall has been recorded, and
in October of this year the figure was
over 14,000 less than in January,
1933. During the twelve months that
ended in October, 1934, more than
26,000 persons have found employment through the agency of the employment exchanges. I trust that our
industries will continue to provide
substantial employment for our workers
during the coming years.
It is a matter of gratification to
know that active steps are being
taken to bring into operation the
comprehensive scheme of unemployment assistance that was authorised by
the Unemployment Act of 1934, and
I trust that the reforms which that
Measure will inaugurate will prove to
be of great benefit to the unemployed
people of Northern Ireland.
I am
also gratified that by an Act of last
Session legally adopted children have
been brought within the scope of the
Workmen's Compensation Code.
For a considerable time past the
serious situation in regard to transport
in Northern Ireland has been the subject of grave concern to my Ministers,
and proposals will be laid before you
A
King's Speech
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
designed with the object of securing |
the most efficient and economic use of
every form of transport.
A Measure will also be laid before
you dealing with the reform of the
laws relating to Summary Jurisdiction
and the administration of Criminal
Justice.
Legislation will be introduced for
the amendment of the Animal
Diseases Acts, to bring them into
conformity with the Milk ond Milk
Products Act of last Session and to
secure the inspection by State veterinary officers of all cows producing
milk.
You will be invited to consider further proposals for the better
organisation and development of the
pigs and bacon industry.
It
was found impracticable last
Session to consider proposals in regard
to charitable donations and bequests,
consequently a Bill dealing with this
matter will be submitted for your consideration.
A Measure will also be introduced
to amend the Derating Code, as certain provisions in the Local Government (Rating and Finance) Act,
1929, have become exhausted.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Estimates for the Public Services will be laid before you in due
course.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I am glad to learn that the schemes
formulated under the Agricultural
Marketing Acts for pigs and bacon are
now established. Full advantage has
been taken of the opportunities which
these provide for the development of
the pig industry, and it is most gratifying that the production of pigs in
Northern Ireland for bacon purposes
has increased since 1932 by over 73
per cent.
The Milk and Milk Products Act is
in process of being put into full
operation. That part of the Act
which secures to suppliers of milk to
our creameries a guaranteed average
price has brought a much needed
measure of relief to the farming community in the creamery districts.
It is a matter for satisfaction that
the efforts to attract visitors to Northern Ireland are meeting with everincreasing success. Apart from the
material advantages thus directly
accruing to our area as a whole, the
annual presence in our midst of so
large a number of our fellow countrymen from Great Britain cannot fail
to promote an even closer relationship
between the people of the United
Kingdom.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your deliberations.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Pigs Marketing Scheme (North-
Ireland) (Amendment) (No. 2)
Order, 1934.
ern
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Report of the Commission on Markets
and Market Tolls.
Presented (by Command) and ordered
to lie on the Table.
ADDRESS IN REPLY TO HIS
MAJESTY'S MOST GRACIOUS
SPEECH.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the
King's Speech.
Mr. GLENDINNING (in morning
dress): I beg to move, "That an humble
Address be presented to His Grace in
the following words:-
"Your Grace,
"We, His Majesty's most dutiful
and loyal subjects, the Senate of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to
Your Grace for the Speech which your
Grace has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament."
I crave that indulgence and sympathy
which has always been shown to those in
my situation, in endeavouring to move
the important Motion with which I have
had the honour to be entrusted.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address.
20 NOVEMBER 1934
We again avail ourselves of the opportunity afforded by the opening of a new
Session to affirm our loyalty to the
Throne and Constitution; our unfaltering determination to remain part and
parcel of the United Kingdom; and our
pride as subjects of the King and citizens
of the British Empire. Ulster people
will join most heartily in the tribute to
the inestimable services which the King
and Queen have rendered to the Empire
when the celebrations take place next
summer to mark the completion of
twenty-five years' reign.
We wish to express our sympathetic
interest in the marriage of the Duke of
Kent-our Baron Downpatrick—and the
Princess Marina of Greece, and we are
indebted to the inspiring lead of the
Duchess of Abercorn, Lady Craigavon,
and other Ulster ladies in representing
the good wishes of the Province for the
bride and groom in the list of wedding
presents.
We view with continuing pleasure and
admiration the high ideals of duty and
patriotism manifested in the daily life of
His Royal Highness the Prince of Wales,
and his brother Princes, both at home and
throughout the Empire. The enthusiastic welcome given to the Duke of
Gloucester by the people of the great
Commonwealth of Australia during his
present visit expresses the fact that this
recognition of Royal obligation is not confined to the British Islands. May we
hope that the next Royal visit to Ulster
may be that of the Duke and Duchess of
Kent?
We rejoice that His Grace the
Governor has renewed his term of office
as His Majesty's representative.
The
interest taken by him and the Duchess of
Abercorn in every phase of social uplift,
practical philanthropy and healthy sport
throughout the Province is appreciated to
the fullest degree.
We are glad to know that Northern
Ireland is gradually gaining a share in
the slow but steady advance of prosperity
brought about in Great Britain by the
sound policy of the National Government.
This part of the United Kingdom may
be somewhat in the rear of the, oncoming
wave of industrial revival, but good times
across the water are a certain forerunner
of better times here. In proof of this
there are certain indications. I think I
can safely say that there is much more
money in circulation in the Province,
while the figures quoted in the Gracious
Speech show a moderate but encouraging
decline in the number of unemployed.
The Government and the local authorities, no doubt, are doing everything
possible within their resources to reduce
the number still further by unemployment assistance schemes, but we know
that one man back in his own job is worth
twenty on relief schemes. I trust that
improved business will re-absorb more and
more workpeople in the future. Other
signs of returning prosperity are indicated
in the returns to date of those important
industries linen and shipbuilding.
There has been a steady increase in the
tonnage of linen exported from Northern
Ireland to Great Britain and places abroad
during the last four years, the figures for
the twelve months ended 30th September,
1934, being about 5 per cent. higher than
that for the previous year, and about 18
per cent. higher than that for 1931.
-
As for shipbuilding, there were under
construction in Belfast on the 30th
September, 1933, five vessels totalling
40,500 tons, whereas, on the corresponding date in the present year there were
twelve vessels under construction with an
aggregate tonnage of 128,550 tons. That,
of course, means so much more work for
men engaged in shipbuilding and kindred
trades, and we can rely with confidence in
the capable and enterprising management
of the yards to continue their praiseworthy efforts to bring more work to the
Lagan. I think Belfast has a legitimate
claim to more Admiralty orders.
a
Before the Session comes to a close
schemes will be completed for placing the
Unemployment Benefit Fund upon
stable and solvent basis, and also for aiding able-bodied persons who do not
receive insurance benefit. The marketing
schemes have been of immense benefit to
the farmers and, of course, what benefits
the countryside must also benefit the
towns. I trust the Government will continue to do all in their power to attract
new industries to their Province.
Despite Measures passed in the last and
previous Sessions with the object of safeguarding human life on the roads, no
diminution in the number of casualties
appears to result. One reads with horror
when an aeroplane crashes with resulting
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Glendinning]
death, but the numbers killed in air
transport, training or exploitation are
insignificant compared to the loss of life.
due to the motor vehicle. I am pleased
to observe that the Minister of Home
Affairs continues to give earnest attention to this question. He has been in
conference, I understand, with the
Ministry of Transport at Westminster
regarding the usefulness of safety lanes,
"Belisha Beacons," and other methods
of dealing with this appalling menace to
life and limb; but the roads themselves
must be made free of death traps, and
the sense of danger instilled into the
minds of motorists and pedestrians until
it becomes the governing instinct..
The most important Bills of the coming
Session will be the Summary Jurisdiction
Bill and the Road Transport Bill.
Regarding the former, there is evidence
of sharply divided opinion, though
happily not on party lines. However,
there is a feeling that the present system
is unsatisfactory and that some reform is
necessary. The ordinary Justice of the
Peace at present is entrusted with powers
of adjudication on important cases, and
denied those powers on some unimportant
cases. It does not add to their dignity
that they should have to retire from the
Bench or remain silent when a variety
of offences are adjudicated upon.
of
The intention of the Government would
appear to be to entrust all the judicial
powers solely to paid or resident magistrates who have been trained in summary
jurisdiction law and are well acquainted
with procedure at petty sessions courts,
leaving to the unpaid magistrates a
number of duties to discharge, which,
though perhaps less important, are
service to the community. This may
seem a very drastic step, and some may
think there is no justification for it, but
I think the House and the public in
general should suspend their judgment
until the Bill is introduced and everyone
becomes familiar with the arguments
which convinced the Government that this
step was necessary. There is this also
to be said. I am informed that this is
not one of those Bills which is to be
rushed through Parliament. It will be
put through Parliament in sufficient time
to allow public opinion to be thoroughly
informed
upon it.
I am myself confident that the Government is actuated
by no other motive than a desire to
ensure pure, impartial and, as far as
possible, equitable administration of
justice in the primary courts.
The Road Transport Bill will, I
expect, be of a very much more complex
character. It is based on Sir Felix
Pole's recommendations which have been
before the public for several months. At
the outset they were the subject of a good
deal of adverse criticism, but now there
is a general agreement that the railways
must be preserved and I expect the Bill
will aim at some sort of co-operation
which will do away with overlapping and
wasteful and uneconomic competition,
but which under an independent board
will not result in the exploitation of the
public. Whatever may be the fate of
these Measures, I am sure Members of
this House will give them that impartial
and reasoned judgment and that careful
and critical consideration that they gave
to Measures that have hitherto come
before them, and that the result may be
to the advantage of the country and of
all the interests affected.
were
With regard to other legislation mentioned in the Gracious Speech I have only
this to say, that although we
virtually forced to accept this Government, these and many
other Bills,
previously passed,
examples of
which
can
are
schemes for the benefit of Northern
Ireland
be expeditiously
incorporated into law and which would
otherwise have been snowed under in the
overcrowded programmes of legislation at
Westminster.
May I say that as time goes on our
confidence in, and our respect for the
Prime Minister steadily increases. His
success is due to his great ability as a
statesman, his intimate knowledge of the
people and their requirements, his kindly
and considerate disposition, and his never
failing readiness to place himself at the
service of every section of the public in
the furtherance of any laudable project
throughout the Six Counties. In the
latter aspect of his leadership only is he
rivalled by Lady Craigavon, whom we
admire as his loyal and indefatigable
helpmate.
Coming back to the Senate after a
period of private citizenship I found
something sadly lacking in its atmosphere.
This was, of course, due to the absence
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
20 NOVEMBER 1934
of the noble Lord, the Leader of the
House, whose genial and courteous personality I greatly missed. Nothing but
the highest praise can be given to his able
and efficient deputy (Mr. Long) in his
conduct of the business. This is fully in
keeping with the reputation he enjoys in.
County Londonderry, where he presides
over several important boards. He has
established a record for the number of
years he has held the important position
of chairman of the Londonderry County
Council. I trust the health of Lord
Charlemont is now re-established, and
that we shall have the pleasure and
advantage of his able guidance throughout the Session now opened.
Finally, I hope I am not invidious in
paying a tribute to the hon. and learned
Member, Mr. Leslie, whose profound
knowledge of legislation in its history and
administration, and the careful and
scrutinising attention he pays to all the
business of the Senate, have been of the
greatest advantage to the various
Measures which have come before us, and
to the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
I beg to move.
Mr. PORTER PORTER (in morning
dress): I am perfectly sensible of and
thoroughly appreciate the honour that
has been conferred upon me in being
asked to second the Motion for the
Address, which has been so ably moved
by the hon. Senator on my right. With
all he has said I am very glad to say I
cordially agree. I shall not reiterate all
the compliments he has paid to the noble.
Leader of the House and the Deputy
Leader, who are so well worthy of them,
but I endorse all he has said. The first
matter referred to in the King's Speech
is the state of the unemployment register,
and a great deal of credit is due to the
Minister of Labour for the wonderful
improvement he has been able to bring
about in the unemployment situation.
According to the figures supplied to us,
there are 14,000 fewer people unemployed
to-day than there were a short time ago.
The Minister of Labour has a very difficult task, and perhaps in referring to
this question I am only encroaching on the
time of those hon. Senators who live in
or near Belfast. The good-looking county
to which I belong is not disfigured with
factory chimneys, and therefore there is
very little unemployment. We are an
agricultural county of small farms, and
unemployment does not affect us a great
deal. I hope those who are responsible
for promoting schemes of unemployment
assistance will succeed in the difficult task
they have undertaken.
With regard to the question of road
transport, I think we are all agreed that
something had to be done about it. It
was getting into a bad state, and some
improvement in the conditions was badly
needed. The railways are not paying as
they ought to simply on account of the
road competition. So far as the
Summary Jurisdiction Bill is concerned,
what magistrates are doing now means a
tremendous upheaval of old fashioned
legislation, but I have no doubt that the
mantle of Solomon will fall upon someone, and that something will be done to
satisfy people all over the country,
because there seems to be a good deal of
dissatisfaction with the present state of
affairs. The only advice I would give is
to leave the matter in the hands of the
responsible Ministers. They have never
failed us in the past, and I think they
will be able to do more than could be
accomplished by any advice we might
give.
We now come to the question of milk,
and perhaps I am more at home with
milk than with anything else.
I am a
country gentleman, and perhaps I know
more about the milk question than some
of my hon. Friends. At the present time
there is a demand for more veterinary
inspection of cows and cowsheds, and I
think that is most desirable. Local bodies
and hospitals and other institutions in
the cities and elsewhere require the best
of milk and butter, and to secure that I
think we ought to go to the root of the
question and have both cows and byres
properly inspected. Of course in the old
days there was a certain amount of butter
and a certain amount of milk sent up.
It is different nowadays. Nobody would
eat the old butter. It would not be seen
on the table of a poorhouse. I trust the
Government, with my Friend Sir Basil
Brooke as Minister of Agriculture, will
succeed in doing things better.
We are referred next to pigs, and I
suppose we must say something about
them. They are an article of the life of
the country more than of the town. I
advise the Senators not to read or at any
rate not to believe all the letters that
have been written because they generally
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Porter Porter]
have been written by people who know
nothing about the subject. There has
been a milk subsidy granted by the
Government to creamery milk and the
low price of 24d. a gallon has been raised
to 5d., which is a very great boon to
the people of the country. I must say
from what I know of them they are all
very glad and jubilant about it, and
about getting the extra price. There is
also a subsidy on the marketing of fat
cattle. When you sell fat cattle in a
fair I find you get 5s. per hundredweight
on the weight basis, that is added to the
price which is very much higher than our
friends below the Boyne get for their
cattle. I will not take up your time any
longer. I am not a politician and I am
very much obliged for the kind way you
have heard my few words. I beg to
second the vote of thanks to the Governor.
Question proposed: "That an humble
Address be presented to His Grace in
the following words:-
"Your Grace,
"We, His Majesty's most dutiful
and loyal subjects, the Senate of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to
Your Grace for the speech which Your
Grace has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament."
our
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): At the outset I desire to
be associated with the congratulations
which have been extended to His Grace
the Governor upon the renewal of his
office for a third term. It would be a
fair, though brief, expression of
regard for His Grace to describe him, in
the words of Pope, as "Ennobled by himself, by all approved, and praised
unenvied," for I suppose we should all
agree that no one could be found better
fitted, not only by his personal qualities
but by his natural prestige in Ulster to
act as the representative in this country
of that Throne which, as we have again
been reminded, continues to stand as the
symbol, not of mere domination, but of
our constitutional liberties: a Throne
which has behind it forces more powerful
than have ever been at the disposal of
military kings of the past. At a time.
when prospective developments of a farreaching character in another part of
Ireland have been canvassed with a great
deal of publicity it is appropriate for us
once
more to remember with gratitude
our own position of security within the
British Empire. In this context it is
gratifying that, although we form such a
small part of the Empire, we have not
been forgotten in the bestowal of honours
upon His Majesty's youngest son, to
whose wedding next week we look forward as a happy celebration.
As the representative of the Government in this House I wish particularly
to thank the hon. Senators Mr.
Glendinning and Mr. Porter Porter, who
have moved and seconded the Address in
reply to the Gracious Speech in such an
appreciative and interesting way. Neither
of the hon. Senators comes as a novice
to the task which once more on this
occasion they have so well undertaken,
and I am very glad to welcome the hon.
Senator Mr. Glendinning back into this
House after his absence from it. (Hon.
Senators: Hear, hear.)
Attention has very properly been
drawn to the continued improvement in
the industrial and agricultural outlook.
As to industry, I am not sure how far
this, or any, Government can assume the
credit. There is little doubt that the
improvement in this direction must in
the main be set down to economic causes
beyond the control of any political
influences in any country. At the same
time we can at least claim that we have
done everything within our power to
remove obstructions which might have
interfered with the free play of these
favourable trade winds and, whatever the
cause, at any rate
the results are a
matter for thankfulness.
As the Gracious Speech records, the
problem of unemployment has fallen
to manageable dimensions and the
advantage of the favourable moment has
been taken to introduce a comprehensive
Measure for dealing with its worst social
consequences for the future. I shall not
expatiate upon this topic, with which,
important as it is, hon. Members must
already be familiar, but I would remind
the House that the new unemployment
assistance scheme is. expected to be in
operation as regards persons now entitled
to transitional payments from January
7th next, and as regards other persons
coming within the scope of the Contributory Pensions Act from the 1st of
March. In the meanwhile the composition of the Appeal Tribunals is receiving
consideration.
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
20 NOVEMBER 1934
The hon. Senator Mr. Glendinning has
given figures showing the marked advance.
which has been made in the linen and
shipbuilding industries. I should like to
add a few words with regard to our junior
industry-the tourist traffic-which in
other small countries has developed into
such a valuable source of income, of
course a certain amount of trade which
comes to us in this way is no doubt due
to the increased spending power in Great
Britain, and to the adverse rates for
sterling offered on most of the
Continental exchanges. There is, however, no doubt that a great deal of the
credit must go to the excellent propaganda on the part of the Ulster Tourist
Development Association. I have had
the opportunity of seeing a number of
reports gathered from all our Northern
Ireland holiday resorts, which show on
the average an increase in the neighbourhood of 25 per cent. in the volume of
tourist business handled this year as compared with last. This impression
confirmed by the statements made by
hotels and transport agencies operating
both by land and sea. As undoubtedly
most of these visitors come from Great
Britain we may congratulate ourselves
upon the increased numbers, not only
from the commercial point of view, but
also because such contracts must necessarily maintain and strengthen the
associative links between Great Britain
and Ulster.
is
Mr. Porter Porter, who seconded, has
called attention to the important work
done by the Government in the sphere of
agriculture. If this has been as effective
as I also think it has been-so far as it
is possible to judge at this stage-in this
respect at least we may fairly congratulate ourselves; for I do not think
the improvement in agricultural conditions can be put down to general economic
causes to the same extent as the improvement in industry as a whole. As the hon.
Senator I think mentioned, it has not yet
been possible to bring into full operation
those parts of the Milk Act dealing with
the sale of liquid milk, but it is hoped that
this will not be deferred long after the
beginning of next year.
year. Meanwhile,
nearly 5,000 licences under the Act have
been issued to producers and distributors
of milk, and the Joint Milk Council,
which will be the price-regulating
authority, has now been constituted and
has entered upon its functions. It is
probable that the decision of the Council
as to the fixed price for milk will be
promulgated within the next few days
and it will then be possible for producers
and distributors to proceed with the
signing of contracts for delivery of milk
under the terms of the price agreement.
There is one particular provision in the
Milk Act in which, as Minister of
Education, I have a special interest and
that is, the power which is given to the
Ministry of Agriculture to subsidise
schemes for the provision of milk to
school children at a reduced rate. I
regard this as a most valuable opportunity of acting in the interests of the
children's health, but of course I quite
appreciate that it is as yet too early to
know to what extent funds will eventually
prove to be available for the purpose. My
right hon. Friend the Minister of Agriculture informs me that he intends to
bring the matter before the Joint Milk
Council at an early opportunity, and I
have his assurance that he is as eager as
myself that some such facilities as are in
existence in Great Britain
may be
extended to school children in Northern
Ireland.
In connection with these marketing
schemes two further projected Measures
are referred to in the Gracious Speech.
One is designed to bring about certain
changes in connection with the administration of the pigs and bacon schemesthe oldest of the schemes now in existence
-which the experience gained both in
Northern Ireland and in Great Britain
during the past has shown to be desirable.
The object of the other Measure is the
centralisation and co-ordination
veterinary services in connection with.
dairy cattle, which, as the hon. Senator
Mr. Porter Porter has already pointed
out is a matter of considerable importance
and urgency.
of
In face of the increasing complication
of the machinery of life a simple policy
of letting things slide in economic questions has long been found wanting, and
the application of the principle of coordination to an increasing number of
matters has become a necessity for the
maintenance of a proper balance between
the various public interests which
modern Government is equally bound to
protect. I have already referred to the
matter in which the Government has thus
intervened in the case of agricultural
a
Debate on the Address
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
marketing and to the further legislation.
in relation to this matter which is to be
introduced during this Session. An
example of the co-ordination of public
services upon a still more ambitious scale
will be found in the proposals with regard
to road and rail transport. It is too
early for me to give more than the most
general indication as to the provisions of
the Bill for this purpose as the final
details of the scheme have not yet been
evolved. In general, however, the
scheme will be based upon Sir Felix
Pole's recommendations, with which most
Members of this House will already be
familiar. Ulster has
Ulster has been extremely
fortunate in securing Sir Felix Pole's
services, and I think we may be confident
that in following the advice of one who
is not only an acknowledged expert in
the sphere of transport, but is capable
of taking the broadest possible view of
the subject, we are likely to go a long
way towards solving the economic difficulties under which our railways
especially labour, without injustice to the
interests of other forms of transport.
This will be the first scheme of the kind
to be put into force in the British Isles,
and it is safe to say that it will be
awaited with considerable interest across
the Channel as well as here. Nor will
this be the first time that Ulster has led
the way in transport matters, for the
principle of ensuring safe and adequate
public motor services by co-operation.
between the various companies under the
supervision of a fares tribunal, which has
now
been adopted throughout Great
Britain, also originated in Northern
Ireland.
The second Measure of major
importance which will be laid before this
House will be the Summary Jurisdiction
Bill. The main purpose of this Bill is
already known. It is also proposed to
make some revision in the existing provisions in regard to the appointment and
qualification of resident magistrates and
of petty sessions clerks, and for the
alteration of petty sessions districts and
courts, and also to extend the power of
courts of summary jurisdiction to deal
summarily with a variety of indictable
these matters,
offences. Apart from
advantage will be taken of the introduction of the Bill to make a number of
amendments in summary jurisdiction law
of a miscellaneous character.
The responsibility of my right hon.
Friend the Minister of Home Affairs
during the present Session of Parliament
will be particularly heavy, for besides.
these two Bills he is also to bring in a
Measure which has in view the Amendment of the Local Government (Rating
and Finance) Act, 1929,
1929, generally
referred to as The Derating Act.
matters with which it is necessary to deal
arise partly through the imminent completion of the general revaluation, but
the Bill is largely concerned with points
of interpretation in regard to the existing
law which it is desired to elucidate.
The
The only Bill referred to in the
Gracious Speech to which I have so far
made no reference is that with relation
to charitable donations and bequests.
This will be the Measure deferred from
last Session for the purpose of consolidating the Acts of 1844, 1867 and
1871 relating to charitable donations and
bequests with only minor changes in the
existing law.
My remarks have, I fear, detained the
House too long and I will say no more
except to express the hope that the spirit
of reasonableness and cordiality which I
think I may say has in the past distinguished the discussions in this House
may be continued, and that by whatever
differences in personal or political views
the Members of this House are divided we
may still co-operate towards securing, so
far as in us lies, the best of all possible
worlds for the people of this Province.
Before I conclude I should like to
warmest
express my
thanks to the
speakers who preceded me for their kindly
references to myself, and I should also
like to say how extremely glad I am to be
able to be back in my place at the
beginning of this Session."
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to move,
"That this Debate be now adjourned."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the Debate be resumed
on Thursday next.
Adjournment.
Resolved, That this House do now
until
adjourn
Thursday next.".
(Viscount Charlemont.)
Adjourned accordingly at
Four o'clock until
Thursday next.
17 Appointment of Joint Committees 22 NOVEMBER 1934

Debate on the Address
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 22nd November, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Hume's Divorce Bill.
Mr. SPEAKER laid upon the Table
a Certificate that the Regulations embodied in the Resolution of the Serate
of the thirteenth day of October last
have been complied with in respect of
Hume's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill as
reported from the Joint Committee without amendment.
Deputy Speakers.
Resolved, "That the Senators following be named Deputy Speakers of the
Senate during the present Session of
Parliament: Mr. McLaughlin, Colonel
Pakenham."-(Mr. Long).
Appointment of Joint Committees.
Resolved, "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Standing
Committee on Divorce Bills:-Mr.
Leslie, Colonel Pakenham, Mr. Quigley."
-(Mr. Long).
Resolved, "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Standing
Orders Committee:-Mr. Barclay, Mr.
Leslie, Colonel Sinclair."-(Mr. Long).
Resolved, "That the Senators followbe named of the Joint Unopposed Committee of Local Bills:-Mr. J. Cunningham, Mr. McLaughlin."-(Mr. Long).
Resolved, "That the Senators following be named of the Joint Kitchen and
Refreshment Room Committee:-Mr. J.
Cunningham, Mr. Hill Dickson, Mr.
Gyle, Mr. McAllister, Colonel Pakenham." (Mr. Long).
KING'S SPEECH.
Motion for Address.
Order read for resuming Debate on
Motion (20th November):-
"That an humble Address be presented to His Grace in the following
words:
"Your Grace,
"We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to
Your Grace for the Speech which Your
Grace has addressed to both Houses of
Parliament."
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the names of Mr.
McLaughlin and Mr. McMahon :-
At the end to add-
"But regret that no mention is
made 'of
adequately
minority."
steps to protect
interests of the
any
the
He
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: In moving the
Amendment on the Paper, I should like,
first of all, to congratulate the mover of
the Motion for the Address (Mr. Glendinning) on the able and reasoned speech
he made. I did remark-perhaps I was
wrong that his heart was not in what
he said regarding the Summary Jurisdiction Bill. It must be admitted that
the J.P. was the poor man's friend.
knew the local conditions and was
familiar with the character and conditions of those who came before him.
In that way he was looked on with confidence by the community of the area in
which he acted, and in all cases the
decisions of justices were looked on as
just. I wonder if under this Bill the
resident magistrates will get the same
confidence of the public.
I wish, on my own behalf and on behalf of my party, to join in the welcome
back to the honoured Leader of this
House (Lord Charlemont). We are delighted to have him with us again, and
we sincerely trust he will be spared
many, many years to adorn the position
which he holds. I am very glad to see
that the number of unemployed is down
by 14,000. I trust this fall will continue and that in a short time we will
have all our people absorbed in work of
some kind. I do trust the comprehen-
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
sive schemes of unemployment assistance
will prove a success, and I trust that all
workmen, particularly married men with
wives and families dependent on them,
will be compulsorily liable to insurance.
so as to secure that when any of them
die their widows and orphans will be
provided for. The Transport Bill will
be of serious concern to every person in
these districts. We do trust that it
will help to solve this most important
question. As Chairman of the Great
Northern Railway Stockholders' Association, might I point out that the railways have been in the past, and will be
in the future, indispensable to the needs
of the country. Railway shares not long
since were a trustee stock, looked on as
a gilt-edged security, and large sums
were invested in the railways for income
purposes. With the changes in transport, Government control and interference and legislative handicaps, no interest is paid on these shares, except the
debenture and guaranteed stock. This
is a serious matter for all stockholders,
and I think I am not suggesting too
much when I suggest to the Ministry
concerned that the railway stockholders'
position should be seriously taken into
consideration and at least sufficient safeguards provided for the railways, so that
they can compete in a fair way to make
their concerns a success. If this is
accomplished it will go far to make the
Bill a popular one, as the railways give
more employment than any other concern in the country, and, in a word,
successful railways mean successful
trade.
I am surprised no reference has been
made to the housing problem. Surely it
is not suggested this question has been
solved. It is a well-known fact that the
subsidy given for building is not enough
to induce people to go on building. Until the subsidy is at least doubled your
building schemes will not be a success.
Moreover, I would suggest that the size
of houses entitled to the subsidy should
be enlarged, as under present regulations the houses being put up under the
subsidy are too small. I trust the Milk
and Products Acts will be a success, and
that it will not create another horde of
officials. I would draw attention to the
resolution passed by the Portrush Urban
Council, which states: "That the Council
call on the Parliament of Northern Ireland to adopt measures to secure a more
economical government of the Six
Counties." This resolution has my
hearty support, and if the Members of
this House as well as the Members of
Parliament are sincere they will see that
as far as possible measures such as suggested will be adopted.
I am an Ulster Irishman, and proud
of the fact, though I cannot say I am
proud of the actions and utterances of
the Prime Minister or some of the Members of his Cabinet. I am as anxious
for the welfare and success of the Six
Counties as any other person, simply
because I know that my success and wellbeing depends on the success of our
trade and people generally. As far as I
can I will at all times do everything in
my power to further the success of trade,
the peace, happiness and welfare of our
people in this area. We are all proud
of this little corner of our country, but
I am prouder still of being an Irishman
in the full sense of the word, and I look
forward in hope to the day when this
country will be united again as one
nation, living in harmony with her
neighbours and doing everything to the
mutual advantage of each other. Since
the 1920 Act was passed and this Parliament was established your Government have done all they possibly could
to victimise Catholics, not, I say it deliberately, on account of their political
opinions, but solely on account of their
religion. As I have repeatedly pointed
out, you gerrymandered the constituencies in such a way as to leave your own
followers-who are in the minority in
two counties, Tyrone and Fermanaghin the majority on the county councils of
these two counties. Other counties and
rural as well as urban districts have
been gerrymandered in the same way. I
say that this is neither fair nor just.
You say that no appointments are
made on sectarian grounds. Well, if
not, it is a remarkable fact that very few
Catholics get any of the key appointments in your services. In a speech by
a Senator from the back benches a short
time ago, in reply to some remarks of
mine, he could point to only two
Catholics holding important appointments in the services under your control,
and if I am right I think these two
officials were taken over when the transfers were made. We Catholics are a
third of the population of the community
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
22 NOVEMBER 1934
under your jurisdiction. We are as lawabiding and as well conducted as any of
your citizens. We pay our proportion of
rates and taxes just as all others do, and
in all justice we have a right to expect
some return in the way of a proportion
of the moneys spent by your Government
out of the rates and taxes which they
pay. Surely any responsible Prime
Minister or Cabinet Minister, fully understanding the dignity and responsibility of his position, should not, to
say the least of it, go out of his way,
as he has done, to insult my fellow
Catholics. As a responsible public man
and as an Irishman and as a Roman
Catholic, I resent it, and I say that if
these gentlemen had the real interests of
their people at heart they would strive
to work for their goodwill, and not go
about making the scurrilous speeches they
do. Surely it is the duty of all statesmen to make their people, the minority.
included, contented and happy. I had
occasion a few days ago to refer to the
question of the mixing of the sexes in
the schools. The Deputy Leader of the
House gave me a carefully prepared
reply. He very astutely referred to the
Killanin Report, signed by His Eminence the late Cardinal O'Donnell. He
did not point out that the schools referred to were one-teacher schools, and
that His Eminence would only go so
far as to sign the report, and to show
that he objected to such a system he
limited the number very distinctly to 35,
which means one-teacher schools.
we
Under your Education Act
Catholics have to pay our proportion of
the rate, and how is it expended? There
are attending the schools under your
jurisdiction 96,537 pupils in your provided and transferred schools, which cost
the community £1,059,202. There are
73,959 pupils at the voluntary schools
under Catholic management, and these
cost your Ministry the small sum of
£75,000. In addition, we Catholics pay
through the rates for the schools of our
non-Catholic brethren-not that we mind.
What we simply want is fair play and
justice in the expenditure of this money,
and in all these matters.
Before passing from that I would like
to point out that in the County of Down
alone the Catholic schools under your
Education Act are deprived of no less
a sum than £89,338 10s 9d, which would
be the sum they would be entitled to if
they received a fair share of sums expended in County Down for maintenance
of schools. The number of Catholic
children attending schools in County
Down is about 11,100 out of a total
number of children attending schools in
County Down of 35,210. Yet in this
area for the period April 1, 1925, to 31st
March, 1933, out of £150,603 17s 3d
spent on these schools Catholic voluntary
schools received the paltry sum of
£13,694 5s 3d. And of the item expenditure on schools for the same period, of
£170,494 11s 7d, there was spent by your
Ministry on Catholic schools about
£4,000. And yet you say we are getting
fair returns for the education rates and
taxes we pay.
I have no hesitation in stating that if
the Ministry persist in their attitude in
this matter of education, I, for one, as
a Catholic, will refuse to pay an education rate from which my people do not
get a fair share of the expenditure for
the education of Catholic children and,
moreover, I will advise my fellow
Catholics also to refuse to pay a rate
from which they get so little benefit.
Should you persist in this question of
amalgamating the boys' and girls' schools
you will find that we Catholics will not
be so docile as we have been, but you will
find us at the beck and call of our bishops
and priests to follow any course they may
advise under their leadership.
You say Catholics, as such, have as
good a chance and get as fair treatment
under your Government as any others.
Yet it is a most significant thing that
of the members recently nominated by
your Ministeries to the Pig Board not
one is a Catholic, and I understand that
of the recently set up Unemployment
Assistance Board, consisting of, I think,
17 members, all nominated by your
Ministry, not one is a Catholic. And yet
you expect the Catholics to have. entire
confidence in such a board, to sit quiet
and say nothing. Well, to my mind, it
remains to be seen how long they will
submit to such a state of affairs. You
have, as far as possible, deprived us of
any advantages we had under the Constitution before the 1920 Act came into
force.
Now, in conclusion, I warn you that
whilst we are willing to co-operate in the
welfare and success of this part of the
country in which we are placed, we will
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. McLaughlin]
still go on working for the unity and
welfare of the country as a whole, but
above all, we will not allow your Government to further penalise us on account of
our religious beliefs.
If your Prime Minister was a statesman
in the full sense of the word he would
not, in face of the wording of the 1920
Act, pronounce even in his own Parliament, "This was a Protestant Parliament
for a Protestant people," when 33 per
cent. of the people under that Parliament
are Catholics, and particularly in view
of the fact that when accepting this
Parliament he undertook not to "Make a
law so as, either directly or indirectly, to
establish or endow any religion or prohibit or restrict the free exercise thereof
or give a preference, privilege or advantage or impose any disability or disadvantage on account of religious belief
or religious or ecclesiastical status."
I do not see how he can justify his
statement that this is a Protestant
Parliament for a Protestant people, and
also hold that he is carrying out that part
of the Act of 1920 under which this
Parliament was established. However,
in the words of a famous statesman, "We
I will wait and see."
The Government may have the right
to penalise their citizens for their political
opinions or beliefs. I have the greatest
respect for my Protestant neighbours and
citizens, but I hold that under that
Article in the 1920 Act they have no
right to victimise Catholics on account of
their religious beliefs, and as far as I am
concerned I will not stand silently or
idly by and see my fellow Catholics either
penalised or victimised on account of their
religion. I beg to move.
One
Mr. MCALLISTER: As an Irishman
and a Northern Irishman I feel that it is
a most humiliating and degrading duty
that is forced upon us to make public
protest against the treatment of the
minority in the Six Counties by the
Government of Northern Ireland.
can hardly be startled by anything these
days in Northern Ireland, but when I
read the paper this morning and saw the
declaration by the Prime Minister in
another place, that he intended to maintain this Parliament as a Protestant
Parliament for a Protestant people,
manned by a Protestant and loyalist staff,
I must say I was horrified at that
audacious declaration. When a public
representative makes a speech he must be
able to stand over it. It must stand the
test. What is the Prime Minister's test
of loyalty? According to General Crozier
he was the leader of a movement in
Northern Ireland which was prepared to
shoot down the troops of this country or
the police of this country. That statement has never been contradicted. It
has been published by General Crozier as
his idea of the test of loyalty. Is it the
test of loyalty to run guns in defiance of
His Majesty's forces in Northern Ireland?
Has that Gentleman a right to talk of
loyalty in any sense of the word?
I say deliberately that the Prime.
Minister realises the dissatisfaction and
discontent in Northern Ireland at the
present time. He knows the unemployed
people are in a state of dissatisfaction and
discontent, and that business is depressed
to a great extent, and in some areas, such
as Derry, it has gone almost beyond regeneration, and in order to try to draw a
red herring across that state of affairs
and to draw men's minds away from the
material issue, for his Own selfish
interests, he is inflaming public opinion.
As I understand the duty of a Prime
Minister, he holds in his hands a great
trust. He is the custodian of the rights
and liberties of the people of this country
of which he is Prime Minister, and I say,
taken by both tests, the Prime Minister
has been a tremendous failure. It is
degrading, I say, to have to defend the
interests of one-third of the population of
Northern Ireland in this supposed
advanced era. The Prime Minister has
stated that he intends to make this a
model State. If we follow his example
we are going back to the state of primitive
The people of Northern Ireland,
a whole, I believe, have a more
generous outlook and more humane sense
of public duty than the Prime Minister.
What is the position of the Catholic body
in Northern Ireland?
man.
as
My Friend, Senator McLaughlin, has
given figures of how you gerrymandered
us out of the local bodies. We resent
your gerrymandering the constituencies
so that two Unionist votes are equal to
three Nationalist votes. It has been said
that the principles of the Government of
Ireland Act have not been broken. True,
it has not been put to the legal test, but
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
22 NOVEMBER 1934
in spirit they have been broken day after
day. The duties of that Act have been
evaded, got round, and thwarted by every
move the Government
of Northern
Ireland can conceive. Certainly, if they
have not broken the Act in fact they
have broken it in spirit. In appointments to the Civil Service, to public
offices, and to every position in Northern
Ireland the fact that a man is a Catholic
debars him from any chance of being
appointed.
My Friend, Senator McLaughlin, has
dealt with the question of education. We
are called upon to pay a fair share of the
rates. I do not intend to cover the
figures he has given, but he has proved
most conclusively, by figures and facts,
that we do not receive anything like the
proper benefits we should receive under
the Education Act. As I say, it is a
most degrading thing to have to make this
public protest. I would appeal to those with
a higher sense of duty and a more truly
loyal spirit to use their influence to put
an end to the system that has been going
on in Northern Ireland in recent years.
Question proposed, "That the words,
'But regret that no mention is made of
any steps to protect adequately the
interests of the minority,' be added to
the Question."
Mr. LESLIE: There are, in effect, two
Questions before the House this afterand I imagine it would be more
noon,
convenient to take them both together,
that is, to make one speech to serve both
the Amendment and the main Question
of the Address. I think, speaking from
memory, that is the course usually
adopted to save time.
I should like to begin the few
remarks I am about to make this
afternoon by expressing my compliments to the mover and seconder of the
Address for the admirable way they discharged their duty. The mover of the
Address, Senator Glendinning, is no
novice in this House. As has already
been stated, he is one of those who, if not
actually an original Member, joined us
very soon after our inception. He left
us, as we know, for a short time. We
regretted his loss, and we are very glad
to see him back again. I cannot close my
reference to him without thanking him
very heartily for the very kind words he
was good enough to use in respect of my
own poor services. One gets so much
criticism in any form of public life that
one appreciates very highly anything in
the way of kindly reference such as it was
my good fortune to enjoy on Tuesday at
the hands of the hon. Member. The
seconder of the Address is not with us
this afternoon, but he is one we are
always glad to see, because we rcognise in
him the type of old Ulster sportsman who
perhaps, some day, will become extinct,
but we are glad to say he is not extinct
yet. I should also like to join in the
congratulations to the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House, on his recovery
from an illness which has kept him away
from us so long.
Taking first the Amendment which has
just been moved, Senator McLaughlin
complained incidentally that there was
nothing in the Speech suggesting the
possibility of a united Ireland. He did
not tell us what kind of a united Ireland
he had in his mind, whether he meant an
Ireland united in loyalty to the Crown
and Constitution, or whether some
separate establishment under the Crown
perhaps as a Dominion, but consisting of
the whole of the island which we call
Ireland, or whether he meant an
independent Republic. He left us in
complete doubt as to what he meant about
that. He also complained of the unfair
treatment of those holding the same faith
as he does himself in the matter of what
he described as the education rate. I am
going to suggest that the expression
"education rate" is entirely a misnomer.
There is no such thing in that sense as an
education rate. I think that the allusion
was really to the additional shilling to be
imposed next year and sixpence this year
on the rate fund in aid of the cost of
education. That is not really quite the
same thing as an education rate in the
way in which that expression is generally
understood.
The purpose for which the sixpence, to
be followed by a shilling, was imposed
was, as plainly stated at the time, to form
a fund in aid of the costs of education,
that is, costs which are not incurred
entirely by the local authorities in control
of education, but the cost of education as
a whole. One of the main items in the
cost of education is undoubtedly the payment of the teachers' salaries. I have yet
to learn that there is any school recognised by the Ministry under whatever
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
class of management-provided, transferred, or voluntary schools, I have yet to
learn that in any one of those shools the
teacher does not draw his salary from the
State fund. If I am wrong, no doubt the
Minister will correct me.
That is my
recollection, and that is my belief.
Certainly I know that in the case of one
voluntary school for which I have some
responsibility my teachers are paid by
the State, and I think that is the case in
respect of all voluntary schools, whatever
form of religious doctrine may be inculcated in those schools.
I am
Again, complaint was made of nonobservance by the Government of the
conditions of the well-known Section in
the 1920 Act-Section 5. It is the Section
relating to prohibition to impose any disability in respect of religious belief.
Complaint was made that the Government
had disregarded the provisions of that
Section, or at all events, if they had not
actually done anything running counter
to the Section, they had gone as near it
as they could possibly manage.
afraid I have got to say that there is
