some truth in the latter part of that
accusation. I think that the Government has sailed remarkably near the wind
in relation to that Section 5, and the
occasion in which they did it, and the
reason for which they did it was to enable
further consideration to be given
voluntary schools, and in practice the
voluntary schools that have got that further
consideration have been particularly those.
under Roman Catholic management. I
think that is so, though, no doubt, the
noble Viscount will be able to develop
what I have said.
to
Another charge was made as to which
I cannot say anything. It was suggested
that in an Unemployment Assistance
Board, consisting of, I think, seventeen
members, there were not to be found any
people belonging to the Roman Catholic
religion. As to that I cannot say anything, except to express my surprise that
that should be the case, if indeed it has
been correctly stated. I am not quite
sure what unemployment assistance board
was referred to. There is a board-I
think board is right-consisting of three
members, but I cannot for the moment
recall any board consisting of seventeen
members. No doubt that will be dealt
with later on. Taking it altogether, I
cannot find anything in the Speech or in
the past history of the Government which
would justify this House in adding to the
ordinary Address in reply to the Gracious
Speech words such as are on the Paper.
Therefore, I pass from the Amendment,
and come back to the main Question.
I am not going to say anything this
afternoon about those matters to which
the Speech does not allude, and I only
want to run very rapidly through one or
two
matters particularly alluded to,
because there is one special matter to
which I do wish to ask the attention of
the House for a few minutes. First of
all, one cannot pass over the reference to
the Unemployment and Public Assistance
Acts which are expected to come into full
operation on 7th January and 1st March
respectively. I think those dates are
somewhat earlier than those originally
anticipated, and I am sure we are all
very glad it has been found possible to
accelerate their progress. There is also
a reference to the report by Sir Felix
Pole dealing with the re-organisation of
our transport system. I think we shall
all welcome that. The hon. Member who
moved the Amendment spoke particularly
about the necessity of doing something to
revivify the railways and of something in
the shape of consolation for their unhappy
shareholders. Something must be done
to save the Northern Ireland railways
from extinction, and it will be generally
recognised that the rather long but very
exhaustive report of Sir Felix Pole does
contain something more than the germs
of a settlement. As a matter of fact, as
indeed is stated in a foot-note to the report,
something has already been done to
relieve the railway companies of some of
the undue obligations imposed upon them
in past years under which they are still
suffering, or were suffering until a few
months ago when a Bill was passed
enabling them, amongst other things, to
get rid of some of their superfluous land.
There is also a reference, towards the
end of the Speech, to a measure which is
to be introduced to amend the derating
code, as certain provisions of the Local
Government Act have become exhausted.
We all anticipated something of that sort,
because Section 13 of the Act gave a
fairly plain intimation that when the five
years was up the five years' plan would
cease to operate, and some thing would have
to be done. I do not know how far the
Government is prepared to go in amend-
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
22 NOVEMBER 1934
ing that Act, but I am particularly
anxious about the rating of turbary and
turbary rights. There seems to be some
confusion in the matter. The income
tax authorities treat the matter as farm
land and apply Schedule B, where it can
be applied, but the rating authorities do
not seem to take that view and they treat
it, not as derated land, but as un-derated.
I hope the Government will clear up the
confusion that exists in this respect.
a
I now pass to what the noble Viscount
described as the second Measure of major
importance. That is the Summary
Jurisdiction Bill. You are aware that
the position is little complicated,
because since this debate began, rather
contrary to general expectation the Bill
itself has been introduced in another
place, although this House has no knowledge of it. But the fact remains that
the Bill has been introduced rather
rapidly, and, therefore, although the
introduction was not into this House, it
is perhaps more incumbent on one than it
otherwise would be to abstain from
reference to any details of that Measure.
But I think I am entitled to speak on the
main purpose of that Bill, and the main
purpose is, of course, the proposal to take
away from justices of the peace such of
their judicial functions as are at present
in their hands.
Now that is a very serious proposal
indeed. It raises numerous questions, of
a very serious character. The consideration of those questions ought, as I think,
to be approached, not in any party spirit
at all, but with the greatest moderation
perhaps, and with the utmost deliberation. I lay stress on that, the absence of
party spirit in this matter, because I
think it is in the highest degree important
that nothing relating to the administration of justice should under any circumstances become the shuttle-cock of politics.
I repeat that the consideration of the
question should be entered into with the
most unbiased mind, and with a determination that none of the problems
presented should escape the most careful
and most critical and impartial investigation.
In trying to look at the matter we have
first to ask ourselves, whence comes the
demand for so radical an alteration in our
system. Whence has the demand arisen?
Has there, perhaps, been some recommendation to the Government from the
head of the magistracy-the Lord Chief
Justice? If there has been I have not
heard of it. Has there been any public
Inquiry, any Commission set up presided
over, perhaps, by a Judge, making
inquiry, hearing evidence, weighing the
evidence, and issuing a Blue Book? If
there is such a Blue Book I have failed to
observe reference to it in the local papers.
Can it be that the demand really has its
origin in a Department which cannot
brook the knowledge that its proceedings
are liable to be controlled by a court over
which it itself has no control-a Department which, perhaps, has not yet learned.
thoroughly that it is the servant and not
the master of the public, a Department
which cannot tolerate the administration
of justice-summary justice-except by
magistrates of a particular class appointed
by its own Minister?
If we cannot get and I have not yet
got a satisfactory answer as to the source
and origin of the demand for alteration,
one has to inquire what are the causes
and what are the abuses that are alleged
to exist. Nobody, I imagine, is going to
stand up here and say the system is wholly
perfect, that is to say, that everything
done everywhere by everybody is always
right. I do not think anybody outside
the particular Government Department,
speaking in relation to its own affairs,
would venture to maintain or suggest
that for a moment. Now things
occasionally go wrong. Possibly some
magistrates do not invariably do their
duty. There are, I understand, something between 1,500 and 1,600 magistrates, and of these probably one-third
have been appointed since the present
Government came into office.
Passing from that, what are the things
alleged against the magistrates? There
is, perhaps, superfluity. There may be
too many of them. Irregularity of
attendance is another. It is said some
magistrates never attend the bench at all.
Others only attend when a certain class
of case in which they are interested comes
before them. There have been even
worse suggestions, confined, I think, to a
particular bench, and I am not sure that
they are not confined to a particular
person. That is venality. There is, of
course, the question of incompetence, and
there are anomalies. But for all these
things there are means of redress. It
would be quite possible, if Belfast is held
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
to have offended, to appoint a stipendiary
magistrate for Belfast, as has been done
in the larger towns across the water. It
is still possible to use the writ of supersedeas, that is, the Crown could withdraw from particular persons the
commission conferred upon them. It is
even possible to issue a new commission,
a commission which supersedes the existing commission and renders its possible
to leave out some 20 or 30 magistrates
from the commission, say, of County
Antrim.
There is still another possibility, that is,
to extend the system introduced some 40
years ago whereby a magistrate used
to be informed that it was to be
understood that he would only adjudicate in particular petty sessions
districts. That was enforced forty
years ago. It was much criticised at the
time, but it does seem to have in it seeds
which might be watered and made to produce a plant which would be of some
use in any reorganised system. The particular question of the Betting Acts,
which has been so much referred to in
Belfast, could, of course, obviously be
dealt with, if not by transferring that
particular form of administration solely
to resident magistrates, by slightly extending the powers of the Betting Act
itself and making a repetition of the offence
punishable on indictment, that is to say,
securing the trial of the man, not by a
bench of magistrates but by a judge and
jury. And again, if it is thought that
magistrates are not as good as they might
be, does it not occur to the Department
that some help might have been given to
the magistrates? I have more than once
in Parliament reminded the House that
there was no modern book available for
a magistrate to learn his duties from.
Could not something of that sort be provided?
Passing from that, I come to the chief
objections. The suggestion to deprive
magistrates of their existing judicial
functions is, as anybody must know who
looks back, completely opposed to the
practice followed during the last 30 or 40
years, and is a most retrograde step.
There is no excuse, as it seems to me,
for depriving the public of their proper
share in the administration of justice.
Nor is there any reason that I can see for
depriving the country districts of the
advantage of that magisterial supervision
over the police and constabulary in their
neighbourhood which the present system
provides. Nor does there seem any very
great reason for casting such a reflection
on those gentlemen who have, usually at
some inconvenience and, perhaps, at some
expense, for very many years past, endeavoured faithfully to fulfil their commission and to deserve the confidence
which His Majesty has reposed in them.
If that step is taken, so far as I can
form an opinion, there will be throughout the country the deepest resentment.
No complaint, of course, can be made
against His Majesty, whose commission is
to be practically watered down if not entirely eliminated, for the King can do no
wrong; but there will be the deepest resentment against those who counselled
such a step. There will be general surprise, I think, that such a proposal should
emanate from a Government which is
supposed to be a Conservative Government. It is all very well for people to
remind us of Tennyson's lines:
That man's the true Conservative
Who lops the mouldered branch away,
but it has yet to be shown that this branch
is a useless branch, and if we are to quote
the poets at all we might, perhaps, for
the benefit of that particular Department,
quote the words of the American poet,
once referred to as "that distinguished
Englishman," John Russell Lowell. In
one of his poems he says:
Young folks is smart, but all aint
good that's new.
I guess the gran'thers, they knowed
su'thing too.
I think that is not inappropriate to a
Department or even a Ministry which has
not yet been 20 years in existence. On
the whole, I support the Address and I
do not support the Amendment, and at
a later date I shall have to make reference to the particular subject I have
mentioned.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
would like, in the first instance, to
associate myself with the remarks of
previous speakers, and pay a tribute to
the mover and seconder of the Address.
I would also like to associate myself with
the few words in reference to the noble
Leader of the House. We are all very
pleased to see him back again. I do hope
he will be spared in health and strength
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
22 NOVEMBER 1934
to fill the position. I also thoroughly
agree with what Senator Glendinning
said about the services of our good
Friend, Senator Leslie. We are all
indebted to him for the great interest he
takes in the work of the Senate, the
amount of time he devotes to it, and the
amount of good work he does all round.
We have, after the Address, an Amendment put forward by Mr. McLaughlin,
and that Amendment regrets that no
mention is made of any steps to protect
adequately the interests of the minority.
I listened very attentively to Senator
McLaughlin's speech, and I do not know
that he pointed to any case where the
interests of the minority are not in every
way protected. I do not know that he
pointed out any way in which they are.
not treated just as satisfactorily and as
well as any other persons in the community. We have in Northern Ireland a
Protestant section. We have Roman
Catholics. We have Jews and we have
atheists, and a considerable number of
each of these classes; and I do not know
that Jew complains about the treatment he gets. I do not know that the
atheist complains to any extent of the
treatment he gets. I feel that everyone
of them is treated alike, provided that
they are all law-abiding and loyal
subjects. And there are some countries
where those persons who are not lawabiding and are not loyal subjects are
treated in a different way from that in
which they are treated in Northern
Ireland. But that is just the difference.
any
Mr. McLaughlin complains they are
not being put into certain positions.
Surely he does not want the Government
to put into positions people on whom they
cannot rely? The British Government
made a terrible mistake at one time in
Northern Ireland. They allowed Roman
Catholics to be placed in some of the best
positions. They got all the positions in
the Post Office, and when the rebellion
took place they were there to give all the
assistance possible in opposition to the
British forces. Does he want the
Government of Northern Ireland to act
in a foolish and silly way like that? I
see reported repeatedly that the Prime
Minister states that all people in
Northern Ireland will get a fair crack of
the whip, and I am sure that every one
of them will get an opportunity to fill
any office they are competent to hold,
provided they prove themselves to be loyal
subjects and that they can be depended
upon to carry out the duties satisfactorily.
I, for one, would strongly oppose allowing
them to get into positions where they may
be able to act seriously and injuriously
against the Government of Northern
Ireland. This Government was handed
over to the Protestant people and loyal
people to work it, because they refused to
go into the Free State Parliament. I do
maintain that it is up to them, as loyal
people, to work this Government and to
see that it is not undermined by people
endeavouring, day by day, to do all
possible to weaken the Government,
blacken it, and undermine its power and
strength.
of
as
I think the most satisfactory words in
the Speech that we have heard are those
with reference to the great reduction in
unemployment. I am sure every one of
us is extremely pleased to find such a big
reduction and such an increase in persons
employed during the past twelve months
-some 14,000. I know an industrial
city like Belfast has very good reason to
be thankful that the change has taken
The
place.
ratepayers
Belfast
shouldered a big responsibility in endeavouring to provide for those so
fortunate as to be unable to find employment. Shipbuilding is evidently very
good in our city. Unfortunately it is
confined to one yard. I wish it was
possible for the other yard to be as successful in securing orders Messrs.
Harland & Wolff, because it means a great
deal idle even if one firm is busy and
another firm completely slack. In a
district like ours-I mean York Street and
Duncairn districts-the fact of Workman & Clark's being very slack is very
serious for the business people in the
district of which I have spoken. They
feel it very much, and I would be very
glad if it were possible for some work to
be obtained on that particular side of the
river. Other industries seem to be in a
fairly good position. The rope-making
and tobacco industries are doing very
well, and the ladies are doing all they
possibly can to make the tobacco trade
prosperous by smoking as many cigarettes
as they possibly can.
Reference has been made to the newlyestablished Unemployment Assistance
Board, and I, along with everybody else,
wish that board every success in their
efforts. They are starting under very
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
favourable conditions. There is a considerable reduction in the number of
unemployed, and that reduction is likely
to continue. Therefore the board has
a splendid opportunity, and I hope they
will do good work. There is only one
thing I would like to say with regard
to the establishment of that board. The
Government are now setting up a staff
and appointing their officials, and I hope
they will not create dissatisfaction in selecting their officials and staff. I have already
heard some complaints in that respect.
There are many men in the labour exchanges in Belfast who have been doing
hard work in connection with unemployment, and it is a great pity that when a
board of this kind is being set up their
services are not recognised. I hope the
Government will not be responsible for
creating any dissatisfaction in this respect, and that they will act fairly
towards those who have served the people
well in past years.
The most important matter referred
to in the Gracious Speech is the transport problem. We all recognise that
there is nothing more important to a
community than good transport, and we
must admit that the railways were very
unfairly dealt with in the past.
They
were opposed to very keen competition,
and they were handicapped as compared
with road transport by being required to
pay a good standard of wages and maintain good conditions for their employees. The result was that they could
not afford to reduce their fares to the
same level as road fares. I hope that
when the new scheme is put into
operation it will place the railways on a
sound basis, and that it will act fairly
towards all the interests concerned, so
that the community will reap the benefits of a good transport service in the
future. It would be almost impossible
to do without the railways in Northern
Ireland. They are a very necessary
means of transit, and I hope that whatever arrangements are made will prove
satisfactory to all concerned.
Reference has been made to the Summary Jurisdiction Bill. A considerable
amount of interest seems to be centred
around this particular Measure. There
is no doubt that a good many very fine
men have devoted very valuable services
to the administration of justice. They
seem to have taken a delight in attending the courts regularly and in acting
honestly and in a strictly fair manner,
but, as has already been said, there are
others who have not acted in that way,
and they are responsible for bringing
about this proposed change. I really
think it is a pity that such an old institution as this is to be disturbed, and
more particularly when a very respectable class of people are to be robbed of
the authority they have on account of
the actions and conduct of a few of
their number. It is a great pity that
something cannot be done to avoid the
drastic step proposed to be taken, but
it is very necessary that justice should
be administered fairly and impartially,
and if it is proved to me that there is no
other way out of the difficulty, I shall
be inclined to vote in favour of the new
Bill.
Senator McLaughlin deplored the fact
that no reference was made in the Speech
to the housing question. I do not think
that we in Northern Ireland are inclined
to keep pace with England and Scotland
in this respect. They claim that their
conscience has been moved in favour of
the removal of slums and the better
housing of the people. While builders
throughout Northern Ireland have been
doing good work since the introduction
of the subsidy by the Government, at
the same time an undertaking was given
to the Government that houses could be
built and let at a rent of 7/- a week,
and I do believe the Government gave
the subsidy on that understanding. It
was admitted that the Belfast Corporation could build houses to let at a rent
of 7/- a week, but the houses built with
the assistance of the subsidy, instead of
being let at 7/- a week, have been let for
as much as 8/6 and 9/- a week. That
is a pity, because there are a considerable number of people who are confined
in houses which are not fit to rear a
family in, and if houses were available
at 7/- a week many of these families
would be able to set up house in a
spectable way.
re-
During the past week two very
respectable men came to me and asked if
I would speak to the agent responsible
for the letting of some new houses. One
of them, a shipwright, said his present
house contained only a kitchen and one
bedroom for himself, his wife, and four
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
22 NOVEMBER 1934
children, and he was very anxious to get
one of these new and larger houses,
even though the rent was higher. The
other man who came to me was in
exactly the same position. It is impossible properly to segregate your family
when you have only one bedroom, and
I do think the Government should adopt
some scheme to provide a greater number of working-class houses at a low rent.
To increase the area of a house would
mean increased cost of building and
therefore an increased rent, so that I
think the Government are acting wisely
in confining the area to a certain size.
The Minister of Finance has said over
and over again that 6/- a week is a high
enough rent for a workman's house, but
I do not think it is possible at present
to build a house to let at that rent.
But if houses could be built to let at 7/-
a week it would go a long way to satisfy
the requirements of those people who are
at present so much in need of houses.
I am very pleased to give my support
to the Motion, but I am sorry I cannot
support the Amendment.
Mr. McMAHON: In listening to the
Speech from the Throne on Tuesday I
was particularly struck by the audacity.
of the Government in attempting to take
credit for the reduction in unemployment. We all know that so far as solving the unemployment problem is concerned the Government have done very
little. As a matter of fact they have
added to the number of unemployed.
We all know that the Belfast Corporation were doing their very best to
absorb as many unemployed as possible,
and were getting on very well indeed
until one morning the Minister of
Finance descended upon the City Hall.
He had an interview with our Lord
Mayor and informed him that it was
no longer possible for the Government
to continue the arrangement they had
entered into with the Corporation.
Therefore, for the Government to say
that they have been responsible for reducing the number of unemployed is
not true; in fact, the very opposite is
the case.
How is the Government proceeding to
help the unemployed. They are doing
nothing so far as creating new industries.
is concerned. They have passed an Act
enabling local authorities to give free
sites for new industries, but how many
crosss-Channel firms have taken free
sites on which to erect new factories. I
would say there are very few. On the
other hand, we have cross-Channel firms
coming over to Belfast and starting
shops. We have the Jewish tailors. I
say nothing about the Jews as a race.
We have many Jews in Belfast who are
very good citizens, but we have Jewish
concerns from across the water establishing shops in Belfast. They receive
orders for clothing, and during the same
night those orders pass in the mail to
cross-Channel factories to keep people
employed across the water, while the
tailor who used to make our clothes is
walking about the streets of Belfast, and
in all probability signing on at the
labour exchange.
In the Free State you see new industries springing up almost every day,
and the reason is this. The Government
of the Free State have the necessary
power to enable them to establish new
industries in that country. They can.
protect them, whereas we can give them
absolutely no protection. As a Parliament we seem to be a useless body, and
I can see no hope for the artisans of
Belfast except to continue to put up
with the industries we have at present.
I am told by a stevedore that three shiploads come to Belfast every morning,
and scarcely one cargo leaves at night.
Therefore we are buying from
Channel centres and we are exporting
practically nothing. Our export trade
is almost dead.
cross-
I agree with Senator Joseph Cunningham-and it is not often I agree with
him-in his remarks about the Summary Jurisdiction Bill. To a great extent I sympathise with the magistrates.
They have been at the beck and call of
the Government so long as the Government wanted them, and when the Government find they are of no further use to
them they are prepared to throw them
on to the scrap heap. Another matter
I want to refer to is the false economy
the Government is practising at the expense of a very helpless class of the
people of Northern Ireland, namely, the
blind. I know that the Government has
made it almost impossible for a person
who has gone blind to obtain a pension.
I raised this question at a meeting of the
Blind Persons Act Committee after a
number of applications had been turned
down on the ground that the applicants
421784
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. McMahon]
were not blind within the meaning of the
Act. When we asked Dr. Hall the
reason for this he wrote a letter to the
Committee and stated that in conjunction with the authorities they were
making it more difficult for these people
to get pensions. I do not know whom he
meant by the authorities, but I assume
the authorities are the Government. I
charge the Government with a violation
of the Blind Persons Act. That Act
was not passed by this Parliament; it
was passed by the Imperial Parliament
and transferred to Belfast before this
Parliament existed. There has not
been any change in the system across the
water in connection with the examination of people who are blind, and I say
that to economise at the expense of these
helpless people is false economy.
Indeed, when you come to consider the
enormous amount of money spent recklessly by the Government in all directions
I do say it is a shame and an outrage to
practise this economy against these helpless people.
We come now to the question of
housing. I heard Senator Cunningham
applaud the Government because of the
size of the houses allowed to be built. I
happened to go out to see a housing
scheme that has been established by Mr.
Stewart. If you saw those houses you
would wonder how anyone could live in
them, and certainly if anyone died
upstairs he would have to be taken out
through the window because you could
not get a coffin downstairs. I can only
give as a reason why the Government
brought down the size of the houses, that
they are being built for people with no
families. I suppose the Government is
encouraging birth control, because
certainly no one with a family could
occupy one of these houses for any considerable time.
Another matter I would like to refer
to.
I hear from time to time how fair
the Government is to all citizens, and I
was astounded the other day when I read
of Cabinet Ministers being on the platform in the Ulster Hall at a meeting at
which there were cries of "Get out the
guns!'
"Get out the shots." I
suppose if that meeting had been held in
St. Mary's Hall and I had presided, in all
probability the police would have dragged
me from the platform and brought me
before the magistrates. Evidently the
members of the Government can do
almost anything they like. I recollect an
occasion when a young man was arrested
because he told a meeting something he
believed in. I think the time has come
when the attention of the Imperial
authorities should be drawn to the violation taking place almost daily of the 1920
Act, whereby Cabinet Ministers can go
on a platform where people are incited to
riot and talk about drawing guns. This
is called peaceful Ulster! That is not
fair administration of the law. The two
Cabinet Ministers who occupied seats on
that platform should be ashamed of themselves. They are not fit or worthy to
occupy a seat in the Cabinet.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I should like to join.
in congratulating the mover and seconder
of the Address upon the way in which
they discharged their duty. Their
speeches were most interesting, and I am
sure that with what they said most of us
agreed. I have no doubt every Member
of the House is delighted to see that the
noble Lord the Leader of the House has
so far recovered in health as to be able to
be in his place again.
I wish now to say something in regard
to the Amendment. Of all the debates
I have ever listened to I never heard a
weaker case put forward. We have had
the usual sectarian feeling again introduced and re-hashed without any just
reason. The Amendment is weak and
groundless. Why do not our Roman
Catholic countrymen drop such a spirit
and assist the Government? That is their
duty.
Mr. McMAHON: The Government is
not fair to us.
Mr. QUIGLEY: There is no doubt
that very many
Roman Catholics are
happy and contented in Northern
Ireland. The Government had power to
abolish Proportional Representation after
three years.
At the first election under
Proportional Representation the Nationalists secured twelve seats; after four
years there was the same result. At the
third election, under single member constituencies, the Nationalists again secured
twelve seats. What use therefore was
Proportional Representation to them?
When this Northern Government was
formed the Roman Catholics did not come
to our assistance. Their members stayed
Debate on the Address
22 NOVEMBER 1934
Debate on the Address
away for a considerable time, and the
Roman Catholic people were advised not
to join the Royal Irish Constabulary. In
fact some of the teachers in the Derry
district would not take their salaries from
the Northern Government. Of course
they did so later when they found the
ridiculous position in which they had
placed themselves. Then later complaints
were made that so few members of the
Roman Catholic faith were taken into the
Royal Ulster Constabulary. Appointments were kept open for them, but they
did not avail themselves of them.
The housing question has been referred
to. Some £2,715,000 has been spent by
the Government in aiding the building of
houses for the working classes. Houses
completed to date number 25,000, and
those in course of
2,000. Did that
Government has
matter?
erection total some
not show that the
done well in this
Trade has been referred to as being
in a dull state. I read last night in the
"Belfast Telegraph" that Mr. McMahon
made a speech at a dinner in proposing a
toast, in which he said that trade was on
the mend but not that of the licensed
vinters. What are the facts in regard to
trade in Northern Ireland? We have the
busiest shipyard in the world. Sixteen
ships and one cruiser are being built
there. I wonder would it please the
Nationalist Party to see grass growing at
the slips on the Queen's Island?
Mr. McMAHON: No; it would not.
Mr. QUIGLEY: We never hear any
praise from them at all. It is always a
grumble against the Government. No
Government in the world has done more
for the people than the Northern
Government, and Lord Craigavon and
the Members of the Cabinet are deserving
of great praise. They have had trying
times: they have encountered many
difficulties but they have come out of the
ordeal with flying colours. No doubt the
Government has some critics. It would
be something of a novelty if everybody
praised them. Criticism of a wholesome
character is desirable and indicates an
intelligent interest in public affairs.
Criticism which is not intended to be
helpful but destructive is to be condemned. Our Government has been
subjected to both kinds of criticism but
on the whole there has been warm
approval of its actions.
A number of Bills are foreshadowed in
the Gracious Speech, but as we have not
had the text of them it is not possible to
deal with them to-day, but I would like
to make an allusion to the Summary
Jurisdiction Measure in so far as it
relates to the unpaid Magistracy. We all
admit that is a difficult subject to handle.
There are good magistrates who are a
credit to the Bench. But there are other
magistrates to whom that observation
does not apply. We all know about the
packed Bench. Such a thing should not
be tolerated. It is a disgrace to the
administration of justice, and the sooner
it is ended the better. Then there are
many magistrates who do not attend to
their duties at all. They have received
the honour of J.P. and so appreciate it,
that they are never or seldom seen on
the Bench. During the year 1933, out
of a total of some 181 magistrates in
Belfast, 119 or almost two-thirds did not
attend any of 311 sittings in the courts.
That is not as it should be.
The new Unemployment Act might
fitly be described as a charter for the
workers. For the first time in history we
recognise as a National responsibility the
support and welfare of the able-bodied
unemployed industrial workers and their
dependents. That is a big step forward
and is sure to be the means of relieving
many in distress. Then anything which
improves the health of the community is.
of paramount importance, and the Milk
and Products Act is an outstanding piece
of legislation. I believe that the production of milk in Northern Ireland is about
one hundred million gallons per annum,
and its value approximately £3,000,000.
The industry, therefore, demands special
attention so that its organisation may be
as perfect as possible and the supply of
milk pure and plentiful.
The Local Government Act was a
Measure long overdue. No doubt it
aroused a good deal of discussion. Some
thought that it did not go far enough,
but it remedies many defects and introduces many reforms which cannot fail to
be helpful to local government and public
health administration. The Road Traffic
Act was also very necessary. It is
directed to the problem of public safety
the roads. When it is stated that
during 1933 no fewer than 1,633 accidents
occurred involving the death of 141 persons and injury to 1,757 hon. Senators
on
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
will realise that there was urgent need for
legislation of this kind.
It is gratifying that trade and commerce have shown signs of improvement.
Perhaps this pleasing change in internal
business conditions can be traced largely
to the new fiscal policy which had the
effect of reducing the volume of foreign
goods dumped on our markets. Unemployment is slowly but steadily falling,
and on the whole the outlook is much
brighter.
The credit of Ulster stands high, no
doubt due to the fact that it has been
possible each year since the establishment
of our Northern Parliament to present
a balanced budget. All we want now is
to be left alone. We are proud to be an
integral part of the United Kingdom.
We stand for the unity of the King's
Dominions as a whole, and we are devoted to His Majesty. We are fully
satisfied with our autonomous State.
Senator McLaughlin referred, to-day, to
a speech I delivered in this House a few
days ago, and said I instanced only two
Roman Catholics who were holding important positions in the Northern Government. As a matter of fact I mentioned
three, but surely he did not expect me
to give the name of every Roman
Catholic employed by the Government.
He knows very well that there are many
more. I have pleasure in supporting
the Motion for the Address, but I certainly am not in favour of the Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Before I attempt to reply
to the various matters brought forward
in the course of the debate, I should like
to thank hon. Senators who have congratulated me on my return to this
House, and to say that I am very glad
to be back in my place. Although you
have got a somewhat healthier man than
you might have had two or three months
ago, you would need a very much more
clever man than I am to take up all the
points raised during the debate on the
Address and answer them all. I am unable to do that, and I shall only attempt
to refer briefly to a few of them.
hon. Member feels that he has not received
a sufficient answer to the points he raised
I hope he will ask me a question on the
subject when the House meets again.
If any
Before turning to the subject of the
Amendment, I should like to make a few
remarks in answer to Senator Leslie's
references to the Summary Jurisdiction
Bill. I gathered from his remarks that
he did not altogether care for some parts
of the Bill. Generally speaking, no one
likes a whole Bill, and the business of
the Senate is to regard any Bill as a
disagreeable Measure which it is its duty
to make right.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
and learned Senator asked what were
the reasons for bringing this Bill forward, and in general he made a speech.
which would have been very appropriate
to the Second Reading of the Bill. I
should prefer to postpone my answer to
his inquiries until the Bill is really
before this House. But I would like to
say that a Government, to be of any use
to a country, must look forward some
distance and discount the immediate unpopularity that a Measure arouses, and
if the Government has made up its mind
that a certain course is necessary it is not
necessary for it to accept recommendations from outside bodies. I would like
to dispose of the theory that any insult
to the general body of magistrates is
intended by this proposal. It is perfectly well recognised that the majority
of magistrates have performed their
duties well, but it may be thought that
the complicated life we lead entails the
abolition of part of their functions.
Now I would like to turn to the
Amendment. For the reasons I have
already given I cannot pretend to answer
all the points that hon. Members have
referred to, but there was one suggestion
I would like to refute in the strongest
possible way, and that is the suggestion
that the Government are trying to save
money at the expense of the blind. I
cannot give, at the moment, the details
I would like to give regarding this
question, but I think the hon. Member
(Mr. McMahon) is too much inclined to
take the opinions of other people rather
than that of the authorities responsible
for the administration of the Act.
should prefer that the hon. Senator
would raise the subject at a future date,
when I will be in a position to give him.
a detailed reply.
I
Debate on the Address
Debate on the Address
22 NOVEMBER 1934
I will now turn to my own Department, and reply very briefly to some of
the criticisms and charges of partiality
which have been levelled by hon. Members who have spoken in support of the
Amendment. Senator McLaughlin complained that his co-religionists paid their
fair share of rates and got nothing for
it. As Senator Leslie pointed out, the
salaries of teachers form by far the
greater part of educational expenditure,
and the Ministry of Education pays the
salaries of all teachers, whether Roman
Catholic or Protestant. The current expenditure of the Ministry of Education.
this year is £1,977,688, out of which
teachers get £1,341,450. The teachers
salaries absorb an enormous proportion
of the current expenses of the Ministry,
and they include Catholic and Protestant teachers alike.
Another point made was that the Government, in their expenditure on transferred and provided schools, paid an
undue amount to Protestant schools at
was
the expense of the others. I know perfectly well that the Roman Catholic
Church does not agree to the transfer of
its schools, and I am very sorry. The
chief reason for introducing the Amending Act of 1930
to safeguard
absolutely the religion of those schools
that had been transferred. That was
the main reason for the Amendment of
the Act. Some communities were afraid
that in the future the religious complexion
of the Government might change, and
their religion might be in danger.
Accordingly, the Act was amended to
make schools watertight against any possible interference on the part of any
Regional Committee. or Government
authority, and whatever applies to
Protestants in this case must necessarily
apply to Roman Catholics as well. If
the Roman Catholics say it is against
their religious convictions to transfer
their schools, I am sorry, but as Minister
of Education I have done everything in
my power to meet their wishes. Out of
1,223 voluntary schools in Northern Ireland there are 541 Protestant voluntary
schools which, apparently, do not mean
to transfer, and although the number
of Protestant untransferred schools is
almost as great as the number of Roman
Catholic Schools, they do not set up a
cry that they are unjustly treated and
have to pay for the benefit of other
people.
A third point I would like to deal
with is the question of the co-education
of the sexes.
The answer given the other
day by the Parliamentary Secretary in
another place appears to answer the
objection perfectly; but I would like to
add that in the Free State, which is a
Roman Catholic country, schools with
an attendance of forty or less are automatically amalgamated as from the
following 1st January, while in Ulster
the figure is fifty, and here the needs of
the school in question are carefully gone
into. Amalgamation is automatic in the
Free State, whereas in this country it is
by no means automatic.
In order to
help voluntary schools, and more
especially Roman Catholic schools which
did not wish to transfer, I might say
that the fifty per cent. grant given up
to the end of last year amounted to
£70,000, and it is a matter of regret to
me that the conceding of this sum
the part of the Government has borne
no fruit but that of further complaints
of intolerance and injustice.
on
Question put, "That those words be
there added."

Debate on the Address
Adjournment—
THE SENATE
Resolved: That an humble Address be
presented to His Grace in the following
words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to your Grace
for the Speech which your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament.
Ordered: That the said Address be presented to His Grace by Privy Councillors
of Northern Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint."
Mr. LESLIE: I take it we are not
likely to be called back this side of
Christmas?
Mr. LONG: In about a fortnight.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
Minutes after Five o'clock
until such date as Mr.
Speaker shall appoint.

pleased to give his Assent to the following Bill agreed upon by both Houses:-
Hume's Divorce.
Message from His Grace the Governor.

received the following Message from His

"I have received with great satisfaction
the expression of your thanks for the
Speech with which I opened the present
Session of Parliament.'
""

Adjournment-
The Late Senators
THE SENATE
Motion for Adjournment.
THE LATE SENATORS GYLE AND
MCBRIDE.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving,
"That this House do now adjourn," I
should like to say that we have heard
with deep regret since out last meeting
of the death of Senators Gyle and
McBride. I am sure the House will
desire to extend its sympathy to the
relatives of these hon. Senators. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. McMAHON: I would like to join
the noble Leader of the House in extending our sympathy to the relatives of
both our colleagues who have passed away
since we last met. Senator McBride was
formerly a Member of the other House
and rendered useful service there for a
It
very long time.
was within our
knowledge that he was coming to join
what we call the upper House, and when
he did join this House we were all delighted to see him. As regards Senator
Gyle, he also was formerly a Member of
another place. Later on he became a
Member of this House, and we all regret
he was not spared long enough to render
even more useful service than he did in
the comparatively short time he was with
us. I know from my personal contact
with Senator Gyle that he felt it a great
honour to be elevated to this House, and,
in conclusion, I would say that the
House is poorer to-day for the absence of
both these hon. Gentlemen. I am sure
our hearts go out in sympathy to the
families and other relatives of both hon.
Senators.
Mr. LESLIE: May we be told when
we meet again?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: On Tuesday for Second Readings of the Bills received to-day.
Mr. LESLIE: It is rather a bad day.

until Tuesday next.

McWilliams' Divorce Bill.

Question to Minister.
SETTING UP OF PIG RECEIVING
CENTRES.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is the
intention of the Government to set up
slaughtering of pig centres and pig receiving centres; and, if so, whether a
centre will be set up in Armagh, which
is in the heart of a large pig-raising area
and the centre of the County of Armagh.
In doing so, I would like to point out
to the Leader of the House that I
noticed in last night's paper that there is
a sitting of representatives of the Milk
Council in Newry to-day to hear evidence
from County Armagh and South Down
milk producers with regard to the fixing
of the prices of milk. With all due
respect, I would suggest that Newry is
not a convenient place for the inhabitants
of County Armagh to go to. It is in
County Down and on the borders of
South Armagh. I might respectfully
point out that a great proportion of the
constituency of South Armagh lies within six miles of Armagh and a great
portion of it is no less than 30 miles
from Newry; and seeing that Armagh is
the county town and in the very centre
of the County of Armagh, I think, from
the public point of view, it is not too
much to suggest that at least any meeting of the kind should be held in County
Armagh and in the county town.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The hon. Member will understand that the Government has no intention of embarking on any activity
connected with trading in pigs. Such
action is a matter entirely for the Pigs
Marketing Board. I understand that
this Board is at present co-operating with
the civic authorities in Armagh in the
establishment of a collecting depot for
live pigs, which will subsequently be sold
to curers in Northern Ireland or exported
to Great Britain.
With regard to slaughtering centres,
I am informed that there is no present
intention of establishing such centres in
Northern Ireland.
Diseases of Animals Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading
Diseases of Animals (Dairy Cattle) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: My right
hon. Friend the Minister of Agriculture
is, vicariously, occupying the whole time
at the disposal of the House to-day, but
his major problems are reserved for the
second place on the programme and the
short Bill which I am now introducing
need not, I think, cause us very much
trouble or perplexity. The matter with
which it deals is a purely administrative
one and its principle-for indeed it
raises no principle which is really newhas been accepted by all parties and will,
I am sure, recommend itself to Members
of this House.
Briefly, the purpose of the Bill is to
ensure a sufficient whole-time veterinary
service for Northern Ireland as a whole.
At the present time an elaborate code
of provisions, which strikes me as being
theoretically as nearly perfect as possible,
exists for dealing with animals affected
by diseases and for ensuring that the production of the milk supply is carried on
under proper sanitary conditions.
These
matters have long been dealt with by
the Dairies, Cowsheds and Milk Shops
Order of 1908, made by the Local Government Board for Ireland, and the
Bovine Tuberculosis (Northern Ireland)
Order, 1926, made by the Home Secretary before this service was transferred
to our Government. As an example of
the kind of provisions in force I may be
permitted to quote one Regulation which
is typical:-
"Every cowkeeper shall cause every
cowshed in his occupation to be ventilated by means of apertures to the open
air, of which apertures a number not
less than one-half of the number of
cows for which the cowshed is used,
with an area of not less than fifteen
square inches for each cow, shall be
provided in the sides of the cowshed,
at a height of not more than six feet
above the floor level, and these apertures shall be supplemented by an
opening or openings at or near the
highest point of the cowshed of a total
area of not less than twenty square
inches for each cow."
I do not think that for sheer prevision
and mastery of detail it would be possible to improve upon this kind of regulation. The only unfortunate thing is
that it has been found quite impossible
with the existing machinery at the disposal of local authorities, who are the
authorities under
responsible
these
Orders, to see that these regulations are
This
by any means properly observed.
is because local authorities have had no
alternative but to administer the regulations through part-time local veterinary
surgeons. These gentlemen derived most
of their income from private practice and
it stands to reason, therefore, that it was
contrary to their own professional interests that their duties as officers of the
local authorities should be successfully
performed. I do not say that in spite of
this the majority of them did not act
conscientiously and efficiently, but the
difficulty is obvious and was made the
greater by other factors with which it is
unnecessary for me to deal specifically.
Now it will be within the recollection
of hon. Members that in the recent Milk
and Milk Products Act, which dealt with
the whole milk system from the point of
view of both producer and consumer, a
general provision was made for the inspection of dairies by officers of the
Ministry of Agriculture. It will be seen
that in this way a certain duplication of
veterinary services has arisen. But while
that Act has, therefore, so far added to
the existing objections to the present
system of veterinary inspection, it has at
the same time provided a most fortunate
opportunity for introducing the appro-
The substance of the
priate remedy.
Act now before the House is, therefore,
to abolish the responsibility of local
authorities for this matter altogether and
centralise the administration
veterinary services relating to milk by
transferring their former powers and
duties to the Ministry of Agriculture.
This is really the whole purport of the
first two Clauses.
to
of
Clauses 3 and 5 are of a formal
character, while Clause 4-which protects
existing part-time veterinary officers in
local employment-was introduced during
the Committee stage in another place in
order to meet a general and natural feeling that nothing should be done to
the detriment of these officers who have
in the past carried out their duties with
exemplary proficiency, having regard to
the difficulties under which they worked,
and who have shown a spirit of cordial
co-operation in the drafting of the
present proposals.
57 Diseases of Animals Bill-

11 DECEMBER 1934
Second Reading
I would also call attention to Sub-
Clause (1) of Clause 2, which extends
the powers of the Ministry of Agriculture under the Milk Act to the case of
milk produced for the purpose of manufacture at the creameries. The Milk
Act itself, it should be remembered, deals
only with sweet milk offered for sale in
liquid form. This extension is obviously
a necessary one to complete the scheme
of supervision, for it is clearly just as
desirable that the public should be provided with pure and safe butter and
buttermilk as that the purity of the fresh
milk supply should be safeguarded.
Finally I should perhaps say a word
about the financial side of the Bill. In
this connection perhaps I may anticipate
some criticism from the right hon. Member, the Lord Mayor of Belfast, in view
of the fact that in the city of Belfast
the small additional rate which will be
necessary will not be compensated to the
same extent by savings upon directly rateborne expenditure. But taking the local
authorities as a whole, no additional expenditure will fall upon the rates through
the improvement of the veterinary inspection system, and I would point out
that no new financial powers are being
taken because the Ministry of Agriculture already has power to increase the
rate by which the Animal Diseases Fund
is financed from 1d to d, and this is
the power of which the Ministry intends
to avail itself. This additional id rate
will yield no more than local authorities
as a whole are at present spending on
the part-time veterinary services, and any
additional cost of the inspection scheme
over and above this amount will fall to
be borne by the Milk Fund. And so far
as Belfast is concerned, if there is a very
small increase in financial liability, on
the other hand it must surely be
admitted that no area stands to benefit
more greatly, from the consumer's point
of view, from what I think will prove
to be a very beneficial reform.
I beg
to move.
Mr. McALLISTER: I rise to ask for
some information from the noble Lord,
the Leader of the House, with regard to
Clause 4. He has said that this has
been introduced by way of amendment on
a unanimous expression of opinion in
another place. He has paid a tribute to
the qualified veterinary surgeons for the
very efficient way in which they carried
through their work. I am informed that
in some instances there were assistant
inspectors who were not qualified
veterinary surgeons, but who were
appointed at the same time and in the
same manner as the qualified veterinary
surgeons. I would ask the noble Lord if
it is the intention of the Government to
include these deputy inspectors in the
Bill. I submit that they have a case
equal to that of the qualified veterinary
surgeons.
Mr.
SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM :
There are one or two points on which I
would like some information. First of
all, is it intended that the provision of
Clause 2 of the Bill should apply to a
person who keeps a cow for his or her
own use. Personally I think it is, but
perhaps it would be better to have a
definite statement on the point. Then
in the Schedule the word "cattle" is
defined as having the same meaning as
that expression has in the Act of 1894.
It struck me that it would be very much
better to define in this Bill the exact
meaning of "cattle," instead of referring
us back to the Act of 1894. If I wanted
to find out whether cattle included goats
and sheep it would be very difficult to
obtain a copy of the Act of 1894 unless
one had a law library. It is a pity the
expression "cattle' is not defined in this
Bill.
or
Mr. LESLIE: The only question I
was going to ask the noble Viscount as
regards the Bill has, I think, been
pretty well discussed already, and that
is the exact influence on the finances of
local authorities that the Bill will have.
The noble Viscount said the Bill was
agreed to by all parties, and I do not
know whether he meant to include local
authorities amongst all parties,
whether he referred more to the people
concerned with the production and distribution of milk. Again, it seems to
me there is some reason to apprehend
that a further burden will be placed on
local authorities--and I am not speaking
of the Corporation of Belfast, for, as has
been pointed out, that has been run on
rather different lines. There is power
at the moment to raise a rate up to a
halfpenny for the purposes of the
Diseases of Animals Act, and at present
only a farthing is being raised, and I
Diseases of Animals Bill
Agricultural Marketing Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
gather from the noble Viscount that it is
thought that by raising the farthing to
a halfpenny there will be enough money
got to meet the extra expenses which this
Act will entail. Where I am in doubt
is whether there is going to be any relief
of the expenditure already defrayed by
local authorities out of the farthing rate.
That is the point that rather presents
a difficulty, and the particular point in
that connection is the proposal in Clause
4 that compensation is to be paid to a
veterinary officer who is displaced from
his employment. That would seem to
be a new and direct charge on a local
authority, and one would like to know
how it is to be met.
Again, I understood from what the
noble Viscount said that any additional
expense was to fall on the Milk Fund.
I do not find anything in the Bill which
indicates how that is going to be accomplished. I do not think there is anything more to be said about the Bill as
a whole. Undoubtedly Clause 2 does
involve a very considerable extension of
the Title, and what was done under the
Milk Bill we passed very recently, but
I do not think anybody would be very
much disposed to object to that if the
finances are all right, because no
could want to defend the maintenance of
unclean byres when it is possible at
comparatively small expense to have them.
clean. But I should like a little more
information on the incidence of the
finance.
one
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In reply
to Senator McAllister, I should like to say
that part-time lay veterinary inspectors
are not included in the compensation
Clause, because they will all discharge
other duties and will presumably retain
their employment in connection with those
other duties. In reply to Senator Samuel
Cunningham, the expression "cattle"
means bulls, cows, oxen, heifers and
is sufficiently
calves. I think that
comprehensive.
The Clause does cover
persons who keep cows for their own
use. With regard to the questions
raised by the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie), the chairmen and secretaries of local authorities have been consulted by the Ministry of Agriculture
with regard to the finances of the Bill,
and have expressed themselves as being
completely satisfied, as they have been
relieved of the payment of the salaries of
veterinary inspectors, which comes out of
If there are any other
the farthing rate.
questions I have not sufficiently answered
perhaps they will be raised again on the
Committee stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Monday next.
Agricultural Marketing (Pig Industry)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I understand that my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Agriculture, who is of course
responsible for the Bill which I am now
introducing, has already furnished hon.
Members of this House with very full
and detailed information as to its contents. Hon. Members will, therefore,
have realised that, while the Bill raises
no really new principle, it marks what
statesmen like to call yet another milethe road of economic progress.
stone upon
It is hardly more than a year since the
original marketing schemes for pigs and
bacon, under the Agricultural Marketing Act of 1933, came into force upon
lines broadly similar to those of the corresponding schemes in Great Britain.
Since that time the Pigs Marketing
Board has taken over the live pig industry; quite lately we have seen the
introduction of grading standards; and
now under the present Bill we are to
have a Pig Industry Council.
I shall not enlarge upon the quite
extraordinary success which, despite
occasional setbacks, has attended these
marketing schemes. Pig prices have
been stabilised at a steady level and the
pig population has shown a remarkable
increase, the total number of pigs having
risen between the summers of 1932 and
1934 from approximately 210,000 to
380,000. These figures speak for themselves. So far as the curing side of the
pig industry is concerned, I may add
that it is the aim of the Government
to increase the production of bacon of
the Wiltshire type of cure, and it is
gratifying to note that pig producers are
rapidly adopting the right type of pig
for this trade.
Agricultural Marketing Bill 11 DECEMBER 1934
Second Reading
It is hardly necessary for me to stress
the fact that the interests of the pig producer and the curer are to a very large
extent interdependent. These two interests being represented by separate
Boards, it was of course necessary in the
interests of the industry as a whole, and
particularly as regards the question of
prices, that there should be close and
friendly co-operation between the two
Boards. Hitherto the function of
fixing prices has rested with the Pigs
Marketing Board, but in practice that
Board has consulted with the Bacon
Marketing Board before prescribing
prices which have been binding only
upon producers, and up to the present it.
has not been an offence to buy below
the prices fixed by the Pigs Marketing
Board. It has now been considered
desirable to set up a kind of super Board
empowered to deal authoritatively with
the all-important question of prices, and
the creation of such a body, under the
name of the Pig Industry Council, is
the prime object of the Bill now before
the House. Prices when fixed by this
Board will have statutory force and will
be binding both upon seller and buyer.
The provisions constituting the Council
are contained in Clauses 4 and 5, and
it will be observed that, apart from
fixing prices, the Council has been invested with certain other important
functions of an advisory character.
The rest of the Bill for the most part
Ideals with matters of administrative detail. It might be argued that certain of
these matters should have been left to
be administered by the Bacon Marketing Board, or the Pig Industry Council,
rather than by the Ministry of Agriculture, but it is consistent with the policy
of the Ministry to take over somewhat
invidious functions such as these directly.
A Government Department has to become hardened to making itself unpopular when necessary, and it has to be
practised in finding the best ways and
means of solving what is perhaps the
central practical difficulty implied in the
democratic theory-how to maintain an
efficient administration in a manner consistent with popular responsibility. And
any Government on the British model
has the advantage worked out through
the experience of generations, that through
Parliament it has always one finger upon
the pulse of the people. Parliament
thus acts as a medium between the Government and the people whose daily
lives are affected by its actions, which
enables the executive to function on the
whole efficiently without any danger of
irresponsibility. The Marketing Boards
are obviously in no such well entrenched
position.
This brings me back to the underlying
principle of these marketing schemes, and
I should here like to say a few words
with regard to it. We have recently
heard a great deal about the evils which
arise when the State intervenes directly
in matters of industry or trade. But I do
not think there are many people to-day
who would be ready to declare that the
State should not in any way concern itself
with these matters. Indeed, we are all
anxious that the State should do so up
to a certain point. But when State intervention begins to affect us personally
or to require any modification in the way
in which we carry on our own businesses,
we are very inclined to proclaim that the
action of the State is wrong in principle
and is hampering development.
Let us then consider the position with
regard to the action of the State in connection with the pig and bacon industries. It is just three
years this
month since the then recently formed
National Government announced that,
provided a suitable scheme for the reorganisation of the pig and bacon industries in the United Kingdom could be
formulated, it would be prepared to introduce a Measure of quantitative regulation of imports of bacon and hams
into this country. As a consequence of
this announcement the Re-organisation
Commission for Pigs and Bacon was set
up and the pigs and bacon marketing
schemes in Great Britain and Northern
Ireland were introduced. Meanwhile
the Government has carried out its
pledge to control imports of bacon into
this country. During the nine months
ended 30th September, 1934, imports of
bacon and hams into the United
Kingdom amounted to 6,375,000 cwt., as
compared with 7,707,000 cwt. in the
same period of 1933, and 9,141,000 cwt.
during the same months of 1932. In
other words, as a result of Government
action, of Government interference with
trade, the quantity of bacon and hams.
imported into the United Kingdom
during the first nine months of this year
63 Agricultural Marketing Bill

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
was some two-and-three-quarter million
cwt. less than during the same period of
1932.
But if imports from abroad are thus
restricted in the interests of the industry
at home, it is inevitable that some degree
of control should also be imposed upon
the home market. I am aware that
there are many pig producers and many
bacon curers who, while so far welcoming
the action of the Government, would have
preferred that the Government should
have stopped at restriction of imports and
have left the home trade absolutely unregulated. Such a state of affairs would,
however, have been impossible to contemplate. The dangers to the food
supply of the country are obvious, and
the interests of the huge mass of consumers who make up the bulk of our
population could hardly be so lightly
disregarded. If the Government cuts off
two-and-three-quarter million cwts. of
bacon from foreign sources during nine
months of the year, it is imperative that
the Government should take steps to
ensure that an adequate supply of bacon
and hams will be forthcoming from the
home industry. That is in essence the
reason why it has been necessary to introduce the Pigs and Bacon Marketing
schemes, and why it is necessary to introduce the present Bill in order to render
more effective the machinery which
exists for the control of this industry.
I think that is all I need now say on
this subject. I am sure the House as a
whole will be ready to congratulate the
Minister of Agriculture upon the past
success of his scheme, as well as upon
the present proposals. We welcome the
right hon. Gentleman to this House
to-day, and he will be pleased to reply
to any points raised in the course of the
debate.
Mr. LESLIE: I am inclined to think
that the House will be disposed to accept,
at all events, the principle of the Bill, and
they will accept the Bill really as not being
in itself a new departure, but rather a
development of a policy which has been
brought into force and action now for
some few months. The noble Viscount
spoke of a milestone on the path of progress. Milestones undoubtedly do suggest
roads to our minds. I am afraid that one
has to think that roads are not necessarily
flat. Sometimes they go uphill, and
sometimes they go downhill. We hope
the particular road that this Bill has
started its career on will be, if not flat,
perhaps slightly uphill, or, at all events,
that it will not be downhill, that is to
say, that it will ultimately lead to a
better and fuller production of bacon
than has been the case in the past with
resulting profits for those engaged in
the trade.
I do not think there is anything of a
serious nature that could be alleged
against the Bill, but I do confess to being
somewhat afraid as to whether its terms
are not too wide, that is to say, whether
the Bill is not drawn so as to include in
its-I was going to say net-but its pen
or style every variety of pig. There are
words through the Bill that do suggest
very wide application. I am inclined to
think that, perhaps, the application is
not intended to be so wide. If I understand the Bill correctly it is aimed at
securing better control of the pig which
is a potential bacon producer. All pigs
are potential bacon, but I do not think
we want to go quite so far as that. I
think the Bill is intended to deal with
the pig, be it alive or dead, that is ready
for immediate transformation into
bacon.
or un-
If that is so it would not be desirable
to apply any control to the creel pig, the
small pig, which, if it lives long enough,
passes into the stage described as the
young pig, the intermediate
finished article, which does pass from
hand to hand through the markets
occasionally, and is not really an animal
which comes under the consideration of
the curers or purchasers for the curers.
It is not the sort of pig which would be
shipped alive under control or in any
scheme or would be ready for immediate
transformation into bacon. It occurred
to me that, perhaps, the most convenient method of discussing that matter
is to put something on the Paper for the
next stage relating to the definition of
pigs in order that we might discuss the
subject and see whether any circumscription the Department should be
necessary, and if so, what form it should
take. I do ask the Minister whether I
am correct that the main point of the
Bill is the pig about to be converted
into bacon.
65 Agricultural Marketing Bill—-

11 DECEMBER 1934
Second Reading
There is another matter that I would
bring before the Committee at a later
stage, but I may, perhaps, mention it
this afternoon, that is, the proposal in
Clause 7 to make regulations about grading and that sort of thing. There is, I
understand, a feeling that existing trade
marks and trade descriptions should be
protected and that words to that end
ought to be inserted at a later stage.
I
The only other matter I want to speak
about is the Pig Industry Council. Rather
like the noble Lord, I think this Council
is going to be of very material assistance
to us. It ought, so to speak, to get a
front seat in the gallery, and I would like
reference to it introduced into the title
of the Bill. That would involve a verbal
alteration-but nothing material.
think that ought to be done. It would be
desirable for the purpose of reference
hereafter. I am not going to suggest that
the title as it stands is misleading. It is
in accordance with precedent and, of
course, perfectly accurate, but it might
be made a little more helpful. I do think
the Council is entitled to specific mention.
Taking the Bill as a whole, I certainly
support it, but I would like a little more
information as to the pigs it is intended
to include.
Sir BASIL BROOKE: I am obliged to
the hon. Senator for his concurrence in
the general principle of the Bill. The
point which he would like enlightenment
on is the question whether control is to
cover only pigs that are eventually to
become bacon, or whether it includes other
pigs. May I point out that if there was
no control over all pigs about to be killed,
whether for bacon or for fresh pork, there
would be a liability-in fact it has
occurred of an animal going into the
fresh pork market being bought by curers
for the bacon side. Therefore, it is
necessary for the Pigs Marketing Board
to have control over all pigs with the
exception of a very small class, such as
fat sows. The vast majority of pigs,
whether pork or bacon, are controlled
for that reason. I think that answers
the hon. Senator.
Mr. LESLIE: What about the
younger pig-the very small pig?
Sir BASIL BROOKE: The younger
pig is not controlled, because it is not in
the edible stage. The pig that might
be converted into bacon is controlled. It
does not necessarily mean he is going to
make bacon. His fate is not known
until a later date. The young pig, which
is not likely to make bacon and the fat
SOW are not included in the control of
pigs. With regard to trade marks, that
matter was raised in another place, and
I gave an assurance there and I would
like to repeat it now-that trade marks
will not be interfered with in any way.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Monday next.
Consolidated Fund Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That
this Bill be now read the Second time."
It is unnecessary for me to say anything
with regard to this Bill. It is a Money
Bill, which we have no power to amend.
The Bill explains itself.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, That this Bill be now committed. (Mr. Long.)

until Monday.

speaks about next Monday. I wonder

as to what will happen next week. We
sit on Monday which, I think, is the
17th. We will want to sit on Tuesday,
Wednesday and Friday? Could he tell
us anything? It would be more convenient for making arrangements.
B
Sittings of the House 68
Adjournment-
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Monday is
the 17th. I do not like to forecast
exactly how many days the House will
sit. What I intend to do is to take the
Committee stage of these two Bills on
Monday, the Report on Wednesday, and
the Third Reading and passing on Thursday. But if the House will give me an
assurance that they would not mind
taking all the stages after the Committee stage together, and would make
no reference to the suspension of Standing Orders that any Member of the
Government could possibly object to in
any circumstances whatever, I have got
no objection to taking the Committee
stage on Tuesday and the rest of the
stages on Thursday. I would like to
meet the wishes of the Senate within
the extent of 24 hours one way
another.
or
Mr. LESLIE: Monday and Wednesday?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I stand by
my proposal that the House do adjourn
until Monday.

Agricultural Marketing (Pig Industry)
Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Resolved, "That this House do now
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Power of Ministry of
Agriculture to regulate bacon production), 2 (Restrictions of granting of
new licences to bacon-curers), 3 (Increase
of fee for bacon-curer's licence), 4 (Constitution of Pig Industry Council), 5
Duties and Powers of Pig Industry
Council), and 6 (Payment of contribution
or levy due to marketing board) ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Grading of pigs and
bacon, and prices for graded pigs).
(1) The Ministry of Agriculture may
by regulations prescribe the persons by
whom, the manner in which, and the
places at which, pigs and bacon, or
any descriptions thereof, are to be
graded, and the manner in which pigs
or bacon, or any descriptions thereof,
are to be marked, packed, stored,
adapted for sale, insured, advertised
or transported.
(2) When any regulations under
sub-section (1) of this section with respect to the grading of pigs are in
force, it shall be the duty of the
Council to determine the prices at,
below or above which and the terms
on which pigs, or any description of
pigs, belonging to each prescribed
grade may be sold, and the provisions
of this Act with respect to the sale and
purchase of pigs or any description of
pigs shall apply accordingly.
(3) Any person who sells or offers or
exposes for sale, or buys, pigs of
any description otherwise than in
accordance with the prescribed grade
applying to such pigs shall be guilty
of an offence under this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of sub-clause (1) to insert:
"Provided that no such regulation
as to the marking, packing or adaptation for sale of pigs or bacon or any
description thereof shall be made so as
to interfere with the lawful use by
any person of a particular mark or
brand for the purposes of his trade or
business.'
The matter to which this Amendment
relates appears to have aroused rather
more public attention perhaps than one
would have expected. However, there
seems to be a feeling that some particular
attention should be given to existing
Agricultural Marketing Bill—
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
marks and brands and things of that
sort. I believe I am correct in saying
that the matter was raised in another
place. It has certainly been discussed in
other places, which is not quite the same
thing and not, perhaps, inclusive.
Finally I, myself, mentioned it on
Second Reading. I do not think there
is anything to be said as regards the
actual wording of the Amendment on the
Paper, which follows precedent as nearly
as may be. I therefore think I need
only submit the words on the Paper to
the judgment of the Committee. I beg
to move.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: I will
accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Enforcement of certain
determinations of Pigs Marketing Board).
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, at the end of the Clause to add
the following sub-section:
"(2) Nothing in section five or
section seven of this Act shall operate
so as to render it unlawful for the Pigs
Marketing Board, acting under and in
pursuance of the pigs marketing
scheme, to sell pigs or any description
of pigs belonging to any grade at such
prices and on such terms as the said
Board may from time to time determine, or for any person to buy those
pigs from the said Board."
The object of this new sub-section is
not to place the Pigs Marketing Board
outside the jurisdiction of the Pig Industry Council in regard to the fixing of
prices, but to ensure that the Board will
not be handicapped in what will now be
its primary function of marketing surplus pigs which are offered to it by producers who cannot sell these pigs to
curers or to pig exporters. As hon.
Senators are no doubt aware, the Pigs
Marketing Board has a duty to purchase
surplus pigs from registered producers,
provided that these pigs are offered to it
alive. In purchasing these surplus pigs
the Board will require to pay the prices.
fixed by the Pig Industry Council so that
producers will be assured that they will
always have in the Pigs Board a buyer
for their pigs at the prices fixed by the
Council. These surplus pigs purchased
by the Board will normally be exported
to Great Britain and sold to curers there
—generally at a loss which, except in so
far as it may be reduced by occasional
profits, will then be recouped by means
of a trading levy imposed by the Pigs
Board under the authority of an Order
by the Ministry of Agriculture made
under the Agricultural Marketing Act,
1933. In practice, however, it may be
desirable that the Pigs Board, instead of
exporting surplus pigs itself, should sell
these pigs to dealers in Northern Ireland
who, by virtue of their knowledge of the
requirements of the pig trade in Great
Britain, may be able to dispose of these
pigs to better advantage than the Pigs
Board could do itself.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 9 (Effect of determinations
of Ministry of Agriculture and Pig Industry Council on contracts), 10 (Records
and returns), 11 (Powers of entry and
examination and weighing of pigs), 12
(Power of Ministry of Agriculture to make
regulations), 13 (Offences, prosecutions
and application of fines), and 14 (Pig Industry Fund) ordered to stand part of
the Bill.

*

"Pigs" means pigs produced in
Northern Ireland and includes carcases
of pigs:

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the
definition
to
of "Pigs"
leave out
