It may be remembered that when we
were discussing the Second Reading of
this Bill I said I would put something
on the Paper in relation to this matter.
The exact phrase, I venture to think,
"put something on the Paper" conveys
something in the nature of a special
mearing to Members of this House. The
point seemed to me to be that we ought
to make sure that we were not giving
Agricultural Marketing Bill 17 DECEMBER 1934

TITLE
wider powers than the circumstances of
the case require. We understand, of
course, that the intention is to control
the bacon pig, that is, the pig that is
going to be immediately converted into
bacon. That is, if I may say so, comparatively simple.
It becomes a little more difficult when
we introduce the pig which may be converted into fresh pork, because your
bacon
pig must arrive, if not at years of discretion, at all events, at a certain time
of life, but the fresh pork pig may be
converted into pork almost at any stage
in his life. That is the difficulty, but as
it is important we should not give any
wider powers than are required, I think
the Committee should consider the
matter. In doing that, in taking care
that the powers are not too wide the
Committee, I am sure, will be equally
careful to
that the powers
sufficient for the purpose of the Bill.
Therefore, in order that we may discuss
the subject, and in order that we may
hear what the Minister of Agriculture
has to say on the subject, and what
advice he has to give us, I beg to move the
alteration as it appears on the Paper.
see
are
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is not
possible for the Government to accept
this Amendment. It will be open for
the Council, under Clause 5, to determine
the description of pigs for which fixed.
prices shall be prescribed. In regard to
young pigs, the Council may decide that
no fixed price shall operate, but that the
price to be paid shall be that which
obtains on the open market. I do not
think the hon. Senator need fear that
the Council will fix prices for young
pigs, but the exact age or weight at
which pigs should be brought within the
scope of the prices fixed by the Council
is a matter for careful consideration and,
in the view of the Government, the discretion of the Council in this matter
should be left unfettered.
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite content to
ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 16 (Commencement of the
Act), and 17 (Short title) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
A Bill to provide for the regulation
of the production of bacon and hams.
and to make further provision with
respect to the licensing of baconcurers; to amend the enactments with
respect to the marketing of agricultural
products in their application to pigs,
bacon and hams; to provide for the
appointment of a body of persons for
certain of the said purposes; and for
other matters incidental thereto.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Leave out from "provide for the" to the
end of the Title, and insert "establishment of a Pig Industry Council; and for
others matters incidental to the purposes
aforesaid."
Mr. LESLIE: The amendment of the
long title is, I may say, rather an
unusual occurrence in this House. The
long title is of very considerable importance. It has been drawn with very
great care and has to be drawn with a
certain elasticity, because when the Bill
first sees the light of print, with the long
title appended, it is quite impossible to
forecast what either Chamber of Parliament may do to the body of the Bill.
Consequently it may happen that it does
not in the final stages altogether come up
to expectations-not perhaps expectations
-it is not perhaps quite as explicit as
it might be made. I imagine for that
reason it has always been the practice to
postpone the Title until all the Clauses
have been gone through.
In the case before us it seemed to me
that it is very desirable, as I said on a
former occasion, to bring the Pig Industry Council into the very forefront of
the proceedings. It is entitled, on account
of its importance, to have a place in the
Title of the Bill, and, accordingly, I
suggest a slight alteration. I do not
propose to move the actual words on the
Paper. Investigation shows, I think,
that the best thing to do if we desire,
as I think we do, to give the Pig Industry Council its proper prominence,
would be to amend the Title by means
of two short Amendments. I suggest we
should, after the words, "licensing of
bacon-curers," insert the words, "to provide for the establishment of a Pig In-
Agricultural Marketing Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
dustry Council;".
Further on, which
""
would be a second Amendment, I propose to leave out the words, "to provide
for the appointment of a body of persons.
for certain of the said purposes,
because
that body of persons is now the Pig Industry Council. If the Committee agrees
to both these Amendments-and for the
moment I am moving only the first-the
whole long Title will read:-
To provide for the regulation of the
production of bacon and hams and to
make further provision with respect to
the licensing of bacon-curers; to provide for the establishment of a Pig
Industry Council; to amend the enactments with respect to the marketing of
agricultural products in their application to pigs, bacon and hams; and for
other matters incidental thereto.
I think that on the whole that revised
Title will give satisfaction, and it will
be expressed in language which is
sufficiently clear and sufficiently explicit.
Accordingly I beg to move, that after the
words, licensing of bacon-curers," we
insert the words, "to provide for the
establishment of a Pig Industry Council."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: Consequentially, I now
move we leave out the words, "to provide
for the appointment of a body of persons
for certain of the said purposes.'
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I accept
that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Title, as amended, agreed to.

with Amendments to the House.

resume.

Bill reported with Amendments.

19 DECEMBER 1934
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 19th December, 1934.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Labour:
Contributory Pensions (Exempt and
Excepted Persons) Amendment Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1934.
Ministry of Commerce:
The Census of Production (1936)
Order (Northern Ireland), 1934.
Laid before the House (pursuant to

Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's
sitting for the purpose of taking the remaining stages of the Agricultural
Marketing (Pig Industry) Bill."-
(Viscount Charlemont).
Question to Minister.
SALE OF MILK.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the notice
to milk producers recently published and
affecting proceedings on and after Sunday last has any, and if so what, application to a milk producer selling the milk
of his own cows direct to persons desiring
to consume such milk in liquid form;
and, further, whether any information
can be given as respects the selling prices
of "C" milk in the country districts, and
as to whether any licence for such sales
is at present required or is likely to be
required in the immediate future.
The question really breaks itself into
three parts. I imagine that the House
has seen the notice to milk producers and
distributors which I hold in my hand.
It is rather a long notice, and it appears
to be limited to milk producers and dis-

tributors. It does not on the face of it
apply to people who sell their own milk,
although they are producers, if they sell
it, not to a distributor or third party,
but direct to the consumer. I want first
of all to know whether that notice, which
prescribes amongst other
other things the
price to be charged in Belfast, Lisburn
Bangor and Holywood-I want to know
whether that has any application to the
man who, as I say, sells direct to the
consumer, not through a distributor, and
conducts that sale in one of the country
districts outside that area.
Then I want to know whether we can
have any information as to the price
which is to be prescribed for sales of “C”
milk outside the given areas, and,
further, whether it is necessary to have
a licence at present to sell milk under
those conditions. The question really
raises the point as to whether that particular provision of the Milk and Milk
Products Act of the present year has yet
come into force. That is the Clause
which says the provisions of the Act
with respect to the sale of milk according to grades of quality, and so on, and
in accordance with licences issued in
pursuance of this Act, shall come into
operation on such day as may be
appointed by the Minister of Agriculture. The question is whether such a
day has
has been appointed and made
applicable to the country districts.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The notice to which the
hon. and learned Senator refers applied
to all persons who produce and sell milk
for human consumption. Such persons
are required to hold a producer's licence
for the production and sale of milk of
Grade A, Grade B or Grade C.
All sales of milk by wholesale by a
producer must be made at the price of
1s 24d per gallon for milk of Grades B
and C and at not less than 1s 4d per
gallon for milk of Grade A, but no
retail prices for milk have as yet been
fixed or approved for any areas
except Belfast County Borough, Bangor
Borough, Holywood and Lisburn Urban
Districts. Retailers elsewhere are therefore at present subject to no restrictions in regard to the prices which
they may charge. I understand, however,
that the Joint Milk Council is at
Diseases of Animals Bil.

Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
present engaged in considering whether
retail prices should be fixed for areas
other than those indicated.
With regard to the last part of the
question, I would emphasise that all
producers in any part of Northern Ireland who sell milk for liquid consumption
must hold licences from the Ministry of
Agriculture.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I take it
that producers who sell milk retail can
up to the present charge whatever price.
they like, but retail producers who sell
from their own dairy door will still have
to pay threepence a gallon to the Government, no matter what price they
charge. The difficulty about that is, of
course, that that threepence would come
off the producer, because the consumer
would come for small quantities of milk
and the producer would get so much less.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of the
reply of the noble Viscount, it would
seem that any producer who wishes to
sell to his own clients in the country
districts direct is, unless he has got a
licence, summarily put out of business.
That seems to be the position.
I
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: Arising out of
what Senator Hill Dickson has stated, is
it not a fact that the producer can sell
milk and save the threepence a gallon
which the retailer has got to pay.
understand that the retailer must pay
threepence a gallon to the Government,
whereas the producer need not do so if
he sells direct to the public. I would
like to have that confirmed, because I
think it would be a great injustice to the
general public if a producer, instead of
supplying his milk direct to the public,
supplies it to a retailer, and that that
retailer should be obliged to pay threepence a gallon to the Government, and
thereby pass on that charge to the
public, who will eventually have to
pay it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The licence
fee is due by the producer.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I take it that
no matter who sells Grade "C" milk,
he has to pay threepence a gallon to the
Government for every gallon he sells.
So.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: That is
Diseases of Animals (Dairy Cattle) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before I
actually move the Third Reading of this
Bill I should like to say that my
attention has been drawn to two rather
serious inaccuracies in some of the Press
reports of the remarks which I made in
this House last week on the Second
Reading of this Bill. When I first
noticed them I came to the conclusion
that I had made the mistakes myself,
but I dismissed that idea as unthinkable.
(Laughter.) I then came to the conclusion that the reporters had made the
mistakes, but competent reporters assure
me that they never make mistakes, so I
came to the conclusion that it was the
acoustics of this Chamber that had given
rise to these rather serious inaccuracies.
I have been reported as saying in
reply to the hon. Member, Senator
McAllister, that part-time veterinary
inspectors are not protected by the compensation Clause.
Of course this would
not be correct, as the whole object of the
Clause is to protect the interests of these
gentlemen. What I did say was that
the lay part-time inspectors that is, the
assistant inspectors who are not fully
qualified veterinary surgeons were not
so protected.
The other point is that in the report
of my reply to the hon. Member, Senator
Cunningham's query with regard to the
meaning of the expression "cattle" in
Clause 2 the small but important word
"not" has intruded itself, with the
result that my words have been turned
in the sense directly opposite to that to
which they were intended. The Clause
does, of course, cover persons who keep
cows simply for their own use.
I have thought it worth while to make
this statement in the hope of dispelling
any misapprehension which may possibly
have arisen upon these two points,
which are of some importance to the
people immediately affected. I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Adjournment.
INCIDENT IN A BELFAST
TRAMCAR.
PRICE OF FEEDING STUFF.
Moved: "That this House do now
adjourn."-(Viscount Charlemont).
Mr. BARCLAY: I just wish to bring
before the House a rather serious incident that took place here a short time
ago within the last fortnight. The
wife of a very respectable workingman.
was travelling by tramcar between York
Road and the Antrim Road, by Duncairn
Gardens. This good lady got on the
tramcar at York Road, nothing seeming
to be the matter with her. She got a
ticket for the tramcar, but before she
got to the Antrim Road she took a weak
turn. The tramcar conductor thought
she was under the influence of drink,
and of course he called a policeman, and
the policeman took the woman in the
tramcar to the police barrack. She
was put into a cell, and it is reported
that she was kept there four hours. Of
course, I should say I do not know
whether this is authentic or not. At the
end of that time she was taken to the
Union Infirmary, where she died a few
hours afterwards.
What I want to know is, is a case
like this left to the wisdom of the police
and the tram conductor to say whether
this woman was seriously ill or whether
she was drunk. They came to the conclusion that she was drunk, when she
was not drunk. She was practically
dying, because she had been ailing for a
considerable time previous to that and
troubled with her heart. It was left to
the wisdom of the police force to say
whether the woman was drunk or
seriously ill. I think that is a serious
mistake.
The duty of the police should be to
call in a doctor. I just raise this
question, so that the authorities might
get to understand that the public at
large are not very well pleased at this
incident, and also that in future should
a case of this kind occur, instead of
taking the person to the barrack and
keeping her there a prisoner for a number of hours, a doctor should be called
at once and on his advice the person
should be either kept or taken to some
place where something could be done for
her. I simply raised the question.
I
There is another matter I should like
to mention. I was going to speak on
the Third Reading of the last Bill about
pork, and I will take this opportunity
to mention what I was going to say. I
find out when in the country that the
farming community are well content
with the price they are getting for their
pork, but they inform me that they are
making no money, and they are very
little better off than when they were
taking the pork to the open market.
made inquiry as to why this should be
so, pointing out that over the Border
at that time they were getting from 40s
to 45s a cwt., while here they are getting
58s 5d or 58s 6d, or something like that.
There is a great difference between the
two. That is not where it comes in.
We control pork and the curers are controlled and held up to a specified price,
but the man who provides the feeding
stuff is not controlled and he can charge
what he pleases.
Adjournment-
Price of Feeding Stuff
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
I understand that pig feeding has gone
up to such an extent that the effort the
Government is putting forth to benefit
the farmers is not benefiting them at all,
but the benefit is going into the pockets.
of another. I was wondering if something could not be done about feeding
stuff so that the farmer would have a
chance of making something at the
present time. I understand that the
feeding stuff is got from a very important manufacturer of food stuff in
the city of Belfast, and as it goes
through the hands of two or three other
people, and each one of them gets a
certain percentage of profit, the farmer
has got to pay all these. That is where,
I believe, the money is wasted that the
farmer should have in his pocket.
am not speaking on behalf of the strong
farmer, that is the man who is able to
buy large quantities of feeding stuff
from the wholesaler. He can buy a ton
or five tons or ten tons if he likes, and
can put down the money, and he gets
a percentage of that money returned.
It is the small farmer I am speaking for
who is not in that position. Some of
them have not even the credit to get it
until such time as they receive their
money for pork. They have not the
money to put down, with the result that
it comes very heavy on them to meet the
bills. I ask can something be done for
the poor farmer by controlling food stuff
the same as pork is controlled.
I
Sir DUDLEY MCCORKELL: I have
listened with considerable interest to the
last speaker, but would just like to draw
the attention of the House to something
which may be known to some, but not
to all. If the price of pork satisfies the
farmer that fact is very pleasing to some
of us.
With regard to the price of feeding stuff, I should have thought by now
almost every seller of pigs would know
that the price he gets is fixed on several
factors, one of which is the price of
feeding stuff. As the price of feeding
stuff rises or falls so that factor is taken
into account in the price of pork.
Furthermore, the price factor is the
retail price of feeding stuff ascertained
by the Minister of Agriculture for the
preceding three months. There is sometimes some lag for that reason, but
ultimately the rise of price is shared
between the bacon curer and the seller
of the pig. The factor is controlled by
the retail price as ascertained by the
Ministry of Agriculture, so there is no
force in the complaint.
Mr. McLAUGHLIN: I would disabuse the hon. Senator's mind in regard
to farmers being pleased with the price
they are getting for pork. If farmers
were allowed to sell pork direct to other
people at the present time they would
be getting a much better price. I am
speaking from experience. I was speaking to a farmer on Monday evening, and
he tells me that the farmer when he sells
his pork has got to lose too much time.
The position with regard to the sale of
pork, I understand, at the present time
is that the farmer must first get a ticket.
when he brings the pork into the store.
To get that ticket entails the loss of a
day on the farmer. Again, he has got to
lose a day delivering the pork. When
he delivers it he has got to wait for the
cheque to come along. He has got to
lose another day going into town to cash
the cheque. I submit when everything
is taken into consideration-the charges
of the Pigs Marketing Board and the
different deductions that are made, as
well as the loss of time the farmer is now
put to when everything is taken into
account the farmer has got a great deal
smaller price for pork than what he used
to get in the open market. I have no
hesitation in stating that.
Mr. McMAHON: With regard to the
matter raised by Senator Barclay about
the incident where a lady was humiliated
and placed in a cell for four hours later
on, I think this is a matter the House
should take some cognisance of, because
it is really a serious matter if it is true.
I have no doubt it is true. I think the
servants of the public, conductors of
trams, should use a certain amount of
discretion. I have a great deal of sympathy with the relatives of the deceased
lady who was arrested for being drunk,
and that stigma will probably last and
some people might think it was perfectly
true. I do say there should be some inquiry into the matter.
I happen to know of a case that took
place a few months ago where a lady
boarded a tramcar at York Street railway.
way.
She tendered a coin in the usual
Later on the conductor after pro-
Price of Feeding Stuff
Adjournment-

19 DECEMBER 1934
ceeding upstairs came down and said she
tendered a bad shilling-or two-shilling
piece, I do not know which. That lady
was taken into custody.
Later on an
action took place and she was awarded
the very small sum of £5.
I think our
public servants shoud be more careful
in regard to the public, because, after
all, they are the servants of the public.
As far as this case is concerned, I do
say it should not rest where it is at the
present time. It is the first I have heard
of it. I have no hesitation in saying if
it is sent on to the Tramways Committee
it will be fully investigated. If such a
thing happened in England there would
be a terrible outcry. I do not think it
should pass without some notice. The
Leader of the House should notify the
Corporation.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: In connection
with the question of pork which
Senator Barclay raised, I am absolutely convinced, notwithstanding Senator McLaughlin's remarks, that with the
tremendous increase in the quantity of
pork that has occurred during the last
year or two in this country, that the
price of pork, if it were not controlled,
would be nothing like what it is. I do
not think it would be £2 a cwt. to-day,
with the enormous increase that has
taken place. Of course feeding stuff has
gone up admittedly, but I think that
control.
altogether we are better for
Take turkeys. A lady beside me sells
turkeys at 7d a pound, and I go to a
butcher's shop and he charges me a
shilling. I think we are better under
control.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: With regard to the tramway matter which
Senator Barclay raised, if he had given
me notice of the question I could have
had inquiries made and found out what
truth there was in the whole matter.
Of course tramcar conductors probably
find it very difficult to say exactly what
a person, who is obviously incapable, is
suffering from, and they might make
mistakes. The police might wish to
have a doctor and a doctor might not be
available. These are only answers given
on the spur of the moment. I do not
know anything. I can only regret what
happened, and I will cause inquiries to
be made. With regard to the question
of feeding stuff, I would like to say the
Ministry of Agriculture has no authority
relating to the control of the price of
feeding stuffs as they are nearly entirely
imported.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter before Four
o'clock until Friday
next at Ten Minutes
after Twelve noon.
Message from the Commons.
Royal Assent
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Friday, 21st December, 1934.
The Senate met at Ten Minutes
after Twelve of the
Mr.
clock
noon,
DEPUTY-SPEAKER (Mr. T.
McLaughlin) in the Chair).
Paper Presented.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Report on Administration of Home
Office Services, 1933.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have agreed to the
Amendments made by the Senate to the
Agricultural Marketing (Pig Industry)
Bill without amendment.
Royal Assent.
Mr. LONG: I am authorised by the
Prime Minister, on his behalf, to inform the Senate that His Grace the
Governor of Northern Ireland, in the
name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his
Assent to the following Bills agreed
upon by both Houses:
Consolidated Fund,
Diseases of Animals (Dairy Cattle),
Agricultural Marketing (Pig Industry).
Adjournment.
Resolved: "That this House do now
adjourn until such date as Mr.
Speaker shall appoint."-(Mr. Long).
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty Minutes after
Twelve o'clock noon until such date as Mr.
Speaker shall appoint.
